Gottfried Wilhelm Leibniz, 1704:

‘And we could introduce a Universal Symbolism - a very popular one, better than theirs [the Chinese] – if in place of words we used little diagrams which represented visible things pictorially and invisible things by means of the visible ones which go with them, also bringing in certain additional marks suitable for conveying inflections and particles. This would at once enable us to communicate easily with remote peoples; but if we adopted it among ourselves (though without abandoning ordinary writing), the use of this way of writing would be of great service in enriching our imaginations and giving us thoughts which were less blind and less verbal than our present ones are. Of course not everyone knows how to draw, so that apart from books printed in this manner, which everyone would soon learn to read, some people would only be able to make use of this system by printing of a sort – by having engravings ready to use for printing the pictures on paper and then adding the marks for the inflections and particles by pen. But in time everyone would learn to draw during childhood, so as to be able to take advantage of this pictorial symbolism; it would literally speak to the eyes, and would be much liked by the populace.'

New essays on human understanding, book 4, chapter 6: 'Of universal propositions, their truth and certainty'.

Eric Kindel & Sue Walker

Isotype: design and contexts, 1925–1971 is a product of Isotype revisited', a research project that has aimed to re-evaluate and expand our knowledge and understanding of Isotype, taking in its wide range of activities. The book joins an earlier publication, Otto Neurath's visual autobiography, From hieroglyphics to Isotype (Hyphen Press, 201o), an exhibition, 'Isotype: international picture language' (Victoria & Albert Museum, London, December 2010 to March 2011), and other items of research including articles and papers, symposia, and a website.

Isotype: design and contexts is built in large part on the framework of the v&A exhibition, though it is not intended as a catalogue.¹ Rather, it expands and deepens the summary of the exhibition to demonstrate more fully how Isotype both evolved and endured over more than four decades. Alongside essays by members of the Isotype revisited' project team, which distill their specialist areas of research (Burke, Kindel, Minns, Walker), we are very pleased to add studies by Benjamin Benus and Hisayasu Ihara on topics integral to the Isotype story, and also the core of Robin Kinross's often-cited but unpublished thesis, Otto Neurath's contribution to visual communication (1925-45): the history, graphic language and theory of Isotype' (1979). While we address key aspects and episodes of Isotype, no claim is made to completeness, as there remain many areas of Isotype yet to be fully explored. This is testament to the astonishing productivity of Otto Neurath and his colleagues, and of Marie Neurath throughout a prolific working life that continued for many years after her husband's death.

In bringing this book to publication, we acknowledge and thank the Arts & Humanities Research Council, whose funding provided the wellspring for 'Isotype revisited'. We offer our gratitude to the project's advisory group, Robin Kinross, Michael Twyman, and the late Paul Stiff, for their guidance and encouragement; and also to colleagues in the Department of Typography & Graphic Communication, University of Reading, for supporting our research during the project and after. Finally, we thank our publisher, Hyphen Press, for its continued and undiminished enthusiasm for presenting Isotype in vibrant and substantial ways.

Introduction

Christopher Burke

The trajectory of Isotype could in some respects be considered typical of the modern movement in the twentieth century. It was a method of graphic design created in Central Europe by a group of people with strong left-wing affiliations, who were forced into exile during the 193os and had to adapt to new circumstances. Isotype began as the Vienna Method of Pictorial Statistics, developed during a period of enlightened, municipal socialism in the capital of the First Austrian Republic. Forced to flee from Austrian fascists, its principal figures moved to the Netherlands in 1934, and then, escaping the Nazi invasion they fled to England, where the Isotype Institute was established and remained in operation until 1971.

Yet, in other respects, Isotype was not typical. Unlike some noted examples of modernist graphic design, it was not initially developed by artists moving into ‘applied art'. The artist Gerd Arntz became an essential member of the Isotype team, but its initiator, Otto Neurath, and principal designer, Marie Neurath (née Reidemeister), had scientific backgrounds. The principal motive of Isotype was educational, not formal. While the graphic style of Isotype bore similarities to examples of inter-war 'functionalist' design, it was also informed by the analytical approach to language developed in the Vienna Circle, of which Otto Neurath was a founding member. It is ironic that Neurath, one of those responsible for the 'linguistic turn' in philosophy of the twentieth century, should have been concerned during the last twenty years of his life with developing a pictorial language'.

Creating such a language', with claims to universality, is now considered Utopian. It almost goes without saying that it could never attain its ultimate aim. The ambitions for universality of visionary innovators frequently founder on their lack of power to impose change. (Otto Neurath was more aware than most of the challenges involved in

1. 'Educator describes "Picture Esperanto", The New York Times, 10 January 1933, p. 25.

2. Letter Marie Neurath to Bliss, 30 March 1950 (ic 1/19); published (with slight transcription errors) in 'Semantography and Neurath's Isotypes', Semantography series, no. 172, p. 8. Italic corresponds to underlining in original. It is intriguing that the developers of three 'international languages' of the twentieth century – Ludwig Zamenhof, Neurath, and Bliss – were from similar backgrounds in Central and Eastern Europe.

3. It should not be presumed from this that Neurath was unaware of them. For example, he did not mention two of his visual touchstones in From hieroglyphics to Isotype, Comenius's Orbis sensualium pictus and the French Encyclopédie, although he mentioned them in the contemporary text 'Visual education: humanisation versus popularisation' (1945)

4. In a letter to Mr Burns of the Scottish Reconstruction Committee (6 January 1944) Neurath described Scotland as 'one of the centres of scientific civilization' (1C 1/5).

implementing reform due to his experience in economic socialization after the First World War.) Isotype was more often described as ‘international' than universal', and by this measure it achieved significant success – in the UK and the USA, in the UssR, and in Africa (see chapters 5 to 11). In his less rhetorical moments, Neurath made clear that Isotype could never be a complete language – it was an auxiliary' means, which could at best be described as 'language-like'. While Isotype was at least once described as a picture esperanto', it did not have a grammar and vocabulary that required extensive learning.¹ Marie Neurath reflected on this when writing to Charles Bliss, the inventor of the full, symbolic language of Blissymbolics: 'Isotype seems to me much less utopian, as it is only an addition and uses ordinary words, so it can much more easily fit in and does not ask for any training in the reader (only in the writer who is one of very few).’²

5. Marie Neurath & Robin Kinross, The transformer, p. 97.

## The heritage of Isotype

In his visual autobiography, From hieroglyphics to Isotype, Otto Neurath recollected his personal interaction with educational pictures of the past and attempted to identify threads connecting them with Isotype. His text was not intended as a thorough historical survey of graphic presentation, and he neglected to mention some significant predecessors.³ Perhaps the most surprising omission from Neurath's account was the work of William Playfair, a great innovator in statistical graphics. He was a marginal figure of the Scottish Enlightenment, a period which gave rise to several thinkers admired by Neurath.4 Playfair developed a mature form of line graph in several publications beginning with The commercial and political atlas (1786). Given Neurath's background in political economy, it seems likely that he would have come across Playfair's work; but, as Robin Kinross has suggested, Neurath was perhaps concerned to present Isotype in his visual autobiography as part of a wider and longer tradition, and to thereby counter the impression that it dealt only with statistics.⁵ Although Isotype began as the 'Vienna Method of Pictorial Statistics', it had always included non-statistical graphics in its repertoire.

Playfair has also been credited with inventing several other kinds of statistical graph, including the bar chart. Intriguingly, his inclusion of a bar chart in the first edition of his atlas was a one-off, and he gave the excuse of insufficient data for not having produced a line graph, which he considered a superior form. From among Playfair's inventions, the bar chart leads most obviously to Isotype: the archetypal form of Isotype chart – rows of pictograms along the x-axis with time running on the y-axis – is essentially an illustrated bar chart. Playfair's enthusiasm for line graphs was not shared by Marie Neurath, who considered the lines drawn between exact data points to be meaningless.

![](images/e25f31de945a8a65423e7e4209a37d3679312f40bd9b76c142afe737fcf81840.jpg)

Nevertheless, the use of colour and a certain graphic consistency in Playfair's charts prefigure Isotype strategies. Moreover, there is a remarkable similarity between the reasons given by Playfair for choosing graphic presentation and those given by Otto Neurath. Playfair's belief that the eye is the best judge of relative proportions, and that a graphic method rendered data more memorable than a table of numerals, were echoed precisely by Neurath over a hundred years later.'

A kind of theoretical bar chart appeared centuries before Playfair as a woodcut illustration in Nicolas Oresme's Tractatus de latitudinus formarum (1486).8 Sigfried Giedion called it the first graphical representation of movement', and remarked that it anticipated the invention of x- & y-axes as part of a coordinate system by Descartes in the seventeenth century." Both Oresme's and Playfair's achievements were treated at length by the pioneering historian of statistical graphics,

are subject to much stronger demands for precision than pictorial representation. And so it is all the more embarrassing when they do not fulfil these demands. A method of statistics that works with countable elements, as pictorial statistics

Pages from the article on 'Rounding off and omission in pictorial statistics' in the journal Das Bild (October 1928). The article is uncredited but was almost certainly written by Otto Neurath with Marie Reidemeister. On the right-hand page are given source statistics for marriages in Vienna, and bar charts based on these: the four variants show the effects of dividing the bars into coarser or finer units. The left-hand page shows the pictorial chart that results from taking the bar chart with the coarsest units and replacing these units with pictograms.

7. See the quotations from Playfair usefully collected by Patricia Costigan-Eaves and Michael Macdonald-Ross in 'William Playfair (1759–1823)', Statistical Science, vol. 5, no. 3, 199o, pp. 318–26. Albert Biderman suggested that the emergence of statistical graphs was intertwined with that of positivism, and remarked on the validity of this observation with respect to Neurath. 'The Playfair enigma: the development of the schematic representation of statistics', Information Design Journal, vol. 6, no.1, 199o, p.10.

8. It has been suggested that the chart is misattributed to Oresme and may date from a manuscript c. 139o. See Biderman, 'The Playfair enigma', pp. 9–11.

9. Giedion, Mechanization takes command: a contribution to anonymous history (New York: Oxford University Press, 1948), p. 16. This book appeared three years after Neurath finished writing From hieroglyphics to Isotype. Again, it seems probable that Neurath knew of Oresme, given Neurath's appreciation of medieval scholasticism. Neurath often mentioned his intellectual debt to Pierre Duhem, who wrote about Oresme as a precursor of Copernicus.

6. Marie Reidemeister, 'Die Kunst der statistischen Darstellung', Kulturwille, Jg. 4, Nr. 9, September 1927, p. 194. See also her similar article 'Kurven in der statistischen Darstellung', Das Bild (February 1928) in which she stated: 'Curves

H. Gray Funkhouser, in his thorough study, 'Historical development of the graphical representation of statistical data' (1937). Funkhouser perceived precedents for Isotype in the work of Michael Mulhall (1836– 190o), who suggested that ‘we might represent all statistics by pictures' and Jacques Bertillon (1851–1922), who proposed a consistent, physiological use of colours.

10. Examples can be seen in Marija Dalbello & Anselm Spoerri, 'Statistical representations from popular texts for the ordinary citizen, 1889–1914', Library & Information Science Research, vol. 28, issue 1, 2006, pp. 93-4. 11. 'Bildstatistik' (1927), p. 101.

The representation of statistics with pictures adds an iconic dimension that was considered essential by Neurath for engaging the attention of ordinary people (in other words, non-specialists in economics or statistics). He was naturally aware of the kind of pictorial statistics common in Central Europe around the turn of the twentieth century, which were employed in popular atlases produced under the auspices of the Habsburg Empire.1º These usually indicated greater quantity by enlarging the picture, which Neurath characterized as a systematically false construction', and he defined Isotype against it by a consistent correlation of form and size to quantity.

![](images/03c35a627d4d831c30eabcfdb5ba80766b0ab6a9bda199696ee9eae8e0bf9517.jpg)  
'Romania: figurative map with distribution by counties of the main agricultural products, cattle, and forests'.  
Sketched and drawn (ebosata si desenata) by Jon J. Mierescu. Bucharest, 1905. 840 × 938 mm. (iC 3.1/7)

Example of bad system: signs of different sizes Number of men getting married in Germany out of every 10,000 persons

![](images/9cde74c09122d0a2783210ee79bace7a80bf473735c8d9bc0fe967b8aac5bf1d.jpg)

'Bad' and ‘good' methods of depicting the same data, according to Otto Neurath in his book International picture language (1936). Neurath's objection to the method shown above is that the viewer is not sure what to compare visually: the height of the pictures or their area.

The example on the right shows the first principle of Isotype: 'A sign is representative of a certain amount of things; a greater number of signs is representative of a greater amount of things.' (p.73)

## N521, 522, 524.

Greater quantity is shown by enlarging pictures (sometimes almost imperceptibly) in these examples from Prof. A. L. Hickmanns geographisch-statistischer Universal-Atlas, compiled by Leo Hickmann and first published in 1895. The reproductions here were made from a later edition of this atlas and kept in the reference files of the Gesellschaftsund Wirtschaftsmuseum in Wien (Social and economic museum of Vienna) during the early years of developing the Vienna Method of Pictorial Statistics. Two of the subjects here – number of cars in the world and (below left) races and peoples of the world – went on to be standard themes of charts in the Vienna Method and Isotype, as if to show that they could be done differently.

## Men Getting Married in Germany in a Year

![](images/9de6e4de3de142592ee2212274311431c8f35724fb7f7ef73921e76939ea4fb4.jpg)

## The legacy of Isotype

Otto Neurath explained: 'It is an essential quality of the Isotype method that it avoids emotional means. We want to transfer factual knowledge. Much of this knowledge may stimulate emotions, but that is not an element of the presentation.'¹² This detachment was achieved largely through schematic representation in the pictorial style of Isotype, with the decisive role played by Gerd Arntz in designing modular pictograms. The pictographic nature of Isotype may lead us to detect its influence in the simplified pictograms used on public information signs and, increasingly, in digital interfaces. However, Otto and Marie Neurath believed that the essence of Isotype was the activity of 'transformation' the analysis of data and the shaping of it into draft, graphic form. Near to the end of her career, Marie Neurath reflected on this:

It was often the case that principally graphic designers were acquainted with our work – the style of strict representation made an impression on them, independently of the disciplined statement made in that way. So what remained was mostly superficial imitation. For this reason it seems to me still important to explain and promote the special function of transforation. Perhaps it is necessary to find a better name for it.¹³

The transformer was a prototype of the modern information designer.

Despite the eloquent examples of Isotype, supported by Otto Neurath's explanatory writings, its principles have not been entirely absorbed into graphic culture. This may partly be due to its developers never having fully articulated a theory or method. This was deliberate, and the reasons given were pragmatic; but it means that there is no Isotype manual. Some subsequent, statistical graphics have followed Isotype to varying degrees, but many of the errors that Neurath perceived almost a century ago still persist.

12. Letter Neurath to Secker & Warburg, 23 July 1942 (Ic 1/41). 13. Marie Neurath, 'Żum 40. Geburtstag des Gesellschafts- und Wirtschaftsmuseums in Wien' (1965), p. 26.

It is not the aim of this book to establish that Isotype could or should be revived for general application. However, it is implicit in the following chapters that lessons remain to be learned from Isotype – from the thinking behind it, from its method, and from the skill of its graphic execution. The best way to bring these to the fore is to examine it as a historical phenomenon in all the complexities of its contexts. An attempt is made to present Isotype in its own voice: in the words of its practitioners and in images of its artefacts. The chapters of this book amount to a case study in the systematic development of graphic design with a social conscience and with as scientific a basis as is possible.

Some texts in this book adopt the shorthand (favoured by Otto Neurath in his later years) of referring to work from the Vienna period as Isotype, whereas this name was only invented after the move to the Netherlands. Beyond this, the subjects included here are determined by a relatively strict definition of what constitutes Isotype. Essentially, this means work done at the Gesellschafts- und Wirtschaftsmuseum in Wien or its affiliate, the Mundaneum, and its direct successors, the International Foundation for Visual Education and the Isotype Institute. The graphic output of statistical institutes in the Netherlands, where Arntz worked after the Second World War without the Neuraths, is not included; neither, in general, is work done by rivals or imitators (see chapter 7 for some treatment of this matter concerning the relationship of Rudolf Modley to Isotype). This does not reflect an attempt to close off the idea of influence or connection; it is simply matter of limiting scope. Additionally, the nature of Isotype work was closely bound to the views and preferences of the strong personalities who developed it: Otto and Marie Neurath were often involved in conception and editing as well as design. Marie Neurath summarized their approach, revealing the inevitable human agency in producing apparently neutral graphics:

We think out which is the point that has to be brought home, and then we try to do so in such a way that everybody will grasp it. We avoid distracting the attention from the more important issues 'What is important', of course, is a matter of judgement, and here the educationalist has his part to play.

Some previous accounts of Isotype have emphasized the contributions of Otto Neurath and Gerd Arntz at the expense of Marie Neurath. This book should serve to bring to the fore her essential role as the first Isotype transformer' and the leader of the Isotype Institute for more than three decades after Otto Neurath's death. Her surname changed during the period covered by this book: until 1941 she was Marie Reidemeister. In principle, her name is given as it was at the time of the activity being discussed, although some inconsistencies may inevitably remain.

While no book can possibly encompass the variety of Isotype work, some idea is given here of its remarkable range, from interactive cornershop displays to documentary films and books for children.

In recent philosophical literature, Otto Neurath has been recovered from a lingering reputation as a hardline positivist. Similarly, this book may help to fill out the picture of Isotype as more than an example of reductionist modernism. The use of simplified pictures to represent

14. Marie Neurath, 'Isotype in films' (1946), p. 12.

classes of people and categories of things had some roots in Otto Neurath's unorthodox Marxist standpoint. Yet he knew that conceptual and graphic simplification would not limit individual interpretation of Isotype material. Today, discussion of Isotype must contend with the postmodern fear of a person being 'reduced to a statistic'. Neurath insisted, on the contrary, that Isotype was humanizing' knowledge by enabling a 'procedure from the simplest to the most complicated'. The ideas behind Isotype were not repressive but pluralist:

We may create certain conventions in language without unifying the laws; a world language does not imply a world dictatorship but may help world understanding. For a democratic society it is important to have a common knowledge in a common language.¹⁵

## Pictogram – a note on terminology

In this book the term pictogram' will be used for the simplified, modular pictures designed by Gerd Arntz and others for Isotype. Although this may seem initially anachronistic – because pictogram was not a word contemporary with that design work – it now has common currency and offers a more correct description than the term symbol' Isotype pictures are icons, which directly depict the objects they represent, whereas a symbol 'stands for, represents, or denotes something else (not by exact resemblance, but by vague suggestion, or by some accidental or conventional relation)', according to the Oxford English Dictionary. (Isotype pictograms depicting the sickle or a cog-wheel are exceptions in that, while being iconic themselves, they are symbols for agriculture and industry respectively.) Gerd Arntz himself commented late in his life that pictogram was a word 'blown over' from the usA after his work on Isotype.16 The word perhaps gained its currency in the USA (the earliest usage, from 1895, listed in the OED is American); pictograph' was in use earlier, and this term was applied to Isotype in the American context of Compton's pictured encyclopedia, although there it was used as a name for whole Isotype charts, not individual pictograms.17 So, in order to avoid confusion with this precedent, and to align with common, current usage, pictogram will be used here for denoting the individual, component pictures of Isotype. In fact, there is some evidence that Neurath favoured this word, and may even have thought that he was coining it, when he wrote in 1929 to Paul Otlet:

we must have an international word for every mass of pictures! Quantogram works also with Teleki-Diagrams! Pictogram? Also Pictocartogram, pictochronogram, pictoquantogram. Although I do not find these creations terribly charming. But chemical formulas are also just boring!18

He was apparently suggesting 'Piktogramm' (in his original German) here also as a term for whole charts and diagrams, but nevertheless this shows that he was open to the word.

17. One of the citations for 'pictograph' in the oED is from Rudolf

Modley's How to use pictorial statistics (1937), where it seems to have been used in the same way. 18. Letter Neurath to Otlet, 22 October 1929, slightly modified from the translation given in Vossoughian, 'The language of the world museum: Otto Neurath, Paul Otlet, Le Corbusier', p. 93 (also given there in German).

16. 'Gerd Arntz', interview in Pulchri, p. 4. The words most often used for pictograms at the Gesellschafts- und Wirtschaftsmuseum were Signatur and Zeichen (sign) Piktogram has also now become standard in German: see Martin Krampen et al, Die Welt der Zeichen: globale Kommunikation mit Piktogrammen (Ludwigsburg: Avedition,

## Archive sources

The principal archive used for research towards this book, and for illustration of material, is the Otto and Marie Neurath Isotype Collection housed in the Department of Typography & Graphic Communication, University of Reading. This archive is abbreviated to 'ic' throughout the book, followed by catalogue numbers of items within it. A further system of catalogue numbers is evident in some captions, which may begin with a number prefaced by a 'T' or 'N'. These numbers relate to the photographic reference files assembled at the Gesellschafts- und Wirtschaftsmuseum in Wien between 1925 and 1934, with some later additions made in the Netherlands. (See p.1o8 for further explanation of the T and N files.) Where an image has been taken from these files (or where otherwise possible) its reference number has been given.

The following abbreviations are used for archives:

GWM-MSI Gesellschafts- und Wirtschaftsmuseum correspondence, archive of the Museum of Science and Industry, Chicago.

IC Otto and Marie Neurath Isotype Collection.

IISH International Institute of Social History / Netherlands Economic History Archive, Amsterdam

HDLP-YUL Harold Dwight Lasswell Papers, Manuscripts and Archives, Yale University Library, New Haven.

HwoP-SHC Howard W. Odum Papers, Southern\_Historical Collection, Wilson Library, University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill.

MVKP-SSC Mary van Kleeck Papers, Sophia Smith Collection, Smith College, Northampton, MA.

NYWF-NYPL New York World's Fair 1939 and 1940, Incorporated Records, Manuscripts and Archives Division, New York Public Library.

ÖNB Otto & Marie Neurath Nachlass, Österreichische Nationalbibliothek, Vienna.

SAR-SWHA Survey Associates records, Social Welfare History Archives, University of Minnesota.

USMP-UCL The Unity of Science Movement Papers, University of Chicago Library.

VCA Vienna Circle Archive, Noord-Hollands Archief, Haarlem.

VGA Verein für Geschichte der Arbeiterbewegung, Vienna.

WLL Wiener Library, London.

## Editorial notes

Where no author is given for a publication referred to in a footnote, its author is Otto Neurath. For sources cited repeatedly throughout the book, a short version of the publication title is given in a footnote and the full details can be found in the bibliography. Details of publications referred to more than once within a single chapter are given in full on first mention in a footnote.

Otto Neurath wrote increasingly in English after 1934, and almost exclusively so after 194o. While his English is rarely unclear, it is grammatically unorthodox, and so readers should bear this in mind when encountering citations from his correspondence and unpublished texts.

All translations are by respective authors unless otherwise stated.

In chapter 5, Russian names and place names are transliterated according the Library of Congress system, apart from those with well-established alternative spellings, such as Lissitzky and Joseph Stalin.

Gerd Arntz (right) in a portrait by August Sander titled 'Geistesarbeiter des Proletariats' (Intellectual workers of the proletariat), c.1925. With him are Else Schuler, Franz Wilhelm Seiwert, and the French writer Tristan Rémy (standing).

## Otto Neurath, 1919.

A photograph taken by Heinrich Hoffmann during Neurath's time in Munich as an official of the unstable, revolutionary government in Bavaria. It was published in the booklet Ein Jahr bayrische Revolution im Bilde (One year of Bavarian revolution in pictures; Munich: Photobericht Hoffmann, 1919). There it was featured on a page of vignetted portraits under the title 'Characterköpfe aus der Räterepublik' (Distinctive features from the Soviet Republic). The accompanying text (by Emil Herold) hinted that the Jewish features of many of the Republic's officials correlated with their radical left-wing policies. Neurath was distinguished among those pictured (including Eugen Leviné, Erich Mühsam, Max Levien, Ernst Toller and Gustav Landauer) in being referred to with his title, 'Dr Neurath' (but without his first name): he was described as 'Socialization commissar, whose experiments formed the final stimulus for the proclamation of the Soviet Republic'.

Foreign correspondents were not immune to the prevalent anti-semitic tone of reaction to the Republic: in The Times of

London (2 May 1919) half of a report on 'The Sparatacist programme' was devoted to Neurath – 'an extremist leader'. He was decribed as follows: 'Dr. Neurath, who is a pale, blackbearded man with a strongly Jewish cast of features, is a Viennese.' Perhaps the unnamed reporter based this judgement solely on the monochrome portrait shown here; other, more favourably disposed observers described Neurath's hair as red, not black. When recalling her first impression of Neurath (in September 1924), Marie Neurath described him as ‘a red-bearded giant'.

The photographer Hoffmann went on to be an early member of the National Socialist Party and, as part of Hitler's inner circle, he became party photographer' – only he was allowed to take portraits of the Führer. Hoffmann initiated the illustrated Nazi newspaper, Illustrierter Beobachter, which republished this portrait in 1932 among those of Neurath's Munich colleagues and described him as 'The Jew Neurath'.

# 1 The Gesellschafts- und Wirtschaftsmuseum in Wien (Social and economic museum of Vienna)ª 1925-34

Christopher Burke

## Background

The Gesellschafts- und Wirtschaftsmuseum originated from the interaction of Otto Neurath's educational ambitions with the context of Social-Democratic Vienna in the 192os. Its direct predecessor was the Museum für Siedlung und Städtebau (Museum for settlement and town-planning), which had grown from exhibitions prepared under Neurath's direction by the Österreichische Verband für Siedlungs- und Kleingartenwesen (övsk; Austrian association for settlement and allotment gardens). Neurath had co-founded this organization in 1921 to assist in formalizing a grass roots movement of settlers who had been erecting makeshift houses and establishing allotment gardens on the periphery of Vienna since the end of the First World War. Amid astronomic currency inflation, this example of self-help to fulfil the pressing need for housing included elements of a barter economy, which fitted Neurath's brand of socialism.²

Neurath had returned to Vienna in February 192o on being released from an eighteen-month, suspended prison sentence in Munich. Having been held in custody for six weeks pending trial, he was convicted there of assisting high treason in his role as president of the Zentralwirtschaftsamt (Central office of economics) during the short-lived Bavarian Soviet Republic.³ Although there is a remarkable consistency in the ideas and principles underlying the varied activities of Neurath's adult life, his participation in Bavarian social reform marked a kind of break with his academic trajectory before the First World War.4 At the time he wrote:

The hesitations and vacillations of those called upon to act, the advice of my friends and sundry accidental circumstances, finally moved me, after much reflection, to conclude my life of contemplation and to begin one of action, to help to introduce an administrative economy that will bring happiness.

1. This was a contemporary English translation used, for example, in Neurath's article 'Museums of the future' (1933). A more elaborate English version of the Museum's title was given on its letterhead (c.1931) as 'Museum for sociological and economic science in Vienna'.

2. On the settlement movement, and Neurath's role in it, see Eve Blau, The architecture of Red Vienna, pp. go-131; Klaus Novy & Wolfgang Förster, Einfach Bauen (Vienna: Picus, 1991); Robert Hoffmann, 'Proletarisches Siedeln', in Friedrich Stadler (ed.), Arbeiterbildung in der Zwischenkriegszeit: Otto Neurath – Gerd Arntz, pp.140-8.

3. See Günther Sandner, 'Der Plan der Pläne: der Philosoph Otto Neurath und die Münchner Räterepublik', Neue Zürcher Zeitung (Nr. 187, 13 August 2011), p. 60.

4. This suggestion is made by Elisabeth Nemeth in "Freeing up one's point of view": Neurath's Machian heritage compared', in Nemeth, Stefan W. Schmitz & Thomas E. Uebel (ed.), Otto Neurath's economics in context (Vienna Circle Institute Yearbook 13, 2007), pp. 16–17.

5. 'Through war economy to economy in kind' (Preface dated April 1919, a month before his arrest) in Neurath, Empiricism and sociology, p. 124.

6. The death of Neurath's first wife Anna Schapire, due to complications after childbirth, provoked him to break his ties with Vienna and seek work elsewhere. Letter Neurath to Ferdinand Tönnies 14 September 1911 (Nachlass Ferdinand Tönnies, Schleswig-Holsteinische Landesbibliothek).

7. Marie Neurath, 'Otto Neurath and Isotype', p. 11. The extract above is her translation from Otto Neurath and Wolfgang Schumann, 'Zur Einführung', Wirtschaft und Lebensordnung (supplement to Kunstwart), Nr.1, 1919, pp. 1–2.

8. Letter Neurath to Josef Frank, 8 July 1939 (Nachlaß Otto & Marie Neurath, Österreichische Nationalbibliothek [öNB] 1219/3).

9. Stefan Zweig, The world of yesterday (London: Cassell [Hallam edition], 1953), p. 281

10. Eric Hobsbawm, Interesting times: a twentieth-century life (London: Abacus, 2002), p. 8.

It could be argued that the real break occurred even earlier with Neurath's field work on war economy, which led to the initiation of a new department at the war ministry in Vienna during the First World War, and to his becoming the director of a new Deutschen Kriegswirtschaftsmuseum (German museum of war economy) in Leipzig. The ‘aims' and means' of that museum were recorded as follows:

Aims: general education and research in political economy. Means: comprehensive collections of objects of economic importance, samples, models, photographs, statistics, graphic representations and documents, printed matter, cuttings etc. Sections: exhibitions and travelling exhibitions, archives, lecture service, scientific and educational publications.

As Marie Neurath pointed out, these plans were very close to what was later realized at the Gesellschafts- und Wirtschaftsmuseum.?

Twenty years later, Neurath summarized this period in his life:

Then came the war. Then my directorship of the museum in Leipzig (14 days Vienna, 14 days Leipzig) and then the testimony about socialization that had such far-reaching consequences for me. Then it all came crashing down on me and even good friends considered any further career as ruined. But then it began anew. After a few attempts, the settlement association and museum. Collapse of the foundations. New construction.8

This architectural metaphor could also serve to describe the hope that Neurath, among many others, felt for the new era arising from the ruins of the old order in Central Europe. Nowhere can this rupture have been felt more acutely than in Vienna, former centre of the enormous Austro-Hungarian empire that was now splintered into nation states. 'Austria' did not yet exist, and its borders remained unclear while peace treaty negotiations continued. On 12 November 1918 a provisional government proclaimed it to be 'Deutschösterreich' (German-Austria), part of the new German republic established a few days earlier. When the Treaty of St Germain was finally signed in September 1919, it outlawed Anschluss (union) with Germany and forced the new, independent repub lic to change its name to 'Austria'. The first Austrian Federal Republic has been characterized as 'a mutilated trunk that bled from every vein' and as ‘not only a state which did not want to exist, but a predicament which could not last'.1º This new country was generally felt to be lebensunfähig (unfit to live), cut off from the resources that now lay outside its borders. Essential supplies were scarce during the first few years of its existence: butter and milk were unobtainable; the population was

undernourished and Vienna would have starved without American aid. Until 192o electricity was cut off at 8 pm; neither streetcars nor metropolitan trains ran until 1925 due to fuel shortages.11

Nevertheless, Neurath was fired up by efforts for reconstruction: 'Before 1914 I felt completely helpless and hopeless in the face of the prospects for society. Not that I personally had any excessive complaints. After the lost war there were more difficulties in the world, but more chances that things could change.'¹² A colleague remembered the striking figure he cut in those days:

I just remember the day when I first met you – attending one of your crowded lectures – you with your marvellous beard, wrapped in a k.u.k. 'Attila' (I think that was the proper name for the short officer coats with fur collar) because there was no coal at that time and we had to sozialisieren' without.13

In this respect, Friedrich Stadler has characterized Neurath as a promoter, innovator and fiery orator before common people, occupying a space between proletariat and bourgeosie'.¹4

On resettling in Vienna in 192o, Neurath attempted unsuccessfully to revive the academic status he had secured at Heidelberg University in 1917, which had been revoked due to his imprisonment in Munich. Instead he turned his formidable energy towards other enterprises, although education remained central in his priorities. The Austrian novelist Robert Musil recorded a meeting with Neurath in his diary around this time, describing him as follows: Has a notebook with very many energetic entries. Matters dealt with are neatly struck through. Is apparently always somewhere else with his thoughts. ... Is now up and about a great deal, setting up contacts in all directions.'⁶

wearing the coat in question in the photograph shown in Novy & Förster, Einfach Bauen, p. 29.

14. Stadler, The Vienna Circle: studies in the origins, development, and influence of logical empiricism, p.505.

15. Neurath had originally hoped to achieve Habilitation (a kind of transferable tenure) at the philosophy faculty of Vienna University (letter Neurath to Tönnies, July/ August 1gog) but later claimed to have seen papers that proved he was excluded from this by antisemitism, in addition to his intellectual unsuitability to the prevalent schools of thought there. (Letter

## T198B Rootstock of a settlement house C.1923/4.

Shows the various material sources and the processes that feed into construction. This chart was displayed at the Museum für Siedlung und Städtebau, although it may have been made before that museum was officially established; it is credited to the 'technical department' of the övsk. An altered version of this chart was made later at the Gesellschafts- und Wirtschaftsmuseum, in which the house is depicted differently and captioned as 'Settlement house with stable, type 157'.

Neurath's first position in Vienna after the war, as director of the Forschungsinstitut für Gemeinwirtschaft (Research institute for cooperative economy), was seemingly created especially for him through the influence of Otto Bauer, leader of the Austrian Social Democrats, who had been instrumental (as Austrian minister for foreign affairs) in securing Neurath's release from Bavaria. Although Neurath felt that this institute was ineffectual in itself, he used it as a springboard: his involvement in the settlement association grew out of it. When later recalling the assistance he received for this institute from Vienna city councillor Emmy Freundlich, he wrote to her: That was the start for all the institutes I could organize afterwards, Museum in Vienna etc.'¹

‘The research institute has attempted to create a scientific focus for activity in statistical organisation', explained Neurath.18 He had been hinting at ways of graphically displaying statistics since 191o, and made

(International Institute of Social History, Amsterdam [IsH], D497). 18. Neurath, 'Das Forschungsinstitut für Gemeinwirtschaft', Die Wage, Nr. 24, 25 June 1921, p. 7. Despite Neurath's statement here that the institute should 'design an economic plan for Austria', it is perhaps a sign of its impotence

![](images/bb8c02d78da47ac907e8f07524f6ce3b8ea89be325b1df4d5614cfb36bf88c18.jpg)

Exhibition at the Neues Rathaus of Vienna in 1923, organized by the Österreichische Verband für Siedlungs- und Kleingartenwesen. Neurath was General Secretary of this organization.

(Below) Chart of pictorial statistics on display inside the Neues Rathaus at the 1923 settlement exhibition, Despite Neurath's later recollection that the first principle of Isotype – showing quantity by repetition not enlargement – was already in evidence here (see quoted extract opposite), this shows that the more traditional approach of using bigger pictures to show greater quantity was also employed. The middle example goes some way towards the Vienna Method, in using the number of carriages attached to a train engine to indicate quantity, although the pictures in the lines with greater quantity are also enlarged.

![](images/d9697354637176af473ea9176ba45395c997cec36b298d0e82613d3257984213.jpg)

![](images/f7b991fb61079025eab38c5225e1780041939633cf882575aedad25c3ca30d66.jpg)  
N487 The Museum für Siedlung und Städtebau displayed real building materials for making comparisons in building technique.

![](images/90646dd754dda79b3aa12adcaea3ea0c68e030a1e508f53fcb1b86e2c22a23d5.jpg)  
N474 Chart shown at the Museum für Siedlung und Städtebau, comparing alternative methods of building brick walls.

an early attempt at realizing them in the Kriegswirtschaftsmuseum in Leipzig.¹º Further trials in graphic depiction of statistics were made for the annual exhibitions of the övsK at Vienna's Neues Rathaus (New town hall) and in the ensuing Museum für Siedlung und Städtebau. The guide-book of the final, annual exhibition in 1923 stated that ‘here the attempt is made to represent graphically the activity of the individual branches of the housing office'.2º Neurath added later:

There, for the first time, we showed what the activities of our members were, not only by presenting fine specimens of vegetables and so forth, but also by depicting the results on big wall charts. For instance, the increase of poultry-keeping in small yards and gardens by our members was shown in bright colours. Where the numbers of poultry had been doubled, we showed a double number of well-arranged bird drawings.2

This was a prototype of the Wiener Methode der Bildstatistik (Vienna Method of Pictorial Statistics) that would be worked out in detail at the Gesellschafts- und Wirtschaftsmuseum. Neurath's desire not to waste these charts and other exhibits led him to propose the setting up of a housing museum to preserve them. He suggested it to the mayor of Vienna, Jakob Reumann, who replied: 'Only if you can get the support of two men of influence for the thing.' By the end of that same day Neurath had secured the support of Julius Deutsch and Emil Palla (Deutsch was a member of the Austrian parliament and founder of the Social Democrat militia, Republikanischer Schutzbund; Palla was First Secretary of the Wiener Arbeiterkammer [Vienna chamber of workers]).2

Neurath wrote later about the Museum für Siedlung und Städtebau (in his raw English):

19. See Neurath, 'Die Kriegswirtschaft', Jahresbericht der Neuen Wiener Handelsakademie, Nr. 16, 1910, p. 5. Although no material survives from the Leipzig museum, a good idea of its activity is given by Nader Vossoughian in Otto Neurath: the language of the global polis, pp. 49-54.

20. Führer durch die Wiener Kleingarten-, Siedlungs- und Wohnbauausstellung, 2-9 September 1923.

21. Neurath, From hieroglyphics to Isotype, p. 99.

22. Marie Neurath, 'Zum 40. Geburtstag des Gesellschafts-und Wirtschaftsmuseums in Wien', Arbeit und Wirtschaft, Nr. 6, 1965 p. 24. Marie Neurath stated that Reumann declared himself prepared to be president of the new museum ('Otto Neurath und die Wiener Methode der Bildstatistik', p. 3). Deutsch was also president of GESIBA (Gemeinwirtschaftliche Siedlungs- und Baustoffanstalt), an organization that Neurath helped to establish for providing building materials to settlers.

![](images/49be0638184c942952a3565121be14e801cd29f9fc05d7c53937202e7b686a35.jpg)  
Neurath showing visitors around the Museum für Siedlung und Städtebau, C.1924.

An early chart (c. 1925) from the Gesellschafts- und Wirtschaftsmuseum showing the relative numbers of single-family, low-rise houses (below) and apartment blocks constructed by the Vienna municipality since 1919. Each window indicates 10o dwellings of each type. The quantities (bounded by dotted lines) projected for 1926 show the priority given to apartment blocks.

23. Otto Neurath, 'The Isotype work', typescript dated 23 May 1943 (1C 3.2/58), p. 3.

24. Soon after the Gesellschaftsund Wirtschaftsmuseum opened, Neurath resigned as secretary of the övsk, Its building office had closed in 1924. As a final attempt to influence Vienna's housing policy around the turn of 1924, Neurath was involved with architects Peter Behrens, Josef Hoffmann, Adolf Loos, Josef Frank, and Oskar Strnad in proposing a new 'general architectural plan' for Vienna that integrated zones of garden housing into the fabric of the city. See Blau, The architecture of Red Vienna, pp.161-5.

25. Neurath to Roh, 19 September 1924 (Papers of Franz Roh; Getty Center for the History of Art and the Humanities).

![](images/3afb3910c492967f206790b6268425034222860d69698e2aa35d99122d45c522.jpg)

I suggested to make this museum as a model museum. A world famous architect [Josef Frank] co-operated with us, we took care of attractive photos, models, put real walls before the visitor and real furniture. But all these coarse details were completed by our charts, which, in a symbolic way, told the story of housing, and of the social background. Usually housing exhibitions deal mainly with the surface' and not with the basic social facts.² 23

After mayor Reumann was succeeded in November 1923 by Karl Seitz, the settlers' movement began to lose influence, and the housing policy of Vienna shifted away from constructing housing estates with gardens to high-rise apartment blocks. Neurath recognized the necessity of this, but it was perhaps his feeling of being disconnected from such activity that encouraged him to conceive a museum with a different and broader focus than the housing museum.²⁴ In September 1924 he commented to the art historian Franz Roh: 'I am coming back into scientific work. The Siedlungsmuseum is a stepping stone towards having a new effect. New, official possibilities are opening up, and much else that will be more satisfying to the intellect.'⁵

## Conception of the Gesellschafts- und Wirtschaftsmuseum

In July 1924, within a year of the Museum für Siedlung und Städtebau being established, Neurath proposed a new museum to replace it. As he later explained, the Siedlungsmuseum already contained ‘a section devoted to the Social Sciences in general because the understanding of city planning has to be based on a better understanding of the pattern of national and world relations'.2 It was this aspect he sought to expand, and so he drafted an outline titled Enlistment of the collections and work of the Austrian settlement museum for the preparation of an exhibition about the Austrian workers' movement', which he sent to Julius Deutsch. The stress he laid in this title, as well as in the outline, on the place of the projected museum within the socialist movement reflected his instinct for the political priorities of those he sought to persuade:

It would be of great importance if we possessed a museum as a means of education, which, directed in the spirit of the workers' movement, could fill the gap left by all that our schools systematically neglect. It is not the lies that are the worst things in schools, but the concealment! One does not learn in school that people get much sicker from tuberculosis in those areas where they are poorest; one does not learn in school of how bad the quality of housing is; one learns nothing in school of slavery, nor of the fight for freedom. Now, a museum should not carry propaganda in a vulgar sense – it must give plain facts! But in the choice of material there is already propaganda. Most museums have a dead effect because they appeal too little to the wants of people! Indeed often a small change would suffice to bring people en masse into museums! If a museum shows, for example, how one can have better housing, clothe oneself better, live more healthily and more purposefully – that would surely be of general interest.27

At this point Neurath was suggesting the name 'Wirtschafts- und Gesellschaftsmuseum' (an inversion of its final title), but the central idea of a popular, educational institution that would 'bring things of daily life into meaningful connections' was already clear.28 In this sense his proposal fitted perfectly the profile of the Social-Democrat regime in Vienna, which laid great emphasis on education: 'Against the idea of force, the force of ideas', was one party slogan.29 Although Neurath held no office in the Social Democrat Party, he was involved in many of its educational initiatives - he even gave classes at the Wiener Arbeiterhochschule, which trained promising young workers to be party functionaries.30

26. 'Isotype Institute and adult education', (1942), p. 4.

27. 'Heranziehung der Sammlungen und Arbeiten des Österreichischen Siedtungsmuseums für die Vorbereitung einer Ausstellung über die Österr. Arbeiterbewegung' July 1924 (Verein für Geschichte der Arbeiterbewegung [vGA]) This outline is accompanied by a letter to Deutsch from Neurath dated 14 July 1924, in which he explains that he had already given a copy to Deutsch's former adjutant at the war ministry, Julius Braunthal, who was then editor of Der Kampf, an Austrian socialist journal to which Neurath contributed regularly.

28. Max Weber's book Wirtschaft und Gesellschaft (published posthumously in 1922) may have provided some inspiration for the name that Neurath proposed initially for the Museum. In his book Weber took seriously Neurath's economic theory of Naturalrechnung (calculation in kind), describing his suggestions as 'penetrating'. Weber, Economy and society (Berkeley: University of California Press, 1978), pp. 104–7. Othmar Spann had also written a book with the same title (published 1907), which Neurath cited in his essay 'Nationalökonomie und Wertlehre, eine systematische Untersuchung', Zeitschrift für Volkswirtschaft, Sozialpolitik und Verwaltung, Band 20, 1911, p. 63.

29. Helmut Gruber, Red Vienna: experiment in working-class culture, 1919–1934, p. 87. By 1931/2 the Austrian Social Democrat Party's loose network of more than forty cultural organizations had a total of 400,000 members in Vienna (p. 81).

30. One afternoon per week Neurath gave a class in methods of visualizing statistics. Reported in Kulturwille, Jg. 4, Nr. 9, 1927, p. 196.

32. Letter Neurath to Deutsch,   
21 August 1924 (vGA).

31. 'Heranziehung der Sammlungen ... (VvGA), p.5. This last element of colour coding remained in place throughout Isotype work

The new museum, Neurath explained, 'must be especially prepared to treat the political struggle of the working class, the development of revolutions, legislation, social policy and much else'; yet he also indicated the broader 'geographical-historical' view that would become typical of work in the Vienna Method and Isotype:

The development of capitalist economic order must be shown in its historical and geographical specificity. It is important to show the whole of the earth's surface with its variety of economic forms in different eras, in order to reflect how the capitalist order gradually takes hold of the whole world and eradicates all other forms of economy.

Additionally, here in his initial outline, Neurath stated the intention to exploit the great experience we have gained in exhibition, museum and advertising technique' at the Siedlungsmuseum, and to build on it. He hinted specifically at the elements of standardization and consistency that would become so important in the Vienna Method:

In order to guarantee that the graphic representation, the method of demonstration, shows a certain unity, the existing depictions and overviews must first be reworked so that the difference between forms of representation do not unsettle the viewer: that applies to the smallest detail. When countries tend to be listed on the vertical axis, it is disturbing when they occur in a certain table on the horizontal axis, merely because this was the case in the source information. Quite apart from this, consistent, connecting colours are important (red for city population, industrial workers etc; green for countryside, etc....)

Already in this early conception by Neurath, the Gesellschafts- und Wirtschaftsmuseum represented a unique combination of social commitment and design methodology.

One month later, in August 1924, Neurath rewrote his proposal for the new museum into a Denkschrift (memorandum) addressed to Anton Weber, city councillor for housing. Perhaps, in the meantime, he had been advised by Deutsch to tone down the socialist rhetoric; when sending him a copy of the new memorandum, Neurath explained: 'It is phrased so as to take account of the neutral position of a proletarian municipal administration; on the other hand, you will see that the general conception and consideration of details rests thoroughly on the basis of the workers' movement.'³²Indeed Neurath now emphasized the broader educational purpose of the projected museum. The clarity and detail of his vision were remarkable:

The modern museum should be a teaching museum, a means of education, a schoolbook on a grand scale...

The Gesellschafts- und Wirtschaftsmuseum is not a technical museum, the objective of which is to show and explain the achievements of the human intellect insofar as they reveal themselves in machines and tools. The Gesellschafts- und Wirtschaftsmuseum shows social connections. It only takes note of technology as a social phenomenon and reflects its effects on the social life and the spatial distribution of people. 33

Apart from revealing Neurath's suspicion of the technological determinism becoming common in the avant-garde of Central-European culture at this time, this also signals the holistic approach that he maintained throughout his life. Already at the beginning of the First World War, Neurath had written to his intellectual hero, Ernst Mach, explaining how Mach had influenced him to derive the meaning of the particular from the whole, and not the whole from a sum of particulars'.³4 Hence his idea of the Museum as a big schoolbook – addressing various subjects, but under one cover, so to speak. The same approach is reflected in his attitude to town planning: in his article of 1924 about the 'general architectural plan' for Vienna, he suggested that ‘a whole city may be considered as one, single architectonic construction'.³

Neurath recognized that such an ambitious plan for the Gesellschaftsund Wirtschaftsmuseum could only be achieved 'step by step', and that the existing Siedlungsmuseum would serve as a foundation to be systematically built on. He even included with his memorandum a diagram sketching the structure of the Museum, stating: 'It is more

![](images/533391d4e697c7d9aa4222aa3a72e4773fe0dbb808ffddceb3071f2a59e265ab.jpg)

Page from Neurath's 'Denkschrift' proposing the concept of the Gesellschafts- und Wirtschaftsmuseum. It shows a diagram of the suggested layout of the Museum. Above it, Neurath stated: 'The plan of such a museum, whether large or small, a hall with three main sections, or three rooms with corresponding displays etc., could be laid out so ...'

33. 'Denkschrift', pp. 1, 3. Neurath used parts of his memorandum text almost unaltered in his first article about the Museum published after its opening (see apperidix A below). 34. Neurath cited in Stadler, The Vienna Circle, p.170.

35. 'Generalarchitekturplan und Bauorganisation' (1924), p. [2]. Ten years later Neurath maintained: 'Everything is in connection with everything else.' ('Radical physicalism and the real world' [1934], p.103.)

than an overview of content because the spatial subdivision is important for the conceptual construction.' He explained the need for adaptability in the layout:

Nevertheless it is recommendable to create the initial installations so that all extensions would be possible without too many changes in the system.

No system is logically perfect, above all one that is determined by a building. In many respects it serves more as an orientation.

Such modularity would become integral to the design of the Museum, and Neurath already perceived the potential in this for travelling exhibitions, enabled also by charts being produced in 'one of the common techniques suitable for rapid and inexpensive mass-production'. Neurath's memorandum also specified the other activities that would complement the Museum's collections: 'lectures with slides and films, guided tours through the Museum, archive and library, publications'.³⁶

The Gesellschafts- und Wirtschaftsmuseum was established on 1 January 1925, although, given that it took over the previous exhibits and premises of the Siedlungsmuseum, there was probably a short period of transition.37 Neurath sometimes gave the date of 1924 for its opening, as in the following account written near the end of his life:

## 36. 'Denkschrift', pp. 4-5.

37. Already in his letter to Deutsch of 21 August 1924, Neurath stated that councillor Weber had just agreed to fund the Museum for a 'September opening'. Marie Neurath later recalled that the rooms of the Siedlungsmuseum were 'gradually redesigned by replacement of charts and models and were re-opened as the permanent exhibition space of the Gesellschafts- und Wirtschaftsmuseum in Wien in either 1925 or 1926. (See appendix c below, pp. 527–8.)

38. Otto Neurath, 'The Isotype work' (Ic 3.2/58), p. 4. He also gave 1924 as the year of the Museum's beginning in 'Das Gesellschaftsund Wirtschaftsmuseum in Wien (1931), p. 192.

The exhibition [at the Siedlungsmuseum] was fully successful and the town councillors, interested in our housing organization, treated this kind of enlightenment seriously. I explained to them that in a democracy we can only expect help from citizens who understand a problem and are able to argue pro and con. Newspapers reach only their subscribers, the opposition does not read what the others' have to say. The readership of books is small, the plain man in the street does not even buy books, but everybody enters exhibitions, without any distinction of party etc. It is a remarkable fact that exhibitions are regarded as 'neutral'. They reach the broadest masses.

Therefore I suggested giving me an opportunity to create a museum of international standard, with all departments necessary for progressing at full speed. In 1924 the Gesellschafts- und Wirtschaftsmuseum was founded. I became the director and had all opportunities to select my collaborators and to go on without limitations of any kind. After some deliberation, we decided to make the Museum an independent institute attached to the municipality (the accountant nominated by the municipality).38

A significant step in this direction was taken during the first visit to Vienna by Marie Reidemeister, who became Neurath's principal collaborator and later his wife. She travelled to Vienna in September

![](images/b952edf2aed622691cd8649b00c9602b0766607695e7125a32bf60000f61b36e.jpg)

1924 with a group from Göttingen University, shortly before she took her final university examinations there. She met Neurath through her brother, the mathematician Kurt Reidemeister, who was then teaching at Vienna university and was an early member of the Vienna Circle. She recalled her introduction to the Siedlungsmuseum:

Otto showed me his Siedlungsmuseum one day. Besides estate maps, ground-plans and photographs, there were also sections of wall, in which different ways of arranging bricks were shown; and in one corner was a model kitchen designed by Grete Lihotzky, who worked as an architect with the Siedlerverband. I was especially impressed by some graphic presentations, which dealt with settlement and city development; in spite of their simplicity, they had a striking – and unforgettable – effect and stated things worth knowing. One chart treated population density: dots representing a certain number of people were regularly distributed over areas divided into units, so that population density was immediately apparent and could be easily compared for different districts. Another chart showed, through black dots, the five largest cities in different countries: in centralistic' France, Paris dominating, the next largest rapidly diminishing; in Britain, London

## Distribution of population in Vienna according to the 1923 census

Each dot = 5,000 inhabitants.

This was one of the charts Marie Neurath recalled from her first visit to the Siedlungsmuseum in 1924.

dominating, but then several equally considerable centres in the provinces; in Germany, Berlin, the capital of Prussia, not particularly outstanding along with other capitals and Hanseatic cities. Otto saw how impressed I was, and asked me if I could also perhaps design things of this kind; but what should I say – I had never seen anything quite like it before. 'But', he asked, 'if I started a museum where such charts are designed, would you be willing to join in?' To which I replied, without qualifications: 'yes', and I meant it. Otto went on, more to himself: 'now I know that I can do it'. He started on the preparations at once.³

39. Marie Neurath, 'What I remember', p. 29.

## VERRBRAUCH AN WICHTIGSTEN NAHRUNGSMITTELN

VERBRAUCH IN ENGLAND IST GLEICH 10 GESETZT.  
![](images/25e8ffdb1622f199ccfba183137889fad309662dea9f8007ec58f63e30e81b60.jpg)

Consumption of the most important foodstuffs C. 1924 Figures for England are set to a base point of 10. (There is a spelling mistake in the first word of the title.)

Marie Reidemeister had studied mathematics and physics in Munich, Göttingen and Berlin; Max Planck's lectures on theoretical physics at Berlin university made an especially strong impression on her.4º She had also studied geometrical drawing and statistics, and had taken classes in drawing and calligraphy at the art school in Braunschweig, her native city. It is no surprise, then, that Otto Neurath recognized her immediately as somebody whose particular combination of skills could help to further his museum plans. She was a witness to another decisive meeting that took place in early October 1924:

As the next step he invited the finance minister of the municipality of Vienna, Hugo Breitner, for a discussion in the Siedlungsmuseum; I sat waiting in Grete Lihotzky's wood-lined kitchen, while Otto showed Breitner round, outlining to him the plan for a 'Gesellschafts- und Wirtschaftsmuseum'; it was quite a long time before Breitner responded; he asked what the annual costs would probably be; Otto had not yet thought of that, quickly did some mental calculation and mentioned a sum; to which Breitner replied: 'that can certainly be done'. For Otto, it was now a matter of winning over the mayor and getting the consent of the government of the municipality to found the 'Gesellschafts- und Wirtschaftsmuseum in Wien' as a formal body, and for me – of completing my state exams.⁴1

Schütte-Lihotzky remembered a dizzying taxi ride with Neurath to enlist the support of Mayor Seitz, Deutsch, and others (including the first chancellor of the Austrian republic, Karl Renner) for the Museum, reflecting how well-connected Neurath was in the upper echelons of the Austrian Social Democrat Party.⁴²

Finance minister Hugo Breitner was an important figure in Red Vienna': he had developed the Wohnbausteuer, the special housing tax that enabled the municipal building programme.⁴³ He secured generous subsidy from the municipal government for the Gesellschafts- und Wirtschaftsmuseum, although the Museum constituted a Verein (association) separate from the municipality, which allowed other organizations to contribute to it (see appendix A for list).⁴4 Breitner arranged a special credit arrangement for the Museum from the Zentralsparkasse der Gemeinde Wien (Central municipal savings bank of Vienna), as Neurath explained: 'In Vienna a banker induced his institute to give our institute credit, based on the contracts but without formal “cession" ... a running credit of 10,ooo Schillings.'4⁵ Vienna's Zentralsparkasse also provided the Museum with two floors of office and workshop space in the bank's building from around May 1927 onwards. Neurath tried to give an idea of the Museum's subsidy twenty years later, when in England:

40. Marie Neurath, 'What I remember', p.23. Coincidentally she had been in Munich during the revolution in 1g18 and had heard speeches by Ernst Toller and Erich Mühsam, but not Neurath (p. 17).

41. Marie Neurath, 'What I remember', pp. 29-30. Neurath had in fact given some thought previously to running costs: in a letter to Julius Deutsch of 21 August 1924 (vGa), he estimated the cost of each of the three sections of a permanent exhibition to be roughly 50o million Kronen, and that annual running costs would not exceed 200-300 million. By this time the Austro-Hungarian Krone was almost worthless due to inflation and on 20 December 1924 the Schilling replaced the Krone as Austria's official currency, with 1 Schilling equal to 10,ooo Krone.

42. Schütte-Lihotzky, 'Mein Freund Otto Neurath' in Stadler (ed.), Arbeiterbildung in der Zwischenkriegszeit, p. 41. She stated that this was the beginning of the Museum für Siedlung und Städtebau, but gave the date as late 1924; she must have been confused about the name. Her recollection is clarified by Lola Fleck in Cartwright et al, Otto Neurath: philosophy between science and politics, p. 63.

43. The advantage of the Viennese Wohnbausteuer, in subsidizing construction by ongoing taxation and not with loans, was described (probably by Neurath himself) in the Museum's period ical: 'Above all, this financing policy makes the municipality independent for the future from the prevailing money and property markets.' Fernunterricht, Jg.1, H. 4. July 1931, p. 11.

44. The Museum received an initial subsidy from the city of 20,00o Schillings, followed by 2,ooo more 'on the opening of its permanent exhibition' in November 1925. Between 1926 and 1929 it received a total of 115,000 Schillings in municipal subsidy. Official figures cited in Gerhard Kaldewei, Museumspädagogik und reformpädagogische Bewegung 1900–1933 (Frankfurt a. M: Peter Lang, 1990), p. 451.

45. Letter Neurath to Gustav Neurath, 9 August 1943 (önB 1222/42).

Based on Franz Müller-Lyer's Phasen der Kultur und Richtungslinien des Fortschritts (first published 19o8). c. 1924.

This early chart was taken from the Siedlungsmuseum into the Gesellschaftsund Wirtschaftsmuseum, where it can be seen on display in the bottom photograph opposite. The text of the chart points out: 'This periodization is to be seen as an example · recent research will make amendments necessary.' A second, less wordy chart of this subject was displayed near the entrance of the Volkshalle exhibition from December 1927 (see left of photograph at top of p. 44 and reproduction on p. 51 below).

46. Oto Neurath, 'The Isotype work' (IC 3.2/58), p. 5.

The volume' of our Vienna activity may be pictured by telling that we got about 2oo,ooo Austrian shillings from various sources, besides ‘free' exhibition rooms, and much other help: wardens paid by the municipality etc. 20o,o0o Austrian shillings are equal in purchasing powers perhaps to 15,00o pounds a year [in 1943].

48. Letter Neurath to Mr Coppock, 2 September 1943 (iC 1/2).

50. 'Gesellschaft und Wirtschaft im Lehrbild' (1927), p. 98. 'Gesolei' stood for Gesundheitspflege, sozi ale Fürsorge und Leibesübungen (Health care, social welfare, and physical education).

51. 'Österreichs Sozialversicherung auf der Gesolei" in Düsseldorf' (1926), p. 30. The best and most innovative exhibits at 'Gesolei' were taken over by a new museum formed after the event in Düsseldorf, the Reichsmuseum für Gesellschafts- und Wirtschaftskunde; the similarity of its title to that of the Gesellschafts- und Wirtschaftsmuseum in Wien cannot be coincidental. See Hugo Keller, 'Zur Psychologie des volkstümlichen Zahlenbildes', Beiheft go zur Zeitschrift für angewandte Psychologie und Charakterkunde (Leipzig: Barth, 1941), p. 7.

![](images/c292f500375dc8dcdb676afbf7f23a8db341a4580bd78a4d96f85d4ee3652f90.jpg)

52. 'Das Gesellschafts- und Wirtschaftsmuseum auf der Internationalen Städtebauausstellung in Wien' (1926), p. 63; 'Bildstatistik' (1927), p. 104.

GESELLSCHAFTS-UND WIRTSCHAPTSMUSEUM IN WIEN

The Gesellschafts- und Wirtschaftsmuseum occupied a place firmly within Social-Democratic Vienna, at least during its first five years. Some of its earliest charts were printed in the city's statistical newsletter. The national newspaper of Austrian municipalities stated the following: ‘This Museum is generously supported by the Vienna municipality and will become increasingly important for all municipalities, for it undertakes to represent the municipal, state, cooperative and private economy of Austria in its totality.'⁴ While the outlook of the Museum spread beyond Vienna, it was inextricable from the city's administrative context. Later Neurath reflected: 'Vienna, where we have been in a position to do something for education, presented a kind of island from more than one viewpoint.'4º

Marie Neurath recalled that the Museum's ‘activity, number of employees and stock increased through commissions for many exhibitions', most of which stemmed from the city council.4º Otto Neurath listed three milestones in the rapid development of the Museum's work: the exhibition about ‘social hygiene' in Vienna (1925), ‘Gesolei' in Düsseldorf (1926), and 'Wien und die Wiener' (Vienna and the Viennese, 1927).⁵º The first and third of these took place in Vienna's Messepalast (trade fair palace). He explained that the social hygiene exhibition was the first opportunity for the Museum to show its work to a wide public. For ‘Gesolei' the Museum was asked to 'systematically represent' the achievements of Austrian social insurance.⁵¹ In the same year it also made an important contribution to the Internationale Städtebauausstellung (International town-planning exhibition) by preparing charts, models and plans about Vienna's building programme for display in the city's Künstlerhaus gallery. The Museum's magnetic maps were first shown here: a plan of Vienna was painted on an iron base, enabling a changing display of magnetic symbols. This, along with a backlit map indicating the growth of the Vienna municipality, was a deliberate adoption of advertising techniques for a different purpose.⁵²

ERWERBSTÄTIGE BEVÖLKERUNG ÖSTERREICHS ROTER GRUND: UMSELBSTÁNOIGE. VIOLETTER BRUND: SELBSTANDIGE

T105A Austria's population – commercially active (left) and not (right).

Probably made for the 'Gesolei' exhibition in Düsseldorf, 1926.

The pictograms here (perhaps made by cutting paper with scissors) retain a degree of naturalism, and do not yet carry meaning by themselves; the coloured backgrounds are coded for clarification. The key explains that the violet background to the fur-collared figures in the left-hand matrix denotes people of 'independent' means. Presumably each pictogram denotes a twelfth of the whole population represented by each matrix of twelve units. Published in Otto Neurath's article, 'Statistische Hieroglyphen', Österreichische Gemeinde-Zeitung, Jg.3, Nr.10, 1926. A modified version was displayed from late 1927, with simplified pictograms (see N496, p. 44 below).

Internationale Städtebauausstellung (International town-planning exhibition), Künstlerhaus, Vienna, 1926. On the right can be seen the first magnetic map devised by the Museum, showing distribution of industries and traffic density of the tram network.

## T80 The new Vienna

Loose leaf inserted into the publication shown opposite below. The caption explains: 'In the year of 1927 the apartment buildings and housing estates of the Viennese municipal construction programmme fill an area as large as the inner city [first district].

This map corresponds to a metal map of central Vienna that was placed at the far end of the Museum's exhibition in the Neues Rathaus from December 1927 (visible in the photograph on p. 44 below).

The aim of the exhibition 'Wien und die Wiener' was to show the everyday lives of the Viennese.ª The Gesellschafts- und Wirtschaftsmuseum was commissioned to depict the various aspects of the city government's achievements. As Neurath stated in a special issue of the municipal newspaper produced to commemorate the exhibition: health, housing and education stood at the forefront of the municipality's expanding programme.⁵⁴He claimed that Wien und die Wiener' was the first exhibition ever to have a section dedicated to welfare. On the middle wall of the main exhibition room was a magnetic map, on which the daily reports from Austrian weather stations were mapped. In addition to more backlit charts, a rotating light tower showed the step-by-step development of cultures. 55

The Museum worked on such exhibitions in collaboration with Vienna city councillors Otto Glöckel (education) and Julius Tandler (health). It was perhaps the particular success of 'Wien und die Wiener' in conjunction with Neurath's personal contacts in the administration, that led to the Museum being offered free exhibition space in the Neues Rathaus, the seat of municipal government in the city centre.5⁶ As Paul Neurath observed, this placed it at the very heart of Red Vienna', in

53. See the citation from the 'concept' document for the exhibition in Judith Beniston's introduction to Culture and politics in Red Vienna (Austrian studies, vol.14, 2006), p.7. The title of the exhibition was taken from a famous volume of essays by Adalbert Stifter (1844).

54. 'Der Weg des Gesellschaftsund Wirtschaftsmuseum in Wien' (1927), p. 88. This originally appeared in the third issue of the Österreichische Gemeinde-Zeitung to be dedicated to the Museum's work. The first heralded the opening of the Museum (1925); the second was on the occasion of 'Gesolei' (1926).

55. 'Das Gesellschafts- und Wirtschaftsmuseum auf der Ausstellung Wien und die Wiener" (1927), p. 98.

56. The increasing favour of the Museum among Vienna city councillors may also have resulted from its having produced election propaganda for the Social Democrats (overleaf). On 21 April 1927 the Social Democrats won their largest ever number of parliamentary seats, depriving the Christian Social Party of an absolute majority.

N508, 509b; N515, 516. Press advertisements prepared by the Gesellschaftsund Wirtschaftsmuseum for the Austrian Social Democrat Party during the 1927 national election campaign. The top row claimed increases in the number of children's playgrounds and electric streetlights in Vienna under the Social Democrats. The example titled 'Oh, these Breitnertaxes!' addressed hatred of Hugo Breitner, finance minister of 'Red Vienna' (and supporter of the Museum). His tax on dance entertainment is balanced against the schoolbooks that were purchased with the proceeds.

57. Paul Neurath, 'Otto Neurath (1882–1945): Leben und Werk' in Neurath & Nemeth (ed.), Otto Neurath, oder die Einheit von Wissenschaft und Gesellschaft, p. 61. 58. 'Des Gesellschafts-und Wirtschaftsmuseum in Wien' (1931), p. 194.

59. 'Museums of the future' (abridged version) in Empiricism and sociology, p. 221. The social policy of the Vienna municipality was also considered by contemporaries outside Austria as a great success: American journalist John Gunther (who visited the Museum) declared: 'The achievements of the Vienna socialists were the most exhilarating social monument of the post-War period in any European country.' (Inside Europe, 3rd edn, London: Hamish Hamilton, 1937, p. 318.) A more critical assessment is contained in later historical studies, such as Gruber, Red Vienna.

60. 'Bildstatistik für Kinder' (1928), p. 81. The Österreichische Gemeinde-Zeitung reported in April 1929 that, since its opening in December 1927, the Rathaus exhibition had been visited by almost 30,000 people. (Jg. 6, Nr. 7, p. 13.)

![](images/61dcb056cb6655f46bb50818a6ded6e29408d51c4dd4c21015aaf222d151b4c6.jpg)

a building which workers considered to be their own to some extent, resulting in little reluctance among them to visit the Museum.⁵7 In December 1927 the Museum's new, permanent exhibition opened in the Volkshalle (People's hall), on the ground floor of the enormous, neo-gothic edifice. This principal exhibition of the Museum was, in Neurath's words: 'A kind of accountability report of the municipality.'⁵⁸ In 1933, not long before the Museum was shut down by the Dollfuss regime, he summarized:

The city of Vienna has a progressive municipal government; it has built sixty thousand tenements (to rent at two dollars a month for three rooms), hundreds of kindergartens, playgrounds, health centres, and many other things. But how, it may be asked, is it possible in any city with a democratic government to achieve so much of benefit to the masses unless the people understand what it is all about, at least in its larger outlines, and unless these enormous expenditures out of tax revenues are approved on the basis of a constant accounting to the people? ⁵9

In its original premises the Museum had been open Monday to Friday between 5 and 7 pm, in order to allow people to visit after work; from March 1927 entry had been free. The new exhibition in the Neues Rathaus took over these evening opening times for two weekdays (Tuesday & Friday) and added Sunday morning, 9 am to 1 pm. By April 1928 it was reported that there were often more than 9oo visitors on Sunday.6º A young Englishwoman living in Vienna at that time recalled later that a visit to the Gesellschafts- und Wirtschaftsmuseum in the

![](images/1dbf419bb93315b21521a0d51fc29617739f7f4365df0028da794b5ef278c19a.jpg)

![](images/4c9d2c7106ffc458566c7198c9c8fd7888b48ddb871a0011e511fe438aeb936e.jpg)
