T87B Austria's industries Key to the map above.

## T87A Magnetic map of Austria (with detail). 1927.

Marie Neurath recalled later: 'In the entrance hall [of the Volkshalle, Neues Rathaus] was the 8 m long map of Austria, with the woods, meadows, fields, alpine pastures, barren land and snow differentiated by colours, and an expansive block for the mountains above 1000 m, also coloured; interchangeable symbols for industry were magnetically attached, recording numbers of workers.' ('Zum 4o. Geburtstag des Gesellschafts- und Wirtschaftsmuseums in Wien', p. 24.) In The transformer Marie Neurath explained further that the map was made of an iron sheet and the mountainous area was overlaid in wood; the magnetic symbols (of approximately 2 cm length) were attached only to the areas under 1000 m (pp. 23-5). The specialist consultant for the map was Otto Lehmann of Vienna University: he was doubtful when Otto Neurath first suggested it (in 1926) but declared himself won over by the result in the Museum's supplement to Das Bild (January 1928). However he insisted that it was not a map but a '3-D cartogram'.

Neues Rathaus was the first essential for a visitor.'⁶¹ Guided tours were given by Museum staff; Paul Neurath remembered his father personally leading visitors around the Museum sometimes until midnight.“²

The eight-metre-long magnetic map of Austria made for the 'Wien und die Wiener' exhibition, with its changing arrays of symbols, was the first thing seen by visitors as they entered the anteroom of the main Volkshalle. One of the Museum's maxims was also inscribed on a wall at the entrance: 'To remember simplified pictures is better than to forget accurate figures'.6ª The main exhibition room displayed pictorial charts, photographs, maps, and models; beyond it there was a further, smaller room where slides and films were shown. Experiments in making picture-statistical animated films' began at the Museum in 1927. 64

61. Letter Esther Simpson to Neurath, 7 July 1944 (IC 1/11). 62. Paul Neurath, 'Otto Neurath: Leben und Werk', p. 61.

63. This was the English translation given by Neurath in 'Museums of the future' (1933), p. 462. The original German was 'Vereinfachte Mengenbilder sich merken ist besser als genaue Zahlen vergessen'. See the phrase used in Neurath's writings cited on p.85 and p. 198 below.

64. 'Das Gesellschafts- und Wirtschaftsmuseum im neuen Rathause' (1927); 'Das Gesellschafts- und Wirtschaftsmuseum in Wien' (1931), p. 194.

![](images/77dc622cd5dc926af889df1a7acb6d67ad18cd5832773980e6dd40f9cbc1d022.jpg)

N1751 (above) and N175o (opposite above)

The Gesellschafts- und Wirtschaftsmuseum in the Volkshalle of Vienna's Neues Rathaus, viewed from the entrance, and from the far end respectively. These two wide-angle photographs date from c.1933, whereas the other photographs shown here were taken soon after the exhibition opened in December 1927. The interlude reveals how little this principal exhibition changed.

## Marie Neurath described the exhibition as follows:

'[Josef Frank] suggested that many reflector lamps should be fixed to the stands and turned on the bright charts, and that red carpets should be laid on the dark stone floor. The stands had panels of oak wood underneath the charts, so that the bays were more like separate compartments. A larger space was left open in the middle of the hall, with several models. .

From [the anteroom] the big hall was entered, with its bays left and right of the central carpet, each dealing with a special subject, [such] as agriculture, export-import, health, education etc. Housing occupied the central part; an oversize map caught the eye already from the entrance, as the central red carpet was leading towards it; it showed the distribution of the council housing estates in Vienna.' (See T8o, p. 41 above.)

(Right) N496, 498 Glimpses into two of the bays, for 'population' and 'world economy'.

![](images/7dba40ee73e60915dcc715b0bcca1ef3936f40f17e74087260d0df03c3bafaa4.jpg)

![](images/fd79b1ac768967e261be690abbe33c40354d91e21415ca5997805390a685d9f5.jpg)

![](images/93875e284f617b6df5c55214209ab956820cf21d6da7939de6ebad6cd9442029.jpg)

![](images/7a3535d8d3a0e8a6594f1d29f6fc07e25d7bf05e7ee3329dffc7802a52305704.jpg)  
N493 Section about Vienna's municipal housing programme (this view extends from the left of the top photograph).

## Marie Neurath recalled:

![](images/932c268dc273ed4a557275dcb003555135e7b7d7a61ef37bcbc6e400eda46274.jpg)  
N50o Charts on expenditure by the Vienna town council, 1928 (left); and income to the Austrian parliament, 1927 (right).

The size of the stands was determined like this: the centre of a chart of 126 cm square should be at the level of an average person's eye; underneath a chart sometimes a table is needed for display. The size of the bay should allow a group of 12–2o people to stand within it so that they can look and discuss together. Very often groups of people came from abroad to see what modern Vienna had achieved, and came also to the Museum; there were also school classes in the mornings, and some of us took them round. Very often this was my lot. Frankly, it was a useful experience. As long as we had a museum we had a testing ground for our work; it could be observed whether a chart was successful or not, what was too difficult, what was stimulating etc.'

(Quotations from Marie Neurath's English typescript of the article published in Dutch, 'Gesellschafts- und Wirtschaftsmuseum in Wien en Mundaneum’; ic 8.1a).

![](images/6f473c8883cd5d6218781d30854b0c0a140ce236c47765b2c398e133872ac3c5.jpg)

# GESELLSCHAFTS- UND WIRTSCHAFTSMUSEUM IN WIEN

Vereinfachte Mengenbilder Sich merken, ist besser als Genaue Zaklen vergessen.

Neben die geschichtlichen Museen, die Vergangenheit bewahren, tritt im 20. Jahrhundert das Museum der Gegenwart. Zu den Museen der Technik und der Hygiene gesellt sich heute das Museum für Gesellschaft und Wirtschaft, Die modernen Menschen wollen sich über ihre Lebensbedingungen Rechenschaft geben. Gesellschaft und Wirtschaft konnte bisher mit den Mitteln der Museumstechnik nicht allgemein verständlich dargestellt werden. Die neue, von Wien aus rasch verbreitete Bildstatistik vermag es. An die Stelle schwer deutbarer Tabellen und Kurven tritt das Mengenbild aus Reihen gleich großer, farbiger Symbolzeichen, Bunte Tafelbilder, Magnettafeln, Leuchtbilder prägen sich dem Gedächtnis durch Form und Farbe ein. Zum starren Bild tritt das bewegte, Zeichenfilme des Museums auf Grund der Wiener Methode.

![](images/7dd6a0bf9720eff9d0d9a56372453200d5466c20f20c4d9246f9716619bfb535.jpg)

![](images/7e2a86ab53fbed7888f25d0245f47f86943a4059a414053704e8cf7eb705e99d.jpg)

(Left) Front of a four-page leaflet for the Gesellschaftsund Wirtschaftsmuseum. A5. 1928.

(Right and below) Emblem for the Museum constructed from superimposed letters Gwm, and an illustration giving directions and opening times to the Volkshalle exhibition; both featured on the back of the leaflet shown on the left.

![](images/587d54542c07f1c96bbf5a90e5930f3c7a6f7a370604f87328a67d23d807cc3f.jpg)

EINTRITT FREI FÜHRUNGEN FILME

NEUES RATHAUS. LINKS VOM TURM.TEL.85-4.68 Sondergruppen und Schulen haben gegen vorherige Anmeldung jederzeit Zutrit

N503, 504 The Museum's department of 'Wohnung, Siedlung und Städtebau' that remained in its original exhibition space at Parkring, Vienna. Photographed c. 1928.

![](images/af01f244d3ab4560b9ccce8898e738b6b294dfa9f0b791c34757dd9b5cbdbe8b.jpg)

![](images/1ba6a0a09ef445dfb1d5d1257f76e0b29a39281251cb9f6a9db29cef795cb8da.jpg)

The existing, permanent exhibition of the Museum (at Parkring 12) remained open, containing the department of 'Housing, settlement and town-planning'.6⁵ The Museum had also acquired exhibition rooms for its section of‘Social hygiene and social insurance' in a special wing of the people's apartment building' named Am Fuchsenfeld.6 This was one of the largest communal blocks erected by the Vienna municipality, which spanned several streets in the city's twelfth district and contained 6o4 apartments, 29 shops, baths, laundries, a kindergarten and day-care centre, a clinic, and meeting & lecture halls.

N42, 45 Municipal apartment block Am Fuchsenfeld, Vienna. Constructed 1924-5. Architects Heinrich Schmid and Hermann Aichinger.

In some ways, museum' is a misleading word to describe the Gesellschafts- und Wirtschaftsmuseum: the nature of its exhibits made it an unconventional museum (even by today's standards), and it certainly was not an art gallery. Neurath offered an alternative description that is perhaps most fitting:

It is a popular educational institute for social enlightenment, which seeks to spread social and economic education through exhibitions, publications, lectures, slides, films and other aids, primarily in Vienna & Austria but also abroad.

He was conscious of reshaping the definition of a museum:

The Museum direction is of the view that a decentralization of museums is appropriate in a big city. It currently has exhibitions at three sites and, when possible, will have more. Museums should not be centrally situated, monumental installations, rather movable constructions, so that one can easily redesign them on changing the exhibition.6⁸

Neurath's ambition to reach the broad masses was perhaps best realized in the fourth of the Museum's Viennese locations: a small, street-corner showroom at the head office of the Städtische Versicherungsanstalt (Municipal insurance service) in the heart of the old city.

65. In January 1929 mayor Karl Seitz re-opened this department of the Museum as 'Wohnung und Städtebau' (housing and townplanning). 'Mitteilungen', Das Bild, Jg. 6, H.3, March 1929, p. 72.

66. 'Sondertrakt des Volkswohnungsbaus' – this is how the rooms were described on the invitation to the exhibition 'Weltwirtschaft' that opened there in October 1930.

67. See Blau, The architecture of Red Vienna, p. 292.

68. 'Das Gesellschafts- und Wirtschaftsmuseum in Wien' (1931), pp.192-3.

![](images/d274bcf99bac9cf7072529d0f877eeecf9c81939644472e4976b4004c4af2299.jpg)

![](images/1f611f93868a993dc4bbdd5b476e687a6775d9c08b1db8f6ac507788952bae3f.jpg)  
N1753; N1752, 1747 The 'Zeitschau' branch of the Gesellschafts- und Wirtschaftsmuseum, located at the head office of the Municipal insurance service, am Tuchlauben, Vienna. 1933.

![](images/1f96102a3a682d4d71dad8d9b4bd3c1f9653f8ad868fb0fef62d633822f889a8.jpg)  
Visitors can be seen operating one of the 'self-teaching' devices.

It was called the ‘Zeitschau' – a 'topical show', although Neurath later translated it as ‘march of time', alluding to the well-known American newsreel. Entry was free and he claimed that it received 2,ooo visitors per day.6ª In addition to charts dealing with the Museum's typical themes, this venue contained so-called 'psycho-technical apparatuses', which Neurath described as having the purpose of 'self-teaching'.? Marie Neurath explained later the populist intention behind the Zeitschau:

Rousing a quick reaction is different from the long lasting influence or stimulation which is the aim of education. Our museums and exhibitions were in general of an educational type and rather severe in subject matter, though colourful and attractive. Visitors were not numerous, though certain individuals came again and again and brought friends along. Otto Neurath knew that other attractions must be added if the number of visitors should be larger. He did so at a certain corner show in Vienna where he had not only recent events in photo and diagrammatical explanation; he had certain apparatus displayed where people could test their skill or intelligence. This place was crowded each lunchtime."1

The daily newspaper of the Social Democrat Party, Die Arbeiterzeitung, reported on the opening of the exhibition:

So one passes from chart to chart without noticing that one has entered a museum, because museums are all about hallowed halls, colonnaded passageways, plush carpets and liveried attendants. For instance, if people were really expected to visit a museum of economic statistics, most would turn away in boredom. This is precisely one of the purposes achieved by setting up this shop: that people should absorb pictorial material in passing. This is a novelty here. The concept originates in Austria but comes to us via America. From there, over the great water, the director of the Gesellschafts- und Wirtschaftsmuseum, Dr Otto Neurath, was approached about doing something for clients waiting in banks. The pointless sitting around makes waiting rooms unpleasant places. The Americans, however, wanted their customers to have a good memory of their bank building or shop premises. And so he suggested transforming these waiting rooms into little museums, in which one can gain all kind of useful knowledge while waiting. The idea struck a nerve and now we have such a museum of the street here in Vienna.7²

69. 'The Isotype work' (ic 3.2/58), p. 6 70. From hieroglyphics to Isotype, p. 114 71. Marie Neurath, 'Isotype (1974), pp. 144-5. 72. Extracted, among other press reports, in the Mitteilungen der Gemeinde Wien: Städtische Versicherungsanstalt, Nr. 107, 1933, p. 4.

# GESELLSCHAFTS- UND WIRTSCHAFTSMUSEUM IN WIEN. DIREKTION WIEN, XIV., ULLMANNSTRASSE 44.

ABTEILUNGEN:

Arbeit und Organisation, Wohnung, Siedlung und Städtebau. Sozialhygiene und Sozialversicherung. Geistesleben und Schule.

AUSSTELLUNGEN: Neues Rathaus. Rathausplatz. Bezirksausstellungen : Am Fuehsenfeld ; Parkring 12. Wanderausstellungen: 1927 Deutschland.

## GESCHICHTE:

Gründung als Museum für Siedlung und Städtebau Hygieneausstellung Wien 1925. Cesolei Düsseldorf 1926. Jugendausstellung Amsterdam 1926. Internat. Städtcbauausstellung Wien 1926.

1923, Umwandlung 1924. Beschickt wurden : BurgenIandausstellung Eisenstadt 1927. Wien und die Wiener, Wien 1927. Das junge Deutschland, Berlin 1927 Gesundheitswoche, Calau 1927.

## DARSTELLUNGSWEISE:

Die sogenannte Wiener Methode" gibt grundsätzlich eine größere Menge von Gegenständen durch cine größere Menge von Zeichcn, nicht durch ein größeres Zeichen wieder. Statt Kreisen und Quadraten werden nur Rechtecke verwendet, weil sie allein größte Vergleichbarkeit ermöglichen.

![](images/0d882b5671d229550395b6401c0e9bc29233f45c6ee6c599c6be5b4da432bc39.jpg)

## TECHNISCHE MITTEL:

Farbige Bildtafeln. Schwarz-Weiß-Zeichnungen, Magnetafeln zur Darstellung veränderlicher Tatbestände, bowegliche Leuchttafeln, Zeichenfilme.

# Charts from the Gesellschafts- und Wirtschaftsmuseum exhibition at the Volkshalle, Neues Rathaus, Vienna

There are 84 charts listed in a guide to the Museum's Volkshalle exhibition, which was published in Das Bild in December 1927, the month of its opening. Of these, 43 are reproduced here.\* Charts were usually in colour.

A footnote states: 'The division of humanity into races is very controversial. This chart serves only the most general orientation.'

Most charts here were designed in consultation with Otto Neurath by Marie Reidemeister, Friedrich Bauermeister, Erwin Bernath, and other graphic artists. In general they predate the work of Gerd Arntz for the Museum.

## Emigration [in 1922]

For each region the first row depicts inhabitants in divisions of 100,o0o, and the second row depicts emigrants in segments of 100.

This change of scale between the two rows is misleading because the viewer is naturally inclined to compare them: for example, it appears that Burgenland suffered the emigration of about six times its total population.

Considering the non-modular nature of the quantitative depiction (and the dates in the data), the two charts on this page may predate the founding of the Gesellschafts- und Wirtschaftsmuseum, but they were not discarded and resurface here in the Volkshalle exhibition. See Neurath's comments on the faults of a similar chart on p. 50o.

73. 'Bildliche Darstellung gesellschaftliche Tatbestände' (1927), p. 118.

74. Neurath stated: 'A staff of two dozens and sometimes more collaborators worked together with the museum for many years.' ('The Isotype work'; IC 3.2/58, p. 5). In a letter to Mr Salter Davies (of the Journal of Education) he stated: 'Sometimes our permanent staff was about 50 collaborators.' (10 February 1945; IC 1/11.)

75. Jodlbauer stayed in this post until 1934, having become what was termed a 'Kustos' of the Museum.

76. Letter of employment, 5 March 1925 (önB Ser.n. 31.878) She was also registered at the philosophy faculty of Vienna University between 30 April 1925 and Easter 1927. Initially she lived in a dormitory in the Schönbrunn palace, then in an apartment opposite the Opera building on the Ringstrasse.

77. Marie Neurath, Isotype' (1974), p. 136.

78. Nevertheless Otto Neurath had earned some money by paint ing decorative boxes, which were sold as 'folk art' in a gift shop in Salzburg. Marie Neurath recalled his visual acuity: 'Once I went with him to the greenhouse in Schönbrunn and complained that he rushed through without looking. He said: "Shall I draw you the flower we passed a few yards back?" He did, and I compared and was amazed.' ('26 September 1924 and after', p. 59.) His lifelong signature of a cartoon elephant also demonstrates a raw talent for caricature.

79. Only two things were closed to Reidemeister: the Arbeiterhochschule and Vienna Circle meetings (\*26 September 1924 and after', p. 59.) She remembered that her relationship with Neurath's second wife Olga Hahn-Neurath was also 'wholly without conflict': Reidemeister read to Olga, who was blind, and took her for walks. (Marie Neurath, 'What I remember', p. 40.)

## Design at the Gesellschafts- und Wirtschaftsmuseum

In 1927 Otto Neurath wrote:

Social museums have as their object the person as a social being; here is the place where the methodology of visually representing social connections is cultivated.

The Gesellschafts- und Wirtschaftsmuseum has begun the construction of such a methodology.7³

From around May 1927 both the permanent and travelling exhibitions of the Museum were served by its offices and workshops above the central municipal savings bank in Vienna's fourteenth district There it had about a dozen large rooms at its disposal, where all aspects of its production process could be accommodated. The number of people employed ranged between 24 and 5o."ª

The Museum's first office had been in a prefabricated building of the övsk, where initially only Neurath and an administrator (Josef Jodlbauer) worked.7⁵ A key member of the team began work on 1 March 1925: Marie Reidemeister (later Marie Neurath). As already noted, her expression of willingness to collaborate with the Museum was an important factor in spurring Neurath to move ahead with his plans. Her initial terms of employment stated that she would work for a third of the normal working week (14 hours) on administrative duties, statistical tasks and various preparatory work for the hygiene exhibition.76 Her small salary was supplemented by being paid (from publisher's advances) as Neurath's secretary for writing his book Lebensgestaltung und Klassenkampf (1928).

She remembered that sketches for pictorial charts were made initially by Neurath, and then by them both together: 'what he needed was educational talent (with a visual slant)' – a description that fitted Marie Reidemeister perfectly." Otto Neurath's own graphic skills were sometimes crude, but effective, whereas Reidemeister's were more refined and controlled.78 In these early days she once designed and executed a whole chart herself by hand, which would not have been possible a few years later as the process became more systematized and mechanized. She and Neurath formed a close personal and professional relationship from the beginning: they often met before work, at the Café Dobner near Vienna's Sezession building, and she almost always accompanied him to the frequent lectures he gave in the evenings."ª

The Museum's offices soon moved to the administrative building of Vienna's third district, where there was more space for the artists and architects employed on a temporary basis to graphically execute the charts (among them was Grete Schütte-Lihotzky; see the detailed list in appendix c below). With work on the two large exhibitions of 1926, the team began to expand, notably by the appointment of a full-time artist, Erwin Bernath, in autumn 1926. Bernath was a Swiss who had studied at the school of art in Vienna, and he remained with the team for ten years. At the same time Reidemeister's position became clearer:

My role in the production process was now clearly established, even though the name given to it – transformation – was not yet in use. I put each sketch to Otto; he usually had a further idea, which I then pursued, until we were both satisfied. So developed, step by step, our method, to which Otto one day gave the name 'Vienna Method'.8º

At the beginning of 1927 Reidemeister's working hours at the Museum were doubled and her salary was increased to match that of its best paid draftsperson [Zeichner] in respect of the fact that she now carried out 'predominantly scientific work'.81 Soon afterwards, around the time that the Museum's offices and studios moved to spacious accommodation in the building of Vienna's central municipal savings bank, Friedrich Bauermeister was employed by the Museum as a second transformer. He was a German who had come to Vienna to participate in the settlers' movement. He had worked with Neurath in the secretariat of the övsK, and when that work ended, Neurath suggested that he come to work at the Museum.

The role of the transformer (Transformator in German) was considered by Neurath as the linchpin of the Vienna Method and Isotype. The first use of the term transformation' in print was in 1932, where Neurath explained it as the metamorphosis [Umwandlung] of statistical material into pictorial sketches'.8² A phrase that later became attached to the transformer – 'trustee of the public' – appeared in print from the end of 1929, as Treuhander der Konsumenten in German.8³ This concept became influential later, in the development of information design as a discipline from the 197os.

In the first lengthy essay by Neurath about the Vienna Method published in English, 'Museums of the future' (1933), he summarized the purpose of transformation:

Perhaps the most original section of the Vienna central institution is the department of transformation. On it the educational purpose of the whole enterprise is concentrated. While in other museums it is customary to have each department administered by a specialist who in turn must direct as best he can the painters, modellers and other assistants

![](images/448dbeaa705404146d0cba4667e0ccdb3e0c0407cba0a49bd5952d3f6f19c273.jpg)  
Marie Reidemeister. c.1930.

80. Marie Neurath, 'What I remember', p.35. Elsewhere she recalled that Otto Neurath rejected friends' advice to name it the 'Neurath method' because he felt a debt was owed to the Vienna municipality for its support. ('Otto Neurath and Isotype', p. 15.)

81. Letter from Neurath (as director of the Museum) to Reidemeister, 10 February 1927 (öNB Ser.n, 31.878).

82. 'Bildstatistik nach Wiener Methode in der Sowjetunion' (1932) p. 2og. The term's first appearance in this article about the Izostat Institute in the ussR implies that teaching the Vienna Method to new colleagues in Moscow encouraged clarification of this role.

83. Otto Neurath [unsigned], 'Landesausstellung Posen 1929', Das Bild, Jg. 6, H.12, December 1929, p. 235. The phrase was used here to describe how exhibition curators generally did not yet live up to this role.

84. See Michael Macdonald-Ross & Robert Waller, 'The transformer revisited', Information Design Journal, vol. 9, nos. 2 & 3, 2000, pp. 177-193.

85. 'Museums of the future' (1933), p, 479.

86. Bildstatistik nach Wiener Methode in der Schule (1933). pp.283-4.

87. 'Museums of the future' (1933), p. 462. Indeed the 'Transformator' is defined as equivalent to 'teacher' in International picture language (1936), p. 30. Marie Reidemeister described the work of the transformer without using the term but instead naming the role as 'Pädagoge' (educator) in her essay 'Bildpädagogik', p. 315.

placed at his disposal, in Vienna every one of the scientific specialists must deal with the department of transformation which acts as a liaison between him and the technical departments. This has the great advantage that there is one special department which knows exactly what potential visual resources there are to solve any given problem of presentation. A scientific specialist may be ever so eminent in his own field – indeed, he may even have high qualifications as an educator – but that is no reason for supposing that he necessarily knows what is the best way of translating his intentions into visual reality. Besides, it is a decided disadvantage to have the separate departments of a museum deal with their materials in different ways. Intentionally to aim at variety in this matter is wholly superfluous. The objects to be represented offer chances enough for variety even within a completely unified method of representation.8⁵

Here Neurath emphasized the virtues of consistency and standardization that emerge from the controlling role of the transformer, in accordance with his original concept of the Museum as 'a schoolbook on a grand scale':

we must conceive each individual chart as part of a colossal system, a new Orbis pictus; so, we should not only consider how to harmonize the new chart with previous charts but, further, how it will fit with charts that will only be made later on.

Although he sometimes stressed the potential for international standardization in this respect, he did not advocate simple uniformity:

Each chart may be distinct from all the others – each has its own life – yet all together they form a whole, towards which the representatives of the Vienna Method are working.

From this it follows that the transformation' of scientific material is such a responsible and essential task. The scientists bring data and collected material. They wish to define the area of the problem, but the statistical material thus gathered is not yet ready to be drawn graphically. First the visual conventions must be applied. The reduction of figures is necessary, rounding off, the choice of form and colour. Only when all that is complete does the difficult work of the graphic artist begin. 86

In this sense the transformer was the guardian of the educational principal of Isotype, that he who knows best what to omit is the best teacher'.87 Furthermore, in creatively exploring graphic possibilities through analysis of source material, the transformer fulfilled a role that would now be labelled 'designer'. The modern concept of a designer was not yet established in the 192os, and this is one of the several ways in which the Vienna Method team were pioneers.88 Its leading members were certainly aware of contemporary developments in design (due to connections with the Bauhaus, for example) but the processes of graphic design and museum design at the Gesellschaftsund Wirtschaftsmuseum were subservient to its overriding priority of education. Neurath made this clear from the beginning, when he wrote in 1925: 'Educational considerations are decisive for what is included in a pictorial representation, and what is not.'89 By 1931 he could describe the work in the 'technical department' of the Museum as: 'A typical collective work of modern graphic design [Gebrauchsgraphik], which is being brought to ever greater perfection; a close fusion of scientific research work, systematic pedagogy and artistic design [Gestaltung].’

This description fits best the Museum's activity at its high point around 1929, when the monumental publication of 1oo charts, Gesellschaft und Wirtschaft, was in preparation. For this, many new consultants, artists and technicians were employed. At around this time Marie Neurath proposed new rules for transformation, which Neurath accepted. She recalled: 'This was an interesting time; our “academy" came to meet us every week: a historian, a statistician, a cartographer, an art historian, a historian of technology, and us two.'1 She described her role in an interview late in her life:

It was my task to convert the knowledge that they brought us into pictorial form; in other words, I sketched the charts, then we had our graphic artists [entwerfenden Grafiker], who were handed my sketches in order to design the final image. But I was the connecting link between these scientists and the artists.9²

She went into more detail in an historical essay she wrote about Isotype:

This is how the team worked (except when we had to make charts for some special exhibition): an idea was formed by Neurath; he discussed it with an expert to have his idea checked and get suitable material. The transformer was present at such discussions to get acquainted with the subject, took over the material and developed the way to present it visually. The sketch (in pencil and colour pencils) was discussed with Neurath (and sometimes the expert) until a final rough was agreed upon; this was copied into a duplicate book, and the coloured top copy handed to the artist who took charge over design and finished art work, 93 in constant contact with Neurath and the transformer.

'It was not easy for any designer to always be subjected to this criticism and discipline', she recalled, 'but it was worth it, and so the Vienna Method arose.'4

88. This is suggested by Robin Kinross in The transformer, p. 6. In a memorial speech given for Marie Neurath, Kinross said that she 'would express surprise when we applied this word to her work: she thought of design rather as something artistic or decorative, and therefore not concerned to communicate information'. ('Marie Neurath and Otto Neurath: their visual work [Isotype]', address given at Institut Autrichien, Paris, 25 May 1987, p. 1.) Yet both Otto and Marie Neurath did use 'design' as a description of their work soon after settling in Britain: Marie Neurath called herself an 'Isotype designer' with responsibility for 'the designing of every chart and the supervision of the research and drawing work connected with it'. Letter to Patrick Meredith, 20 May 1944 (IC 1/35).

89. 'Darstellungsmethoden des Gesellschafts- und Wirtschaftsmuseums' (1925), p. 18.

90. 'Das Gesellschafts- und Wirtschaftsmuseum in Wien' (1931), p.195.

91. Marie Neurath, 'What I remember', p. 39. The names of these collaborators are given in appendix c, p. 529 below

92. Translated from an interview featured in the öRF documentary film Otto Neurath: der unbekümmerte Denker (19go), written & directed by Karo Wolm.

93. Marie Neurath, 'Isotype' (1974), p. 137.

94. Marie Neurath, 'Zum 40. Geburtstag des Gesellschafts- und Wirtschaftsmuseums in Wien', p.25.

## T32a Suicide in Vienna C.1927

Two panels from a composite chart. Prepared for the Statistische Amt der Gemeinde Wien (Viennese municipal office of statistics). Das Bild, Jg. 5, H.6, June 1928.

(above) Motives

Each pictogram = 5o fatal suicides.

## (right) The gravity of motives

Relative proportion of fatal and non-fatal cases out of every ten suicide attempts.

The graphic qualities here are naive compared with later examples of the Museum's work (although pictograms for emotional or economic conditions present difficulties, and they were generally avoided).

![](images/81ca31e31599024b3267e0a8933ce3d81cb7fe75631e3f5fdbcbbb44062942de.jpg)

Early attempt at making a chart using pictograms pre-cast as lead type. Published in Statistische Mitteilungen der Stadt Wien, 1925. Letterpress printing dictates that the pictograms remain as distinct modules, although there seem to be some points where the printing surface overhangs the type body so that the units apparently touch (the boy with cap carrying folder and T-square). Neurath felt that this technique did not allow enough adaptability. At this time the Museum did not have an in-house printing press so the typesetting must have been done by printers, which would not have permitted the Museum staff as much freedom in design and layout of the charts as was eventually given by its standard technique of combining single, preprinted components.

![](images/c98b78a19634089cf4c85da92346335d8c06aae731f0473784ca5a2de058503c.jpg)

The pictograms denoting motives (above) are explained as 'illness', 'poverty', 'love', and 'miscellaneous' – the last being an ambiguous combination of images. The ghostly skull behind black pictograms denotes the number of fatal suicide attempts out of every ten cases with each motive. This device causes graphic clutter.

![](images/ff451c8639e3fc6eed514d39258e09c6251764803bde4ed7f9d31f7e594baa6c.jpg)  
(Rach Wngaben des Rerfaffers ansgearbeiter vom Gefeschafte, und Wirtschastämufeum in Wien.)

According to Marie Neurath, early experiments in producing charts by cutting shapes from coloured paper were unsatisfactory: 'the charts did not have yet the typographic quality which Neurath wanted'.9s 'We were still groping for a technique and a style. It was still noticeable to us which artist had made which chart, in shapes as well as in choice of colour.'9 In fact, an attempt was made at an early stage to give the charts a ‘typographic' character by having some pictograms cast as metal type, which could then be composed in the traditional way for letterpress printing. Neurath explained later that this was quickly abandoned ‘because one can hardly get a sufficient amount of differentiation in the appearance of charts and the whole becomes monotonous'.97

The move towards a consistent and apparently impersonal style was due principally to the third key member of the Vienna Museum team – Gerd Arntz, a German artist specializing in woodcut prints. Some of Arntz's work was exhibited in Düsseldorf in 1926 and Neurath saw it there when attending the ‘Gesolei' exhibition (on his first trip back to Germany since being expelled in 192o; see p. 21 above). Neurath immediately perceived a schematic treatment of figurative subjects in Arntz's prints that would suit his intentions for the Vienna Method. Neurath's friend, the art historian Franz Roh, set up a meeting with Arntz (then living in Düsseldorf) and Neurath invited him to work for the Museum.98 Arntz demurred initially but began to contribute work by post. Neurath acquired some of his prints for the Museum at this time and these also contributed to a gradual infiltration of Arntz's style into its work.

In 1928 Arntz went for a few months' trial work at the Museum, and at the beginning of 1929 he moved to Vienna with his family to work almost full-time (he stipulated that one day a week be reserved for his own free' work, but he was not able to really do this until 193o, after the very busy period preparing Gesellschaft und Wirtschaft).” He was impressed by the socialist vigour' of Vienna, which he considered to be stronger there than in Germany, where he had moved in communist circles.1º0 He lived with his family in an apartment in one of the new municipal apartment buildings, Rabenhof, in Vienna's third district.

One of Arntz's significant contributions was changing the technique of producing the pictograms from cutting paper to printing from linocuts, so that identical, repeatable copies could be made:

After numerous experiments with symbols cut out from paper we switched on my advice to cutting the basic figures and symbols in linoleum and sticking the prints made in this way down in the required

![](images/34fb62a9f013872800a6739749ae858849e3c345ebcda35613b981a8075a14cf.jpg)

T302 Blue-collar and white-collar workers in Carinthia according to branches of economy 1929

Each figure = 4,00o workers.

Chart from Gerd Arntz's first year working at the Museum. Workers are distinguished by trade in an early example of incorporating qualifying symbols within human pictograms.

95. Marie Neurath, 'Isotype' (1974), p. 136.

96. Marie Neurath, 'Otto Neurath and Isotype', p. 12.

99. 'Gerd Arntz', interview in Pulchri, p.3.

100. Arntz recalled that in 1928 the Gesellschafts- und Wirtschaftsmuseum put together an exhibition 'on the side' in protest at Fascist Italy. Photographs and statistical charts on the subject were shown in Reumannhof, one of the municipal apartment blocks, where the Republikaner Schutzbund also had its headquarters – plus a shooting range in the cellar. (Arntz, Zeit unterm Messer, p. 22.)

T1d Humanity C.1928 Human groups, economic forms and religious confessions of the world. Estimated data for 1930.

These pictograms show the influence of Arntz, although he may not have personally designed them. The chart at top right is a progression from the earlier 'Human races' (T1a, p. 130 below; T1c, p. 50 above) towards the definitive version in Gesellschaft und Wirtschaft (p. 214 below). In this image, the Museum's photograph of record, the outer edge of the frames have been cropped but the bevelled inner edges joining them into one unit are visible.

101. Arntz, Zeit unterm Messer, p. 24. See pp. 77–8 below for more on Tschichold.   
102. Arntz, '3.7.'72', p.1; and   
'Gerd Arntz', interview in Pulchri p.3.   
103. 'Bildhafte Pädagogik im Gesellschafts- und Wirtschafts museum in Wien' (1931), p. 201. 104. 'The lsotype work'   
(IC 3.2/58), p. 9.

rows. Also the accompanying texts were no longer drawn by hand, rather printed so that they could finally be mounted together with the symbols according to the principles that were propagated in the 192os above all by Jan Tschichold.101

Later Arntz referred to his role as head of the production department' or chief of the workshop' at the Museum.1º²Neurath described the 'technical department' of the Museum as having ‘well equipped, modern workshops for woodwork, spraying processes, reproduction, photography, [letterpress] printing and lithography'.1º³ He later praised Arntz, 'the chief of the department', as 'a man of extraordinary abilities, who worked with us for more than a decade'.104

T192a A sequence of floor plans mounted on layers of glass.

![](images/89fdaafb409b4180b45df7c8ea16a9781943c3a1972652008fc3166f7071918b.jpg)  
T1g3a The trees in this model of a courtyard garden were often taken by children visiting the Museum. 'Neurath, however, did not want more security personnel, but rather larger supplies of these small turned trees.' (Neurath & Kinross, The transformer, p. 25)

105. Arntz, Zeit unterm Messer, pp. 24, 27. Lucius Licinius Lucullus (c. 118–57 Bc) was a Roman soldier, civic leader and patron of arts and scholarship.

106. 'Bildhafte Pädagogik im Gesellschafts- und Wirtschaftsmuseum in Wien' (1931), p. 203 Marie Neurath called this a 'lexicon of symbols' (The transformer, p. 44).

Neurath and Arntz worked closely together in designing the pictograms used on Isotype charts. The following recollection by Arntz shows that this relationship was not free of tension but that he valued Neurath as an important influence on his work:

Since I myself had already, early in my graphic work, arrived at a simplified form of representing people and things, I appeared to be predestined to stamp my mark on the pictorial statistics of the Gesellschaftsund Wirtschaftsmuseum. One of Neurath's questions in the beginning was: Could you also teach others to design in your way, without disturbing personal nuances entering into the total picture?' So I had to carry through my kind of representation, to impose it on others in a certain sense and to justify and defend this imposition', often also against Otto Neurath. ..

It was however the unique personality of Otto Neurath, his deeplybased knowledge, his effervescent vitality and his obsessive desire to establish pictorial statistics in popular education, that was of decisive importance for me and my subsequent life. Not only my assured livelihood played a role in this, but also the fact that I was posed tasks that provided a particular insight into our social order and ways of representing it; quite different than if I would have had to go my own way. Neurath was always stimulating, often exciting, and in addition a 'Lucullus'...105

After the work on Gesellschaft und Wirtschaft was completed, Neurath and Arntz had time to review the Museum's stock of pictograms, and prints from the linocuts were systematically numbered and grouped in what Neurath called a Kartothek' (card index).1º

## T784 School meal provision by the Vienna municipality c. 1932

Each small child = 1,o0o kindergarten pupils; each larger child = 2,00o schoolchildren.

This chart from the mature period of the Vienna Method is an update of an earlier chart (τ51a, p. 40 above). While the configuration of the chart hardly changed, Arntz's contribution to pictogram design is quite apparent. (See the page from the Museum's reference files that features these pictograms on p.73 opposite.)

## Schülerspeisung der Gemeinde Wien

![](images/0246a6da6ba326b8b216355f8fadaa87d2bcc93188b83ce9cc622c7810c78d0e.jpg)  
Jede grosse Figur 1000 Kinder in Schul-Ausspeisestellen Jede kleine Figur 1000 Kinder in Kindergarten-Ausspeisestellen

Geellchefts und Wirtshomusum in Wien

![](images/92c792ca975648c7beec8285956094da33f05707cdce993b6312610b2caf2972.jpg)

Pages from the Museum's 'picture dictionary' reference files. 1930–3 (ic 4/1–2). Pictograms designed by Gerd Arntz and assistants.

![](images/176bc7a368a7e0713fa75e0155df15272ce977fe3702bef26bb81ae1c025f47d.jpg)

N490 & 491.

Two views of the travelling exhibition structure designed by Josef Frank for the Gesellschafts- und Wirtschaftsmuseum. 1927. The introductory panel states: 'Transportable walls with built-in lighting apparatus. Suitable for installation in any kind of space without precaution and in a very short time. Exhibition possible with pictures, illuminated and magnetic charts etc.'

107. Letter Neurath to Roh, 19 June 1924, cited in Blau, The architecture of Red Vienna, p. 114.

108. See Christopher Long, Josef Frank: life and work (Chicago: University of Chicago Press, 2002), p. 27.

109. 'Das Gesellschafts- und Wirtschaftsmuseum in Wien' (1931), p. 194.

110. 'Der Weg des Gesellschaftsund Wirtschaftsmuseum in Wien' (1927), p. g0.

Attempts at systematic design extended beyond the graphic work of the Museum to its exhibition spaces. Neurath commissioned the architect Josef Frank to design exhibition furniture. Frank, who had designed some of Vienna's housing settlements for the övsK, was considered by Neurath as the most versatile and adaptable socialist architect then at work.¹º7 Frank had previously designed an acclaimed interior for a museum of East Asian art in Cologne, which had featured customized cabinetry.1º⁸ For the Gesellschafts- und Wirtschaftsmuseum he designed a flexible system of wood framing for the charts, based on a standardization of chart sizes. This system stemmed from Neurath's conviction that the Museum's exhibitions should be easily modifiable and transportable, given that they did not deal with rare or unique treasures' but rather with reproducible items. He considered the use of standardized modules as particularly suitable for travelling exhibitions, the construction of which the Museum always keeps in mind'.109

The framing structures were already in use at the Museum's first premises on the Ringstrasse. In May 1927 Neurath stated: ‘The Museum is currently showing a segment of such a travelling exhibition, with built-in lighting, which is designed so that it can be erected without damaging walls or floors, or without removing furniture that stands against the wall.'1º The same technique was subsequently applied to the central exhibition in the Neues Rathaus. The Volkshalle there was, of course, not a purpose-built exhibition space, and Neurath was concerned that its gothic vaults would be distracting.111When asked for advice on how to overcome this, Frank replied: 'light up your colourful charts very strongly, put down red carpets, and no one will look up into the dark vaults. Who notices the Baroque towers on the houses in Mariahilferstrasse, which have large display windows beneath?'2 He also advised that the chart frames be left in their natural, light oak colour instead of being stained black as they had been previously, so that the colours of the charts would be accentuated further. Neurath described the intention for this central display:

![](images/a4592096c4942096336544b05488d721471070b8b681c75b1a15fdde00b327c1.jpg)

The frames are of narrow proportion and light colour, so that the images have the strongest effect possible and the framing apparatus does not encumber the eye too much. The charts are slotted into the collapsible framework [Fallrahmen] designed by the Museum with an equally thin profile, and they can be interchanged without difficulty at any time, All framing structures are able to be dismantled, subsections are standardized. The attempt was made to design a whole museum so that it is easily movable and prepared for any change of location. The avoidance of any heaviness or force [Wucht] in representation allows the pleasure of the viewer to be raised through simple clarity and to make the Museum something approaching a club; so, in the spirit of Museum architect Josef Frank, an undemanding accommodation of the Museum objects is striven for, in order to let the presentations speak for themselves as much as possible.11³

111. Neurath commented on the stylistic eclecticism of the buildings on this part of the Ringstrasse: '[T]he influence of the bourgeoiscapitalist era with its free competition made itself visible. What corresponded to this Zeitgeist, more than a recognition of an ideal of beauty, was to allow classical next to Renaissance and gothic, without having the courage to be something of itself.' ('Generalarchitekturplan und Bauorganisation' (1924), p. [1].)

112. Marie Neurath, 'What I remember', p. 38. Neurath remarked to Waldemar Kaempffert: 'I experience more and more that it is difficult to install a modern museum in an old building. I also had to make many concessions, which then have long after-effects.' (Letter of 19 September 1929; Gesellschafts- und Wirtschaftsmuseum correspondence, archive of the Museum of Science and Industry, Chicago [GwM-MSi].)

113. 'Bildhafte Pädagogik im Gesellschafts- und Wirtschaftsmuseum in Wien' (1931), p. 198. The idea of the Museum as a kind of 'club' was echoed later by Marie Neurath when she described it as a 'visual centre', taking into account the service it offered to teachers in lending out lantern slides of charts. Marie Neurath, 'Isotype' (1974), p. 143.

## The Vienna Method in the context of the modern movement

![](images/c0170e13d1a7a36083ffa1a4b6056665915ae79bd316cd0e3b1cab55281d690d.jpg)  
T384 Poster for the Werkbund exhibition in Vienna, 193o. A model housing development was also announced here but this was not built in Vienna until 1932 (see p. 83 below).

114. This subsection builds on suggestions made in a subsection (bearing a similar title) of Robin Kinross's essay 'The work of Otto Neurath in visual communication'.

115. An English translation of Werkbund was once given as 'Quality League' in the association's journal, Die Form, Jg. 5, H. 11/12, 1930, p. 302. From October 1929 any member of either the German or Austrian Werkbund automatically became a member of the other, and from the following month Die Form served as an organ of both. See Joan Campbell, The German Werkbund: the politics of reform in the applied arts (Princeton: Princeton University Press, 1978), ρ. 233.

116. See Long, Josef Frank, pp. 122–3. In 1932 the Gesellschafts- und Wirtschaftsmuseum also collaborated with the Austrian Werkbund on two exhibitions in Vienna: 'Der gute billige Gegenstand' (the good, inexpensive object) contained a section of material designed by the Museum about Viennese housing; and the Museum designed a poster for the Siedlung of model houses constructed in Vienna's sixth district under Frank's direction (see p. 83 below).

117. 'Das neue Bauhaus in Dessau' (1926), p. 211

In 1929 the Gesellschafts- und Wirtschaftsmuseum became a member of the Austrian Werkbund, an affiliate of the German Werkbund originally established in 1907 to foster quality in industrial design. 115 Through its conferences, exhibitions and publications, the Werkbund had been involved in debating key issues of modern, material culture – of architecture in particular, with many of its leading members being prominent architects. The Austrian Werkbund reformed in 1928 from split factions under a new committee that included architects Josef Frank and Josef Hoffmann; in June 1932 Neurath was also elected to the committee. Through his close friendship with Frank, it appears that Neurath was involved in this reunification of the Austrian Werkbund, which occurred around the same time that Neurath became active in another Viennese forum – the Verein Ernst Mach (Ernst Mach society), where the ideas of the Vienna Circle were first made public. (A connection between these two initiatives is indicated by Josef Frank giving a lecture at the Verein Ernst Mach about 'The modern world-conception and modern architecture'.) During their collaboration in the settlers' movement and at the Museum, Frank and Neurath had developed similar views on architecture, which did not conform entirely to the functionalism that dominated the German Werkbund. Frank attacked the 'radical moderns' at the Werkbund's annual conference of 193o, which was held in Vienna.11⁶

Frank and Neurath had both been on the editorial board of a monthly journal of 'settlement and town-planning' titled Der Aufbau (Construction), published in Vienna for one year only, in 1926. In early December of that year the iconic Bauhaus building designed by Walter Gropius opened in Dessau; Neurath attended the opening ceremony and reported on it for the journal. In his balanced account, he identified the source of conservative reaction to the building in the conscious 'disavowal of the past' evident in Bauhaus work. He saw the school as ‘a powerful creation of lively people', which was ‘setting energy loose in all quarters', but added: 'Just as we are divided, so is the Bauhaus – how could it be otherwise. Each energetic push forward must, it seems, have something tormented or over-acute about it.'117

The historian of science Peter Galison has taken the word Aufbau as a keyword to draw some intriguing links between logical empiricism and 'architectural modernism' (as well as being the title of the Viennese journal Der Aufbau, it also appears in the title of Rudolf Carnap's book,

![](images/30dc41bc5f207894acf373585e598d1385cd9adb8c51ac7ae6452bc417805922.jpg)  
Photomontage from Der Aufbau, Jg.1, Nr. 6, 1926. Probably not made at the Gesellschafts- und Wirtschaftsmuseum.

Der logische Aufbau der Welt [1928], and there is an obvious, semantic overlap between 'Aufbau' and 'Bauhaus'). Further details about the personal connections behind these links have been documented by Hans-Joachim Dahms. As part of the Austrian Werkbund's new lecture program, Bauhaus director Hannes Meyer was invited to Vienna in March 1929. Two months later Neurath went to the Bauhaus, where he spoke about 'Pictorial statistics and the present day'.118 This inspired two Bauhaus students, Walter Allner and Lotte Beese, to go and work briefly at the Gesellschafts- und Wirtschaftsmuseum. Neurath returned to Dessau in summer 193o to give two lectures on 'History and economy', while in the meantime his colleagues in the Vienna Circle, Herbert Feigl and Carnap, both spent several days each giving lectures at the Bauhaus.119

A catalyst in the formation of such connections was the art historian Franz Roh. He had known Carnap since before the First World War, and had encountered Neurath during the Munich Räterepublik. Not only did Roh introduce Gerd Arntz to Neurath, but he was also instrumental in bringing Carnap to the Vienna Circle.12º Roh had been an adviser for the exhibition of paintings at Mannheim in 1925 which introduced the term die neue Sachlichkeit to the art world. This term soon began to be applied loosely to many aspects of German, avant-garde culture, including functionalist architecture, photography and typography. A missing link in this respect, perhaps, is Jan Tschichold, who was a close friend and collaborator of Roh, and whose 'handbook for contemporary designers', Die neue Typographie, was published in 1928.121 As Arntz mentioned later (p. 68 above), this book was important for him in his

118. Dahms, 'Neue Sachlichkeit in the architecture and philosophy of the 192os'. Neurath's lecture provoked the communist cell at the Bauhaus to label him a 'right-wing enemy of the people' (p. 365).

119. Feigl remarked later: 'I owe him [Neurath] a special debt of gratitude for sending me (I think as the first "emissary" of the Vienna Circle) to Bauhaus Dessau'. (Feigl, 'The Wiener Kreis in America', p. 637.)

120. Dahms, 'Neue Sachlichkeit in the architecture and philosophy of the 1920s', p. 364.

121. Die neue Typographie is placed in its historical context by Robin Kinross in his introduction to the English translation, The new typography. Tschichold's book contained a reproduction of some pages from the 1925 catalogue of Gerd Arntz's woodcuts that was designed by Franz W. Seiwert (p.170).

![](images/5aa0cbf7b1282af16117c8b4cd6ea785657c5b852a7ce2ac33f1e90c4d617386.jpg)  
Jan Tschichold's layout for a standardized letterhead of the Gesellschafts- und Wirtschaftsmuseum (never printed), 1929.

design work at the Vienna Museum. Indeed Tschichold worked briefly at the Museum some time in the first half of 1929 and toyed with the idea of taking a full-time position there. His contribution should not be emphasized too much, but it is likely that he assisted in the design work for Gesellschaft und Wirtschaft.12² The Werkbund exhibition Film und 123 foto', co-curated by Tschichold, was also brought to Vienna in 193o.

The Vienna Method of Pictorial Statistics could appear to be a concrete activity linking logical empiricism with the modern movement in art and design – it was a method of graphic design developed under the leadership of a principal member of the Vienna Circle. The reduction to essential graphic components apparent in its mature period, with the adoption of elements from ‘New Typography', seems to align it with die neue Sachlichkeit.1²⁴ But the case is not so simple: the Vienna Method did not ensue from the kind of geometrical elementarism at the root of much Bauhaus design, for example. As Volker Thurm-Nemeth has observed: 'much that was described in movements of art and architecture as "sachlich", "functional" and “rational” must be counted, in a Neurathian sense, as pseudo-rational.'1²⁵ Some early instances of the Vienna Method show experiments with geometrical design of human figures, but these did not persist and, particularly in the skilled hands of Arntz, Isotype pictograms represented objects in simple form but without forcing them into a geometrical schema (see p. 511 below). This unforced attitude, respectful of convention, extended generally to all graphic configuration in Isotype. An index of this is the use, from 1929, of the typeface Futura for typesetting on all Vienna Method and Isotype charts. Futura is often lumped together with Bauhaus achievements but it was developed outside that school. Unlike the famous attempts at geometric script design made at the Bauhaus by Herbert Bayer and Joost Schmidt, Futura (designed by Paul Renner) balanced geometry with a knowledge of traditional letterforms. One should not perhaps

122. Tschichold, in turn, was well acquainted with the Russian designer El Lissitzky, who became an important connection for the Museum in the uSSR.

123. See Burke, Active literature: Jan Tschichold and New Typography, pp. 105-7. Roh was also involved with this exhibition when it was shown in Munich under the title 'Das Lichtbild'.

124. Herbert Feigl stated that both Neurath and Carnap referred to logical empiricism as ‘an expression of the neue Sachlichkeit which was part of the ideology of the

Bauhaus'. (Feigl, 'The Wiener Kreis in America', p. 637.) There may have been some confusion here between Sachlichkeit (a catchword of the Werkbund) and the 'new' Sachlichkeit of the late 192os. Eve Blau has pointed out the distinction made by Neurath when he wrote to Franz Roh that the Vienna Museum was 'full of old Sachlichkeit' – he specifically meant the 'old' version, implying a plainness and simplicity in technical and aesthetic matters, as opposed to the ideologically charged connotations of the 'new'. (Blau, 'Isotype and architecture in

Red Vienna', p. 238.) Neurath's article 'Rationalismus, Arbeiterschaft und Baugestaltung' is permeated with the term Sachlichkeit, which he defines in a rather humanistic way (p. 49). In his view, Constructivism (a prime contributor to die neue Sachlichkeit) 'deałs with rationalism as a spectacle instead of encouraging a deeper penetration of it' (p. 52). Neurath declared to Waldemar Kaempffert: Through my entire museum work, through articles and lectures, I attempt to propagate Sachlichkeit!' (Letter of 9 January 1930; GWM-MSI.)

125. 'Die Konstruktion des modernen Lebens – ein Fragment', in Thurm-Nemeth (ed.), Konstruktion zwischen Werkbund und Bauhaus: Wissenschaft-Architektur-Wiener Kreis (Vienna: Hölder-Pichler-Tempsky, 1998), p. 61. Thurm-Nemeth doubts Galison's thesis that there was a 'common vision' shared by the Vienna Circle and the Bauhaus. Neurath defined pseudo-rationalism as 'a belief in powers that regulate existence and foretell the future' in his early essay 'The lost wanderers of Descartes' (1913), Philosophical papers 1913–1946, p.9.

read too much into its adoption at the Museum, since it was the only typeface commercially available in the 192os that properly embodied the idea of letterforms reduced to their essentials – although this was partly because the Bauhaus attempts were too theoretical and not workable as widely usable typefaces.12⁶

Neurath perceived the potential for formalism in Bauhaus style' when considering the interior design of its new building:

Taken all in all, in its plan and in its details, the Bauhaus shows how, without the traditional props, one can achieve pleasing effects, of the kind that are given to us by large steam engines or turbine installations: smooth planes, rectangular constructions, shiny tubes, doors without frames – these turn into the means of shaping space. But we see here, as well as with the individual objects, how the delight in industrial forms issues in a life of its own: a light and yet comfortable easy chair made from steel tubing is certainly a very welcome piece of work. But must the stress on rectangularity go so far that the tubular frames of the table, seat or chair run on horizontally where they rest on the floor, thus causing wobbling and unsteadiness on an uneven surface? It seems that the delight in industrial appearance' misleads the constructors into renouncing the long tested four supporting points, which could indeed be compatible, without too great a difficulty, with the new redesign.127

Neurath criticized the 'ocean-liner' style of modernist architecture in the pages of the Werkbund journal, Die Form: ‘We want to pose a serious question: are all of these dwellings by our Werkbund friends, all of these ships, not actually modern? Perhaps a paradox becomes readily apparent: that “modern" is not modern?'128 Neurath reflected on the differences between Germany and Austria in a letter to Josef Frank:

certain realistic actions in music, enlightenment, house building etc. were more at home in Vienna than in Germany, where most everything was more strained and perhaps more verbal. I sometimes consider why our museum work has lasted. Occasionally, when I am kindly disposed towards it, I believe it is because it was relatively timeless and realistic. And therefore it is less subject to fashion. Bauhaus and much else from that time were strongly modish.129

The Museum's work and the Social Democrats' municipal building programme both flourished in the Viennese atmosphere described by Neurath here, and architectural historian Eve Blau has explored parallels between them. She describes the pictographic elements of the Vienna Method as ‘type-forms', which were 'informed by an idea of type as convention that is very different from the concept of generative prototype underlying the idea of neue Sachlichkeit., 130

126. See Burke, Paul Renner: the art of typography, pp. 86–115 Neurath referred to the choice of Futura with characteristic pragmatism: 'The individual symbols should be easily understandable, quickly recognized and strongly differentiated from each other. This can sometimes be achieved by signs drawn in a complicated way, as shown by Fraktur script, the individual letters of which are occasionally more characteristic than those of roman. But in the interest of teaching it is recommendable to use signs, and with them a script, which can be easily reproduced, like grotesque types (Renner's Futura etc), the legibility of which in accompanying text one could possibly dispute. They also suit best optically the quantitypictures in the Vienna Method, for which reason they are used for the inscriptions on them.' Bildstatistik nach Wiener Methode in der Schule' (1933), pp. 282–3.

127. 'Das neue Bauhaus in Dessau', p. 21o. (Translation by Robin Kinross.) A similar criticism of Bauhaus objects was made later by Josef Frank: see Blau, The architecture of Red Vienna, pp. 196–7.

128. 'Die neue Zeit im Lichte der Zahlen' (193o), pp. 174–5. See also Frank, 'Was ist modern?', Die Form, Jg. 5, H.15, 1930, pp.399–406.

129. Letter Neurath to Frank, 7 April 1940 (öNB 1219/4).

130. Blau, The architecture of Red Vienna, p. 396. However, Neurath was critical of Vienna's municipal architecture: 'You see, I liked Vienna very much and our progressive government in Vienna, but the architecture was mostly sad and low, as Josef Frank may tell you.' Letter Neurath to Kaempffert, 21 April 1942 (IC 1/46).

![](images/72bf0a7e2e8b4112e7cb922a38890034ecf36361ba4deef433344d45a5751591.jpg)

## T168 (above) Central laundry of a city apartment block

![](images/2ebbc54af8b145afeed8bb961bc02ffc50117343f19ed657e240380c5677b2c1.jpg)

Shallow relief model made at the Gesellschafts- und Wirtschaftsmuseum for the exhibition 'Wohnung und Siedlung in Stadt und Land' (Housing and settlement in town and country), Linz, 1929.

N63 (left) Photograph of the laundry from the Viennese apartment block Karl-Marx-Hof, designed by Karl Ehn and constructed between 1927 and 1930.

![](images/e704fbe305107ba330a045be302d6d11c13c9ada9c73e8d3b43629c7967f2a45.jpg)

![](images/23f3ffbcc57ab3da6a2663ea42218ae44fac2f12559e9a999ee1b4ac7c135b11.jpg)
