Shallow relief plan of an apartment designed by Josef Frank for Wiedenhoferhof (1924/5).

## T169 (left) Minimum requirements of the Austrian association for housing reform

For new buildings | for old buildings.   
The statement running up the left side states: 'Built area as large as unbuilt area'.

Both made for the Linz housing exhibition, 1929.

![](images/87d6497cceb6e136c180958996e5afbb5a78b82d39b3e2fc09b707a17e8cecc6.jpg)  
One of the charts seen in the photograph above.

131. 'Grafische Typisierung', Die Form, Jg. 5, H. 2, 1930, pp. 36–9 (following Neurath's article; signed L.').

132. Jäckh, 'The new era', Die Form, Jg. 5, H. 11/12, 1930, p. 302. This is an English translation of the German text originally published in Jg. 4, H. 15, 1929; between publication of the original and its translation, the date of the exhibition was postponed further from 1932 until 1933 (it was originally intended for 1929). This debate brings to mind the farcical discussions about the nebulous Parallelaktion in Robert Musil's Der Mann ohne Eigenschaften, which he began writing in 1924 (first volume published in late 1930). See The man without qualities (translated by Sophie Wilkins; New York: Knopf, 1995), vol. 1, pp. 436–7. English readers may aiso be reminded of the similar soul-searching that occured in 1999 about what exactly would happen in the newly constructed Millennium Dome in London.

133. 'Die neue Zeit Köln 1932' (1929), pp. 134, 136–7. Jäckh had given a lecture in Vienna in 1929 inviting the Gesellschafts- und Wirtschaftsmuseum to represent Austria in 'Die neue Zeit': he expected much from its 'collective work method' and 'normative representation'. Lecture summarized in Das Bild, Jg. 4, H.12, December 1927.

Neurath wrote a lengthy essay for Die Form titled 'Das Sachbild' (The factual picture), illustrated with examples of the Vienna Method. Some illustrations of pictorial statistics not made at the Vienna Museum were mischievously appended to his article with accompanying comments by the journal's assistant editor Wilhelm Lotz. These freer and more painterly' representations, Lotz suggested, could add ‘a certain charm' [Reiz] to an exhibition.1³1 Neurath did not respond to this directly but instead made a contribution to a larger debate in the Werkbund journal about the form to be taken by its planned exhibition for 1932, Die neue Zeit' (The new era). This exhibition never took place, due no doubt to economic factors, but from 1925 the plans for it had taken on a life of their own as a forum for discussing modernity. 'What does the idea of a “New era" mean? Not novelties and fashions, not modernism or “new objectivity" [neue Sachlichkeit]', proclaimed the exhibition's 'General commissioner' Ernst Jäckh in his outline for the event. The first stated aim was a decisive alteration and development of the whole antiquated Exhibition idea'.¹³² (Presumably Jäckh had in mind the London Great Exhibition of 1851; Neurath presumed so and paid tribute to the organizers of that pioneering exhibition in his published response to the outline.) The conceptual foundations' of the Werkbund exhibition could be debated endlessly, Neurath pointed out, but the question for an exhibition practician'was whether it could be made into an effective educational affair'. Nevertheless, he took seriously the philosophical ambitions of Jäckh's proposal for Die neue Zeit':

It makes good sense that the introductory section of the Werkbund's outline draws attention to the conception of the world in our time, even if it must be said that this is in many ways a matter of logicalmathematical thought processes, which cannot easily be made visible to the viewer, but can be made so nonetheless. For it is characteristic of our time that many of its conceptually most important innovations have already occurred in the field of logic.

He felt, however, that the effects of the modern scientific conception of the world' were limited to a small part of the global population, and he argued that the exhibition should also represent the world beyond western Europe in a way that its representatives would acknowledge to be factually correct'. Praise and blame are not the aim of this show,' he added, rather plain explanation and representation of that which is, and that which is becoming; but also an account of earlier steps, insofar as they make the present day comprehensible'.1³³ His proposals here naturally overlap with the exhibition strategy of the Vienna Museum.

![](images/a6a391cd279ea2e6b85fa0badac35d9a3e1c3a6e58b0aea34b6f960f43af1228.jpg)  
N1401 Houses under construction at the Werkbund Siedlung, Vienna. 1932. Architects (left to right): Schütte-Lihotzky, Max Fellerer, Hans Vetter, Adolf Loos.

![](images/3d3a7a9c5160cb66132a294de863e90f75f1ded67dff93ba5f447b04cc88f67e.jpg)

In his second contribution to this debate, Neurath was more combative in his attempt to anchor the plans for an exhibition about the new era'. He argued for a basis of empirical information, graphically displayed:

N2015 Poster designed at the Gesellschafts- und Wirtschaftsmuseum for the Werkbund Siedlung, Vienna. 1932.

Statistics talk. There is not, adjacent to the world that we perceive, another, which as Lotz says is made up of the secret, strange, instinctive forces of the people of an epoch and of mass psychology'; there is not adjacent to the facts a particular force of ideas'. It is just in what experience shows us – in the figures for suicide, in the figures for birth rate, in the figures for cremation, in the figures for houses with bathrooms – in everything that we record, that the so-called instinctive forces’ and the force of ideas' are to be seen.

He concluded: 'We fight for the “new era” but always from the firm ground of reality.'134

When the Werkbund's housing development in Vienna was finally constructed in 1932, Neurath wrote about it for Die Form. He praised its stylistic diversity, stating that 'typification and standardization must not hinder diversity and personal existence'. Once again some qualifying, editorial remarks were attached to his article on publication, forcing a response from Neurath.1ª⁵ It is worth noting that Neurath's views seemed controversial to the Werkbund journal's editorship for two different reasons: in the case of pictorial statistics because he went too far with standardization; and in the case of architecture because he did not go far enough. This not only reveals that different standards were applied to graphic design and architecture, but also that Neurath and the Gesellschafts- und Wirtschaftsmuseum occupied a place outside the mainstream of German-speaking modernism between the World Wars.136

134. 'Die neue Zeit im Lichte der Zahlen' (1930), pp. 173, 176.

135. 'Die internationale Werkbundsiedlung Wien 1932 als Ausstellung" (1932), p. 221; and 'Unproblematisch und traditionsbetont?' (1932), pp. 227–30.

136. In this sense, Neurath's attempt to bring graphic standards to architectural plans of the international architectural association ciAm was perhaps bound to fail. See Vossoughian, Otto Neurath, pp.113-39.

137. Bildstatistik nach Wiener Methode in der Schule (1933), p. 275.

138. 'Bildstatistik' (1927), p. 99. In referring to the matters conveyed by statistics Neurath often used the term Tatbestände (facts, or facts of a matter), but at other times he used Zusammenhänge (connections) instead. The word 'fact' made it on to his list of 'expressions one should try to avoid' (his 'index verborum prohibitorum'; see From hieroglyphics to Isotype p. xvii). There is some similarity in Neurath's use of the phrase 'social facts' with Émile Durkheim's, although it is not clear whether Neurath was familiar with Durkheim's work.

139. 'Statistik und Proletariat' (1927), p. 84.

## Statistics in pictures

As an empiricist, Otto Neurath considered statistical data to be a precondition for sociology and modern planning: 'All discussions of social and economic questions link to statistical data. Planned control of healthcare, transport, production and consumption today needs extensive statistics.'¹³7 He perceived this need to have arisen at the beginning of the modern era: centralizing capital needed statistics but so too did critics of capitalist economy, such as Marx and Engels. Neurath was quite aware of the controlling purposes for which statistics had been employed, not least by official bodies of the Austro-Hungarian empire, yet he felt that this did not render them unusable:

Statistics have the reputation of being boring, incomprehensible and forged. Statistics are really often wrong; but not because this way of examining things has a falsifying' effect, rather instead because it is applied in an unskilled and unclear way, or because it consciously employs falsehood. This happens frequently, but statistics should not be marked with the responsibility for this. Statistics really are often boring and incomprehensible'; but this is principally due to their not being purposefully represented, and to, among other things, the fact that statistical writers mostly use too many numbers and rows of numbers, and in general include too many details and thereby confuse the reader. To represent purposefully' means this: using the results of this extremely useful research method, which provides the decisive relationships of earthly and social things in countless areas by numbers and proportions, in educating people only when they are simplified enough for looking through; when one has extracted from the immeasurable stock of figures, of which most are only the concern of specialists, the really important and in broad terms instructive material, and synthesized it into clear and meaningful part-representations of our worldscape.138

It was a central task of the Gesellschafts- und Wirtschaftsmuseum in Wien to represent such data in attractive and memorable form so that it could be understood by all kinds of people. In the years around 193o, Neurath wrote about the possibilities offered by pictorial statistics in children's education, but also, with an augmented rhetoric, about the role that they could play in the class struggle. In an essay titled 'Statistics and proletariat' he wrote: 'Statistics are a tool of the proletarian struggle, statistics are an essential part of socialist order, statistics are joy for the international proletariat struggling hard with the ruling classes.'139 Neurath went so far as to propose pictorial statistics as a Marxist tool, in an article about their applicability to the workers' movement:

But such quantity-pictures [Mengenbilder] are not only easy to grasp visually and thereby well suited to the worker. They give from the outset a fundamentally Marxist philosophy, because they reproduce all that can be objectively represented in terms of quantities. The quantity pictures force a certain completeness of view, they immediately excite comparison. They give a feeling of security, as one has no fear of the little figures as one does of curves and numbers.¹⁴º

He also pre-empted criticism for what today would be called 'dumbing down':

The over-educated and the over-refined will find something to carp about in all this, and will think that the rounding off in the figures is too coarse. The Museum and its collaborators counter with the position: to remember simplified pictures of quantities is better than to forget accurate figures! Or more precisely: than not having looked at accurate figures in any way correctly! Just at this moment, when understanding of the whole world should be opened up to Central-European workers, it is important to have such a means of modern enlightenment at our disposal.141

Instead of condescending to less-educated adults, Neurath wanted to empower them with far-reaching information and show them how they fitted into the world's complex of interconnections. In his essay Colonialpolitical enlightenment through picture statistics' he suggested:

The worker has an interest in knowing how war and peace can depend on the oil conflict between Standard Oil and the Shell group, and wants to understand how it happens that the Soviet Union makes its oil available to the usA when it stands against the capitalist world in other ways. ...

It is not sufficient to learn about the interdependencies, one must know how to assess their relative implications, the extent of the individual measures and movements! 142

This last sentence makes explicit that the Vienna Method could never be simply neutral: the adaptation of raw data into what Neurath called Merkbilder (memorable pictures) required opinions and decisions on the part of the producers about what elements were most important. Pictorial education with the Vienna Method and Isotype moulded information into ‘visual arguments' (as Neurath later came to call them) for educational and sometimes political purposes. Perhaps this partly explains the mysterious remark made by Neurath in 1929 that, not only was the Vienna Method less accessible to bourgeois intellectuals due to its optical content', but that 'for certain reasons the spread of this [Vienna] Method goes against bourgeois ideology.' 143

140. 'Bildstatistik und Arbeiterbildung' (1929), pp. 141–3. 141. 'Kolonialpolitische Aufklärung durch Bildstatistik' (1928), p.132. 142. 'Kolonialpolitische Aufklärung durch Bildstatistik' (1928), pp, 128–9. 143. 'Bildstatistik und Arbeiterbildung' (1929), p. 139.

![](images/2fb3fa6aa9f5a1ef242f9aaaba2c1092d5366940d00883863da540984e8ea92e.jpg)

Folder of loose-leaf charts issued by the Gesellschaftsund Wirtschaftsmuseum. The chart on the right was made for the World Social Economic Congress, 1931, and was probably published in this form in 1932. The cover in English dates from 1933, as it gives the address of the London Mundaneum branch established in that year (see pp.98–9 below). Printed in black and red.

144. 'Bildstatistik nach Wiener Methode' (1931), p. 189. 145. 'Bildstatistik und Arbeiterbildung' (1929), p. 140. 146. Gruber, Red Vienna, pp.88-9.

147. Bauer, 'The concept of the nation' (1907), translated in T. Bottomore & P. Goode, Austro-Marxism (Oxford: Clarendon Press 1978), p. 111. The original German text formed part of the chapter about 'socialism and the principle of nationality' in Die Nationalitätenfrage und die Sozialdemokratie.

![](images/5d988cdc5e02e3cb5879987c7a50d975c2bf44431698abab332a8c2966d69bd8.jpg)

Beyond its appeal to the visual sensitivity of modern people accustomed to cinema, advertising and illustrated magazines, Neurath believed that 'pictorial education is a means of opening chances for learning to little educated adults (who tend to be more receptive visually) and to less fortunate youths, which would otherwise be out of the question for them'.¹⁴4 In addition to the Museum itself, he considered the distribution of handbills to be useful in this respect, and small, printed reproductions of charts were made available for this purpose.¹⁴⁵ Surveys made among readers of socialist periodicals during this period in Austria showed that workers had little appetite for economic subjects;1⁴ Neurath evidently felt that a populist approach was suitable for engaging their interest in such matters.

The international outlook of the Gesellschafts- und Wirtschaftsmuseum aligns with the tradition of 'Austro-Marxism', which developed in some ways as a response to the 'national question' within the multinational Austro-Hungarian Empire. The leader of the Austrian Social Democrats, Otto Bauer, had addressed this matter in an important study of 1907; the following passage from it, outlining the primary importance of socialist education, seems to offer a kind of intellectual blueprint for Neurath's Museum:

This revolution in the consciousness of the masses will be consolidated by the everyday praxis of socialist society, which gives the masses for the first time the power to determine their own destiny, to decide by free discussion and resolution their own future, and thus make the development of human culture a deliberate, intentional, conscious human act. It will be made possible by socialist education, which will provide every individual with the cultural objects of the whole nation and indeed a good part of the whole human race. Only in this way can the individual be liberated from the traditions of restricted local circles, broaden his views, and be enabled to establish his own ends and make an intelligent choice of the means to those ends.147

## The Vienna Method, anti-metaphysics, and socialist education

From around 1928, there was a confluence of three streams in Neurath's work: the Gesellschafts- und Wirtschaftsmuseum & the Vienna Method education for socialism, and his contribution to the logical empiricism of the Vienna Circle.148 Although the beginnings of the Vienna Method date from around 1923/4, it acquired a maturity with the collaboration of Gerd Arntz from 1928. This was also the year in which Neurath's book Lebensgestaltung und Klassenkampf was published,149 and in which he became active in the Verein Ernst Mach. Neurath led the participation of Vienna Circle members as officers and lecturers at the Verein Ernst Mach, initiating the public phase of logical empiricism. In a printed announcement of the society, 'Otto Neurath, museum director' was listed as its secretary and he was scheduled to give a lecture on 'Antimetaphysics and social science'. Friedrich Stadler suggests that Neurath intended to ally the educational work of the Museum with adult education of a 'popular-theoretical' kind through the Verein Ernst Mach. Neurath indicated these intertwined objectives when summarizing the trajectory of the Vienna Method of Pictorial Statistics up to 1933:

Lectures and instruction continue to take place about this new method and its application to general adult education, and in particular workers' education, which served as the original starting point for these endeavours.1⁵1

Neurath's ‘anti-metaphysical' approach was certainly a common feature of all his work at this time. In terms of workers' education, he expressed it as follows:

In this, enlightenment about human beings and material things, about connections of all sorts, plays a decisive role. Excitation of enthusiasm and the portrayal of glorious goals do not come to the fore, rather much more the purely factual description of that which is. 152

These words are taken from an article in the socialist newspaper Die Arbeiterzeitung about the 'scientific world-conception' ('Wissenschaftliche Weltauffassung'). This was also the title of the Vienna Circle manifesto published in the same year and co-written by Neurath.¹⁵³ Here it was explained that‘The scientific world-conception rejects metaphysical philosophy', reflecting Neurath's strong antipathy to the German philosophical tradition. By collaborating with the Verein Ernst Mach in its programme of lectures and publications, the Vienna Circle aimed 'to fashion intellectual tools for everyday life, for the daily life of the

148. In 1937, by which time his socialist agenda had waned, Neurath was able to look back and identify Isotype and the Vienna Circle as twin stimuli for his continuing project of unified science. 'Unified science and its encyclopedia' (1937), p. 180.

149. The title of this book is difficult to translate: 'Form of life and class struggle' is a literal but clumsy rendering; 'Personal life and class struggle' was given as the title for the translated text in Empiricism and sociology. Its working title was 'Sozialismus als Wirklichkeit' – 'Socialism as reality (Marie Neurath, 'What I remember', p. 36). The original edition contains a section on 'statistics and socialism' that largely repeats Neurath's article of 1927, 'Statistik und Proletariat', and is omitted from the English translation.

150. Stadler, The Vienna Circle, p. 329. Another token of the relationship between the Verein Ernst Mach and the Museum was that Josef Jodibauer was an administrator in both. Julius Tandler, Vienna city councillor for health, was on the board of the Verein Ernst Mach.

151. Bildstatistik nach Wiener Methode in der Schule (1933), p.267.

152. 'Wissenschaftliche Weltauffassung' (1929), p. 345.

153. See Thomas E. Uebel, 'On the production history and early reception of The scientific conception of the world. The Vienna Circle', in Stadler & Uebel (ed.), Wissenschaftliche Weltauffassung: der Wiener Kreis (Vienna/New York: Springer, 2012), pp.297-304.

154. 'Wissenschaftliche Weltauffassung: der Wiener Kreis' (1929), pp. 305, 307, 317.

156. 'Unified science and psychology' (1933), p. 4.

157. 'Wissenschaftliche Weltauffassung: der Wiener Kreis' (1929), p.306.

158. Kinross, book reviews in Information Design Journal, vol.17, no. 1, 2009, p. 71.

155. 'Personal life and class struggle' (1928), p. 252.

scholar but also for the daily life of all those who in some way join in working at the conscious re-shaping of life'. Pictorial statistics were just such a tool, with which Neurath hoped to reach beyond scholars – leading a solitary existence on ‘the icy slopes of logic' – to the masses.1s4 In Lebensgestaltung und Klassenkampf he had outlined a connection of empirical information with new ways of thinking:

Statistics show what social events mean for social groups. A new world outlook is forming, which will catch everything in a net, so to speak, and assign to each thing and each event a definite place and time. Things that are countable can be most surely determined. This new world outlook spreads wherever traditional theology and philosophy shrivel. ... Scientific attitude and solidarity go together.1⁵s

For Neurath, an essential prerequisite of achieving a scientific attitude was the 'elimination of confusing and crippling verbal clutter'.1⁵⁶ The Vienna Circle manifesto addressed this objective in terms that bring to mind the Vienna Method:

From this aim follows the emphasis on collective efforts, and also the emphasis on what can be grasped intersubjectively; from this springs the search for a neutral system of formulae, for a symbolism freed from the slag of historical languages; and also the search for a total system of concepts. Neatness and clarity are striven for, and dark distances and unfathomable depths rejected. In science there are no 'depths'; there is surface everywhere: all experience forms a complex network, which cannot always be surveyed and can often be grasped only in parts. 157

Neutrality, neatness and clarity were certainly all aims of pictorial education at the Gesellschafts- und Wirtschaftsmuseum (the 'dark distances and unfathomable depths' he referred to were avoided by eschewing perspective and intrusive backgrounds to the pictograms). The Vienna Method stems partly from Neurath's distrust of verbal discourse, but it should not be simply interpreted as an outgrowth of Vienna Circle philosophy. Robin Kinross has criticized an ideas-driven explanation' of Isotype.15⁸ Beside the fact that Neurath was not the sole creator of the Vienna Method (as he often pointed out), to interpret it as an embodiment of his philosophy would be to fall into the very trap that Neurath himself warned about when explaining his standpoint in the Vienna Circle: there is no realm of ideas that stands above or beyond experience';159 'Philosophy as an independent system of definite doctrines is obsolete.'1⁶º Philosophy possessed no sovereign status, in his opinion, and he considered academic philosophers as irrelevant to the modern era: 'Those philosophers are only rarely involved with

the theoretical or practical transformation of the social order.'¹⁶1 For Neurath thinking itself was a 'tool', dependent on 'social and historical conditions'; so too were pictorial education or architecture, and they were arguably more effective than traditional philosophy in achieving practical goals.¹⁶² Referring to his work in the settlement movement of the early 192os, he commented later: 'I remember how I took on the settlers' cause, with its really rather unpleasant ramifications, ... in the consolation that helping to have houses built is surely just as gratifying as writing books.'163

There is no need, then, to legitimate a close study of the Vienna Method by an appeal to philosophy, although it may be fruitful to examine cross-connections' with other areas (which Neurath did think were important). The Vienna Method and logical empiricism were parallel streams of activity, which is reflected by significant overlaps in Neurath's writings about both matters during the Vienna period. One particular example is very illuminating: his oft-repeated catch-phrase of the Museum, 'Words divide – pictures unite', was mirrored in an article about 'unified science' as 'Metaphysical terms divide - scientific terms unite.'¹⁶4 Isotype pictograms were intended to make direct connections to experience, circumventing the pitfalls of verbal language, and therefore creating more potential for international understanding.¹⁶s Later Neurath reflected on the development of Isotype in terms of his demand for a simplified language based on everyday expressions, which he called a universal slang' or ‘universal jargon':

Let me add that this whole problem of making Universal Jargon was put before me in a different shape when I was working out together with my collaborators an International Picture Language for educational purposes. The rules of picture writing are different. Starting with icons' implies far-reaching limitations of language, but these limitations sometimes eliminate much danger. 166

161. 'Unified science and psychology' (1933), p. 9. Here Neurath -echoed the last of Marx's 'Theses on Feuerbach' (1845): 'The philosophers have only interpreted the world in various ways; the point is to change it.' In Lebensgestaltung und Klassenkampf Neurath quoted Epicurus: 'Vain is the word of a philosopher which does not heal any suffering of man.' ('Personal life and class struggle' [1928], p. 288.) Other members of the Vienna Circle did not share Neurath's view, as Carnap explained in his autobiography: 'All of us in the Circle were strongly interested in social and political progress. Most of us, myself included, were socialists. But we liked to keep our philosophical work separated from our political aims. ... Neurath criticized strongly this neutralist attitude, which in his opinion gave aid and comfort to the enemies of social progress. We in turn insisted that the intrusion of practical and especially of political points of view would violate the purity of philosophical methods.' Carnap, 'Autobiography', in P. A.

Schilpp (ed.), The philosophy of Rudolf Carnap (La Salle, IIl: Open Court, 1963) p. 23. Karl Menger recalled: 'Otto Neurath was a man of immense energy and curiosity and was very fast in grasping new ideas. However, he looked at everything – ideas as well as facts – through an often distorting lens of socialist philosophy and with an eye to the possible effects of the ideas and facts on a socialization of society. I have never seen a scholar as consistently obsessed with an idea and an ideal as Neurath.' Menger,

Reminiscences of the Vienna Circle and the Mathematical Colloquium (Dordrecht: Kluwer, 1994), p. 6o.   
162. 'Ways of the scientific world conception' (1930), p. 46.   
163. Letter Neurath to Josef   
Frank, 31 October 1939 (öNB 1219/3). 164. 'Unified science and psychology' (1933), p. 23.   
165. Neurath called the picto  
grams 'sprechende Zeichen'   
(speaking signs) in 'Bildstatistik   
nach Wiener Methode' (1931), p. 183. 166. 'Universal jargon and terminology' (1941), p. 215.

In this sense, the Vienna Method could be seen as a manifestation of 'physicalism', which was the term Neurath used to describe his version of logical empiricism. As the word implies, physicalism restricted discourse to descriptions of the physical world; it only recognized 'statements about spatio-temporal things'.167 The Vienna Method was perfectly suited to this, using pictograms and maps to show quantities (changing over time) and spatial distribution. Neurath's colleague in the Vienna Circle, the physicist Philipp Frank, recalled later Neurath's intentions for a physicalist language:

He suggested that sense data should be dropped as elementary concepts of the logical structure of the world and replaced by physical things. Instead of building up the system of human knowledge upon concepts like 'red spot' or 'feeling of warmth', one should use elementary symbols expressing concepts like 'rock' or ‘table', and define 'redness', or 'warmth' as derived concepts. ... Neurath worked out a system based on physical things as elementary concepts and called by him physicalism'.1⁶8

With its use of pictograms to iconically represent physical objects, the Vienna Method corresponds directly to this strategy. As Neurath explained:

This means of course a retreat to some extent from the predominant scholastic tradition based on words and concepts, which often works against an empirical attitude, while pictorial education favours empiricism. Pictorial statistics operate from the outset with spatio-temporal patterns, while in verbal language the possibility exists of using senseless links, which can often only be got rid of with difficulty.169

167. ‘Empirical sociology' (1931), p.325.

168. Frank, Modern science and its philosophy (Cambridge, MA: Harvard University Press, 1949) pp.35-6.

169. 'Bildstatistik nach Wiener Methode' (1931), p. 190.

170. 'Unified science and psychology' (1933), p. 11.

171. 'Wissenschaftliche Weltauffassung: der Wiener Kreis' (1929), p. 315.

172. 'Physicalism: the philosophy of the Viennese Circle' (1931), p. 50. See also his response to Lotz, p. 83 above.

173. This was the translation of Mengenbilder into basic English in International picture language (1936), p. 73.

Neurath's physicalism, as a kind of materialism, naturally mapped on to his efforts in socialist education: 'Marxism' he declared 'is basically physicalistic'.17º The Vienna Circle manifesto stated that ‘The object of history and economics are people, things and their arrangement' - all of which could be adequately represented with the descriptive resources of Isotype.171 Neurath set physicalism in opposition to 'transcendental metaphysical entities' such as the ‘spirit of an age' or 'the powers of the spirit', which could not possibly be represented by Isotype.17² Yet pictorial education at the Gesellschafts- und Wirtschaftsmuseum was not restricted to number-fact pictures',17ª as Neurath called statistical charts; he made it clear that the techniques of the Vienna Method were equally appropriate for treating anatomical and technical subjects, just like the language of physicalism:

T396 Blood circulation system C.1930

We are always searching for correlations between magnitudes that occur in the physicalist description of events. It makes no difference in principle whether the descriptions are statistical or non-statistical. Whether the statistical behaviour of atoms or of plants or of animals is being investigated, the methods of stating a correlation are always the same.

It does not make sense to speak of different ‘essences' that rest 'behind' the events. What cannot be expressed by the relations between the elements cannot be expressed at all. Therefore it is meaningless to speak of the essence of things' beyond their correlations. 174

A reductionist tendency could be interpreted from these words, and it is this that made some people uncomfortable (and still does) with both logical empiricism and Isotype. But the (deceptive) simplification in Isotype had an educational objective, and there was never an intention to provide cold, dull information: on the contrary, within the constraints of a consistent language-like technique' (as Neurath called it later), the attempt was made to produce a colourful and attractive variety of information. He summarized this approach in 1933:

About a decade of the Vienna Method has taught us that it is possible to interest hundreds and thousands of people with well-designed pictorial charts and models, worked out with strict logic, without having to resort to unreasoned variation, fooling around or so-called amusing drawings. ... To show the richness of reality in an understandable way is colourful and delightful enough; there is no lack of stimulation.17s

The tonal variant of the Vienna Method version shown here was also published in Österreichische Gemeinde-Zeitung, Jg. 4, Nr. 9, 1927.

In explaining the benefits of a unified style not only for statistical charts but also for health education and accident prevention material, Neurath asserted:

'Accident prevention pictures should not reflect the accident of an individual, rather make clear schematically the process, danger and its avoidance. As it is not only about instruction here, rather also about urgency and insistence, these pictures are closely related with those of advertising.' ('Form und Farbe der Mengenbilder des Gesellschaftsund Wirtschaftsmuseums', 1929, p. 193.)

Later he commented that it is important 'not to create fear but to create responsibility, a social feeling, in factories and on the street'. (Letter to Paul Rotha, 20 October 1942; IC 1/43.)

## Diversification

Although keeping archives is a conventional task of museums, it may not seem obvious that the Gesellschafts- und Wirtschaftsmuseum, with its orientation to the present and future, should keep a historical archive: yet, alongside the photographic records it kept of its own work, an Archiv für bildhafte Pädagogik (Archive of pictorial education) was assembled at the Museum by collecting 'descriptive pictures [Sachbilder] from all times and peoples'.17 This reflected Neurath's interest in the history of visual education and his willingness to learn from past attempts.¹" Also within this archive was a collection of what Neurath called 'sociological graphics', consisting of prints by artists associated with the Museum but not made specifically for the Museum's purposes (see chapter 4, pp. 234–5 below).

Additionally, the Museum built up a collection of original photographs taken by its staff photographer, Walter Pfitzner. These included a record of the new housing constructed by the Vienna municipality, but also series of photographs documenting work procedures of different professions for career advice purposes (see pp. 249–56 below).

Material from both of these archives was used for showing alongside pictorial charts in exhibitions both in Austria and abroad. The Museum's task of showing the achievements of the Vienna municipality was not only carried out in Vienna itself but also in the Austrian and Viennese

176. 'Bildhafte Pädagogik im Gesellschafts- und Wirtschaftsmuseum in Wien' (1931), p. 203. 177. See From hieroglyphics to Isotype.

![](images/ef56ae64e8fdde9cd86260f3ea1a5a1be3fca18ee4b8323cfb65ff34554a3566.jpg)  
Austrian section at the 'Pressa' exhibition, Cologne, 1928. Prepared by the Gesellschafts- und Wirtschaftsmuseum.

178. Otto Neurath listed foreign exhibitions to which the Museum contributed in 'Das Gesellschaftsund Wirtschaftsmuseum in Wien (1931), p. 195; and in 'Bildhafte Pädagogik im Gesellschafts- und Wirtschaftsmuseum in Wien' (1931), p. 201. See also appendix c below.

179. 'Die Ausstellung Wien im Bild" in den Räumen des Gesund heitshauses am Urban in Berlin', Das Bild, Jg. 6, H. 4, April 1929, p. 92. Travelling exhibitions from the Vienna Museum had already been shown in the German cities of Breslau and Stuttgart.

sections of foreign exhibitions: these included two exhibitions in Paris (1928), the printing industry exhibition Pressa' in Cologne (1928), an exhibition about Vienna at the London School of Economics (1931), and an international hygiene exhibition in Dresden (1931), where the Austrian section was declared one of the three best.178

A visit to the Museum by a group from the Russian embassy led to a request for assistance in establishing the Vienna Method in the Soviet Union. This resulted in the most intense and sustained experience of working outside Vienna: staff from the Museum acted as advisors to a Soviet institute called Izostat (see chapter 5). Around the same time a visitor from Berlin inquired about setting up a museum there, which would be modelled on the Gesellschafts- und Wirtschaftsmuseum. This led to a meeting, and long friendship, between Otto Neurath and the mayor of Berlin-Kreuzberg, Carl Herz. With support from other Austrian organizations, the Museum prepared an exhibition called 'Wien im Bild' (Vienna depicted) in rooms of the Gesundheitshaus (health centre) am Urban, Berlin, which opened in March 1929. A report issued by the Museum gives an idea of what the exhibition was meant to achieve:

Misconceptions about Viennese conditions abound in large parts of the Berlin population. Many Berliners still believe that Vienna is the life-affirming city of cheerful hedonism, in which the calm spell of Schubertian melodies does not permit a gloomy atmosphere to arise. It is often overlooked that Vienna is, like Berlin, a city of serious work, economic need and far reaching social tensions, and the achievements of the municipal administration are known almost only in specialist municipal circles. 179

![](images/2ac414f6ff49ce90b2f6c6de1d07b0dfd90ca0428c1dff3f7495f761b79df87a.jpg)  
T780 German elections 1932  
First of two versions of the same chart, made for the Berlin branch of the Museum. The first may have been put together in Berlin because Marie Neurath was only compiling the data during her train journey there – hence the manual lettering (although two of the torso figures can be seen in use at the Vienna workshop in photo N1705, p. 71)

![](images/443a6bbaa2cff279c43c70302a21a8db8c3e11430ad98041ba028a6c2d64537e.jpg)  
T789 German parliamentary elections 31 July 1932

This second version was probably made afterwards in Vienna, and the decision was made to dispense with the human figures and use the party symbols alone.

Each pictogram/symbol = 500,o0o votes (both charts).

Alongside models, photographs and historical images of Vienna, statistical charts compared the social conditions of Germany and Austria. One series about the development of Berlin was intended to form the basis of a permanent exhibition at this venue, which then became the first branch of the Gesellschafts- und Wirtschaftsmuseum outside Vienna. Marie Neurath remembered her one and only visit to it, by train via Prague, after the German elections of July 1932: 'I kept buying newspapers [during the journey] for the most recent results and worked on the corrections which would be needed on a map of the state of the parties in the regions of Germany.'18º This exhibition space remained open until the National Socialists dismantled it.1⁸1

The Berlin branch stayed essentially within the bounds of activity centred on the Vienna Museum, which Neurath later defined as follows:

1. permanent exhibitions, travelling exhibitions etc. ...

2. leaflets for distribution to school children, adults in adult education classes, in exhibitions etc.

3. booklets and books, collections of charts with tables and short notes, no text.

4. periodicals of the same type.

5. charts prepared for other people's books, for official reports etc., for periodicals and newspapers.1⁸2

180. Marie Neurath, 'Otto Neurath and Isotype', p. 13. She recalled these as the 'last pre-Hitler elections' but the last free elections before the Nazi takeover were in November 1932.

181. Later Neurath recounted to Josef Frank: 'Did I tell you, how [in] 1933 the Nazis liquidated the exhibition, but executing German orderliness returned all Vienna property to us, with a label on ss sTuRm with a signature etc. [T]empi passati.' Letter Neurath to Frank, 2o November 1944 (öNB 1219/5). See more charts from the Berlin branch on p.171 below.

182. 'The Isotype work' (ic 3.2/58), p. 6. The Österreichische Gemeinde-Zeitung reported: 'The income of the Museum consists on the one hand of subsidies from the Vienna municipality, the Vienna Arbeiterkammer and other offices, and on the other hand from reimbursement of costs for exhibition work, publications, etc.' (Jg. 6 Nr. 7, 1 April 1929, p. 14.) Friedrich Bauermeister reported at the beginning of 1931: 'Unfortunately we cannot live on the subsidy from the Vienna Municipality, and so we are always allocated outside commissions.' (Letter to Modley, 24 January 1931; GWM-MSI.)

Japan's development towards modern economic reform

A new departure from 1931, which Neurath probably included under the fourth category above, was the publication of the Museum's own journal, Fernunterricht (Distance learning/teaching). This featured pictorial charts (some recycled from other sources) and statistical tables with short texts, including some written (anonymously) by Neurath.183 He outlined his intentions for the periodical to Else Herz (wife of Carl mayor of Berlin-Kreuzberg):

It should be a beautiful thing. We have high hopes for it. I want to add a children's supplement to it later so that we create a new type of enlightening literature! I believe that you and your husband will see immediately that, in these dreadful times of crisis, when the organizing of exhibitions meets with difficulties, such a publication can be used to achieve real success.184

Indeed the world economic crisis was a recurrent theme in the journal, as well as providing the likely explanation for its increasingly irregular appearance until its eleventh and last issue in 1933. (For its last two issues the publication was renamed Bildstatistik [Pictorial statistics].)

The introduction to the first issue appealed to readers for suggestions on content; in the sixth issue this was reiterated: 'Not we, the editors, but the readers should direct Fernunterricht and determine or influence its content.’ In this spirit, a page of each issue in its first year contained answers to readers' questions. With both its title and editorial policy, Fernunterricht seems to have been intended to create the kind of reading community that is now so easily achieved with the interactivity of the internet.

Further outposts of the Museum were established, not as foreign branches but as part of an affiliated institute named Mundaneum, which was announced as a special organization that has taken over the international agenda of the Gesellschafts- und Wirtschaftsmuseum in Wien, with which it continues in close collaboration'.18⁵ From 1929 the Museum had contributed increasingly to foreign exhibitions independently of representing Austria or Vienna. The name Mundaneum was borrowed with permission from Paul Otlet of the Palais Mondial Brussels, who had used it in connection with his idea of producing 'copies of museums in standard series' for international distribution – an idea that obviously aligned with Neurath's own.18⁶ At the Congress of the World Federation of Educational Associations (July-August 1929, Geneva), where charts made with the Vienna Method were exhibited, Neurath and Otlet announced a plan to collaborate on a wide range of publishing and exhibition projects.187 The Vienna Museum began to use

187. On Neurath's connection to Otlet see Vossoughian: Otto Neurath, pp. 103-10; and 'The language of the world museum: Otto Neurath, Paul Otlet, Le Corbusier'.

## MUNDANEUM VIENNA

Pictorial Statistics according to the Vienna Method of the Museum for Sociological and Economic Science in Vienna XIV Ullmannstrasse 44

Bronches: Berlin Amsterdom n prepara

Corresponding Centres: far Great Bntain in London for U. S. A in New York

T86o Sign board for the Mundaneum, possibly made for display at one of the corresponding centres mentioned here.

188. The exhibition was opened by Mayor Karl Seitz under the title 'Weltwirtschaft (Mundaneum) at the apartment building Am Fuchsenfeld. In 1931 Neurath described this Mundaneum as a Sonderschau (special exhibition) of the Museum, which also contained a small selection from its affiliated Internationalen Institut für bildhafte Pädagogik, including images of exhibition buildings of the past such as 'the colossal London Crystal Palace of the [18]5os, a truly modern building of glass and iron'. ('Das Gesellschafts- und Wirtschaftsmuseum in Wien' [1931] p. 193).

189. Robin Kinross suggested that the work in the ussR from 1931 may have prompted its formation, in 'Otto Neurath's contribution to visual communication (1925–45)', p.32.

190. Fernunterricht, Jg.1, H.6,   
1931, p. 15.

191. Marie Neurath, 'What I remember', p. 54.

the name Mundaneum from October 193o, when it was applied to the exhibition of original charts made for Gesellschaft- und Wirtschaft.188

The Mundaneum Wien was established formally as an institute in April 1932, when Neurath was named as its director.189 Exhibition material was produced under its auspices at the Vienna Museum for distribution through international offices. The first of these was established in Amsterdam, building on strong contacts in the Netherlands that had been developing since 1929. In particular, Neurath's participation in the World Social Economic Congress (Amsterdam, August 1931) prepared the ground for a foothold there: charts prepared in Vienna were exhibited at the congress and Neurath also showed them as slides to illustrate his lecture. The congress was convened by the International Industrial Relations Institute (IRI) in order to initiate international cooperation for solving the world economic crisis of the early 193os.1º The founder and director of 1RI, Mary L. Fleddérus, and its American associate director, Mary van Kleeck, 'became, in the difficult years that were to come, something like guardian angels for us', recalled Marie Neurath.¹9i Following the congress, the Mundaneum was offered permanent exhibition space in Amsterdam at the Economisch-Historische Bibliothek (Economic-historical library). Charts about

![](images/3a2e3419bb1403e7111a4b0a4938cc62bcb680c46a83ab5bfb089797a12e2130.jpg)

Pages from Neurath's essay 'Das gegenwärtige Wachstum der Produktionskapazität der Welt' (The current growth of production capacity in the world), based on his lecture at the World Social Economic Congress and published in World social economic planning (The Hague: International Industrial Relations Institute, 1931). On the left is a visualization of Quesnay's economic table; on the right is a chart demonstrating growth of production after rationalization, with a fold-out flap of supplementary statistics. 247×170 mm.

economy in the Netherlands were presented by the Mundaneum (Vienna) for this purpose and Marie Neurath worked there for a few weeks to assist in setting up the exhibition. More exhibition space was then found at the Museum van den Arbeid (Museum of labour), also in Amsterdam.

Marie Neurath explained that part of the motive for establishing offices outside Austria and Germany was Otto Neurath's desire to secure avenues for possible emigration, in order to continue the work begun in Vienna. His acute sensitivity to the winds of political change alerted him to the coming storm in Central Europe. 'I never saw him so depressed as in 1932' – recalled Marie Neurath late in her life – 'things could not carry on much longer in Austria, hemmed in between Mussolini and the threat of Hitler; but where to go? We had friends already in Holland.' 192

Both Neurath and Reidemeister travelled to England in 1933 to help set up a second branch of the Mundaneum in London. The existing Dutch connection proved valuable: a former colleague of Mary Fleddérus at the IRı, Brenda Voysey, was the secretary of the London branch, which had a correspondence address at the World Association for Adult Education.19³ A series of charts in English were made for exhibition in London (see pp.178–85 below). During his visit Neurath spoke with ‘secretaries of cooperatives' in Manchester and London about the potential of pictorial statistics but met with little interest.194

Connections in the USA developed from 1928, when Neurath's American cousin Waldemar Kaempffert visited the Vienna Museum in his role as director of the planned Museum of Science and Industry in Chicago. Kaempffert then advised Neurath about applying to the Rockefeller Foundation for financial support in establishing an International institution for pictographical education'. In his (unsuccessful) proposal Neurath stated:

The Museum of Sociology and Economics of Vienna has reached the conclusion that the time is ripe to extend its work internationally, to plan psycho-pedagogical experiments so that they may acquire a wider usefulness, and especially to establish an international bureau of information and communication.¹9s

In 1933, with the help of Mary van Kleeck, an 'Organizing Committee for the Institute of Visual Education' was set up to make the Neurath technique' available in the USA, which implies that Neurath briefly considered America as the most favourable destination for possible re-establishment.19⁶ For a number of reasons, the American Institute

![](images/491065f0a282f5848d7e257fa110981b0f675279f0d577e5d0236b14e1db136f.jpg)  
Cover of booklet Ten years IR/ (The Hague: International Industrial Relations Institute, 1935). 270 × 200 mm. Presumably designed by Gerd Arntz.

192. Marie Neurath, 'What I remember', p. 54. See also her recollections, '26 September 1924 and after', p. 62.

193. See Voysey, 'The Vienna Method of Pictorial Statistics', The Labour magazine (May 1933). See appendix c for more details of those involved with the London Mundaneum. Marie Neurath lists it there under 1932, but this branch was not established until 1933: an article in the New York Herald Tribune of 22 January 1933, p. 9, mentions it as being 'in process of establishment'. In 'Pictorial statistics – an international problem' (September 1933), Otto Neurath mentioned that a branch was also being established at the Ministry of Labour in Prague (p. 471). This branch was in fact never established, although some Czechlanguage charts were made (T701-15).

194. Letter Neurath to Emmy Freundlich, 1 August 1942 (öNB 1219/9). However, charts were produced by the Mundaneum for one English client at this time, the London Press Exchange.

195. English translation made in Chicago of Neurath's proposal dated 16 February 1929 (GwM-MSi).

196. Leaflet 'Pictorial statistics for the United States', shown on p. 3og below.

T532a 1931 Produced in Vienna for the Museum of Science and Industry, Chicago. (See more charts made for Chicago on p. 3o5 below.)

![](images/108665698858cd0a8b2a9a384cbdf0619b5e276a3f6ab9385cb1bac42de36505.jpg)

was never established (see chapter 7); instead an organization with almost the same name was created soon afterwards in The Hague. Mary Fleddérus helped Neurath to legally register there an 'International Foundation for the Promotion of the Vienna Method of Visual Education' in July 1933. The Foundation's statutes were carefully drafted to point out that it was an unpolitical' organization. When political conditions in Vienna became dangerous for Neurath in 1934, the Hague Foundation became the refuge for a core group of the Museum team.197

197. See Marie Neurath's description of the enforced move to the Netherlands in '26 September 1924 and after', pp. 62–3. The Foundation's name was usually shortened to International Foundation for Visual Education

198. 'The Isotype work' (iC 3.2/58), pp. 13-14 (capitalization in original). Neurath's Germanic use of verb tenses in the original typescript (e.g: 'It has been a Vienna and Austria institute’) has been corrected. The booklet Ten years IRI (1935) reported that the International Foundation for Visual Education 'took over in 1934 the function of the Vienna Mundaneum (Mundaneum Wien), It has, since, delegated its production process to another body, likewise incorporated under the Dutch law, the "Mundaneum The Hague" '. (p. 9) See also Mary van Kleeck, 'The Neurath Museum', The Survey, June 1934, p. 205.

Neurath summarized later the connections between the Museum and its related institutes:

We found out that a multiplicity of institutes has many advantages. 1934 e.g. existed three independent institutes. Each of them with particular purposes and particular contacts. GESELLSCHAFTS- UND WIRTSCHAFTSMUSEUM, supported by the Municipality, could not remain untouched by political changes in Austria. It was a Vienna and Austria institute. The Mundaneum Institute, which made the international contacts for the Gesellschafts- und Wirtschaftsmuseum could not live much longer than the Museum. But the INTERNATIONAL FOUN-DATION FOR VISUAL EDUCATION could remain wholly untouched. We had an office in Vienna for months after the end of the Museum and the Mundaneum with the DUTCH FLAG on our doorstep. The International Foundation was the legal successor of the MUNDANEUM vIENNA, which was an Austrian institute, but the authorities accepted, as we suggested, that automatically the International Foundation would become the legal heir of the Mundaneum Vienna. In this way we got the international material out of Austria and some of our collaborators came with us. 198

## Dissolution

After Austria's brief civil war in February 1934, and the subsequent seizure of power by Austrian fascists under Engelbert Dollfuss, police came to search Neurath's office at the Museum. Luckily he was in Moscow at that time, and from there he travelled via Czechoslovakia to The Hague, never to return to Vienna. Marie Neurath later recalled the prescience of her husband's preparations:

The plans that Otto had thought out proved themselves well: to rent a nearby room in Vienna, at once, to equip it with the name of our ‘Stichting' (in Dutch), and to take there everything that was essential for the continuation of our work in Holland. This had in fact been accomplished by the time that our office in Ullmannstrasse was officially sealed, with a small piece of paper.199

Some vital reference material was saved in this way – including the files of pictograms, charts and photographs produced by the Museum. (By swift action of friends in the Hague, these then survived the Nazi occupation of the Netherlands and were returned to the Isotype Institute after the Second World War.) The rest of the Museum's equipment and material in Vienna was seized and valued at 15o,ooo Austrian Schillings. The Volkshalle was cleared of exhibition material, which was subsequently destroyed in a warehouse fire.2ºº The organizing body of the Museum was reconstituted in 1936 as the Österreichisches Institut für Bildstatistik (Austrian institute for pictorial statistics), and then, after the Nazi take-over of 1938, as the Institut für Ausstellungstechnik und Bildstatistik (Institute for exhibition technique and pictorial statistics). As such it was connected with the propaganda office of the NsDAP in Vienna and even carried out some work for Goebbels's propaganda ministry in Berlin.2º1An account in a German scientific journal of 1941 hinted at the changes to the Vienna Method itself:

Since 1935 the institute (under new direction) has been attempting to achieve a greater variety, also in the style of representation, while maintaining the most important principles. Nevertheless the strictness of of the previous representation procedure has become looser in certain details.202

Differences become immediately apparent in a comparison of charts produced before and after 1934 in Vienna: the usurpers introduced folkish elements of style, such as semi-gothic script instead of Futura type and more naturalistic pictograms reminiscent of Soviet socialist realism.2º³ Neurath believed that the true Vienna Method (like true logical

201. See Franz Rauscher, 'Zerstörung und Aneignung des GwM', in Stadler (ed.), Arbeiterbildung in der Zwischenkriegszeit, pp. 360-1.

202. Hugo Keller, 'Zur Psychologie des volkstümlichen Zahlenbildes', p. 8.

203. Marie Neurath called it 'Austrian peasant realism', in 'Otto Neurath and Isotype', p.14.

empiricism) could not be used for fascist purposes. A former colleague at the Museum (evidently unaware that the Viennese successor institutes worked for the central NsDAP) once asked Neurath's opinion on why Goebbels did not make use of picture statistics; Neurath replied: 'He cannot, for our method places things out in the open; he, on the other hand, must ensure that they remain out of sight.' 204

![](images/e38890fba29ec191786dc2c8d03ce7237c9b8467021d5f2da021c26805a7f04c.jpg)

204. Heinz Umrath, 'Wissensund Freundschafts-Coop' in Stadler (ed.), Arbeiterbildung in der Zwischenkriegszeit, p. 48. Umrath (who worked briefly at the Museum in 1932) continued: 'I am still doubtful today if it really was so, although [Neurath] showed me as proof some picture statistics published by the Dollfuss regime in Austria. They were certainly hideous. But does that have to

be the case? Or was the fault in the design? And was the design doomed to failure because some where behind it there must reside an idea to really make something visible that was previously hidden?'

## Die bunte Welt (The vivid world) 1929

Die bunte Welt was the first substantial book conceived, written and designed at the Gesellschafts- und Wirtschaftsmuseum. It was also the first publication of charts made with the Vienna Method of Pictorial Statistics to feature colour extensively. The book reflects the dual nature of the Vienna Method in producing material that could be understood simultaneously by schoolchildren and by adults with little formal education. Although it is subtitled 'quantitypictures for youth' (Mengenbilder für die Jugend), the graphic qualities and complexity of its charts are equal to those made largely for adult audiences. The Museum stated: 'Its design makes it suitable principally for older children, in order to broaden their horizons and to awaken in them interest for important questions of life.'¹

Not long after the book was published, Marie Reidemeister discussed Die bunte Welt exclusively in terms of workers' education. She proposed it as a body of factual information presented in a unified way, instead of piecemeal through various lectures and courses: 'The great advantage of these pictures ... is that they are more rapidly grasped than words, that they cannot be so easily misunderstood, that they stick better in the memory, and that they awake and leave a clear impression.'²

Despite some surprising simplifications, the book's concise text deals with political and economic issues in a way that is still interesting today, even for adults. Its deceptively simple summary of world cultures moves subtly away from a Europe-centred point of view, and is driven by an inherently critical stance on Western capitalism. (cB)

Marie Neurath explained that Artur Wolf Verlag was prepared to take the financial risk of funding a colour publication. (The transformer, p. 27.) Previous publications issued by the Museum itself had been limited to monochrome leaflets (see p. 57 above). Artur Wolf also published the manifesto of the Vienna Circle in the same year.

The charts of Die bunte Welt show the Vienna Method on the cusp of maturity, just at the point when Gerd Arntz was becoming more involved in the work. Several charts reveal a lack of Arntz's clarity and precision compared with others in the book that attain an almost definitive form. For example, compare the two charts at the top of this page: Arntz's influence is clear on the left, where ethnic differences are marked by distinct articulations of a basic form; the pictograms on the right are more complex and prone to clutter.
