# 2 The graphic formation of Isotype 1925-40

Robin Kinross

This discussion attempts to show the development of the graphic approach to the Vienna Method and Isotype.¹ The examples considered have been drawn only from the years between 1925 and 1940 and, principally, from the first five of these years. It is in these first years that the most intense development took place. By 193o one may consider the work as having attained maturity. And certainly when, around 1935, the name Isotype was devised, it could refer to a coherent approach to ordering material in graphic form. The work continued to develop after 1935, of course. The discussion of configuration here shows that the work of the later 193os was marked by notable extensions in this aspect of its approach. An examination of the work done in England after 1945 would show further extensions (see chapters 10 and 11). But these are extensions of, rather than departures from, a way of working that had been established. And it is the formation of this typical approach to ordering material that is examined in this chapter.

After a discussion of issues that refer generally to this body of work, particular examples are discussed. In the cases of the charts on births and deaths and the charts on unemployment, every known example (from the years 1925 to 194o) of a chart on the subject is reproduced and discussed. In the cases of the other examples discussed, a selection has been made; examples that seem to show best the formation of the graphic approach have been chosen.

of it, I referred to 'the graphic language' of Isotype. This was a notion of the time and context in which was writing – though it would be several years before (in 1988) 'The graphic language of Neville Brody' was celebrated in London, with an exhibition and a book of that name. The claim that Isotype was a language of any kind is not one that

## Considerations in dating charts

## The T files

Illustrations in this section have been drawn principally from the set of files compiled in Vienna and in The Hague between 1929 and 1940, and known as the ‘T files' (1C 5/13-17). These contain bromide prints of photographs of charts (for the most part exhibition charts rather than charts designed for publication); each print is numbered and given the prefix ‘T', standing for ‘Tafel' (chart); the numbers of these prints have been retained here as a means of referring to the charts.²

Up to about T16o, charts are grouped by subject, and from this point the order is approximately that in which the charts were completed. The T files can thus be of help in dating charts, from the point at which the order of compilation matches the date at which the chart was completed and photographed. After 1929 it became a matter of routine to photograph charts as they were completed. The T files are thus the best index to the work discussed here, in the sense that they offer a very full picture of its development, and in that they provide a reference number for each chart that they reproduce.

## Criteria of dating

The position of a chart in the T files is a means of dating charts made after 1929. Other more general criteria of dating are:

– the evidence of the dates in the material of chronological charts; this may provide a date after which the chart was made;

– the date of publication of those charts reproduced in contemporary publications; this provides a date before which a chart was made; the chronology of graphic conventions that may be constructed from those charts that can be quite precisely dated; this chronology may then be applied to those charts whose date is otherwise unclear. There are however dangers in an unconsidered application of these criteria; the chief of these dangers will now be discussed.

2. A second set of files held in the Isotype Collection is known as the 'N files' (Ic 6/1–6), where 'N' stands for 'Natur'. These files contain photographic prints of any subject that is not a chart; they include many photographs of municipal housing in and around Vienna, and of other aspects of the work of the Gemeinde Wien. (See also pp. 249-56 below.)

## Revision of charts

The material of some charts was revised or added to. In the case of a revised version, the date suggested by the material (if it is chronological) may be later than the date suggested by the graphic conventions employed, and later than the date at which the chart was actually designed and constructed. Thus, on the evidence of the heading to its last row, one might date T5ob at 1928. But the earlier state of the chart,

T182a (left) & T182b (right) Water power of the earth C.1927 Europe | America | Rest of the world. Each waterfall = 2.5 million horsepower (lightning means exploited power). Each human figure = 100 million inhabitants.  
![](images/5af3f3797bcd72b8f2d56575c75cdff04c4b4a7dd8bfecc76f27f46597ab21bc.jpg)

![](images/066d74c237c9567af33037a5a234744fd19fa2b618cefad32cbaec28459e11c0.jpg)  
T50a (left) & T50b (right) Viennese youth relief organization C.1927 Each row signifies the totality of children at school in Vienna. Each figure = 10,000 of these children. Grey background = stay in city; green background = stay in the country.

T5oa, shows that the last row of T5ob was a later addition, and that the chart is more likely to have been designed in 1927. Such a dating accords with the evidence of the graphic conventions. But without the record of T5oa one would have to regard T5ob as a puzzling anomaly: the comparatively large quantity of verbal information and the use of explanatory pictograms in the key, for example, both suggest a date earlier than 1928. Another cautionary example is provided by T182a and T182b. Looking at the main body of T182b, one would, from the style of the pictograms and the heavy load of information presented, take it to have been made in around 1927 – certainly before the date (late 1928) which the use of Futura metal type in the title and captioning suggests. This difficulty is resolved by the record of the earlier state of the chart: T182a. The type matter on T182b was simply a later addition to a body that had been designed some time before. This case suggests one of the dangers of dating charts merely on the evidence of graphic conventions, such as the use of metal type in titles.

![](images/597b3d56e81a326350b1530d810e285f6e85aec3df7bc221faf66f3aa6e73a47.jpg)

![](images/78382289297a9a2f1cd897beb5ea792300fc0424476bd6719f2cf534e8e7c0bb.jpg)

The records of the earlier states of charts in these two examples allow us to explain the apparently anomalous revised versions. But there may be charts whose earliest states were not recorded, and whose apparently anomalous later states must remain unexplained. A more general lesson that may be drawn from these examples is that one cannot assume the process of making charts to be simple – they should not be taken ‘at face value' – and any discussion that touches on the date or process of manufacture of charts will need to be interpretative and conditional.

## Unevenness of development

Besides the physical alteration and revision of charts – which was perhaps not a usual procedure – there are other difficulties in dating charts, and particularly in the attempt to date on the evidence of style or of graphic convention. Retrospective commentary on the work will tend to make an assumption of even progression – that once a particular feature has appeared, it will appear in all possible subsequent charts - and the claim of the work to the status of 'method' or 'system' encourages this assumption. But, though there are certainly clear progressions and developments, these are not absolute: conventions may not be used where one would have expected them to be used; there are occasional inconsistencies within a single piece of work; and one may detect experiments - pictograms or configurations that appear once and which were then replaced by other versions, perhaps new or perhaps previously tried. These things will disturb only a very abstract view of the work. For each chart had its particular circumstances, being the outcome of particular sets of people confronting particular and various materials. During the years in Vienna, the composition of the working team was never stable; even in the years of comparative maturity between 193o and 1934, the composition of the team would have changed frequently, largely due to the commitment to keep five people in the ussR as supervisors to Izostat (see chapter 5). There were always part-time and occasional members in the team. In the light of this frequent variation in the composition of the team, and in the light of the external difficulties of life in Vienna at that time, the consistency that was achieved was remarkable. After the upheaval of 1934 and the move to The Hague, the team became smaller and more stable and, in this respect, conditions for ‘even development' were more favourable than in the Vienna years.

## Reuse of charts

Another kind of evidence that may be adduced for dating is the ascription of charts to exhibitions whose date is known. For example, the large exhibitions to which the Museum contributed and which were marked by special issues of the Österreichische Gemeinde-Zeitung: the 'Gesolei' exhibition (1926) and 'Wien und die Wiener' (1927). Charts may be taken to have been exhibited at these exhibitions on the evidence of their reproduction in the accompanying publication, or of some verbal description there, or for reasons of visual similarity with charts that can be ascribed to the exhibition with certainty, or for reasons of thematic consistency with the subject of the exhibition. But even if such evidence suggests that a chart was shown at a known occasion, it may yet have been made some significant time before that occasion. Charts were specially commissioned and made for exhibitions, but charts were also shown that had been made for some previous occasion. This was certainly a practice of the formative years; the photographs of the Linz exhibition of 1929, for example, show charts that are by no means a stylistically united set. Certainly too, the permanent exhibition areas in Vienna were largely supplied with charts that had been made for other, specific occasions; this was the basis on which the Museum had been founded (see appendix A). And one may notice that Neurath's articles were often illustrated with charts that had been made some time before the date of publication; occasionally the lapse of time is a matter of years – a long time in a period of rapid development.

As well as the assumption of an even development of graphic conventions and of style, retrospective examination may tend to assume in the minds of the producers an attitude that continually discards work that has been stylistically superseded. This was clearly not the attitude of the makers of this work, and it would not have been consistent with Neurath's characteristic approach of using the materials that were to hand (rather than waiting for perfect circumstances to arrive). Charts continued in use – in exhibitions, or reproduced in printed form, or as slides – for some time after the point at which they had been stylistically superseded. A commentary on the graphic development of this work may need to remind itself that a chart can be stylistically surpassed and yet still educationally useful.

## The evidence of contemporary comment

The general commentary that follows draws principally on two kinds of evidence: the work itself and Neurath's written comments on the work. It is necessary to consider the discussion of graphic method and the descriptions of charts in Neurath's writings because this can provide evidence that cannot be conveyed by the reproductions through which we largely know this work. It is only from such descriptions that we know anything of the colour or size of the early exhibition charts. Neurath's comments also illuminate those aspects of the work that do survive in reproduction; and, besides this, his discussion of graphic method allows us to see what was significant in the work in the mind of its chief architect.

## The guideline notes

Neurath's published comments on graphic method are, however, not extensive. As will be explained, it did not seem necessary to him to discuss publicly the details of the processes of designing and constructing charts. The most detailed discussion of this dimension of the work is a set of notes, each on a particular aspect of graphic method and each typed on a separate sheet of paper; they are called here the 'guideline notes'.³

It seems likely that these notes arose from discussions of the core members of the Museum's team (Neurath, Reidemeister, Arntz) and that they were a record of current practice.4 Though it is tempting to see these notes as something more than this – as guidelines for all those working in the Museum – one must say that they do not have the formality of expression or physical form that would have accompanied a set of notes with that purpose. There is no evidence (of a material kind, or in the recollections of those who worked in the team) to suggest that instructions were ever communicated through a set of guidelines.

3. The notes are in a file entitled 'Grundsätzliches zur Methode' (Principles of the Method; Ic 3.1/4); this is one of a set of files of research material that comprises the bulk of the material in the Isotype Collection that survives from the years before 1934.

4. This was suggested to the author by Marie Neurath, who did not remember seeing or using these notes at the time at which they must have been composed.

5. In Neurath's article 'Bildhafte Pädagogik im Gesellschafts- und Wirtschaftsmuseum in Wien' (1931; p. 20o), the caption to a reproduction of a chart from Gesellschaft und Wirtschaft states that the 'Vorlage' (artwork) measured 94.5× 63 cm, which was reproduced at 'half linear size' in the publication.

Certain features of these notes allow a quite precise estimate of their date of composition; one may assign them to the time of the preparation of Gesellschaft und Wirtschaft, in the latter part of 1929 or early in 193o. The clearest evidence for this dating is this passage in the note on format (given in full in the next section): 'taking into account the pre-printed map charts, which measure $4 0 \times 8 0$ , the basic element of 63 × 94.5 was arrived at'. It was only by this time, for this publication, that pre-printed maps were used for charts; also the charts for Gesellschaft und Wirtschaft were made to a size of $6 3 \times 9 4 . 5 \mathrm { c m } .$ Elsewhere in the notes there are references to conventions of the work of this period; these will be pointed out in the commentary that follows.

From the detailed examination of the work conducted in this chapter, it seems clear that only by about 1929 or 1930 could notes of this kind have been written. Only by this time had the work achieved a consistency of practice that could be described in these terms. But the notes remain guidelines, not rules. It seems significant, and indicative of the exploratory and self-critical character of the work, that among these notes there are some that are designated as 'questions'. These suggest practices that seem possible, and which are raised for consideration and testing. The guideline notes, then, make explicit some of the conventions of graphic method in the period of first maturity, and are thus an important source of supplementary evidence for any commentary on the development of the work.

## The size of charts

The dimensions of the Museum's exhibition charts seem to have been standardized from the start; Neurath refers to their 'unified dimensions' in his first article on the work of the Museum, in 1925. The maximum vertical dimension of charts was at first fixed at 128 cm. In a discussion of the charts shown at the Gesolei' exhibition of 1926, Neurath refers to this as the dimension of the square charts.7 One may assume from later discussion of chart size by Neurath that the dimension of 128 cm was chosen primarily in order to obtain a size that was convenient for the viewer – to allow a perception of the chart untroubled by the necessity for movement of the head or other parts of the body. The unit of 128 cm was later changed to 126 cm, to obtain a dimension that was easily divisible by three as well as by two; and, as it happened, this had a further advantage of approximating to 4 ft. All references to the measurements of the Museum's charts, subsequent to the reference of 1926 referred to, imply a basic unit of 126 cm.

The principles of the sizing of the Museum's charts were explained by Neurath most fully in 1933:

Every effort has been made to choose the dimensions of the standard chart so that, when the eye of the viewer is level with the centre point, the chart will still allow room for a table or glass case to complement it. Thus the bottom edge of the chart should generally be placed about 9o cm above the floor level. This produces, as the basic measure for the charts, a 126 cm square. The square is visually especially suitable, and is used, where possible, in the exhibitions of the Museum and the Mundaneum. If smaller charts are needed, they are derived from these squares. The derivation is a visual one. This would not be the case if the basic unit was a square metre.

6. See appendix A, p. 524 below. Neurath's lectures in Saxony of 1919, explaining his proposals for a planned economy, contain strong arguments for standardizing objects of all kinds, especially in his context of post-war scarcity (see Die Sozialisierung Sachsens, Chemnitz: Verlag des Arbeiter- und Soldatenrats im Industriebezirk Chemnitz, 1919). To standardize the components of the Museum would have been quite natural to his way of thinking and acting.

7. 'Statistische Bildertafeln zur Sozialversicherung' (1926), p. 74.

8. Bildstatistik nach Wiener Methode in der Schule (1933) pp. 293-4. These principles were outlined again by Neurath in International picture language (1936), p.72.

![](images/d4fd33e3f83e966cede45f1b19f9b0d52831f85aa6afce65bd2c7da08ab8f7b6.jpg)

The diagram shows the range of sizes in which the Museum's charts were made. Examples of charts in several of these sizes exist in the Isotype Collection and at the International Institute of Social History (IisH); and the guideline note on format, or other contemporary written references, describe most of these sizes. All but two of the sizes on this diagram are thus covered: the 63 × 126 cm and the 63 × 63 cm sizes;

these are recollected by Marie Neurath. The 63 × 63 cm size seems likely to have been used particularly for those charts designed specifically to be reproduced as lantern slides. Such charts were often made in black and white, thus achieving better contrast on a black-and-white negative than would have been possible with a coloured chart; the lantern slides were then coloured by hand.

![](images/a40b120cfc39e81b752c118451808ea50542ee1028d8c6dc7f28915e4d75868d.jpg)

Illustration from International picture language (1936) showing the principle of fitting together different sizes from the modular system. This also illustrates Neurath's point (made in the quotation on p. 113 above) about positioning the centre of the basic module at the height of the viewer's eyes, while allowing room for a display case underneath the charts.

The guideline notes on format (Format’) and type/image area ('Spiegel’) add further details:

## Format:

taking into account the pre-printed map charts, which measure $4 0 \times 8 0$ the basic element of $6 3 \times 9 4 . 5$ was arrived at; this includes the border round the type/image area (6 cm on average)

Standard sizes: 126 cm square. The smaller formats are derivations from this: $^ { 1 } / _ { 2 } = 6 3 , ^ { 1 } / _ { 3 } = 4 2 , ^ { 2 } / _ { 3 } = 8 4 , ^ { 3 } / _ { 4 } = 9 4 { \cdot } 5 ,$ and so on. These designations all take frames into account. The frames always have a width of 2 cm and cover 14 mm of the edge of the chart.

Type/image area:

$$
\mathrm { i n } 6 _ { 3 } \times 8 4 : 4 8 \times 7 0
$$

$$
\mathrm { i n } 6 3 \times 9 4 { \cdot } 5 : 4 8 \times 8 0
$$

$$
\mathrm { i n } 6 3 \times 1 2 6 : 4 8 \times 1 1 0
$$

In exceptional cases the type/image area may, in so far as this is aesthetically reasonable, be overridden. It can also be reduced – if the physiognomy of the chart compels this.

As already mentioned, the basic element of $6 3 \times 9 4 . 5 \times$ referred to in the note on format, was the size of the charts made as exhibition charts and as camera copy for Gesellschaft und Wirtschaft; lengths were reduced by half in reproduction. Charts consisted of a wooden base on to which a sheet of cartridge paper was pasted; the paper that was used for the elements that were then pasted on to this background sheet (the printed pictograms and pulls from metal type, after 1928) was not cartridge, but much thinner, so that the edges of the added paper elements should not cast shadows. The possible source of disturbance to the viewer of the second paper surface would have been countered further by the glass sheet (held in place by a wooden frame) that covered every chart. The base of the charts was at first made from Eternit (a hard artificial material in use in the building trade); however, this proved to be too heavy and too fragile, and plywood was then used instead. Gerd Arntz (who came to the Museum in September 1928) remembers only the use of plywood for charts and recalls the basic measure as being 126 cm (rather than the previous 128 cm). Arntz suggests also that the largest size in which sheets of plywood could be obtained was $1 2 6 \times$ 189 cm. This would imply that, as well as being a suitable dimension for viewers, the basic measure of 126 cm was a means to making charts that would leave no wastage from the sheets in which the wood was supplied.

In addition to these standard sizes, it should be mentioned that at least one chart, and probably others, was made to a size of 252 × 252 cm. The size of 126 × 189 cm seems to have been used only exceptionally. Another, later, housing map was made in this size (T83d, below).

![](images/c5ad59afa9db427ee8300bbaf4f81b8115408e818abb9c58f574ef5e1eba3460.jpg)  
This was among the material given in 1932 to the Nederlandsch Economisch-Historisch Archief, now held at the IISH.

## T83d The home building programme of the Vienna municipality C.1931

Dwellings built up till the end of 1930.

Dark blocks = 5oo dwellings in housing estates;

In the original, the background is green, the numbered districts yellow, the river blue, and the roofs of houses red.

![](images/6f4742273f16eac7613cc7cd268973d7b3934527699134aa0c35a4f134a87fa8.jpg)  
T83f Detail of this threedimensional chart.

9. This was the map of housing in Vienna that is referred to by Marie Neurath in her published account of the Museum: 'Gesellschafts- und Wirtschaftsmuseum in Wien en Mundaneum', p. 207.

12. 'Statistische Hieroglyphen' (1926), pp. 43-9.

## The graphic and typographic elements of charts

## Colour and form

The original charts of the Museum that survive date from around 1933, and they show a standard range of colours (see pp. 178–84 below). But in the earliest years colours were not strictly standardized, nor were the limits placed on the application of colours as clear as they were to become. This is known from the personal testimony of Marie Neurath and from certain remarks in Otto Neurath's writings. For example, commenting on charts made for the Gesolei' exhibition of 1926, Neurath referred to these colours: red (used for men), a lighter red (for women), blue, orange, green, grey, violet, brown.1º There is no attempt yet to specify a limited number of colours. Limitation to, perhaps, eight standard colours is evident in Gesellschaft und Wirtschaft (193o) – though not in the cartographic charts, which show a more lavish use of colour. In 1936, however, Neurath wrote: ‘There are only seven colours for use in Isotype pictures: white, blue, green, yellow, red, brown, black; and only some of them may have a further division (as into: light blue, dark blue, light green, dark green, light red, dark red, light brown, dark brown), or be mixed with others, as white and black to make grey, yellow and red to make orange etc.'11 These seven, with the addition of a pure grey (not a toned black) were the colours that were used in Modern man in the making (1939).

A feature of the early charts, and something that was discussed by Neurath in his writings of the first years, was the use of background colour to carry meaning. This device was employed extensively on the ‘Gesolei' exhibition charts.1² In an article published shortly after that exhibition, Neurath remarked that: 'the membership of the different groups of the insured, as well as the fact of the uninsured people, is expressed merely through background colour' (see images overleaf). And he goes on to refer to the disadvantages of this device:

In future the Museum will try to make this kind of thing more directly understandable, through alteration of the human figures or through appropriate symbols. It is not sufficient to denote land workers insured against illness through a green background, when perhaps the sickle could be introduced as a symbol for agriculture. It is certainly very hard to design the characterizing marks to be as schematic and as simple as possible. To start with, we must he satisfied with striving for simpler forms.

From this point (autumn 1926) therefore it was a matter of principle that charts should not rely on colour to convey meaning. Colours were

13. 'Statistische Bildertafeln zur Sozialversicherung' (1926), p. 74.

![](images/afa564765e4434ec836f689aef20b26465716b8cc1cd4084630355fe6d9dee4e.jpg)  
T19a Wage-earners with health insurance 1926 Both charts made for the 'Gesolei' exhibition.

![](images/7746f962f958943197934f46a7a3c8664f161267b7e025ba338e54c720fa36e5.jpg)  
T19b Wage-earners with accident insurance 1926 Each figure = 250,000 people.

(Above)

In T19b the attempt is made to convey with black alone the colour background distinctions in T19a.

assigned to pictograms so as to conform, where possible, to any associations that might attach to what the pictogram denoted – the skin colour of a racial group, green for agriculture, and so on.¹ª

The guideline notes mention practices that are not discussed by Neurath in his published writings:

As few colours as is possible on a chart: in which case, black is always used for the worse thing, red always for the better. Grey is used for what is not determined. Yellow is to be avoided on pictograms, because it is too weak.

There are two guideline notes on the colour grey:

Grey:

has yellow mixed in with it, in pictograms and as the background colour on maps; with this it achieves a certain lightness.

Grey in guide-pictures [Führungsbilder] and grey to denote the undetermined: the grey that is used within the main body of the chart for what is not determined [Restgrau] and the grey of the guide-pictures [Führungsgrau] must be a single colour – though the Restgrau is kept darker than the Führungsgrau.

14.\_International picture language (1936), p. 48.

This last note seęms to relate to the need to give each pictogram on a chart an equal weight. Grey pictograms, like yellow ones, run the risk of being too weak; a darker grey would help to give them a weight comparable to that of pictograms in other colours. But a retiring, unemphatic grey would be appropriate to the guide-pictures and to their secondary function within the whole chart (see pp. 124–6 below).

## Male and female

The differentiation of male and female through the use of darker and lighter coloured pictograms is one of the principles explained in the early chart on method (p.146 below). And, in support of the convention, in an article of 1927, Neurath refers to this differentiation as a convention of Egyptian wall paintings.¹⁵ The practice seems, however, to have been dropped from the Museum's charts soon after this time: Marie Neurath remembers Otto Neurath's reservations about showing women as ‘weaker' than men – this offended his feminist convictions.

There is evidence of one early (1926) chart showing women as red and men as blue – following the popular convention of 'blue for boys, pink for girls' – though this was not a settled convention: it does not appear in later charts (for example, those reproduced in colour). And in the first (1925) charts on clothes makers, women had apparently been shown as blue and men as red.

From about 1928, male and female were always signified through differences in the form of the pictograms, whether or not any extra differentiation of colour was used. The move from symbolizing through colour to symbolizing through form was, it becomes clear, a general tendency in the work of the first years. The differentiation of gender through form is the subject of one of the guideline notes:

## Masculine-feminine:

the visible, outer differentiation of male and female, which occurs in all manifestations of nature, must also be maintained in the pictograms. In black and white, the rectangular forms of the male and the rounded forms of the female will be stressed. Through this, the imagination of the viewer receives considerable support – though this remains an unconscious process.

The development of the ways in which the gender of pictograms was shown may be seen in the charts on clothes makers and in those on the membership of free trade unions. (See pp. 169–7o below.)

## Flags

At various points in his writings, Neurath remarks on the use of flags to denote nationality. For example, in an article of 1926, he discusses the signification of the nationality of people through their clothing; but, as he points out, national costume is no longer worn in modern countries, and so he mentions flags as a possible device.1⁶ Flags to denote nationality were used on certain charts; those reproduced overleaf may serve to illustrate some of the uses of the device; and flags are also a feature

15. 'Bildliche Darstellung gesellschaftliche Tatbestände' (1927), p. 120.   
16. 'Statistische Hieroglyphen' (1926), p. 43.

Each oil can = ½ million tonnes of crude oil.  
T176j China in Asia c.1929 The flag areas correspond to the area of states. Each inscribed square = 500,00o km².  
T238b Merchant navies of the world 1930 Gesellschaft und Wirtschaft, 1930, no.55. Each ship represents 5 million gross register tons (1oo cubic feet of a vessel's interior volume).

![](images/23e8f4a1fdb8ace7cc0519f76670f9c53f9ccc18a603ee3a1a94a5efdd90d431.jpg)

of the series of charts on world powers. In some charts flags are altered from their conventional proportions - either enlarged or reduced – to correspond to variations in quantity. Although the flags themselves do not show quantity – this is done by repeated pictograms – the distortion is disturbing, particularly where diagonal lines are affected. The first rule' of the work (showing quantity through repeated rather than enlarged units) is not broken here, yet there is some contradiction of an underlying principle of fidelity to material.

The guideline note on flags seems to recognize this:

Flags:

are used for subdivision; they cover the extent of the area allotted to different countries. Then the use of flags influences the colour of the pictograms – differentiation of which further accomplishes the subdivision. Unless special circumstances suggest a different treatment, flags are employed in their proper proportions.

## Writing in 1933, Neurath raised another difficulty:

In some cases flags are appropriate for denoting nationality. But, visually, serious doubts can be raised against them. If, for example, one fills an area with the blue-white-red of the tricolour, one recognizes very easily that it is French', but one retains in one's memory [only] the image of the three stripes: advantages and disadvantages that must be weighed against one another.¹7

The possibility of using flags and national colours derived from flags is mentioned by Neurath in International picture language (p.5o). By this time (1936) however, the device is not much in evidence in the work: nationality is now explained verbally in labels, supported where possible by colour.

## Qualifying symbols

The need to group pictograms within a chart and to show subdivisions within groups of pictograms led to the use of secondary symbols qualifying the principal pictograms. As has been discussed in the section on 'Colour and form', the function of qualifying pictograms had at first been performed by colour – in the pictogram itself or as background. The charts reproduced in this section show the development of qualifying devices. Two kinds of qualifying symbol have been distinguished, and the development of each is shown through two sets of comparable charts.

The charts T64f, T175b, and T247i show the development of the means used to group related pictograms in a chart. T64f uses, as well as verbal headings, backgrounds composed of symbols signifying agriculture', 'industry', 'trade and commerce', to distinguish these three groups of the principal pictograms. These qualifying symbols can be seen clearly in the copy of the chart as it was published (Entwicklung von Landwirtschaft und Gewerbe in Deutschland, 1928, p. 13). The chart was drawn for black-and-white reproduction in this publication (see overleaf).

17. Bildstatistik nach Wiener Methode in der Schule (1933), p.283.

![](images/6b8cac19811e68341a0d466849591158f2b1a9b120dc904397b45fbad22fbcd7.jpg)  
T64f Social subdivision of those commercially active in Germany

Entwicklung von Landwirtschaft und Gewerbe in Deutschland, 1928.

![](images/e9a0c15ee0bf43ff4e30d84211eebf18618a822c87fd8b469e1ccd40d1e06c14.jpg)  
T175b (Later version of T64f) Each figure = ½ million workers (both charts).

![](images/d42ee7eeb502f9842d746f9473d280d1ec66d730072645ed367a453af35558fe.jpg)  
T247i Commercially active people around 1920, ordered by economic groups Gesellschaft und Wirtschaft, 193o, no.76.

![](images/187079b4fd788a0e5c25623e328f49f8196c6b8c49198d7d9c3a12eb74d10fe0.jpg)  
T14b Apprentice protection agency of the Vienna chamber of workers 1926 Male and female apprentices. Each rectangle = 100 cases of apprentice protection.

T175b is a later version of the material of T64f, made as an exhibition chart. The distinction of the three economic sectors is now made by single background symbols; the symbol for agriculture is a plough, replacing the grass of T64f. The use of large background symbols appears in a few charts made at this time – late 1928 or early 1929 (see also T314c; p. 173 below). It was evidently an experiment and one that was soon rejected; Gerd Arntz remembers arguing against it (it seems to have been introduced shortly before he joined the team). No further versions of this material appear to have been made, but a comparable chart, T247i, shows what came to be the conventional way of making this kind of distinction. T247i was made for Gesellschaft und Wirtschaft (1930, no. 76). The advantages of these qualifying symbols over those of T175b seem clear: they do not disturb perception of the principal pictograms; they are of a uniform size and are thus more open to standardization, whereas the large background symbols must be specially manufactured for each use; the background symbols, though they do not show quantities, are nevertheless incidentally magnified in rough proportion to quantity – and thus contradict the principle of repeating pictograms.

The charts T14b, T74i, and T74ii show the development of the second kind of qualifying symbol. These are symbols that serve to make distinctions within a group of pictograms. In T14b (probably shown at the Gesolei' exhibition of 1926) the qualifying symbols are in apposition to the principal pictograms. T74i shows material that is different from but comparable with that of T14b. It was probably shown in Berlin-Kreuzberg, and thus would date from 1929. Qualifying symbols are now incorporated' within the pictograms. T74ii is a later version of the material of T74i, and may date from 1931. It shows developments in the forms both of the pictograms and of the qualifying symbols. (The configuration of pictograms has also been revised and, it seems clear, improved.)

![](images/eac9caa1fc2769a1f22b28b47e0aaefb05a315b6af0e4e338f2a68e7c053c56f.jpg)  
T74i Professions of Berlin's commercially active C.1929 T74ii (right; later version of T74i) c.1931 Each figure = 100,o00 workers (both charts)

![](images/2da8ec5680014fc0e616b5e46897d484e8d43b65be24e9a63bf07d02cd23525f.jpg)  
Jede Figur 100000 Erwerbstälige

The progression of this second set of charts, and of the charts on world trade union membership (p.167 below), suggest that the device of incorporating the qualifying symbol was devised in 1929, some months after Arntz joined the team. It would seem likely that only after he had begun to affect, quite fundamentally, the typical forms of the symbols, could the possibility of combining them by incorporation suggest itself. Before Arntz joined the team, symbols did not have the necessary modular quality for incorporation, and they were neither physically stable (existing only as paper cut-outs) nor formally stable (varying according to who had drawn them). Whether or not Arntz himself proposed the device of incorporation, it was certainly as a consequence of his talent for drawing modular symbols that incorporation became the solution to the problem of how to qualify the principal, human pictogram.

Incorporation became the conventional way of distinguishing subcategories within a group of people, and it features in the exposition of method in International picture language (1936, p.52)

## Guide-pictures [Führungsbilder]

The image standing outside, usually above or to the left of, the main body of the chart, was termed the Führungsbild' (translated as 'guidepicture' in International picture language). The guide-picture was seen by Neurath as having two functions: to give some indication of the subject matter of the chart, and to make apparent any differences or divisions in the content.18 In this second respect, the guide-picture performs much the same function as what has been identified in the preceding section as the first kind of qualifying symbol – those secondary symbols that serve to group or to associate pictograms. And when, in the mature work, these qualifying symbols are moved out of the area of the pictograms – as in T247i – they might be regarded as guide-pictures. But they remain individual symbols, rather than pictures' or collections of symbols, and so are not considered here to be guide-pictures proper.

18. Bildstatistik nach Wiener Methode in der Schule (1933), p.282.

19. 'Darstellungsmethoden des Gesellschafts- und Wirtschaftsmuseums' (1925), p. 22.

20. 'Gesellschaft und Wirtschaft im Lehrbild' (1927), p. 94.

The guide-pictures exhibit the same kind of development as the pictograms of a chart (discussed in the next section). Neurath refers to 'pictures that will perhaps indicate the content of the columns or of the whole presentation' in an article of 1925;¹º though in the charts of the first two years proper guide-pictures can hardly be said to appear. Neurath first uses the term in an article of 1927, referring to these devices as means to making charts less dependent on verbal explanations in titles and captions.2º This corresponds to the first function for the device that Neurath later identified – to give an indication of subject matter.

Of the charts reproduced here, T5oa, which must date from 1927, is one of the first charts in the work to show a guide-picture that meets the terms of the definition of functions: it acts as a pictorial heading to the body of the chart and it helps to differentiate content (between the broad categories of those children remaining in Vienna and those for whom some provision has been made outside the city).

![](images/8e7f214c2262f205198ba0a5d04530051b0591735faf49bbf433d5d0afe6b1a6.jpg)

T222c shows a mature guide-picture, and one of a kind that became a conventional way of ascribing items in a chart to regions of the earth In this chart and, more clearly, in T918, the burden of differentiation is carried out by the guide-picture with the support of captions, whereas in the earlier chart, T5oa, it is the pictograms themselves that make the differentiation. T918 is a revised version (dating from, say, early 1933) of a chart published in Gesellschaft und Wirtschaft (no.56) – T237a (p. 211 below).

![](images/e736578e7a493e79ad2185292aaffd765fe4c72e0b2691929220bc2fb09a391b.jpg)

Kraftwagenbestand der Erde  
![](images/2f145b6a725751165ebb8081c6c581fc69cf326c65153c60b35cddd41bcdcc75.jpg)  
T50a Viennese youth relief organization C.1927 See larger reproduction and explanation on p.109 above.  
T222c Development of coal and oil production since 1870

Americas | Europe & rest. Each black briquette = 50 million tonnes anthracite; each brown briquette = 50 million tonnes lignite; each green oil can = 50 million tonnes oil.

T918 Number of automobiles in the world 1933 uSA | Rest of the world. Each car = 2.5 million automobiles.

The extension of the guide-picture in T918 to show 'the rest of the world', rather than, as in T237a, leave this implied by not showing anything, is significant of the development of the device.21 The tendency is to let the guide-picture take over from the other elements the function of making distinctions in the body of the chart. This was clearly present as a conscious issue in Neurath's mind, for it is raised in the group of guideline notes that are designated as questions':

## Quantities and classes:

differentiated merely through the guide-picture?

Looking at the body of work, the answer that suggests itself is ‘yes, as much as possible'. But clearly the guide-picture could never differentiate totally: captions would always be necessary, and differentiation through tone or colour was also maintained.

Two other guideline notes concern guide-pictures:

Standardization?:

The guide-picture cannot be standardized in the same way as the pictogram; for, in many cases, it would then be incomprehensible. The degree of simplification must accordingly be less than that of a pictogram. Nevertheless, only as much is shown in a guide-picture as is absolutely necessary to the statement in hand.

## A lexicon of guide-pictures:

This must develop, at least with time, just as with pictograms. For imports and exports, it can be fixed: a ship (or train) arriving, departing; characterized by position and smoke direction. For production: a factory.

## Design of pictograms

Stages in the development towards the mature pictograms may be identified as follows.

21. A report on the use of pictorial statistics in a Viennese school stated that, while chart no. 56 from Gesellschaft und Wirtschaft proved engaging to the children, the distinction between America and the rest of the worid eluded them until it was pointed out. Perhaps this was the reason for the elaboration of the guide-picture in the later version. 'Bericht über die Verwendung der Bildstatistik an der Knabeshauptschule Schweglerstrasse', IC 3.1/5, p. 3. The typescript bears the name 'Pointner' (its author, perhaps) written in Marie Neurath's hand.

1925: pictograms are drawn in ink; some are cut from paper; a comparatively realistic approach predominates.

1926–8: pictograms are cut from paper and painted; some are drawn in ink on occasions, for black-and-white reproduction; a more abstract style is evident, but unevenly so. Certain phases, distinguished by particular modes of pictogram design, may be noted within these years: pictograms that seem to manifest their scissor-cut origin (T5c, T5g); a tendency in some charts of 1928 to encase pictograms in rectangular elements (T5d, T5h) – this seems to have been an attempt to solve the problem of inequalities of weight and the consequent obstacle to

comparison of pictograms. Also, in at least two charts of this period, a human pictogram with a perfectly circular head was used; this device was explained in the early chart on method, and it is discussed in the commentary on that chart (see pp. 146–7 below)

![](images/dced5c21a14f5a1c8770dbe8faedfae57c29bb37606b02278ec8c4d8ae856392.jpg)  
T5c Development of free trade unions in Austria Organized | unorganized.

![](images/ddf8f7f0b053adbcef708c5e7ee038dd0c5fa14e2f362d3a0d999c06f37ede71.jpg)

## T5g Social subdivison of men and women in Vienna

Men: employed; self-employed; unemployed.   
Women: employed; self-employed; unemployed;   
housewives.

![](images/505f7576bef02de2496f3278a3788ca82f9c9574f7f0b92c61b288017ecfd22a.jpg)

## T5d Strikes and lockouts in Germany

Political strikes | economic strikes and lockouts.   
Each pictogram = 5 million lost work days.

All charts on this page published in Die Gewerkschaften, 1928.

![](images/1f0ebe29ad2f0639ecffeb9078a60778ecc14c4954f20621a2300ec26e39bb63.jpg)  
T5h Work intensity and the eight-hour day Each clock = 1 working hour;

each cigar = 25 cigars produced (tobacco industry);   
each armature = 11 coiled armatures (electrical industry);   
each loaf of bread = 4o loaves produced (baking).

From late 1928: pictograms are cut in lino and printed; they are characterized by a formal simplicity; but also forms that are subtle and not rigidly geometric now develop; pictograms now become naturally modular, thus leading to other devices such as incorporation of qualifying symbols and 'marking-off'. This breakthrough was a consequence of Gerd Arntz joining the team as chief artist; his influence may be detected even before he came to Vienna in September 1928; and, equally, the styles of other artists can also be discerned in charts made some time after he joined the team; but, by late 1929 or early 193o one may speak of the pictograms as having attained maturity.

The guideline notes contain a much more detailed consideration of details of pictogram design than appears in any of Neurath's published writing. In Bildstatistik nach Wiener Methode in der Schule (1933), Neurath's first extended exposition of the work, he explained his decision to pass over the work of artists and technicians by saying that 'this very important part of the production process is however not necessary to an understanding of the pedagogical dimension of the Vienna Method'.²² That Neurath should not have discussed pictogram design in his published writings is consistent with his conception of the work as only open to generalized explanation to a limited extent. The guideline notes, dating as they seem to do from the period of work on Gesellschaft und Wirtschaft (1929–30), refer to the practices of the start of what is designated here the mature period’:

## Schematization:

comes partly from the repeated use of already available elements Detailing [Nuancierung] is thereby discarded – the pictogram attains a certain austerity; but this is the means by which it maintains some distance from the naturalistic and, to a corresponding degree, achieves an abstract character.

## Charakter:

the pictogram may not denote more than is necessary to the statement of facts for which it is chosen.²³

22. Bildstatistik nach Wiener Methode in der Schule (1933), p. 291.

23. This would seem to repeat the point made by Neurath in his discussion of the pictograms showing arrests of people under the influence of alcohol: the detail of these symbols 'says more than one knows'. (See p. 50o below.)

## Signatur – Symbol:

A heap of planks is not a symbol for wood. A cog-wheel is a very good symbol for industry. The crown for a monarchy, the Jacobin cap for a republic, crossed hammers for mining – these are entirely satisfactory symbols. Not only does a partial manifestation stand for a whole phenomenon, but, just because it no longer exists as a practical object (or only in modified form), it has power to stimulate the imagination, particularly towards the sphere of the intended whole phenomenon [Gesamtgebiet]. There are of course problems here that are hardly soluble.

Chemical substances or, as mentioned above, wood, belong to such areas of difficulty – their nature can be made visually clear only with great difficulty.²4

Equality of weight [Gleichgewichtigkeit]:

the individual pictograms should aim, at least within a single chart, to cover the same area – so that each shows the same area of colour. If there is too much white in a pictogram (for example in that of a car), a new drawing must be made.

In International picture language Neurath touches on this question and again refers to the automobile pictogram – but points to the case of its possible use on a road sign, where inequality of weight would be desirable:

It is important to have the signs for ‘automobile front' and ‘automobile back' very different from one another, because only in this way will the direction of the automobile be clearly seen at the first look. The automobile has been made as dark as possible, the front much lighter.2

![](images/6be66cdde0aa4dd1e8a1114a905a6f0e21417ff4df92f0ba7a597813b69a2774.jpg)  
From International picture language (1936). See p. 512 below for a reproduction of the whole page from the book.

The remaining guideline notes that are designated by the heading 'Sign.[atur]' will be given without comment; the question of showing fractions of pictograms forms the subject of the next section.

The ability to halve pictograms:

will certainly be taken into account in the drawing. The use of asymmetrical profiles will be limited to the unavoidable.

Pictograms orientated on the diagonal:

to be avoided if possible. They disturb the rhythm of the whole, appearing to have a different weight, and are not open to halving.

Perspective in pictograms:

only if two dimensions are insufficient for recognition.

Complete characterization already in contour [Kontur]:

of course – for otherwise a black-and-white reproduction would not be possible.

After-images at corners:

black, or similarly dark-coloured, full rectangles or squares (the coal pictogram for example) whose corners are cut away, produce in certain arrangements the effect of after-images. The dark spills over into the white ground, so that the spaces between adjacent corners appear grey. To be avoided by bevelling the corners as little as possible.

Radii of a circle:

are not drawn right to the centre point, avoiding the disturbing effect produced by the accumulation of lines.

24. Underlined in typewritten original. It is not clear whether Neurath intends, in the heading to this note, any difference of meaning between the terms 'Signatur' and 'Symbol'. Although he uses only 'Signatur' in the body of the note itself, he refers principally here to qualifying symbols, which stand for a broader concept – 'monarchy or 'mining', for example – and are used in combination with pictograms. The German term 'Signatur' is generally translated in this book as 'pictogram', a sign which directly depicts the object it represents.

25. International picture language (1936), p. 37.

Standardization [Typisierung] of the whole work:

as far as possible, use what is already available. In theory it is conceivable that one will arrive at a situation in which nothing new needs to be drawn – neither pictograms nor guide-picture. At any rate, this must be put forward as the objective – to approach this ideal situation as nearly as possible.

## Fractions

The use of fractional parts of pictograms in rows or columns does not occur in the earliest charts; this would seem to follow from the use in these charts of greater numbers of pictograms (in relation to a given number of things symbolized) than appear in later charts. Fractional pictograms begin to appear with some frequency only in 1928. Certain 'checker-board' charts show half pictograms, from their earliest versions; but the comparatively large size of these pictograms and their more illustrative function, make them different in essential respects from the pictograms that appear in rows or columns. T1a, probably dating from 1925, is an example of a checker-board' chart showing half pictograms (cut horizontally); T176a, the first of the charts on the world powers, isanother of these charts (showing vertically cut fractions; see p.164 below).

T1a Human races C.1925 Each full figure = roughly 100 million people.   
White races; dark races;   
yellow races.

![](images/4f9ed5692deeb9d984a2fc265585d04a29d3f55c47bd47e09f16479309f4909c.jpg)

![](images/c557b2af63c9a37e60406d261ce715ab73815a1da049537c36a0667beb9f8193.jpg)  
T299 Strikes and lockouts Gesellschaft und Wirtschaft, 193o, no.88. Each fist = 10 million lost work days.

The division of pictograms was normally limited to halving. Only halves are mentioned in the two guidelines that refer to fractions (‘The ability to halve pictograms' and 'Pictograms orientated on the diagonal', quoted in the preceding section). But quarter pictograms do occur occasionally, and this was acknowledged in what seems to be Neurath's only published reference to fractions: 'One should try to avoid half figures, though all pictograms should be designed so that they may be halved, and, if necessary, even quartered.'²⁶

In isolated cases one may find apparently indeterminate fractions: T5d (p.127) and T299 show examples. But these indeterminate fractions only occur singly, not in a row that includes whole pictograms; their function is to signify something, not nothing, but not as much as a quarter or half pictogram.

The differing and particular circumstances of each piece of work, the great variety of the information presented, would have helped to ensure that no absolute and inflexible rules for showing fractions (or anything else) could be practised. Some pictograms are cut horizontally, some vertically; diagonal pictograms are cut in the way that makes best sense. T8o2 shows the kind of problem that asymmetrical pictograms present: direction of halving is clearly determined by the need to leave a half that still signifies intelligibly.

From an inspection of the whole body of the work, the general principles concerning fractions would seem to be: that, if the pictogram's form does not suggest otherwise – the first consideration – then the cutting axis should correspond to reading direction; thus, horizontally

![](images/c97923b99f4dc49cc73fa08e1a936a125a84ba76660933cdcb891f3909b6fd11.jpg)

T802 People and production 1931

Average 1870–9  average of the decade after the [First World] War.

The average amounts for 187o–9 are set with a value of 1.

26. Bildstatistik nach Wiener Methode in der Schule (1933), p.285.

cut if the (primary) reading direction is vertical, or vertically cut if the pictograms are read horizontally; where pictograms are grouped in 'clumps' rather than rows, and with groups of pictograms placed on maps, the direction of reading is less significant, and cutting direction correspondingly more arbitrary.

## Juxtaposition of pictograms (direction of reading)

The two principles that came to be adopted in deciding whether pictograms should be placed on a vertical or horizontal axis were: that the conventional pattern of reading, from top left to bottom right, should be followed; that pictograms should be disposed in the way that the objects depicted usually dispose themselves in reality.²’ This meant that most simple charts concerned to compare quantities would dispose their pictograms in rows, rather than columns. There are, however, certain charts that contradict these principles – but very few, and almost all of them made in the first year of work. T3e is an example from 1925 where the coffins are unnaturally disposed; later versions change the axis to horizontal and show circular coins. The births and deaths charts provide a series of charts with unnaturally disposed objects and these, together with T41, reproduced here, are almost the only examples of this that were made after 1926.

Vertical stacking of pictograms was occasionally employed in charts that show the ‘height' of money values: T398d is concerned with pension sums, and T525b with interest on capital outlay.

## Marking-off

27. Bildstatistik nach Wiener Methode in der Schule (1933), p. 287. 28. 'Darstellungsmethoden des Gesellschafts- und Wirtschaftsmuseums' (1925), p. 22.

Neurath's desire to present ‘easily countable groups of objects’ expressed in an article of 1925, would seem to refer to what will be called here ‘marking-off’.28 This was the convention of marking pictograms off into groups of five, ten, or some other number appropriate to the unit employed. The convention may be discerned, operating not very distinctly, in some early charts, but it does not appear clearly in the work until around 1929. The appearance of the convention in that year would seem to be connected with the fact that before this time the pictograms were not sufficiently open to treatment as units: only when the pictograms were printed, easily obtainable in large quantities, and designed to have the capacity for combination in rows, would marking-off become possible. On this explanation, the convention is, with the device of incorporation, a consequence of the breakthrough in pictogram design of late 1928 and early 1929.

WELTERZEUGUNG VON WOLLE UND GESPINNSTPFLANZEN 1909/13  
![](images/9582f2b0b92cc65a51f5474429264bf0a0fcb772e0ec300ac1ad627eef1fe05c.jpg)

## T41 World production of wool and spinning fibre plants c. 1927

Cotton, wool, jute, hemp & flax; artificial silk.   
Each pictogram = ½ million tonnes production;   
half pictogram (spinning top) = 10o,ooo tonnes production.   
Backgrounds colour coded to designate national production.

## T3e Income and death from tuberculosis in Vienna 1925

Districts with: high; medium; and low income.

![](images/cccedec5e4760e0178d5d54dd50ea58893ade748851e0c7f5396a7604e0e75be.jpg)

![](images/5caf30eda8e95de17c3386f6d4c249b5e4e321a01131666de9f654201b9068ff.jpg)

![](images/6cef3ec53c0e25766789604ad9d2d94b3c4e654860e0b2e5c4ac5ebbf036ea7c.jpg)

## T525b Capital outlay demanded by rationalization 1931

Each empty circle = 12o,ooo Reichsmark of interest in and amortization of the rationalized rolling train of a mill;

each full circle = 12o,ooo Reichsmark of economy in production costs due to rationalization;

each blue symbol [parallelogram} = 12,ooo tonnes of production.

The practice of marking-off may be observed in the charts on unemployment (see pp.155–63 below).

## Titles and other non-pictorial elements

The principal feature of the development of the typographic elements of the charts is the decisive change in treatment and method of production that occurred towards the end of 1928. Before this point, lettering was produced by hand (usually with a 'Redis' pen), titles and any text-matter were placed centrally and, in the earlier charts especially, there was a tendency to make 'block layouts' (to extend or compress lines so as to produce a rectangular shape); some charts of 1928 employ calligraphic lettering. From late in 1928, lettering was printed from metal type and placed so as to range from the left.

The point at which the conventions for treating typographic elements changed is also exactly the point at which metal type (Futura) was introduced.2º But these two things seem not to have been directly connected: Gerd Arntz was responsible for determining the new typographic conventions of the charts, but not for introducing the use of lettering printed from metal type, or Futura in particular; this had been decided before he joined the team.³º

30. In conversation with the author, Arntz remembered that the Futura type had already arrived at the Museum by the time of his arrival in September 1928 but had not yet been unpacked.

29. T176j (p. 120 above) is apparently unique in showing a title set in metal type and centred

One of the typographic practices introduced by Arntz, something clearly in the style and spirit of New Typography, was the rule placed above the information (usually the year numerals) on the left of a row. This was primarily an aid to reading: to making the connection between the numerals and the items within the row. At first the rule was bold, by comparison with the weight of the numerals and often placed so as to overhang on the right (as in T6oL). This came to be modified, so that any overhang was equal on both sides, and thinner rules were used (as in T737). These are the tendencies shown by the charts on births and deaths and on unemployment; though contradictory examples may be found, for example T237a (p. 211 below) and T918 (p. 125 above). The more notable tendency is for this rule not to appear even in the decreasing number of simple row charts where it had been used. The introduction of bold numerals may account for this, but also there is evidence of growing skill in disposing elements so that connections could be made without the help of this non-signifying addition.

A number of references to typographic elements in Neurath's published writings show the considerations behind some of the modes employed in the work. For example, the early chart on method refers to the ‘Blockschrift' (capital letters) then employed for headings, as

![](images/159348a1607a752ed3b0a108f8eff5ba9f55b7167363fb955d6e2d16730f86af.jpg)  
T6OL Accidents in Carinthia 1929 Each figure = 50 accidents (fatal, serious, and minor).  
USA | Rest of the world.  
Each pictogram = 5 million tonnes of pig iron.

Roheisenproduktion der Erde USA Obnige lander   
1900 Fe   
1913   
1920 时   
1921   
1929   
1931   
Jede Signatur 5 Millionen † Roheisen

'simple and thus easily readable', though 'for longer explanatory texts, upper and lower case is used'. Given these criteria of 'simple' and 'easily readable', Neurath's choice of Futura, when acquiring metal type, was quite natural.

Titles continued to be set in capitals after the introduction of metal type. Among the earliest charts to show upper and lower case in titles were the charts made for the peace exhibition in The Hague (late 1929) and some charts published in Das Bild, January 193o. The guideline note on ‘Schrift' (lettering), translated in full below, clearly dates from after the change of mode, for it declares: 'We set everything in upper and lower case, except in the case of geographical designations on maps, which are set in capitals'. There are a few examples of charts made after 193o that use only capitals in the titles, such as those made for the Museum of Science and Industry in Chicago. This was due to the demands of Rudolf Modley, a former worker at the Gesellschafts- und Wirtschaftsmuseum who then established himself at the Chicago Museum. He rejected the use of upper and lower case in charts in English for America.³¹ The use of capitals in charts made for Chicago fits with their generally brasher character, their relatively large pictograms – by comparison with other charts of the time. (Modley provided revised layouts to Vienna and instructed that they be followed; see pp. 303-5 below.)

Charts made for the London branch of the Mundaneum also feature titles in capitals, which implies that a notion of this style as applicable to the English language was carried over from the Chicago experience (see pp. 178–85 below).

31. Correspondence between Marie Reidemeister and Modley, September 1931 (GwM-MSI). One may notice that there exist in the T files versions of the same Chicago charts that differ only in the mode in which their titles are set and in the content of their signatures. For example T534ii is signed 'Gesellschafts- und Wirtschaftsmuseum in Wien' and uses upper and lower case, while T534iïi uses capitals and is signed 'Produced for the Museum of Science and Industry Chicago by Gesellschaftsund Wirtschaftsmuseum in Wien'. The signatures suggest that T534ii was kept in Vienna and T534iii sent to Chicago; if, indeed, these are two charts and not merely one body with different titles and signatures added for each photographed version. (See images overleaf.)

![](images/907ea46fb95e6cd879be10bd37837f7f2acbbd12a83f205e2db5011be6ede934.jpg)  
Each symbol represents 600 Ibs of coal

T534ii The loss of coal is still enormous 1931 Produced by the Gesellschafts- und Wirtschaftsmuseum for the planned Museum of Science and Industry in Chicago.  
![](images/03933d88aff9d924b6de70a2cd874c0e7fbf57b7b4e0d48d4f7addc6af3a510b.jpg)  
T534iii (alternative version of T534ii)

In 1932 a bold Futura came into use in the work – in titles and else  
where. One may place its introduction in the second half of this year;   
this is suggested by its appearance in the sequence of charts on unem  
ployment (pp.16o-3 below). That the guideline notes make no mention   
of bold type confirms a dating of their composition at around 193o. The guideline note on these matters runs as follows:

<table><tr><td colspan="3">Lettering &amp; type [Schrift]:</td></tr><tr><td>Available sizes:</td><td>14 Cicero</td><td>4.5 cm</td></tr><tr><td></td><td>10</td><td>2.8</td></tr><tr><td></td><td>7</td><td>2.2</td></tr><tr><td></td><td>6</td><td>1.8</td></tr><tr><td></td><td>S</td><td>1.5</td></tr><tr><td></td><td>4</td><td>1.2</td></tr><tr><td></td><td>3</td><td>0.9</td></tr><tr><td></td><td>2</td><td>0.6</td></tr><tr><td></td><td>Mittel</td><td>0.4</td></tr><tr><td></td><td>Garamond</td><td>0.25</td></tr><tr><td></td><td>Petit</td><td>0.2</td></tr></table>

The titling of a chart will rarely exceed 7 Cicero and rarely be less than 5 Cicero; the same size is used for setting the year numerals. Foreign language explanatory titles will always be chosen from a smaller size. The lengths of lines will play a part in the choice. The type may never reach into the chart itself; this impairs the physiognomic effect. If type is to be put on a map, the surrounding area will be filled in with the colour appropriate to the background. We set everything in upper and lower case [in der Kategorie ‘Gemein], except in the case of geographic designations on maps, which are set in capitals.

The treatment of the signature of the Museum, usually placed in the bottom right hand corner of the chart, corresponds to that of the title: the change from capitals to upper and lower case occurs at the same time. A monogram composed of the initial letters of the Gesellschaftsund Wirtschaftsmuseum appears in the signature of some charts made in 1928 (T5f for example), and perhaps in some that were made early in 1929. It was, however, dropped in 1929.3² Charts made in Vienna for exhibition abroad, after 1931, carried the signature 'Mundaneum Wien' (T815 for example). Then, after the move to the Netherlands, the signature at first adopted was 'Mundaneum Institute The Hague' (or some variant of this) as in T1139. But in 1935 the Isotype symbol was designed

![](images/d7a75366d8abbf94c1a549dba328a70eb19766c1d92d5fc5ce96da1ab884d1b8.jpg)  
T5f Men and women in free trade unions Die Gewerkschaften, 1928. Relative proportion per 1o members of free trade unions in Austria.

![](images/9cc7038d6a6b5c34b55809f9127808f4624610ee55bdb65c8aee6a87ce7d6977.jpg)  
T1139 Industrial disputes USA c.1934 Each pictogram = 10o disputes.

32. Gerd Arntz was not happy with this monogram; his dislike for it would have been a factor leading to its discontinuation.

![](images/6fd08c36fa69eac7802ae07f4de60755dba2c6d7b6fc4dd7bbc27eb3a6221022.jpg)  
T815 Amsterdam's trade with Spain and Portugal 1591–1602 (during the eighty years war) 1932  
Made for display at the Economisch-Historische Bibliotheek, Amsterdam.

![](images/04b2ead817b3a1c1f2a99a8e11de3d9e9809f0ab5ba6bdaab64fae432c217de3.jpg)  
T1221 Import and export of the Netherlands C.1935 Each line = 10 million guilders. In original chart: import red; export black.

![](images/4e7f58c7ded57216c040d186e14d77a4c8f28be6199ea5194583423356073586.jpg)  
T13C Apprentice welfare action in Vienna 1925 'How many of all the Viennese young workers and apprentices were sent on a trip to the country as part of the initiative.'

and, from this time, all charts carried it as the chief element in their signature; T1221 is an early instance of this.

## Data and keys to charts

Other non-pictorial components of the charts show certain developments. In general, the earliest charts carry verbal and numerical matter that was later dispensed with; the treatment of numerical data exempli fies this. In the charts of 1925, exact numerical information is included in the body of the chart. And in his first article on graphic method, discussing the lack of precision that pictorial methods necessarily entail, Neurath suggests that 'pictorial inexactitude may be easily allowed for, indeed the exact figures can be placed nearby'.³³ The charts shown at the ‘Gesolei' exhibition of the next year (1926) show data placed in the margin of the chart; by 1928, this information was normally omitted from charts altogether.

33. 'Darstellungsmethoden des Gesellschafts- und Wirtschaftsmuseums' (1925), p. 19.

A similar development may be noticed in the way in which pictograms were identified. In early charts, pictograms were reproduced in the margin of the chart where the quantity represented by each was given. The explanatory pictogram appears usually in the same size as the pictograms in the body of the chart, though in some early charts the pictogram in the key is a reduced version (thus T13c). This latter practice could be the cause of confusion, and in contradiction to the principle that forbids the magnification of pictograms. It was soon dropped and, from about 1927, the function of pictograms was, except in unusual circumstances, explained in words. One such exception is T1823; this is essentially a 'cartogram', employing many symbols, the meanings of which are most easily explained by showing each symbol in a key (in the size in which they appear in the body of the chart).

## Configuration: principles of ordering material

The body of work under discussion may be classified according to the kind of material shown and according to the ways in which the material is shown – the conventions of configuration or disposition that are employed. It is difficult to separate these two aspects (content and form) but the list that follows may serve as a working scheme of configurations. Even in the earliest work the full range of these configurations may be discerned (see examples in chapter 1).

Charts showing quantified material:

1. rows or columns

2. division of a whole (usually a checker-board’)

3. geographically ordered (cartograms and more diagrammatic charts)

4. quantities related to area (usually showing densities)

5. flows.

Charts showing non-quantified material.

The lack of any subdivision within the category of charts showing non-quantified material reflects the fact that the great bulk of this work was based on numerical information, and that any other kind of chart must seem exceptional. An inspection of the whole body of work suggests, however, that after about 1936 a significantly greater proportion of charts fall into the 'non-quantified' category. This tendency becomes very clear in the work done in England after 1945.

In one of his published discussions of configuration Neurath confined himself to the first category, of rows or columns, within the larger group of charts showing quantified material.³4 He broke down this category as follows, indicating the purpose for which the particular configuration might be used; each of these subcategories is illustrated here by a chart dating from around the time of Neurath's discussion of the issue (1928).

![](images/f1327c028a333f5c8f586e7111136c230c58b85426a6ad4735d07048af4d4c01.jpg)  
T1823 South America: use of land Compton's pictured encyclopedia, 1939.

34. See the article 'Anordnung der Zeichen im Mengenbild' (1928), pp. 149–50; see also the later discussion in International picture language, pp. 85–6.

One may distinguish a further configuration not mentioned by Neurath in this discussion. This is the principle by which most of the births and deaths charts are ordered, intended to make a surplus or deficit clear in a comparison of two items (overleaf):

![](images/86717faa9e6ff59c27ac2c3eeedbef0f1a7f2396572891b95c7da2abfbc60094.jpg)  
T3d War and increase of population in Austria c.1928  
Each baby = 25,00o births; each gravestone = 25,00o deaths.

![](images/1019e63591ea0d4512ef61c8e4a8ae6a590a70ef16104eaf4276c353fc043a49.jpg)

The categories suggested here (including those put forward by Neurath himself) are no more than an initial, rough scheme. That they may be applied most easily to the earlier work – that of, say, the first five years – is significant. One may offer the following interpretation: the work of the first year shows a perhaps surprising variety of configuration of quantified material; in the next few years, the work is concentrated largely in just one of these categories of configuration – rows or columns; these were the years in which basic elements of the Vienna Method were being discovered and, though the concentration on rows and columns was largely a consequence of the tasks asked of the Museum at that time, this provided a necessary delimitation of the field within which basic elements such as the colour and form of pictograms, interrelation of pictograms, and typography, could be explored; after these basic elements had reached a stage of first maturity (around 1930), the other and perhaps most basic element of configuration could be taken up again as something to be explored; the work of the 193os – and particularly after 1936 – provides a large number of charts in the categories outside that of ‘quantified: rows or columns’.

The discussions of charts that follow are intended to show the graphic formation of the work. For the reasons just advanced, this means that the examples come primarily from the category of quantified: rows or columns'. To correct any impression that this category of chart might be typical of the work as a whole, this discussion of categories of configuration will conclude by reproducing and commenting on charts that show both the crudeness of the categories and something of the scope and variety of the work in its maturity.

![](images/0278b673dd529b17559df2ead2a73a018a48c6024f6968ec6e7cd8a9153333a5.jpg)

## T72C Provision of trainee positions by the Industrial Commission of the Federal district of Carinthia.

Made for the 'Kärnten' exhibition, 1929.

This is a variation on a simple row configuration: main rows show those who left professional training and found the jobs they wanted; the stepped rows show those who found 'other jobs'. The images at each end of the row – showing the training establishment and the places of apprenticeship – are read as both 'guide-pictures' and as part of the body of the chart.

![](images/3f32233a2f4e67194626fd335e0b048c59f2f05f2bac04bfbdc68e219e1ac329.jpg)

![](images/72df87d09b774b740d775da7bbc1a1c59a7e617ec05a4cbcdad35c6615cad316.jpg)

![](images/bfda5f586430c6a219de3215988c62808cad2afbab147c8f9da811087c0ca761.jpg)

![](images/eeaf08d64fbe88909d9cafd2408c65b19a848c0b5eea08b1d0d2a813b897fba3.jpg)  
T340 Customs tariffs in Europe before the [First World] War 1929

## T439 Manpower in the transport of a heavy object c.1930

Technik und Menschheit, 1932, Heft 1, no. 2.

Each group of people = 6o workers;

each individual person = 1 worker;

each group of horses = 2o horses (corresponding to the strength of 6o workers);

Quantities are shown through the illusion of threedimensional height, and ordered geographically.

each black rectangle = 5o tonnes weight.

The men and group pictograms show quantities, but the configuration, far from being merely one of rows or columns, includes other elements that allow one to read the chart as a narrative – this is the function of the depiction of the tools and the heavy loads (shown as grey, within the area of black rectangles that quantify weights) – so that one learns something of the process of lifting, as well as the weights and the numbers of people involved.

![](images/ae397d466c052a72c27fc24256050f80eb6f7f2ee31ce3a2f9592f36789fe861.jpg)

![](images/13c07a9a88a6bbd20875255ec339256f90ee6a7b3354acf1a87679433173f5d1.jpg)

## T1810 How long do animals live?

Compton's pictured encyclopedia, 1939.

This chart defies all the categories that have been suggested here. It is clear that any more precise attempt to classify Isotype charts would have to include a category of neither rows nor columns nor 'checker-boards' but, say, 'zig-zags' or 'coils'. The latter term would describe the flexibility of the device in being able, by vertical extension or compression, to accommodate variations in density of items placed on it, while maintaining a constant length. The use of colour in this chart, explained in its key, should be noted.

T1816 How modern tools grew out of primitive devices Compton's pictured encyclopedia, 1939.

The rows and the columns (which are emphasized by the use of colour) together constitute a pictorial matrix, to which the negative category of 'non-quantified' does no justice at all.

![](images/c9d16aa3e5c44b3c73ac57501b93bd319d9cc0951581ba54e1edccc97c32a6b6.jpg)

## T1795 Railways around the world

From the exhibition 'Het rollende rad' (The rolling wheel), 1939. Each horizontal unit = 100,o0o km of railway; each green [grey] unit [of area] = 1 million km each black figure = 10 million inhabitants.

The elements of this chart are certainly 'quantified'. Then looking at it in the light of the five subdivisions of this category: it shows rows and columns, in an implied third dimension; it has 'checker-board' aspects; it is ordered geographically (west to east, with 'other countries' on the extreme right); the chart relates quantities to area and to length of railway line; it even suggests (through the train and carriage) a sense of flow.

![](images/6dc2b3f93e797262ecd6364751961d14e5f6647bc01b54e06d039e1d541db043.jpg)

![](images/91f26e101212646d3f2de642736f0b24b979410ce44175eb72d005d976045be7.jpg)  
T1417 & T1418 Newcomen's and Watt's steam engines C. 1935

One would categorize these charts, straightforwardly, as diagrams that explain (partly by the comparison that they offer) the workings of machines. Diagrams of this kind were to become a staple ingredient of the educational books produced by the Isotype Institute in England.

![](images/c87d94b9d6b2d7a6de7165d446ed45942c192ec3b5adcfce79885c543a99cd6a.jpg)

## T391 Human embryo 1930

This chart belongs to the vague category of 'non-quantified'. It works in the same way as the maps in the chart on the development of cities (see pp. 212–13 below): presentations of the most important features of the object at successive stages in its development are read as units of a narrative. (See also p. 434 below.)

In addition to those images shown here to indicate the variety of configuration in Isotype work, one could also point out the sequence of charts from Gesellschaft und Wirtschaft showing plans and population of cities (T260, 261, 262 & T290; pp. 212–13 below), the time chart from 'Rondom Rembrandt' (T1698; p. 291), and chart no.2 from the first series of posters for the National Tuberculosis Association (T1456; p. 342).
