## Technik

Es wird hauptsächlich Scherenschnitt verwendet, der Einheitlichkeit der Farbflächen sichert, die für Wirkung recht wesentlich ist. Der schriftartige Charakter der Zeichen wird deutlich sichtbar Scherenschnitt ist leicht erlernbar und von der modernen Schule in den lehrplan aufgenommen. Es werden auch geographische und anatomische Tafeln in dieser Tečhnik erzeugt

Ausschnitt aus einer Karte (Stadtgebiet)

![](images/581eaba6ae67050821ee18dffb9ea923aefc00df751b7b0a638d172ee2ef2cde.jpg)

Auschnitt aus einer anatomischen Tafel (Nerven)

![](images/ef0646e769fbd0f343cece4d6eed316cda4e2427ac614045f69007ca3b5a31d2.jpg)

## Schrift

Die Bildertafeln sind so angeordnet, dass die Bilder möglichst allein sprechen, die Schrift nur als Beigabe erscheint. Durchweg Blockschrift,die einfach und daher leicht lesbar ist. Für längere Erläuterungen werden grosse und kleine Buchstaben verwendet.

For example:</td></tr><tr><td>Old people: grey</td><td>children in the country, green background;</td></tr><tr><td></td><td>children in the city, grey background</td></tr></table>

# SOCIAL AND ECONOMIC MUSEUM OF VIENNA

## Departments

Work and organization

Public health

Housing and town-planning

## Purpose

Information on questions concerning life in a human community, through exhibitions, lectures, films, publications.

## Charts and models

Schematic emphasis of essentials through a picture-script of generally comprehensible hieroglyphs, that may be used as international pictograms – for example, to explain the content of statistical tables.

Forms
<table><tr><td>Chosen methods of presentation More objects are shown by more signs.</td><td>Modes of presentation avoided</td></tr><tr><td>Schematic example: cars</td><td>More objects shown by larger signs. Naturalistic counter-example: cars</td></tr><tr><td>Germany France</td><td>Germany France</td></tr><tr><td>100,000 300,000</td><td>100,000 300,000</td></tr></table>

Bars formed of symbols, comparisons can be easily made. Example: zinc production

Schematization of drawing taken as far as possible, in order to create 'speaking pictograms' that do not say more than is necessary.

Squares and cubes are very hard to compare. Counter-example: zinc production

Where heads are to be stressed as items to be counted, it is enough to represent them as circular discs. For example, 1 figure = 1,000 people

Colours as essential means of differentiation

## Colours

## Technique

For the most part, scissor cutting is used; this ensures the unity of the area of colour that is essential in achieving the desired effect; the letter-like abstract character of the pictograms becomes clearly apparent. Scissor cutting is easily learned and has been taken up by the teaching programme of the modern school. Geographical and anatomical charts are also produced by this technique.

Section of a map (town area)

Section of an anatomical chart (nerves)

## Lettering

The pictorial charts are so designed that the pictures speak for themselves as far as possible; lettering appears only as an accompaniment. Capitals, which are simple and thus easily readable, are used throughout. For longer explanatory texts, upper and lower case is used.

The chief interest of the chart on p. 146 above is in its references to conventions and practices that were to be superseded later. Notable among these are:

objects (cars) are stacked in columns; the principle of natural arrangement had evidently not yet been formulated;

— a human pictogram with a perfectly circular head is to be employed in charts 'where heads are to be stressed as items to be counted';

— men are to be dark in colour and women light, children to be green and old people grey;

— background colours are to be used for signification;

— a method of producing pictograms that is easily learnt (scissor-cutting) is to be employed; this consideration of a method that could be easily learnt by the layperson was passed over when linocutting was adopted;

— capital letters are preferred over upper and lower case in titles.

One may note too that the idea of using pictograms 'to explain the content of statistical tables' was proposed again in International picture language in 1936 (p. 18).

These features of the chart suggest that it was made in the second half of 1925 or early in 1926. It seems certain to have been made after Neurath's article about 'Graphic methods of presentation for statistics' had been written (published in August 1925), for it is clearly an advance on that inevitably limited discussion.3⁵ It cannot have been made after the time of the 'Gesolei' exhibition (summer 1926), for by then background colour was consciously avoided as a means of signification (see the section 'Colour and form', pp. 117-18 above). Another strong reason for assigning an early date to this chart is its assumption that objects are to be stacked in columns rather than arranged in rows.

If the chart was made in the period suggested then it is perhaps surprising that it should have been published only in September 1926, and again early in 1927. And the chart may well have been shown at the 'Städtebauausstellung' held in Vienna in the autumn of 1926; in an article in the special number of the Österreichische Gemeinde-Zeitung published for this exhibition, Neurath referred to 'a special explanatory chart' that 'shows the basic rules of method that are used by the Museum'.36

This would, then, seem to be a case of a chart being reused some significant time after the occasion for which it had been made (see the section 'Reuse of charts’, p. 111 above). It was inevitable that the quite detailed and explicit explanation of method of this chart, made at a time of rapid development, should have gone out of date very quickly – although much of the content of the chart was still valid in the autumn of 1926. One would suppose, nevertheless, that soon afterwards, in 1927, the outdated features of the chart would have become so anachronistic as to prevent its further use.

The idea that discs or circles could be used as heads in human pictograms seems not to have been much put into practice. One chart that shows it (T5o) is reproduced on p. 167. Another is shown below; an exhibition version may be seen in a photograph of the Museum, N499. The circles of this chart were repeated on an accompanying map of Vienna, which thus showed density of population (see p. 54 above). Both map and chart were reproduced in the same issue of Der Aufbau in which the chart on method was first published.

![](images/acff2bcc99d2dde7e7902d09f42d16de9d57d1c50368f88983d5bbc78b571bd5.jpg)

Die nach der Niedertegung der Festungswerke errichteten Neubauten wurden für Wohnzwecke in Anspruch genommen. Die Zaht der Béwohnēr des Bezirkes.Innere Stadt"wächst bis in die achtziger Jahre an, um dann stark abžunehmen, während gleichzeitig die Zahi der Geschåftsräume zunimmt

1857 53.072   
4869 63.901   
7680 69.635   
4690 67.029   
1990 58.503   
4810 53100   
4840 43.619   
1983 43.045

Inner city and city development 1926 Der Aufbau, Jg.1, Nr. 8/9, September 1926, pp.176.

35. 'Graphische Darstellungsmethoden für Statistik' (1925). 36. 'Das Gesellschafts- und Wirtschaftsmuseum auf der internationalen Städtebauaustellung in Wien' (1926), p. 67.

![](images/499f8a0bf84092bcbd05e64277fe271190428fd85a164966bb14a41b36a93218.jpg)

## T3C Births and deaths in Vienna 1925

Österreichische Gemeinde-Zeitung, Jg.3, Nr.10, 15 May 1926, p. 26.

This is likely to be the chart referred to in the original caption to chart A as 'another treatment of the same material' that had been published in the 'Statistische Mitteilungen' of the Gemeinde Wien in 1925. This would, at least, certainly mean that T3c existed at the time that A was first reproduced.

![](images/35379afa09db5b7c8d754fec866de91698906f0c9a22f1a5f64f455dd07563ea.jpg)

![](images/34fda67b652413ba37eb59fd78365547a017f6718cd007fc6d045068104bd911.jpg)

## A Births and deaths in Vienna 1925

Österreichische Gemeinde-Zeitung, Jg. 2, Nr.16, 15 August 1925, p.19.

Chart A shows in one configuration material that occupies two separated configurations in T3c; this may be considered as evidence for the chronological precedence of T3c – and Marie Neurath confirmed that this was indeed the order in which these charts were made. Chart A can be considered as an advance on T3c in using what was to become the normal way of making visually apparent a surplus or deficit in two sets of data. On the other hand, chart A incorporates the essentially non-pictorial element of the bar graph to state the surplus or deficit, thereby repeating the statement that the pictograms would make if left unencumbered by these added elements. Later versions of charts on this subject show that the bar element was judged to be superfluous.

Both charts are primitive', in terms of the developments of the work of the next few years. They are strongly influenced by the conventions of non-pictorial graphic statistics: the inclusion of exact numerical data; the labelling of columns; the use of a key to the columns in chart A.

## [Untitled and unnumbered chart] c.1926

This chart was not included in the T files, but is present in a collection of offprints in the Isotype Collection – so it was reproduced somewhere. It stands apart from most other charts in this sequence in showing births and deaths as a proportion of a constant total number (4oo inhabitants). Two later charts (T386 and T476; p.153 below) use the same principle. The material is configured to let one see the relative decline in births over the years, with the difference between figures for births and deaths evident but not emphasized, as it is in other charts here. Given their overall configuration, birth and death pictograms necessarily have about the same height.

![](images/b3b67b6c5b52a37b84c5c780070d2778b2a9dafda76d85f8796cb7ecee9c12d7.jpg)

Gravestones replace the coffins of chart A and T3c. Neurath explains this change in his discussion accompanying the reproduction – the small coffins had been taken as signifying infant deaths only, where the pictogram ought to denote deaths of people of all ages (see the quotation on p. 501 below). The symbols for births are now solid (and red) and thus achieve visual equality with the (black) gravestones.

Made for the 'Gesolei' exhibition; Österreichische Gemeinde-Zeitung, Jg. 3, Nr. 10, 15 May 1926, p. 27. Each child = 5,ooo live births; each gravestone = 5,ooo deaths in all age groups.

![](images/917aa16fc5179961edf13b36fade3bd5eed3dcef3451f6773da1951906e5969d.jpg)

T3d War and increase of population in Austria c. 1928 Each child = 25,00o births; each gravestone = 25,00o deaths. Surplus and deficit inside the red lines (outlined segments).

The chart probably dates from 1928; the Museum's monogram, in the signature, is the strongest evidence for this date; it could well have been shown in the Frau und Kind' (Woman and child) exhibition of this year.

## T3f Births and deaths in Vienna 1926

Columns are supported by light coloured (according to Neurath's discussion) year bands – to encourage a vertical reading. And these bands also serve to relate the appropriate births and deaths columns. The bar elements showing surplus and deficit are now incorporated into the column of pictograms. The exact numerical data are no longer given.

Though the subject of the chart is Austria, not just Vienna, one can legitimately compare it with the births and deaths charts of 1925 and 1926, as if it was another treatment of their material. The major change is that material for five years is shown, where the earlier charts had treated two years. This move to a more selected and visually clearer statement of the material is consolidated by all subsequent births and deaths charts.

The problem of showing the pictograms as constituting (vertical) columns, rather than (horizontal) rows is now met by a more subtle pattern of space between columns. And the background strips are now incorporated into the pictograms – in that the pictograms are reversed out of implied rectangular elements. This device seems to have been a feature of charts made at this time; it was soon to be dropped, being made redundant by the use of more essentially modular pictograms. The reversing-out of pictograms also enforces a visual equality between births symbols and deaths symbols; this was an issue that Neurath had raised in his discussion of T3c and T3f, but which had not perhaps been satisfactorily resolved in T3f. The gravestone is now drawn in projection.

According to the statement on the chart, where the pictograms constitute a surplus or deficit they are bounded by a red line; this is not clear in the black-and-white reproduction, though a similar line may be seen in T3f.

The visual key to the pictograms is now omitted, and these are explained verbally; this became conventional practice.

![](images/ea688e25a64947c7726caf0159adf54d395034daa058824a5b2b7f052e62df77.jpg)

![](images/26077910560943df3ea6f00370093265d51ff23227b3cae606531a3e7e94d3ab.jpg)

T179a (above) Births and deaths in Germany 1928

Die bunte Welt, 1929, p. 43

(see also International picture language, 1936, p. 87).

Each child = 1 million live births; each gravestone = 1 million deaths.

T6of (right) Births in Austrian federal provinces 1926   
1929

Within marriage | outside marriage.

Each figure = 250 births.

T17ga marks the change of axes in this series of charts. The convention of time running down the vertical axis and a horizontal direction of reading had been employed in almost all the Museum's charts, from the earliest days. That it had not been used in these births and deaths charts before 1g28 (the year in which this chart must have been made) is remarkable and not easy to explain. Perhaps the force of precedent, of the previous versions, prevented fresh thought; perhaps only by 1g28 had the principle of showing time running down the vertical axis become fully conscious as a convention to be applied generally. Marie Neurath suggested that a factor may have been the force of the convention of showing, in the surplus and deficit graphs from which these charts derive, positive values running up from the horizontal axis. This chart also shows,

## B Births and deaths per 200 inhabitants in Calau district 1928

Der Kreis Calau, 1928, p.9.

The graphic treatment of material in this chart is similar to that in T3d. The backgrounds to the births symbols and the deaths symbols are made tonally different, probably in correspondence to a differentiation of colour (red for births, black for deaths) in the exhibition chart, on which the black-and-white reproduction is based. The information given in the key in other charts on this subject is here incorporated into the title.

![](images/82cf5e8dcbec20791a29e41161dace102f87c29a4de12d682b2244c245da67f2.jpg)

for the first time in the series, the use of Futura type and linocut pictograms. These new elements (and the addition of Gerd Arntz to the team) may have helped in the rethinking that led to the change of axes.

The births symbol is in outline; this may have been the form preferred for black-and-white reproduction, and this chart was probably designed to be reproduced, rather than to be exhibited. An outline births symbol may have been developed in black-and-white charts – this is suggested by chart A and by T3c – but it was not used exclusively on such charts. Reference may be made here to T6of, shown at the 'Kärnten' exhibition of 1929 – an exhibition chart that uses an outline birth symbol (though there is here no need for comparability with a deaths symbol). An outline form does allow a risk of inequality to arise between the births and deaths symbols; though in T179a (and T3b, discussed overleaf), given all the other elements that constitute the whole chart, this is not a significant problem.

## T3b Births and deaths in Vienna 1928

Bildstatistik nach Wiener Methode in der Schule, 1933, plate xiv.   
Each child = 20,00o live births; each gravestone = 20,00o deaths.

The early position of this chart in the T files and the similarity of its graphic and typographic elements to those of the quite precisely datable T179a suggest that it was made late in 1928 or in 1929. It was published only in 1933. The lack of a signature suggests that it was not exhibited. Any comparison of T3b with T179a should note the differences in their subjects (Vienna as against Germany), in the years shown, and in their scales of symbols to data.

The direction of the rows of births and of deaths is reversed in this chart, which makes it an exception among all the known treatments of this subject. This configuration shows perhaps more visibly than the others the excess of deaths over births in the war years. But the excess of deaths – a deficit in terms of human life – is made to appear as a surplus. If the visual statement of τ3b is clearer than that of the other versions it is also, in showing loss of life as a surplus, misleading. In all the other charts births run in the positive direction of left to right (or vertically upwards in the earliest versions), while deaths run negatively; an excess of births over deaths thus appears as a

T179b Excess of births in Germany 1929  
![](images/bd5ff3ffaf81221911ef970e992a729910bbb7ccaa3ce5be29cd7a35ee9e6b86.jpg)

Made for the Berlin branch of Museum.   
Each child = 1 million live births;   
each gravestone = 1 million deaths.

![](images/6e0de8659d0997a0be85d4cda7cba23a90a71b6977b8ef679f75fabd6ee2d3de.jpg)

surplus (to the right of or above the dividing line) while an excess of deaths appears as a deficit.

A detail that confirms the suggestion that these two charts, T179a and T3b, were made at the same time is that the keys to both describe the gravestone as 'Sarg' (coffin). This is a simple error that does not occur in the other versions that show the gravestone as the symbol for deaths.

This chart shows the same material as T179a; but, where T179a was almost certainly designed for black-and-white reproduction, T179b is an exhibition chart. T179b uses a bounding line to show surplus and deficit; the absence of this feature in T179a and t3b would seem, given the later charts in the series, to be an advance – so one might conclude that T179b was made before T179a and T3b. This is not wholly impossible: the Berlin exhibition opened in March 1929, while Die bunte Welt was announced as to be published at around Christmas 1928. But a more likely possibility is that though T179b was made after T179a and T3b, as an exhibition chart it followed the precedent of T3d and B and thus took over this bounding line from those charts. Only in the next chart in this series was the chronological distance from the previous chart great enough to allow the freshness of thought that could consider this line as unnecessary. There are no rules over the years in this chart. This seems to be another indication that the precedent of the last exhibition chart, T3d or B, was more forceful than that of the more recent book charts. And, in the light of later charts in the sequence, the lack of these rules makes T179b appear to be an exceptional case; a judgement that is confirmed by the births symbol that is employed in the chart. The form of this symbol is unique in the series; one may account for it as being simply an experiment. And the symbol differs from immediately preceding versions in being solid; this was the mode preferred in all subsequent births and deaths charts.

![](images/93a75aacf56e7477f906ca6c89c9fc5b7affb62cef59360389dd7edba0e674dc.jpg)

## T386 Birth and death in Vienna C.1930

Each child = 1 birth per 2oo inhabitants;

each gravestone = 1 death per 2oo inhabitants.

The place of T386 in the T files and its relation to T476 (which must have been shown at the 'Maria Theresa' exhibition of 193o) suggest a dating of 1929 or 193o. Both charts show relative rather than absolute data – the number of births or deaths for every 2oo inhabitants. This is signified by the groups that form the background to the rows.

![](images/1e80a219f968b2fa0b9ab2e2835cb07d690efe16b2bdb944cf843d266f337781.jpg)  
T468 Birth and death in Vienna before and after the war c. 1931

Each child = 5,ooo live births; each gravestone = 5,00o deaths.

Rules appear over the years, extending equally on each side beyond the width of the year numerals. Looking again at the previous charts in the series with this detail in mind, one can say that these rules would not have been of any assistance in T386 and T476 (the longer line of the year spans and the top of the block of grouped pictograms make their own connection), but that the absence of these rules in T17gb makes that chart appear, again, as exceptional. The central vertical rule is here, as in T3b, thicker than the rules over the years.

![](images/78dafe9f445c6a8980ad4bca7efe28bb92571260755428c7211dadc21421f920.jpg)

## T476 Birth and death in Vienna since 1700 1930 Made for the 'Maria Theresa' exhibition.

T476 shows five rows where T386 shows twelve. This, in the light of the general tendency in the work to move towards greater selectivity, would suggest that T386 was made before T476. The precedence of T386 in the T files also suggests its chronological precedence, despite the fact that it treats more recent data than that used in T476. Further evidence for this order of precedence is provided by the different treatment of the Museum's signature – T386 shows capitals (despite its upper and lower case title) while T476 uses upper and lower case.

![](images/f9b6e0c229d76dfe8dfc064bb8c75fd4361b18b2c4ecb0edd223280f0d5ea174.jpg)

![](images/022a8ca5985b596f5c420a59331445b1ed440eb1a97210a6654b42681adfc4c8.jpg)  
T564 Birth and death in Berlin C. 1931

Probably made for the Berlin branch. A double chart including marriage data (other charts shown here had a separate companion).

There is no significant divergence here from the approach evident in T468. Half pictograms are placed at the right end of the row – to generalize from the single occurrence in the rows for 1g21. This differs from the principle that seems to operate in the cases of T386 and T476. The advantage of the approach in T564 is that here all the pictograms align vertically, although halves do not appear in the surplus and deficit zones. (Note that the marriage symbol is halved along its horizontal axis, so as not to isolate one figure.)

The treatment of rules here corresponds to that in T468.

![](images/b436fbbcf2d81ea7ac14f8d733bc8e3e7d80ca4dcc3ff0c4ea4e44541de5b38a.jpg)  
Each red symbol represents 25 births per 10,000 population Each black symbol represents 25 deaths per 10,000 population  
Low death-rate is combined with high birth-rate in the Netherlands

## T1710 Births and deaths in 1935 1938

Compton's pictured encyclopedia, 1939.   
Each child = 25 births per 10,o0o population;   
each gravestone = 25 deaths per 10,ooo population.

The material of this chart differs significantly from that of preceding births and deaths charts; here the comparison is between figures for different places at a fixed time. The ordering principle that is evident here also differs from that of the preceding charts; this would seem to reflect the difference in the material, and the particular fact that no country shows a deficit. This allows the right half of the chart to be headed Excess of births over deaths'. In the absence of a strictly comparable chart among earlier charts of this series one cannot make any general deductions about development of method from this apparently new ordering principle; though one may conclude that force of precedent was overcome and that the previously applied principle was here replaced by one which would make a clearer visual statement of the material. A point that emerges from the chart is that countries with a high death rate tend also to have a high birth rate.

One comparison with earlier charts is possible. The figures shown here are relative, not absolute. But where in T386 and T476 the controlling group had been signified rather literally as a background to the rows (as if to say visually 'this number in every 2oo'), this group is now shown as a heading applying to all the rows. There would seem to be a considerable gain in visual clarity in this later convention.

One may note that the list of countries is ordered geographically, from north and west to south and east.

![](images/e03b4d6075968c4bfe2ed0260c12b093e35544bf73730ce5b8addd125cea5ddb.jpg)  
Eoch red symbol represents 25 births per 10,000 population Each black symbol represents 25 deaths per 10,000 population

T1740 Low death-rate is combined with high birth-rate in the Netherlands C.1938

Made for the Netherlands Government health exhibition.   
Same values as in T1710 (left).

The principle of ordering here is that evident in T171o, and treatment of graphic detail corresponds essentially to that of that chart.

![](images/5cc2af16467e40d2e905d73169b9de751acc4f533fa5a5c0f9f2cd25edf057bb.jpg)

N499 A photograph of the exhibition at the Rathaus, taken in or soon after December 1927, shows a births-and-deaths chart that must fall between T3f and T3d – charts which may be dated at 1926 and 1928 respectively. The date at which this photograph was taken is, then, quite consistent with the date suggested by the visual evidence of the births and deaths chart that it shows.

## Charts on unemployment

The charts on unemployment form one of the largest subject groups within the whole body of work. Not only do they allow one to compare treatment of graphic detail over a quite extensive period, but their subject matter allows a more precise dating than is usually possible, particularly when these charts show data for periods within a year. It is from these charts that one can often draw the most precise conclusions about the chronology of the graphic conventions in the work as a whole.

![](images/1d1c71b8028ce731b3a75cf5db07dfb7244f9e05b4b086b0fe748162b1e58a2a.jpg)

![](images/4646da4c914415ceb74361e4e1826cced90fe04884057b1565e320cf1986f9c0.jpg)

T5s with T5t Unemployment in Austria c.1928 Proportion out of 1o unemployed: in Vienna | elsewhere.

The occasion for which this chart was made is not known;   
its position in the T files provides no clue.

The chart is based on percentages, not absolute figures, and this differentiates it from most of the other unemployment charts. The need to show a constant ten pictograms divided into two parts leads to the use of a face-on pictogram. But the need to signify unemployment through form has not allowed the symmetry that a pictogram should have in order to achieve a completely natural divisibility; only a form that is symmetrical about its axis of division shows an unequivocal half unit. The styles of pictogram and lettering suggest a date for the chart earlier than 1929, the date that the content might suggest. Both these elements seem to predate the presence of Arntz in the team (from September 1928), though the chart could well have been made not long before this time. The pictogram is

more developed than the obviously scissor-cut forms of charts made earlier in 1928. If the chart was made in 1928 then the figures for 192g would have had to be estimated, and one would have expected an explanation of this on the chart.

T5t shows the dangers of dating these charts on the evidence of their content: this chart is merely a revised edition of T5s, adding data for 1931. A revised or second edition seems to have been planned, for T5s shows the space for an extra row. Charts normally show no such generous space between last row and signature.

The existence of T5t allows one to put forward another theory concerning the disparity of style and apparent date of T5s: could T5s have been made in 1928 when data up to 1927 were known, leaving space for two rows (for 1929 and 1931) to be added?

T5t should be compared with T8o4 (p. 161 below), which shows exactly the same material treated in a later manner.

T6ok Carinthia 1928: maximum unemployment; minimum unemployment

![](images/dbff7c3dba40c2d8f52a4a06b9e846d0ba9b087d0e6f36f2f0eed6f788ff6911.jpg)

The material of the chart is dated January and August 1928; one may therefore take this chart as likely to have been made later in that year.

A differentiation of gender and of occupation is made, and in this respect the chart is unique among the unemployment charts. Though the most notable difference of this chart is that the notion of unemployment is signified through the woman or man being seated; this would seem to have been an experiment. The pictograms seem to show the influence of Arntz.

![](images/0acb99d7d62ae9667dee16347f477958a34ca36558580ddf6f145070f0696a4f.jpg)

![](images/0081bfb944aa90dc4b026d072b121d079841f7af3412e3abee9198c35ead9b71.jpg)  
T303b Unemployment in Austria 1929  
Each figure = 100,000 unemployed on support.

All three charts were made for the Berlin branch of the Museum.  
![](images/f50a62749427e351c75382832d37dc315629b891e7d7911936fb58b599e037c5.jpg)  
T104a Unemployment in Berlin 1929 Each figure = 10,000 unemployed (unemployment insurance and public welfare).

These are the first charts to use what came to be the normal symbolization of unemployment; this pictogram is the first in these charts that is certain to have been drawn by Gerd Arntz. The idea of showing unemployment through a stooping posture and a hand in a pocket does appear already in the pictogram of 5s and T5t; the graphic treatment of the idea in these charts of 1929 shows clearly Arntz's contribution to pictogram design.

The data that these charts show do not allow certainty in assigning an order of priority in their making. But the use of upper and lower case for the months of T1o4a suggests that it may have been made after the other two; this was the mode adopted in subsequent charts. The title and signature of T1o4a are (still) set in capitals. A further possible difference may be that in T1o4a the groups are marked off by a greater space than in T3o3a and T3o3b. Due to the differing scales of reproduction one cannot be sure of this These are among the first charts to show a clear policy of marking-off, and it would be natural for the appropriate spacing not to be discovered at once: the groups seem more clearly 'countable' in T104a.

![](images/7b74709ee38dc9308ed3adcd139934142d197843e728a8f48076df679c0912a2.jpg)

![](images/3b138f7f4e8ea81e02d8bf82467c75de2fa7b186e2bed0035e745bcdc0b69c96.jpg)

## T298 Unemployed

Gesellschaft und Wirtschaft, 1930, no.87.   
Great Britain; France; Germany.   
Each figure = 250,000 unemployed.

The data indicate that the chart was made before the figures for 1929 were known: earlier rather than later during the period in which the charts for Gesellschaft und Wirtschaft were made. The principal change shown by this chart is that the pictograms now face left to right and thus follow the conventional reading direction; though Marie Neurath suggested that the left-facing symbols have the advantage that the rows read better as queues, being lengthened by additions at the end.

The heading and signature are now in upper and lower case. There is no marking-off of pictograms; the rows may not have been considered long enough to demand it.

With this chart there is a significant change in the language of the title: what had been termed 'Arbeitslosigkeit' (unemployment) now becomes 'Arbeitslose' (the unemployed). The shift is from a concept to the human beings: from the abstract to the concrete. This movement of thought was very typical of Otto Neurath's drive for material reality.

## T104b Unemployed in Berlin 1931

Made for the Berlin branch of the Museum.

Each figure = 10,ooo unemployed (in receipt of unemployment benefit and welfare support).

T104b is an updated version of the material of Tto4a; though the different scales of reproduction make difficult an exact comparison of the size of the elements of each. One can note these changes: pictograms now face to the right, following the reading direction; pictograms are marked off in groups of ten – this change would seem to follow from the fact of showing a greater number of them; the size of pictogram may be smaller in the later chart; title and signature are set in upper and lower case; a rule is now placed over each year, and years are not repeated at each quarterly division. The change in treatment of the year and month headings – from the conventional form and single size of T104a to a visually more effective form in T1o4b – is clearly an improvement. The new form means that the months now lead to the shoulders of the pictograms, rather than to the heads; this would seem to have visual advantages. The relation of month to row of pictograms in T1o4b is perhaps too close for comfort at the rows for January; one wonders why Januar' was not abbreviated.

![](images/a10e5f6e6fd3361a6eae871224c14ce491d125e6b17c3b637e7ae7edfe1b4631.jpg)

![](images/47941bb1d2fbf64bb6f56764e0e05a646203c77b93bb590e0f427a3216e8176a.jpg)  
T454 World unemployment C.1929 One among a group of Russian-language charts commissioned by the Soviet trade delegation in Vienna. Each pictogram = 50o,o0o unemployed.

All text here was lettered by hand due presumably to a lack of Cyrillic type at the Vienna Museum.

![](images/381eab4ddc8af6376a36dc531f1bd304e8feaa01cf47ffc63bd758762fda6a50.jpg)  
Aus Wells: Arbeit, Wohlstand und das Glück der Menschheit Paul Zsolnay Verlag T 704

![](images/4647862dd2e69a1326592c1f7f4598f32b6cf781df178639d80ce5b656bf31b8.jpg)

These charts are German and Czech versions of the same material. If, as their data suggest, they were made in 1932, then they show retrograde aspects: the pictograms are facing in the less natural direction, in contrast to those of T298; the two long rows for 1931 could have been marked off. Pictograms are halved along the horizontal axis, rather than the vertical halving that would conform to reading direction. The half pictogram that results from the horizontal cut must have been judged as signifying more adequately than the vertically cut half, for the convention of horizontal halving is employed throughout these unemployment charts.

T704a was printed as a loose leaf for inclusion in portfolio envelopes published by the Museum/ Mundaneum. These published charts were the first to incorporate T numbers. This practice applied particularly to the Mundaneum charts, and may be taken as an indication of the increasingly well organized system of production of the last years in Vienna.

![](images/180c676bb1a890b13f1293a31c898a130086a7fadd5e7af951ab9d322f22cfc3.jpg)  
Jede Signatur 25000 Arbeitslose  
Arbeitsloser

![](images/498956102a1b8f0f2b1b7420cb902341b7d173179be5a3564bba3b3f49f9a507.jpg)

## T736 Unemployed in Berlin

Bildstatistik, Jg. 2, H.10, 1932, p.14.

Each pictogram = 25,00o unemployed.

Certain features of this chart distinguish it from its predecessors and successors that treat essentially similar material. The pictogram used here seems to be a variant not appearing on other charts; it is almost certainly no. go6 on the leaf from the 'dictionary' of pictograms reproduced below. T788 and T8oo, for example (see overleaf), use what seems to be no.596. The title and the year numerals are, in comparison with other versions, comparatively large in size in relation to other elements of the chart. The chart was reproduced in the magazine published by the Museum, Bildstatistik; if, as seems possible, the chart was made to a smaller format than would have been the case with the other versions, then this might help to explain the unusually large size of the title and years. The disparity in the sizes of charts T736 and T8oo, as reproduced here, and the sizes of pictograms nos. go6 and 596, as shown on the leaf from the dictionary, suggest that T736 was made not as a full-size exhibition chart, but rather as a chart for reproduction.

The larger size of numeral for the years in this chart allows the rule used in previous charts to be discarded. This treatment of the years is adopted in all succeeding charts on unemployment. But this chart shows one exceptional detail: the months are ranged to the right.

Page of pictograms for 'unemployed' from the 'picture dictionary' of the Gesellschafts- und Wirtschaftsmuseum, 193o–3 (ic 4/2). These are prints from linocuts.

The versions featuring a white arm/sleeve echo figures in Arntz's woodcut prints – see p. 225 below for an example.)

Each pictogram = 25,00o unemployed (both charts).

![](images/45bc9717e85ca5f83a6ce8bb37fe8c1e5f93e834225cdd3291845e39d6f90fa0.jpg)

## T80o Unemployed in Berlin 1932

Both charts above made for Berlin branch of the Museum.

The use of bold type for the title of these charts is a new feature. If their titles were not later additions to the charts – and there is no reason for thinking this – then one must take this as indicating that the acquisition and first use of Futura bold took place in 1932.

Comparing T8oo with T736 (p. 159 above), there are some further differences in addition to those already noted: data for July 1932 have been added, and omitted for January 1928; data for July 193o have been revised. The key refers to a differentiation between kinds of benefit being received: those receiving unemployment insurance and 'crisis assistance' are shown in red, those receiving social welfare payments are shown in black.

## T801 Welfare care in Kreuzberg and the rest of Berlin 1932

Made for the Berlin branch of the Museum.

Each individual figure = 2,50o unemployed on welfare in Kreuzberg;   
each group = 25,ooo unemployed on welfare in the rest of Berlin.

This chart is evidently a companion to T788 and τ8oo. It makes a comparison between those receiving welfare payments in the Kreuzberg district of Berlin (the single pictograms) and those in the rest of the city (groups of ten).

![](images/e597e3883679f5b6ace71f552d9d9402bfb26b959f736b52ff345f9b8dabbb05.jpg)

Wohlfahrtspflege in Kreuzberg und dem übrigen Berlin

## T762 Unemployed in Austria 1932

Each figure = 10,000 people;   
red (grey): in Vienna; black: in rest of Austria.

This shows features similar to T788 and T8oo. Space has been allowed for additional rows to be added at the foot of the chart. This chart seems to show a new pictogram for the unemployed – see the commentary to t8o3 (where the pictogram is more clearly visible).

Wien Obriges Osterreich   
1921   
1923   
1925   
1927   
1931

![](images/6984c669f80676edd7a8bbf5fd82c66060b1e48d67bec42fc7e5a0b845a5e318.jpg)

τ804 (above left) Unemployment in Austria 1932 Each figure = 100,000 people (both charts).

This chart may be compared with τ5t (p.155 above), which treats the same material. The pictogram is now symmetrical; it is essentially a frontal interpretation of the stooping form that had not yet been devised when the pictogram used in T5t was drawn. The guide-pictures have replaced the crests of the earlier chart. As Neurath had demanded of the guide-pictures, they directly and pictorially characterize the information in the body of the chart, whereas one needs to have learnt the special code by which the crests may be understood.

## T803 (above right) Members of free trade unions and unemployed in Austria 1932

This shows clearly the altered pictogram for the unemployed: no. 1117 on the leaf from the 'Picture dictionary' (see p. 159). The arm is now solid and indicated only by a white outline. This change in treatment of the arm could perhaps have arisen from the need to draw the frontal version (no. 1o6o) for T8o4: clearly a companion chart to T8o3. In drawing a frontal version of the earlier pictogram (no. 596, say), the 'white' arm would have presented itself as a problem. This is solved by redrawing the side-elevation with a solid arm, with which a solid frontal version is compatible.

This explanation for the redrawing is merely hypothesis. One could also discuss the difference between the two pictograms purely on the visual evidence that they present: the new version is more solid and stronger; or, in terms of meaning, the irrelevant question of what the white arm signified (a shirt-sleeve?) is now removed from consideration. From the evidence of Neurath's observations on pictogram design, one could suppose that both formal and semantic considerations would have affected this change. All subsequent charts showing absolute data for unemployment use the solid version. This new pictogram for the unemployed is, like its predecessor, halved along a horizontal axis; though the pictogram for the employed is halved along a vertical axis. This apparent inconsistency perhaps makes sense visually.

![](images/54fcf6d29728883a7f1b0fc6532594eb3f667609e6b1672257d98776e97f62a4.jpg)

T845 Unemployment in British trade unions 1933 Each black figure = 1 unemployed out of 10o trade-union members.

Both charts above are from a group of English-language charts made for showing at the London office of the Mundaneum. (See also T852, shown on p. 181, which belongs to the same group.)

![](images/104d69a590e306a95978f765a1ba51704db01497552bc6b11ac14627770e2729.jpg)

## T851 Unemployment in Germany 1933

Each figure = 250,000 unemployed.

These consolidate the developments of the charts of 1932: the new pictogram, the use of bold. The chief difference from their immediate predecessors is in the use of capitals for titles. This practice seems to have been associated at the time with English-language charts (see p.135).

![](images/1bb13e391d44573689b18b9df32a2a8d0d70d9fe9dfa5c9de247dd75ff982e24.jpg)

## T984 Unemployed and employed in shipbuilding and ship repairing in the United Kingdom 1933

Each figure = 10,000o insured people.

From a second group of charts made for the London Mundaneum (iC 7.2/16). 42 × 63 cm.

The title is set in upper and lower case Futura medium: this is not consistent with the use of capitals for Englishlanguage charts, nor with the general use by this time of bold for titles. Another apparently odd feature of this chart is that pictograms are halved on the vertical axis, contrary to the principle formulated in the commentary on T704a. But, with T8o3, this chart is exceptional within the subject group in showing data for the employed. Here, unlike T8o3, both employed and unemployed pictograms are halved vertically. This halved unemployed pictogram is not perfectly satisfactory, though the solid pictogram (no. 1117) now in use does produce a more coherent half than the previous version (no. 596) would have done.

![](images/332225b16cc16cb0f3bb23b05e1d4830021b2b6d9649af7297032ec9902329ae.jpg)  
T878 Unemployment in English trade unions 1933

German language version of T845. The use of upper and lower case type in the title is the only point of difference, and confirms the suggestion, by providing an exactly comparable German language chart, that for some reason capitals were thought appropriate for English language titles.

![](images/6066409b60267b34d89b5c5fc105b80fa21cf8c268dd1918eabf04a1ead54422.jpg)

Unemployment Cycles  
![](images/b2c025326e9ea745cf430959040da350def50a6f586d04b5c23750aa84bcdee0.jpg)

T1628 (above) Unemployment cycles 1938 Modern man in the making, 1939, p.14.

This shows essentially the same material as T845. The background controlling group is now treated as a heading to all the rows, with a consequent gain in visual clarity.

## T1155 Unemployment in English trade unions 1934

This is a Norwegian-language version of T845, made in The Hague, soon after the move there. The difference in the position of the title in this chart seems to be a consequence of the square format; the chart was one of a set designed for reproduction as lantern slides. The group pictogram has been redrawn to show outlines within the group.

![](images/3c675a59aaed6e60eb36ebc84c44bafddc176a0c1a810b5231f19515e1b82ba2.jpg)  
T176a World powers C.1926 Das Bild, March 1927, p. 59.

![](images/ee6a7cae6b9d97c77f782977cad3a8aaca96e02245ffce073be6bf7df9768604.jpg)

T176h World powers c.1928 British Empire | Independent states of Asia Medium and small states | France, ussR, America.

Each figure = 10 million people, rounded off.

These charts treat essentially the same subject: the political and national groupings of the world population.

T176a would seem to have been designed for black-and white reproduction: it shows a key to the tones employed. Besides the distinctions made through background tone and through dress, the pictograms show some incorporation' of qualifying symbols – the rising sun, the hammer and sickle – though this became common practice only in about 1929. C and T176b were also clearly designed for black-and-white reproduction, but no attempt is made in these charts to distinguish through tone: signification is achieved purely through form. T176h is a group of exhibition charts, which breaks the material of the charts in this series into four discrete parts. One may assume that it

made extensive use of colour and, probably for this reason, it uses the device of flags (see also 176c; overleaf). As has been discussed (in the section Flags' above), this device was recognized by Neurath as causing problems. But, particularly around the time at which the chart must have been made, flags were quite frequently used to denote nationality. (Flags appear in the related charts T176d–g, published in Die bunte Welt and reproduced with the use of at least six colours; see pp. 104–5 above.)

All the charts of this group are ordered in four rows, the middle rows are occupied by China and the British empire, the first and fourth rows are taken by the other powers. T176a differs from later charts in using pictograms that are not required to be units so much as distinguishing forms for the cells of the 'checker-board' configuration. The later charts discard the 'checker-board' configuration, increase the number of pictograms to be shown (against the tendency noted elsewhere) and thus require their pictograms to function as modular forms.

![](images/119f8b0dc7b1c4cb9b3b55eab3492a5f492860893a2bc4ea41375b0c43035dbc.jpg)  
c World powers 1928 Arbeit und Wirtschaft, 1 August 1928, One figure = 25 million people, rounded off.

![](images/e841d7d06135aa3114250bf59704e23f76dc82f06e9fc16452dba3e0e9ae4fe1.jpg)  
T176b World powers 1928 Die bunte Welt, 1929, p. 29. Each figure = 25 million people.

T176a was drawn by one of the local Viennese artists, Bruno Zuckermann. The pictograms of C were not drawn by Arntz, but do seem to show his influence – the artist couid have been Erwin Bernath or Erich Meixner – although the chart was published in the month before Arntz first came to Vienna. The tendency of the pictograms of the later charts, drawn by Arntz, is towards forms that are more rounded and more equal in weight (see overleaf).

The charts show the expected progression from handwritten script to metal type, capitals to upper and lower case; C shows the calligraphic lettering that features in certain charts made in 1928.

T176c World powers C.1929 Each figure = 25 million people. This exhibition chart presumably employed a variety of colours for national flags.

![](images/66f1d7e36fcb4049dd8e87e029ad20c56fe6383d4ee3923773d4653b5a44b3a9.jpg)  
T265a World powers  
Gesellschaft und Wirtschaft, 193o, no. 23.

Here flags are not included, allowing the form and colour of the pictograms to identify ethnicity and (along with verbal labels) nationality.

![](images/3c60c432b4b26ab6f707c2bf44d04f76b21fd19665971bafa9417ee6d42687f9.jpg)  
T50 Trade unions of the world 1924 c. 1926

![](images/a3ef86d10271e7aa28144acc928904c9e96945c8e5995491c42a5fd76932f37c.jpg)  
T5a Trade unions of the world 1928  
Die Gewerkschaften, 1928, p.3. Each figure = 5 million members.

![](images/f426241bd52c1b98b28380795f8881d0c600165cc052d52810f8ff6129dab1b9.jpg)  
T116a Trade unions of the world 1928 Die bunte Welt, 1929, p.31. Each figure = 5 million members.

![](images/0353da770f9d4fb4d4ca45e1fe4e12c67c4db4638e580bb0f91444f8594d6638.jpg)

T116b Trade unions of the world 1928 C. 1929 Each figure = 2.5 million members.

These charts show essentially the same material; T282 (overleaf) differs in that it compares two sets of data, where the other charts show one. T5o shows a block of unit pictograms, and one pictogram represents 1oo,ooo members. This scale is radically altered in T5a – one pictogram for 5 million members – and the configuration is now that of the 'checker-board'. T116a retains the scale and basic arrangement of T5a, though the 'checker-board' is loosened. T116b doubles the number of pictograms – one pictogram for 2.5 million members – and the sense of a checker-board' pattern has disappeared, for the pictograms are now separate units and no longer contained in cells. T282 is essentially different in configuration from the other charts, being concerned to compare two groups of people.

![](images/df1b8f4ad0265ee12c93a8d7e7c4b95c4aebf2b4796a4fe5eabffa47f24f7e55.jpg)  
T282 Trade unions of the world 1930 Gesellschaft und Wirtschaft 193o, no.83. Each row = 15 million members; each figure = c. 1.5 million members.

The sequence of five charts illustrates the progression from signifying meaning primarily through colour to signification primarily through form; T5o and T116b were exhibition charts and thus potentially less constrained in their use of colour than the other three chars, which were designed for reproduction. In T5o the subgroups are showr by the colour of pictograms and by the lines that bound them. In T5a, the main burden of signification is transferred to the background, and the various devices used there; the need to design for black-and-white reproduction seems to have been the primary factor in this change. T116a, as reproduced in Die bunte Welt, uses colour to distinguish between the four main groups, but the chart is still intelligible in black and white, through the qualifying symbols that are placed over the pictograms. These are not quite incorporated, for groups of two or three pictograms share the same qualifying symbol: this was clearly a convention that could only be used in the rare charts with only very small groups of pictograms to be qualified. T116a also employs the large background symbols (printed in light grey) that feature in some charts of late 1928 or early 1929; these symbols (in this case loosely connected forms suggesting 'machinery' and industrial work') have no strictly qualifying or distinguishing function, but serve only to reinforce the general subject matter of the chart.

The qualifying symbols are properly incorporated in T116b, which also now makes distinctions through the form of the main pictograms. T282 consolidates the principles of incorporation evident in T116b.

The pictograms of τ5o are an attempt at a radically simplified form, such as features in the early chart on method. The heads are circular and aim (according to the chart on method) to function as tokens to be counted. The uniform colour of bodies and the varied colour of heads, implied in this reproduction, suggest that bodies were a constant colour in the chart, while the various colours of the heads did the work of signification. T5a uses pictograms that manifest their scissor-cut origin. T116b shows early pictograms in the Arntz mode, though their style seems not quite to be that of Arntz himself; they could well have been drawn by Augustin Tschinkel (on the evidence of resemblance to work known to be by Tschinkel). T282 shows what one may call a 'mature' human pictogram – though modifications to this form were to continue for some years to come. Comparison of this pictogram with that of T5o shows the contribution of Arntz in achieving Neurath's desire for a 'human' human pictogram – and yet a pictogram with the qualities of form that would allow it to act as a (modular) unit of the 'picture script'.

ZUNAHME DER ARBEITENDEN FRAUEN IM BEKLEIDUNGSGEWERBE

1895 1   
1905   
1910 F 15.702 444 WELTKRIEG   
1915 4   
1920 13 SG3 FRAUEN   
1923 10.210 FRAUEN   
1924 DERHIENERANENSELLUDEN KLEIDEDMACHER

## D Increase in number of women working in the clothing trade 1925

Österreichische Gemeinde-Zeitung, Jg. 2, Nr. 16, 15 August 1925, p. 23.   
Each figure = 1,000 tailors/   
dressmakers.

T5n Men and women in the Viennese clothesmaking trade 1928 Die Gewerkschaften, 1928, p.11 Each figure = 2,00o workers.

T5r Men and women in the Viennese clothesmaking trade 1928 Die bunte Welt, 1929, p.33. Each figure = 2,000 workers.

These charts represent different treatments of the same material. Gender is denoted through colour and form, then tone and form, then purely through form; T5n and T5r were designed for black-and-white reproduction. The caption to the reproduction of D states that (in the original) the men were red and women blue. The convention of showing women through pictograms lighter in colour and tone than those of the men is evident in T5n, and it survives in the pictograms of T5r.

The qualifying symbols (scissors and irons) that denote 'clothes makers' show a progression: women hold irons and men hold scissors; this is then reversed; and in the third version, both hold scissors (though the qualifying symbol is now in apposition to the main pictogram, rather than 'held' by it). There are obvious objections to the first two usages, for they imply that either gender is confined to a particular instrument (and that no other tools are used). This unnecessary confusion is resolved by showing only one of these symbols, the scissors (probably the symbol most characteristic of the trade), and allowing it thus to mean 'clothes making' in general, rather than merely 'cutting' – as it would tend to do when opposed to the iron. No later versions of this chart seem to have been made, but

![](images/72f8c5949106e50560c27d77727659e06a792845a156ccc876e18f4429c1c0f1.jpg)

MÄNNER UND FRAUEN IM WIENER KLEIDERMACHERGEWERBE

1905   
1915   
1925   
Jede figur - 2000 Beschäfhgte abgerunder

one would expect that the idea of clothes making, common to both kinds of symbol, would have come to be shown by a guide-picture or single qualifying symbol removed to the margin of the chart.

T5n adopts the 'shift' configuration, where D had used the simple 'comparison of quantities' configuration. The difference in the titles to the two charts indicates a difference of purpose: D intends to show the increase in women (but the arrangement does not really help one to see this), while T5n presents the numbers of men and of women for comparison. This change of intention and of configuration would seem to constitute an advance towards a clearer, more 'visual' statement.

An obvious progression in the three charts is in the greater selection of data: seven rows are shown at first, then four, then three (perhaps encouraged by a landscape format); also, from T5n, the value of the pictograms is increased (so that fewer pictograms are shown). In T5r the minimum means are used to make the essential statement of this material, that during the war more women were brought into the trade, while after the war men continued to decrease in number, while women maintained the superiority of numbers that they had assumed in the war.

![](images/bbf4e7ef3451fce589956e2cab9edd01c1e2e58c10a180f70a07cbc60be25cbf.jpg)  
GESEILSCHAFTS-UND WRTSOHAFTSMUSEUM IW WIEN  
T5p Free trade unions in Austria since 1904 1925 Each figure = 10,000 people (men |women).

T5f Men and women in free trade unions 1928 Die Gewerkschaften, 1928, p. 9. Relative proportion per 10 members of free trade unions in Austria.

T562 Development of free trade unions in Berlin C.1931 Each figure = 25,000 members.

T5f is based on the material presented in T5p; T562 shows material for Berlin rather than for Austria, but otherwise provides points of comparison with the earlier charts.

As against T5p, T5f shows a radical selection of material: three rows are shown, where there had been twenty-one (with provision for two more). T5f shows percentage figures, where T5p had shown absolute figures, and its configuration is that of the 'shift'. The change in the title emphasizes that the intention of the chart is now to show the changing proportions of men to women members; this is the reason for the use of relative figures and the new configuration. In the light of this changed intention, one cannot infer from its adoption in T5f that the 'shift' configuration was a later development – and there is the example from 1925 of 56h (p. 50o) where the 'shift' is already in use. One can merely suspect, after looking through the body of the Museum's work, that the use of the visually more striking shift was used more readily as time went on. This is certainly the inference one draws from the charts on clothes makers.

![](images/e2c4a9d0e65dd41f30ff27fc31a29c5f92c80fb3bd87ef9d93aaa0b446170524.jpg)

Entwicklung der freien Gewerkschaten in Berlin ADGB   
1.895   
1910   
1915   
1920   
1925   
1930   
jede Figur 25000 Maglieder

Comparing T562 with T5f, the most significant development is in the forms of the pictograms: from manifestly scissor-cut to forms that do not state their method of origination; from asymmetrical to symmetrical; gender is now distinguished purely through form, where colour had been used in T5p and tone in T5f (designed for black-andwhite reproduction); the linked hands of the earlier pictograms (signifying solidarity) are now only suggested by arms drawn so as to form visual connections when placed in rows – this pictogram appears only in charts on trade unions.

One may notice that the pictograms of T562 are marked off in groups of five, though their unit values of 1 = 25,ooo suggest a group of four as more appropriate. T5p is typical of the earliest charts in showing exact numerical information by the rows of pictograms, and in not marking pictograms off.

The earlier versions must have been made in 1929 (the Museum started to show charts in Berlin in March of that year); the later versions seem likely to have been made in 1931 – the strongest evidence for this is the data on the unemployment chart T104b (p. 157 above), evidently (from its style and proximate position in the T files) contemporary with the second versions reproduced here. These charts may be taken as indicating the stages of development of the Museum's work at, say, mid-1929 and mid-1931.

![](images/104bb91095e5b1e250abab27c25e00e47c0a3f1878aa9c618e93ac82132543ad.jpg)

![](images/2e645e6fcdbcac8b20a59b37882a391731c552f49a87352557e36ecadffdb422.jpg)  
Der Berliner Verkehr 1930

![](images/c478308a390a359851ff6217c9ed3168764775cd8a61bdfffe0efda1d917f5ed.jpg)

T98a & T98b Motorcycles and cars in Berlin 1929 & 1931 Each pictogram = 10,ooo vehicles (both charts).

The later chart shows differences both in its material and in the manner in which it treats this material. Thus, as well as showing information for another year, Tg8b, for example, divides the category of 'Automobil' into cars and lorries. Changes are introduced in the pictograms: the paired or doubled pictograms of τg8a are replaced by single pictograms; the car is now shown in a front view, which has the virtue of enabling it (and the lorry) to relate in visual weight and in scale more easily to the motorcycle. A guide-picture is introduced; this signifies 'traffic in Berlin'; no attempt is made to let this image carry the distinction of motorcycle against automobile. The year numerals which had been placed, unconventionally, on the central axis are moved to their usual left margin position.

Jede Figur 100000 Fahrgöste

T97a & T97b Berlin transport 1927 / 1930 1929 & 1931 Each figure = 100,000 travellers (both charts).

The material remains essentially the same in the two charts, but some changes are introduced into its ordering in the later version. The two pictograms for 'Autobus' users are brought down into the second row, with 'Strassenbahn' users; this groups the material more coherently into two categories, of street transport and of transport that runs above or below the street. One assumes that the pictograms of the first version were disposed so as to produce two rows of equal length. In the later version, meaning takes precedence over visual tidiness.

![](images/4b78ce7189381d7d9b930d89cacfd001e7f8b8596a24e25bae1dfb01286772c6.jpg)

![](images/0358c7415f0656ddbc3cf9718f9046665af079e8bc866e30bd77f2cc22c9423a.jpg)

## T100a & T100b Social subdivision of the Berlin population

Workers | [white-collar] workers  people of independent means, unemployed and home help.

T100a – each figure = 12,500 people;

T100b – each figure = 100,000 people ('home help' is moved to join 'workers' in the first category).

In the later chart the social composition of two areas of the city is contrasted, as against the three areas of T1ooa; also, the unit values are changed, so that fewer pictograms are shown in the later chart. The difference in social composition of the two areas in T1oob is stressed visually by the 'shift' configuration. One might repeat the observation made in concluding the commentary on T97a and τ97b: the later version is visually more articulate.

Finally, one may note these changes common to the three later charts. There is a greater control in handling the material: pictograms are ordered more coherently; the size and position of all the elements – pictograms, titles, headings and captions, empty space – are now related to each other more effectively than in the earlier versions. The pictograms now ‘read' better, as modular forms.

Charts on the fate of soldiers in the First World War

![](images/ec09a0dd589fdeb2fc29fe32232ac0a0d95795ef4e8951747394065180098c9c.jpg)  
T314a Warring factions in the World War 1914–18 1928 T314b Fate of soldiers 1914–18 1928  
These two charts form a pair: both show the number of soldiers taking part in the war. One can estimate numbers more easily from T314b, and this chart also indicates the different fates of the soldiers (killed, seriously wounded or 'returning home').

Die bunte Welt, 1929, pp. 26–7.   
Each figure = 1 million soldiers (both charts).

![](images/4698ab4cb77a8ead8550a5ba650ba512cf5bc31ea830ec2f19255ba7a56e218e.jpg)

T314c World War 1914-18 c.1928

Each figure = 1 million soldiers (killed, seriously wounded or returning home).

T314c is an exhibition chart that shows the material of T314b, but takes over the parallelogrammatic and threedimensional aspects of T314a. The peculiar configuration of T314c (and its subsequent versions) within the whole body of the work derives, in the first place, from being a marriage of two preceding charts. If one wishes to trace ancestors further, one must consider T314a. The obvious precedent for the configuration of this chart is outside the Museum's work, in the representations of battles that are, at the same time, maps showing locations and quantifications of fighting strength. These are prominent in the visual tradition in which Neurath saw the work of his team as belonging (see overleaf). T314c shows large qualifying symbols as background elements – a feature of the work around 1928 – though the gun, tank and aeroplane do not 'qualify' or signify in any strictly informative sense (and might be misleading).

![](images/027671db1c5b7246137157407d131b57f2411d4a9679fed514b075ca92926e8b.jpg)

![](images/cc554540a2ab5e860d9281044e293527654dee36da6aa9729b2708e44b32f483.jpg)  
T893 & T894 World War 1914–18 1932

Each figure = 1 million soldiers (killed, seriously wounded -- or returning home).

These charts are similar to each other in essential respects; they vary in format and thus in the disposition of elements within the chart. τ893 may be regarded as a condensed version of τ8g4, the square form of which suggests that it was made for lantern slide reproduction. T893 carries the signature 'Mundaneum Wien' – one of the few German-lanquage charts to do so – but its place of exhibition is not known.

These charts show the material that forms the basis of T314c. The gain in clarity over the earlier version is achieved through the new pictograms, through improved ordering of the qualifying symbols and typographic elements, and through their plain white background. One may note that the two sides are signified through differences of uniform – even the graves carry different helmets. These symbols for the dead are, of course, based on the special war graves; but it is perhaps not entirely fanciful to see them as also referring to the idea of helmeted skeletons This is a good example of the imagination and care for detail that went into the design of pictograms in the mature period.

![](images/014f8273a2e1154988ba967ffafe098d10a2785c0f2baaf44e907436b1b6c088.jpg)  
T986 Great War 1914-18 1933

Made for the London Mundaneum (IC 7.2/16). 42 × 63 cm.

This chart was shown at the London branch of the Mundaneum. It shows a new pictogram for the wounded: the coat is now white allowing the crutch to be shown as solid rather than outline. This new form for the crutch is more faithful to reality (objects are solids not outlines) and it means that the crutch now achieves visual equality with the rifle. Also one may read the white coat as further signification of 'being seriously wounded', for it hints at the white shirt worn when receiving medical treatment. And the new pictogram allows one more easily to pick out the wounded, as a middle term between the solid living and the skeletal presence of the dead. In these ways the essential meaning of 'being seriously wounded', which was quite adequately signified by the pictogram of T893 and τ894, is enhanced.

![](images/ef741e5c3e24a573cc6b64924ffe1f446294edd81c413df9890e006ea85cb66f.jpg)

Carlo Cappi, Abriss des Posto zue Bressnitz From Matthäus Merian, Theatrum Europaeum (c.165o). (IC 9.5/21a)

Detail of a battle plan collected as part of the Archive of Pictorial Education at the Gesellschafts- und Wirtschaftsmuseum. Such material provided reference for the design of Isotype charts.

## Summary

## 1925

This is the period of 'first steps'. Principles for ordering or configuring material are already quite developed; though material is not yet always ordered in the most visually effective form – rows are presented for simple comparison, where later a 'shift' configuration would be used.

In this summary the period described is broken down into stages. These do not, of course, correspond to exact calendar years: the year designations are no more than approxımate markers in the day-to-day continuum.

Graphic aspects of the work are quite undeveloped and primitive. Pictograms are comparatively realistic; the materials of pen and ink provide little encouragement for the drawing of abstracted forms. Colours are used without much sense of order or constraint.

Charts of this period are noticeably influenced by the conventions of non-pictorial graphic statistics; for example, in their inclusion of exact numerical information and in employing elements of the bar graph.

## 1926

This is a time of rapid development, stimulated notably by the task of making charts for the 'Gesolei' exhibition in the summer of this year. Most exhibition charts now use elements cut from paper; this imposes considerable constraints on form. Simplification of form is taken to an extreme in a few special charts in which human pictograms have circular counters for heads. After the realistic extreme of the first year and the abstract extreme of the second, subsequent forms will attempt to find a satisfactory intermediate position

The 'Gesolei' charts use colour, in pictograms and in backgrounds to pictograms, as principle means of signification; but after this point formal devices are employed instead. This change of method is caused by a growing awareness of the need to find ways of showing that can be understood in black-and-white reproduction,without special adjustment.

## 1927

Charts of this period consolidate the achievements of 1926. The guide-pictures become more evident and develop their function of making distinctions within the body of the chart. The use of background colour or form comes to be replaced by the use of secondary symbols that qualify the main pictograms.

## 1928

There are signs of an increased pace of development and experinent in the earlier part of the year; this would be stimulated by the preparation of charts for publications, culminating in the Museum's first book, Die bunte Welt, published early in 1929.

Some charts show pictograms reversed out of rectangular elements. This is a device that achieves modular forms by a short cut: the signifying forms themselves are not modular and are relatively undeveloped. The aim of finding modular forms is certainly apparent, but the means of achieving them has not yet been found. Another experiment in charts made in the second half of the year is the use of large background symbols with some of the functions of the guide-pictures. The anomalous vertical arrangement of pictograms in the births and deaths charts changes in this year. This is perhaps a sign of a greater consciousness of the work as being informed by a set of principles, some of which can be made explicit. The use of the monogram of the initials of the Gesellschafts- und Wirtschaftsmuseum on charts and publications may be regarded as another sign of self-consciousness.

## 1928-9

This is the period of the breakthrough, in which the means (principally in the person of Gerd Arntz) were found to realize Otto Neurath's intentions. Through Arntz's talent for drawing abstracted but subtle forms, the pictograms could now become genuinely modular. His introduction of the practice of cutting pictograms in linoleum enables these forms to be retained in reproduction more easily than scissor-cutting would have allowed – and linocutting makes possible easy multiplication of pictograms. Ease of reproduction and of multiplication allows pictograms to be treated much more as units, and, as a consequence, fractions of pictograms appear more frequently, and so does the practice of marking pictograms off into small groups. Qualifying symbols become incorporated into main pictograms: another practice made possible by the new modular forms. Another aspect of the breakthrough of this period is the introduction of printing with metal type for the verbal matter of charts, and, coincidentally, a policy of ranging rather than centring any verbal matter.

## 1929-30

In these years the work begins to achieve maturity, when the practices associated with the breakthrough have been assimilated. It is the period of work on Gesellschaft und Wirtschaft; this commission allows and necessitates an expansion of the working team which, in turn, brings a new awareness of the need for coordination and consistency. Thus the guideline notes come to be written. This is also the point at which the T and N files start to be compiled: another sign of greater organization within the Museum.

## From 1930

A way of working graphically is now formed in its essentials; it will be refined and it will expand, but the approach to ordering material in graphic form has been established.

## Original exhibition charts 1933

The following pages show thirteen of the small number of exhibition charts made at the Gesellschafts- und Wirtschaftsmuseum to have survived. This series was made under the auspices of the Mundaneum for exhibition at its London branch, established in 1933. The Mundaneum had a correspondence address in London at the World Association for Adult Education, although it is not clear that these charts were exhibited there. In The Labour Magazine (May 1933), Brenda Voysey mentioned a small exhibition of Mundaneum work then on display at the bookshop of Bumpus Ltd, London. A sign-board prepared in Vienna (right) may relate to this installation. Subsequently these charts were kept at the office of the agent Kathleen Boland, from whom they were reacquired by the Neuraths in 1941. (CB)
