# 3 The atlas Gesellschaft und Wirtschaft (Society and economy) 1930

Christopher Burke

Otto Neurath described Gesellschaft und Wirtschaft as merely the first part of his grand project for an illustrated encyclopedia or ‘visual thesaurus'. The project had germinated around 192o with his idea for a series of indexed pamphlets, and Neurath received encouragement from Albert Einstein, who wrote to him that 'such a well-planned collection would serve the same function for the masses today as did the French Encyclopedia for the intellectual groups in France during the eighteenth century.'¹

Neurath recognized an intent similar to his own behind Paul Otlet's plan for a 6o-volume Atlas of civilization', and the Gesellschafts- und Wirtschaftsmuseum formed a partnership with Otlet's Palais Mondial (Brussels) in order to realize it. However, Neurath lamented that it was not possible to accomplish any concrete work, and the idea of a new Orbis Pictus, of a Visual Thesaurus remained, and still remains an idea, a plan for the future'.²

Gesellschaft und Wirtschaft was initially unconnected to the collaboration with Otlet; the commission came instead from the Bibliographisches Institut of Leipzig, publisher of the well-known encyclopedia, Meyers Konversationslexikon, as well as of Duden's standard dictionary of German orthography. To commemorate its hundredth anniversary, the Institut asked the Vienna Museum to produce a special publication, funded by an advance payment of 45,ooo DM.³ As Neurath later recalled, this

Otlet and informed Neurath that Otlet's project 'seems to connect with your plan, or at least could connect with it. ... The basic idea is even more ambitious than your own.' After Neurath had visited Otlet's museum, Kaempffert commented: 'I believe you are right when you say that Otlet is more concerned with systematic collecting than with educational design.'

Letters Kaempffert to Neurath, 27 March & 19 June 1929 (GwM-MSt). 3. Neurath, 'Suggestions for adult education by Isotype' (typescript December 1943; IC 1/31), p. 4i Neurath estimated that this amount was then equivalent to £3,ooo. Gesellschaft und Wirtschaft was listed for sale in the uk in 1933 for a price of £4 and 4 shillings, roughly equivalent to £245 in 2013.

N959

Otto Neurath (on the right) towering over the Mayor of Vienna, Karl Seitz, as he shows him and other guests around the exhibition 'Weltwirtschaft', which included charts published in Gesellschaft und Wirtschaft. These exhibition rooms were housed in the Am Fuchsenfeld apartment complex, Vienna. October 1930.

4. Letter Neurath to Patrick Meredith, 15 May 1942 (IC 1/35). See p. 529 below for a list of the collaborators. In fact, work on the atlas lasted for more than a year: some preparatory material dates from April 1929, and it was published in autumn 1930.

5. Bildstatistik nach Wiener Methode in der Schule (1933), p. 268.

6. A prior plan for an encyclopedic publication was announced around the turn of 1g28 in Bildstatistik (the printed exhibition guide for the Museum) with the title 'Das Zahlenbild', to be published by Schlüter & Co. (Leipzig) and the Dürerbund. It was described as a 'statistical work of charts and text, in grand style and with comprehensive content'; and 'a means of self-education for all circles of the population', as well as teaching material for schools. Its projected sections were listed as: 'world and earth / earth and life / humanity / society and economy [Gesellschaft und Wirtschaft] / profession and class / production and consumption / Germany and Austria / Vienna / German cities / epidemics [Volksseuchen] / tuberculosis / sport'. These followed the subjects already developed by the Museum for its exhibitions.

7. Ihara gives details of changes made to the content of Gesellschaft und Wirtschaft during its preparation in ‘Otto Neurath's atlas "Society and economy" '.

![](images/ea9f3256d9e120fd5a55f5bc8006337ab81336f2d74771add0a5aec3cb1a1f3e.jpg)

enabled work for ‘one year [by] a whole staff of scientific and graphic collaborators.'⁴ During the preparation of Gesellschaft und Wirtschaft Neurath proposed to align it with his and Otlet's larger publishing plan, but the Bibliographisches Institut would not commit to this. A few years later Neurath gave the evident reason that the difficult economic conditions have made it impossible to gather a group of publishers or institutes for this plan'.⁵

Gesellschaft und Wirtschaft was the most extensive publishing project undertaken at the Gesellschafts- und Wirtschaftsmuseum in Wien. Despite the name of the Museum being reflected in its title, the publication was not financed by the Vienna municipality and its scope extended beyond the themes of previous exhibition work. The team in Vienna was allowed great freedom in determining the content of the atlas as well as in designing it.' Nevertheless, the pictorial plates in Gesellschaft und Wirtschaft were produced in the Museum's customary way, as hand-crafted exhibition charts, and these were used as artwork for reproduction in the atlas at half the original dimensions. They were printed lithographically: some are monochrome but others feature up to eight separate colours of ink. The original charts were exhibited at the Museum's Mundaneum' branch to coincide with publication.

![](images/206a871dc8b2448355e6e8043601e80fbd8344bab1af78fd5212f835d8d88f2d.jpg)  
N957 Visitors to the exhibition 'Weltwirtschaft', 1930.

(Below) Original exhibition charts that were used as artwork for reproduction in Gesellschaft und Wirtschaft (nos.7 & 8). The flat colours were painted with gouache and place names on the maps were lettered by hand. Separation of these elements for printed reproduction by lithography would have been a challenging process.

![](images/7e7e0f3de527471b0f87a141fd452f2bdd6e7cfe4c6cf901cebc20f541300be1.jpg)

N958b Otto Neurath showing a copy of the newly published Gesellschaft und Wirtschaft to Karl Seitz at the opening of the 'Weltwirtschaft' exhibition. October 1930.

![](images/54f9aceb7bb5defe30decff559dd99d0582dba9f9b27c0f54ba85892a4623a3e.jpg)

## GESELLSCHAFT UND WIRTSCHAFT Bildstatistisches Elementarwork

PRODUKTIONSFORMEN GESELLSCHAFTSORDNUNGEN KULTURSTUFEN LEBENSHALTUNGEN

The enclosing folder of Gesellschaft und Wirtschaft opened to reveal the title-page.

![](images/05c465e5b8d62a08854930c24852f3a379c3ae85eca2166aef105e6b6b24c5ef.jpg)

Sketches of pictograms made on tracing paper in preparation for Gésellschaft und Wirtschaft. Perhaps the work of Gerd Arntz, although notes are written in several hands.

The forms here can be compared with the pictograms finally used in the publication: at top left are two sketches for 'raw steel' (iron) – see chart no. 52 (p. 21o below); top centre is a sketch for hydro-electric power (with red bolt) – see chart no. 51 (p. 2og below).

Certain themes and modes of presentation were developed from Die bunte Welt but Gesellschaft und Wirtschaft displays a significant advance in the creation of a consistent visual style: in particular, the design of pictograms benefited from the presence of Arntz as a full-time employee at the Museum and from the appointment of Peter Alma and Augustin Tschinkel to assist him. Valuable contributions were also made by members of the so-called academy' of specialist advisers assembled for compiling the publication. Among them was the cartographer Karl Peucker, a German who taught at the Hochschule für Welthandel (College of world trade) in Vienna. He was a prolific theoretician of map making: the use of colour on maps was of particular concern to him, and Neurath wrote with approval about Peucker's favouring of physiological colours’.8 Much attention was also given to the variety of projections used for the maps that were integral to many charts in Gesellschaft und Wirtschaft (see overleaf). A note on the atlas's cartographic principles explained that equal-area projections were preferred to Mercator's famous projection, which distorts the relative size of land masses. Neurath took inspiration from accuracy in map-making for the design of Isotype in general, and the aim of minimizing distortion in two-dimensional, geographical representation can be aligned with the basic rules of the Vienna Method (non-perspective depiction and consistent pictogram size).

8. From hieroglyphics to Isotype, p.107.

## (Above)

## Cartographical overview

Gesellschaft und Wirtschaft, 193o, no.100.

A visual summary of map projections used in the atlas. The equal-area planisphere used in charts showing the whole world is based on that developed by Max Eckert in 19o6 (specifically his pseudocylindrical projection known as 'Eckert vi'). In comparison with a Mercator projection, Europe appears smaller in relation to Africa or South America on this planisphere.

![](images/d741d9f0ca958447a3b6f8028d446ff7a85ac006d8455504574078d79f7d3f42.jpg)

## (Above & right)

Neurath asked cartographer Karl Peucker to make a sketch showing the distortion of area in the Mercator projection (ic 3.1/8). This was then adapted at the Museum into a diagram with a human figure overlaid, in order to convey the degree of distortion more effectively (IC 3.1/27).

![](images/19a2d0db9777eadde76fbe29cc1d0381638e317173bfc2008b9a484d2e3ec3a1.jpg)

Mongolenreich nach 1250: Städte und Strassen

Mongol empire after 1250: cities and streets Gesellschaft und Wirtschaft, 193o, no.9. Drawing by Karl Peucker (top; IC 3.1/27) and the final chart.

The Vienna Museum periodical Fernunterricht stated that this chart 'shows what a developed administration this powerful empire had at its disposal.' (Jg. 1, H.3, p. 13.)

Gesellschaft und Wirtschaft, 193o, no.79.   
Yellow = areas with significant distribution of slavery;

Each figure = 500,000 slaves.

Alois Fischer's drawing for the chart (IC 3.1/32) was closely followed in the published atlas.

9. Hickmann's atlas had been published since the 18gos in Vienna by map-makers Freytag und Berndt (see images on p. 13 above). The same publisher issued Fischer's Geographisch-statistisches Handbüchlein (1935).

10. Statistical tables that he compiled as early as September 1928 fed directly into the published charts (IC 3.1/18).

Another member of Neurath's ‘academy' was the statistician Alois Fischer, who had worked with the Museum since 1927. Fischer represents continuity with a longer tradition of Viennese pictorial statistics: from 1921 he expanded Prof. A. L. Hickmanns geographisch-statistischer Universal-Atlas, which the Vienna Method defined itself against to a certain extent; and, after the Museum was closed, he compiled statistical pocket-books bearing a strong resemblance to Isotype work.’ For Gesellschaft und Wirtschaft Fischer did extensive historical and statistical research, and even made basic transformations into graphic form. 10 Several of his detailed map drawings were faithfully adapted as charts in the final publication.

![](images/298868854c953c9f6185dd87c8ec5d510839080dfba5dbac1f90168b9c61b0d2.jpg)

## Verbreitung der Sklaverei in der Gegenwart

![](images/596305d4acd7afb547576592ecfc5a953534c82e4b6cf645e4611ee9a8dd1a41.jpg)

![](images/9e96b1e9e3f4bb989a93e2778c3a602e340b81361497c8a6f0e13ae3acfa8b1b.jpg)

![](images/f4ab660c5ab2b48dbeef698e7c339e559961f77c022dee5bf7ace0609f2bb820.jpg)

![](images/753c54872eee0ce1b898329a2b50490e1ba7afb4a65dabf0095665e02a647dd9.jpg)

The process of transformation shown in sketches for the chart about 'Distribution of religions' (iC 3.1/33). The first sketch, with notes on the map, is by Alois Fischer; the second and third are by Marie Reidemeister. Initially she attemped to colour code the religions with a key, and then (on the third sheet) she sketched pictograms with qualifying symbols for religion on them. It was evidently decided that placing these variegated pictograms on a map would make the configuration too complex, and in the final chart (Gesellschaft und Wirtschaft, no.95, shown on p. 214 below) the map was dispensed with in favour of non-cartographical distribution by geographical area.

The pre-printed map used here for sketches is like that used in Die bunte Welt, more schematized than the one finally used in Gesellschaft und Wirtschaft.

## Contemporary reactions to Gesellschaft und Wirtschaft

Gesellschaft und Wirtschaft provided the first opportunity for comprehensive results of the Vienna Method to be seen widely outside of Austria, due to its distribution by a major German publisher. In one of the earliest articles published in English about Isotype, Jan Tschichold praised the admirably-drawn' plates of the atlas and the method behind it." The German journalist & satirist Kurt Tucholsky welcomed the publication in his regular book-review column for Die Weltbühne as ‘a masterpiece of educational statistics', which should be acquired by popular libraries and educational institutions (although he reserved suspicion, 'not towards the producers of this atlas, who honestly give their sources, but towards statistics itself, that sweet form of lies'.)1² Serious criticism was made by Vladimir Woytinsky, a veteran of two Russian revolutions and former editor of Izvestia, who was then working in Germany as a political economist. His own, multi-volume work of statistics, Die Welt in Zahlen (The world in figures, 1925–8), had been criticized by Friedrich Bauermeister of the Vienna Museum for its unsystematic attempts at visualizing statistics. 13

![](images/5e42419ec7017f5061ccd1b4c01c77e490e2e9e4a8c9d644fe8af19ecf7158be.jpg)

Pages from Vladimir [W. L.] Woytinsky, Die Welt in Zahlen, book 1 of 7, subtitled 'The earth / population / national wealth [Volksreichtum] (Berlin: Rudolf Mosse, 1925).

In his review of this book, Friedrich Bauermeister remarked on the disadvantages of pie charts (see note 13).

11. Tschichold, 'Statistics in pictures: a new method of presenting facts', p. 114. (See p. 78 above on Tschichold's connection with the Vienna Museum.) Perhaps the first lengthy consideration of the Vienna Method in English was Glynn Faithfull's review of Gesellschaft

und Wirtschaft titled 'Picturestatistics', in The New Era in Home and School, vol. 12, August 1931, pp, 280-1.

12. Tucholsky (writing as Peter Panter), 'Auf dem Nachttisch' (March 1931) in Gesammelte Werke, Band 9 (Reinbek: Rowohlt, 1975), p. 144.

13. Book review in Das Bild, Jg. 4, H. 6, June 1927, p. 120. Neurath had recognized political kinship with Woytinsky (a Social Democrat) in his essay on 'Statistik und Proletariat' (1927), p. 84. From 1929 Woytinsky was director of the statistical department of the

Allgemeine Deutsche Gewerkschaftsbund (German federation of trade unions) and between 1933 and 1935 he worked at the International Labour Organization, Geneva. See his autobiography Stormy passage (New York: Vanguard Press, 1961).

Woytinsky made fundamental objections to the Vienna Method, and even questioned the legitimacy of calling it a 'method':

I need not address here the question of whether a technical approach [Handgriff] to statistical visualization should be described as a method'. The Vienna Museum has certainly created a style; what harm can it do to name this style a method'! Its misfortune, however, is that the Museum direction believes it can apply one technical approach in a unified way to reproduce a complete range of statistics. This is an error: the search for a universal philosopher's stone' of pictorial statistics contradicts the nature of statistical method. A universal means of visualization would perhaps be possible if, as the Vienna Museum management seem to believe, statistics always concern a greater or lesser number of objects. Statistics operate with all kinds of quantities.¹⁴

Neurath recognized that the style of the Vienna Method was to a great extent determined by objects' – this was a principle behind its iconicity.¹⁵ He described the Vienna Museum's statistical charts as 'representations of objects from the sociological collection'. And he would not have seen it as a fault that this partly determined the choice of subject matter that could be addressed, as Woytinsky did. Neurath admitted: Not every body of figures is suitable for visualization.'¹⁶ Towards the end of his life he conceded: Some correlations are even distorted and falsified by being pictorially represented.'¹7

In addition to the repetition of pictograms to indicate quantity, Woytinsky identified as principles of the Vienna Method that it created ‘statistics without figures' and that it exercised a great degree of rounding off on the original data. Again, these were features readily recognized by Neurath: ‘Any vivid representation, even if it is defective, attracts the attention more and stays better in the memory than a table of figures.'¹⁸ In September 1931, some six months after Woytinsky's review was published, an essay by Neurath titled Bildstatistik nach Wiener Methode’ (Pictorial statistics by the Vienna Method) appeared in the educational journal, Die Volksschule, in which he seems to specifically address Woytinsky's objections. Giving chart no. 96 of Gesellschaft und Wirtschaft – ‘Völkergruppen der Erde' (Human groups of the world) – as an example, Neurath explained:

In the pictorial chart each human figure represents 1oo million people. Many statisticians are appalled by such rough rounding off, which can only graphically accommodate quarter figures (25 million), when necessary. Even 13 million can be rounded up and represented by a quarter figure, albeit reluctantly. Groups of fewer people, of 1o or 5 million, fall

14. WI.[adimir] Woytinsky, 'Schriftenübersicht: Gesellschaft und Wirtschaft“ ', Die Arbeit, Jg. 8, H.2, 1931, p. 159.

15. 'Form und Farbe der Mengenbilder des Gesellschafts- und Wirtschaftmuseums' (1929), p. 193.

16. 'Bildstatistik nach Wiener Methode' (1931), p. 185.

17 From hieroglyphics to Isotype, p.10g.

18. 'Orientierendes Diagramm und sprechendes Merkbild' (1927), p.185

![](images/52e1c02504e57804bb504d603eca5715337292e70aecc4a3ca402d9e47c85f28.jpg)  
Human groups of the world Gesellschaft und Wirtschaft, 193o, no.96, (See p. 214 for larger image.)

19. 'Bildstatistik nach Wiener Methode' (1931), pp. 184–5 (italics in original). Indeed Neurath preempted Woytinsky's critique in the article 'Abrunden und Weglassen in der Bildstatistik' (Rounding off and omission in pictorial statistics; 1928). His complaints are so directly anticipated there that it seems probable there was some exchange of views around that time. (Indeed an article by Woytinsky is interspersed with others by staff of the Vienna Museum in an issue of Kulturwille subtitled 'statistics and the class struggle' – Jg. 4, Nr. 9, September 1927.) These points were taken up again by Neurath in his major text of 1933, Bildstatistik nach Wiener Methode in der Schule (1933), p. 286: 'Sometimes one must abandon the common rules for rounding off in order to bring out the decisive proportions. ... quantity-pictures in the Vienna Method stick well in the memory and so remain forever at the disposal of somebody who has once seen them.'

20. Woytinsky, 'Schriftenübersicht: Gesellschaft und Wirtschaft“, pp.159-61.

entirely out of the picture. These critical adherents of precision forget that there is also in cartography a simplification corresponding to the scale; only bad cartographers reduce proportionally – a good cartographer, as an educator, does not reduce automatically but meaningfully [sinngemäß]. These stern critics forget that the average person continually makes errors of much larger dimensions. On the other hand we representatives of the Vienna Method' take the position: To remember simplified pictures is better than to forget accurate figures.

We have always indicated with all frankness that there can be difficulties in rounding off. Every experienced educator can bring to mind the tricks that may be used to overcome such difficulties. For it is clear that the 'Vienna Method' is, unlike the usual graphic methods, not a machine into which one throws sequences of figures in order to get quantitative pictures. The 'Vienna Method' requires creative [gestaltende], educational work.

Educational effects are achieved by simplification, by highlighting. He who knows best what to leave out, is the best teacher.1º

Woytinsky considered this approach unsuitable for modern, popular statistics: Figures are the language and soul of statistics. ... Whoever feels that figures are something abominable, and is ashamed to write them down, must seek an occupation outside statistics.'²º Although the team at the Vienna Museum included statisticians (most notably Alois Fischer), its central task was to transform statistics into 'pictorial education', as Neurath began to call it from 193o.2¹ The key concept of transformation' began to be clarified in writings by Neurath and Marie Reidemeister from 1932, perhaps partly in response to criticism such as Woytinsky's. (See also pp. 63-4 above.)

Some of Woytinsky's points were consciously taken up by Benedikt Kautsky in his critical review of Gesellschaft und Wirtschaft for the Berlin journal Die Gesellschaft. Kautsky's comments came from closer to home: he was secretary of the Wiener Arbeiterkammer, which had given financial support to the Gesellschafts- und Wirtschaftsmuseum from its beginning.2² In Kautsky's opinion, Neurath's assertion of 'educational reasons' for unorthodox rounding of figures was unjustifed and turned the common methods of arithmetic learned in school ‘upside down'. He suggested that the nature of the Vienna Method forced this approach: the specific value assigned to a pictogram was completely unelastic', and the use of halved or quartered symbols was a violation of the Method's own principles. (While Kautsky realized that Isotype pictograms were not naturalistic pictures, he was troubled when human figures placed on a world map spilled off a land mass into the ocean.)

Kautsky believed there to be a wider public mistrust of statistics than Neurath realized, and he warned that Gesellschaft und Wirtschaft could give more cause for such mistrust. He considered pictures to be a diversion that provided only a vague retinal residue' of the actual statistics:

But statistics are not to be separated from numbers, and graphic presentation should do nothing else than facilitate our way to them; Neurath, however, wants to replace numbers with pictures and this puts him on the wrong track.

While Kautsky agreed with Neurath that statistics were the basis for 'clear-eyed politics' and planned economy, he did not share Neurath's faith in pictorial statistics as a tool of socialist education: 'We Marxists - who strive to explain the diversity of the present environment and the past – have so much more cause not to be satisfied with a few examples of coarsely rounded-off data. That would amount to proclaiming the triumph of the slogan.'²³

Another contemporary critic of Gesellschaft und Wirtschaft was the influential French historian Lucien Febvre. He wrote a short review in the journal Annales d’histoire economique et sociale, which he had co-founded and which gave its name to the school of French historical thought. Febvre, who went on to co-write the standard text on the history of the book L’apparition du livre, was naturally sensitive to the artefactual qualities of Gesellschaft und Wirtschaft:

Now, the work is seductive at first glance; it is even luxurious, with its beautiful plates painstakingly printed in several colours, the minute care brought to the details, the incontestable resources of a material execution that is almost without fault. And certain plates testify to an evident ingenuity. But when, after having examined each in turn, one puts them back in their case, it is difficult not to feel a question come to one's lips: all this great effort, all this care, all this luxury, are they really justified?

Febvre recognized that some charts (such as no. 37, overleaf) required a significant degree of reading and calculation to appreciate their full meaning, and he found this difficult to balance with the eloquent figurative depiction [figuration parlante]'. He wondered whether the graphic means could not be 'economized'. Also, he felt that the charts 'in the atlas of Mr Neurath sing a resounding hymn – a bit too resounding – to quantity':

In the universe, they only want to see masses – massive masses, if I dare say. Underneath so many millions of tons, underneath so many millions

23. Kautsky, 'Statistik in Bildern?', Die Gesellschaft: internationale Revue für Sozialismus und Politik, Jg. 9, 1932, pp. 170–4.

# Brotgetreide- und Reiswirtschaft der Erde

![](images/27d7b3734ca4871fb40cbb909321c521f605d7ba0fae432f0dc1ef8e12347b97.jpg)

## Grain and rice economy of the world

Gesellschaft und Wirtschaft, 1930, no.37.

Each sheaf = 2.5 million tons produced and consumed domestically;

each sheaf on solid boat = 2.5 million tons produced and exported;

each sheaf on hollow boat = 2.5 million tons produced and exported.

24. Febvre, 'Un album de statistique figurée', Annales, vol. 3, nº.12, 1931, pp. 587-90.

25. It is explained in the notes to Gesellschaft und Wirtschaft that its charts contained no geographical maps, but exclusively Kartogrammen (p. 103). By this was meant that the maps served as carriers of pictorial statistics or other schematic representations. (The current English definition of cartogram is different, denoting a map which indicates relative quantities by distortion of area.)

of inhabitants, production itself does not count; a state or a city are nothing. No doubt. From a certain point of view. But all the same, those people who are nothing, or who have nothing very special in this sense - if they are added together, they end up making quite a mass in the world; and a mass which perhaps (even from the narrowest point of view of the economy) has the right, and the duty, to make its voice known?²⁴

Febvre's objection to the flattening of local detail is telling, as he was a pioneer of a historical approach that took geographical context into account. Such matters also interested Neurath and his colleagues but it was impossible in the Vienna Method to show local details when indicating quantities on a map of the whole world, at least at the scale of the charts in Gesellschaft und Wirtschaft.2⁵ Neurath stated in his introduction: 'In order to make the essentials stick in the memory, many details must be omitted.' The decision about what is ‘essential' was naturally open to question; Neurath believed that selection and simplification were legitimate educational strategies for providing encyclopedic information that could help individuals to find their voice.

Both Woytinsky and Febvre suggested that Gesellschaft und Wirtschaft could be more suitable for children than for grown-ups. Undoubtedly it was not aimed primarily at sophisticated readers such as them, as Neurath made clear:

Pictorial education provides tools that make it possible for the less educated to gain a surer orientation in a short time than the highly educated usually ever reach, in spite of more exact study. The fully educated person, precisely through his word-education, is less suited to learn by pictorial education, so that the Vienna Method is more difficult to access for him than for thé worker who predominantly soaks things up with his eyes. In practical life, however, a reliable, rough orientation, such as pictorial education provides, is often much more important than more detailed knowledge that is not mastered with complete assurance. This mastery only comes to a small number of specialists.

He added that the Vienna Method could be a 'bridge' between illiterate people and the rest of the world, and asserted that ‘all these problems of social education are also problems of school education'.²⁶

Since 1927 the Gesellschafts- und Wirtschaftsmuseum had cultivated links with schoolchildren and teachers. In its regular supplement to the educational journal Das Bild, the Museum stated that its work in pictorial statistics was a part of Viennese school reform'.²7 The subtitle of Gesellschaft und Wirtschaft – bildstatistisches Elementarwerk (elementary work in pictorial statistics) – implied that children were intended to be at least part of its audience. It made a conscious link to the earlier Elementarwerk of 1774, a nine-volume work by Johann Bernhard Basedow, featuring numerous copper-engraved illustrations by Daniel Chodowiecki.2⁸ Neurath connected Gesellschaft und Wirtschaft explicitly with another standard schoolbook by commenting that the atlas was intended as the beginning of a new Orbis sensualium pictus in the spirit of Comenius. He was critical of the images in both of these publications, and he stated how Gesellschaft und Wirtschaft was different in his introduction to it:

Unity of conceptual representation requires unity of pictorial reproduction. Each chart can be compared with the others, and by comparison new connections become apparent that could not be shown on a single chart. The design of each individual chart always takes the following principle into account: where the same object is represented, the same symbol is used, essential divergences in form mean divergences in content.

Neurath also stressed that it gave a colourful picture of human civilization and its development'.²ª

The nature of the atlas as a collection of unbound, loose leaves enabled it to be used as a kind of portable exhibition: multiple charts could be displayed simultaneously. There are several mini-series within the 10o charts that suggest such a strategy (see pp. 207–15 below).

26. Bildstatistik nach Wiener Methode in der Schule (1933), p. 272.

27. 'Internationale Werkbund-Ausstellung "Die neue Zeit" Köln 1932’, Das Bild, Jg. 6, H. 12, December 1929, p. 234.

28. The editor of Die literarische Welt, Willy Haas, related Gesellschaft und Wirtschaft positively to Basedow's Elementarwerk; his comments (from 193o) are cited in International picture language, pp. 112-14.

29. 'Gesellschaft und Wirtschaft' (193o), p. 144. Neurath's criticism of the pictures in Orbis sensualium pictus and Basedow's Elementarwerk, from the standpoint of children's education, are contained in 'Visual education' (1945), pp. 278–9 When writing to a colleague about ongoing plans to develop an encyclopedia from Gesellschaft und Wirtschaft, Neurath proposed 'a real Orbis pictus, a fully modern Basedow, with the difference that the non-naturalistic pictures directly serve intellectual enlightenment'. (Letter to Hans Stein, 18 March 1936; IISH 865/30.)

![](images/2e83c84cc0a442efa80f4e60d22239133b617f7050fb786cb5493eb28dc6e7e8.jpg)  
Vegetation zones of the earth  
Gesellschaft und Wirtschaft, 193o, no.33.

Gesellschaft und Wirtschaft was used in this way as part of trials made with pictorial statistics in schools in Austria and Germany. Reports of these trials provide valuable information about the use and reception of the publication. Vera Stenzel-Deutsch, a teacher at a Viennese secondary school, reported the following:

My experiences were with a form of 11–12 year old girls of low intelligence. Though officially the curriculum is the same as for the grammar schools, I have to restrict myself to a bare minimum if I want to have any lasting effect at all. The difficulty with such children is first to arouse their attention and comprehension, then to impress anything into their memory.

In geography the curriculum for this age group is Africa and Asia: for history – scenes from Greek, Roman and medieval life.

I used charts from Gesellschaft und Wirtschaft for the first time when I had dealt with Africa and wanted to summarize. I used chart 33, 'Vegetation zones of the earth', and started with the colour explanations concealed. With the help of the children I enlarged part of this chart to the scale of our wall map of Africa, and now it gave great pleasure to recognize the various areas and to compare them with each other. The next chart was no. 22, 'States and population 193o' [see p. 207 below]. The children themselves related density of population with the types of vegetation and wondered why the Gulf of Guinea was more densely populated than other areas. So we were led to talk about cocoa and

palm oil and of the position of Africa in world trade. Up to then the children had looked with pleasure at the economic map included in their atlas and had derived many erroneous ideas. But when numbers were added to words, the whole book knowledge came alive.

As soon as things are put before children in simple symbols and numbers they stimulate imitation. The rather laborious and inadequate copies of maps are replaced by diagrams, and this active participation furthered the grasping and remembering of facts greatly. In the course of time we have made our own independent table summarizing things referring to Africa and have tried the same approach for Austria with data we got from the Vienna Fair.

According to the children's view learning has become easier and more attractive. 'Why did we not learn like this always? It is so much easier to remember.' They asked for the same method when dealing with Asia. So we have at the side of the usual map of Asia a sketch of the outlines in the same scale, on which vegetation and production are drawn. I cannot imagine any longer how to teach geography without such diagrams. They are my greatest support not only in the summary but also at the initial and all later stages of teaching the subject.³º

Such an experience encouraged Neurath's later view that Isotype 'may be used only in addition to the usual educational technique, but there is also a possibility of putting the Isotype technique in the centre and building up the educational process around this backbone, without giving the visual material too much space'.³1

Other teachers, while generally positive about the value of pictorial statistics in school, had some reservations: a teacher of arithmetic and geometry shared Woytinsky's view about excessive rounding off of numerical data; a teacher of geography and history considered Gesellschaft und Wirtschaft not specifically suitable for the classroom because some charts were very complex, and suggested that the charts should also be larger to enable group viewing. (The publication was already expensive and printing in a larger format would have made it more so, and also unwieldy.) Hans Pemmer, a school principal involved in the Viennese school experiments, concluded that not all charts from Gesellschaft und Wirtschaft were suitable for 1o-14 year olds. He recommended it for less gifted' pupils, and especially for teaching geography, history and science. He concluded:

If pictorial statistics are well chosen and not too often used in education, they will render a useful service particularly for the visually gifted ones to make things clearer and to remember them better. .. Let us not forget that pictorial statistics has an educational value regarding taste because of its modern style.³

30. 'Experiences with the publication Gesellschaft und Wirtschaft in German and Austrian schools, 1930-1933', undated typescript (öNB, Ser.n.31.894). Most probably translated from German by Marie Neurath.

31. From hieroglyphics to Isotype, p. 116.

32. All of these reports are included in Hans Pemmer, 'Versuche über die Verwendung von Mengenbildern im Unterricht', Die Quelle, Jg. 1933, H. 4 & 5; typescript English translation by Marie Neurath (ic 8.2 Pem)

33. It seems that these 'naturalistic' examples were made especially for the test, and Von Bracken implies that a typical model for them was the statistical calendar of the German socialist publisher Laub.

34. Dr Helmut von Bracken, 'Gesellschaft und Wirtschaft', Der Volkslehrer, Jg. 13, H.7, 1931, p.94.

35. International picture language (1936), p.8.

36. From hieroglyphics to Isotype, p. 115.

37. Letter Neurath to Adler, 6 May 193o (Labour and Socialist International Archive; IISH 2488/2). Neurath hoped that Arbeit und Wirtschaft would be a colourful cheap paperback printed in 300,ooo copies. He told Adler that Gesellschaft und Wirtschaft would be printed in 10,o00 copies; the edition is stated to have been 1,00o in Christoph Behnke, et al (ed.), Atlas: spaces in subjunctive (Lüneburg: Verlag für Wissenschaft und zeitgenössische Kunst, 2004), p.8, although that figure does not appear on the publication itself.

38. Technik und Menschheit was planned concurrently with Gesellschaft und Wirtschaft: in June 1929 Neurath and Kaempffert discussed collaborating on it. Kaempffert referred to it as 'Menschheit und Technik', which was retained as the running title on the charts of the final publication. (Letter Kaempffert to Neurath, 19 June 1929, GwM-MSI.)

In addition to these informal, observational reports, some 'psychotechnical' tests were made on Gesellschaft und Wirtschaft at a technical college in Braunschweig. Psychologist Dr Helmut von Bracken and his colleague W. Meyer tested two charts from the atlas alongside two examples depicting the same data with the usual, naturalistic representation.'³³ Twenty-one students were asked to interpret the sense of the charts on seeing them, and then to do the same a week later without seeing the charts again, in order to test what they had retained from each style of statistical representation. Von Bracken reported that charts in the Vienna Method already gave better results on immediate interpretation, but that the really significant differences emerged after one week: the more precise figures in the naturalistic' examples were rounded off in the memory of the test subjects, with many errors resulting, whereas the figures contained in the schematic representations of Gesellschaft und Wirtschaft were retained much better and more accurately. He concluded that 'representations according to the Vienna Method are remembered about two and a half times better than the traditional representations'. 34

The design of this experiment would no doubt be considered primitive by cognitive psychologists today, but Neurath's willing submission of material to such a test reflects his ambitions to make Isotype a method of ‘turning the statements of science into pictures'.³⁵ Neurath was not shy of publicizing the advantages of the Vienna Method but he did not seize on these test results as an opportunity to lend it a stamp of scientific authority; he was aware that ‘the importance of the whole Isotype technique cannot be analysed by means of such single investigations'.³⁶

## Further plans for an ‘Isotype encyclopedia'

Before Gesellschaft und Wirtschaft was printed Neurath had already approached Friedrich Adler (son of Viktor Adler, founder of the Austrian Social Democrat Party) at the Labour and Socialist International in Zurich to request his collaboration on a book titled Arbeit und Wirtschaft which Neurath implied would develop material from the atlas then in preparation.³7 Nothing came of this, nor of various other proposals Neurath made towards realizing the ‘visual thesaurus/Isotype encyclopedia'. Strangely, it seems that he did not regard the atlas of 3o charts produced by the Vienna Museum and published in 1932, Technik und Menschheit, as a partial realization of the larger project along with Gesellschaft und Wirtschaft.38

39. 'An international encyclopedia of unified science' (1936), p. 142. Here Neurath referred readers to his book of the same year, International picture language, which concludes with an optimistic outline of his larger, encyclopedic aims. In his article on 'the development of the Vienna Circle' (also 1936), Neurath detailed Leibniz's plans for an 'Atlas universalis', which seem to prefigure his own for the International Encyclopedia. ('Die Entwicklung des Wiener Kreises und die Zukunft des logischen Empirismus', pp. 686–7.)

40. Morris, 'On the history of the International Encyclopedia of Unified Science', Synthese, vol. 12, no. 4, December 196o, pp. 518–19. 41. Letter Neurath to Foges (Adprint), 26 September 1944, IC 1/32.

42. Letter Neurath to Mannheim, 7 October 1943 (ic 1/41). In an addendum the next day, Neurath made some detailed proposals for how the new encyclopedia could differ in physical terms from Gesellschaft und Wirtschaft: relevant text could be printed on the back of charts, and they could be bound together. He felt that the danger of sheets going missing outweighed the advantage of being able to place them side-by-side for comparison.

43. 'Annual cost', appended to typescript memorandum 'Visual encyclopedia for the common man', c. 1943 (IC 3.2/58). The practical difficulties of producing such an encyclopedia were apparent to H.G. Wells, whose ideas for a unifying, international encyclopedia (or 'World Brain') were quite close to Neurath's. Neurath received a copy of Wells's 'Memorandum on the project of a world encyclopedia' (1938), in which Wells admitted: 'But to point out a necessity and sketch an objective is one thing; to carry it toward realization is quite another' (Vienna Circle Archive [vcA], Noord-Hollands Archief, Haarlem, R.62). Wells had visited the Gesellschafts- und Wirtschaftsmuseum, which provided charts for the German edition of his A short history of the world.

44. Typescript by Neurath titled 'Information on Isotype plan, dealing with comprehensive education, museums and exhibitions, wall posters, illustrations, picture-textstyle books, Isotype films', p. 1. He sent it to Julian Huxley in December 1942 (IC 1/35).

From 1936 Neurath aligned his plan for a pictorial encylopedia with plans for the International Encyclopedia of Unified Science, of which he was editor-in-chief. He proposed to supplement the projected 26 volumes of this encyclopedia (only a fraction of which were ever published) with a ten-volume visual thesaurus. 'It is evident that this Encyclopedia will tend towards the unification of not only the scientific language, but also graphic representation', Neurath stated; 'All pictures that the Encyclopedia will present will be made of standardised elements, if technical, biological, sociological or other subjects are to be represented. ... The Mundaneum Institute in The Hague will adapt the picture language ISOTYPE (International System Of TYpographic Picture Education) to the particular needs of the Encyclopedia; so far it has used this language elsewhere.'³º Neurath's co-editors, Charles Morris and Rudolf Carnap, both had doubts about this component of the plan;⁴º yet that did not deter Neurath from working towards the visual thesaurus. He later claimed that a significant amount of preparatory work for it had been done in the Netherlands, but the material had to be abandoned there.

Neurath realized that support from a publisher or other funding body was necessary to make progress and he continually sent outlines of the project – which he called 'our great plan' – to individuals who could possibly help.4¹ The book packaging firm of Adprint, for which the Isotype Institute designed many charts from 1942, declined to spon: sor it, so Neurath proposed an International Isotype thesaurus' to Karl Mannheim (editor of a series of books published by Routledge Kegan Paul), suggesting that a guild of publishers would be necessary, along with additional subsidy. His plans were not Utopian, he explained, because the concrete example of Gesellschaft und Wirtschaft proved that it could be done.⁴² However, the extent of Neurath's plan was daunting: documents were prepared at the Isotype Institute including a preliminary scheme for the first 1,2oo charts' of the encyclopedia. The subjects of these projected charts can be seen as an elaboration of themes from Gesellschaft und Wirtschaft. It was estimated that the scheme would take four years to produce at a total cost of £1oo,ooo.4ª Neurath's concept of the visual thesaurus expanded to encompass all media in which the Isotype Institute sought to work: 'It is important that all kinds of Isotypes, charts, panels, maps, models, films etc may be regarded as parts of one Encyclopedia', he explained.⁴⁴ Such a plan was unlikely to have found support in wartime Britain.

## Entwicklung der Brotgetreide- und Reisproduktion seit 1860

In chart g6, 'Human groups of the world', the graphic solution for showing relative population is not as obvious as it may seem. It should be compared with chart no. 22, 'States and population 193o' (see p. 2o7 above), which derives from the same data but features smaller, generic [hu]man' pictograms positioned on a world map. The colour code used for the pictograms is common to both charts, but in chart g6 the geographical dimension is omitted and differences in the form of pictograms are introduced to carry the theme visually. Neurath wanted to show here the striking correlation that emerges if humanity is simply arrayed in three groups – 'white'’, 'brown/black/red' and 'yellow' people (as he called them):

Experience shows that even people with academic training (discounting specialists) are inclined to accept that there are about four times as many yellow' people as 'white'. Lesser educated people are content with two or three times as many. If, on the other hand, the chart about 'Human groups of the world' from the Gesellschafts- und Wirtschaftsmuseum, or its elementary work in pictorial statistics Gesellschaft und Wirtschaft, is allowed to work on you, it is difficult to forget – as has been tested – that there are roughly as many 'yellow' as 'white'.45

It is perhaps tempting to perceive the colour-coding and cultural stereotyping in these charts as naive or even chauvinistic, with the 'whites' in neutral shades – as if a 'default' – and the other groups marked as different by colour. But closer examination belies this: the whites here are culturally stereotyped in equal measure to the other groups, with selective headwear providing differentiation. Indeed, in their grey apparel, the whites recede by comparison with the other, colourful groups. This chart is a neardefinitive Isotype presentation of this subject, which had been essayed many times since 1925, and its shows a great reduction of physiognomic, racial features compared to previous iterations (see pp. 51, 68, 13o above). The graphic features here can be considered in terms of their function in a kind of pictorial 'grammar', which gives meaning to changes in single elements. Colour is one such element, and, although it is used here iconically, it also functions as part of a simplified graphic repertoire designed to maximize impact. It seems clear that colour is not intended to be naturalistic, any more than the schematic forms of the human figures. The notes on this chart in the appendix to Gesellschaft und Wirtschaft state:

We have counted as 'coloured' [Farbigen] all those whom the English call 'coloured men'. For the social problems of today the Indians and Arabs belong in one group, not for instance the Indians and Europeans, despite linguistic relations. ... Although skin colour is not the decisive reason for differentiation – south Italians look more brown than white – the human groups are differentiated by colour in the picture.46

Further clarification was given in the Vienna Museum's periodical, Fernunterricht, in response to a reader's question – 'What should one understand by "human groups"?':

In its elementary work in pictorial statistics [Gesellschaft und Wirtschaft] the Gesellschafts- und Wirtschaftsmuseum uses the expression 'human groups' in order to indicate the political situation, not to clarify descent or questions of race.47

It would be only natural, in any case, for Europeans and their descendants to be presented as the 'norm' in such charts, which are contained in a German-language publication intended primarily for a Central-European audience. But one intention of Gesellschaft und Wirtschaft, as of much work in the Vienna Method, was to educate Central-Europeans about their brethren in the rest of the world, and its even-handedness in this respect was undoubtedly forward-looking in its time. It was stated in Fernunterricht (probably by Neurath) that 'Modern empirical sociology attempts to describe all peoples as other natural subjects and not to insert praise and blame from a Central-European standpoint into each description.'48

It is clear from Neurath's writings that he was not a supporter of Western hegemony: Roughly speaking, the whites are above all carriers of capitalism and imperialism. They are the “masters”, with battleships and poison gas at their disposal.' 49

45. 'Bildstatistik nach Wiener Methode' (1931), p. 184. 46. Gesellschaft und Wirtschaft, p.125. 47. Fernunterricht, Jg.1, H.6, September 1931, p. 15.

48. Fernunterricht, Jg.1, H.3, June 1931, p. 11. 49. 'Kolonialpolitische Aufklärung durch Bildstatistik' (1928), p. 130.

![](images/f03e8f6b1551098754808f6f30a1035cda1611f2b8e6b9c1a0f79c40da1abd1c.jpg)  
Gerd Arntz, Kleine Fabrik (Small factory). 1926. Oil on canvas. 800 × 600 mm.

Arntz did not produce many oil paintings, concentrating instead on monochrome woodcut and linocut prints. This is the earliest of his oil paintings to have survived.

# 4 Figurative constructivism and sociological graphics

Benjamin Benus

In August 1929 the Dutch artist Peter Alma and the Czech artist Augustin Tschinkel joined Gerd Arntz as employees of the Gesellschafts- und Wirtschaftsmuseum in Wien to assist in designing charts for the atlas Gesellschaft und Wirtschaft. All three artists were affiliated with the international artists' organization known as the Gruppe progressiver Künstler (Group of progressive artists).¹ Together with the group's leading figures, the Cologne painters Franz Wilhelm Seiwert and Heinrich Hoerle, these artists developed a distinctive pictorial language, to which historians have applied the stylistic designation figurative constructivism'.² While Arntz, Alma, and Tschinkel produced their free' work (as they sometimes described their prints, drawings, and paintings of the 192os and 193os) independently of their design work with the Vienna Method of Pictorial Statistics, these two types of production share several key iconographic features. Indeed, some of the most important and frequently recurring Vienna Method pictograms, including those for the basic human figure, the worker-type, and the factory building – as well as those pictograms connected to automobile production and coalmining – were adapted directly from the artists' free work. Yet, the relationship between these two realms of activity has remained obscure. The nature of this relationship will be reassessed here by considering

the circumstances out of which the formal and thematic features of figurative constructivism originated, and by examining the ways in which the free and applied works were presented and discussed together in a variety of publications during the period of collaboration in Vienna.³

As their writings demonstrate, each of the participants in the Vienna collaboration conceived of the relationship between art and design in different terms. Arntz, in his later statements concerning the connection between the Progressive style' and the Vienna Method, always emphasized the distinction between his free and applied work.ª Alma and Tschinkel, by contrast, were far more emphatic in their writings about links between the two areas of production. For Seiwert and Hoerle (who, in any case, plaved no direct role in the design of pictorial statistics), the Vienna project may even be seen to run counter to certain aspects of their artistic programme.⁵ In considering this relationship, then, I do not wish to suggest that pictorial statistics were the culmination of the Progressives' work as printmakers and painters. To the extent that this essay draws parallels between the two types of work, it is with the goal of explaining why the Progressives' prints and drawings had such appeal for Otto Neurath, why the Progressives so actively promoted pictorial statistics in their own publications, and why certain graphic conventions associated with figurative constructivism were so effectively adapted to the design of Vienna Method pictograms.

## The origins of figurative constructivism

3. 1929 and 1930 mark the years during which all three artists were together in Vienna, though Arntz and Alma's collaboration with Neurath extended beyond these years. Arntz, whose employment at the Museum began in September 1928, continued to work with Neurath until 194o. Alma worked as a member of Neurath's design team until 1934.

4. See, for example, Gerd Arntz, Zeit unterm Messer, p. 22.

5. Lynette Roth puts forth this argument in her catalogue, Painting as a weapon: progressive Cologne 1920-1933. Seiwert – Hoerle – Arntz (Cologne: Walther König / Museum Ludwig, 2008), p.123.

Although the essential features of figurative constructivism originated in Cologne around 192o, it was only with the publication from 1929 of A bis Z, the Progressives' official journal, that the style's loosely shared character and international dimensions were self-consciously articulated.‘ The journal's first issue, which appeared in October of that year, featured five woodcuts and linocuts by members of the group who worked in this mode.7 Seen together, these works immediately reveal a

common visual grammar: human anatomy is reduced to simple shapes; facial characteristics, where they do occur, are limited to one or two circular forms and lack any individualizing characteristics; figures are set in frontal or profile views; and forms are generally composed along vertical and horizontal axes. The figures, meanwhile, refer to professional types or social classes (rather than specific individuals), and settings are limited to social institutions and sites of production and consumption (such as the factory and department store - though scenes set in harbours, prisons, and army barracks figure prominently in other works).

![](images/9bc39e63256082967daf5b3981b283f97a8f16e1043f3c8563afaafdbdecea8f.jpg)  
Heinrich Hoerle, Mann (konstruktive Figur) (Man – constructive figure). 1920, graphite.

![](images/1247337f056bf7693b40471e9b4be2738f644a29fb79d78436dc4be67ee1bb0a.jpg)  
Heinrich Hoerle, Der Europäer (The European), 1923, linocut. This was a second, modified version of the image (published in a bis z, 3. Folge, Blatt 21, January 1932).

Among the many pictograms designed by Arntz, Alma, and Tschinkel at the Gesellschafts- und Wirtschaftsmuseum, it is in the form of what Arntz called the standard-figure' that the Vienna Method's debt to the earliest manifestations of figurative constructivism is most evident above all, in the drawings and linocuts produced by Heinrich Hoerle between 1920 and 1923.8 In drawings such as ‘Mann (konstruktive Figur)', as well as in later linocuts like Prothesenkopf' (p. 219 above) and Der Europäer', Hoerle constructed his figures from geometric and mechanical forms – an approach to figuration eventually adopted by other Progressives. In introducing these mechanized beings into his work, Hoerle was responding to what had then become a widespread pictorial practice among artists of the international avant-garde, many of whom had recast the human image in a variety of uncanny guises, including automaton, puppet, mannequin, and tailor's dummy. Hoerle's engagement with this motif beginning in 192o can be explained, in part, by his encounter with the work of Giorgio de Chirico and Carlo Carrà, to which his friend and fellow artist, Max Ernst, had introduced him.ª In contrast, however, to the classicizing world in which the Italian painters frequently set their figures, Hoerle's mannequin imagery explicitlyaddressed a contemporary phenomenon, namely the plight of wounded veterans in the aftermath of the First World War. (Hoerle had served at the front as a field artillery telephone operator in 1917, and though he suffered no physical injuries himself he was deeply affected by the experience.)1º The dummy-like figure in Der Europäer', with its truncated limbs and mechanical parts, directly references the prosthetic anatomy of wounded war veterans. Indeed, it was the dramatic increase in the visibility of amputees in everyday life after the war – many of them fitted with prosthetic limbs – that in large part accounts for the prevalence of mannequin and automaton imagery among the international avant-garde during these years."

8. Despite Arntz's later expressions of ambivalence about the Progressives' influence on Isotype design (and his assertion that the pictograms he designed in Vienna all 'carried [his] own stamp'), he made an exception in the case of this human figure pictogram, conceding that 'an exact analysis would perhaps show that works, linocuts, by Hoerle still correspond for the most part in their essential form to the basic- and standard-figure established later'. (Arntz, '3.7.'72', p.2.)

9. In 1920, Hoerle published a portfolio of lithographs by Ernst, Fiat modes pereat ars (Let there be fashion, down with art). The work was largely inspired by Ernst's encounter with work by Giorgio de Chirico, about which he had learned during a trip to Munich the previous summer. There he had come across an issue of the Italian journal Valori Plastici, featuring de Chirico's paintings. See Dirk Backes, Heinrich Hoerle: Leben und Werk, 1895-1936 (Cologne: Rheinland-Verlag, 1981), pp. 24–5, 37–9. See also Sabine T.

![](images/5928b3ffec1e678d9a5487f0f303033eb320f7b47c71ce26e34edc8821aca674.jpg)  
Gerd Arntz, Krankenhaus (Hospital), from Zwölf Häuser der Zeit (Twelve houses of our time). 1927. Woodcut. 251 × 163 mm.

![](images/8d3402bc524ade13ee2593c7c14b840e1ad448e843ed8d019cad5692675b904b.jpg)  
Peter Alma, De oorlog maakt de man (War makes the man). 1936. Drawing (destroyed).

![](images/70d1aed2a9694f9a1a89ecc0f244ba587f670171b6cdc9eccdd7c3466eee95a3.jpg)  
Gerd Arntz, pictogram of amputee (no. 1232, categorized under 'soldier') in the Isotype 'picture dictionary'. C. 1932 (IC 4/2).

More so than for any of the other artists in his circle, the amputee is a persistent theme in Hoerle's work, though wounded veterans do make occasional appearances in works by Arntz and Alma.¹² The amputees, for example, in Arntz's 1927 woodcut, 'Krankenhaus' (Hospital), as well as the truncated figures in his earlier woodcut 'Matrosen' (Sailors, 1925), were intended as a direct reference to the victims of the First World War.¹³ The amputee war veteran reappeared years later in Alma's 1937 oil painting, Oorlog (War), as well as in his anti-war (and anti-fascist) poster design of the previous year, 'De oorlog maakt de man' (War makes the man). In this last example it is noteworthy that Alma alluded to the conventions of the Vienna Method – in the repeated rows of amputeepictograms and the crosses that run along the poster's bottom edge, suggesting the immense scale of war's destruction.1ª

12. Just prior to his first figurative constructivist works, Hoerle produced a portfolio of 12 lithographs, titled Die Krüppelmappe (Cripple portfolio), depicting both the physical and psychological traumas experienced by wounded veterans returning from the front.

## 13. See Arntz, Zeit unterm Messer, p. 6o.

14. The numbers of war dead and war wounded were, in fact, a subject which received repeated treatment in Isotype charts, and could have served as precedents for this image. See the pictogram reproduced here and the charts reproduced on pp. 172-4 above.

![](images/9a1a3208b528cd92a4cce3bb0e492a75ef543fb926c46284a47b29e6d011ef67.jpg)  
Heinrich Hoerle, Fabrikarbeiter (Factory worker). 1922. Oil on canvas.

15. See Fineman, 'Ecce Homo Prostheticus', pp. 9o, 103.

16. See Flip Bool and Kees Broos, Gerd Arntz: Kritische Grafik und Bildstatistik, p. 7; see also Arntz, Zeit unterm Messer, pp.14-15.

17. Arntz, Zeit unterm Messer, p.6o.

18. On the impact of Henry Ford and the popularity of his autobiography in Germany, see Mary Nolan Visions of modernity: American business and the modernization of Germany (New York: Oxford University Press, 1994), p. 34.

Deeply connected with the experience of the war and the vision of the amputee's reconstructed body (which had supplied the source material for so many of Hoerle's automatons) was the image of the factory. The replacement of wounded veterans' lost limbs with prosthetic devices designed to perform specific assembly line tasks was a central component of post-war rehabilitation programmes in Germany. 15 Hoerle appears to have been responding to such developments with his 1922 oil painting Fabrikarbeiter (Factory worker), which shows a mechanical worker, limbs replaced by tools and face supplanted with what appears to be a pressure gauge, standing against a background of factory machinery.

The factory image would have had particular significance for artists and audiences in the Rhineland, a region which, over the previous half-century, had emerged as among the densest and most rapidly industrialized on the European continent. For Arntz especially, who descended from a family of local industrialists and for a time had worked in his father's Remscheid factory, the subject had personal significance.16 (See Arntz's oil painting on p. 216 above.) His factory depictions may be distinguished from those of his colleagues, however, in their frequent inclusion of elements taken from popular culture and allusions to fashions and entertainment imported from the United States. Arntz's fascination with the intersection of industry and entertainment is clearly articulated in his 1924 woodcut, 'Amerikanisches' (Things American), where rows of cars and female performers signify these two realms of culture. In this work each motif occupies its own horizontal register: the lower band depicts six female figures – faceless bodies in bathing suits arranged in chorus-line formation – while the row of cars resembling Ford Model-Ts fills the space above. In a particularly jarring contrast, the figure of a hanged man - the victim of a lynching – overlaps and connects the two registers at the left side of the image. The work, as Arntz later explained, was a response to the literature, films, and illustrated magazines then flooding into Germany from the usA, through which he became familiar with images of both racial violence and bathing beauties'.17 Above all, Arntz cited as an inspiration Henry Ford's autobiography My life and work (1922), which had become an instant bestseller in Germany after the publication of the German edition in 1923.18 Through standardized forms and precise, geometric execution, Arntz sought to evoke Ford's rationalized assembl line production process, while his comparison between Model-Ts and showgirls, facilitated by their diagrammatic arrangement in separate registers, suggests a general equivalence between the mechanized character of production and the standardized forms of consumption. In this equation of production and consumption, work and leisure, Arntz's image foreshadows the analysis of Siegfried Kracauer, who later described the synchronized performances of chorus-line dancers and other forms of popular entertainment as 'the aesthetic reflex of the rationality to which the prevailing economic system aspires'. 19

![](images/d623d0f3fbdc9e41e983fe62156ea8e5024bd3bed9df102a645f66ee7a5f94d0.jpg)  
Gerd Arntz, Amerikanisches (Things American). 1924. Woodcut. 195 × 251 mm.

Arntz reiterated this notion – that life outside the factory might become indistinguishable from time spent inside – with even greater clarity in a woodcut made two years later, titled 'Ruhe und Ordnung (Calm and order; see overleaf). The figures distributed throughout this image, representing a variety of professions and activities, all echo the forms and rhythms of the factory operations depicted in the lower right register; meanwhile, the repeating figures within the factory compartment have become nearly indistinguishable from the machines at which

19. Siegfried Kracauer, The mass ornament: Weimar essays (Cambridge, MA & London: Harvard University Press, 1995), pp. 75–86.

![](images/10be955400d546d7d5b156eb7b4bfe13c96b046cb4b496efeb69917a2326a076.jpg)

Gerd Arntz, Ruhe und   
Ordnung (Calm and order). 1926. Woodcut.   
274×327mm.

The handwritten date of 1925 at top right was perhaps added much later by Marie Neurath (it seems to be her handwriting). This work is dated 1926 in the catalogue edited by Bool & Broos, Gerd Arntz.

they are stationed. This conflation of the body and machine was depicted in still more literal terms in a woodcut produced the following year titled Fabrik' (Factory), where, in another chilling synthesis of the amputee and assembly line motifs, workers' hands are replaced with tools.

This fusion of soldier and worker in the mechanized figure of the automaton should not be understood, however, as a condemnation of rationalization per se; rather, such depictions aimed to highlight the perversion of technology under the current political and economic system. Under alternative (i.e. socialist) conditions, the Progressives believed, technological rationalization might be redirected to serve human needs. Thus, beyond a critique of the dehumanizing impact of modern warfare and the mechanized production process, the 'constructivist' figure also emerged as the expression of an ideological position. For Seiwert especially (who, early on, had established himself as the Progressives' principal theoretician) the deployment of 'constructivist' forms in the depiction of factory workers was linked to the political project of council communism. This movement, which emerged in the aftermath of Germany's November Revolution, promoted a system in which local councils, elected from within workplaces and factories, would manage both government and economy, and operate independently of any centralized party or state bureaucracy.2º The grassroots character and participatory political structure of the council system had great appeal for artists like Seiwert, who aimed in his art and his writing to promote a more inclusive, egalitarian, and radically democratic social order.

![](images/d83539272bdb62478268375357dd087bf5f2f219bf4244f40869fd1f5ed70b73.jpg)  
Gerd Arntz, Fabrik (Factory), from Zwölf Häuser der Zeit. 1927. Woodcut. 251 × 162 mm.

20. Council communism was an offshoot of the larger workers' council movement, which enjoyed mass support among the working class throughout Central Europe in the days leading up to and following the end of the First World War, gathering particular strength within the Rhine and Ruhr regions of Germany. Modelled on the Russian soviets that had emerged in the revolutions of 1905 and 1917, German soldiers' and workers' councils quickly spread in the early days of November 1g18 and seized power in cities throughout Germany. In addition to briefly assuming the functions of local governments, soldiers' and workers' councils pressed for a wide range of social reforms. These councils - though at times supported by or connected with various parties on the political left – were, by and large, the spontaneous creations of the working class and had as their primary

goal the introduction of greater democracy and equality in the workplace and in society at large. Fearing that they would carry out a repeat of the Bolshevik revolution, the Social Democrat-led provisional government (which had initially cooperated with and participated in the movement) ultimately took a position against the councils, in some cases suppressing them with military force. The councils, in actuality, were much more moderate

and reformist in their aims than was perceived by either their opponents or their more radical supporters. The council communist ideology to which Seiwert and the other Progressives subscribed, which had as its goal the socialization of the economy and the permanent insti tutionalization of the councils, was always a minority position within the councils. See Eberhard Kolb, The Weimar Republic (New York: Routledge, 2005), pp. 151–4.

![](images/5767ee34aa95d2339ec96c74dd17c3e7438f813cb886937afa29803cf6f439a9.jpg)  
Franz W. Seiwert, Betriebsorganisation (Factory organization). 1922. Woodcut. 170 × 120 mm.

21. Die Aktion, which was founded in Berlin in 1911 by the critic and publisher Franz Pfemfert, was one of the few avant-garde journals to take an explicit antiwar stance from the very start of the First World War. It was influential for the artistic as well as political development of Seiwert and Hoerle, both of whom had contributed graphics to Die Aktion since 1917. After 1920 Die Aktion became an important advocate for council communism, publishing the manifestos and guidelines of council communist factions such as the Allgemeine Arbeiter Union - Einheitsorganisation (AAU-E; General workers' union – unity organization), of which Seiwert and Arntz were both supporters. While the actual council movement had all but disintegrated by 1923 (and had reached the height of its political relevance in the period immediately following the November Revolution), Die Aktion continued to advance the cause of council communism well into the later part of the decade.

One of the most important channels through which Seiwert articulated his commitment to this movement was Die Aktion, a Berlin-based journal for art, literature, and politics, wherein artists and intellectuals debated the role that art should play in the revolutionary cause.2¹ Between 1922 and 1926 Seiwert's prints appeared on Die Aktion's cover on nine separate occasions, while Hoerle's work was featured on four journal covers during this period – and works by both artists appeared with even greater frequency within the journal's pages. These reproductions were of tremendous importance for the artistic development of other members of the Progressives, who first became acquainted with Seiwert and Hoerle's graphic work, as well as with the tenets of the council movement, through this source.2²

With works like the 1922 woodcut ‘Betriebsorganisation' (Factory organization), which first appeared on a 1923 cover of Die Aktion and later became one of the artist's most reproduced graphic works, Seiwert attempted to describe the political reality facing the council movement while simultaneously suggesting possibilities still open to it.23 In this

22. See Arntz, Zeit unterm Messer, p.15; Tschinkel, 'Parallelen und Nachwirken', in Hoerle und sein Kreis (Frechen: Kunstverein zu Frechen, 1971); and Kees Broos, Augustin Tschinkel.

23. This work was first reproduced on the cover of Die Aktion, Jg. 13, Nr. 10, 1923. It was later reproduced in several Czech publications including the journal Naše cesta (Our path), roč. 2, číslo. 8,

1932, p. 10o; the booklet Soziale Grafik (1932), p. 16; and it served as the cover for the compilation F. W. Seiwert - Gemälde - Grafik - Schriften (Prague, 1934).

work, a cluster of near-identical factory workers appears crammed into the space between factory transmission belts, caught up in the machinery of capital as exploited and de-subjectivized human material', as art historian Uli Bohnen has described them. Yet, Bohnen also observes that Seiwert has invested this image with a certain dialectical character, since the workers – in their compositional placement as an organized and collective body within the machine – appear capable of tearing through the strained movements of the transmission belts, which encompass them'.²⁴

As Bohnen's reading implies, the print's formal structure is inseparable from its narrative elements: thus, the collective strength of the workers is communicated by means of their compositional organization into a single monolithic form, achieved through the overlapping of figures and the repetition of shapes. In this way, Betriebsorganisation' follows a kind of diagrammatic logic, expressing structural relationships through the placement of forms: gears and transmission belts are superimposed upon the workers, while the image of the factory looms above their heads; yet, the group's central location within the image suggests a tactical advantage – a point further emphasized by the compositional placement of the letters BO' (an abbreviation for Betriebsorganisation). Positioned within the factory gears, these letters reinforce the idea that the workers occupy a strategic position within the production apparatus and remind one of the councils' capacity to jam up the wheels of production through organized strikes.

With this foregrounding of the artwork's formal construction (what Seiwert later referred to as the lawfulness' [Gesetzmäßigkeit] of the picture),²⁵ works like Betriebsorganisation' offered an alternative to the social-realist artistic model favoured and promoted by Germany's political-left establishment. Seiwert's pictorial strategy finds certain parallels in the work of Russian and international constructivists, who sought to engage audiences through dynamic arrangements of abstract, geometric forms.26 Seiwert, however, in his representational version of constructivism, invested this aesthetic engagement with a more explicitly political character: by fusing abstract geometric forms with figurative elements and narrative content, he imagined that the formal relations of the artwork could in turn function as a vehicle for the contemplation of class relations.²7

It is also important to note that Seiwert, in his promotion of the council communist project, did not show a victorious proletariat or depict the revolution as a fait accompli. Instead, his work presented the

24. Uli Bohnen, Das Gesetz der Welt ist die Änderung der Welt: Die rheinische Gruppe progressiver Künstler, 1918–1933 (Berlin: Karin Kramer Verlag, 1976), p. 71.

25. For a discussion of Seiwert's use of the term Gesetzmäßigkeit and its significance for his artistic theory, see Roth, Painting as a weapon, p. 34.

26. As a participant in the Kongreß internationaler fortschrittlicher Künstler (Congress of international progressive artists) held in Düsseldorf in May 1922, Seiwert forged close connections with many of the international constructivist movement's most prominent members, including Theo van Doesburg, El Lissitzky, and László Moholy-Nagy.

27. As Roth writes in Painting as a weapon (p. 34), 'for Seiwert, surface and structure should also reflect the conditions of society, thus encouraging the workerviewer to consider these as well'.

![](images/6426689bbca1842cd1e55330e6d3fe881b59246910fa98bb029f3815496def7c.jpg)  
Augustin Tschinkel, BO (Fabrik) (BO - factory). 1928 Linocut. 169 × 110 mm.

28. See Bohnen, Das Gesetz der Welt ist die Änderung der Welt, p.72.

29. Published by the council communist organization AAU-E, Die proletarische Revolution was a Frankfurt-based journal to which both Arntz and Seiwert regularly contributed graphic work through out 1927 and 1928.

30. Several years later, Seiwert illustrated the structural organization of the council system by means of an actual flow chart diagram (opposite), which appeared in one of the last issues of A bis Z, alongside an essay distinguishing the organizational principles of state socialism from those of the council movement.

revolution as a yet unrealized, though possible, outcome. In this way, his images left the audience to reach an independent conclusion and invited their participation in the completion and fulfillment of the revolutionary project. This conformed to Seiwert's understanding of the revolution, which – rather than a vision dictated to the masses from above, to which they were expected to conform – would have to be a participatory event, created by the masses themselves and organized from the ground up. For Seiwert, the Betriebsorganisationen were emblematic of such grassroots participation: comprised of all workers in a factory and created to serve their interests, these organizations elected their leaders through a democratic process and constituted the basic units forming the workers' councils.

The factory organization also became a recurring subject in prints by Arntz and Tschinkel. Tschinkel's 1928 linocut, ‘BO (Fabrik)', with its grouping of geometrized workers' heads at the lower edge of the image, framed above by factory wheels and transmission belts, reads like an abstract version of Seiwert's woodcut from several years earlier. Bohnen has, once again, interpreted this work in a way that highlights its diagrammatic character: here the two transmission belts form a triangle at the picture's right edge, which encapsulates the initials 'bo' (the placement of which is nearly identical to that in the earlier Seiwert print) and effectively drives a wedge between the workers at the lower part of the image and the factory machinery which subjugates them from above.2⁸

This diagrammatic quality is equally pronounced in another woodcut by Arntz titled 'Räte- und Betrieb Organisation' (Council and factory organization), which appeared on the cover of a 1928 issue of Die proletarische Revolution (Proletarian revolution).29 Here the forms are arranged like a flow chart, suggesting a structure in which workers are organized (literally from the ground up) through local factory organizations, and then connected to one another through larger workers' councils.³º

These later works by Arntz and Tschinkel may be seen, in part, as a synthesis of Hoerle and Seiwert's respective approaches. In their precise execution and geometric approach to figuration, these works draw upon Hoerle's images of mechanized automatons and suggest a similarly ambivalent attitude towards technological modernity. At the level of iconography and pictorial structure they are undoubtedly informed by Seiwert's work, as well as by his revolutionary politics. It should be understood that the Progressives conceived of this pictorial language – the geometrized figuration, the tendency towards diagrammatic compositions, and the typological treatment of subject matter (about
