(Left) Cover of Die proletarische Revolution (January 1928), featuring Gerd Arntz's woodcut Räte und Betrieb Organisation (Council and factory organization, 1927).

(Above) Franz Wilhelm Seiwert, Schema des Rätesystems (Plan of the council system), A bis Z, 3. Folge, Blatt 27, September 1932, p. 107.

which more will be said shortly) – as serving both an educational and a political function. This is, indeed, how contemporaneous critics discussed these works. Writing about the Progressives' prints and drawings in the introduction to the 1932 publication, Soziale Grafik (Social graphics), the Czech poet Břetislav Mencák described how the clarity and simplicity of the artists' formal language should guarantee accessibility to a broad audience:

For such sections of social reality a graphic expression is being sought here with the intention of immediately revealing the relationships and differences between social classes to the masses in a clear, symbolic, visual language, easy for all to understand. These drawings are as simple as building blocks and spelling books, so that people of all ages can at least construct the simplest socialist attitude towards society and learn the ABC of the new world order [Weltgericht].³

Similarly, Seiwert described his intention in employing a 'figurativeconstructive pictorial form' as to present a reality divested of all that is sentimental and contingent, and to make visible the functioning, the order [Gesetzlichkeit], the connections, and the tensions of this reality within the framework and rules of the picture'.³2

31. Břetislav Mencák, foreword to Soziale Grafik (1932), p. 1.

32. Franz Wilhelm Seiwert, autobiographical sketch in Kunst der Zeit, Jg. 3, H.6, Sonderheft 'Rheinland', 1929, p. 171.

33. Between March and May of 1919, Neurath ran the Central office of economics in the shortlived Bavarian Räterepublik, and shortly after took a position with the Central Committee of the German Trade Union Association in Czechoslovakia, leading a training institute for factory council teachers. See Nancy Cartwright, et al, Otto Neurath: philosophy between science and politics, pp. 43–56, 6o.

34. Neurath later described this design principle as follows: 'A picture made according to the Vienna method shows at the first glance the most important aspect of the subject; obvious differences must be at once distinguishable. At the second glance, it should be possible to see the more important details; and at the third glance, whatever other details there may be. A picture that has still further information to give at the fourth and fifth glance is, from the point of view of the Vienna school, to be rejected as pedagogically unsuitable.' ('Museums of the future', p. 479.)

## Sociological graphics at the Gesellschafts- und Wirtschaftsmuseum

35. As Arntz later recounted their first meeting: 'Neurath showed particular interest in those works, wherein identical figures were presented in horizontal registers and vertical sequences.' Arntz, Zeit unterm Messer, p. 21.

36. Held between 8 May and 15 October 1926, this exhibition was among the largest in Germany during the years of the Weimar Republic. See Kunst, Sport, und Körper: Gesolei, 1926–2004 (Weimar: VDG, 2004).

37. Franz Roh, 'Zur jüngsten niederrheinischen Malerei' Das Kunstblatt, Jg. 10, H. 10, 1926, p. 365.

38. See Arntz, Zeit unterm Messer, p. 24. Arntz recounted that it was Seiwert, in fact, who first brought Tschinkel to his attention while he learned of Alma through the Dutch journal ito. Alma was also a friend of Karl Menger, a member of the Vienna Circle.

While these ideological underpinnings would likely have appealed to Otto Neurath (who had played an important role in the council movement in Munich),³³ it was, above all, the implications of figurative constructivism's visual language for the design of standardized pictograms that were paramount in Neurath's decision to recruit Arntz for the Vienna Method project. Arntz's formal approach already corresponded to Neurath's demand that educational pictures eliminate extraneous detail and provide the most important information within the viewer's first few glances at the picture.³⁴ Furthermore, the duplication of forms and their ordered arrangement in rows and columns – features characteristic of Vienna Method charts – were already recurring motifs in Arntz's own woodcuts. 35

The critic, photographer, and art historian, Franz Roh (a long-time friend of Neurath), was the first, in 1926, to suggest the suitability and potential application of Arntz's work to the Vienna Method of Pictorial Statistics, and appears to have arranged the first meeting between them. Neurath had come to Düsseldorf that year to exhibit charts in the Austrian pavilion at the ‘Gesolei' exhibition,3⁶ though he was not yet satisfied with the graphic solutions that had so far been developed. Roh was in agreement and, in an article for Das Kunstblatt, suggested Arntz's stylistic approach as a potential improvement:

Where the necessity arises to find somebody capable of depicting coloured graphic symbols for industry or of presenting that new vivid form of statistics, as the hall of Austrian economy at ‘Gesolei' aspires to do with sensuous yet not always irreproachable stylization, one should consider this still young man.³7

Arntz remained in Düsseldorf for an additional two years after this initial meeting, collaborating on pictogram design with Neurath by post. Finally, in September of 1928 Arntz moved to Vienna to begin work at the Gesellschafts- und Wirtschaftsmuseum – initially on a temporary basis – and was hired the following January for a permanent appointment as director of the Museum's graphic department. Alma and Tschinkel, both of whom had only come into the Progressives' circle in the previous year, joined the team in Vienna the following summer on Arntz's recommendation.³⁸

Even before Arntz's arrival in Vienna, however, the design of pictograms at the Museum began to conform to graphic conventions derived from his woodcut prints, which Neurath had begun collecting and making available at the Museum since their first meeting.³º Beyond his contribution to exhibition charts and publications, Arntz's characteristic pictorial style also reached the Viennese public through the posters, models, and large-scale wall paintings that he produced for the Museum after his arrival in 1928. In his triptych depicting the city's newly constructed Amalienbad swimming hall, as well as in a Vienna cityscape (titled ‘Sozialarbeit der Stadt Wien’), Arntz imagined Viennese society as a continuous architectural cross-section (see pp. 232-3 below).4º This device (first developed in woodcuts such as Ruhe und Ordnung’; p. 224 above) allowed Arntz to suggest a complex set of relationships by bringing together a diverse group of social settings and contexts within a single pictorial framework

![](images/57408158c4c5667955bb1a8ea72542eb31b3f4642778f93173dcd7763c7826c3.jpg)

39. As Arntz recounted: 'Under my influence the initial paper cutouts, which were pasted on the exhibition charts, had meanwhile been replaced by linocuts . . The applied fiqure-symbols were already heavily oriented towards the prototype, taken from the woodcuts,

which Neurath, in his enthusiasm to find a draftsman who corresponded to his intentions, had purchased from me since 1926. The technique of wood- or linocut had largely contributed to the simplification of the symbols.' Arntz, '3.7.'72', p. 1.

40. While these works, which were lost after the Museum's closure in 1934, are an exception in Arntz's oeuvre, murals were in fact a significant medium for several members of the Progressives circle, including Seiwert, Hoerle, and Alma. See Bool & Broos,

## T72A Scope of functions of the Chambers for workers and employees

Published in the pamphlet accompanying the 'Kärnten' exhibition, 1929.

This was also published in the Museum's supplement to Das Bild, Jg.7, H. 2, February 1930, p. 40. The accompanying caption stated:

'Here the method of schematic characterization is applied, in order to show the organization and activity of an office. As in pictorial statistics, naturalism is not required here. Sparse variation in form emphasizes similarity and unity, typical features express the difference between groups.'

Gerd Arntz, p. 88; and Peter Hielscher, 'Die Wandbilder der "Progressiven" ', in Politische Konstruktivisten: die 'Gruppe progressiver Künstler' Köln (Berlin: Neue Gesellschaft für bildende Kunst, 1975).

![](images/7824349dfecfec9fd8a5682f930a5357de051fbd1fda3ed95b29c688bf0968ce.jpg)

![](images/0a2622a4fbce569bf5e23a564fe3fc5fdf6ce4142f161fecbec7c5f71dd7f3bb.jpg)  
T133 Gerd Arntz, Amalienbad. c.1929. Oil on wood. This Viennese municipal swimming hall was completed in 1926.

![](images/a2e4a80301424e69dc947967c514250ec1365faa90d808c339cbc4e6fd046ba0.jpg)  
T148 Gerd Arntz, Sozialarbeit der Stadt Wien (Social work in the city of Vienna). 1929. Oil on wood. This was displayed in the Volkshalle of Vienna's Neues Rathaus.

![](images/f57df34ef1acc2aeae1f8658a1f0223a601bade14ab21c20e45b924882e79cff.jpg)

![](images/003ce0bc557dc517a12cfdea5df43394f8003dfe3b38d4c8e53885138cf79377.jpg)

Alongside the cross-section presentation, the standardization of figural types represents another point of overlap between figurative constructivism and pictorial statistics. The three figures at the left side of Sozialarbeit der Stadt Wien', for example, function in a manner similar to statistical pictograms, indicating social class through variations in their respective uniforms: the worker on the left, his arm outstretched and holding a single coin, is unadorned; in the middle stands a figure in identical pose, but adorned with a bowler hat and suit jacket – a petit bourgeois – his arm in the same position but holding a mediumsized money bag; the third figure (on the right) retains the same pose and proportions but, in addition to the suit jacket, wears a vest and top hat, and holds a larger money bag.4 Thus, through minimal variation these figures were able to suggest a wide range of meanings.

In depicting themes relevant to the Museum's work, and in making reference to the programmes of the Viennese municipal government (which largely financed the Museum's projects), Arntz's paintings served to link figurative constructivism with both the Museum and the socially progressive agenda of the city of Vienna. This link was reinforced in the

![](images/ec92f9413fb200eb6f923ddb719f46818157e5de2bbdd3c433ced8f22b6d7fbd.jpg)  
Detail of the mural shown opposite.

41. Hielscher interprets this as a reference to the city's progressive income tax policy. In any case, the three figures point to the theme of income distribution and wealth inequality – one of the subjects treated in the Museum's charts See Hielscher, 'Die Wandbilder der "Progressiven"', in Politische Konstruktivisten.

(Left) T132a Ohne Berufsberatung, Gefahr! (Without vocational guidance, danger!). c.1929.

Two paintings made by Arntz for the Gesellschaftsund Wirtschaftsmuseum:

![](images/db4ca7a6add3620d676b5ee9883512948dc3e66dec2785436bc0d297f8ef47f8.jpg)

![](images/421d55f16e7c2dc6dcbf1bd378fc7120d43815b21c48c9e5b341a27d37822df4.jpg)

42. Years later Arntz would comment on this tension between his sympathy for the Museum's programme and his scepticism about the state-administered socialism of Vienna: The working [of] this institute fitted quite definitely into my political vision. It was above all the enlightenment on social relationships in which I could give shape to my ideas. Only I was a bit more revolutionary, more to the left than the socialists in Vienna. I was at the time for a real overthrow of society.' Quoted in 'Gerd Arntz', interview in Pulchri.

43. The term appears to have been introduced by Neurath in 193o in his article 'Das Sachbild' (p. 159), which featured a reproduction of Arntz's woodcut 'Bank' from his Zwölf Häuser der Zeit series. Alma and Tschinkel quickly followed suit, employing the term in several articles published that same year. See, for example, Alma, 'Beeldstatistiek en sociologische grafiek' (Pictorial statistics and sociological graphics), Wendingen, serie 11, nr, 9, 193o, pp. 3–7; and Tschinkel, 'Zobrazení množství a kolektivní tvary: k výběru ze sbírky sociologické grafiky sociologického a hospodářského musea ve Vídni' (Quantity-pictures and collective form: a selection of sociological graphics from the Social and economic museum of Vienna), Výtvarné Snahy, roč. 11, číslo. 8, 1930, pp. 136-7.

following year when Arntz's painting was reproduced in the Progressives' journal A bis Z and in the Wendingen issue devoted to sociological graphics and pictorial statistics' – appearing within the latter as a large-scale reproduction on the first page (see p. 238 below). It should be noted, however, that there were also significant differences between the position taken by Arntz and the other Progressives, who expressed support for the revolutionary workers' council movement, and that of the Viennese Social Democrats, who advocated a program of 'revolution through reform'. Nonetheless, the social-educational mission of the Museum itself, and its origins in Neurath's own efforts to promote self-management programs among the Viennese working class, had great appeal for Arntz and the Progressives.⁴²

In addition to artworks by Arntz, Neurath also collected graphic work by other Progressives, including Alma, Tschinkel, and Seiwert As an expression of the themes that these artworks shared with the Museum's statistical presentations, Neurath and several members of the Progressives circle began at this time to describe figurative-constructivist prints and drawings as sociological graphics.'4³ In several articles Neurath praised the Progressives for their collective vision, and on occasion used the Museum as a venue to promote their work.⁴

Likewise, the artists viewed their relationship with the Museum as an opportunity to publicize and exhibit their work, and reach a still greater audience. Tschinkel, for example, in an article about the Museum that appeared in a 1929 issue of the Czech arts journal Výtvarné Snahy (Artistic endeavors), made a point of mentioning the Museum's 'collection of sociological graphics, from which exhibitions are occasionally staged'.⁴⁵ This collection was drawn on extensively in the following year, when the Museum contributed several print series from its holdings to a major exhibition of international contemporary art, 'Die Kunst in unserer Zeit' (Art in our time), held from March to May 193o at the Künstlerhaus in Vienna, and organized by the Gesellschaft zur Förderung moderner Kunst in Wien (Society for the promotion of modern art in Vienna).⁴

Perhaps the most significant articulation of the partnership between the Progressives and the Museum came in the form of an announcement that appeared in the November 193o issue of A bis Z, describing the creation of a new 'Department of sociological graphics' at the Museum, with the goal of connecting the Museum's work to the most progressive formal and critical artistic creation of our time'.47 While this department was likely a relatively informal affair, the announcement suggests that the relationship between the Progressives and the Gesellschafts- und Wirtschaftsmuseum was soon conceived in broader terms than just the design of charts and pictograms – even if this remained the primary and most enduring aspect of their collaboration.

## Avant-garde publications

While their contributions to Gesellschaft und Wirtschaft throughout 1929 and 193o left less time for Arntz, Alma, and Tschinkel to pursue their independent artistic production, these years of collaboration in Vienna did, in fact, mark the most fruitful period for the dissemination of their earlier graphic work – mainly in the form of avant-garde publications.

Frans Masereel, Diego Rivera, and Vladimir Krinskii. The Museum also supplied architectural models, posters, and advertisements to the exhibition's applied arts section, along with charts displaying pictorial statistics. See Die Kunst in unserer Zeit (Vienna: Verlag der Gesellschaft zur Förderung moderner Kunst in Wien, 193o). 47. 'Soziologische Grafik,' A bis Z, 2. Folge, Blatt 12, November 1930, p. 46.

In many respects, the exhibition was conceived in a spirit remarkably similar to that of the Gesellschaftsund Wirtschaftsmuseum: rather than displaying artworks solely for the aesthetic contemplation of a small, initiated audience, Tietze's exhibition sought to engage a wider public by framing recent artistic developments within a larger social and historical context. To this end, the exhibition was organized into several thematic sections, including organ der gruppe progressiver kunstler kóin mal 1930 herausgober heinrioh hoorio

Additionally, all three artists utilized these publications to explain and promote the work of the Gesellschafts- und Wirtschaftsmuseum, in some cases drawing parallels between the Museum's work and their own artistic projects.48 Between 1929 and 193o all three artists produced essays for avant-garde journals in their respective languages, elucidating the principles of the Vienna Method, reproducing examples of charts, and considering the relationship between the free and applied work.

The journal A bis Z was particularly significant in this regard. During the course of its three-and-a-half year run (from October 1929 to February 1933), it featured the work of the Gesellschafts- und Wirtschaftsmuseum on five separate occasions (along with passing references in other issues).4º Images of the Museum's work first appeared in the journal's May 193o issue, accompanying an essay titled Bewegung in Kunst und Statistik' (Movement in art and statistics), wherein Arntz reflected upon the changing social roles that painters and statisticians would play as contemporary society underwent political transformation.

49. Illustrated articles on the Museum's work appear in: [1. Folge, Blatt] 8, May 1930; [1. Folge, Blatt] 9, July 1930; 2. Folge, Blatt 13, January 1931; 3. Folge, Blatt 28, November

1932; and 3. Folge, Blatt 29, December 1932. For additional references to the Museum see 2. Folge, Blatt 12, November 1930; and 3. Folge, Blatt 22, February 1932.

While Arntz's rather abstract discussion makes no direct mention of the Gesellschafts- und Wirtschaftsmuseum, the accompanying reproductions make clear that the questions he tried to address with this essay had arisen from his work at the Museum. The illustrations, which include two pages from the recently published Gesellschaft und Wirtschaft atlas, an image of Arntz's painting for the Neues Rathaus in Vienna, and Seiwert's linocut Feierabend' (Quitting time), served to indicate both formal and iconographic points of overlap between these two types of work.⁵º The charts chosen from the atlas for reproduction (no.85, 'Workers in commercial enterprises in Germany', and no.88, 'Strikes and lockouts')⁵1 would have had particular resonance for the creators and readership of A bis Z: the iconography featured in these charts – the worker-types, factory smokestacks and saw-tooth rooftops – had long been persistent features within the Progressives' artworks (as exempli fied by Seiwert's linocut).

Among the several avant-garde publications in which figurativeconstructivist artworks and pictorial statistics appear together, the August 193o issue of Wendingen deserves special attention.⁵² This issue, devoted to the theme of pictorial statistics and sociological graphics' remains valuable both as an introduction to the two subjects and as one of the only primary sources to bring these two areas of production together in such an elaborate manner within a single publication. The issue follows the same format as nearly all the issues of the journal's thirteen-year run: exclusive treatment of a selected theme; a cover designed specifically for the issue by a selected artist; a short essay introducing the issue's subject; and a rich assortment of illustrations.⁵3 In this case, Alma produced the cover design, the introductory essay, and nearly half of the featured artworks.

when compared to the other publications discussed here in that it did not conform to the ideas of New Typography. In contrast to A bis Z, Výtvarné Snahy, and Soziale Grafik, each of which utilized austere and functional layouts (informed by work from the Bauhaus and the Ring 'neue Werbegestalter'), Wendingen's ornamental and decorative design – with its echoes of Jugendstil and Arts & Crafts – would have appeared more appropriate to the pre-war era: the lettering designed by Wijdeveld for the journal's masthead, for example, composed of thin lines and large blocks, echoes the geometric ornamentation that borders the pages and encloses the text and reproductions. Its square paper format was based on the Japanese tatami mat proportion and, along with the occasional use of rice paper, it employed a Japanese binding method, wherein pages were printed only on one side folded, and hand-bound in blockbook style using raffia. Wendingen was thus a luxury item and, in this sense, could not have been further from a publication like Soziale Grafik (discussed below), which embraced standardization and inexpensive production with the goal of maximizing accessibility. See Martijn F. Le Coultre, et al, Wendingen: a journal for the arts, 1918–1932 (New York: Princeton Architectural Press, 2001).

![](images/3b818ce48059459e1ad965dc5b68a3aecb446933e903eb8046c0ca5c3cef9c15.jpg)  
Wendingen, serie 11, nr. 9, 1930.

(Right) Cover by Peter Alma.

(Below) Pages with reproductions of Arntz's wall painting, here titled 'Het nieuwe Weenen' (The new Vienna); and from Gesellschaft und Wirtschaft.

![](images/b8807cee8957139a981a9dfb5ecae8482c21c305d5984deb150f7d25c78d7a95.jpg)

![](images/0fd201db8a40de718efca076fa3a01042531a5d8ecf2abbf4c8157641b05f4a1.jpg)  
At the top of the left page: Woodcuts from Peter Alma's series Geld (1929).

As was typical of all Wendingen issues, the story was largely told through the illustrations themselves. Thus, the large-scale reproduction on the issue's first page of Arntz's wall painting for the Museum immediately linked Vienna and the work of the Gesellschafts- und Wirtschaftsmuseum with the international artistic current here designated by the term ‘sociological graphics'. The image of Arntz's painting is followed by twelve reproductions from Gesellschaft and Wirtschaft, and more than twenty additional examples of sociological graphics, including works by Alma, Seiwert, Tschinkel, El Lissiztky, and Helios Gómez. The atlas charts illustrating the first section of Alma's brief, two-part introductory essay, Pictorial statistics and sociological graphics', appear to have been selected both for the diversity of conceptual and stylistic approaches they represent, as well as for their thematic variety. Similarly, the survey of sociological graphics provided by the selected prints and drawings reveals great stylistic diversity within the movement, demonstrating that the search for universal and collective forms did not, in fact, preclude individual differences in artistic temperament, despite the rhetoric of anti-individualism that prevails throughout the artists' writings.

At the same time, the selection of works also reveals certain consistent features, such as the prevalence of series-based works among these artists, and their tendency towards typological treatment of subjects.⁵⁴ This tendency is especially evident in the issue's featured woodcut series, which include two prints from Alma's series Geld (Money, 1929),

54. The publication of print series was a central aspect of the Progressives' artistic practice. The format complemented and facilitated a typological approach to subject matter, as can already be seen in Seiwert's early drawing series, 'Sieben Antlitze der Zeit' (Seven faces of our time). Published in the journal Die Ziegelbrenner in 1921, this series established many of the figurative-constructivist conventions for the representation of professional types (corresponding to both anatomical and dress features) and institutional types (corresponding to architectural features). Significantly, this series appears to have been the inspiration for other series-based publications by members of Seiwert's circle, including August Sander's famous photographic album, Antlitz der Zeit: sechzig Aufnahmen deutscher Menschen des 20. Jahrhunderts (Face of our time: sixty images of German people of the 2oth century; Munich: Kurt Wolff, 1929), as well as Arntz's 1927 portfolio, Zwölf Häuser der Zeit, and Alma's 1929 series, Acht portretten.

![](images/58f83646b537c090cb5b87b124a9b26902d1172792377eb29011e3140dfbdba7.jpg)

Wendingen, serie 11,nr.9, 1930.

Pages featuring woodcut prints by Alma from Acht portretten (1929) and by Arntz from Zwölf Häuser der Zeit (1927).

his entire series Acht portretten (Eight portraits, 1929), and four prints from Arntz's series Zwölf Häuser der Zeit (Twelve houses of our time, 1927), which has since become one of his best-known works. Produced during Arntz's initial period of long-distance collaboration with Neurath, Zwölf Häuser der Zeit features twelve architectural cross-sections each representing a different type of social institution. The institutions chosen by Arntz vary widely and represent the prevailing social order in a number of different ways. Generally speaking, though, the depicted settings seem to fall into two broad categories: the first relates directly to fundamental relations of production, power, and authority; this category includes a factory, prison, army barracks, bank, hospital, and apartment house.s⁵ The second category, to which the remaining prints in the series belong, relates more obliquely to relations of production and power; this category revolves around culture, consumption, and entertainment, and includes a department store, stadium, theater, hotel, bar, and bordello.⁵⁶

The typological approach, which figures so prominently in the Zwölf Häuser der Zeit series, is evident not only in the categorization of institutions, but also in the types of figures that inhabit those institutions. Rather than particular individuals, these faceless, identical figures represent social categories, distinguished from one another only by their respective uniforms and compositional placement. In Arntz's 'Stadion' (Stadium), for example, figures are distributed within the arena's seating according to type: the upper echelons of society – military officials, politicians, business elites and their wives – occupy the front tiers; behind them, in greater numbers but with fewer distinguishing characteristics, sit the masses, indicated only by their workers' caps and fedoras. This typological approach is even more pronounced in Alma's 1929 woodcut series Acht portretten, reproduced in its entirety in the Wendingen issue. There is some irony in the title, given the contrast between the connotations of individuality associated with portraiture and the series' reduction of individual figures to symbols for their respective professions. In this case the categories are limited to professions associated with power, wealth, and authority: military official, government minister, diplomat, banker, priest, lawyer, judge, and jailer.⁵7

The depiction of figures as social types was a central feature, not only in figurative-constructivist artworks, but within pictorial statistics as well. Among the pages from Gesellschaft und Wirtschaft reproduced in Wendingen, the chart depicting Social subdivision in Vienna’ is exemplary in this respect (see image overleaf). It presents a cross-section of society organized according to profession, charting developments between the eighteenth and twentieth centuries. By classifying the population of Vienna along professional lines, the social impact of industrialization becomes immediately evident: certain professional categories, such as nobility, clergy, and itinerant workers appear to have dropped out (or at least constitute such a small proportion of the population that they no longer register), replaced by new categories such as industrial workers and government administrators. Most notably, the proportion of labourers (coloured red and, within the context of the chart, distinguished from the self-employed) appears to have increased

55. Conspicuously absent from the series, as Arntz himself later remarked, are educational and religious institutions. See Arntz, Zeit unterm Messer, p. 82

56. As Arntz later explained, the series was conceived as a worldly analogue to the twelve celestial 'houses' of the astrological horoscope, and in this way it provided a critique of metaphysical worldviews: in these vignettes the fate of individuals is not to be explained by unseen, outside forces; rather, Arntz asserts, a rational explanation is to be found in the material and social forces that shape the course of their lives. See Arntz, Zeit unterm Messer, p. 22. In Arntz's anti-metaphysical stance, we encounter yet another affinity with Neurath, who founded the Vienna Method on the principle that only concrete and empirically verifiable objects could be depicted – thereby precluding the expression of metaphysical content. On this point, see pp.87-92 above.

57. Images of a bishop and a pensioner were added to the original 'eight portraits' in 1931, which were then published in the following year by the Socialistische Kunstenaarskring (Socialist artists' circle) as a small concertina-folded booklet titled Kapitalistische orde (Capitalist order).

![](images/6d83322eb5aba7401c84bfa4387a96e0b15634a8b235af209687576bc0f61c93.jpg)  
Social subdivision in Vienna Gesellschaft und Wirtschaft, 193o, no.81.

from less than half of the population in 17oo to more than two-thirds in the present, with the vast majority employed by the industrial sector. Seen through this typological lens, figures in both sociological graphics and pictorial statistics become collective protagonists, representing class dynamics in ways that individually recognizable actors could not – or would be less effective in so doing.

Beyond its illustrations, the Wendingen issue is especially noteworthy for Alma's introductory essay, particularly the second section, which self-consciously articulates sociological graphics' unifying features and common objectives. Above all, these artists employed what Alma described as a ‘sociological perspective' in their work, taking subjects (here Alma used the term 'data') similar to those handled by pictorial statistics: ‘movements of the masses', relations of ‘wealth-poverty labor-capital, rural and urban life', and the conflicts arising from the contradiction of class interests'. Such a perspective, according to Alma, required an emotional distance from the subject matter – an attitude of 'objectivity' (zakelijkheid). For Alma (as well as for Tschinkel), objectivity in art meant the elimination of individualizing features that

might express emotional or subjective content, and their replacement with generalized characteristics. 'We are interested in worker X', Alma explained, not for his private life but as a social driving force in a factory with 15,ooo workers who are jointly producers and class warriors'. Likewise, 'the personal interests of a factory owner do not interest us, but the fact that the goods he produces have an important place in the world order, thereby becoming a source of power and making him a "socially” influential man'. From this 'objective' vantage point, Alma could see little room for the accommodation of subjective expression or, it must be added, purely aesthetic' considerations. He was careful, moreover, to distinguish his notion of objectivity (which, like pictorial statistics, aimed to document general trends) from the kind associated with the Neue Sachlichkeit (New objectivity) in painting. The latter tendency, Alma argued, foregrounded the particularities of surface appearance at the expense of social content. Yet this approach retained its appeal among mass audiences, who, Alma lamented, were still too under the spell of the traditions of naturalism and individualism'.s8

Tschinkel was similarly critical of the Neue Sachlichkeit painters' reliance on naturalism – a tendency that he, like Alma, associated with an anachronistic individualism. Tschinkel articulated this position in his article ‘Quantity-pictures and collective form', published in Výtvarné Snahy, where it was illustrated with ‘a selection of sociological graphics' from the collection of the Gesellschafts-und Wirtschaftsmuseum.⁵ In place of the emphasis on appearances that characterized the painting of the Neue Sachlichkeit, Tschinkel advocated a new collective' mode of figuration, free of naturalistic detail and private-subjective contents'. In developing this collective form', he found a model in modernist typography: ‘The letter d is all the more a letter d’, Tschinkel noted, the closer it approaches its standard form, and becomes less so when it is individually adorned'.6º Typography, in this case, offered more than a mere analogy; indeed, modernist typography provided an assortment of forms that could serve as the basis for the new figuration. In Tschinkel's illustrations for the Czech journal Slunce, as well as in Seiwert's advertisement for his design practice, dashes, punctuation marks, and rules are recombined to create elemental figures akin to the unadorned

![](images/f2cf037c7f29d39d10048d0b0e16939b82ea9c6464d6c89ebc303cfcd5833253.jpg)  
Typographic construction by Augustin Tschinkel, published in Slunce, roč.3, číslo. 3, September 1931.  
Advertisement for Franz W. Seiwert's practice in 'modern typography, design for print, and photomontage', published in A bis Z, 3. Folge, Blatt 29, December 1932, p.116.

franz w. seiwert eigelstein 147|I köln moderne typografie und druckgestaltung fotomontage

letterforms advocated by practitioners of 'New Typography'.1 Like the sanserif typefaces that Jan Tschichold championed as 'the only [letterforms] in spiritual accordance with our time', Tschinkel and Seiwert conceived of their type-inspired figures as accurate reflections of the mechanized and standardized character of contemporary life.6² Such depictions, which ‘pay no attention to whether one is shaved or not, how many wrinkles he has in his face, or how many calluses he has on his hands', stood in stark contrast to the Neue Sachlichkeit painters' literal representations, which, Tschinkel maintained, failed to 'grasp the character of the time and of socialism'.63

Tschinkel elaborated these points in a subsequent essay that appeared in the November 193o issue of A bis Z, titled Tendency and form', which featured eight graphic works by Progressives, including Arntz, Alma, Tschinkel, Seiwert, and Hoerle. Tschinkel again contrasted the Progressives' work with the various social-realist strains championed by many on the political left, asserting that the latter were merely revolutionary at the level of subject matter; at the level of form – which, for Tschinkel, carried equal importance – social realism appeared reactionary. In its lack of formal innovation and its preoccupation with narrativè content at the expense of form, this linkstendenziöse Kunst' (left-partisan art) was, by Tschinkel's account, sentimental, impressionistic, and dependent upon clichés. 'Real social art', he maintained, if it were to 'present people as products of their relationships', must also be formally tendentious'. To illustrate this point, Tschinkel turned to the prints reproduced in the adjacent column depicting factory scenes that show individual people as actual constituent parts of an operation, which the employer can calculate numerically, like other inventory'.4 The factory workers in the accompanying reproduction of Seiwert's

61. The New Typography movement, for which Jan Tschichold's 1928 book Die neue Typographie became the seminal text, advocated a position that embraced standardization in production and demanded that function (rather than purely aesthetic considerations) dictate form. Such ideas had great appeal for the Progressives and there exist many points of intersection and collaboration between them and the designers who championed New Typography: Výtvarné Snahy, the journal in which Tschinkel first published his essay on 'collective form', carried the first Czech translations of Tschichold's text in 1929, one year after the book's publication in Germany. Ladislav Sutnar, the journal's editor and designer as well as Tschinkel's long-time colleague and collaborator, was one the major proponents of the new typography beginning in October 1930, adopted Paul Renner's Futura typeface for captions, subheadings, page numbers, and footer. Again, the points of overlap and intersection are numerous: Seiwert had been featured in Tschichold's 1928 book and later participated alongside Tschichold in the 1932 'Modern typography' exhibition in Warsaw and Łódź. Futura, moreover, had earlier been adopted as the official typeface for publications of the Gesellschafts- und Wirtschaftsmuseum in Vienna, having been recommended to Neurath by Tschichold and put into use under Arntz's direction. On Futura, its utilization at the Gesellschafts-

und Wirtschaftsmuseum, and   
Tschichold's connection to   
Neurath, see Christopher Burke,   
Paul Renner: the art of typography, pp. 86–91; and Active literature: Jan Tschichold and New Typography, p. 119. On Sutnar's design work and collaboration with Tschinkel, see lva Janáková & Jeffrey Buehler (ed.), Ladislav Sutnar – Praha – New   
York – design in action (Prague:   
Argo, 2003).   
62. Jan Tschichold, The New   
Typography, p. 73.   
63. Tschinkel, 'Das Mengenbild und die Kollektivformen'.   
64. Tschinkel, 'Tendenz und   
Form', A bis Z, 2. Folge, Blatt 12,   
November 1930, p. 45.

die ablehnung von tendenz in der kunst durch gelehrte wie ungelehrte meinung von rechts ist längst alsablehnung linker tendenz durchschaut, da andere tendenz, sei sie religiös, dynastisch oder sonstwie reaktiondr, rlen ,kunstgenuß" nicht stört. wohl hatte die kritik ein wichtiges argument zur hand. alle linkstendenziöse kunst reichte qualitatv an die bürgerliche bestenfalls nur heran; sie war nicht imstande, eine neue qualität aus sich auszulösen. es wurde eine kunstanschauung von armen leuten vertreten, die das bürgerliche an dieser armeleutekunst nicht sah. verschiedene faktoren trugen dazu bei, daß das ganze problein allgemein sentimental und nur im hinblick auf den literarisch deutbaren inhalt betrachtet wurde: schwielige füäuste, freiheitsfackeln, augenhöhlen, aus denen das elend blickt, aufgehende sonnen und was sonst noch eindruck macht. formal: impressionistisch, neusachltch. ülbersehend, daß das streben nach vulgäirer Hhnlichkeit eine verherrlichung der reaktionär-individualistischen verschiedenheit sei. was aber die wahre ähnlichkeit, die wesentliche ähniichken und rleren dialektische ver-Hnderung aufzeigt, das zeiht man des utopismus und vergißt, daß bereits der monopolistische kapitalismus eine Gberwindung des individualismus schon heute andeutet. bezeichnenderwcise war die masse seilten dargestellter gegenstand. am wenigsten: die nicht leidende, die in jedem sinn aktive und produktive masse. wirkich sozxiale kunst ist auch formal tendenziös. so erst kann sie den menschen als produkt seiner verhliltnisse darstellen. so erst wird sie ihren wert behalten, wenn auch ihr inhalt der vergangenheit angehören wird. das fabrikbıld zeigt einzelmenschen als wirkliche bestandtetle eines betriebes, die der unternehmer zahlenmißig kalkulieren kann, wie anderes inventar. das zu zelgen sst wichtiger als runzeln und schweißtropfen. wo der mensch in der masse, die masse im produktionsrythmus aufgeht, werden die bisherigen pathetschen massendarstellungen als verschiedene spielarten des defreggerstils erledigt ob die massen selbst die progressiv-soziale kunst verstehen werden? da muß man schon sagen, daß es daneben noch eine menge anderer wichtiger dinge gibt, die zu verstehen die massen leider ebenfalls noch wert entfernt sind. doch vor allem müßte die masse diese kunst Überhaupt zu sehen bekommen! von den bekannten proletkulturwarten aus wird noch läingere zeit hindurch ihr allerhand proletkunst in verkapptem großvaterstil vorgesetzt werden wir danken der zeitschrift vytvarné snahy", prag, für die überlassung der klischees von den arbeiten von orntz, krinski, seiwert, tschinkell.

A bis Z, 2. Folge, Blatt 12, November 193o. Tschinkel's article 'Tendenz und Form' featuring reproductions of the prints:

Peter Alma, Straße (Street) 1928; Franz W. Seiwert, Die Fabrik (Factory) 1923; Augustin Tschinkel, Gang zur Fabrik (Way to the factory) 1929.

linocut, 'Die Fabrik' (Factory), do in fact appear as constituent parts' of the factory operation: their bodies are integrated into the machinery so that their potential movements seem governed by the wheels and belts that frame them, and their facial features have been replaced with the same printed numbers that appear on the surfaces of the factory machinery. Indeed, these workers appear as though they could literally be calculated numerically' by an employer.

Břetislav Mencák, in his introduction to the booklet, Soziale Grafik (1932), reiterated Tschinkel's emphasis on the collective nature of standardized, geometric forms.“⁵ With the subtitle, ‘a picture book with an international selection', Soziale Grafik assembled nineteen graphic works by six members of the Group of progressive artists, including Alma, Arntz, Hoerle, Seiwert, and Tschinkel. Echoing Tschinkel, Mencák differentiated these works from social realism by means of a typographic analogy:

These forms are as far removed from the elements of form used in bourgeois realism and its poor-people-art as contemporary typography is from ornate parchment manuscripts: their content and thematic

65. The design of Soziale Grafik exhibits numerous formal similarities with A bis Z and again reveals the influence of Tschichold's ideas: the exclusive use of lowercase; a sanserif typeface; and the use of bold type to articulate text. The booklet Soziale Grafik further follows the example of Tschichold's book, in listing the names and addresses of the publication's participants. See Tschichold, The New Typography, pp. 96-106.

66. Břetislav Mencák, foreword to Soziale Grafik (1932), p.1. Mencák's foreword appears to derive largely from ideas articulated by Tschinkel in his essays of the previous two years. 'Poor-people-art' was, in fact, a term that Tschinkel had previously employed to deride social realism in his article, 'Uměni století stroje' (Art in the machine age), Naše cesta, roč. 2, číslo.8, 1931, pp.99-100.

67. See Arntz & Tschinkel (ed.), F. W. Seiwert – Gemälde, Grafik, Schriften (Prague, 1934; reprint, The Hague: Edition bo, 1976).

68. See the exhibition catalog De olympiade onder dictatuur (DooD): tentoonstelling: sport, kunst, wetenschap, documenten (Amsterdam: Augustus, 1936); see also Peter Hofland, et al. Die Olympiade unter der Diktatur. Rekonstruktion der Amsterdamer Kunstolympiade 1936: Kunst im Widerstand (Berlin: Stadtmuseum Berlin, 1996). Alma was one of the exhibition's organizers, and contributed a

The booklet Soziale Grafik, produced in 1932 in Czechoslovakia, was the last publication to bring together work by this international network of artists at such a scale. The Progressives' journal, A bis Z, published its last issue in February 1933, a month before the collapse of the Weimar Republic. With the National Socialist seizure of power in Germany and the subsequent defeat of the Social Democrat government in Vienna, Czechoslovakia took on an increasingly important position in the promotion of both figurative-constructivist art and pictorial statistics. As ‘an act of protest against the powers of reaction' in Germany – but also as a commemoration of Seiwert, who had died during the summer of 1933 - Arntz and Tschinkel published an anthology of Seiwert's art and writings in Prague in 1934, largely collected from Die Aktion and A bis Z.“7

starting point is the more important reality of mechanization, rationalization, collectivization, and the overcoming of individualism.

As the decade progressed and the political situation on the continent continued to deteriorate, international ties became increasingly difficult to maintain and the possibilities for artistic collaboration in the form of publications and exhibitions quickly dried up. Arntz, Alma and Tschinkel did participate together in one last group exhibition in Amsterdam in 1936 – an anti-fascist exhibition titled 'De Olympiade onder dictatuur' (Olympics under dictatorship; for which the acronym Dood ominously spelled out 'death' in Dutch), organized to coincide with the Berlin Olympics.6⁸ The three artists remained active as painters and printmakers through the remaining years of the decade, though each survived primarily on commercial work, which largely involved the design of statistical charts. Of the three, only Arntz followed Neurath to The Hague, where they continued a collaboration that produced the major publication Modern man in the making (see pp. 325-9 below). Alma, after working for several years in the Soviet Union, returned to Amsterdam where he set up his own graphic design studio, independent of Neurath.69 Tschinkel, who in 1931 returned to Prague to take a position

series of drawings entitled 'Fascistische terreur' (Fascist terror) that included the aforementioned work, 'De oorlog maakt de man', featuring amputee symbols in an arrangement evoking the Vienna Method's rows of pictograms. While Tschinkel was the only artist included from Czechoslovakia, he was one of the best-represented artists there, having contributed nine works to the exhibition. A woodcut by Arntz (who now worked and exhibited under the pseudonym 'Dubois'), which was critical of the Nazi regime, created some controversy when German officials intervened to have it removed from the exhibition.

69. In a pamphlet from 1935 advertising his practice, Alma listed among his clients the municipal government of Amsterdam, the port of Rotterdam, and AVRO (the General Association of Radio

Broadcasting in the Netherlands). The pamphlet's text was largely taken from the first section of his Wendingen essay, wherein Alma had first defined and described the Vienna Method for a Dutch audience. Significantly, this updated text omits the earlier version's references to Neurath and – perhaps more importantly - references to the anticipated social and political functions of pictorial statistics.

![](images/fd5f0a2b074a6463b8e7ce14d6963f5adc84fc917af0b614e5f4994b24614ae7.jpg)

as a book designer with the state publisher, worked in the ensuing years to introduce the Vienna Method of Pictorial Statistics into the Czechoslovak state school system. His greatest achievement in this regard was the 1935 publication, Malá vlastivěda (Little civics reader) – an elaborately and elegantly designed atlas of Czechoslovak history and geography, largely modelled after the Gesellschaft und Wirtschaft atlas, on which he had worked five years earlier. Co-designed with Ladislav Sutnar and printed by the state publisher, Malá vlastivěda ranks – alongside Gesellschaft und Wirtschaft and Modern man in the making – as one of the era's great works of pictorial statistics.

## Conclusion

The relationship between figurative-constructivist graphics and pictorial statistics may best be understood as one in flux. At the outset, the connection appears to have been merely practical: many of the forms that Arntz had developed already satisfied Neurath's requirements – or at least pointed towards formal solutions - in their tendency towards

Bedřich Mendl (author), Malá vlastivěda (Prague: Státní nakladatelství, 1935). Compiled, edited and designed by Ladislav Sutnar, with maps and illustrations by Augustin Tschinkel. The lower spread emulates the charts about cities and population from Gesellschaft und Wirtschaft (see pp. 212–13 above).

70. Some pictograms designed by Sutnar in 1946 for charts in Fortune magazine are very similar to those in Malá vlastivěda, which are more geometric than Viennese counterparts. See Janáková & Buehler (ed.), Ladislav Sutnar, pp. 210-11.

reduction and standardization. But as the Gesellschafts- und Wirtschaftsmuseum's expanded activities brought other members of the Progressives to Vienna (who in turn began to promote pictorial stat istics in their own publications and saw in the Museum an opportunity to promote their free work), the connection was increasingly framed in terms of shared values. Such values included a belief in art's communicative and social-educational function, and the conviction that education could be reconceived in more democratic and participatory terms. In promoting these values, both projects sought to create strategies of presentation that would communicate social content at the broadest level possible and provide audiences with new analytical tools with which to reflect upon social issues that affected them. Above all it was in their intention to appeal to a mass audience at an intellectual level that both projects most clearly reflect the shared belief that the general population possessed the ability to draw well-informed conclusions and participate responsibly in a democratic society. However, after the dissolution of the Progressives group in 1933 and the forced exile of the Vienna Museum's core team (including Arntz) in 1934, the initial excitement over the two projects' potential connections began to abate. By the mid-193os, Alma and Tschinkel appear to have backed away from the claim made earlier in their writings that sociological graphics and pictorial statistics represented two fronts in the same struggle. The short-lived period of their collaboration in Vienna, from 1929 through 1930, represents the high-water mark of this notion.

## Photography at the Gesellschafts- und Wirtschaftsmuseum

The Gesellschafts- und Wirtschaftsmuseum in Wien kept extensive files of photographs, mostly taken by its staff photographer Walter Pfitzner (right). In addition to the photographic record of its pictorial charts, known as T[afel] files, there were the N[atur] files, which documented aspects of life in Social-Democratic Vienna. Achievements of the municipality were photographed, including its housing programme and provision of modern Kindergartens. In addition, several sequences of photographs documented professions for use in vocational guidance seminars. Otto Neurath described this part of the Museum's work:

A photographic studio is engaged mainly in producing factual photographs – not intended to evoke aesthetic sensations, but to emphasize the more significant aspects of the object, in preparation for drawings that will further simplify the outlines for graphic presentation. ('Museums of the future' [1933], p. 479.)

This implies that the photographs were used as source material for the design of pictograms (see p. 92 above); but also that the aim in making the photographs was an objectivity akin to that of the 'New Photography' developed in the 192os. Pfitzner's attempts to capture typical actions, particularly in the photographs of professions, can be likened to the typological portraits by August Sander published in his Antlitz der Zeit (Face of our time; 1929). Indeed Sander was an associate of the Group of Progressive Artists (three of whom contributed to the Vienna Method), and he visited the Gesellschafts- und Wirtschaftsmuseum in 193o. (cB)

# 5 Picturing Soviet progress Izostat 1931-4

Emma Minns

The time spent in the ussR by Otto Neurath and his colleagues from the Gesellschafts- und Wirtschaftsmuseum between 1931 and 1934 is an intriguing episode in the history of the Vienna Method and Isotype. It has attracted considerable interest but has not previously been examined in detail.¹ Indeed some mystery still surrounds the institution that Neurath helped to establish, the All-Union Institute of Pictorial Statistics of Soviet Construction and Economy – Bcecoroçíûié xo3ařctBa; Vsesoiuznyi institut izobrazitel'noi statistiki sovetskogo stroitel'stva i khoziaistva – commonly abbreviated as Izostat (İçoctat).²

Links between Austria and the ussR had been developed since 1925 by an Austrian society for the promotion of intellectual and economic relations with the ussR'.³ Among the proponents of this society were Moritz Schlick, of the Vienna Circle, and Hans Kampffmeyer, Neurath's colleague in the settlers' movement. Neurath became active in the society along with others from the Vienna Circle. The society's point of contact in the ussR was.the All-Union Society for Cultural Relations th Frig ntris ( 3arpaíöeé; Vsesoiuznoe obshchestvo kul'turnoi sviazi s zagranitsei – vOks), from which the Gesellschafts- und Wirtschaftsmuseum had sometimes obtained research material. Neurath offered the Volkshalle at Vienna's Neues Rathaus to voks for an exhibition planned to mark the tenth anniversary of the October Revolution, but the Vienna city council denied permission for this on political grounds. The Museum did host a smaller exhibition in early 1931 of Soviet children's books.

In autumn of 1929, and again in early 193o, Neurath proposed to voks that an exhibition could be made by the Gesellschafts- und Wirtschaftsmuseum for travelling in the UssR.⁵ Through such contacts the Soviet embassy in Vienna was made aware of the Museum's work. Marie Neurath remembered visits by Soviet embassy staff, who were interested in the representation of statistics, as everybody is who is proud of progress'. But the Soviet authorities did not commission the Vienna Museum to make pictorial statistics for use in the UssR; instead they decided to invite the Viennese team to help establish a Soviet institute. In preparation for the work of Izostat, the Council of People's Commissars passed a decree on 12 September 1931 stating that all public and cooperative organizations, unions and schools are obliged to apply pictorial statistics according to the method of Dr Neurath'.' In November 1931 the Izostat Institute opened with Neurath as its director, and it was contractually agreed that at least five members of the Vienna Museum staff would always be present in Moscow, principally to instruct the Russians in the application of the Vienna Method.8 Neurath himself agreed to spend sixty days per year in Moscow.º This arrangement continued until the end of 1934, at which point the Izostat Institute was reorganized; it continued to operate without Viennese involvement until 1940.¹º

The Izostat Institute was not part of Neurath's planned Mundaneum museum network; instead it came under the jurisdiction of the Soviet government." It produced pictorial statistics during one of the most tumultuous decades in Soviet history. By means of the First and Second

4. This was opened by the Soviet ambassador Konstantin lurenev at the Parkring venue of the Museum and it was on display for one week. It had previously been shown in other European cities. See Köstenberger, 'Otto Neurath und die Sowjetunion', p.103.

5. Köstenberger refers to this as consisting of 100 charts, so Neurath could have intended it to be a version of Gesellschaft und Wirtschaft. The Soviet trade delegation in Vienna had also already commissioned nine charts from the

Five-Year Plans Joseph Stalin attempted to transform the Soviet Union through massive economic and industrial growth. Financial investment was targeted at heavy industry, but the most infamous outcome of this economic strategy was the forced collectivization of agriculture, a process that brought suffering to millions.1² During the time that Vienna Museum staff worked at Izostat, the Institute was primarily engaged in producing charts that depicted the success of the First Five-Year Plan (1928-32) and others that predicted the fulfilment of the Second Five-Year Plan (1933-7). In both of these cases, a notable feature of Izostat charts is the frequent inclusion of estimated data for future years. In Vienna, as a rule, charts were produced only according to empirical data;¹³ in the ussR, visualization of projected targets was an important part of Izostat work. It must be acknowledged that much of the statistical data provided to Izostat staff for transformation into charts was undoubtedly exaggerated, distorted, or falsified.¹⁴ This is a troubling and contentious issue: how, for example, could Neurath and his team actively participate in visualizing statements about the availability of consumer goods that were simply not true?1⁵ (See example overleaf.)

It seems unlikely that political ideology prompted Neurath to agree to work in Moscow; he was a Social Democrat, not a Communist, and his time in the ussR did not change his outlook.16 Indeed, in a letter to the Vienna Circle philosopher Rudolf Carnap, Neurath admitted to putting ideological differences to one side in order to work in Moscow: 'Over there I'm a technical specialist and abstain from all arguments which only seem to lead to differences. ... But I accept the consequences

14. Wheatcroft and Davies comment that 'Published statistics ... suffered a further distortion as compared with internal statistics for official use: Soviet successes were exaggerated and failures minimised or simply omitted from the

15. The most outspoken critic of Neurath's work in the Soviet Union has been Clive Chizlett in his article 'Damned lies. And statistics. Otto Neurath and Soviet propaganda in the 193os'. Robin Kinross responded to this in 'Blind eyes, innuendo and the politics of design: a reply to Clive Chizlett'. Kinross highlighted a number of flaws in Chizlett's piece, such as his assertion that Neurath was a Communist. The mistakes and accusatory tone in Chizlett's article overshadow a number of important issues he raises, such as 'the degree to which Neurath ... found credible the raw data supplied to them by Soviet officials' (p. 307). Chizlett asserted: 'It can be shown that Otto Neurath helped the Soviet authorities in the early 193os to

compile and publish false statistics for the purpose of propaganda' (p. 321). This implies collusion on Neurath's part, which cannot be proven. Before the establishment of Izostat, Neurath was aware that the general Soviet population endured difficult conditions. In Lebensgestaltung und Klassenkampf (1928) he wrote: 'There are cases where one can speak of socialist community life there in the Soviet Union, but socialist enthusiasm is dampened by misery and the restrictions of the social and economic order.' ('Personal life and class struggle', p. 253.) By the early 1940s Neurath's scepticism concerning life in the ussR became evident in the marginalia he left in books and pamphlets on various aspects of Soviet society that he acquired in Oxford (now in the Isotype Collection). Often Neurath simply underlined

passages, but there were occasions when he was motivated to write in the books. One example is / am a woman from Soviet Russia, by B. Moore-Pataleewa (London: Victor Gollancz, 1943), which was presented as a truthful biography of a woman's life in the Soviet Union in the 192os. In the preface the author stated 'most names ... are recorded in full'; Neurath noted: 'endangering them – if the autobiography is genuine'.

16. Marie Neurath recalled that other Germans and Austrians working in the Soviet Union at this time did become communists, including one female member of Gesellschafts und Wirtschaftsmuseum staff. ('What I remember', p. 49.) This was possibly Edith Matzalik, who remained in Moscow after the official contract with the Vienna Museum expired in 1934.

Chart from The struggle for five years in four. 1932. 184×227mm.

One subject frequently presented in Izostat charts was the production of shoes, and they always showed a rise in production. However, as the historian Sheila Fitzpatrick has explained: 'Clothing, shoes, and all kinds of consumer goods were in even shorter supply than basic foodstuffs, often being completely unobtainable. ... Shoes were one of the worst problems for consumers.' (Everyday Stalinism, New York & Oxford: Oxford University Press, 1999, pp.44–5.)

17. Neurath to Carnap, 1 October 1932, quoted in Galison, 'Aufbau/ Bauhaus', p. 741. Neurath gave details of one disagreement with Soviet officials in a letter he wrote (in faulty English) to Carl Herz: 'I remember that communists [were] very irritated, as in usSR, after they have told me of the decrease of illiteracy I said, of course you have an increase of suicide, but they explained, since the living conditions increased the suicides have to decrease (I do not know, whether this is Marxism or not), but by chance I know that the suicide figures increased up to 1927 and then – the government ceased to publish them, perhaps out of an ANSCHAuUNG [ideology].' (Letter of 25 October 1942; Carl Herz papers 60/7, IISH.)

18. Marie Neurath, 'Otto Neurath and Isotype', p. 13.

19. 'Gerd Arntz', interview in Pulchri, p. 4.

![](images/a165ffb463f3d983b66c8ed0339cfc31db603e51e0e4474198525925484f9858.jpg)

of this ideological abstinence and concentrate on the technical.'¹7 As Marie Neurath explained later:

Otto Neurath had foreseen that certain things might be asked from us which were against our convictions; in the negotiations on the contract he had therefore insisted on a clause that we could not be forced to execute work which we thought to be against our principles; to counterbalance this, the Soviet institute would not publish or exhibit any of our work of which they did not approve.1⁸

Gerd Arntz recalled that ‘There was nothing that we were forced to do, and moreover [we] could refuse, we were entitled to do so.'19

Izostat offered a state-sponsored opportunity to expand the Vienna Method in terms of staff, resources and distribution. At one stage the Izostat staff numbered more than 70, many more than the staff of the Gesellschafts- und Wirtschaftsmuseum in Vienna. Neurath's hopes for the development of pictorial statistics in the ussR are clearly expressed in an article he wrote for the German-language Soviet newspaper Moskauer Rundschau in June 1932:

The Institute of Pictorial Statistics, newly founded at the end of 1931, which is to make the whole of the Soviet Union aware of the new method from its base in Moscow, is training a team of Soviet specialists, who are to devise pictorial statistics for newspapers, schools, business operations and many other purposes. Special games, teaching aids and other tools of enlightenment will be developed. Exemplary museums and touring exhibitions are planned according to the Vienna Method, and the construction of a large museum in Moscow is already being considered, along with the establishment of an Institute building with all the necessary test facilities. 20

Marie Neurath recalled that plans for the museum went as far as to commission a design from Josef Frank, and that a central Moscow location was earmarked, opposite the Bolshoi Theatre on what is now Teatral’naia Square.²¹

Another factor that may have contributed to Neurath's decision to work in Moscow was the friendship Neurath and Marie Reidemeister had developed with the Russian artist Lazar (El) Lissitzky and his wife, art historian Sophie Lissitzky-Küppers. The two couples first met in spring 1928 at the International Press Exhibition Pressa' in Cologne (Lissitzky had overseen the design of the Soviet Pavilion there and the Gesellschafts- und Wirtschaftsmuseum contributed to the Austrian section; see p. 94 above.) They all met again in Lissitzky's 'dacha' half an hour by train from Moscow; 'I spent any free day there, when I was in Moscow without Otto', recalled Marie Neurath.2² Yet this was more than a personal relationship; Sophie Lissitzky-Küppers later wrote that her husband was fascinated by the Vienna Method: For Lissitzky this was a discovery which seemed to him to have importance for Moscow. Dr Neurath was very interested in the Soviet Union and there arose a relationship based on mutual understanding, which later led to the arrival in Moscow of the Neurath group.'²³ This 'mutual understanding was perhaps not the primary reason for the establishment of Izostat, but such enthusiasm for the Vienna Method from a respected figure in the world of Russian art and design (although not always officially in favour) must have encouraged Neurath and his team.

.The first staff members from the Gesellschafts- und Wirtschaftsmuseum to visit Moscow at the end of 1931 were Otto Neurath, Marie Reidemeister, Gerd Arntz and Josef Scheer.²⁴ Marie Neurath recounted:

In Moscow we stayed at the Grand Hotel; for an office, we were given first a room in the state publishing house; Otto had his table in a niche separated off by a glass wall; I sat with the others at a long table and

24. A long passage describing arrival in the ussR from Marie Neurath's memoir 'What I remember' is quoted by Kinross in 'Blind eyes, innuendo and the politics of design'. One of Neurath and Arntz's first activities in Russia was a day trip to the newly established Ford Motor plant at Nizhnii Novgorod (known as Gor'kii between 1932 and 199o). The plant inspired Arntz's 1935 woodcut Fabrikbesichtigung. (Arntz, Zeit unterm Messer, p. 101.)

could hardly move my elbows. When I began to teach transformation to Maria Alexandrovna Orlova (who later married Bauermeister), I was able to do this in Otto's room, and he listened with half an ear, smiling. 25

Most of the artists and technicians from the Museum spent some time at Izostat during the following three years: Friedrich Bauermeister was head of research;26 Arntz was in charge of design, with Bernath as his assistant; and Edith Matzalik led the graphics department.² Peter Alma spent less time than others in Moscow, as he was sent to establish a branch of the Izostat Institute in the Ukrainian city Khar'kov. Very little is known about the Khar'kov branch. In 1933 Otto Neurath stated that ‘a central institute for pictorial statistics, called Isostat, has been established in Moscow, where whole staffs are being trained for local bureaus, the first of which has just been opened in Khar'kov'.28 In addition to Neurath, Izostat had a Russian director who was a member of the Bolshevik Party and who reported directly to the Central Executive Committee. This role seems initially to have been fulfilled by Artemii Bagratovich Khalatov, chairman of the Union of State Publishing Houses (oGiz), given that Izostat was housed in the OGIz building 29 He was succeeded by at least three more Party members between 1931 and 1934. The first was Erik Adol'fovich Asmus, who on one occasion took Neurath to meet Nikolai Bukharin.³º Asmus was followed by a man referred to by Marie Neurath only as 'Komarovskii', but who seems to have been crucial in the development of Izostat. Under Komarovskii's supervision Izostat rapidly expanded to a staff of 75 and he allowed Reidemeister and Neurath to revise working methods: 'we divided this mass up into several parallel groups, each of whom could then learn every phase of the work and how to operate as a team.'³1 According to

25. Marie Neurath, 'What I remember', pp. 48–9.

26. Bauermeister was almost permanently based in Moscow between 1932 and 1934. Indeed Bauermeister divorced his German wife, who had accompanied him to Moscow, to marry Izostat staff member Maria Orlova. After the Museum's contract with Moscow ended in 1934, Bauermeister remained in Russia and later worked at a geographical institute. Marie Neurath, 'What I remember', p. 44.

27. Krichevskii, 'Izostatistika i "Izostat", p. 65.

28. 'Museums of the Future' (1933), p. 479. Arntz recalled that Alma worked in Khar'kov alongside Matzalik and a Russian artist named Korotkov. ('3.7.72', p. 4.) According to a study of the work of Alma, he first trained with Arntz in Moscow in the autumn of 1932 before traveling to Khar'kov to set up the new institute there along with other, Russian Izostat staff members. E. Luermans, Peter Alma: een documentair verslag van een beeldend kunstenaar in het interbellum (MA thesis, University of Amsterdam, 1978). My thanks to Wim Jansen for this information.

29. Arntz, Zeit unterm Messer, p. 30. Khalatov's wife also later worked at Izostat. The OGIZ

building's address was 9 Bol'shoi Komsomol'skii pereulok (now Bol'shoi Zlatoustinskii pereulok). Khalatov was a Party administrator who headed ogiz until some time in 1932; he was later arrested and died in prison in 1938. See Gregory P. M. Walker, 'Soviet publishing since the October Revolution', in M. Beaven Remnek (ed.), Books in Russia and the Soviet Union: past and present, (Wiesbaden: Otto Harrassowitz, 1991), p. 68. In 1930 the state publisher Gosizdat (which was subsumed under oGiz in that year) had published a remarkable folding broadsheet about the First Five-Year Plan that featured some elements of pictorial statistics. It was designed by Aleksei Mikhailovich Laptev. See the reproduction in Michael Twyman, A history of chromolithography: printed colour for all (London: British Library, 2013), pp.280-1.

30. Bukharin was a senior Party member and editor of Pravda but also an opponent of Stalin and was expelled from the Politburo in 1929. He was restored as editor of Izvestiia in 1934, before finally being arrested and executed in 1938. Bukharin had been known for his 'contacts with the creative intelligentsia' (Robert Service, Stalin: a biography, London: Macmillan, 2004, p. 301); hence the visit from Asmus with Neurath. Asmus was later made the Soviet ambassador to Finland (1935-7) He was then recalled, arrested and later executed sometime during Stalin's extensive purge of Party members and government officials. 31. Marie Neurath, 'What 1 remember', p. 49. Komarovskii was later arrested and imprisoned and is mentioned in Alexander Weissberg's account of his own arrest and imprisonment in 1937, Conspiracy of silence (London: Hamish Hamilton, 1952), p. 322: 'Komarovsky, a Communist and high official of the Party, an intelligent even-tempered man.' Following Komarovskii, Arntz named the next Russian Izostat director as 'Vasilevskii'. ('3.7.'72', p.3.)

Arntz, Izostat consisted of around thirty persons in the drawing and printing department, an 'indeterminate' number in the transformation department and many interpreters.³² Matzalik's graphics department was divided into four ‘brigades' (work teams), each consisting of a chief artist, an artist, a linocutter, and paste-up artists.³3

Marie Neurath remembered two Russian staff in particular: Maria Aleksandroyna Orlova, whom she taught transformation; and Ivan Petrovich Ivanitskii. Ivanitskii was perhaps the most significant Russian staff member at Izostat and is credited in many of its publications. He was employed at the Institute throughout its existence, avoiding dismissal or arrest. According to Arntz, Ivanitskii was one of the senior Russian transformers'. Marie Neurath described him as ascientific collaborator' who had made pictorial statistics in the ussR before we arrived, but then had to acknowledge that ours were better, and he worked with us quite happily'.³⁴

Ivanitskii's modesty should not diminish the importance of his contribution to the development of pictorial statistics in Russia prior to the arrival of the advisers from Vienna. Izostat had a predecessor in the Department of Pictorial Statistics (Otdel izobrazitel'noi statistiki), which existed only briefly but produced at least one publication of significance: a series of 72 postcards, the title of which translates as ‘To catch up with and overtake the leading capitalist countries in technical and economic relations in ten years'.³⁵ Ivanitskii oversaw the compilation of this set of cards, which was accompanied by a pamphlet explaining that their purpose was to give a numerical account of the results of the Sixth Congress of Soviets, but not in the form of columns and tables of dry and boring numbers, but in the form of images or pictograms,

33. A number of the Russian Izostat staff are listed by name in Arntz's memoir '3.7.'72', and a group photograph of Russian staff appears both in Bool & Broos, Gerd Arntz, p. 55, and Arntz, Zeit unterm Messer, p. 33. Included in this group is the artist Natal'ia Abramovna Ushakova (1899-199o) who was a well-known graphic artist and children's book illustrator. She is credited as one of the principal artists in the Izostat collection of charts The struggle for five years in four (Moscow: Ogiz-lzogiz, 1932; see pp. 269-73 for further discussion of this work).

34. Marie Neurath, 'What I remember', p. 49. Ivanitskii appears to have remained at Izostat until at least 1939.

35. I. P. Ivanitskii (ed.), Dognat' i peregnat' v tekhniko-ekonomicheskom otnoshenii peredovye kapitalisticheskie strany v 1o let (Moscow/Leningrad: Ogiz-Izogiz, 1931). The series was first issued as a collection of 72 cards; a second edition contained 7o cards. The title is based on a speech made by Stalin at the First All-Union Conference of Workers of Soviet Industry, 4 February 1931, in which he stated: 'We are fifty or a hundred years behind the advanced countries. We must make good this distance. Either we do it, or we shall go under.' Translated in R. G. Suny (ed.), The structure of Soviet history: essays and documents (New York & Oxford: Oxford University Press, 2003), pp. 294–5.
