![](images/cb500d2efa989c12e0aca30246934a668294523da225a0b24205e320a2b6436b.jpg)

Growing numbers of students in Soviet upper schools Each figure = 20,00o students (at colleges, universities, etc.).

Three cards show the filmstrip method. In translations on the reverse sides, the pictures in film frames are awkwardly referred to as 'shown in inset'. It is not stated that the sprocket holes divide each frame into increments of ten per cent.

capable of exciting the interest of every worker in the Soviet Union and every member of the international working class.'³⁶ The postcards depict the progress of the ussR in various areas of economic and social life, and include charts showing growth in sugar production, combine harvester production, electrification of the ussR, coal mining, and oil extraction. They are fascinating examples not only of Soviet visual propaganda – if the charts are to be believed, all areas of Soviet industry, society and economy were improving and those of capitalist countries were falling behind – but also of experimentation with pictorial statistics and in particular with the Vienna Method. The series demonstrates that Ivanitskii and his fellow artists were engaging with the work of the Vienna Museum sometime before Neurath arrived in Moscow, even if they did not adhere strictly to Vienna Method principles at that time. Indeed, the postcards employ a variety of graphic techniques to present data, some more successfully than others. For example, Ivanitskii created the graphic device of charts constructed by the means of an illustrated strip (like a filmstrip)’. This, he argued, combined the Vienna Method with the more familiar means of data presentation – comparison by length like with ordinary bar charts.'³7 According to Ivanitskii, the principal benefit of the filmstrip method was that each frame of film could be divided into ten parts (marked by sprocket holes on the edge) and therefore could present data with greater accuracy than Vienna Method pictograms.

Modifications to the Vienna Method, in particular the addition of backgrounds and illustrations, were seen by Ivanitskii and the Soviet authorities as crucially important for emphasizing the success of socialism and comparing it favourably to pre-revolutionary Russia and conditions in the capitalist West: we tried by the means of pictorial backgrounds to trigger certain associations, to interest the viewer, to acquaint them with the subject without prejudice to the actual chart.' The examples reproduced here show that the background illustrations did impinge on the presentation of statistical data in some cases, and, in one notable passage of Ivanitskii's introductory text for the postcards, he acknowledged such faults. He stressed their experimental nature, arguing that both the Vienna Method and the filmstrip method were new to the ussR, and therefore a number of errors and defects in the series is quite natural'. The poor quality of printing was partly blamed: 'machinery was not up to scratch and therefore the background of the cards sometimes conflicts with the charts.' His pamphlet concluded with the note that ‘a detailed explanation of the issues mentioned here

36. 'Ot izdatel'stva' (Publisher's preface), Dognat' i peregnat', p. 3 The Sixth All-Union Congress of Soviets took place in Moscow between 8 and 17 March 1931. 'Ot izdatel'stva' outlined the design methodology behind the creation of the cards and although it was not signed, Ivanitskii is most likely to have been the author. (My thanks to Howard Garfinkel of Productive Arts for providing a copy of this pamphlet.) The postcards were available as a box set, but it would appear that they could be individually purchased, as each card had a price of 1o kopecks printed on the reverse. Although the postcards only feature Russian within their main design, the title of each card was provided in German and English on the reverse.

37. [Ivanitski], 'Ot izdatel'stva', p. 6.

Front cover of Ivan Petrovich Ivanitskii, Izobrazitel'naia statistika i venskii metod (Pictorial statistics and the Vienna Method). 1932. 180× 258 mm.

will be given in a special brochure by Ivanitskii to be issued in the near future'.38 This was eventually published in April 1932 with the title Izobrazitel'naia statistika i venskii metod (Pictorial statistics and the Vienna Method). In this short book Ivanitskii attempted to explain to a Russian audience-the importance of pictorial statistics and to expound the virtues of both the Vienna Method and his filmstrip method. He included reproductions of some charts from Gesellschaft und Wirtschaft and of some of his postcards. Ivanitskii stressed the challenges faced by Izostat and the difficulty in creating suitable pictograms. Stating that amateurishness in this matter could not be tolerated', he justified the necessity of the Viennese contribution to the establishment of a ‘specialist institute' in Moscow.³9

38. [Ivanitski] 'Otizdatel'stva', pp.6, 8.

39. Ivanitski, Zzobrazitel'naia statistika i venskii metod, p. 43.

40. Ivanitskii, Izobrazitel'naia statistika i venskii metod, pp. 43-4.

![](images/7b88de52274fd6081bc87fbc775c56cccebcd9023ccc06d08a9e1a4814d88a02.jpg)

Ivanitskii and Neurath clearly shared an enthusiasm for disseminating the Vienna Method of Pictorial Statistics throughout the Soviet Union. Ivanitskii concluded his book by outlining a plan for the cheap production of pictorial statistics by non-specialists:

It is essential to simplify and standardize the manufacture of charts, so that they will take a minimal amount of time, so that they can be made not only by an artist, but by every worker, and so that the cost of a chart will not be ten roubles, but a few dozen kopecks. ... Anyone, who needs to construct a chart, will be able to stick ready-made figures, like postage stamps, onto a ready-made standard grid and will get a nice, cheap, attractive chart. In the case where there are no ready-made figures available, it will be possible to order a rubber stamp of one figure and print this as many times as required.⁴º

It seems that such a mail-order pictogram scheme was never realized during the time Neurath and his team were in Moscow. Neurath was not in favour of fully devolving the design of charts to the public; instead he wanted the process to be controlled by centralized offices, like Izostat.

Between 1932 and 1934 Izostat produced numerous books and albums published in Russian, English and other European languages, as well as contributing charts to publications for which it received no credit or acknowledgement.4¹ Apart from Neurath's own references to the activities of Izostat, the work of the Institute appears to have attracted little contemporary comment. In May 1934 the Soviet magazine Prozhektor (Spotlight) published an article that provided a brief outline of Izostat work.“² In addition to charts in publications, the article highlighted its more ephemeral outputs:

Recently the Institute has initiated an extensive programme of exhibiting its work. Among the most important displays made was the exhibition for the Seventeenth Party Congress, the street decorations for May Day, an exhibition in the Central Park of Culture and Recreation to commemorate the eleventh anniversary of the ussR constitution, project layout charts for the construction of the Moscow metro, a series of charts displaying the saving of the crew of the Cheliuskin,4³ flights into the stratosphere, the aeroplane Maksim Gor'kii', 'Bobriki', 'Dneproges' and others. In the exhibition of its work the Institute has penetrated many centres of the Union, including Alma-Ata and Ashkhabad.

The article was accompanied by images of Izostat charts in public spaces: one photograph showed a chart as part of a shop window display; another chart, showing the transformation of the Soviet economic base from agricultural to industrial, was apparently painted on the side of a Moscow apartment building (see overleaf)

During the period between 1931 and 1934, Gerd Arntz was apparently more involved with the training of Russian staff and the production of pictorial statistics at Izostat than Otto Neurath or Marie Reidemeister. Arntz visited Russia a number of times and one of his first tasks in Moscow was to take responsibility for a series of charts that appeared in the daily newspaper Izvestiia under the title U nas i u nikh' (Them

41. In addition to works highlighted in this chapter, Izostat charts featured in the following publications between 1931 and 1934: Na stroike sotsializma, (Moscow: lzogiz, 1933); Izo-bloknot agitatora, (Moscow: Izogiz, 1933); Pod maskoi razoruzheniia voina, (Leningrad: Lenizogiz, 1933); Aviatsiia i vozdukhoplavanie, (Moscow: Izogiz, 1934). Between 1935 and 194o Izostat continued to produce books and charts, including large albums that juxtaposed charts with photographs and illustrations. One of the most notable examples of these is the album Moskva rekonstruiruetsia (Moscow: Izostat, 1938)

on which the artists Aleksandr Rodchenko and Varvara Stepanova collaborated. See Mikhail Karasik, Paradnaia kniga strany sovetov, (Moscow: Kontakt-Kultura, 20o7) for an extensive overview of Soviet photographic albums fromn the 193os, a number of which contain Izostat charts.

42. Vsevolod Vasil'evskii, 'Izobrazitel'naia statistika', Prozhektor, Nº. 7, 1934, pp. 18–19. 43. The ss Cheliuskin was a Soviet steamship that became trapped in the Arctic ice in 1934. 44. Vasil'evskii, 'Izobrazitel'naia statistika', p. 19.

#

45. Five of these Izvestiia charts were reproduced in a short feature titled 'Otto Neurath visits Russia' in the American journal Survey Graphic, vol. 21, no.8, 1932, pp. 538-9. It also contained the inaccurate information that 'These charts were made by a staff of sixteen young Austrians trained by Dr. Neurath for the Russian Institute.' There were never as many as 16 Vienna Museum staff working at Izostat and those trained for the Institute were all Russian.

and us). The purpose of each chart was to show readers of the newspaper how the ussR continued to flourish in a particular branch of industry and surpass nations in the West. Sometimes the statistical charts were accompanied by lengthy explanatory text or additional numerical data but the main focus was the Izostat chart. The first instalment appeared in Izvestiia on 20 March 1932 – three small charts concerned with the production of agricultural chemicals. The following day's edition contained a chart showing that the ussR had overtaken Venezuela in the extraction of oil. Due to the nature of the publication, the charts could not include the detailed embellishments of the earlier postcards; they had to be simple black-and-white images – a requirement that suited Arntz's style. Basic ‘guide-pictures' were occasionally used, such as the oil well indicating the subject matter of the chart on oil extraction. Over the course of 1932 Izvestiia featured 29 charts on subjects including coal mining, shoe production, blast-furnace construction, and the cultivation of cotton.4⁵ Moreover, Izvestiia – the official newspaper of the Soviet government – had a circulation of

N1472 The production of steel in the USSR and in capitalist countries

N1471 The Soviet Union overtook Venezuela in oil extraction

N1473 Our construction and construction in the USA

approximately one and a half million and therefore these early Izostat charts would have reached a very large audience.⁴

In spring 1932 Izostat produced a small folder of more than 6o charts to mark the Ninth Congress of Soviet Trade Unions held in Moscow in April 1932. Editions were published in both English and Russian: the title of the English version was The struggle for five years in four, a reference to the completion of the First Five-Year Plan in four years rather than five.4 The charts were concerned with various areas of Soviet economy, industry, society, and trade union membership, and sought to show

46. The chart on oil extraction is credited to the Izostat Institute in its bottom right hand corner, but usually no reference was made in the

charts to their origin. Notably, although the decree of 1931 had ordered that the Vienna Method be used, Izostat charts often shared a page with other pictorial statistics that employed differences in size rather than quantity to show an increase or decrease in an amount.

47. L. M. Kogan (ed.), The struggle for five years in four / Profsoiuzy ssSR v bor'be za piatiletku v chetyre goda (Moscow: Ogiz-lzogiz, 1932).

how these had improved over the course of the First Five-Year Plan. Two charts also highlighted 'the crisis in leading capitalist countries' contrasted with the achievements of the ussR. The Russian edition contained a preface by Ivanitskii, in which he not only explained the quantitative system of the Vienna Method but also seemed to pre-empt criticism of the collection by admitting that it was ‘assembled in haste' and that Izostat did not have the opportunity to put to use all the experience achieved by the Vienna Museum ... that is why we have employed only the general principles of this method'.48 The variations within the album demonstrate that this was still a period of transition for Izostat, during which the rules of the Vienna Method were not yet consistently followed; indeed some similarities to the earlier postcards persisted. The English-language edition must have been published some time after the Russian, as a number of charts were revised, indicating the intervention of Arntz and his team in the meantime. This is most striking in the two charts concerned with the progress of collectivization.

48. Ivanitskii, 'Ukazaniia k pol'zovaniiu diagrammami', Profsoiuzy sssR. He also admitted that there had not been the opportunity 'to amend some misprints, which occurred in the printing process'.

![](images/4f86cbde773788b365d18c764f30b52dff749486cc49a944b9ca2474afeaf76a.jpg)  
Equivalent charts from Profsoiuzy sssR and the Englishlanguage edition, The struggle for five years in four, 1932. 183×227mm.

The Russian version consists of three rows of naturalistic peasant dwellings placed on a background of small fields; the rows are dated 1928, 1931, and 1932. The process of collectivization over these years is shown through a number of visual devices: in 1931 the fields lose their piecemeal appearance, a grain silo is under construction, tractors appear on the horizon, and the wooden huts are transformed into standardized buildings bathed in a red that also colours the earth. In 1932 the emphasis on modernization is even more pronounced: the silo is now complete, and a train carries the processed crop away; more tractors are on the horizon, and the traditional peasant dwellings have almost all been replaced by new buildings. This chart is in sharp contrast to its equivalent in the English edition, The struggle for five years in four. Here the peasant huts are presented as pictograms, rather than realistic drawings, on a buff background with white lines to indicate individual fields. The spread of collectivization is once again shown through the replacement of black with red, but there are no longer any additional pictures. In both charts one hut represents five per cent of the total number of peasant farms

51. For a brief moment in 1930–1 political posters abandoned the traditional worker iconography and individual heroes for the promotion of 'ordinary workers' who were often represented by silhouettes. Victoria Bonnell argues that 'the silhouette, with its lack of detail or facial expression and its general vagueness, appealed to political artists groping for new imagery in a period that stressed the ordinary man'. Iconography of power: Soviet political posters under Lenin and Stalin (Berkeley and Los Angeles: University of California Press, 1997), p. 35. Bonnell also notes that in this period 'as old class markers began to disappear, colour symbolism became more important than ever before'. Both these aspects parallel the Vienna Method and this may partly explain why the authorities were so keen to establish Izostat in 1931. However the trend for abstraction in posters was shortlived, as it was rejected by both critics and the public: 'artists soon moved away from silhouette, and by the mid-193os posters of this type seldom appeared. ... The glorification of anonymous little heroes had been replaced by praise for the individual hero.' (p. 36)

52. Marie Neurath, 'Otto Neurath and Isotype', p.14.

Although Ivanitskii is credited as overseeing the creation of the charts in this album, only four of them featured his filmstrip method; the rest showed an approach that was closer to the Vienna Method. In chart no. 37, ‘Growth in public food service in the ussR' (see opposite), the modularity of the table pictograms is compromised by the use of perspective and the attempt to contain the plate pictograms (which have a separate value) on them; but such a complex configuration was not used elsewhere in the album. Grey guide-pictures appear in the majority of the charts, and they are generally separated from the statistical pictograms according to Vienna Method principles, although some effort was still concentrated on their illustrative qualities. Ivanitskii explained that ‘in the chart “Oil production in the UssR", oil is represented by a pictogram of an oil storage tank, but an additional drawing portraying oil wells points to the subject of the chart, namely extraction rather than the transportation of oil or the consumption of oil'.4º The purpose of the guide-picture, as Ivanitskii outlined it, was to help the viewer understand the subject matter of a chart, or divide the chart into sections. The Vienna Museum's guideline notes stated that the guide-picture cannot be standardized in the same way as the pictogram' but thatonly as much is shown in a guide-picture as is absolutely necessary to the statement in hand'.5º Yet many of the guide-pictures in Profsoiuzy SSSR / The struggle for five years in four drew attention away from the actual data in an attempt to stress the success of socialism rather than simply indicate subject matter. In chart no. 44, 'Growth in capacity of city nurseries', not only is the baby pictogram executed in some detail, but the guide-picture presents an idealized image of a modern, Soviet city nursery with children fed and cared for by attentive staff. In chart no.58, ‘Total circulation of trade union publications', the artist has attempted a narrative guide-picture: the worker submits his manuscript, it is printed, and then made available to other trade union members.

To some extent, the charts in Profsoiuzy SSSR / The struggle for five years in four may reflect wider tensions in the visual arts of the ussR during the early 193os.⁵¹ Throughout their time in Moscow, the Vienna Museum staff seemed to have been aware that their position was not very secure. A shift towards celebrating individual, identifiable Soviet heroes in art and literature was felt by those at Izostat almost from the start. Marie Neurath recalled: 'While we were still there, a separate group inside the office made a different kind of symbolism, men with ears and eyes, according to “Soviet Realism"'.⁵² Arntz felt the rejection of the Russian avant-garde artists even more keenly as he attempted to engage with them and their work. He recalled that he attended an exhibition of work by Vladimir Maiakovskii but found himself alone in the gallery, and 'when I explained in the [Izostat] Institute how I liked it, they preferred not to speak of him: he was already out of favour'.⁵³ Nevertheless Arntz seems to have enjoyed his first few months in Russia in 1932 and he socialized with Russian colleagues (they spoke a mixture of German and French); he visited Leningrad with some of them for the 'white nights' in June.⁵4 He found it satisfying that the Izostat staff were willing to learn and follow his artistic direction:

![](images/31045fe888cd58d2c8fd3e956f45c5a469c1f435cfb81d0d51dc0b253ec41d2b.jpg)  
Charts from The struggle for five years in four. 1932.

When I was in Moscow every artist's work went through my hands so a style resulted that was much simpler than ever in Vienna, where left-overs had to be contended with. ... As far as the visual form is concerned, or the main pictograms – these were dictated by the Viennese: Arntz, Bernath and Matzalik.

53. Arntz , Zeit unterm Messer, p. 30. Arntz also attended the exhibition 'Artists of the RSFSR during the last 15 years, 1917–1932' ('Khudozhniki RSFSR za 15 let 1917– 1932') which was held in the State Historical Museum (Moscow) in the summer of 1933. There he spoke with Vladimir Tatlin, 'who stood under his aeroplane', and saw work by the architect Vladimir Krinskii (member of the Asnova group), whom Arntz had met the previous year at the Izostat Institute. (p. 32)

54. Marie Neurath recalled: 'It was interesting to see Russian life so closely from within a workshop and to meet people and converse with them over many cups of tea for hours at night.' ('26 September 1924 and after', pp. 61–2.) 55. Arntz, '3.7.'72', p. 4.

![](images/bdf8a9faaad2eb6a44224501fbd4f1d01d114087110b280a1a77bed0b90f7460.jpg)  
'Businessman'. Pictogram no. 1579 (top left) depicts a bearded man wearing a cap, tall boots, a traditional Russian shirt (worn long over the trousers), and a waistcoat.

![](images/19f1e45f64e861d575f46540224b59913f9fe4874849a3f47f9bbbdccd10631b.jpg)  
'Transport', featuring rickshaws, which must have been common in some Asian Soviet republics.

Some pictograms created at Izostat are recorded in a version of the picture dictionary' that appears to have been compiled by Arntz or one of his Viennese colleagues during their time in Moscow. It is a particularly valuable source, as the proliferation of certain themes indicates the focus of Izostat work on the industrial and agricultural goals of the First Five-Year Plan. It contains numerous variations of the tractor pictogram, and guide-pictures of factories and industrial landscapes, as well as of the Kremlin, Lenin's Mausoleum, and the Triumphal Arch. The Moscow‘picture dictionary' also indicates that Izostat staff recognized the need to use visual markers of national identity in order to engage viewers with their charts. A number of human pictograms have features that could identify them as Russian: for example, no. 59 shows a student walking to school in a fur cap with ear-flaps. However, most human pictograms that have a recognizable Russian' identity are associated with pre-revolutionary Russia. No.1661 (opposite, above left) is presumably meant to represent a Russian peasant, with a beard and wearing traditional bast shoes fixed by means of cloth wrapped around the leg (clearly shown through the careful design).

![](images/c91154ffcabda540b2f658bd90812132aff62a1ddd5890b46e51619b4dfb9325.jpg)

Excerpt from a page of 'animals'. This group of ten rabbits was used to show their prolific breeding habits in an Izostat poster titled 'We will produce rabbit meat for the usSR / We will improve worker nourishment' (shown in The avantgarde applied 189o–1950, Palma/Cuenca: Fundacíon Juan March, 2012, p. 159)

Box cover and charts from 15 let Oktiabria (15 years since the October Revolution). 1932. Charts 284× 582 mm.

![](images/52e7edd8255890654b01243257e33cd374fed803818d257996c8216587df63ea.jpg)

![](images/7c5fe8ce05e0c27b0388c1989da9e4a4fb815cf266fa799fb585980df704c51f.jpg)

![](images/6aaa54c29045cfa64459d974906e8c2b37677c5ad3c64c1ff9b781ff23c8fbba.jpg)

The transition of workers in the ussr to a sevenhour working day Each figure = 10% of all employed people.

Soviet machinery construction

Each circle = machinery produced to the value of 500 million roubles.

The rate of collectivization in agriculture

Each rectangle = 10 million hectares of cultivated land.

The construction of new housing in the usSR Each building = 2.5 million m² of newly completed housing.

Arntz soon established control over the design of Izostat pictograms, as is evident from another set of charts published in 1932, 15 let Oktiabria (15 years since the October Revolution). Here, simple pictograms arranged according to Vienna Method principles superseded the discrepancies and eccentricities evident in some previous examples. This boxed set of twenty charts celebrated Soviet economic and social achievements since the 1917 October Revolution, particularly under the First Five-Year Plan. The charts were printed on poor-quality paper and mounted onto card, probably intended for display in a schoolroom or workers' club; they were accompanied by instructions giving the order in which they should be arranged. A number of the charts were modified versions of some first published in Izvestiia, but unlike the newspaper charts they could employ colour as a visual indicator. Red, long associated with revolution and socialism, was the most widely used colour in Izostat charts for any outcome achieved since 1917. So, in the chart about coal production, the amount of coal mined in 1913 is represented by a blue wagon pictogram whereas those for the later years are coloured in red.

![](images/5b6e2e8b2be8e9f38628d22a6ccdd50bcc27bc939e8f13ab75c4fbc5733ea66e.jpg)

![](images/c423619de33a8781be77a13577ac6339fae209339fb0e0a7023f28a5c83974b8.jpg)

![](images/89817de1ac5d3b45ec3982fc82de86b07c3161f404e63c5cc0594cbf718db525.jpg)

![](images/108038d98adac376f20f786eac3057bb1bff491f092d55cb2b78fea90425ce5d.jpg)

![](images/ca29ad2d7e0ee8c44f902a10d5b4640232cda2565e1b3bfa29322cc9f8828ebd.jpg)

![](images/dccfb18c700d746248f523d9b234a4638673f987fbb468d65b41c152234f7e2f.jpg)

Red was also used in charts to differentiate the ussR from capitalist countries, which were shown in blue or black; and to differentiate urban from rural, which was shown in green (as in the chart about school attendance). This use of red was in accordance with the Vienna Museum's guideline note on colour – black is always used for the worse thing, red always for the better' – and it naturally conformed to the use of colour in Soviet visual propaganda.s⁶ Ivanitskii explained that the colours used in Izostat charts were significant: for the pre-Revolutionary period we always use gloomy dark colours.'⁵7

Throughout 1933 and 1934, Arntz and his colleagues worked against the rising tide of Socialist Realism to produce charts that followed Vienna Method principles. The output of the Institute prompted Otto Neurath to remark that ‘particular understanding for pictorial statistics with the Vienna Method is shown by the Soviet Union, where statistics

56. See p.118 above. Experience in the ussr may have informed Neurath's statement in International picture language (1936, p. 50):

'Red is industry, machine, metal, warm, present, higher stage of development, worker'.

57. Ivanitskii, Izobrazitel'naia statistika i venskii metod, p. 33.

Production of tractors in the uSSR

Each tractor = 10,000 tractors.

Growth in turnover in the number of railway passengers and the length of railway lines in the ussR Each figure = 50 million transported passengers.

Reach of universal compulsory education in the ussR Each figure = 2 million pupils.

Capitalists extinguish blast furnaces, we build new ones Each symbol = 25 blast furnaces.

Coal mining in the ussR Each wagon = 10 million tons.

Growth of world unemployment Each figure = 2 million persons.

are cultivated more than anywhere else in the world'.58 However, the picture of economic success conjured by these statistics did not tally with the everyday reality of life experienced by Arntz when he returned with his family to Moscow for nine months in January 1933. ‘The food situation was still difficult and there was a severe lack of goods. It was awkward to live in a hotel as a privileged foreigner and to have better access to food and consumables than my Russian colleagues.'º The situation for those living outside Moscow was much worse, as Arntz encountered in the summer of that year. For his outstanding work at Izostat – he later described himself as the Institute's first Udarnik (shock-worker) – Arntz and his family were offered a month's holiday at the Black Sea resort Sochi. This was the destination of choice for privileged members of Soviet society and Party officials, and its luxury hotel provided Arntz with an uncomfortable contrast to what he saw during the 52-hour train journey through Ukraine:

... the homeless children travelling along under the wagons, the starving farmers, and again and again the throngs of begging children at the stations. ... Much could be reported about sacrifice on all sides, caused by collectivization. The sight of the swathes of shelter-seeking exiles at the stations could in itself have provided material for such reports. One really had to close one's eyes and only read the victory announcements' in the newspapers in order to live in peace with this, even in Moscow.

Arntz also observed the persecution of individuals for alleged disloyalty to the Communist cause: 'From close quarters I witnessed purification" in business, with self-accusations during business assemblies, and I had good interpreters at my disposal in these. That was a chapter of Soviet life in itself.' He experienced the growing atmosphere of secrecy and caution: whereas in 1932 he had enjoyed mixing with his Russian co-workers, in 1933 he seldom met them outside of the Institute because they had to be extremely careful about their associations; therefore his contact was restricted to a small circle of foreigners.“º

Arntz made his last visit to Moscow between the middle of October and the beginning of December 1934, after having left Vienna to settle in The Hague with Neurath and Reidemeister. He remembered that Izostat was inundated with work for the Second Five-Year Plan: 'enormous amounts of propaganda were designed, exhibitions organized and plans explained'.61 One example is the English-language publication, The Second Five-Year Plan in construction (1934). The charts in this book are evidence of the tremendous effect that the Vienna Method and Arntz's influence had exerted on Soviet pictorial statistics since 1931. However, a chart such as Creches' also demonstrates why the work of the Izostat Institute was, by the end of 1934, regarded as contrary to official cultural policy. Arntz explained:

![](images/46f9b65530f5dd70146110ddb25189e52ca93f2f52746693c9e76ed760c50896.jpg)

We were asked why our figures had no faces. Facelessness' was an undesirable attitude with the Party. Also the 'western', constructivist, 'decadent' design [Formgebung] no longer toed the line of the now prescribed 'socialist realism'. There followed a discussion with the management, which allowed tests to be prepared with more 'Russian'- seeming figures. After our contract ran out, it would not be long before the 'Vienna Method' came to an end and a different method of design gained influence at Izostat. 62

Charts from The Second Five-Year Plan in construction (Moscow/Leningrad: Ogiz-Izogiz, 1934). These are rectos; all versos are blank. 335× 230 mm.

62. Arntz, Zeit unterm Messer, pp.35-7.

63. Marie Neurath, 'What I remember', pp. 58-9.

64. Berezin, 'Vozrodim izobrazitel'nuiu statistiku!’, p. 62. In this article Berezin made no mention of Neurath or the involvement of Vienna Museum staff in the 1931-4 period.

65. Letter Matzalik to Neurath,   
10 July 1935 (öNB 1222/4).

66. I. Sautin & I. P. Ivanitskii (eds.), ussR: an album illustrating the state organisation and national economy of the usSR, (Moscow: Izostat, 1939). Lissitzky worked with two other artists on the album, Aleksandr Grigorovich and Mikhail Nikolaev. The album also featured engravings by the notable Russian graphic artist Vladimir Favorskii. According to Lissitzky-Küppers, Lissitzky was also working with Izostat on an album to celebrate the Republic of Georgia's sixteen-year membership of the ussR. She recalled that it included 'some excellent charts, with exceptionally attractive symbols' and 'was indeed the finest and artistically most successful of Lissitzky's albums'. The original design was taken to Georgia for approval by an Izostat representative but it was lost after the outbreak of the Second World War and never printed. (El Lissitzky, p. 100.) In 1939 Lissitzky also signed a contract with lzostat to design a poster with the title 'Moskva – stolitsa sssR' and in 1940 he designed another for the Institute, titled 'Nuzhno usilit' i ukrepit' internatsional'nye proletarskie sviazi rabochego klassa sssR s rabochim klassom burzhuaznykh stran'. See El Lissitzky, 1890–1941: catalogue for an exhibition of selected works from North American collections (Cambridge, MA: Harvard University Art Museums, Busch-Reisinger Museum, 1987), p. 200.

At the end of 1934 the Soviet authorities wrote to Neurath claiming that the contract between them did not comply with Russian law and that the payment due at the end of the contract would not therefore be made. Otto knew that he would not find justice in any court in the ussR,' recalled Marie Neurath, he asked me only to write to Sophie Lissitzky about it, but nothing came of that.'³ The pictograms, charts and publications produced at Izostat after 1934 are markedly different to those produced under Viennese influence. A. Berezin, who became director of Izostat in 1935, claimed We had to return to groups of pictograms and additional illustrative drawings. Our pictograms were also subject to significant decoration – small figures acquired realistic form.'⁶⁴

Despite the financial hardship at the International Foundation for Visual Education in The Hague (caused principally by the Soviets' refusal to pay), Neurath sent money in July 1935 to Edith Matzalik, one of the Vienna Museum staff who chose to remain in Moscow. She reported on the situation at Izostat when writing to thank him:

Mind you the work here has become engaging again to some extent, due to things being most tolerable in my department; hardly anyone interferes. But also, apart from that, the Vienna Method' is really the guiding principle, above all concerning the formal side of things, since, despite many thoughtless trials with naturalism, nothing clever has resulted – QED. Everything suffers now under appalling material scarcity: colours, brushes, papers are gruesome, ... no brand-named goods, no continuity.⁶s

She added that an in-house facility for lithographic printing had been established and that, while a strict adherence to the precision in execution that was learned from Vienna' was maintained, there were many sins' committed in the new work.

Perhaps the most notable publication to be issued during Izostat's latter years was the English-language publication USSR: an album illustrating the state organisation and national economy of the USSR, which was one of a series of books produced by the Soviet authorities for the 1939 New York World's Fair.6 El Lissitzky oversaw the design of this album which contained a mixture of pictorial statistics, wood engravings, and photographs of notable Soviet figures. The pictorial statistics here remain close in spirit to the Vienna Method, while departing from it in certain details.

## SANATORRUMS AND REST HOMES

Het resultaat van dezen jarenlangen arbeld wordt thans in dienst gesteld van Industrie. handel en verkeer, door een speciaal daartoe opgerichte organisatle, de N.V. (SOTYPE. Wij leven in het tijdperk van het\_ aenschouwen"; wie snel en blijvend indruk wil maken, bedlent zich van beelden.

Steeds wear opnieuw heeft men ervaren, dat met behulp van de ISOTYPE-beeldentaal verrassende ultwerkingen worden beralkt. Kleine tentoonstellingen trekken een ongewoon groot aantal bezoekers. Musea, die op deze methode berusten. verwekken blijvende belangstelling.

In de geheele wereld zijn deze opvallende beelden bekend geworden, niet alléén door groote internationale tentoonstallingen, doch ook door couranten, tiidschriften, boeken en mappen van de bovengencemde beeldstatis. tische instituten.

Zooals men ult de beeldstatistiaken van dit prospectus kan zien, wordt sen grootare hoeveelheld van een bepaalde zaak door een grooter aantal van dezelide leekens aangegeven Het iSOTYPE. lexikon beval reeds 2000 zorgvuldig ultgawerkte teekens; het wordt steeds verder uitgebreid. De moeilijksle laak ls, met deze duidelijke teekens sprekende gralische voorstallingen te vormen. De jarenlange ervaring der aan het instituut verbonden medawarkers garandeerl het goede resuitaat hatwolk berust op de konsekwentle en helderheid van de methode die als grondgedachle heeft.

Het is beter vereenvoudigde beelden te onthouden dan nauwkeurige getallen te vergeten.

Men wende zich voor nadere inlichtingen tot:

## ISOTYPE

is de Internationale taal die beelden inplaats van woorden gebruikt. Het is reeds meer dan tien jaar geleden, dat de eerste tentoonstelling werd geopend, waar de moderne beeldstatistlek en het beeldonderricht konsekwent werden doorgevoerd. De staf van eminente medewerkers, die voor deze beeldentaal internationale bekendheid heeft verworven, werkt voort onder lelding van haren directeur, Dr. Otto Neurath, tanelnde deze methode in toepassing te brengen op steeds weer nieuwe gebieden. De centrale van het Isotypewerk Is In Den Haag gevestigd. De Internationale Stichting voor Beeidpaedagogie, gavestigd te s-Gravenhage en Naw York, heeft ten doel deze beeldentaal in de geheeie wereld te verbreiden, het Mundaneum Instituut in Den Haag verricht hel wetenschappelijke en grafische werk. Van het Bestuur dezer belde installingen maken onder anderen de volgende personen deel uit: Mary L. Fleddérus, Director International Industrial Relations Institute, Den Haag; Dr JosefFrank, Architect, Stockholm, Professor Dr Philipp Frank, Praag. Dr. Mr. P. J. da Kanter, Advocaat en Procureur, Den Haag: Mary van Kleeck, Direclor Department of Industrial Studies, Russell Sage Foundation. New York; C. H.van der Leeuw. firmant van de Ervan de Wed. J. van Nelle, Rotterdam

# 6 Interlude in the Netherlands 1934-40

In 1934 the International Foundation for Visual Education in The Hague became the base for a small group from the Gesellschaftsund Wirtschaftsmuseum in Wien: Otto Neurath, Marie Reidemeister, Gerd Arntz, Erwin Bernath and Josef Scheer. Among the first projects undertaken there were two books that broadened the international dimension of their work: International picture language and Basic by Isotype, both written in Basic English, a stripped-down version of the language developed by Charles Kay Ogden. It was in this context that Marie Reidemeister thought of a new name to replace the Vienna Method of Pictorial Statistics – Isotype, an acronym for International System of Typographic Picture Education

The Foundation in The Hague did not benefit from any government subsidy equivalent to that given to the Vienna Museum, and so the first few years in the Netherlands were difficult financially. Additionally, Neurath was often occupied with organizing conferences of the International Institute for the Unity of Science. His frequent visits to the USA during the 193os resulted in several substantial jobs being carried out for American clients (see chapter 7). Some commissions in the Netherlands took the work into new areas: there was a foray into children's theatre with a multi-layered production of a Punch and Judy'-style puppet show; and the first Isotype exhibition about art, 'Rondom Rembrandt'.

![](images/f0055b3fe721cad467400bc7f38d956c1a79243a8a49d975624df98aa7701c72.jpg)  
N2016 Isotype puppet show produced in the Netherlands.

Exhibition work in the Netherlands and in the USA maintained continuity with the work done previously in Vienna. The team made plans and photomontage visualizations of ideal exhibition layouts, in the hope of re-establishing a permanent exhibition centre. Otto Neurath pursued this ideal for the rest of his life, yet it was never to be achieved.

## International picture language 1936

This was the first book about Isotype in English. Neurath co-wrote it with Marie Reidemeister in Basic English, a version of the language with a core vocabulary of only 85o words. (BAsic = British American Scientific International Commercial.) This leads to some peculiar phrases; indeed Neurath later described Basic's idiomatic rules as 'devastating'. Nevertheless, this text remainsthe best and most concise summary of Isotype.

![](images/6ac6ab74127226ddbbc0bbe998ab2ebcf315e62a4f0c6835751ef19e13294a2e.jpg)

## ISOTYPE AS A HELPING LANGUAGE

## Basic by Isotype 1937

This book is a pictorial/verbal primer of Basic English, presenting vocabulary and simple formulations in Basic with accompanying visualizations in Isotype. The examples in the book are all descriptive ('boy on a bridge', 'boy among girls’); emotions are not dealt with (there are no pictures for 'happy boy' or ‘sad girl’). This accords with Ogden and Neurath's shared principle of separating 'the symbolic and the emotive' functions of language.

The book is atypical of Isotype in its attempted equivalence of words and pictures. Their functions were usually complementary in Isotype work.

'Psyche miniatures general series no.86'. London: Kegan Paul, Trench, Trubner & Co., 1937. 150×98 mm.

## Rondom Rembrandt (Around Rembrandt) 1938

The exhibition 'Rondom Rembrandt' was innovative in several ways: it did not include original artworks but instead examined the historical background and social context of Rembrandt's work; and it was not housed in a museum or art gallery but instead was shown simultaneously in three branches of the Dutch department store De Bijenkorf.

Otto Neurath explained the curatorial approach: 'We object to a mixture of original paintings and explanations, we want to separate explanation and immediate impression, therefore no originals within the room, where the explanations have to be presented, only copies, in colours or in black and white.' (ic 3.2/42)

The idea for the exhibition occurred to Marie Neurath when, as a visitor to a major exhibition of Rembrandt's paintings in Amsterdam, she was struck by how little had been done to help the public understand them. In 'Rondom Rembrandt' techniques,such as colour coding and interactive apparatuses were employed to make the subject appealing and understandable to a diverse audience.

## Which belong together?

These columns of grouped buildings, ships, and paintings formed part of an interactive apparatus. Visitors to the exhibition were asked to choose which groups they thought belonged to the same historical period. When a button underneath a column was pushed (as shown in the diagram on the left, T1814), a recording of 'right' or 'wrong was played. (The right answers are columns 2, 6 and 7.) The apparatus represents a pioneering example of a technique that has since become familiar in exhibitions.

![](images/709c9ae3315f7d05acf98cedb4782f6133838cfc9a9fd5975e16e474aa2fd433.jpg)

# Social Showman

![](images/8f58fd323638e5b1917b6df1ac0e051d2f771984e9303721073dcba16930d192.jpg)

![](images/c04c8f2d6df7052ac3e1266468e2dba9cc1c5c9a1442b8cd5e616948d99bc354.jpg)

HE Big Man who created the Little Man is with us from overseas this fall. Though the two of them have not been heralded on Broadway, the Little Man is a sound actor—and Otto Neurath is his impresario. The significant Little Man, who represents a hundred thousand or maybe a million of us, made his first appearance in America in the pages of Survey Graphic in 1932. Row on row like the chorus of a review, or among the symbols of the things men buy and sell and cat and use, or perhaps at a halting place in man's long pilgrimage from savagery to peace and plenty, he has become the hero of act after act in which he may not even enter the stage at all. In business roles he signifies the man earning over \$2500, or owning an automobile, or belonging to country clubs; or—in the field of social welfare—having a job, the toothache, tuberculosis, naturalization papers, or no job, or what not. But he is not really so elementary as all that. The Little Man was begot in a bed of statistics, trained by economists, and styled by imaginative designers. The true secret of his significance, and of the significance of all Neurath's methods, is that Neurath, the social showman, is also a philosopher. Authenticity in research, and precision in visualizing the finding of the experts, do not cramp the style of Neurath's large mind.

Like the man in the moon, whom he resembles when his great countenance is reflective, Neurath's orbit swings clear round the earth. He sets the imagination on fire as Van Loon, or H. G. Wells, sometimes does simply by giving us a surprising glimpse of ourselves in a moving social procession. But with this difference. Neurath is never carried away by his fancies, or his day dreams, into conjecture. He is, first of all, a mathematician, disciplined in the natural sciences.

In bringing about the "renascence of hieroglyphics" Neurath wrested from the hierarchy of his own scientific colleagues their monopoly of learning. Our civilization. he feels, is still under the sway of a Middle Ages pattern in which a word language is the property of one class alone. In the Middle Ages, it was the monks with their Latin. Today it is the scientists whose polysyllabic books are over the heads of most of us. Yet if democratic cooperation toward the solution of complex problems is not to fail, we must all understand the great forces which affect our lives.

To Neurath the pic-chart, the bar-chart, the fluctuating lines of the graph-makers and the map makers are almost as inadequate as fancy words. Neurath is never without a huge pencil rooted to his hand to chart the ideas that transcend words. Not that he is inarticulate. A sociologist and the son of a sociologist, a logician and the husband of a logician, he knows and speaks the language of the scientists. In conversation, especially in German, his vocabulary carries all the nuances of ideas and images. In his English, which he has sharpened into the

colloquial by reading dozens of detective stories on his voyages to America, he is precise, exact. But no matter how fluently one may describe something, he feels that to be fully understood, it must be visualized. Back of the automobile and the skyscraper, for example, lies r.p.m.s. and complex mathematical formulas, translated into action by the engineers. Back of Neurath's pictures and museum exhibits lie profound research, statistics transformed into ideas, ideas then designed into a picture narrative, a drama of social interpretation.

with research and educational organizations in half a dozen countries besides the United States. He created and directed the famous Gesellschafts-und Wirtschaftsmnuseum of his native city of Vienna. It was in that museum that he blossomed out as a showman, the Barnum of man's life and work.

That was in 1924. Neurath was then, at forty-two, a mature social scientist of growing repute; his household was a center of practical as well as intellectual discussion. Wartime experience in the economic division in charge of civilian supplies in Poland had thrown him into first hand contact with the source and flow of commodities. His teaching experience, in Heidelberg and Vienna, had developed his gift for relating science to daily life. Then as general secretary of the Austrian Federation for Housing and Garden Cities, he expressed himself through posters and graphs and popular expositions that indicated his bent. When Vienna began its great program of rehabilitation and social welfare, Neurath started the Social and Economic Museum. There, with a group of first rate collaborators in graphic work, he directed

SURVEY GRAPHIC

# 7 Isotype in America Otto Neurath and Rudolf Modley 1930-9

Hisayasu Ihara

Otto Neurath's early efforts to disseminate the Vienna Method internationally were aimed in several directions, including towards the United States. In attempting to spread the Vienna Method to the usA, Neurath came to rely on many friends and allies. But despite the commitment of everyone involved, a number of factors made progress difficult. The first was the restrictive economic situation of the early 193os brought on by the Great Depression. Next were the increasingly common imitations of the Vienna Method that impeded efforts to establish the genuine article. But another – and crucial – factor was Rudolf Modley. Modley, who had worked at the Gesellschafts- und Wirtschaftsmuseum in the 192os, left for the usA in 193o. There, in the years that followed, he produced pictorial statistics best described as midway between the Vienna Method and its poorer imitations. Although Modley helped introduce the Vienna Method to the USA, he later pursued his own direction, eventually competing with Neurath and even criticizing him. As the popularity of pictorial statistics grew in the usA, the differing views of Neurath and Modley raised a series of issues about how pictorial statistics should be made and used, among them the value of standardized pictograms, and strategies to control Vienna Method imitations. The intersecting activities of these two men during the 193os is the focus of this chapter.

## Early contacts

Around 193o, three Americans interested in Otto Neurath's work contacted him in succession. They were Waldemar Kaempffert, science writer for The New York Times and director of the Museum of Science and Industry in Chicago; Ann Reed Brenner, member of Survey Associates, publisher of The Survey and Survey Graphic, magazines of social and

Acknowledgements Much of the research for this chapter was carried out in autumn 2oog, when I visited the following archives in the United States: Social Welfare History Archives, Elmer L. Andersen Library, University of Minnesota; Howard Washington Odum papers, Southern Historical Collection, Wilson Library, University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill; Sophia Smith Collection, Smith College; and Manuscripts and Archives Division, New York Public Library. My thanks to the archivists for their kind help, especially David Klaassen and Linnea Anderson at the Social Welfare History Archives, and Susan Boone at the Sophia Smith Collection. This work was supported by Grants-in-Aid for Scientific Research, Japan Society for the Promotion of Science (JSPS KAKENHI 21520143).

political research;¹ and Mary van Kleeck, director of the Department of Industrial Studies at the Russell Sage Foundation.

Waldemar Kaempffert first met Otto Neurath in 1911 when Neurath was teaching at the Neue Wiener Handelsakademie (New Vienna academy of commerce).² Kaempffert had been a science writer for The New York Times since 1922, and in 1928 he became director of the Museum of Science and Industry, which was then in its planning stage. By the end of that vear, he had visited ‘all the important museums in western Europe' for the purpose of familiarizing himself with museum practice'; while on his travels he renewed his acquaintance with Neurath.³ As he later recalled: Neurath ... had done more with less money and apparatus than any of the museum directors with whom I became acquainted. ... Above all, Neurath approached the task of interpreting as a sociologist. It was this that struck me most.'⁴ Kaempffert became interested in bringing the Vienna Method to Chicago and in summer 1929, when Neurath proposed that the Vienna Museum make charts for the Museum of Science and Industry, Kaempffert immediately agreed.⁵ The two museums began cooperating soon after.

The following year, between April and September 193o, Ann Reed Brenner was also touring museums in Germany. The purpose of her trip was to study 'developments which have a very direct bearing on [the] attempt through Survey Graphic to visualise and hence to vitalise the facts of modern life'. According to Paul Neurath, it was the director of the Deutsches Museum in Munich who suggested to Brenner that she should meet Otto Neurath: ‘So she took a train to see Otto, without any previous arrangement' and in this way established a connection between Survey Graphic and Neurath.7

The third American to contact Otto Neurath was the social researcher and reformer Mary van Kleeck. In 1911, Van Kleeck had become a director of the Russell Sage Foundation where she surveyed

3. Letter Kaempffert to Edwin M. Embree, 8 October 1936, box 100, folder 756, Survey Associates records, Social Welfare History Archives, University of Minnesota (hereinafter SAR-SWHA). On the cooperation between Neurath and Kaempffert, see Yann Giraud and Loïc Charles, 'Economics for the masses: the visual display of

5. Letter Kaempffert to Neurath,   
19 June 1929 (GWM-MSI).

7. 'From Otto Neurath's son, the sociologist Paul Neurath', in Neurath, Empiricism and sociology, p. 37.

![](images/28673db5ae8b4f77b9142da9327e9e6146bf49ec673b74b0a50761e74f64be19.jpg)  
Cover and pages from Survey Graphic (vol. 20, no. 6, March 1932), featuring Neurath's article 'World planning and the usA'.

the working conditions of women in New York City.8 Her research interests widened in the 192os, and in 1925 she helped create the International Industrial Relations Institute (IRI) whose aim was 'the study and promotion of satisfactory human relations and working conditions in all industry'.º She became associate director of the IRI, when, at the same time, her close friend Mary L. Fleddérus became its director. Through the IRı, Van Kleeck and Fleddérus began addressing international economic and social problems. The two women probably met Neurath sometime in 193o while planning the World Social Economic Congress, convened in Amsterdam in summer 1931. Neurath was invited to the congress where he delivered a lecture illustrated with slides employing the Vienna Method. Van Kleeck and Fleddérus were impressed both by Neurath's theory of economic planning and his new approach to visual education;1º after the congress, Neurath joined the IRI board.

Following their various contacts with Otto Neurath at this time, Kaempffert, Brenner and Van Kleeck began working together to introduce the Vienna Method to the UsA. In March 1932 Survey Graphic ran a cover story on economic planning titled 'When we choose to plan' in a special issue prepared by Van Kleeck and Brenner."1 This issue was the first published discussion of the Vienna Method in the uSA, and included Neurath's article 'World planning and the usA', with seven charts made by the Gesellschafts- und Wirtschaftsmuseum. In translating Neurath's article into English, The Survey's editors noted the care they had taken in adjusting it to 'the psychology of American readers to make the most of it'.12 Later, in November 1932, Survey Graphic published

Neurath's notional 'Flying World Secretariat' was a (mobile) group that would offer instruction and design (Entwurf); it would include himself, a transformer, a designer (Graphiker), an assistant, and a technician. Work would be completed back in Vienna.

several charts designed by the Izostat Institute together with an article reporting on Neurath's activities in the UssR.1ª These articles gave Neurath his first public platform in the USA.

## Neurath's first visit to the United States

In autumn 1932, Mary van Kleeck began formulating plans to bring Otto Neurath to the United States. That October, Mary Fleddérus visited Van Kleeck in New York to discuss the IRı's next annual conference. Fleddérus had recently received a letter from Neurath in which he expressed his desire to open a branch (Nebenstelle) of the Mundaneum in the usa,¹⁴ and with this in mind she proposed bringing him over from Vienna.¹ª In regard to the proposed visit, Van Kleeck remarked that, while 'the present does not seem a favorable opportunity for launching new enterprises in the United States, nevertheless I have been infected by Miss Fleddérus' conviction to believe that now is the time'. Neurath was duly invited and Van Kleeck began raising funds to support the trip from people interested in his work.

Neurath arrived in New York on 3 January 1933 for a ten-day visit His first engagement was on 9 January at the IRI, where he addressed around one hundred social research workers, statisticians, welfare workers, and a number of economists and bankers; he also showed films made according to the Vienna Method.1 The following day he attended a lunch with the staff of The Survey¹7 and in the evening addressed a joint meeting of the Progressive Education Association and the American Museum of Natural History.18 A few days later his stay was at an end and he returned to Europe.

Neurath was energized by the interest the Americans had shown in his work. Back in Vienna he wrote to Van Kleeck pressing her to set up an organization to support 'service for visualization': 'if we can dispatch a permanent representative for the branch, he/she will prepare the consultation under your help so that the role can be carried out perhaps with the “Flying World Secretariat" for one or two months in a year'.19 Although Van Kleeck needed little convincing, she only began moving the proposal forward in late March; in addition to Neurath's enthusiasm, three factors spurred her on. The first was Van Kleeck's awareness that Neurath had already been contacted by several organizations asking him about the possibility of compiling pictorial statistics in the uSA, based on the Vienna Method. She knew, for instance, that Harry Laidler, president of the League for Industrial Democracy, had asked

![](images/150461f018226dc3eed5019f93b5cc6299d1a1879384c57ee4bc00f80872aaae.jpg)  
Charts designed by the American Library Association, published in The Survey (vol. 69, no. 3, March 1933), p.120.

Neurath if a member of the League could be trained in Vienna at the Gesellshafts- und Wirtschaftsmuseum.2º A second factor was somewhat more urgent: the emergence of Vienna Method imitations. Ironically, these had been inadvertently encouraged by The Survey: in its January 1933 number, for example, the magazine reprinted charts originally published by a group in New Jersey that used pictograms taken from Vienna Method publications;2¹ and in its March number, an article reporting on a meeting of the American Library Association described one chart designed by its members as 'similar to those devised by Otto Neurath', despite clear differences.2² But it was the third factor that most unsettled Van Kleeck: Rudolf Modley's activities in the field of pictorial statistics.

## Rudolf Modley

Rudolf Modley was born in Vienna in 19o6 and studied law at the University of Vienna. While at university, he was employed part-time in the administration department of the Gesellschafts- und Wirtschaftsmuseum.²³ In summer 193o, having completed a doctorate in law, Modley left Vienna for Chicago. Although the purpose of his trip was mainly to do post-doctoral work at the Academy of World Commerce at the University of Chicago,²⁴ he had also been asked by Neurath to help secure the Vienna Museum's collaboration with the Museum of Science

23. In her summary of activity at the Gesellshafts- und Wirtschaftsmuseum (appendix c), Marie Neurath listed Modley as principally involved in guiding visitors in Vienna during 193o. Modley claimed that he 'worked there since 1923 joining the permanent staff in 1928'. (How to use pictorial statistics, p. 128.) Later he recalled: 'When I was still in high school, I became acquainted with Otto Neurath's Isotype. I went all out for him. Through high school and university years, I spent all my spare time working for him and with him'. Modley, 'The challenge of "Symbology"', in Elwood Whitney (ed.), 'Symbology': the use of symbols in visual communication (New York: Hastings House, 196o), p. 19. This would mean that he was involved with the Vienna Museum during its first years of existence.

24. Obituary for Rudolf Modley, Report on credit unions, vol. 19, no.10, 15 October 1976, p. 73, in Harold Dwight Lasswell Papers (MS 1043), box 15, folder 306, Manuscripts and Archives, Yale University Library (hereinafter HDLP-YUL).

25. Letter Neurath to Waldemar Kaempffert, 27 February 1930 (GWM-MSI).

26. Six charts among them were displayed at the Coal Exhibition opened in summer 1933. See, 'Catalogue of exhibits in the North Court' (prepared by the museum staff), 1 July 1933, in Waldemar Kaempffert, From cave-man to engineer: the Museum of Science and Industry (1933), pp. 114–15.

27. Waldemar Kaempffert offered his resignation as executive director at the beginning of 1931, and left the museum on 1 March, 1931 returning to The New York Times. Otto Theo Kreusser took over as executive director and under his direction the museum continued the preparation of exhibits, eventually opening on 1 July 1933. See Herman Kogan, A continuing marvel: the story of the Museum of Science and Industry (Garden City, NY: Doubleday, 1973), pp. 37–9.

28. Letter Modley to Neurath,   
18 November 1931 (GwM-MSI).

29. See p.135 above.

and Industry. In a letter to Waldemar Kaempffert, Neurath introduced Modley as a 'clever, adaptable and sociable' person who could establish a 'connection between us institutions and the Gesellschafts- und Wirtschaftsmuseum'; Neurath expressed his hope that Modley would be accepted as a ‘volunteer' at the Chicago museum.²⁵ After his arrival that summer, and with Kaempffert's approval, Modley became a curator in the Social Sciences division at the Museum of Science and Industry; in early 1931 collaborative work with the Gesellschafts- und Wirtschaftsmuseum began under Modley's direction. First, three charts were made in February; three orders then followed: for seventeen charts in June (four of these were later cancelled), for nine charts in August (three later cancelled) and for twelve charts in November. By summer 1932, the thirty-four charts had been completed.²⁶

That Modley would handle this work in the manner of the Gesellschafts- und Wirtschaftsmuseum was surely assumed by Neurath, though this proved not to be the case. Instead Modley worked with greater independence: he drew sketches for charts based on statistical data, then sent these to the Gesellschafts- und Wirtschaftsmuseum; the Vienna Museum's work was thus limited to completing each chart's design and choosing or devising any necessary pictograms or guidepictures. While this process was incompatible with the Vienna Museum's usual procedure for making charts (consisting of three main phases: scientific analysis, 'transformation', graphic work), in practice Neurath's team had little choice but to follow Modley's lead. In part Modley was following the Chicago museum's own wish that its charts differ from those typical of the Gesellschafts- und Wirtschaftsmuseum;27 but he also continued to request detailed revisions to charts produced in Vienna, in particular to the design of the pictograms and the guide-pictures. In a chart showing coal consumption in the usA, for example, Modley directed that 'the symbols for domestic use, the locomotive, the furnace and the water tower must be changed. It would be advantageous if most of these symbols would show smoke'.28 Other charts suggest a deterioration in the standard of work typical of the Vienna Museum.29

Faced with these unsatisfactory results, Neurath remarked on their causes and recommended changes in how the work should be done:

There is a risk that the charts intended for Chicago will be too monotonous and not very impressive. This is due to the fact that the Gesellschafts- und Wirtschaftsmuseum in Wien receives from Chicago more and more sketched schemas so that the task of the Gesellschafts-

PRESENT CONSUMPTION OF BITUMINOUS COAL IN THEUNITED STATES

Each coal symbol represents 50 millions short tons of bituminous coal

![](images/0bac9aa4e3e85ee12f101fe87871eaa084e8677eb4bc76358796162fabeb26bd.jpg)
