![](images/41be2ffa05540428f57159f628dd1c87bc4be40ed04ef06e1177244b5372fe4a.jpg)

T543, 545, 546 Charts produced by the Gesellschaftsund Wirtschaftsmuseum for the Museum of Industry and Science, Chicago, under the direction of Rudolf Modley, 1931–2.

Each unit represents 100 killed miners

und Wirtschaftsmuseum is increasingly reduced to the designing of pictograms and guide-pictures. In this way, the real work of the Gesellschafts- und Wirtschaftsmuseum – which consists of the cooperation between science and graphic design, in the 'transformation'– is suffering considerably. ...

It is therefore recommended that in the future, statistical material should be sent, if possible in a certain selection, with an indication of the task the Museum should undertake and certain technical viewpoints to be followed in designing pictograms and guide-pictures. Sometimes a picture that is technically less correct can have a better visual effect.30

30. Otto Neurath, 'Bemerkungen des Direktors zu den vom Gesellschafts- und Wirtschaftsmuseum in Wien für das Museum of Science and Industry in Chicago angefertigen Tafeln' (Comments by the director of the Gesellschafts- und Wirtschaftsmuseum in Wien on the preparation of charts for the Museum of Science and Industry in Chicago), typescript attached to letter from Neurath to Modley, 13 February 1932 (GWM-MSI).

31. Neurath's proposal may have gone unanswered because Modley was in Vienna between December 1931 and March 1932 to obtain a visa. Letter from the attorneys for the Museum of Science and Industry (Law offices of Rosenthal Hamill & Wormser) to Howard W. Odum, 10 February 1932; folder 267, Howard W. Odum Papers, Southern Historical Collection, Wilson Library, University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill (hereinafter HWOP-SHC).

32. Letter J. B. Hayford to Neurath, 21 July 1932 (GwM-MSI). 33. Marguerite E. Schwarzman, 'Statistics for all', Educational Screen, September 1933, pp.189-90.

34. A series of charts were made, with the caption 'Prepared for Dr. J. A. Schwarzmann [sic] by Dr. R. Modley'. Soon after, three of them appeared in The New York Times (30 April 1933) under the title 'America's foreign trade – the trends charted' and captioned 'Pictographs prepared for Dr. J.A. Schwarzmann'. Modley later mentioned these charts as 'the first ones to be produced in the United States by [him]'. See Modley, How to use pictorial statistics, p. 161.

35. Extract from letter Marguerite E. Schwarzman to Neurath for Mary van Kleeck, n.d. (box 79, folder 1, MVKP-SSC).

36. Letter Neurath to Marguerite E. Schwarzman, 28 March 1933 (box 79, folder 1, MVKP-SSC).

37. Letter Van Kleeck to J. A. Schwarzman, 1 May 1933 (box 100, folder 755, SAR-SWHA).

38. Letter Van Kleeck to Neurath, 30 March 1933 (box 10o, folder 755, SAR-SWHA). Quotes that follow in this paragraph are also from this letter (pp. 2-4).

But Neurath's proposal went unanswered – and in any case it was soon irrelevant.31 Finding itself in financial difficulties in the summer of 1932, the museum closed the Social Sciences division, and Modley, along with other members of staff, lost his job.³²

Following his dismissal from the Museum of Science and Industry, Modley continued working on pictorial statistics. A letter of March 1933 from Marguerite E. Schwarzman to Otto Neurath reveals something of Modley's new direction. Schwarzman, former member of the American Museum of Natural History, and founder and director of the Children's Laboratories in Westchester county, New York, had met Neurath during his visit to the usA. The meeting sparked her interest in his work and she began investigating ways to incorporate the Vienna Method into school education.³³ Unable to afford Neurath's help in creating instructional materials, she asked Modley instead.³⁴ Schwarzman wrote to Neurath about the plan³⁵ and his disapproving reply included these remarks:

In all other respects, Modley, who has been at the administration of our Museum, is surely adequate to flexibly and suitably participate in the suggested field service. I would not entrust him, however, with control and organization of graphic work. We have realized that special training just for that is indispensable. I hope, however, that we will succeed in creating a real branch in usA and to use Modley, just like any other co-workers, in the formation of a generous propaganda organization.³⁶

Mary van Kleeck was also disturbed by Schwarzman's collaboration with Modley, thinking that it promoted 'similar work without approval by Dr. Neurath or even notification to him'.³7 She met with Modley and asked for an explanation. According to a lengthy letter written by Van Kleeck to Neurath, headed 'Dr. Modley's work in the United States', Modley claimed that personal problems meant he needed to earn a living in an area in which he had some expertise.³8 He told Van Kleeck that he would be quite ready to turn over his work, if he develops it as he hopes, to any organization, when it is established'. Since Modley – like many during the Depression – had no regular employment, Van Kleeck accepted his explanation, though warned him that he should not do anything without communicating with Neurath first, if only for ethical reasons. Van Kleeck remarked to Neurath:

[H]e cannot recognize any ethics in the situation. He believes that if he had taken the matter up with you at first you would have negatived it and that it is a great deal better to show first what he can do and then present it to you as a fait accompli... He said that he had hoped to go into industry but had not succeeded and therefore, in view of the fact that he had spent so much time in the Vienna Museum and that experience was at his disposal he could see nothing else for it but to go ahead and use his experience.

What Van Kleeck found hard to accept, however, was that Modley 'did not believe in the international aspect [of pictorial statistics] and did not wish to work on that basis'. She believed that Modley 'felt that the drawing of pictorial statistics was exactly like any other statistics, namely, you presented the facts quite apart from any social philosophy back of them'. Concerned that Modley's independent efforts would become an obstacle to developing Neurath's work in the USA, Van Kleeck decided to remain in touch with Modley, to keep an eye on him.

## The Organizing Committee for the Institute for Visual Education

In early April 1933, following discussions with Harry Laidler and Ann Reed Brenner, Mary van Kleeck called a meeting with the intention of forming an American committee to co-operate with Dr. Neurath'. The meeting was attended by sixteen people, six of whom formed an Executive Committee; it included Laidler as chairman, Van Kleeck as secretary, and Evans Clark, a director of Twentieth Century Fund, as treasurer.³9 Formed around the Executive Committee was an 'Organizing Committee for the Institute for Visual Education', which grew to eighteen people.4º

The principal aim was 'to establish in the United States an organization which can develop and promote the graphic methods of presenting social and economic facts which have been characterized by the Vienna Method as exemplified in the work of the Gesellschaftsund Wirtschaftsmuseum in Vienna under the direction of Dr. Otto Neurath'.4¹ Such an organization, or institute, would have two functions:

40. In addition to Laidler, Clark, Van Kleeck, Brenner, Coit and Brown, members of the Organizing Committee included Helen Alfred (Public Housing Conference), Sara Berbheimp (Labor Bureau, Inc.), Elisabeth Christman (National Women's Trade Union League), Sidonie M. Grenberg (Child Study Association), John Herring (League for Independent Political Action), Henry Ittleson (Vocational Adjustment Bureau), Alvin Johnson (New School for Social Research), Waldemar Kaempffert, Paul U. Kellogg, John Klaber (League for Industrial Democracy), Harlow S. Person (The Taylor Society) and Pierce Williams (Reconstruction Finance Corporation), 'Plan for establishment in the United States of an Institute for Visual Education by the Vienna Method', 5 May 1933 (box 10o, folder 755, SAR-SWHA). Despite its impressive size, the Organizing Committee performed few, if any, significant functions, other than deploying its members to canvass institutions and individuals to identify any that might be interested in Neurath's work. In practice, the work of cooperating with Neurath was undertaken wholly by the Executive Committee.

41. 'Memorandum regarding organization of the work of Mundaneum Institute in the United States', 19 April 1933, p. 1 (box 100, folder 755, SAR-SWHA).

39. Other members of the Executive Committee were Ann Reed Brenner, Elenor G. Coit (Affilated Schools for Workers) and Betrand Brown (Milbank Memorial Fund). 'Informal meeting of group to consider a branch of Mundaneum for Visual Methods in the United States', 18 April 1933 (box 10o, folder 755, SAR-SwHA). Two further members joined later: Virgil Jordan (National Industrial Conference Board) and Spencer Miller, Jr.

42. 'Report to International Foundation for the Promotion of Visual Education', 11 May 1934, p. 1 (box 10o, folder 756, SAR-SWHA).

43. Letter Van Kleeck to Neurath, 3 April 1933 (box 100, folder 755, SAR-SWHA).

44. 'Memorandum regarding organization of the work of Mundaneum Institute in the United States', 19 April 1933, p. 2 (box 100, folder 755, SAR-SWHA).

45. 'Memorandum regarding organization of the work of Mundaneum Institute in the United States', p. 2.

46. 'Pictorial statistics for the United States' (leaflet), June 1933 (IC 7.1 Mun).

47. 'Meeting of Temporary Committee on organization of the work of Mundaneum Institute in the United States', 19 April 1933 (box 100, folder 755, SAR-SWHA).

48. Confidential letter from Van Kleeck to Evans Clark (and Ann Reed Brenner), 24 April 1933 (box 100, folder 755, SAR-SWHA)

'promotion through membership of individuals and organizations wishing to co-operate and to utilize the materials'; and making charts at ‘a workshop, composed of a technical staff selected by and responsible to Dr. Otto Neurath as consultant'.4² Setting up the workshop would be the priority since, as Van Kleeck had assumed from the outset, 'the work will have to be produced in the United States; otherwise we would miss a great many possibilities as persons think it is not feasible to have the production work done in Vienna and hence they put out of their mind all possibility of [using] the Vienna Method in relation to a particular undertaking'.⁴³ She saw the workshop as 'the regional office for the United States of [the] Mundaneum Institute', and thus [i]n accordance with the plans already developed by Mundaneum, members of its staff would probably travel from one center to another'.⁴⁴ She also envisaged that

Plans for a regional office in London contemplate some service to the United States, since the language difficulty in filling orders for America in workshops in Vienna would thus be removed. This close linking up of the American workshop (which might service other parts of the American continents) with Mundaneum is obviously desirable in the interest of developing world travelling exhibits and also in the interest of the highest possible technical perfection in the work.4⁵

The workshop would operate as a ‘non-profit but self-supporting organization, furnishing its services at cost' and its fees ‘based strictly on operating expense, including adequate salaries and reserves'. Fees for institute members, however, would be 'a lower proportion than nonmembers of the Work-Shop's total costs'.“⁶

At an Executive Committee meeting on 19 April, it was suggested that ‘a full-time worker of known ability should be appointed to canvass the field and see how much support it would be possible to obtain for the Institute and from whom it might be expected'.47 Van Kleeck saw Modley as a suitable candidate for the job, not because of his experience but rather because she feared what would happen if he continued to work independently. In a confidential letter to Brenner and Clark, she wrote: 'I am sure that he [Modley] has something to contribute, but we cannot let him run away with it. If we leave him to himself, he may pick up an occasional order, though I fear that he is having a discouraging time. If we were to give him backing, he might create some very serious problems for us by a competitive attitude towards Dr. Neurath'.48

![](images/8162d41c87a1c4a86be17da3e317d3f477d7be4b855508cdb79a1ed0d69ac525.jpg)

Despite Van Kleeck's reservations, the Executive Committee voted in May to appoint Modley as field secretary' to conduct the survey suggested the previous month.4º Modley was provided desk space (by Van Kleeck) in the offices of the Russell Sage Foundation where he worked under Clark's direction between 26 May and 7 July. He prepared a letterhead and leaflet for the committee and interviewed some 67 people representing 56 institutions in various fields, who it was thought might be interested in Neurath's work. A report was submitted to the committee on 11 July and discussed at a meeting two days later. It revealed that 'relatively few of those interviewed were ready to make commitments for orders or members, but interest in the idea was fairly general'. Modley highlighted three problems impeding progress:

1) Difficulty of visualizing, from the material available, the advantage of charts according to Dr. Neurath's method for the purpose of those interviewed;

2) Lack of information as to probable cost of charts;

3) Lack of information as to time required for preparation of charts.⁵º

49. 'Minutes of meeting of Special Committee on Finance', 24 May 1933 (box 100, folder 755, SAR-swHA). Rudolf Modley was not a member of the Organizing Committee for the Institute of Visual Education.

50. Modley, 'To the Chairman and Members of the Executive Committee of the Organizing Committee for the Institute for Visual education', 11 July 1933, p.1 (box 100, folder 755, SAR-SWHA). Quotes that follow in this paragraph are also from this report (pp.1–2).

To address these problems, Modley proposed 'the establishment of a small experimental workshop which, without great expenditure, could make charts for those interested'. He also reported on discussions he had conducted with various members of the committee whose views differed on the exact nature of the workshop's activities. According to Modley's summary, while some committee members felt that 'only charts complying with the international aspects of Mundaneum should be produced', others held that certain criteria regarding the accuracy of statistical material and its suitability for visualization would be sufficient. To resolve these differences, Modley suggested a two-fold approach, namely that

All work done by the workshop and complying with the standards of Mundaneum could appear as produced by Mundaneum America' or any name chosen by the group to represent this point of view.

To widen the Institute's range of activities, the workshop might accept other work meeting the requirements ... . Certainly the wider the field of activity, the lower the cost of production and the less the danger of imitation. This work, statistical accuracy of which would be guaranteed, would appear as produced by the ‘Workshop of the Institute' and would in no way represent a responsibility for Mundaneum.

Although Modley's report does not identify how individual committee members were aligned, it is likely that Clark favoured a workshop serving a wide field, a view opposed, it appears, by Van Kleeck. Indeed she suggested that ‘instead of pushing the formal organization of an Institute, get the method actually used under the circumstances to make it favorably known'.s¹ For her, it was most important to bring Neurath to the uSA where three or four organizations might be found to employ him as a consultant. Clark, on the other hand, argued that the establishment of a workshop, however modest, should be the priority and that ‘if by corresponding with Dr. Neurath an estimate of production of average charts could be worked out on the basis of costs in New York[, t]hat would afford something definite on which to work. It was felt [by Clark] that to have an actual office, however small, made a good talking point in developing further support'.

51. 'Meeting of the Executive Committee of the Organizing Committee of the Institute for Visual Education', 13 July 1933, p. 1 (box 100, folder 755, SAR-SWHA). The quote that follows is also from this source.

Nothing happened immediately after the 13 July meeting. Then, about a month later, Clark urged Van Kleeck to carry on with plans to bring Neurath to the usA, by the middle of September at the latest. Clark explained his urgency to Van Kleeck:

I think that speed is essential in getting the organization under way. The method is already being copied with very little success by important concerns like the McGraw-Hill Company, the Metropolitan Life Insurance Company, the New York Times, etc. It is of the utmost importance that authentic material be produced as soon as possible and given wide publicity. The greatest weakness of the whole program is, of course, the difficulty of restraining imitations. If it is impossible to work out copyright or patent restrictions, the work-shop would have to rely primarily on capturing public attention quickly and then producing at a minimum cost and time.⁵²

Clark proposed that before Neurath's arrival some preliminary work be done for prospective clients of the workshop. He argued: 'It is obvious that the best way to carry on this preliminary work would be to have someone put full time on it under the direction of the Executive Committee. As Dr. Modley has already made the contacts that would naturally be followed up, he would be the logical man to do this work.' Van Kleeck apparently rejected the proposal, believing that the selection of staff should be in Neurath's hands. She understood - perhaps more acutely than other members of the committee – Neurath's negative attitude towards Modley.⁵³ When the committee next met in November 1933 it finally resolved to bring Neurath and Marie Reidemeister to the uSA for three ten-day visits in 1934, though not to set up a workshop.

The Executive Committee's decision clearly frustrated Modley. In a letter to Brenner (but directed at the committee as a whole) he complained: 'There is no necessity to bring anybody from Europe here to select or transform statistics or to direct the production. Charts of equal or better quality can be produced here immediately.'⁵He went on to emphasize how the operations of his own experimental workshop would compare favourably with Neurath's, arguing that 'the Vienna Museum uses hand methods where hand methods are not only unnecessary but even dangerous'. His workshop, by contrast, would produce charts 'without unnecessary hand work in about half the time' using a photomechanical process. 'Through improved coordination', he claimed, 'charts can be produced here about 5o% cheaper than in Vienna; this is despite wages which are at least 1o0% higher.'⁵

Three months after its decisive November meeting the Executive Committee's plans were thrown into disarray when, in February 1934, civil war broke out in Vienna. Otto Neurath was forced to escape to The Hague where the previous summer he had established the International Foundation for (the Promotion of) Visual Education with the help of Mary Fleddérus. In March and April 1934, the committee convened two meetings to address matters. At the first, Van Kleeck asked

52. Letter Evans Clark to Van Kleeck, 8 August 1933, pp. 5-6 (box 100, folder 755, SAR-SWHA). The quote that follows is also from this letter (p. 7)

53. Neurath apparently rejected the plan to set up a workshop: 'Better is an institution, which does not possess a workshop, because we wish to organize a new, really first-class workshop'. 'Notes about the Mundaneum-work in usA according the conferences in Vienna (4–6. Sept.) Miss Van Kleeck, Miss Fledderus, Miss Reidemeister, Mr. Neurath', September 1933 (box 79, folder 1, MVKP-SSC).

54. 'Meeting of the Executive Committee for the Organization of an Institute for Visual Education by the Vienna Method in the United States', 17 November 1933 (box 100, folder 755, SAR-SwHA). In the event, financial circumstances would delay the plan until the following year.

55. Letter Modley to Brenner, 7 December 1933 (box 10o, folder 755, SAR-SWHA). At the same time Modley also sent a letter to Marie Reidemeister in which he criticized Neurath; see below, p. 336, n.129.

56. Letter Modley to Brenner, 7 December 1933 (box 10o, folder 755, SAR-SWHA). Modley may have been referring to the Vienna Museum's use of linocuts to produce pictograms for charts.

57. 'Minutes of Meeting of Executive Committee', 7 March 1934, p. 1 (box 10o, folder 756, SAR-SwHA). According to Brenner, 'the exhibit in the museum in the Rathaus belonged to the City of Vienna, but the Institute and all its materials were privately owned, and included the original clichés for the exhibit material owned by the city'. Quotes that follow in this paragraph are also from this source (pp.1-3).

58. This estimate, for removing essential materials, was later revised to 2,00o dollars. 'An appeal for the Vienna Social Economic Museum', 7 May 1934 (box 100, folder 756, SAR-SWHA).

59. 'Minutes of meeting of Special Committee of the Executive of the Organizing Committee for the Institute for Visual Education', 6 April 1934, p. 3 (box 10o, folder 756, SAR-SWHA).

the committee to consider 'how to obtain immediate funds to help move the Institute [i.e. the Vienna Museum & Mundaneum] to a place of safety'.57 Fleddérus, who had recently been in Vienna as president of the International Foundation for Visual Education, estimated that between 50o and 1,ooo dollars were needed to remove materials to The Hague.s8 Committee members agreed to find funds for this purpose. Van Kleeck then asked members to again consider how to work out some plan for Dr. Neurath's work here, and to get orders, if possible'. While the committee was agreed that it could not bring Neurath to the UsA at that time, it was divided about the alternatives. Van Kleeck suggested that work might still be done by correspondence, but most committee members saw this as impractical, including Brenner. Citing the experience of the Survey in trying to furnish by correspondence an adequate explanation of what they wanted to show in their charts', she observed: 'Despite great care taken, some of the charts could not be used, as a perfect understanding had not been reached as to what was wanted.' Clark, for his part, declared that 'from the beginning he had been interested chiefly in getting an organization at work which could make charts by the Vienna Method', noting that 'At present it seemed practically impossible to achieve this'. On the other hand, he continued, here was Modley, ready to act quickly and, in his opinion, fairly satisfactorily'. Van Kleeck, still opposed to this suggestion, re-emphasized Neurath's view of Modley: 'the Committee could hardly overlook Dr. Neurath's criticism of Dr. Modley's technical work, and must be guided by Dr. Neurath as the Committee's consultant in all technical questions'

The Executive Committee now had to choose: respect Neurath's wish to pursue work that was internationally coordinated, but which would be delayed; or begin making charts immediately, in the usA, in any possible, practical way. By the time of the second meeting in April, the latter view had gained in favour. Modley was invited to the meeting to confer with the committee regarding proposals he had made to individual members. The minutes record Modley's position:

He had waited, hoping that the Committee would take some steps in the autumn [of 1933]; but as no action was taken, he had gone ahead independently.  He had always been willing to co-operate with Dr. Neurath. He felt that he had improved the technique of the Vienna Method and was now in a position to produce charts more cheaply and rapidly by standardizing reproduction of symbols.⁵

Though Van Kleeck questioned whether standardizing symbol reproduction could be effective beyond a certain point, her views were now generally in the minority.6º At the meeting, Modley revealed his plan to establish an organisation called 'Pictorial Statistics, Inc.' with 'the intention to [enable] co-operation between [it and] the International Foundation'.6¹ It forced the issue of'whether the Committee and the International Foundation would wish to recognize in Dr. Modley's organization the American branch'.62 Modley's plan was discussed by the committee, which determined that Neurath should decide the matter. But nothing further transpired and the meeting proved to be the committee's last.

Thus despite prolonged efforts, the attempt to establish an American branch of the Mundaneum ended in failure. The work of the Executive Committee demonstrated the difficulties involved in launching an international enterprise in the middle of the Great Depression. Despite a growing interest in the Vienna Method in the USA, without reliable information about the costs or time for the work, very few organizations or institutions would put so much money into [a] foreigner's travelling expense for consultation purposes'.6³ Meanwhile, the increasing number of Vienna Method imitations suggested that there were easier and more immediate ways to access comparable work. These were problems the committee was unable to overcome. Had Modley been given approval to establish his proposed experimental workshop within the institute envisioned by the Organizing Committee, it is quite probable that the Executive Committee's efforts would have been successful. But by discounting Modley's abilities, Van Kleeck and Neurath forestalled the contributions he might have made.

## Pictorial Statistics, Inc.

In August 1934, following the inconclusive activities of the Organizing Committee for the Institute for Visual Education, Modley established Pictorial Statistics, Inc. The plan for the organization followed the description originally submitted to the committee: a non-profit-making membership corporation executing orders [for pictorial statistics] at cost'.4 Evans Clark was appointed president of its board, with Modley in the roles of executive director and secretary.6⁵ Clark's involvement in the enterprise is not surprising given his earlier support for Modley's plan as a member of the Executive Committee. Clark had also already published the book Stock market control that included charts made by Modley in February 1934.

60. 'Minutes of Meeting of Special Committee of the Executive of the Organizing Committee for the Institute for Visual Education', 6 April 1934, p. 3 (box 100, folder 756, SAR-SwHA). The minutes also record that Waldemar Kaempffert, who had been invited to the meeting, regarded Modley's plans as 'desirable in order to give a complete picture of the American scene'.

61. Modley, 'Pictorial Statistics: report to the Special Committee of the Executive of the Organizing Committee for the Institute for Visual Education', 19 April 1934 (box 100, folder 756, SAR-SWHA).

62. Van Kleeck, 'Report to International Foundation for the Promotion of Visual Education', 11 May 1934, p. 2 (box 10o, folder 756, SAR-SWHA).

63. 'Minutes of Meeting of Executive Committee', 7 March 1934, p. 3 (box 10o, folder 756, SAR-SWHA).

64. Modley, 'Pictorial Statistics',   
19 April 1934, p. 2 (box 100, folder   
756, SAR-SWHA).

65. A number of artists including Franz Hess, Karl Koehler, Henry Adams Grant, Theodore Scheel, and John Carnes were also employed to do the graphic work. The typographical work and printing were handled by Huxley House.

The first large commission begun by Pictorial Statistics, Inc. was a series of charts for the Mississippi Valley Committee (MvC) of the Public Works Administration.6⁶ The commission grew out of a plan formulated by the MvC's chairman Morris Llewellyn Cooke in spring 1934. Cooke, a consulting engineer based in Philadelphia, wanted 'an exhibit to educate the people of the area in the facts gathered by the Mississippi Valley Committee'.‘7 He had asked his friends Paul Kellogg and Mary van Kleeck about involving Otto Neurath in this 'American planning project', hoping to integrate 'something of the technique of Russian planning – especially its graphics'.6⁸ But Cooke, according to Kellogg, was a bit fearful that his associates might shy off at putting Neurath in charge of such work; both because he [was] a foreigner and because of his association with the Socialist regime in Vienna and his activities for Soviet Russia'.“In May, Cooke withdrew from the plan citing difficulties in giving employment to a foreigner – especially non-residents'.7º The commission was instead given to Modley, possibly through Kellogg's mediation."

The result was a lengthy report published in October 1934 containing a number of charts. The charts were made with the assistance of 'Sociographics, Philadelphia'. Sociographics was a group of nineteen previously unemployed architects brought together in 1934 by the Civil Works Administration; the central figure was Philip E. Ragan, an architecture graduate from the University of Pennsylvania."² The group had begun by making charts and illustrations mainly for bulletins issued by Pennsylvania's Department of Labor and Industry. Although the contributions of Sociographics to the Mississippi Valley Committee report somewhat disturbed the visual consistency of its charts, the report's successful completion marked the beginning of a long and productive friendship between Cooke and Modley."³ Cooke held a number of senior posts in the New Deal administration, serving as the director of the Water Resources Section of the National Resources Board (1934) and as the administrator of the Rural Electrification Administration (1935-7).

![](images/11294008b37d5b8d0d2ee30e680b1d50e9bc3cdd936032fb6332a836d936874c.jpg)

![](images/fe11779b501be5231984412c69a3035bff0454b9747363e7826f42d8e879b646.jpg)

![](images/c204356ca4bddd86e1c0e609b4c3e056b54568fc0af9843e00d776d7e9ac4834.jpg)  
Cover and charts from Report of the Mississippi Valley Committee of the Public Works Administration (United States Government Printing Office, 1 October 1934). 279×233mm.

![](images/bee9ba2a328183d5a38ac9aef25d0806cb4a0f775b56d137be9ce74a01d9b430.jpg)  
The first chart shown here, 'Installed capacity and potential hydro installation', was probably designed by Pictorial Statistics, Inc. The other two were probably designed by Sociographics, Philadelphia.

![](images/0cde2733f38e01e9259d6a6f87a26f1845ddd47b5c4a9ee52743a81da614d895.jpg)

![](images/ce703b64d9d79024df2134336042ff1b9411e7ff9afda19a18149d7d6f985dad.jpg)  
Cover and chart from Pennsylvania labor and industry in the Depression (Department of Labor and Industry, Special Bulletin no.39, 1934). Designed by Sociographics, Philadelphia. 227×152 mm.

Cover and pages from pamphlet Our job with the wPA (us Government Printing Office, c. 1936). 230×139mm.

![](images/d9104a4e76794f4b53ccad317e32d614fd95614a1ead87578e66571634ed1bac.jpg)  
Cover and chart from On relief: general relief program, May 1935. Prepared by the Graphic Unit of the Research Section of the Federal Emergency Relief Administration (later Works Progress Administration) in October 1935 under the direction of Calvin Banwell and Rudolf Modley. 200×265mm.

He was able to offer Modley regular work making charts for a variety of publications, such as the report of the National Resources Board (November 1934) and the book Electric power on the farm (1936) for the Rural Electrification Administration. These commissions also increased the amount of work Pictorial Statistics, Inc. received from other agencies of the federal government. An important collaboration also developed with the Works Progress Administration (wPA). The wPA had established the 'Graphic Unit' in its Division of Social Research that regularly employed some half-dozen people to make charts for wPA research monographs, posters, pamphlets and reports. Modley helped direct the unit's work."⁴ Educational bodies also dealt with Modley, including the Office of Education and the National Education Association.7⁵

74. Modley, 'Facts told pictorially', The New York Times, 15 September 1935, p. xx6. The Graphic Unit was established in 1934 when the wPA was known as the Federal Emergency Relief Administration.

75. Modley assisted the NEA by making a number of charts for their publication: 'Modern social and educational trends', Research Bulletin of the NEA, vol. 12, no. 5, 1934.

76. This organization was formed in 1935 under a grant from the Rockefeller Foundation. See 'Data for decision', The Survey, October, 1935, p. 302.

Alongside the expanding activities of Modley's Pictorial Statistics, Inc., Vienna Method imitations were also in evidence. In addition to work by Sociographics or the Graphic Unit of the wPA, examples could be found in the Headline Books' series issued by the Department of Popular Education of the Foreign Policy Association.7 Edited by Rhyllis Alexander Goslin, the series was rich in pictorial statistics, but of a relatively poor standard." By mid-1935, increasing numbers of

![](images/ac4dadd161dc0de8ddbc2285a29be1b6b54d5c556e34d3fec352ebd17f803b87.jpg)  
Cover, back cover, and pages from Ryllis Alexander Goslin (ed.), War tomorrow: will we keep out? (Headline Books, no.1, The Foreign Policy Association, 1935). 233×177mm.

imitations were threatening to overwhelm efforts to establish work of higher quality. As Marguerite Schwarzman warned: 'The pictograph from Vienna has started a real vogue for pictorial charts in this country. This vogue promises to engulf the public and the educator with a deluge of "Americanized" pictographs produced by the trial and error method.'78

In the midst of this ‘vogue', however, the work of Pictorial Statistics, Inc. stood out, in part because clients regarded Modley as reliable, as someone able to draw on the experience he had gained in Vienna. This distinguished him from the inefficient business men' who had no training at all, as one client put it." Modley was also able to exert some influence on the direction and quality of pictorial statistics through his collaboration with groups such as Sociographics or the Graphic Unit of

77. Some 25 volumes were published in the series by 1940. In 1935, Rhyllis Alexander Goslin and Omar Pancoest Goslin co-authored Rich man, poor man, a popular book that contained numerous pictorial charts. Survey Graphic described their work as 'Neurath's method Americanized' (vol. 24, no. 6, June 1935, p. 308). Modley later criticized it, claiming 'the symbols often failed to represent the things they supposedly visualized; some charts have no symbol explanation'. See Modley, How to use pictorial statistics, p.135.

78. Schwarzman, 'Chart intelligence for all', Educational Screen, vol. 14, no. 6, June 1935, pp. 153–4, 157. Here, as in other references to Isotype in an American context, the term 'pictograph' is used to refer to a whole pictorial chart (not a pictogram).

79. Letter Edward A. Filene to Van Kleeck, 9 May 1934 (box 100, folder 756, SAR-SWHA).

the wPA. In these ways Modley established himself as a key figure in the development of the Vienna Method in the United States.

![](images/2716c197da3a54fb08220fc8a1253748562d76619f729bf7fe42e3042c19c701.jpg)  
Neurath as pictured in a review of the International encyclopaedia for unified science in The New York Times (7 August 1938).

80. Letter Fleddérus to Van Kleeck, 14 January 1935 (box 25, folder 5, MVKP-SSC).

In contrast to Rudolf Modley's growing success with Pictorial Statistics, Inc., 1934 and 1935 were years of hardship for the International Foundation for Visual Education in The Hague. One setback of particular seriousness was the Izostat Institute's non-payment of \$6,ooo for work done by Neurath and colleagues in Moscow, which took the wind completely out of the Mundaneum sails'.8º In September 1935 Fleddérus and Van Kleeck, now the foundation's vice-president for the United States, completed a document entitled 'The International Foundation for Visual Education: a three-year's program (with special reference to the United States)'. The document set out plans to direct the foundation's activities towards promoting the principles of the Vienna Method and securing the necessary support and budget to do so. The belief was that ‘there [was] a task of study and promotion of principles which [went] beyond the actual production of visual material'.⁸¹ Included in the programme would be the use of a newly invented name, 'Isotype', to replace ‘Vienna Method’.82

81. 'The International Foundation for Visual Education: a three-year's program (with special reference to the United States)', 9 September 1935 (box 17, folder 6, MVKP-ssc). This document states that the work of the International Foundation for Visual Education would encompass the study and promotion of [visual education] methods, while the Mundaneum (The Hague) would function as a laboratory and workshop for making charts.

## Neurath's return to the United States

An unexpected but valuable contact made in spring 1935 was with Harry E. Kleinschmidt, director of health education for the National Tuberculosis Association (NTA) in New York. Kleinschmidt was visiting German museums at that time and, having heard good words about Neurath's work, made a detour to The Hague to meet him.8ª Following their meeting, a programme of cooperative work was begun. And, towards the end of 1935, Van Kleeck had a welcome offer from the Oberlaender Trust to sponsor a visit by Neurath to the usA on the condition that he receive support from other organizations, too.84

82. The acronym/name 'Isotype' was devised by Marie Reidemeister for use in International picture language and Basic by Isotype. It enabled Neurath and his colleagues to identify their work without reference to the 'Vienna Method', which, in The Hague, clearly no longer applied. 'Isotype' designated both the 'system' of the former Vienna Method and its products, and the 'business organization' that would receive orders from 'commercial as well as educational institutions';

it would produce materials such as books, pamphlets, and secure funds for the Mundaneum (The Hague). See 'The International Foundation for Visual Education: a three-year's program'. The acronym/ name, in the form of the now familiar 'Isotype' mark designed by Gerd Arntz, was thereafter applied to Isotype work to clearly identify – or ‘brand' – it and so distinguish it from the imitations, particularly in the uSA. For the remainder of this essay 'Isotype' will be used instead of 'Vienna Method'.

83. Harry E. Kleinschmidt also saw the health exhibition 'Das Wunder des Lebens' in Berlin. See, Harry E. Kleinschmidt, 'A promising visual method of teaching, injured by imitation, goes begging for lack of appreciation', unpublished typescript, n.d. (ic 8.2 Kle).

84. In November, Van Kleeck received a letter from Wilbur K. Thomas, a director of the Oberlaender Trust, in which he wrote ‘if there were other organizations that were interested and were ready to contribute something toward his coming, our board might consider being of some service'. Quoted in letter Van Kleeck to Kellogg, 5 December 1935 (box 112, folder 860, SAR-SWHA). She replied to Thomas stating that Neurath could secure support from a number of organizations including the National Tuberculosis Association, Twentieth Century Fund, Russell Sage Foundation, Pictorial Statistics, Inc. Survey Graphic and others. Letter Van Kleeck to Wilbur K. Thomas, 4 December 1935 (box 112, folder 860, SAR-SWHA).

In early 1936, with support mainly from the NTA and the Oberlaender Trust, preparations got underway for Neurath's eagerly awaited return. A 'hospitality committee for Dr. Otto Neurath' was formed by some of his supporters, with Modley, perhaps curiously, serving as committee secretary. Modley's role may have resulted from efforts by Paul F. Lazarsfeld to reconcile Modley and Neurath. Lazarsfeld, a well-known sociologist, had previous dealings with both men and apparently felt indebted to each.8⁵ In March an agreement between Neurath and Pictorial Statistics, Inc. was also reached, described by Van Kleeck in a letter to a colleague:

I am happy to say that within the past few weeks a working agreement has been consummated between Dr. Neurath's organization and Pictorial Statistics. This will be a great advantage in the general movement. Pictorial Statistics has been carrying on its work quite independently and have lacked, therefore, the quality which the work would have if it were closer to Dr. Neurath's staff. On the other hand, Dr. Neurath's work in this country has suffered from the fact that he had no representative here. This is going to bring about a co-operative relationship which will make available the highest standard of graphic material for use in [the] presentation of social and economic studies here.⁸⁶

Although Neurath's arrival was initially planned for April, it was postponed on account of other work. He finally arrived in New York in early September. The trip would last for some six months and include a lengthy visit to Mexico. To support his activities, the International Foundation for Visual Education set up a us advisory committee.⁸7 While Neurath was in the usA, several articles promoting Isotype would also be published.88

85. Lazarsfeld had worked with Neurath when Lazarsfeld was at the Wirtschaftspsychologische Forschungsstelle (Research centre for economic psychology) in Vienna. In October 1933, after receiving a one-year travelling fellowship from the Rockefeller Foundation, he visited the usA; he decided to remain there following the outbreak of civil war in Austria in February 1934. When Lazarsfeld became the director of a social research centre newly founded at the University of Newark in New Jersey, he proposed the establishment of a 'socialeconomic museum in Newark' and sought funds to secure Neurath's service as consulting director. Letter Van Kleeck to Wilbur K.

Thomas, 4 December 1935 (box 112, folder 86o, SAR-SwHA.) Lazarsfeld apparently also had a working relationship with Modley, serving as a 'research advisor' for Pictorial Statistics, Inc. from 1936. (Letter Modley to Brenner, 9 March 1936; box 10o, folder 756, SAR-SWHA). Lazarsfeld's efforts to reconcile Neurath and Modley are implied in a letter from Fleddérus to Van Kleeck, 24 January 1936 (box 25, folder 7, Mvkp-ssc). Later, Lazarsfeld asked Neurath to produce graphic work for 'an extensive federal research project on certain problems of unemployment' while, at the same time, making his offer conditional on working with Modley. As Lazarsfeld explained, 'It is evident that while

you are in this country, I could not suggest any use of your methods without your being personally involved in the deal. On the other hand, you know my attitude to Modley and especially since he has done so much work with the government, it would be absolutely unfair to exclude him from this opportunity. You should never forget that it is due him that every official of the federal government is very accessible to the idea of using your methods.' Letter Paul F. Lazarsfeld to Neurath, 7 December 1937 (box 27, folder 44, MVKP-SSC).

86. Letter Van Kleeck to Shelby M. Harrison, 13 March 1936 (box 17, folder 5, MVKP-SSc).

87. The 'advisory committee' included Ann Reed Brenner, John M. Glenn, Henry Ittleson, Alvin Johnson, Waldemer Kaempffert, Harry E. Kleinschmidt, and Knud Lönberg-Holm. The committee's designation and membership appear on a letterhead of the International Foundation for Visual Education, possibly prepared for Neurath's us visit; see, for example, letter Neurath to Charles Morris, 13 October 1936 (box 2, folder 6, The Unity of Science Movement Papers, University of Chicago Library; hereinafter usMP-UCL). Knud Lönberg-Holm, architect and researcher in the Research Department of Sweet's Catalog Service, is a notable member. Early evidence of his interest in Isotype is found in a letter he wrote to Survey Graphic (5 April 1934) asking to 'obtain a dictionary of the graphic symbols developed by Dr. Neurath at the Vienna Mundaneum (box 100, folder 756, SAR-SWHA). Through Ann Reed Brenner's recommendation to Mary van Kleeck, Lönberg-Holm joined preparatory meetings for the IRI's Regional Study Conference held in New York in November 1934, together with his colleague, C. Theodore Larson. (See K. Lönberg-Holm and C. Theodore Larson, Design for increasing productivity, IRI, 1939, p. 4.) In July 1937, the Architectural Record, a journal for which Lönberg-Holm worked as research consultant with Larson, published Neurath's article, 'Visual representation of architectural problems'.

88. These include Victor Weybright, 'Social showman', Survey Graphic, vol. 25, no. 11, November 1936 (see p. 298 above); Waldemar Kaempffert, 'Language of Isotype', The New York Times, 7 January 1937; and Neurath, 'Visual education: a new language' (1937).

89. H.E. Kleinschmidt, 'Otto Neurath, social showman', American Journal of Public Health, vol. 27, January 1937, p. 81.

90. In 1931 Neurath, with the help of Van Kleeck, had proposed an exhibit (apparently unrealized) to Howard W. Odum, head of the social science division of the Chicago International World's Exposition entitled 'A Century of Progress'. (See Odum's notes in folder 261, HWOP-SHC).

91. 'Theme of a fair: a fair for "the man in the street"', 'Memorandum submitted by R. D. K., 16 June 1936, to the Board of Design as a tentative draft', p. 3 (box 135, folder 11, New York World's Fair 1939 and 1940, Incorporated Records, Manuscripts and Archives Division, New York Public Library; hereinafter NYWF-NYPL).

92. 'Principles of technique for visualization in expositions, notes for an address with lantern slides before the staff of the New York World's Fair 1939 at the Empire State Building', 24 September 1936 (box 147, folder 1, NYWF-NYPL) Quotes that follow in this paragraph are also from this source. In his remarks Neurath also referred to his earlier association with the planned Werkbund exhibition 'Die Neue Zeit' (The new era; see pp. 82–3 above).

93. For further discussion on Neurath's contribution to the Fair, see Pieter van Wesemael, Architecture of instruction and delight: a socio-historical analysis of world exhibitions as a didactic phenomenon (Rotterdam: 010 Publishers, 2001), pp. 445-558.

94. H.E. Kleinschmidt, 'Otto Neurath, social showman', p. 81.

95. Marie Neurath & Robin Kinross, The transformer, p. 51.

According to a report by Harry E. Kleinschmidt, by the middle of October Neurath was 'consulting with a number of groups, including officials of the New York World's Fair'.8º Neurath's association with the Fair is suggestive of an ongoing interest in large world expositions with an educational purpose.9º The work had begun already in June 1936, when architect Robert D. Kohn, chairman of the Committee on [the] Theme of the New York World's Fair 1939', asked Van Kleeck to secure Neurath's services as a consultant. Kohn was at work formulating a plan to emphaze the educational and sociological aspects of the Fair: he wanted it to be for ‘the man in the street' – a characteristic phrase of Neurath's.9¹ Soon after arriving in New York, Neurath gave a presentation to the Fair committee, in which he approved the concept of the Fair: It is very interesting that for the first time a world's fair will concern itself with the future and not with the past'.9² Then he applied his customary philosophy of visualization: 'Visual co-ordination instead of visual competition!', and 'The philosophy of an exposition can be consistent; and each booth, each gallery and each hall can be an actual part of the whole'. Despite Van Kleeck's advocacy of work being done by 'Dr. Neurath's group' for the World's Fair, Neurath served only as a consultant to the Fair committee until December 1936.9ª

Other groups with whom Neurath consulted included the New York Public Schools, the New York Maternity Center, the National Tuberculosis Association and the American Medical Association. Kleinschmidt also arranged for Neurath to attend the 65th annual meeting of the American Public Health Association in New Orleans, from 2o to 23 October, where he delivered a paper to the health education section, illustrated with slides.4 Neurath also visited Talladega University (Alabama) and the University of Iowa before travelling to Chicago. There he discussed plans for the International encyclopaedia for unified science with Charles Morris and Rudolf Carnap on 27 and 28 October, before returning to New York

Back in New York, at the offices of the National Tuberculosis Association in Rockefeller Center, Neurath concentrated on NTA work together with Marie Reidemeister, who had arrived in early November The NTA had asked Neurath to develop a travelling exhibition to educate Americans about tuberculosis. The situation at the Rockefeller Center was conducive to fruitful collaboration with NTA staff since its office was a‘single large room' that had 'desks everywhere, with enough distance between them; it was always easy to ask a specialist to come over.'9⁵ Neurath and Reidemeister designed a series of twenty exhibition charts, which were then executed graphically under the direction of Gerd Arntz in The Hague.96 Marie Neurath later described the result: 'The 5,ooo reproductions of our charts, in the size of the originals, went to all corners of the United States as a travelling exhibition, and it was reported that they were studied with great interest and understood by Inuit, Indians and everyone else alike'.97 (See pp. 342–8 for a full showing of the charts.) Booklets were produced using the same material.98

Pages from Tuberculosis: basic facts in picture language. 1939. 156 × 230 mm.

While in New York, Neurath also received an unexpected invitation from Mexico's National Council of Higher Education and Science Research 'to collaborate in its program of establishing centres of higher education for the Mexican working class'." Neurath and Reidemeister travelled to Mexico City in mid-February 1937, where they consulted on the foundation of a National Industrial Museum being set up by the council.1ºº They returned to New York on 29 March and sailed for the Netherlands two days later.

Following his return from the USA, and until 194o, Neurath and the International Foundation for Visual Education continued working for the NTA. The first series of exhibition charts sold out and was reprinted; it was followed by a second series published in 1942 (see pp. 349-53).1º1

tuberculosis through the eye: suggestions for the use of Isotype charts (1938) and Modern man fights tuberculosis: description of a set of Isotype charts and suggestions for their use (1942).

Cover of Tuberculosis: facts in picture language. 1941. 190 × 118 mm.

![](images/bbbba3be2122e61c1600a74949ba28b0625f7223792ce5315d2f9a955abe36e0.jpg)

99. 'Otto Neurath, "Visual Education" inventor, in Mexico', Mexican Labor News, vol. 2, no. 26, 4 March 1937, p. 3 (box 10o, folder 756, SAR-SWHA).

100. Otto Neurath described the work in Mexico in a letter to Charles Morris: 'I am very oppressed [sic] by the Mexican work. It is very

interesting to give consultations to very clever people and to discuss the Isotype Symbols in connection with the Aztecic Symbols [sic]. The chairman of the museum committee is a man with a special knowledge on this field and he brings the facsimiles of the codices and we discuss the possibility to use the one or the other symbol for the representation of the economic and social history of old Mexico.' Letter Neurath to Charles Morris, 12 March 1937 (box 2, folder 7, USMP-UCL).

101. 'Modern man fights tuberculosis', NTA, 1942. This second series of 17 charts dealt with 'the social and public health aspects of tuberculosis'. Work on it began in 1939. A few days after completion of the charts, the German invasion of the Netherlands (10 May 1940) forced Neurath and Marie Reidemeister to flee, leaving the art-work behind. The charts were reconstituted after they settled in England. See Modern man fights tuberculosis, p. 5; and p. 349 below.

Isotype charts published in Compton's pictured encyclopedia (1939). 264× 200 mm.

102. 'Dr. Neurath confers with Compton editors', The Dotted Line, vol. 5, no. 17, 24 April 1939 (pictured on p. 324 below). Again here, 'pictograph' means a chart.

During this period, work was also completed on two publications, Compton's pictured encyclopedia (1939–40) and Modern man in the making (1939). Compton's, a popular illustrated encyclopedia, contained 43 Isotype charts presenting a variety of subjects; the charts were interspersed with numerous other, more conventional, illustrations. In preparing the charts for Compton's, Neurath emphasized the importance of their relationship to the text: Each Pictograph must be specially made to fit the text where it appears. Then text and Pictograph work together to give the same message to eye and mind’. 102

![](images/d80997c18714689535825c0df3865c191cb15a439babe4f74326b8bf8fcbe797.jpg)

## specially prepared for Compton articles by the INTER VISUAL DUCATION

![](images/59e33bec62a44b18933885db7c362508c9f0b8492bc454cb5383a34d6a885cb9.jpg)

under the direction of Dr. Otto Neurath of The Hague, world-famous social scientist and educator

Here, at last, is a scientific "picture language" that visualizes the essential ideas of the modern social studies!

The Compton pictographs-created by Dr. Neurath and his great organization at The Hague-are the result of 20 years of study and experiment with "eye learning."

They have an almost magic influence. They bring abstract principles to life. They give young students a clear grasp of social relationships, often difficult to explain by older methods.

![](images/1d19120a7fdb78de764e819d65f0949796d57545f7e011fd199d8d3a7394d096.jpg)

They stimulate attention, interest,imagination, and understanding. They leave with

DR. OTTO NEURATH

the student a permanent "visual memory" of what he has learned. They train him to new and clearer ways of thinking for himself.

Experts on the needs of American students worked with Dr. Neurath in preparing each of the 43 Compton pictographs. The six shown here give only a suggestion of the wealth of material covered.

COMPTON LEADERSHIP IN VISUAL EDUCATION ONCE MORE TAKES A GREAT FORWARD STRIDE!

# Dr. Neurath Confers with Compton Editors

![](images/143c0c61a1ebeaf5925eb3d588d2a1c5ec937f02d5eced5b2247cc16ab752f58.jpg)  
1. Here Dr. Neurath is explaining to Editors Rollins and Stevers how the clearness of each Compton Pictograph was tested before it left his laboratories at The Hague

![](images/1e921cf2004654500ca3c271a0bdae2bbf72e468bc1a5caa1d6f42410cd9aa4e.jpg)  
2. "Each Pictograph," he says, "must be specially made to fit the text where it appears. Then text and Pictograph work together to give the same message to eye and mind."

![](images/3a7ba53440c978b44e80375d91dd0888178f9883ffa030cd14ee57213aeb79de.jpg)  
3. "We know how to write in our picture language because we have worked out every detail of it for more than fifteen years. Such things cannot be done by guess or accident."

![](images/ca7d10e4adee6f99ac0fe5ad1fc8bd543358d15f1b0a40ccb9463793faf047cf.jpg)  
4. "To work with Compton editors is a great pleasure, for they understand so well the problems of visual educationt" says Dr. Neurath as Editor Stevers raises a critical point.

When Dr. Otto Neurath came from The Hague to confer with Compton editors on the Encyclopedia's visual education problems, Ronald Millar, of the editorial staff, took his camera along to the conferences. The result is this series of excellent candid shots that show something of the exuberant personality of this internationally famous educator.

Here we see him applying himself to problems laid before him by Athol E. Rollins, managing editor, and Martin Stevers, editor for the physical sciences; "holding forth" with vigor upon his ideas and theories; enjoying the reproduction of his Pictographs in the recent publicity booklet; and finally, saying good-bye.

He was most enthusiastic about the Compton handling of the Pictographs, which he and-his staff of experts at the International Foundation for Visual Education had worked out.

A clue to Dr. Neurath's truly vast range of expert knowledge and ability may be seen in a comparison of the two projects that shared his time on this trip to America. We see him here as an expert in the visual interpretation of ideas to make those ideas comprehensible to young students.

He was also working on one of the most "high-brow" of current educational publishing projects — the International Encyclopedia of Unified Science, which is being brought out by the University of Chicago Press. It is the object of this work to so interpret the findings in each branch of science as to make them readily available to the specialists in other branches. Dr. Neurath is the editor-inchief of this project as well as the expert for the social sciences. Other editors and writers include outstanding specialists from all parts of the world. To mention but three: Niels Bohr, the Danish physicist, who won the Nobel prize in physics in 1922 for his theory of the structure of the atom. His picture heads the Compton article PHYSICS. Earl Bertrand Russell, the famous English mathematician and philosopher, whose writings have an international following. John Dewey, the American philosopher, psychologist, and educator, whose ideas have so profoundly influenced American educational methods.

![](images/f011b33c8ca1a598b276faf42ee59cb67fe3f25f73268e7a197ec4bc2e032b68.jpg)  
5. Editor Rollins shows Dr. Neurath a new set of facts and figures which are going to be "translated" into a Pictograph when the master of visualization returns to The Hague.

![](images/fa0ec5957b7423ab475ab0d94f4fff6c9398249d22e487d5f4dd1210d713cd4b.jpg)  
6. Here we see him examining a manuscript in Editor Stevers' field of physical science. Presently he will offer suggestions for pictographic treatment of the subject.

![](images/c11c28d0bcfd3bc3dbcfc3338af13d5cbc0e8ce444db8bf2c8ee757334f390d5.jpg)  
7. Speaking as a world-famous sociologist, he says: "The organization of subjects in Compton's is so good that we are using it as a model and guide even in our very advanced work."

![](images/c94768c6da6e8f66edc17ae9f7b024a6db2f4b50f5e1e0b6fe69a3bd2515c3e8.jpg)  
8. At the end of three days of conferences, Editors Rollins and Stevers accompany Dr. Neurath to the front door of the Compton Building and speed him on his way back to Europe.

Work on Modern man in the making was probably begun in 1936, 103 though its origins can be traced to 1933 when Neurath proposed two publications to publisher Alfred A. Knopf: 'Pilgrimage of man' and 'Atlas: mankind in our times'. The first appears closest to what became Modern man in the making; according to Neurath, it was to emphasise the connection of all ethnic groups and peoples from primaeval times onward, a kind of “Sukzessiver Internationalismus".1º4 To an even greater extent than in Compton's pictured encyclopaedia, Modern man in the making was notable for its closely related charts and text, its picturetext-style'.1ºs As Neurath explained in the book's foreword:

Impressive visual aids do not merely act as illustration or as eye-bait in this book; they are parts of the explanations themselves. The reader may not understand the contents by reading the text only; he must ‘read' the pictures as carefully as the text.10⁶

In some ways, this 'picture-text-style' was prefigured in a book published two years earlier, The United States: a graphic history, which featured pictorial charts by Modley. Indeed Modley was credited as co-author of the book, with the following reason given in the foreword: ‘The visual aids it employs, pictorial statistics in this case, are an integral part of the book rather than simply illustrative material.' Modley's charts (some of which are clearly based on Viennese examples) were not as closely woven into the text of The United States as were the charts in Modern man in the making, and Neurath's book also benefited from a much more extensive use of colour (see overleaf).

103. 'Knopf pushes Neurath: Isotype book', The Publishers' Weekly, 12 August 1939, pp. 457–8.

104. Letter Neurath to Alfred A. Knopf Inc., 21 November 1933 (box 79, folder 1, MVKP-SSC).

106. Otto Neurath, Modern man in the making (1939), p. 8.

![](images/121566ea8510e73f1620d6d47716466f8f1c2a351305951efdcfd447d93679a1.jpg)  
Cover and pages from Louis M. Hacker, Rudolf Modley, & George R. Taylor, The United States: a graphic history (New York: Modern Age Books, 1937)

Charts in the book were placed alongside corresponding text but always on separate pages, whereas charts in Modern man in the making were fully integrated (overleaf).

Jacket, cover, and pages from Modern man in the making. 1939. 273× 220 mm.

Charts on the pages illustrated here show the full range of seven solid inks used to print the book (see also p. 117 above).

## Modern man in

the making

The chart on the righthand page here gives statistical 'silhouettes' of four basic measures of the standard of living in various countries. In his essay 'Inventory of the standard of living' (1937) Neurath had.proposed these as 'crude tools' for showing 'complexes"... composed of various quantities', and explained that he chose the term 'silhouettes' for them 'with a view to graphic demonstration'. It is strange, then – given that Isotype pictograms are a kind of silhouette – that this chart is a traditional bar chart, with pictograms only used as a supplementary key.

The 'Silhouettes of war economy' shown here do employ pictograms. During the war that was to follow, Neurath often referred to these charts as an indication that the Axis Powers could not win it, due to the superiority of resources on the Allied side.

Reviewing the body of us work, Modern man in the making was the outstanding accomplishment of Neurath and his colleagues, while material made for the National Tuberculosis Association was the most extensive. But the work in total demonstrated that despite the efforts of Neurath and others, Isotype's impact in the uSA was mainly confined to the field of health education, and to appearances in just a few (albeit major) publications. This seems to have discouraged one of Neurath's most important allies, Mary van Kleeck, whose motivation for supporting Neurath's work had in large part been based on its use for social and economic purposes. By summer 1937, Van Kleeck had stepped down as vice-president of the International Foundation for Visual Education in the usA and was replaced by Kleinschmidt; thereafter her correspondence with Neurath fell away.

## Standardized pictograms

As already mentioned, it seems that before Otto Neurath's arrival in the USA, he and Modley had been reconciled. In his role as secretary of the Hospitality Committee, Modley had worked out a plan to ensure the success of Neurath's visit. But in practice Modley's plan achieved little, only a lunch held in honour of Neurath hosted by the committee on 28 September 1936.1º7 There is also evidence that the relationship between the two men soured again. This is found in Modley's book How to use pictorial statistics, published the following year, in which he presents a comprehensive study of pictorial statistics in the USA. Polemical statements in the book appear to have fanned the Neurath-Modley controversy', as one contemporary reviewer called it.¹ºª

In his foreword, Modley first expresses admiration for Neurath: 'Any book which attempts to deal with the method of pictographs must acknowledge its indebtedness to the genius of Otto Neurath. He more than any one man created that method and made it into a significant tool'. But Modley then criticizes: ‘This makes it more than regrettable that Dr. Neurath has not found it possible to follow what seems to me the inevitable trend in the development of the pictograph. I hope that the success of our American experiments will convince him that the restrictions he has set up are not fundamental implications of the method.'Modley is here referring to the international standardization of pictograms. 'Dr. Neurath holds firmly to the belief that pictorial symbols should be international, designed within rigid limitations so that a pictorial Esperanto will be created. He wishes to circumvent a pictorial Tower of Babel and produce at once an international picture language. With that ultimate aim I heartily agree, but I believe his method of achieving it is Utopian.' 1º9 Modley felt that a different method was needed:

I believe that pictographs must gradually be accepted as a tool of communication. To achieve this, the symbols must first grope their way into the consciousness of a restricted national audience. They must speak in terms which are comprehensible. Obviously, such symbols must be prepared with their specific audience in mind.110

Modley gave as an example the inevitable differences that would be required between pictograms of an American farmer and a Chinese farmer, if each were to be recognized by their respective audiences. Pictograms of an American labourer and farmer designed by Modley were in turn different from those designed by the Gesellschafts- und

Wirtschaftsmuseum. Modley believed that a kind of 'Americanization' of Neurath's method with respect to pictogram design was essential if they were to be accepted by Americans.

Modley's belief that pictograms should be first understood in the specific community using them had informed his own work in the usA, work that he saw as an experiment' in that process. The 'ultimate aim' of establishing pictograms internationally would take more time. Even signs like ‘+’ and ‘-’, Modley argued, were only understood and accepted gradually; therefore 'If abstract symbols of such simplicity as these developed so slowly, it would be indeed sanguine to expect pictorial symbols to be internationally accepted without a period of trial.'111 This trial period was essential since To accept it [a given pictogram] and learn from it opens the way to a future cooperation between the various agencies from which, in due time, an international symbol language may arise.' 112

Modley's reference to cooperation between 'the various agencies' is notable, as he went on to describe: 'An attempt is being made to establish cooperation between all the groups which produce pictorial statistics in this country and abroad, so that they can make use of the best methods and symbols'.1³ Modley's interest in the problem of standardized pictograms probably grew out of his experiences working with a variety of organizations and agencies. He may have recognized the need for coordination among those producing pictorial statistics, but to Modley, Neurath's approach was counterproductive:

Monopolistic tendencies of any one group are not only hopeless but hurt the cause of graphic presentation since they lead to inferior replacements and necessitate modifications of the highest standards. It is regrettable that Dr. Neurath has not found it possible to cooperate with any group which does not agree with the rigid standardization of symbols he demands. 114

Modley's criticisms arose after his contact with Neurath in September 1936. One might assume that Modley proposed to Neurath that they work together towards a gradual standardization of pictograms, but that Neurath rejected the proposal.

But why had Neurath refused to cooperate? Neurath certainly did not rule out the possibility that variant pictograms were sometimes needed for specific audiences, though he was very careful about introducing them. Work done for the NTA, as recounted by Marie Neurath, offers an example: 'The only challenge to our usual symbolism came when we were told that crosses should not be used for "death" in our

![](images/378318e08de8690d8982a7260a891c78d4e5d6a108ecba4e7122c8b34a317154.jpg)

Pictograms of labourer and farmer:

designed by Gerd Arntz (upper pair);

designed for Pictorial Statistics, Inc. (lower pair).

111. Modley, How to use pictorial statistics, p. xv. This argument is partly spurious since 'pictorial symbols' (pictograms) were intended to be understood and thus accepted immediately, whereas abstract symbols would indeed take time to be learnt and accepted.

112. Modley, How to use pictorial statistics, p.xv.

113. Modley, How to use pictorial statistics, p. 136. Modley seems to have been involved in two standardization projects around this time: 'Dpw graphic standards project', undertaken at the Teachers College of Columbia University; and 'Pictorial standards project, wPA'. See, J. W. Wrightstone, 'Conventional versus pictorial graphs', Progressive Education, vol. 13. October 1936, p. 460.

114. Modley, How to use pictorial statistics, p. 136.

Pages from Tuberculosis: basic facts in picture language. 1939

115. Marie Neurath, 'Isotype' (1974), p. 147.

116. Letter Neurath to Marguerite E. Schwarzman, 26 August 1933 (box 27, folder 44. MVKP-SSC).

117. International picture language (1936), p. 32. In a letter to Van Kleeck discussing the benefits of Isotype, Neurath compared it to the tendency in modern architecture towards international standardization: 'In one point we are even much better to it: the architecture in a country is based on a large number of architects, building masters, furniture carpenters etc., in contrast the Viennese Method of the visual education for the whole earth can be supplied or organized by a relatively small group of people at least'. Letter Neurath to Van Kleeck, 28 March 1933 (box 79, folder 1, MVKP-SSC).

118. In 1939, Neurath would make two further trips to the usA, which are not otherwise discussed in this chapter. The first was in late February when he delivered the lecture 'Man and his world' to the Institute of Arts and Sciences of Columbia University at the McMillin Academic Theater in New York. The second was in early September to attend the Fifth International Congress for the Unity of Science at Harvard University; he was still in the usA when war in Europe was declared. For Neurath's visit in February, see, 'Announcement of Dr. Neurath's visit to the United States', International Foundation for Visual Education, 10 February 1939 (box 100, folder 756, SAR-SWHA).

statistical charts about mortality: they remind too much of death, and the Americans don't like that. After long deliberation we decided on urns instead since they seemed to be less objectionable'.11⁵ But as Otto Neurath remarked of Modley's early work in the UsA: 'the differences in the treatment [of his pictograms] always interfere. It would be bad, if instead of a pictorial Esperanto (Bildesperanto), a small Tower of Babel produces several picture languages. That would be a disadvantage, even if each variant was actually good.'¹⁶ Such remarks indicate that Neurath did not believe that cooperative work was required to establish pictograms of a higher order since Isotype pictograms were already standardized. His concern was instead to diffuse Isotype internationally, but control it centrally:

In building up such a language, great care has to be taken to see that as much as possible may be said with the help of the smallest number of words. That is to say, for our picture language one general list of a limited number of signs is needed for international use, and this has to be worked out by or under the control of one chief organization. (This organization is now the IsOTYPE work-room at the Hague.)117

Modley was clearly opposed to this approach and sceptical of its 'Utopian' dimension. Instead, he proposed that pictograms should be standardized for a specific community before they were used internationally; to achieve this required cooperative efforts.

## The problem of imitations

In late September 1937, Otto Neurath returned to the UsA for a visit lasting several weeks.118 As he had the year before, he attended the annual meeting of the American Public Health Association in New York with

![](images/1db04882393e6eb11f537db09b98cbe20b0690935209b9498e5f3ea650b1e530.jpg)

Harry E. Kleinschmidt (5-8 October).11 There the two men contributed to an exhibition mounted each year at the meeting to highlight methods of presenting scientific information. Kleinschmidt led a session on Isotype methods as part of the meeting's Health Education section 120 while Neurath exhibited a series of panels titled 'Isotype exhibition technique' based on the concept of an 'Exhibition on exhibits'.121 They hoped that Isotype would appeal to public health workers who might then adopt it for health education.122

## ISOTYPE Exhibition Technique

ISOTYPE exhibition principles are the same whatever purpose the method may be applied to

From general exhibition principles the way leads to problems arising from single exhibits, thence to the functional components of the exhibits, the symbols in particular.

The ISOTYPE method is calculated to look after visitors' interests and not to govem them.

Combine multilicity and deamess, avoid overcrowding!

Satisfy many interests, avoid monotonyl

Stress daily life matters; avoid too learned problems!

Do not word what you can visualize!

Unify visualization in charts and models, use the same visual dictionary, grammar and style throughout!
