Charts made for use in training staff at the Department of Health in New York City. 1939. 280 × 430 mm. (IC 7.3/57 & 52)

![](images/db04a56bf0cd29a9037d76ac7a28e79f004d96091d337157c02ee05ec6c45809.jpg)

In his new role as vice-president of the International Foundation for Visual Education in the UsA, Kleinschmidt took over the work of promoting Isotype, though mainly within the health education field. In addition to the already mentioned exhibition and publication work done for the National Tuberculosis Association, the film On the firing line featuring Isotype charts was made and released in 1939.¹²³ Kleinschmidt and Neurath co-authored Health education by Isotype, a booklet published by the American Public Health Association, also in 1939. It described the principles of Isotype and explored its application to health education. An order was also received for a series of Isotype charts about public health in New York City, commissioned by the Department of Personnel Training of the City Department of Health in New York City; these, too, were published in 1939.

123. 'Motion pictures', American Journal of Public Health, April 1939, vol. 29, pp. 384-5. For an interesting account of the cultural meaning of the film, see Gregg Mitman, 'The color of money: campaigning for health in black and white America', in David Serlin (ed.), Imagining illness: public health and visual culture (Minneapolis: University of Minnesota Press, 201o), pp, 40–61.

124. Kleinschmidt, 'A promising visual method of teaching' (ic 8.2 Kle), p. X−1.

Despite such accomplishments, Kleinschmidt looked back on them with some regret:

No experience in my career has saddened me more than the failure of public health workers to appreciate and adopt a visual education method which I believed to be made-to-order for health education, namely, Isotype, a sharp-edged tool of precision and functional beauty and yet one which received only supercilious attention when it was offered by its author.124

Kleinschmidt drew his own conclusions about why it had been obstructed:

One reason is probably that its value was depreciated by imitations. And unfortunately these imitations came to our country before the genuine. Alert publicists were attracted at once by Neurath's original symbols. They caught the superficial aspects of the new technique and capitalized [on] them without taking the trouble to master the more fundamental principles. The cute little men' were a welcome relief from the pie graphs, bars and curves from which the public shied as from a plague. It was so easy, furthermore, to improve' the symbols by adding here a little, changing there a little to make them more 'snappy'. 125

It was a problem that Neurath and his allies had contended with throughout the 193os.126 Modley was also aware of the problem, and recognized his own part in it:

We take part of the credit for the rapidity with which the Americas have adopted and adapted the language of pictures. We must also take part of the blame for a large number of pseudo-pictographs, which have been produced by others; meaningless or misleading applications of our symbols.1²7

But however contrite Modley may have been, Kleinschmidt and Neurath quite probably included him among the imitators, given his work independent of Neurath; indeed Kleinschmidt's remarks just quoted could be interpreted as referring to Modley, among others. Modley's own view would have been more nuanced: that his work did not imitate Neurath's but followed it and yet was distinctive in its responsiveness to conditions in the USA.

Modley attempted to establish this distinctiveness in part by asserting a general separateness of work in the usA, as suggested by the history of pictorial statistics. In 1937, he set out such a history in his book How to use pictorial statistics.¹²8⁸ There he cites a chart shown in Willard Cope Brinton's Graphic methods for presenting facts (1914) as a

127. [Modley] (ed.), 100o pictorial symbols, p. v.

128. Modley, How to use pictorial statistics, p. 126. Modley's is one of the first historical accounts of pictorial statistics. A similar account was qiven in Funkhouser, 'Historical development of the graphical representation of statistical data'. While both accounts were published in 1937, Modley was probably unaware of Funkhouser's, as he makes no reference to it in his book.

Number of passengers carried on the railroads of the United States in 1899 and in 1911 compared

From Willard C. Brinton, Graphic methods for presenting facts (1914), p. 39.

129. Modley's interest in assembling an historical account of graphic methods for displaying statistics apparently developed after his arrival in the usA. In 1932, he joined the American Statistical Association, an organization to which Brinton also belonged. ('Members added since June, 1932', Journal of the American Statistical Association, vol. 27, no. 179, September 1932, p. 334.) By 1933 Modley had discovered Brinton's book, since he referred to Brinton in a letter to Marie Reidemeister: 'Now finally our experiment [Modley's proposal to the Organizing Committee of the Institute for Visual Education] is nothing more than a continuation of Neurath's idea, as Neurath is nothing more than a continuation of Brinton's, Brown's, or others' ideas' (Modley's use of 'continuation' [Fortsetzung] refers to a sequence in time, not the influence of one on another.) 'Auszug aus einem Brief von Dr. Rudolf Modley an M. Reidemeister' (Extract from a letter from Dr. Rudolf Modley to M[arie]. Reidemeister), 9 December 1933 (box 25, folder 13, MVκP-SSC).

130. Modley, How to use pictorial statistics, p.126. Neurath's own view of the matter can be found in a letter he sent to Brinton, 30 August 1933 (box 27, folder 44, MVKP-ssc), in reply to another sent by Brinton to Neurath (whereabouts unknown). Judging from Neurath's letter, Brinton asked him to send material related to pictorial statistics for inclusion in his 'new' book (probably Graphic presentation, 1939). Neurath promised to send the requested materials, then mentioned Brinton's earlier book,

# 14,591,000 1899 ONE MILE 1911 32,837,000 ONE MILE

precursor of Isotype and identifies it as the earliest example of pictorial statistics. Thus the introduction of the Vienna Method to the USA around 193o was, from an American perspective, a reintroduction' of pictorial statistics.¹²ª In this way, Modley gave a kind of legitimacy to his independent work in the UsA, since Brinton demonstrated that Isotype was neither unique nor first. But if Modley gave priority to Brinton's example, he did not claim that it had directly influenced Neurath – though he did imply that it was Neurath who was catching up:

It is also interesting to note that in spite of the similarity of this American sample [Brinton's] to our presentations of today, it was not known in Europe and consequently the European pictograph had to go through numerous stages of development before it arrived at a stage comparable to the chart shown in Brinton's work.1³0

Still, Modley acknowledged the substantive difference between them:

While [Brinton's chart] was nothing but an accidental occurrence in the search for a better way to present facts, Dr. Neurath tried to build up his method on a logical basis. He visualized a system in which picture words would be combined into a symbol dictionary, forming the working stock of an international picture language.1³1

Neurath's method was indeed better because logical and systematic, and it was this that Modley saw himself as inheriting from Neurath and Isotype. But thereafter, Modley criticized Isotype as 'too rigid to permit general adoption of the method' because its pictograms were designed for an international audience; his, by contrast, were more acceptable

Graphic methods for presenting facts: 'We are glad to have in our library a copy of your book, which already at such an early date gave an overview of graphic methods.' Neurath had therefore read Brinton's book by 1933, a fact that may support claims that Neurath was influenced by Brinton. But it is doubtful that he had read Brinton's book when first devising the Vienna Method. In his reply to Brinton, Neurath cited battle plans as precursors of his method, as he often did. For discussions of Brinton's putative influence on Neurath, see Chizlett, 'Damned lies and statistics: Otto Neurath and Soviet propaganda in the 193os'; Vossoughian, Otto Neurath: the language of the global polis; Neurath & Kinross, The transformer, pp.99-10o. Elsewhere, Neurath acknowledged precedents in the use of pictograms: when, for example, in the article 'Social showman' (Survey Graphic, vol. 25, no. 11, November 1936), his work was introduced with the phrase ‘The

significant Little Man made his first appearance in America in the pages of Survey Graphic in 1932' [i.e. in the work of the Vienna Museum]. Neurath commented on the proof: 'As a historian I must say that we find such little men before Neurath in different countries and in different periods – but without a system, only as single attempts'. Letter Neurath to Brenner, 12 October 1936 (box 100, folder 756, SAR-SWHA).

131. Modley, How to use pictorial statistics, p.130.

to Americans. This was what distinguished his work from Isotype and gave it an advantage, in his view.

Those who imitated Isotype in the 193os apparently saw it as little more than a system governed by rules that were easily mastered, and on this basis they produced work that was superficially similar. Neurath's criticism of such imitations emphasized that Isotype was not merely made up of pictograms configured according to a method determined by definite rules, but was instead distinguished by the work of transformation. He had already made this point in his reflections on work directed by Modley for the Museum of Science and Industry:

Public opinion has recognized that the achievement of the Gesellschaftsund Wirtschaftsmuseum in Wien – the 'Vienna Method' – does not only consist of following rules, but far more in the particular cooperation, through years of joint work, of a well-attuned group of people. ...

If imitations of the 'Vienna Method' have hitherto failed so badly, it is because 'transformation' – the cooperation between science and graphic design on the basis of a group tradition – cannot be secured by following rules.¹³2

Although transformation' was defined as a process of converting the bare factual data and the main idea into the basic layout',1ª3 Neurath always considered it as a process that was difficult to explain precisely:

There are many transformation rules, some hundreds of them. Since the application of the rules cannot become standardized, but each new picture needs, as it were, a somewhat new invention of combinations, There is no possibility to transfer the rules in a simple way, one has to become acquainted with the whole structure of rules and to learn how to weigh them from case to case, i.e. transformation needs rules plus much routine.

Not only, therefore, were there many rules of transformation to learn, they should also be applied flexibly, as circumstances demanded and not automatically. To do so required experience, but in doing so a greater variety of work resulted. In this respect, the efforts of those who sought to capitalize on Isotype 'without taking the trouble to master the more fundamental principles' (Kleinschmidt) frequently lacked variety and interest, as well as rigour and clarity.

That Modley himself placed relatively little emphasis on the role of transformation is indicated both by Neurath's criticisms of his work (at the Museum of Science and Industry and subsequently) and by Modley's failure to mention transformation in How to use pictorial statistics Instead, he limited himself in that book to a 'how-to' discussion of the technical aspects of chart production. Although Modley emphasized

133. Neurath and Kleinschmidt, Health education by Isotype, p.14.

134. Otto Neurath, 'Memorandum: dovetailed plans on series of books, textbooks, books for children', typescript, c. 1944, (IC 3.2/61).

Chart made by pupils using symbol sheets. From Shoen, 'The making of maps and charts', p. 95. A similar chart, designed by Modley, appears in The United States: a graphic history (see p.325 above).

135. Modley, How to use pictorial statistics, p. 145. The term 'transformation' does not occur anywhere in Modley's publications but does in some unpublished documents. One document suggests that he undertook transformation work for Pictorial Statistics, Inc. See Modley, 'Pictorial Statistics: report to the Special Committee of the Executive of the Organizing Committee for the Institute for Visual Education', 19 April 1934 (box 100, folder 756, SAR-SWHA).

137. Earl G. Millison, 'Review of How to use pictorial statistics', Journal of the Statistical Association, vol. 33, no, 202, June 1938, pp.489-90.

136. Harriet H. Shoen, 'The making of maps and charts', in Ruth West (ed.), Utilization of community resources in the social studies, Ninth Yearbook (The National Council for the Social Studies, 1938), pp. 83-98.

138. Modley's symbol sheets were only available in the usA.

the need for 'a highly specialized and trained group' to make pictorial statistics, he seemed unable to recognize that the neglect of transformation, as Neurath understood it, was a likely cause of poor imitations.' 135 Instead, apart from the explanations he offered in his book, Modley's strategy to minimize such imitations was to supply pictograms readymade. Thus, around 1936, he began the trial sale of ‘symbol sheets'; each sheet included 95 symbols classified into 25 subject areas.1ª⁶ Although Modley indicated that the supply of symbol sheets should mainly serve an educational function, they were intended for anyone who wanted to make charts. As a reviewer of How to use pictorial statistics pointed out, 'one very important factor involved in making [a] pictorial chart' was the difficulty of 'obtain[ing] the service[s] of an artist who has had training in this method of presenting statistics. ... Mr. Modley is undoubtedly aware of this problem, however, for he has made available various symbols which may be purchased.'1³7 The reviewer also endorsed the notion that the supply of well designed pictograms would stimulate good work, at least in part.

## Neurath and Modley

Modley's initiative to supply symbols sheets pointed to what, in future, would become the focus of his activities associated with pictorial statistics. These activities were guided not only by the notion that widely available, standardized pictograms would increase the likelihood of good work in the usa,1³8 but also by the belief that pictograms and symbols were important in themselves. Thus in 194o, in collaboration with the social scientist Harold Lasswell, Modley proposed a 'Dictionary

![](images/7c91400e93dbecb51bbcff1a8490f80effd3c4ffc7d851faeb81f0f8d5cae2dc.jpg)

Pages from 100o pictorial symbols ('designed and copyrighted by Pictograph Corporation. New York, N.Y.', 1942). 217 × 140 mm.

In the foreword to this book Modley explained that 'many designers' had worked on these pictograms, including Walter Huxley, Henry Adams Grant, Karl Koehler, and Theodore Scheel.

of the graphic symbol in current use in America' that 'would provide an authority for reference, promote standardization and the wider use of symbols, and afford an opportunity for the study of change in symbols and their significance'; to fund this he submitted the proposal to the Carnegie Corporation in May 194o.¹³9 That same year, Modley set up Pictograph Corporation, a commercial offshoot of Pictorial Statistics, Inc.14º Two years later, Pictograph Corporation published the book 100o pictorial symbols, a collection of pictograms arranged by subject and drawn from the resources of Pictorial Statistics, Inc. Modley explained

139. Modley, 'A proposal to compile a dictionary of graphic symbol in current use in America' (box 65, folder 866, HDLP-YUL). The request for funding was apparently unsuccessful.

140. According to The New York Times, Pictograph Corporation would 'service commercial users of pictorial statistics' while the parent company Pictorial Statistics, Inc. would 'limit itself to the experimental and educational work for which it was organized'. 'Advertising news and notes', The New York Times, 19 September 1940, p. 45. Modley was named as the representative of Pictograph Corporation.

that the purpose of the book was ‘to be an aid to all of those who endeavour to master the rules of pictography', adding ‘to them we gladly extend the right to use in experiments the symbols shown here'.141

Neurath, for his part, never sought to supply pictograms on their own to the public, and while Isotype pictograms were compiled in a 'picture dictionary', this was done as a working reference to support further Isotype work.¹⁴² Neurath instead approached the distribution of Isotype material through the concept of a 'visual thesaurus', which would serve not only as a work of reference but also as ‘a kind of visual centre from which one could provide exhibitions, books, etc. with visual material of any kind'.¹⁴³ The relationship between the thesaurus and any particular application would be a flexible one, as Neurath explained:

If the thesaurus [is] published, writers may add textbooks, articles, may write explanatory texts etc., but the primary body of the Isotype Thesaurus has to remain free of any kind of additional sayings – even my own. The people interested in the thesaurus material, may use it in different combination[s]. That is a question of organizing the copyright etc. That is a matter of the publicity body, not of the Isotype body. Both separațed carefully. That [has been] our unchanged policy through two decades. This policy enabled us to co-operate with the most different types of institutions, governments, writers and publishers. 144

Neurath kept the plan for the visual thesaurus as one of the aims of the Isotype Institute that was established in Britain, along with preparing Isotype charts for various educational products including films and books. Neurath certainly advocated an ideal of pictograms (picture language’) as a tool for overcoming language barriers.¹4⁵ But his priority was to use the Isotype method for visual education. To detach the pictograms from the method for the purpose of disseminating them to the public was out of the question.14⁶

Neurath's outlook and Isotype work were fundamentally international. Modley, by contrast, 'did not believe in the international aspect

145. Significantly, in his consultation for the World's Fair in New York, Neurath did suggest the independent use of pictograms 'as labels or signs': 'We can use symbols ałso for daily work. For instance, it is important to use certain symbols as a picture language in hotels in all countries, and not use different picture language in different hotels. ... Symbols can be both attractive and information

146. See Christopher Burke, 'The linguistic status of Isotype', p. 51; and p. 499 below.

of the pictorial statistics and did not wish to work on that basis',147 an assertion well demonstrated by his work. He simply saw pictorial statistics as awonderful new method' that could be put to use by anyone;148 in this respect his activities helped make them suitable to 'American needs’.14ª His wish to ‘go into industry' (as noted by Van Kleeck) was fulfilled through his work and with the establishment of Pictograph Corporation, though this interest later waned when in 1945 he sold the company to colleagues. His subsequent activities mainly involved independent consultancy work that was for the most part unrelated to ‘visual service'.1⁵º Modley did however maintain his interest in the standardization of symbols both in the usA and internationally. Between 1956 and 1959, he collaborated with Marie Neurath and the industrial designer Henry Dreyfuss in a project to compile a dictionary of graphic symbols for Western countries (see pp. 513-14 below). In 1966, working with the cultural anthropologist Margaret Mead and others, he established Glyphs, Inc., an organization whose aim was the development of international graphic symbols. Modley devoted himself to this work until his death in 1976. 151

147. Letter Van Kleeck to Neurath, 30 March 1933 (box 100, folder 755, SAR-SWHA).

148. Letter Modley to Brenner, 2 April 1933 (box 10o, folder 755, SAR-SWHA).

149. Modley, 'Facts told pictorially', The New York Times, 15 September 1935, p. xx6.

150. Examples of Modley's work in ‘visual service' after 1945 include the design of graphic material

for four books published by the Twentieth Century Fund: Thomas R. Carskadon, usA: measure of a nation (1949) and uSA in new dimensions (1957); and Arnold B. Barach, The new Europe and its economic future (1964) and USA and its economic future (1964). He also co-authored Pictographs and graphs: how to make and use them (New York: Harper & Brothers, 1952) with Dyno Lowenstein; it was in effect a revised edition of How to use pictorial statistics. Modley described his work at this time in terms of 'symbology', suggestive of his shifting interest from pictorial statistics to graphic symbols. See, Modley, Graphic symbols: new dimensions for mass communications (leaflet), 1964.

151. For accounts of Modley's activities after the Second World War, see Hisayasu Ihara, 'Rudolf

Modley's contribution to the standardization of graphic symbols', Proceedings of IASDR 2011, the 4th world conference on design research, Delft, 31 October - 4 November 2011; and Keith Bresnahan, '"An unused Esperanto": internationalism and pictographic design, 1930–70', Design and Culture, vol. 3, no. 1, 2011, pp. 5–24.

This exhibition of charts was printed in an edition of 5,ooo copies and distributed in the usA and Canada (for which French-language versions were also made). The introductory text of a promotional leaflet is reproduced below:

# FIGHTINGTUBERCULOSIS

## 20 EDUCATIONAL CHARTS IN ISOTYPE

measuring 24" x 36" each, lithographed in lively COLOR on strong paper. Packed in corrugated card cartons weighing about eight pounds per set. Sold in complete sets only.

Universities, colleges, high schools, grade schools, professional training schools, health departments, tuberculosis associations and other institutions engaged in Health Education will be interested in these twenty charts.

With each set is furnished FREE a manua/ telling how to use the charts to best advantoge —in the classroom, from the lecture platform, as wall charts, as window displays, etc. The manual also includes directions for building inexpensive exhibits from the kind that can be used at county fairs or community meetings to the kind that will satisfy the standards of the museum.

In this series the colors have a definite meaning. ORANGE denotes health, or the desirable. BLACK stands for sickness or death; BLUE and GREEN for environmental factors; RED for protective measures. This color scheme is consistently applied on all charts. The SYMBOLS are characterized by their simplicity. The skillful combination of symbols produces the /SOTYPE PICTURE in such a way that the picture visualizes the idea it is intended to express, without distraction. The reading matter is supplementary. The /SOTYPE CHART i\$ the masterful combinātion of Isotype pictures and fext. Each Isotype chart teaches a lesson. The complete set is the story of tuberculosis in visual language.

Visualized Facts Prepared for

The National Tuberculosis Association

by the International Foundation for Visual Education

Sixteen original lithographed charts are reproduced here (ic 7.3/2–28). The remainder of the twenty are reproduced from the T files. The printed numbers on the original charts do not correspond strictly to the sequence given in the accompanying booklet Teaching tuberculosis through the eye (see p. 348 below), and there are alternative versions of some charts, which are shown here at a reduced size. All charts measure 920 × 610 mm.

## Modern man fights tuberculosis 1942

The booklet accompanying these charts, Modern man fights tuberculosis, stated the following:

'The 17 charts now offered were planned with Dr. Neurath when he last visited the United States in 1939. His staff in the workshop in The Hague set to work immediately upon his return but a few days after the charts were completed the German invasion forced him and his co-workers to flee to England, leaving all their possessions, including our charts, behind. The National Tuberculosis Association had however, received miniature photographs of the charts and with these as guides, together with instructions received from Dr. Neurath, who is now in Oxford, it was possible to reconstruct the charts here. The National Tuberculosis Association credits his creative genius for the attractiveness and educational value of these charts, but at the same time assumes full responsibility for any inadvertent lapses of technic, since the war made it impossible for Dr. Neurath to supervise each step of the reproduction.' (p. 5).

![](images/a4b41600a499af6de7b219c385326a36d96617024561241c9e24827b5cd30321.jpg)

This new year card for 194o proved uncannily prophetic. Of the emissary Isotype boats sent out from The Hague, one shows an elephant: Otto Neurath's personal signature was a cartoon elephant. When German troops reached The Hague in May 1940, Otto Neurath and Marie Reidemeister fled at the last moment, without any possessions, and were luckily able to board a motorized lifeboat, which set off in the direction of Britain. It was picked up by a British destroyer, and they were conveyed to England. They were sent to (separate) prison camps for 'enemy aliens' on the Isle of Man, until it could be established that they posed no threat.

8 The Isotype Institute, Oxford War, welfare, and post-war reconstruction 1941-8

After being released from internment by the British government in February 1941 – and then immediately getting married – Otto and Marie Neurath settled in Oxford, where a small amount of teaching had been arranged for Otto Neurath at the university. They began to work in visual education soon after their release due to the remarkable interest shown in employing their expertise for the British war effort and for explaining social policy. In June 1942 the Isotype Institute was officially established.

Apart from the collaboration with Paul Rotha in making documentary films (see chapter 9), a fruitful partnership developed from early in 1942 with the company Adprint. Established in 1937 by another Viennese émigré, Wolfgang Foges, Adprint was not a publisher but a book packager. The first projects coordinated by Adprint to involve Isotype were two booklets that visualized information about the budding welfare state: Social security and Social insurance. In this way, Isotype reconnected with the issues of social welfare and planning that had been integral to its development in Vienna. Otto and Marie Neurath were entrusted with secret documents in order to carry out this work, demonstrating the confidence shown in them by the British authorities.

Towards the end of the war, as a contribution to promoting mutual understanding between Britain and its principal allies, Adprint produced two series of books – 'America and Britain' and ‘The Soviets and ourselves' – which featured a mixture of text, Isotype charts, and photographs. After the war, the Isotype Institute contributed charts to the 'New Democracy' series of books examining issues of post-war social reconstruction.

In April 1948 the Isotype Institute moved to the offices of Adprint in London. There Marie Neurath continued work on a variety of projects (see chapters 10 and 11).

## Social security 10).4

This booklet summarizes the report by the economist William Beveridge that sowed the seeds for the British welfare state. At the time the booklet was published, Beveridge and his report were out of favour with politicians, despite great public support.

Editions were published in Dutch, French, and Italian, each with the same lsotype charts.

![](images/e250f3c7bf87ab414ded4478860b4c87e7d7a35288311a0b6a9bb2c64306f09c.jpg)

![](images/2ff2dcc14871485ab092ac2e234ba6126ca2a7fe2fc7bd4e9671c35cd262fbaa.jpg)

Some charts in Social security were printed with process screens, in order to produce tints and combinations of the solid blue, red, green and black inks. The screens are rather coarse and, when printed on the rough paper (presumably dictated by wartime restrictions), the results lack the precision of most previous Isotype colour work, which had generally employed solid colours only.

London: George G. Harrap and Co., 1943. 185×124 mm.

SIR RONALD DAVISON

A L'ABRI DU BESOIN

EN GRANDE BRETAGNE

## Social insurance 1944

This booklet outlines government proposals based largely on William Beveridge's recommendations. It was the first published summary of a government White Paper to include diagrams, and was criticized for this reason in parliamentary discussions.

## America and Britain 1943-4

In this series of books, differences in ways of life were examined, with contrasts of geography, climate, population, and government depicted by a mixture of Isotype charts and photographs (not shown here).

Both this series and 'The Soviets and ourselves' (overleaf) were begun during wartime and, while certified to conform with the Book Production War Economy Standard, they were subsidized by the Ministry of Information. This explains the extensive colour printing afforded to the Isotype charts.

The series editor was Philip Sargant Florence, an American economist based in England.

![](images/deda945abd4b29d30e24d985eb011667daae8d306039d70d4e3cf3358f536387.jpg)  
Vol.1. Lella Secor Florence, Only an ocean between. London: Harrap, 1943.

![](images/48f2219657d46689c07ed3ee0df2ff896c585866c2049c9041a756bd6de8a098.jpg)

The charts shown here from Only an ocean between relate to earlier Isotype charts:

chart 6 to Modern man in the making, p.109;

chart 8 to Gesellschaft und Wirtschaft, no. 23 (shown on p. 166 above);

chart 14 to Gesellschaft und Wirtschaft, no. 55 (shown on p.120 above).

## The Soviets and ourselves 1945-7

The editor of this series of books was John Macmurray, but during its preparation Otto Neurath also dealt directly with the head of the Russian division at the Ministry of Information, Peter Smollett. Originally named Hans Peter Smolka, Smollett was also an Austrian émigré, and was later exposed as a Soviet spy (after having received an oBE for services to the British Empire), The author of the second book in the series was historian Christopher Hill, under the pseudonym K. E. Holme. Hill was a Communist and served on the Russian desk at the British Foreign Office during the Second World War. It has been alleged that he used this position to promote Soviet policy. In his text Hill certainly took an idealized view of collectivization and the single-party system.

Series cover size: 228 × 167 mm.

Vol.1. Maurice Lovell, Landsmen and seafarers. London: Harrap, 1945. Cover design by John Heartfield.

# 9 Animated Isotype on film 1941-7

Christopher Burke

It was fortunate for Otto and Marie Neurath, in terms of their professional re-establishment in Britain, that film maker Paul Rotha contacted them soon after they were released from internment in 1941. The timing was propitious for Rotha, too. He was eager to make documentary films for the British Ministry of Information (Mor), where the situation had recently become favourable for him: since summer 1940 its director was Jack Beddington, who had set up the Shell Film Unit (and commissioned Rotha's first film Contact in 1933); Beddington appointed his colleague at Shell, documentarist Arthur Elton, as Moi Film Officer in January 1941. Rotha seized this moment to establish his own production company, Paul Rotha Productions Ltd, and set about implementing the ideas he had for wartime documentaries. He stipulated to the Mor that he would make films concerning the 'social impact [of war] on civilian life'.1

Rotha had a deep scholarly appreciation of film, having written the landmark book The film till now (193o) when he was a mere 22 years old, followed by the study Documentary film (1935). In his first book he grouped films into 13 categories, one of which was 'The cartoon film'. He wrote:

To many writers at the moment, the Disney cartoons are the most witty and satisfying productions of modern cinema. Their chief merit lies in their immediate appeal to any type of audience, simply because they are based on rhythm.

He also praised the silhouette animation films of Lotte Reiniger, and especially the quality of her craft, which her imitators' work lacked.³ Rotha's sensitivity to such qualities is to be expected from a man who had studied theatre costume design at the Slade School of Art in 1924. He recalled later:

1. Rotha cited in David Pearson, 'Speaking for the common man: multi-voice commentary in World of plenty and Land of promise', in Paul Marris (ed.), Paul Rotha, p. 65 His wish was respected and his unit was never asked to make a combat film.

2. Rotha, The film till now: a survey of world cinema, p. 11g. John Grierson recalled that Rotha was interested as a young filmmaker in 'abstract' film and implied that Rotha was involved in the acquisition of rights for the film of Moholy Nagy's Light-space Modulator by the Empire Marketing Board Film Unit during his brief time working there in 1931. However, Rotha later disclaimed responsibility for this. See Elizabeth Sussex, The rise and fall of British documentary (Berkeley: University of California Press, 1975), p. 16.

3. The Neuraths surely knew Reiniger's films, such as The adventures of Prince Achmed (1926; the first feature-length animated film), from which there is a still-image print in the Isotype Collection.

4. Rotha, Documentary diary: an informal history of the British documentary film, 1928–1939 (London: Secker & Warburg, 1973), p. 4. When his money for fees at the Slade ran out, Rotha made 'a kind of living' with 'commercial hackwork' in illustrating and designing posters, menus, and book-jackets.

5. In the full text of his obituary for Neurath, Rotha made the following point about Isotype: 'Imitations of the technique in America and England have been many, but none has mastered the fundamental scientific principles underlying the Isotype method.' (Paragraph not included in the version published in The Times (9 January 1946); typescript 'Dr Otto Neurath. An appreciation', p. 3; IC 1/45a.) Rotha would certainly have seen the animated statistical charts included in the landmark documentary Enough to eat: the nutrition film (1936), which bear some superficial similarities to Isotype but contravene its principles by conveying increase in quantity by increase in size of picture or pictogram.

## 6. Letter Rotha to Neurath, 7 April 1941 (IC 1/42).

7. This was the subtitle of his book Documentary film (1935). Note the remarkable similarity of Neurath's opening sentence from his first essay about the Gesellschafts- und Wirtschaftsmuseum

It was at the Slade that there arose my first and everlasting interest in typography. I find as much aesthetic as well as functional delight in [Eric] Gill's Perpetua as I do in a [Augustus] John drawing. I suppose it is called graphics. It has a lot to do with the cinema.

This helps to explain how Rotha knew of Isotype work, which he could no doubt distinguish as possessing qualities lacking in some other graphics.⁵ This was reflected in his initial letter to Otto Neurath:

Mr. Ritchie Calder, the Science Editor of the “Daily Herald", tells me that you are at present in England. I am anxious to get in touch with vou to see if you would be interested in assisting my Company with some pictorial diagrammatic work in connection with a scientific film that we are making on the history of blood transfusion. I have in mind the using of the cinema screen for animated symbolic explanation of the constitution of the blood and the description of the blood groups according to the discovery of Professor Landsteiner (19oo).

Having admired your work in pictorial symbolism for many years I feel that there is no-one more qualified to help us in this matter than yourself. If you are free to undertake such work, I should be glad if you would get in touch with me to discuss the matter.

Rotha had seen Modern man in the making and so would have appreciated that Neurath's ideas on society and economy were broadly in line with his own. Both men were primarily concerned with education for citizenship. Rotha's view of documentary film reveals a striking parallel with Isotype: he defined it as ‘the use of the film medium to interpret creatively and in social terms the life of the people as it exists in reality'.?

The Neuraths had some experience already in making animated films – indeed their first efforts at the Gesellschafts- und Wirtschaftsmuseum in Wien were contemporary with Disney's Mickey Mouse cartoons of the late 192os, which Rotha esteemed so highly. No copies of the Vienna Museum films survive, and there is only one archival document relating to their preparation – a mysterious diagram.8 There was a public showing of these early films at the Russell Sage Foundation in New York in January 1933, and subsequent press reports give an

8. A chart published in 1928 (T5c, p. 127 above) states that it was based on an ‘animated film' [Zeichenfilm] of the Museum. It him and Neurath was that Rotha's father was a museum curator, as Neurath had been. Rotha's father William Thompson was Clerk of the Museum at the Royal College of Surgeons, London, between 1928 and 1957.

![](images/1a7e29a7714de78d1f013dea9e44912f89163138ba822b5f296196d73b7e9026.jpg)

A sketch kept in a file of the Gesellschaftsund Wirtschaftsmuseum entitled 'Trickfilm: Gesellschaftsentwicklung' (Animated film: social development). c. 1929 (IC 3.1/15).

The purpose of this chart is unclear: perhaps it is a schematic diagram of movement and rhythm in a film. (A similar chart was made for the later film The endocrinology of the menstrual cycle; see p.387 below.)

indication of the action in them. The New York Times reported 'movies in which symbolic trains of cars dumped imaginary coal into animated charts', and the New York Herald Tribune published the following description:

Instead of having these facts written out in dull and lustreless fashion, Dr. Neurath shows a motión picture. The scene is a dining room with a long table. A queue of little boys and girls file into the room. Each child represents 1oo children. They sit down at the table, and an object of food – appearing to be a gob of mashed potatoes or rice or grits – miraculously flies through the air and is deposited on each child's plate. Over the table is suspended the sign 1914. There is a black-out, then the same scene with the sign 1919. A longer line of children files in and the same things happens. The audience is bound to remember the scene – and the facts are more likely to remain imprinted in the memory than if the statistics had merely been read from a book.9

It is clear that these early films – presumably made in-house at the Vienna Museum - featured movement while maintaining the principles of Isotype, a pattern that Otto and Marie Neurath would follow in their

9. 'Picture writing is suggested as aid to student', New York Herald Tribune, 22 January 1933, pt. 2, p. 9. Marie Neurath recalled this film vividly, and clarified that the food flying through the air was 'Knoedel' – dumplings. ('Isotype in films'.)

10. Letter Neurath to Foges, 17 May 1942 (emphasis in original; IC 1/43). The suggestion related to an article for the projected periodical Future Books about airways, and the Disney film in question was probably Victory through air power. Film historian Richard Schickel noted that this film, produced hurriedly, was a rare instance of Disney using 'limited animation', in The Disney version (New York: Simon & Schuster, 1968), pp. 201, 274.

11. Letter Neurath to Rotha, 25 October 1942 (iC 1/43). Rotha concurred with Neurath: 'Disney has no scientific mind or understanding of the diagram technique for education.' Letter Rotha to Neurath, 8 October 1943 (ic 1/43).

12. In a footnote in the second edition of The film till now (1948) Rotha lamented: 'The subsequent development of Disney is, in my opinion, one of the greatest disappointments in the cinema. In the typical Hollywood manner, he has appeared to concentrate on the elaborate development of the mechanics of his technique and paid less and less attention to the fundamental creative ideas underlying his work.' (p. 12o.)

13. They also designed an animated 'trailer' titled Scottish national savings (1943) for the MOI, produced by Byron Films.

14. Letter R. Nunn May to Rotha, 31 May 1941 (IC 1/42). Around the same time Julian Huxley recommended Neurath to another division of the Moi. Neurath had been in touch with Huxley since 1936, when he invited him to the Unity of Science congress in Copenhagen. Huxley was an important figure in the British documentary film movement, having been the first scientist to present a film on screen (Enough to eat, 1936). Timothy Boon calls him 'one of the most prolific public scientists of the mid-twentieth century' (Films of fact, p. 83).

15. Letter Neurath to Rotha,   
18 August 1942 (IC 1/43).

collaboration with Rotha. As such, their films were of a quite different kind to Disney's cartoons, which rejoiced in exploiting the fantasy and elasticity allowed by animated drawings. Neurath reflected on this difference in a letter to his and Rotha's mutual colleague, Wolfgang Foges, when it was suggested that Isotype charts be printed alongside pictures from a Disney film:

This mixture of Disney photos and our own drawings is unbearable – either – or. The reason is, that his maps and other drawings in principle do not intend, what we intend to do, i.e. giving some educational information.10

Neurath explained the point in similar terms to Rotha:

I maintain again: we see in Isotype a complicated method of visually arguing, a kind of visual language. The point is not (for us) to make new inventions for fun only, or as expression of an author's ideas (we admire this kind of work very much, indeed) but as part of education."1

Such a view would have struck a chord with Rotha, who saw the purpose of his own documentary films as primarily to educate not to entertain, in contrast to the customary output of Hollywood.¹²

## A few ounces a day

Work on the film about blood transfusion was soon overtaken by an urgent project to make a film about salvage for the Moi, in order to convince British householders not to waste material that could be useful for the war effort. The production of this film, which became A few ounces a day, is worth documenting in some detail, as it is the only surviving film that consists entirely of animation designed by the Neuraths for Rotha.¹³ In order for them to work on this project it was necessary that they first be cleared by the Ministry of Labour, due to their status as registered aliens. Rotha received a letter from the Moı Films division stating: 'Naturally your request has the support of this Ministry. It is regarded as important that the skilled services of Dr. and Mrs. Neurath should be obtained for this film.'⁴ Apparently there were no lingering official doubts about the loyalty of the Neuraths to Britain. Neurath was grateful to Rotha for the confidence shown in them: 'Imagine that we as Anti-Nazis are very glad to have an opportunity to support the war effort, within our own field. You represent your country's friendliness to us.'1s

The salvage film was discussed and outlined in a remarkably short time: after a meeting at the Moi attended by Rotha and Neurath at the end of April 1941, Rotha's scriptwriter Wolfgang Wilhelm visited the

![](images/e14b42fac21b6a840eeb5558f58460234308943d495e0944a56098f51cbb792c.jpg)

Neuraths' office in Oxford on 1 May. The document arising from this meeting outlined a film provisionally titled Four ounces a day', which would show how the public could help to fill the gap in materials lost when supply ships were destroyed in the Atlantic. Here it was intended that the film would have introductory and concluding live-action sequences, but that idea was eventually dropped.1⁶ The title of the film was changed due to an objection from the Ministry of Supply that four ounces a day was an impossible amount for people to save, suggesting instead two or three ounces. This led to the compromise ‘a few ounces', although Neurath regretted the change because 'the effect of Isotype pictures depends, to a certain extent, upon stressed quantitative facts'.¹7

In Rotha's opinion, Disney's greatest achievement was the clever use of synchronized sound in his animated films. Planned interaction of sound and image was also a feature of Rotha's film-making, and Marie

16. The formal commission from Rotha (3 June 1941) detailed a total payment to the Neuraths of £250. This was revised on 24 July to be £5oo for two versions of the film (1C 1/42).

17. Letter Neurath to Rotha, 4 June 1941 (IC 1/42). An alternative, provisional title of A few ounces a day was 'You fill the gap'. A different animated film with the title Filling the gap was made by Halas & Batchelor for the Moi in 1942 to encourage domestic food production. It featured music by Ernst Meyer, who also composed the soundtrack for A few ounces a day.

![](images/1aeaaa8c244832863dd2db5d689d6062505b31f5ae9fd01ccb00f501484a22d8.jpg)

18. When discussing the motion of a parrot for Scottish national savings, Neurath stipulated that it should move 'Isotypically', not naturalistically. (Letter Neurath to Rotha, 27 April 1943; IC 1/44.)

19. Saraga (19o8–8o) had been a lecturer at the Heinrich-Hertz-Institut of the Technische Hochschule Berlin. There he built a lightwave instrument: when the light projected by the machine was interrupted at a certain point it altered the pitch of the sound produced. He emigrated to Britain in 1938. He explained his proposed instrument for lsotype films as consisting of 'a rotating or oscillating spot of light which is produced by a similarly moved beam of cathode rays in a cathode ray oscillograph'. Letter Saraga to Neurath, 17 June 1941 (IC 1/42).

Neurath prepared an instructive scenario for A few ounces a day featuring correlated descriptions of action and sound in two columns. Rotha's appreciation of the importance of sound in film made him receptive to a fanciful scheme to include electronic sounds, which Otto Neurath thought would be the most fitting accompaniment to Isotype imagery. This indicates that Neurath considered Isotype as an artificial construct, which – while its duty was to reflect reality – was not a naturalistic exercise.18 During his internment on the Isle of Man, Neurath had met a German scientist named Wolja Saraga, who was a pioneer of electronic sound-making. This idea fascinated Neurath and he proposed to Rotha that Saraga contribute to the film.19 But, in the short time available, it was not possible to track down a specific kind of battery needed to power the apparatus which Saraga had in mind. There was no music in the film, and the final sound effects were credited to composer Ernst Meyer.

![](images/2429c7da2c73c008671a155e2d9699b15419b131fea0b642e9b57abb33010d9a.jpg)

First and last pages from the script of the commentary for A few ounces a day. 1941 (ic 3.2/267). The amendments are in Otto Neurath's expansive handwriting. The film's provisional title 'You fill the gap' is crossed out and overwritten. On the final page Neurath deleted the last three stanzas and replaced them with the phrases 'Fill up the gap / A few ounces a day', repeated in alternating order as a memorable chant.

At the suggestion of the Ministry of Information, a commentary in doggerel verse was written for A few ounces a day by Rotha's colleague Donald Alexander. Otto Neurath made a significant contribution to the script of this commentary, which is made up of short, explanatory phrases and delivered in an insistent rhythm by actor Henry Hallatt. Even on the page it brings to mind the famous, rhythmic commentary written by W. H. Auden and set to Benjamin Britten's music for the GPo documentary, Night mail (1936).2º Neurath also suggested an introductory sequence in which the Isotype 'characters' (pictograms) were presented and identified by the commentary; Rotha liked the idea and it was effectively implemented.

20. Rotha's friend and collaborator Eric Knight expressed doubts about such an approach. On seeing Night mail, he wrote to Rotha: 'I am utterly convinced that poetry - and most especially rhythm poetry – has no place in the spoken field of films. I do not know why yet; I can only guess at the reasons and tell you. The poetry of a film lies in its visuals. When it is also placed in the aural, we get an over-effect that goes beyond its goal and reaches over into the comic. It was a laudable try with Auden yet not a success. But it is fine that such experiments should be made.' (4 November 1937; cited in Rotha's Documentary diary, p.181.)

![](images/4ad0438e2caa5b8e009e7db06b838f2641f145e3ad1f7394d165b5fe415aab22.jpg)  
Sketches by Marie Neurath for scenes in A few ounces a day. 1941 (IC 3.2/267).

![](images/bc72fe6cd2f1f2c2eaf004f0c41eef7e7f5b6f1c12e306c034bf37a0a723d5b9.jpg)  
Page from the first draft of Marie Neurath's storyboard for A few ounces a day, dated 15 June 1941.

![](images/fef53bd51b497beb9e9ce85ae7445ed1b054da13eca62d01ab9e10a19430d554.jpg)  
First two pages from a second draft of the storyboard for A few ounces a day, dated 3o June 1941.

In a sense the Neuraths were the directors of A few ounces a day, given that they determined the mise-en-scène when designing it. The film bears no director's credit, stating instead that it was 'designed by Isotype Institute'.2¹ Rotha saw film-making as a task of planned montage. He considered 'preconception of dramatic content by scenario organisation' to be essential, and his films were meticulously scripted, especially those featuring a mixture of dialogue, voice-over, stock footage and Isotype animation. This approach to planning was already familiar in Isotype due to the central role of the transformer. Moreover, Rotha recognized the importance of drawings in making a screenplay more instructive.²² For A few ounces a day Marie Neurath prepared a complete storyboard of the film, which must have been an essential guide for the animation studio, along with sample pictograms and other visual material that were prepared at the Isotype office.23

Rotha was under no illusions about the time and expense involved in making even short animation films. In The film till now he had recognized that the laborious draughtsmanship, necessarily entailed in the production of the thousands of small drawings needed as material for smooth movement, has naturally limited the output of these animated cartoons'.24 The Neuraths, then in the process of reconstructing a small design office in Oxford, did not have the technical means to animate their drawings on film. Rotha suggested initially that animation could be carried out at the Technicolor film laboratory, which had the necessary equipment, but the task of animating A few ounces a day was instead given to Science Films, based in in Letchworth Garden City, about 35 miles north of London. This company worked mainly for the Air Ministry on training and instructional films for the Royal Air Force. A team of three animators there led by Norman MacQueen worked on transferring Marie Neurath's sketches and artwork to celluloid.²⁵

A few ounces a day was completed and showing in cinemas by October 1941. It seems to have been generally well received. Neurath observed that 'the public was apparently satisfied, laughing and applauding, particularly the soldiers'. He added: 'In one cinema I heard my neighbour, a woman in uniform, saying "awfully clever these thing[s]".,2 Rotha reported: 'The film has had great applause at all London cinemas where I have been.'²7 It seems fitting that a report on audience reaction to A few ounces a day was prepared by Mass Observation, the social research organization, which shared methodological aspects with both Isotype and the British documentary movement (see overleaf). Responses were gathered by similar methods to those pioneered with visitors to the

![](images/9f51548b757746101bb7e3f4b5d0da4fe6925e2341cfac921efd608be5d0c312.jpg)

![](images/c9dc9ce84769f52a598c78e3f175eaaa34abc9f9224af051474a57e98786f3a8.jpg)

![](images/904a235be4542c2c3d184bf63a914c18be5631e6fc5b7c0271331078b72144da.jpg)  
Stills from A few ounces a day, 1941.

21. This was a wishful statement because the Isotype Institute was not officially registered until June 1942.

22. 'It is when the literary medium fails that the scenarist should be helped to a clear expression of his ideas by the draughtsmanship of the designer. When in the studio, the director should be able to work from drawings as well as from words in the realisation of the theme.' The film till now, p. 349.

23. Storyboards, which are now customarily made for Hollywood blockbusters, are generally thought to have been first developed for Disney animation films in the early 1930s.

24. The film till now, pp. 118–19.

25. Rotha also valued the animated diagrams made at the Shell Film Unit by Francis Rodker, In an article titled 'Films for science' (in Discovery, April 1944) Rotha showed three stills of Rodker's animation, describing it as 'brilliant work'.

26. Letters: Neurath to Rotha, 12 November 1941 (IC 1/42); Neurath to H. E. Kleinschmidt, 26 November 1941 (IC 1/46).

27. Letter Rotha to Neurath,   
20 October 1941 (Ic 1/42).

28. Indeed Neurath retrospectively applied the term 'mass observation' to the recording of visitor reactions carried out at the Gesellschafts- und Wirtschaftsmuseum ('Isotype plan' written for Julian Huxley in December 1942; IC 1/35). Later, Rotha reported to Neurath that Christian crosses symbolizing death in the charts for the film Blood transfusion cause laughter in the audience. Neurath replied: 'Mass Observation should find out what it is that offends the British and created laughter.' Letters between Rotha & Neurath 23 & 24 September 1942 (IC 1/43).

29. 'Report on short diagrammatic film from Mass-Observation', M-0 25.11.41 (IC 1/42).

# Report on

# SHORT DIAGRAMATIC FIIM

## I. SCOFL

A limited investigation wes mede of the impaot of the film, "A Few Ounoes a Day", made by the Paul Rotha unit as an M.o.I. five-ninute short. This film is entirely diagramatio, end therefore unlike the run of MoI produots.

Observations were made in a wide range of oinomas all over North and Central London. supplemented by observations in'Watford, Letohworth, Ruislip and Birningham.

## II. GENERAL EFFECT OF THE FILM

The generel effect of the film wes to erouse exoeptional impression and interest.

220 Londeners were questioned about the film -- 40% of a random sample had seen it and remembered it in the first week in November.

The vast majority of this sample approved of the film primerily beoause of its novelty. Nine persons out of ten who had seen the film thought it was good; one person in ten thinking it bad or having no opinion. This is an exoeptionally high degree of opinion of any short film - indeed only l% of the sample had no opinion, whereas in any ordinery film at least l0% would have no opinion on en investigation conducted on these methods in these areas.

## III.、UNFAVOURABLE COMMENTS

Women were slightly more oritioel of the film than men. Criticim was mainly beceuse some people thought that the oartoon teohnique was unsuitable to to a serious subjeots

Some thought it was an imitation of Walt Disney and resented it for that reason, others just thought it "silly"t

Gesellschafts- und Wirtschaftsmuseum in Vienna.28 The report stated that the ‘general effect of the film was to arouse exceptional impression and interest': ninety per cent of viewers thought it was good, mostly due to its novelty and humour, while ten per cent thought that the animated technique was unsuitable to a serious subject; the report noted that 'the frequency with which Disney parallels were drawn even among those in favour, was striking'. The film generated much laughter (more than was intended perhaps) and it was remarked in the report: The amount of laughter in some cinemas (e.g. Letchworth, Watford) was high, and when it reaches such an extent there is some danger that it will defeat itself.'29

## Animated inserts for documentary films

Otto Neurath was pleased with the result of A few ounces a day, but problems arose during the production of further animated sequences that were to be cut together with live-action sequences in subsequent Rotha films. At first glance these would seem to have been simpler to animate than A few ounces a day, but they provided continual difficulties due to the division of labour between conception, design, and execution. For A few ounces a day, Neurath remarked, 'we composed the whole story and our layouts were exactly executed by the animating firm'.³º After completion of the next film, Blood transfusion (1942), Neurath complained to Rotha that 'some points in the blood transfusion animation [were] made by the animator and not by us, which directly destroy our Isotype method'. He continued:

Please believe me, we make these remarks not as pedants, but knowing the audience. You see, subconsciously they feel the difference. ... since we have to bear the responsibility that Isotype diagrams are really made by us, please show us, without exception the diagrams before they go to distribution, we shall come immediately to London. You see we are very interested on our prestige, and of course, too that a Rotha production with Isotypes should be the best diagram animation that we can make.³¹

Yet the difficulties continued and Neurath was forced to reiterate: 'Let me repeat our suggestion that Science Film[s] should change not even a bit of our designs without our consent.'³²Marie Neurath recalled later: For this film work we provided charts for the key positions and gave verbal instructions for how the transitions were to be made.'³3 They had no effective means of proofing the animation themselves and were sometimes unpleasantly surprised by the results. As Neurath remarked: 'The triability of designs is limited on paper.'³⁴ Rotha explained that animation was only a sideline for Science Films; they accepted the work with 'bad grace' and took too long to do it.³⁵ Some of the staff from Science Films moved to London in late 1943, which at least made it easier for the Neuraths to combine visits to Rotha and the animators.³⁶

Neurath often proposed new ideas for film work to Rotha. One such suggestion was for Isotype 'postscripts' – short animated films, each on a single theme, with which the Moi could communicate important statements' through the popular medium of film.37 This idea fed into a 'Cine magazine' that the Mor commissioned from Rotha Productions,
