23 May 1942 (IC 1/43).   
32. Letter Neurath to Rotha,   
16 July 1942 (IC 1/43).

33. Marie Neurath & Robin Kinross, The transformer, p. 62.

34. Letter Neurath to Rotha,   
27 April 1943 (IC 1/44).   
35. Letter Rotha to Neurath,   
23 September 1942 (IC 1/43).

36. During the war the Neuraths had to have a special permit to stay in London; otherwise they had to return to Oxford in time for curfew each day.

37. Letter Neurath to Rotha,   
12 November 1941 (IC 1/42).

38. No.1 featured two segments with Isotype: 'Science vs. sand: workshop device aids British desert fighting', showing the principle of filters for engines used in desert warfare, and 'Good news for spiders! Special webs spun for big guns', showing the remarkable use of spiders to spin thread for manufacturing gun-sight crosshairs; no. 5 featured 'Ancient craft joins war-front' showing the technique of charcoal production; no.7 included 'Broken barriers: lease-lend on British farms', showing centralized distribution of farm labour and equipment. Some other episodes featured animated diagrams not executed by Isotype: no.8 contained an amusing and effective warning against sepsis in factory workers, animated from drawings by cartoonist Giles; and no. 9 included a diagrammatic animation of drilling diamonds, similar to Isotype but less schematic.

39. It was claimed that 18.5 million people were reached in this way from 1943 to 1944. See Sussex, The rise and fall of British documentary, p. 160.

which became the documentary series Worker and warfront (18 issues between 1942 and 1946). Three of these short films featured Isotype sequences with effective visual explanations of production processes geared to the war effort.³8 Worker and warfront films were not shown in cinemas but in factories; the Moi created an effective, non-theatrical system of film distribution.³º Given the intended audience, Rotha urged Neurath to simplify the Isotype charts: 'You should remember that our audience is mostly factory workers snatching a quick meal in the canteen break. They haven't time to concentrate on puzzling out diagrams unless the latter are immediately clear.'⁴º

Both Neurath and Rotha wanted to gain more control over the animation process, and they began to discuss the possibility of acquiring the necessary apparatus from an early point in their collaboration. At the beginning of 1942 Neurath was already thinking of ways to bypass the necessity for traditional animation, so that artwork from the Isotype Institute could be photographed directly onto film without the necessity of being redrawn by a third party.⁴¹ He proposed that movement could perhaps be achieved with magnets, and admitted that motion was not integral to Isotype:

You see the real animation' is, as far as we can see, undispensable [sic] only there, where the outlines have to be changed, as being ‘in motion', but in our case, mostly permanent bodies are in motion.⁴²

The Isotype principle of avoiding arbitrary changes in form was not to be overridden by the possibilities of motion. Neurath was not convinced that movement in itself was always an educational advantage: sometimes he complained that the Isotype action was too quick and asked the animators to hold the image of a pictogram still for a brief moment before it moved, in order to introduce it to the audience. 'Isotype should avoid vague and "sliding" shades', he declared, in resistance to an inherent potential of movies.⁴³

41. Neurath approached his cousin Gustav, who managed an electroplating factory in Birmingham (uκ), about making an animation table. He also enquired if it was possible to produce multiple copies of Isotype pictograms as thin metal plates: 'Until now symbols in motion had to be drawn again and again – if we had such thin plates we could move these plates and take them by camera.' Letter Otto

42. Letter Neurath to Rotha, 1 January 1942 (iC 1/42). What Neurath gives here is a definition of 'limited animation', which is what almost all Isotype animation is. It is not clear that the technique of cel animation (progressive drawings on acetate overlaid on a static background) was always used for Isotype animation; it is likely that paper cut-outs were also used. See the letter from J. D. Griggs

43. Letter Neurath to Rotha, 4 June 1942 (IC 1/43). On screening the first quarter of À few ounces a day, before the Neuraths had seen it, Rotha had also judged the movement to be too fast. (Letter Rotha to Neurath, 11 August 1941; IC 1/42.)

Neurath was dissatisfied with the 'primitiveness' of the animation technique: he wanted more automation in it, not so much hand designing, more photographic business.'⁴ Rotha told him: 'One answer is to build our animation table and that we are going to do. But there will be another problem here; having got our table, who is going to operate it?'⁴⁵ He feared that no specialist could be found during wartime, and that it would be necessary to train somebody first for several months. In the meantime Rotha engaged an assistant, Peter Bradford, to act as liaison between Isotype and the animators. (Rotha initially filled this role himself, often in his own time outside of normal working hours.) Bradford had studied at the Reimann School, an offshoot of a German applied art academy established in London by émigrés, where he learned about photography, lettering and layout. During the Second World War he set up a photographic unit in Oxford for the British Admiralty, producing charts and maps for naval intelligence. He visited the Isotype Institute to advise on photographic matters before his appointment by Rotha to work on animated films.4 Neurath welcomed Bradford's photographic expertise, although he worried that another link in the chain of communication would further complicate the production of Isotype animation. 47

Bradford recalls his involvement with animating Isotype:

I realized, when I met Otto Neurath in Oxford, that he was working with a film company and he was having very great difficulties in being able to simplify his charts so they were really suitable for Rotha and for film. And Otto Neurath asked me to do various experiments with the movement of still photographs. ... Actual movement of symbols, and the isolation of some and the movement of another, and so on, to fit a 48 commentary – well, it was pioneering, really, at the time.

One of the first films for which Bradford mediated was Total war in Britain, based on the government report Statistics relating to the war effort of the United Kingdom. The film includes some maps of Europe featuring animated aircraft, swastikas, and arrows to indicate German invasion, which may have inspired the similar animated title sequence of the

46. Bradford was teaching parttime at Oxford School of Art, and a that Neurath could have been instrumental in his employment by Rotha; another connection was the architect Basil Ward, who was a good friend of Rotha and who stumbled across Bradford working late one night in Oxford. (Interview with Peter Bradford, September 2011.)

47. Neurath suggested that Bradford spend half of his time at

48. Bradford interviewed in 'Close-up: recollections of British documentary'.

![](images/aabc4088ef0f778c64e92874c19b0dc1f1c6bcc65f0633c849a40e566a5629c6.jpg)  
Sketches prepared by Peter Bradford for animated sequences in Total war in Britain. 1945 (iC 3.2/271).

beloved BBC comedy Dad's army.4 Total war in Britain and Land of promise were the first two films produced by Rotha under a new company name, Films of Fact. The animated sequences in them were executed by a unit called Diagram Films and perhaps due to this change, as well as to the assistance of Peter Bradford, there is a marked improvement in the quality of animation compared to the previous work done by Science Films.

Three years into the collaboration with Rotha, Neurath continued to seek a more permanent basis for it. Rotha was forced to remind him of the difficulties he faced:

49. The closing credits of Dad's army were an homage to those of Carol Reed's wartime film The way ahead, and so it is possible that Rotha's film similarly served to inspire the opening credits. Total war in Britain was shown on the BBC in 1954 for the tenth anniversary of D-day and an appeal was made to anybody who recognized themselves in the film to make contact.

50. Letter Rotha to Neurath, 13 April 1944 (IC 1/44). Between 1941 and 1945 Paul Rotha Productions and Films of Fact made 25 fitms for the Moi, plus many espisodes of two series, Worker and warfront and Britain can make it. See the filmography in Marris (ed.), Paul Rotha.

51. Letter Rotha to Neurath,   
25 April 1944 (IC 1/44).

Please remember also that we are obliged to put our services at the disposal of the Mor Films Division for 99% of our time – and that the Films division do not always share my enthusiasm for Isotype. ... You must always remember that I – and all this Unit – are only deferred from military service on three month's periods because we are making MOI films.' [sic]5º

I think perhaps you don't understand how our activities are controlled by the Films Division of the moı, and that I am by no means free to use Isotypes as and when I please in Ministry films.ª

Rotha explained that his battles with the moı to include Isotype were 'long and bitter'. He struggled to persuade them that Isotype needed a design fee separate from the fee for animation: ‘This in itself was difficult because in all other diagrams in Mor films, the cost of animation includes cost of designing.' Rotha claimed that on every film he produced with Isotypes, his company lost money because the animated sequences eventually worked out more expensive than foreseen in the script. He concluded, with mixed feelings: 'Rotha has been pleased and proud to use exclusively Isotype in film. Isotype has not reduced its fees at all.' ⁵2

Rotha dealt patiently with such comments from Neurath as: 'We have real objections to making within the same film Isotype and anti-Isotype symbolism.'⁵³ This attitude became relevant when maps were to be included alongside Isotype; while Neurath claimed to be no expert, he had strong views on map projections. There was much tense discussion on the matter with regard to an abortive film about the usA. Rotha took the opportunity to set up a central committee to oversee maps in official films; he commented to Neurath:

I have been trying to bring this united action into being for a long time and have only just succeeded. I hope that it may mean a wide recognition of Isotype and its principles. We cannot expect, nor indeed perhaps should we, to get a common style accepted among the several units making maps in films, but I do think we can get accepted certain common principles.⁵⁴

Rotha exaggerated Neurath's expertise on maps to the committee, and when Neurath corrected him, Rotha replied: 'I have absorbed so much regarding the works and ideas of Dr. Neurath, and have so much respect for them, that I got some facts a little muddled.'s⁵

## World of plenty and Land of promise

The two most significant films directed by Paul Rotha and featuring Isotype animation were World of plenty (1943) and Land of promise (1945), the first two parts of a loose trilogy about planning for wartime and its aftermath (the third film was The world is rich, 1947). In these films Rotha originated a distinctive, dialectical style based on a tight combination of stock footage, interviews, animated diagrams and multi-voice commentary.⁵⁶

World of plenty – known as the strategy of food' film until a late stage of production⁵7– was mainly inspired by the views of Scottish nutritionist John Boyd Orr, who appeared in the film directly addressing the audience in extreme close-up. Yet there was a group of like-minded collaborators involved in writing and making the film, including Eric Knight and Otto Neurath.58 In his book Fighting for what? (1942), Boyd Orr stressed the 'primary material necessities of life' - food and shelter - as Neurath had done previously.⁵9 In a letter to Knight, Rotha wrote:

52. Letter Rotha to Neurath, 10 February 45 (IC 1/45). In reply, Neurath appreciated Rotha's 'conviction that Isotype is something good and extraordinary' and recognized that the Isotype Institute owed a lot to him. (Neurath to Rotha, 20 February 1945; IC 1/45.) A lack of appreciation for the Isotype Institute's role in designing animation was also shown by the Pilot Press, which published without permission images of its work for World of plenty in the book by John Boyd Orr, Food and the people ('Target for tomorrow', no. 3, 1943). The stills had been provided directly by the Mol, which considered that it owned rights to them given that it had paid for the production. Yet Neurath was annoyed and Rotha complained on his behalf. The Pilot Press apologized but then repeated the offence in another publication.

53. Letter Neurath to Rotha,   
24 September 1942 (IC 1/43).   
54. Letter Rotha to Neurath,   
1 July 1943 (IC 1/44).   
55. Letter Rotha to Neurath,   
8 July 1943 (IC 1/44).

56. See David Pearson's wellresearched analysis of these films in Marris (ed.), Paul Rotha, and Basil Wright's comments in the same publication.

57. The final title was given by the film's composer, William Alwyn. (Rotha, Documentary diary, p. 284.)

58. Knight (an Englishman who settled in the usA) created the canine character Lassie. He was killed in a plane crash while serving in the us Army in 1943 (the year in which his novel Lassie come home was made into a film).

59. 'World planning & the USA' (1932), p. 621.

Stills from animated sequences in World of plenty. 1942–3.

(Below left) Distribution of food production.

(Below right) Effect of improved diet on average height.

![](images/4929d66727e9783968ae12b4dd9d820c0e435689669c5c49b0fd3a5b7b4b9460.jpg)

![](images/06d416d46f89c3bc7887dc01e368da81a28dfb9a8b3178e42b7b12d76de17cdf.jpg)

![](images/a83a104d2ff90ee3d0cc3fe55d10136d323b35438364a61f22468d3731c76431.jpg)

The great thing about Neurath is that he is a brilliant economist first and a diagram king second. The more I talk with him, the more I am impressed with the man's brain. His ideas on planning are sane and inspiring. He has been at all of this for twenty years and, in his own field, is as brilliant as Orr and the others.6º

60. Letter Rotha to Eric Knight, 6 May 1942; cited in Boon, Films of fact, p.136.

61. Neurath received the consultant's fee between May 1942 and March 1943. After this Rotha asked Neurath to continue in this capacity, for which he would be paid on a case-by-case basis. (Letter Rotha to Neurath, 6 March 1943; IC 1/44.)

62. See International picture language (1936), p. 73.

Neurath's status as a consultant on World of plenty was reflected by a monthly fee of £25 paid by Paul Rotha Productions to the Isotype Institute during the film's production, separate from any fees for carrying out animation design work.6¹ It is possible that Neurath contributed to naming one of the six voices on the soundtrack the Man-in-the-street', a catchphrase he often used when writing about Isotype.6² This character (spoken by Eric Knight in a mid-Atlantic accent) consistently doubts the conventional wisdom voiced by an authoritative commentator (E. V. H. Emmett of Gaumont British newsreels).

This dialectical approach extended to presenting the first Isotype charts in World of plenty ironically, and even to questioning the reliability of animated statistical graphics. After a sequence of Isotypes correlating world population with national food production and international exports, the voice of authority states: 'National food needs thus formed one of the basic elements for strong flow of healthy world trade.' The Man-in-the-street replies: 'Healthy world trade! Yeah, you can prove anything with diagrams. Give me a half hour with Walt Disney and I could pay my income tax and never feel it.' This echoes comments from the Mass Observation report about A few ounces a day, in which some viewers considered that a serious subject matter should not be treated with animation due to its popular association with Disney. It was quite unconventional for a documentary film to playfully undermine its own authority in the way that World of plenty does. Neurath's opinion of Isotype being linked with Disney and having its authority questioned is not recorded; it is unlikely to have been a suggestion of his.“3

![](images/990f41a1b19bf6c09819cab3f9d48ae1da6709f1a56a685cd332086680e94801.jpg)  
Cover and pages of World of plenty: the book of the film (London: Nicholson & Watson, 1945). 227 × 147 mm.  
Animated charts from the film were adapted here for printing in colour (red, yellow, and green), and combined with the script and photographic stills.  
The dialogue shown on the right-hand page above contains some of the sceptical remarks about animated statistical graphics made by the character 'Man-in the-street'. The voice of authority, with whom he disputes, is here named 'Newsreel'.

World of plenty was completed in early 1943 and it generated some controversy in the British government, delaying its release for six months. Rotha explained to Neurath that an Isotype chart showing global wheat surplus and deficit had to be removed from the film because the Ministry of Agriculture feared that it was contrary to the terms of the International Wheat Agreement.4 The film reached a wide audience through theatrical distribution by Paramount (unusual for a

63. It is much more likely to have been Knight's idea, as co-scriptwriter. 64. Letter Rotha to Neurath 13 May 1943 (IC 1/44).

65. In 1943 typographer Anthony Froshaug wrote an appreciative review of World of plenty, republished in Kinross (ed.), Anthony Froshaug: typography & texts (London: Hyphen Press, 2ooo), pp. go-2. In summer of that same year Froshaug approached the Isotype Institute (via Rotha Films), with a request to collaborate on film animation. No work could be found for him at that time.

66. Letters: Neurath to Rotha, 11 January 1943; Rotha to Neurath, 1 July 1943 (iC 1/44). The title desigr of Land of promise was made by graphic designer, Abram Games, based on his posters 'Your Britain: fight for it now' (1942), made for the Army Bureau of Current Affairs. Games occasionally employed techniques reminiscent of Isotype (inside his cover for the HMso booklet Roof over Britain [1943], for example) and he designed covers for Pilot Press books, some of which featured pseudo-Isotype charts (not by Games).

67. See Neurath, 'Visual aids and arguing' (1944).

68. Saraga suggested using the only Vocoder then operating in Britain. (Letter Neurath to Rotha, 8 November 1944; IC 1/44.) In an early draft of the script 'Isotype' is listed among the 'characters and voices' with a role described as: Explains, where necessary, his symbol language which makes arguments so clear.' (ic 3.2/270)

69. An early draft of the script has the following for Isotype's first utterance: ""Isotype", that's me. "Isotype", I talk in symbols. You'll understand: I'm quite entertaining really.' (iC 3.2/270) This implies a perceived need to overcome an impression that animated statistics were boring or difficult to understand for cinemagoers.

government-sponsored production) combined with 13o prints circulating on the MOI exhibition circuit.6⁵

Soon after the completion of World of plenty Neurath began consultation with Rotha for a 'film on planning', which became Land of promise. Neurath insisted that the film should show Britain in a global context, but the film's sponsor (British Commercial Gas Association) rejected an outline with such international scope and so, instead, Rotha proposed a film about shelter.“ Land of promise was finally subtitled an argument about our homes and houses', echoing the way Neurath referred to Isotype charts as ‘visual arguments'.67 Indeed some of Neurath's ideas are most clearly recognizable in the script of this film – in particular concerning the nature of planning and the application of war-economy measures to peacetime.

Land of promise featured the same multi-voice commentary technique and dialectical structure as World of plenty, but this time Isotype itself was given a voice. Neurath revived the idea for an electronic voice but, again, this proved unworkable and Isotype was instead given a dispassionate, male voice (similar to another voice in the film that utters facts from parliamentary records).68 On Isotype's first appearance the voice declares:.

I, I am Isotype. I use symbols to make diagrams. Here is my symbol for a family: father, mother and children. Here are houses people live in. Different kinds of houses. We shall be needing builders because we shall be building houses. I am here to help. I am Isotype.

As in World of plenty, some doubt is cast on statistics by one of the film's characters. When an estimate is given of the number of children who died due to bad housing conditions, the doubting character (named in the credits as the 'Know-all') comments 'I don't trust these estimates'. The voice of Isotype replies: 'Isotype does not estimate' and introduces animated statistics about deaths from tuberculosis connected with slum housing in Liverpool. This does not persuade the Know-all', who complains: 'I don't believe all these Isotypes; I want first-hand evidence.'

Land of promise was offered free to the new Minister of Health in 1945, but the film was rejected by the post-war Labour leadership due to political uncertainty and indecision. Without the wartime distribution network of the Moı (which was superseded in April 1946 by the Central Office of Information; cor), the film was not shown as widely as World of plenty. It received generally positive press notices during its showing at the Academy Cinema in London's Oxford Street: the News Chronicle's reviewer felt that the Isotype diagrams ‘stole the film', while The Times noted how the animated diagrams were woven tightly into the film's argument:

The film is not ashamed of its political convictions, and planned economy is the flag it nails to its masthead. Look what happened between the wars, say camera, commentary and diagram, moving together in militant step.

The Times Educational Supplement added that 'often, however, the time allowed to absorb both the figures and their significance is too short, and much of the argument is left to the sound-track alone'.7º

One project which would have contained even more thematic influence from the Neuraths than Land of promise was a film called 'I am a scientist', which did not progress beyond the script stage. The idea for it may have arisen from the First Scientific Films Conference, organized by the Association of Scientific Workers in London, August 1942: Rotha's production Blood transfusion was shown there and Otto Neurath attended the event.71 Rotha's intentions to incorporate ideas on science and planning into documentary film coincided with the preoccupations of Neurath, whose work – in all its aspects – was concerned with furthering a 'scientific attitude'.7²

The Neuraths submitted their ideas for 'I am a scientist' to Rotha in the form of a typed dialogue between themselves about how they would like to show science on film. The initial treatment was inspired by Neurath's recollections of his boyhood interest chemistry, which he also included in his 'visual autobiography.'7³ The film was to have been sponsored by icı but it was presumably not developed due to more urgent projects for the war effort commissioned by the Mo1.

## Unifilm – a joint initiative

By late 1942 Neurath had already proposed forming an Isotype unit within Rotha's film company:

Further, we should like to be in relation to a film firm, which would act as our permanent ‘selling centre'. If people ask me, how to get Isotype films, I should answer: Isotype Department Rotha Films, or something like that.74

An arrangement like this would only be achieved (briefly) after Neurath's death with a joint company named Unifilm.

Rotha and Neurath's shared desire to acquire an animation table remained unfulfilled until 1945; it required custom design and manufacture and Rotha commissioned a workshop in Manchester to do it.7⁵

![](images/80bcbb148553df1e8157866a59bb196c401da22da37321e4fd3a02c441be4a30.jpg)  
LAND OF PROMISE A PAUL ROTHA FILM  
Poster for Land of promise. c. 1945. Not designed at the Isotype Institute.

70. News Chronicle, 27 April 1946; The Times, 26 April 1946; Times Educational Supplement, 4 May 1946.

71. Blood transfusion had also been shown at a meeting of the London Scientific Films Society in January 1942. In March 1943 Neurath attended a meeting of the British Association for the Advancement of Science, held at Burlington House. Rotha spoke in a panel on Radio and Cinema, but Neurath was disappointed by the discussion. In the programme for the event, under the session titled 'Science and the Press', it was stated that 'Mr H. G. Wells will be present, and may speak.'

72. In April 1941 Neurath had attended the meeting in Oxford of a group called 'Education – Science – Society' along with J. D. Bernal, Joseph Lauwerys, Lancelot Hogben, and Julian Huxley. The meeting was organized by Patrick Meredith, an educator with a strong interest in film.

73. From hieroglyphics to Isotype, p. 20.

74. Letter Neurath to Rotha,   
25 October 1942 (Ic 1/43).   
75. Letter Rotha to Neurath,   
8 October 1943 (ic 1/44).

![](images/d47f370e9d63ece88df17abf1843152a11743286d4f52a8a1af432c5b92e79be.jpg)

Letter from Paul Rotha to Otto Neurath, 14 September 1945 (ic 1/45) proposing the name Unifilm for their joint company. Rotha has sketched the name with the same shadow type style printed on the Rotha Films letterhead, showing his sensitivity to typography. Rotha described 'control over the design, type and speed of the main and credit titles of a film' as one of the 'special pleasures among the many that add up to the making of a complete film'. (Documentary diary, p. 153)

In March 1945, Peter Bradford asked for material from the Isotype Institute to make a trial of the table in-house at Films of Fact.76 In September Rotha proposed the formation of a new company, Unifilm, with Rotha and Neurath as directors, to be joined later by John Wales as business manager. Films of Fact gave £1,ooo in initial working capital to Unifilm, and then a further £3,ooo for 'experimental work'. Rotha explained that Unifilm would produce animated films and educational filmstrips (for static projection, an idea which had gestated in discussion with the book packager Adprint)." Rotha wrote enthusiastically: 'Films of Fact is now definitely in the position to set up its own Animation Department and Visual Education Unit! I shall engage a cameraanimator as soon as I get back.' 78

Nevertheless, animation of Isotype sequences continued to be executed by Diagram Films for some Rotha productions made after Neurath's death. These included A city speaks (1946), about the workings of Manchester City Council, and a film made for the British Waterworks Association that told the story of water from rain to tap'. Waterworks (1947) features effective diagrammatic animation of technical processes, sometimes mapped on to live action landscape photography by optical dissolves. The film benefited from a successful collaboration between its directors (Rotha & Wales), Marie Neurath and a new intermediary, Sylvia Crammer, who was employed as an assistant to Bradford." Both Crammer and Marie Neurath were less pleased with the next film, History of writing (1947), made with the Cor for the Ministry of Education This was the first film featuring animation executed at the Unifilm studio and, as may be expected of a first attempt, it lacks fluidity and finesse in this respect. (This was also partly due to the inherent difficulty in animating letterforms effectively for educational purposes.) History of writing formed part of an experimental 'visual unit' along with an accompanying filmstrip, wall charts, and a handbook containing notes for teachers who would be present at the film's showing. A further unit was completed on the History of printing and papermaking, including two films on these subjects.

76. Bradford suggested a 'confusion scene' in which 'houses run about'; Marie Neurath, with unimpeachable Isotype logic, replied: 'But why should houses run about?' Letters Bradford & Marie Neurath, 17 March & 10 May 1945 (IC 1/45).

77. Wales had been in charge of the Dartington Hall Film Unit during the Second Worid War. Unifilm was to have 12 members, six appointed by Films of Fact and six by the Isotype Institute. (Letters Rotha to Neurath, 14 September & 1 October 1945; IC 1/45.) Rotha stated later

that Unifilm was incorporated in March 1946, but had been in operation for some months before that. (Letter Rotha to Benjamin Seebohm Rowntree, 30 October 1947; IC 1/45.)

78. Letter Rotha to Neurath, n.d.   
[October 1945] (IC 1/45).

79. Crammer was initially interviewed at the Isotype Institute, just before sitting her final examinations at Oxford University. Marie Neurath proposed that she work for Unifilm 'to integrate the intellectual, research-work side of documentary films with the artistic and visual'. (Letter from Crammer to her parents, 3 June 1946.) Crammer married Peter Bradford. She had been secretary of the Oxford University Film Society and chief film

## Historical Background of Printing and Papermaking in Europe

![](images/eed46503cb114856d8df9dfa0f201ab2a411061ec3867e40d87aaf37ca689397.jpg)  
Wall chart made for the 'visual unit' that included the films History of printing and The story of papermaking. c.1949.

The Isotype Institute and Unifilm also collaborated on a fully animated, colour film titled The endocrinology of the menstrual cycle. The subject was proposed by gynaecologist Alfred Loeser to Rotha in 1942, but animation work began at Unifilm only in 1946. This film now seems to be lost.80

![](images/8142c6053d066ffead3159f211d996947ab44054d371d646f8df47a8c389747d.jpg)

Late in 1947, Marie Neurath was unhappy about some animation made by Unifilm without consulting her (as a member of its board), and she also complained to Rotha about not being paid for some work. Rotha's company finances were in decline by this time: Films of Fact went into liquidation at the end of 1947 due to a debt of several thousand pounds owed to Humphries Film Laboratory. Rotha endeavoured to keep Unifilm going as a separate animation studio by seeking to increase its capital; but he admitted in February 1948 that it also had to be wound up.81

![](images/499e225380cc70f5719ffef2ff7d16faee642f3ab6c235c8515249290264f959.jpg)

As Timothy Boon has summarized: 'The Isotype-enriched historicodialectical multi-voice commentary film on major scientific or public health issues died with the solvency of Rotha's company.'82 Soon afterwards Rotha made clear the extent to which he and the Neuraths had been in sympathy. In a new preface to the second edition of his classic book, The film till now, he paid tribute to three people: scriptwriters

![](images/23f9e3b85b56ba4c34bdd4d3070b3467aaa8d43b2be687c2af6751c9ef7a5544.jpg)  
Two successive layers of painted acetate cels overlaid on a card background. Preparatory artwork for The endocrinology of the menstrual cycle. 1946 (IC 3.2/274).

animation of 'diagrams by the Isotype Institute' to Jack Saunders, Mary Bough, Hazel Bryan, Gwyneth Parker, Olwen Roberts and Anthony O'Leary. The film was funded by the pharmaceutical company John Wyeth and Brother (IC 3.2/274).

81. Rotha approached confectionery magnates Benjamin Seebohm Rowntree and Laurence Cadbury about acquiring more capital. He also made an overture to the Crown Film Unit about taking over Unifilm. 82. Boon, Films of fact, p. 16g.

Letter from Otto Neurath to Paul Rotha. 26 February 1943 (ic 1/45a). Among the images Neurath has drawn is one subtitled 'Caligari’: it is a thumbnail sketch of the famous expressionist sets for Das Cabinet des Dr. Caligari (written by fellow Austrian Carl Mayer; see note 83 below).

Neurath's cartoon elephant signature predates Disney's Dumbo.

83. Mayer (an Austrian) was an important figure in German cinema before sound: he wrote screenplays for Das Cabinet des Dr. Caligari, Der letzte Mann (The last laugh), and conceived the idea for Walter Ruttmann's Berlin: die Sinfonie der Großstadt. He also wrote the script for F. W. Murnau's first film in Hollywood, Sunrise: a song of two humans, although Mayer remained in Europe. He emigrated to Britain in 1932 and worked (uncredited) with Rotha's film company. He died of cancer in 1944. See Rotha's warm appreciation of Mayer in The film till now, pp.713-15.

84. Rotha, The film till now, p. 6o (preface dated August 1948). Rotha wrote this soon after collaborating with Marie Neurath on editing Otto Neurath's 'visual autobiography'. Rotha cited Neurath's text extensively in his essay 'The film and other visual techniques in education', The Museums Journal, vol. 46, no.8, November 1946, pp. 144-5. In an obituary of Neurath

Dear Mr, Rotha,

I shall be in tow , Thursday, and fre, for you   
in the afternoom, for looking at things and talk .   
We have some ideas dealing with MAGAZINE. Neither letter, nor Janunry sheoue ereived .

![](images/ada1bd93931d2ba34f688111469a9a40e88fe32db124456357854b9e1af5a25b.jpg)

Eric Knight and Carl Mayer,83 who both worked on World of plenty, and 'Otto Neurath, who of all others I have met, understood most fully how the film could extend the consciousness of the international man-inthe-street'.84 In 1956, Rotha gave credit to the Isotype Institute for helping to explain complex economic aspects of World of plenty and The world is rich: Their long experience in developing an international picture language with symbols without doubt helped to secure the world audiences for these films.'8⁵

The collaboration of the Isotype Institute with filmmakers did not quite end there. In 1954 Marie Neurath was asked to prepare some short animated sequences for the television series The world is ours, which was produced during Paul Rotha's short tenure as head of the new documentary department at the BBC. In 1963 Peter Bradford asked Marie

for The Times (9 January 1946) Rotha wrote: 'Otto Neurath had an exceptional understanding of ordinary people and always believed that the man-in-the-street was a great deal more interested in acquiring knowledge about everyday things and the history of mankind than the intellectual will generally admit.. His warm, disarming behaviour made him immensely popular in whatever circles he moved.'

85. Rotha, 'Presenting the world to the world' in Duncan Petrie and Robert Kruger (ed.), A Paul Rotha reader (Exeter: University of Exeter Press, 1999), p. 276.

Neurath to prepare some treatments for a film about water sponsored by Unilever. This became The physics and chemistry of water (1965), produced by Bradford and directed by Sarah Erulkar, with animation by Michael Ricketts. This film finally realized Otto Neurath's dream of combining electronic sound with animation. It won the award at the Venice Film Festival for best documentary/educational film.8⁶

In his 'visual autobiography', From hieroglyphics to Isotype, Neurath pondered the relative merits of moving and still images. In his view, several still images presented simultaneously had the advantage of comparability, whereas filmed images disappear to be replaced by others, without the audience being able to retrieve them. Home video technology now enables the viewer to control the sequence of moving images, but Neurath's objection could have been overcome to some extent in his time by the use of split-screen technique – showing two sequences side by side with a dividing line in the image. Rotha seems to have implied to Neurath that splitting the screen was troublesome; for live action it would have entailed expensive optical processing but in animation it could have be done on the drawing board, with no extra cost. Yet it was not a technique ever exploited in Isotype animation.87

The suitability of Isotype style for combination with live action could perhaps be questioned. Neurath's persistent struggle to protect the rigour of Isotype from the pitfalls presented by motion reflect an uneasy marriage. The simplicity and clear definition of Isotype charts and diagrams combine well with type in print, but the transition from cinematography to animated statistics in some of Rotha's films can be jarring, despite the sophisticated soundtrack accompaniment. (In addition, on film Isotype was typically deprived of a key component of its armoury – colour.) Neurath insisted: We stress the point, that the “realistic” and the “symbolic" should be separated, scrupulously.'\*8 This may make sense intellectually but it creates a conscious disharmony that counteracts the familiar viscerality of many movies. Rotha may well have valued this effect as an antidote to Hollywood superficiality.

88. Letter Neurath to Rotha, 18 August 1942 (IC 1/43). In the booklet Health education by Isotype (p. 27), Neurath hinted at the potential problem when discussing the design of printed charts: 'Photographs may be combined with symbols in the same chart, but there is danger of an incongruous effect.'

89. The more naturalistic animation designed by Isabel Alexander for Rotha's production Defeat diphtheria (1941) presents an alternative that is not limited by the deliberate flatness of Isotype graphics. By simulating an impression of spatial depth, which is such an integral feature of cinema, her drawings map on to the live action more comfortably and accord with Neurath's professed intention to 'humanize' knowledge. Defeat diphtheria was erroneously included in the list of films featuring Isotype animation in the University of Reading's exhibition catalogue, Graphic communication through Isotype, p. 45. Diagram Films executed the animation of Alexander's drawings Alexander was also credited with set decoration for Land of promise.

![](images/cfe0d745c8ef4b45c259341953dc712d4d57a17ab5718dc71374c7eff2601a47.jpg)  
Marie Neurath, December 1945.

# 10 Graphic explanation for children 1944-71

Sue Walker

The children's books produced by the Isotype Institute provide an excellent insight into Marie Neurath's work as a transformer and show that she had a particular skill in making charts and illustrations that were accessible to children of all ages. She did this through careful and consistent use of pictograms, drawings, colour, and page layout, combined with text that was relevant, accurate, and written with the needs of younger readers in mind. She was gifted in providing clear explanations. A ten-year-old child wrote to her and asked 'Where did you get your information on “Rockets and jets"? Did you draw the pictures or did someone else? Is it fun to write books?';1 in reply Marie Neurath offered a child-friendly explanation of how the book was made:

We got the information from many books and periodicals, one of our institute went to the library and read the latest material. Then we talked, and she explained everything to me, and I sat down and made new sketches, and talked them over with other people, and showed them to a man who knows everything about the subject, and then the final drawings were made by the designers in our institute. You see, this is like a little factory making picture books, we make one after the other, it is great fun.²

This brief description encapsulates the process that underpinned an extremely prolific period in the work of the Isotype Institute. It began in the 194os, soon after Otto and Marie Neurath settled in England. After Otto Neurath's death in 1945, Marie Neurath continued to work on children's books as author and transformer until the early 197os.

## Otto Neurath's ideas about children's books

Otto Neurath seems to have begun serious work on children's books in England in the mid-194os when he started a series of negotiations with

A sketch by Otto Neurath showing some of his ideas for presenting information for children: a leperello and a Punch and Judy stage (1C 1/32). 259 × 203 mm.

![](images/05bdb0b49b2f1da02703dfbb0a6be50a20cdf638e379b742cbd59dda52ec78b7.jpg)

the book-packaging company Adprint about working with the Isotype Institute. In a letter dated 2o January 1944, to Walter Neurath (Adprint's production manager, no relation to Otto), Otto Neurath identified three kinds of collaboration. First, 'Isotype service', described as Routine work, coņveyor', in which the Isotype Institute would prepare charts for Adprint publications under the direction of Adprint's editors and writers; second, Isotype-Adprint cooperation in which there would be collaboration between the Isotype Institute and editors; and third Isotype-Adprint cooperation in which Isotype would assume the editorial role.³ Neurath included suggestions for children's books, many of which were practical applications of how visual education could enhance learning and teaching in schools. He proposed nine different kinds of Isotype publication, which included a ‘visual education series' for teachers intended to be a guide through the multiple visual material' including illustrations, drawings, cartoons, exhibitions; and an 'Isotype corner' in a future Adprint periodical in which explanations in the form of printed Isotype charts could be cut out and used by teachers in the classroom. Four of the items in Neurath's list related to younger readers: ‘a certain type of isoTYPE Children's books' with very simple language in rhyme for very young children; textbooks for schools, which he regarded as a 'big matter' requiring care and caution; picture collections to accompany textbooks ‘on physics, chemistry, biology, public health, social sciences, geography, history, astronomy etc.'; and finally, picture-text books ‘which deal with certain inventions, actions, short text, many pictures e.g. AROUND WHALING, AROUND

3. Letter Otto Neurath to Walter Neurath, 20 January 1944 (IC 1/31).

![](images/507fc92b2f4553bf2127349fb1a2239c4743a82e8612651720bd5a8d2cc6c744.jpg)  
Cover design and pages from a handmade mock-up for 'Tips for tots'. 1944 (iC 3.2/51–7). 259 × 197 mm.

![](images/e2a26e2a6002061bd7902e5339786b184cf90a912ddc79bffc71080792ad21d5.jpg)

![](images/4f085ec645af920cce83622b55dd8e6c0b29bf4e0ce4c5b864b1bfd080b1853e.jpg)

PAPER, AROUND ROCKETS'. Many of Neurath's ambitious ideas stemmed from his belief that children could understand complex phenomena, and he saw a role for himself and his colleagues in enabling this. His plans were recorded in vigorous annotated sketches and in notebooks, and extended to ideas about book formats and other ways of presenting information to children.

Otto Neurath's letters to Walter Neurath in April 1944 were accompanied by book dummies and pages showing ideas in a visual form for two projected series for children, Tips for tots' and Just boxes'. Tips for tots' took forward the idea of Isotype characters in an Isotype world: Iso and Typie (a boy and a girl) and their cat were the main characters. Just boxes' was about the inner workings of everyday boxlike objects and was later developed by Marie Neurath. Iso and Typie's adventures were restricted to events that demonstrated changes in the seasons, but it is evident from Neurath's notes that he envisaged escapades on a much grander and more complex scale.⁴ However, characterization did not sit happily with the Isotype notion of generic, iconic form: characters with no faces and rather static actions did not convey much excitement or the means through which younger readers might identify with them. There was little potential for story-telling or for the inclusion of language to enhance meaning. Compared to those in the later books, the charts presented in ‘Tips for tots' were rather bland, and the two characters did not feature in any published titles. Walter Neurath thought the first ideas to be too complicated and lacking in focus, and Otto Neurath responded with a 'loyal compromise' – 'books

4. 'Iso and Typie climb a mountain with a tower, they come down in a glider and see the landscape from above – "bird's eye view" of landscape, houses and men, which introduces them to more views of this type, of an elephant etc., and they make up a picture of all mankind and find out how much space they would take. They try out map designing. ... Iso and Typie want to see larger parts of the surface of the earth and try to go by stratosphere balloon. Many adventures. Tornado, whaling etc. They are separated, hear from one another by radio, see tropical and arctical [sic] countries.' Otto Neurath's notes, n.d. [c. 1944] (ic 3.2/51).

5. Letter Otto Neurath to Walter Neurath, 30 April 1944 (IC 1/32). An earlier letter made the same point: 'These children's books are particularly suitable for this purpose of gap filling, because they are fully in our hands, not depending upon any authors, editors etc.' Letter Otto Neurath to Walter Neurath, 19 April 1944 (IC 1/32).

6. See From hieroglyphics to Isotype, especially chapters 2 and 3.

7. Typescript 'Isotype books for children', 30 April 1944 (Ic 1/32).

8. Marie Neurath and J. A. Lauwerys, 'Visual science', First book (London: Max Parrish, 1950), p.7.

centred around one theme only' – and also by noting that it was important to the Neuraths to have full control of the work:

Preparing the children's books has the great advantage, that we are wholly free in the process of work. Of course, we always ask experts, e.g. whatever will deal with details and of course, where English is concerned. We have collaborators, who will help us, ... the CHILDREN's IsOTYPE BOOK series will enable us to go on free of all outside limitations, making the best we can do, responsible for each detail.

Accompanying this letter was a detailed typewritten rationale, 'Isotype books for children', that summarized Neurath's thinking about how books should be designed for children. Many of these ideas were drawn from his childhood experiences and memories as well as his views on visual education. He suggested that children's learning benefited from pictures designed to encourage children to look, compare and reach conclusions for themselves. He wanted to find ways to encourage children to engage with the content being presented through familiar objects and situations, and characters and conventions that they could get to know and relate to:

There are many fine books, giving explanations to children and adults, but usually they are learned'. These IsOTYPE BOOKs try to avoid that in any case, therefore just the creating of an environment for a child is the point. It is not so much the question, how to transfer in the most direct and simple way some knowledge, but how to satisfy the possible questions of a child, his love of action, his identifying himself with a person on the page etc.

The Neuraths' commitment to what they described as the active attitude' underpinned the view that children should be encouraged to engage with the charts and pictures in order to find out information for themselves. Young readers were thus advised in many of the introductory texts to the children's books that the more you look, the more you will see':

There are no special rules about the way you should read' these charts, but you will find it useful to make sure first that you understand what each symbol stands for. Then look at the whole chart and try to see how the different parts fit together. ... You will be able to answer most of the questions by looking carefully at the pictures ..

Each Isotype chart or illustration was carefully designed so that every graphic element was there for a reason. Marie Neurath recalled:

First page of typescript by Otto Neurath, 'Isotype books for children', dated 30 April 1944 (Ic 1/32).

I had to ask myself: what are the essential things we want to show, how can we use comparison, direct the attention, through the arrangement and use of colour, to bring out the most important things at the first glance, and additional features on closer scrutiny. Details had to be meaningful, everything in the picture had to be useful for information.

This approach to designing pervaded all the children's books produced by the Isotype Institute. Accuracy, clarity, and attention to detail were important not only in the design of the final chart or illustration, but in the way that Marie Neurath and her team approached the work. Much time was spent gathering materials from libraries and museums, and asking experts in particular fields whether the sketches and drawings they produced made sense.

10. An account of the educational and design rationale is given in Sue Walker, 'Explaining history to children: Otto and Marie Neurath's work on the "Visual history of mankind" ', Journal of Design History, vol. 24, no. 4, 2012, pp. 345–62.

11. Marie Neurath & Robin Kinross, The transformer, pp. 63–5. 12. Neurath & Kinross, The transformer, p. 65.

13. In one letter, for example: 'The British Museum did not grant me a reader's ticket. Otto had one. I don't really require a seat for any length of time – I have only to see the books and illustrations our collaborator has collected, to make my choice. They want some letter to say that my work is of sufficient importance. Could you write me such a letter – if you think it right to write it.' Letter Marie Neurath to Lauwerys, 18 February 1946 (Ic 1/39). Lauwerys was happy to oblige.

14. See Matthew Eve, 'Isotype in trouble, 1946–1948'.

## Production challenges in the early days

The first project that appealed to people at Adprint, and which brought together Otto Neurath's ideas about the kind of content that would benefit children's learning and Marie Neurath's approach to visual organization, was the 'Visual history of mankind'. This was an ambitious, collaborative project involving the Neuraths, the educationist Joseph Lauwerys, and others.¹º As had been the case with Isotype work in Vienna, it approached the teaching of history through consideration of issues that affected society as a whole, rather than individual people. It looked at the past through the lens of everyday activities covering topics such as ‘Making light' and 'Travelling on land', rather than kings, queens, and battles.

Marie Neurath worked very closely with her husband on the first two of the three 'Visual history' books, as she noted later:

I always showed my sketches to Neurath, right to the last, before I gave them for production. If he proposed another approach, it now often happened that I had already tried and rejected it. But if we found that his suggestion was an improvement, he was pleased, he said, that something could still occur to him too. It was, however different with the work on the visual history of mankind: with his extensive historical knowledge and vivid imagination, Neurath came very much to the fore, and I was just his helping hand. He contributed to the graphic details, for example in the plan and elevation of the medieval town. 11

After Otto Neurath's death, Marie Neurath continued to work on the 'Visual history' and some of his other ideas, too. She wrote:

Of the many continuing jobs, the completion of the visual history was the hardest. ... Joseph Lauwerys was always available for advice over the choice of subjects for the charts to be done in the second and third books, and we managed it in the end.1²

It was also a challenging time for Marie Neurath in other ways, and it is evident from the correspondence that Lauwerys helped her in connection with her work.1³ The Isotype Institute's relationship to Adprint between 1946 and 1948 was uneasy.¹4 She recalled:

... difficulties arose from dealing with a new man for the book work: Foges enlarged his firm and created his own publishing department with Max Parrish in charge. Unlike Foges, who had known about us already in Vienna, Parrish had never met Neurath, and our whole approach was foreign to him. I gave him International picture language to read, but that did not help; he said only that it was utopian. 15

Despite this, Foges invited Marie Neurath and her team to set up office at Adprint's premises in Rathbone Place, London, and the 'Visual history' was completed there.

The interest and support of Foges and others at Adprint was timely. Adprint's approach to publishing through 'book packaging' meant that the books, printed in many colours, could be produced in times of austerity. However, Max Parrish was particularly concerned to ensure that the books were financially viable. In 1946, for example, he summarized his concerns about production costs (as a result of the many colour printings required by the Neuraths for the charts in the ‘Visual history') in a paper ‘Visual schoolbooks' for colleagues at Adprint. Parrish assumed that there would need to be 'school book' and 'bookshop' editions of the series to cover costs, with print runs of 2o,ooo in each edition. The plan was to print 40,ooo using the same colour plates, but different black printings for the text (the idea seemed to have been that the bookshop edition contained the answers to the questions that complemented the charts; see p. 402 below). Parrish wrote of the issues that concerned him over the schoolbook edition:

I am still of the opinion that if the books are to have a real chance to get into the schools in quantity they must be priced at not more than 2/6d. This means cutting the format to the bone, but in fact the new design gives a not unpleasant book, which we might expect to seem value at half a crown. We cannot, however, give it anything but a stiffish paper cover, printed in two colours. If we publish, and if we take our normal profit, the amounts left for Isotype (.5o [pence] per copy) and plant are negligible. Nor is there any margin for additional profit for another publisher. Of course, we should continue our research to find whether the schools can be expected to pay more than half a crown [2 shillings 6 pence]; but I hardly think we can get much further than this without a book in proof. 1⁶

Later in the paper he noted that he could not see how to reduce the manufacturing costs further, but suggested that ‘For the geographies, where drawing has not started, we might try to redesign; we should certainly cut the number of colours to six'. Such deliberations are likely to have contributed to what Marie Neurath perceived as reluctance by Foges and Parrish to move towards publication as quickly as she would have liked, as she noted in a letter to Kenneth James:

Mr Parrish is very much a pessimist and very cautious, and I have only his opinion now; Mr. Foges' may be different. But his may be too optimistic again, as it has been at earlier occasions, so that it is not much

15. Neurath & Kinross, The transformer, p. 65.

16. Max Parrish, 'Visual schoolbooks', typescript, 28 May 1946, (ic 2/1). Parrish did indeed go on to produce a proof of Living in early times, dated 1947, and this was sent out to experts and teachers for comment: 'I am sending you a copy of the Visual History, with Mr Parrish's permission, who wants to hear lots of more or less kind criticism from the expert world, but does not want this to be seen by the publishing world, for obvious reasons.' Letter Marie Neurath to Kenneth James, 18 October 1947 (IC 1/21). In the 1949 Max Parrish catalogue the 'Visual history' books were priced 3 shillings.

Panels from an exhibition of Isotype work that opened on 11 May 1948 at Adprint House, London. (ic 6/10)

17. Letter Marie Neurath to Kenneth James, 14 May 1948 (1C 1/21).

![](images/3c3723d6fce92651d51cb3574154d2078627b01a343b44a1094ca230f580f438.jpg)

use to ask. So the only secure thing is to rely on our own situation which is secured till the end of 195o, and our reduced income which we can share among ourselves.

Nevertheless, and despite such issues, in 1948 the Isotype-Adprint relationship was affirmed through an exhibition at Adprint House promoting 'the work of the Isotype Institute for industry, commerce, public information and education' (as the invitation put it).

The Isotype Institute, under the direction of Marie Neurath, went on to produce many series of children's books. Many were worked on concurrently and also alongside other Isotype projects, such as the work in West Africa (see chapter 11). It is difficult to establish just how many people worked for the Institute at any one time; many worked on a part-time basis and did so from their homes, and some took on a variety of roles. In 1974 Marie Neurath recalled the various stages involved in the making of the charts:

It is the responsibility of the transformer' to understand the data, to get all necessary information from the expert, to decide what it is worth transmitting to the public, how to make it understandable, how to link it with general knowledge or with information already given in other charts. In this sense the transformer is the trustee of the public. He has to remember the rules and keep to them, adding new variations where advisable, at the same time avoiding unnecessary deviations which would only confuse. He has to produce a rough of the chart in which many details would have been decided: title, arrangement, type, number and colour of symbols, caption etc. It is a blueprint from which the artist works.18

She used the term ‘designing' to refer to her role in this process in relation to the planning of the ‘Visual history of mankind':

My personal contribution will be the designing of every chart and the supervision of the research and drawing work connected with it, within our institute. 19

In practice, and perhaps indicative of the collaborative ethos that both Otto and Marie Neurath considered important, there was considerable overlapping and sharing of roles: the designated 'designers', for example, undertook research and commented on the wording of the text. Marie Neurath was actively involved in the supervision of various stages of the work from writing briefs for those involved in the research, to annotating final drawings with comments about accuracy and detail.

Thus, making the charts and illustrations for the books involved research and gathering of background information; transformation, including drafting the text in many cases; drawing the components of the illustration and making artwork for printing, which involved pasting down elements of the illustration, applying mechanical tints and producing overlays for colour printing. It is evident from notes and correspondence that each of these stages was undertaken by different people. An undated scrap of paper in the archive, under a heading 'Textbooks', implied that making 6o charts a year required one transformator [sic], four designers and one research worker.2º There seems to have been a relatively high turnover of people working for the Isotype Institute in these roles and others between 1941 and 1948.21

18. Marie Neurath, 'Isotype' (1974), p. 136.

19. Letter Marie Neurath to Patrick Meredith, 20 May 1944 (1C 1/35).

20. The use of the term 'textbooks' suggests that this was referring to the 'Visual history' and 'Visual geography' series (though the latter was not produced).

21, Staff lists and letters of reference reveal that Olga Bursil worked as a designer between October 1945 and September 1946; Clive Weatherhead as a transformer between June 1946 and April 1948: 'his special task was to find ways to explain historical, organizational, geographical and technical relationships in diagrammatic form to a general public'; and P. G. Findlay as an illustrative artist between March 1946 and July 1947. Kenneth James (Jimmy) and Dennis & Barbara Young worked as designers for a relatively long period (1941 to 1946/7); James went to the usA and Marie Neurath continued to seek his opinion; the Youngs seem to have worked part-time from their home in High Wycombe, sending drawings and comments by post. Ilse Reichenbach was employed from February 1946 'to do research work for the Isotype Institute and to supervise certain work which is being executed in a studio in London'.

## Developing ideas about children's books

Work that began in the mid-194os resulted in If you could see inside (1948), I'll show you how it happens (1948), Railways under London (1948), and in the two series ‘Visual history of mankind' (1948) and ‘Visual science' (1950-2).

Otto Neurath's idea in Just boxes' had been that approaching things from an unexpected angle could interest young readers: 'modern implements present themselves usually in boxes, therefore one has to look into the boxes, to know how they work.' His sketches showed his initial thoughts, and his proposal to Adprint in the form of a handmade mock-up comprised double-page spreads showing the inner workings of particular kinds of box', such as a greenhouse and a diving bell.

Some of Otto Neurath's preliminary notes and sketches for Just boxes'. C. 1944 (IC 3.2/51).

Cover and pages from a handmade mock-up of Just boxes' 1944 (1C 3.2/57). 324 × 256 mm.

The Neuraths worked in double-page spread units so that they could think about the text-picture relationship from the outset.

![](images/56f7ef3a3abf109db9eb7e638d7d3eda44ac4589fbd2b6c43118e96360a8e726.jpg)

Marie Neurath was quick to realize that the boxes' idea was too constraining, and moved instead to the more general notion of 'what's inside' that resulted in If you could see inside. She recalled:

... it played with the idea that boxes which look similar from the outside are inside quite different; jack-in-a-boxes, cameras, radios, etc. But one then got on to things that were too difficult, and so it was not yet satisfactory. I avoided this by giving up the similarity of outer form: so I could take a wasp's nest and a lighthouse, volcano and cave with stalactites.²²

If you could see inside (1948), the first of the 'Wonders of the modern world' series, was a 32-page book that comprised a series of explanations in words and pictures of something likely to interest curious young minds: 'The wonderful thing that happens inside an egg'; 'The underground home of the mole'; as well as enigmatic titles such as 'If they could see inside!', which showed how tunnels could be dug for escape from a castle under attack. The seemingly miscellaneous choice of topics was connected through the use of cross sections, thus following through on Otto Neurath's original idea. Marie Neurath ensured that other graphic attributes reflected Isotype principles: omission of unnecessary detail, simplicity of shapes, and use of Isotype pictograms as appropriate. She wrote the text that accompanied the illustrations so that it was relevant to the needs of younger readers (see p. 424 below). I'll show you how it happens was produced at the same time as If you could see inside and followed the same format. A somewhat miscellaneous selection of topics, it contained visual explanations ranging from the birth of a dragonfly to how fire can lift a balloon.

![](images/47c7f4e1d9396b384f27dcbe73b4fb709fa8db7d78e8a569c6227630ebf39fcc.jpg)

In both books, rules for graphic presentation were not yet fully formed and the illustrations lacked the consistency evident in later publications. In If you could see inside, for example, the sun was yellow on one occasion, and red on another. The layout of text and pictures varied considerably throughout the books. Typographic inconsistency was particularly noticeable: type-size, line-spacing, justification, and style of headings varied from page to page. In one case lines of text extended across the entire width of the double-page spread (Tower Bridge makes way for a tall ship'), on another the text was arranged in small clumps around the illustrations (‘The wonderful; birth of the dragon fly'; see p. 425 below).

![](images/a228f3ef052f65e2de1df256b7beac3243048374936204c870b216ef50a23d05.jpg)

Railways under London (1948) was aimed at children aged 9-12, and was also concerned with answering the kinds of questions that children might ask, in this case about the London Underground: 'How does the

22. Neurath & Kinross, The transformer, p. 66.

![](images/705a11a1baa13ef5c7e5ec7d14d9bbcd5da677aca89a7b66e2e86917b3b27abf.jpg)

ticket-machine give the right change?' or ‘How do the train doors open when nobody touches them?'Marie Neurath and her team worked closely with London Transport in the production of this book. The company provided detailed technical information and checked the accuracy of all the sketches made at the Isotype Institute. The archives contain files of detailed preparatory drawings for the charts that appeared in the book. As with many kinds of Isotype technical illustrations, understanding the concept being illustrated required considerable engagement on the part of the reader. Some of the drawings/charts, however, were deceptively straightforward and clear, and particularly engaging because of this. One of the most compelling showed how the escalators work, and equally effective was the drawing that showed the workings of the underground system as a whole (see pp. 426-7 below).

The first editions of these three books were published at the same time, and were reviewed for the most part as a set, and in favourable terms, as in this example from Book Review, 31 July 1949:

In each book the textual explanations are succinctly enlightening, supplementing the illustrations. These are diagrammatic in their clarity, fascinating in detail and appealing in their crisp modern style.

![](images/4bfabf13842a13f0cfd3696c7ad123b6d81515fc09061b78dcb2860eae913c5f.jpg)

The 'Visual history of mankind' series, comprising Living in early times, Living in villages and towns and Living in the world, was published by Adprint under the Max Parrish imprint. Intended for classroom use, each of the books comprised a sequence of 2o double-page spreads, each of which contained an Isotype chart and a list of questions, the answers to which could be worked out by examining the chart (see pp. 428-3o below). The charts made for ‘Visual history' provide excellent examples of transformation and different approaches to the design of narrative structures, the depiction of space, and the representation of distance, quantity, and time. Many of the charts contained a vast amount of information and affirmed the Isotype principle that readers should learn something from a first look and then glean more information from looking more closely. In this series colour was used carefully and consistently and in many charts the questions helped readers work out what the colours meant. The 'Visual history' was described by the Times Educational Supplement (11 December 1948):

The first of a series designed to show how our ancestors began to evolve methods of living which culminated in modern civilization. Thus such stories as that of the ship, the wheel, the machine are depicted, but in the new visual method known as Isotype. This may be described as a

picture language, but it is by no means simply an easy way of escaping the more rigid discipline of the printed text-book: it requires an equal amount of thought and effort on behalf of the pupil to be fully effective. The special value of this method lies in its providing the teacher with an inherently more appealing background of interest on which to base lessons.

Like the 'Visual history' series, ‘Visual science' (1950-2) was intended primarily for use in schools. It aimed to present the instruction of science in a way that was creative and stimulating for pupils and teacher alike. The six books each combined Isotype pictures with short pieces of text and sets of questions; readers could only answer these by making a careful study of the accompanying visual material. Each book also contained a bibliography of other relevant works. Look and Listen (December 1953) praised this innovative approach and suggested that the series ‘should both revolutionise science teaching and do much to help alleviate the acute shortage of science teachers and equipment'.

The teacher's notes that prefaced each of the books in the series explained the Isotype approach, the reasoning behind the books and how the Isotype charts were intended to be used. The notes in Second book, for example, began with a very clear description:

As you turn the pages of this book, you will notice that it uses more pictures and fewer words than most schoolbooks you have seen. These pictures, called Isotype charts, are not meant to show you exactly how things look but to give you information about them, like a map or an engineer's blue-print. Because they have an unusual job to do they are made in an unusual way. Everything which would not help you understand the meaning, or which would confuse you, is left out. Colours are used only to help make the meaning clearer, never simply as decoration. This means that every line and every colour in these pictures has something to tell you.

![](images/c99cc28fe13ce76106bc1e35a6df38696afe442b88d1646b0c3b88ae0ae48455.jpg)

The notes explained some of the graphic conventions: green dots were used for air throughout the book; scales and eyes were left out in the depiction of a fish because they would not add to the meaning of a chart about a fish's weight. The notes included a selection of the pictograms used in the book with verbal definitions. They explained how the charts should be used, that they were intended to encourage questioning and comparison, and were related to activities in everyday life. The third book in the series, Science in the home, reinforced this:

Science is not simply a subject' which you learn in the classroom, in the laboratory or by reading books. It is a way of observing, or understanding, of doing things. In your own home, among the things you see and handle every day, there are many opportunities to observe, to understand and to do things in a scientific way.²3

![](images/71ee02f595b563dbc7a72c901d6ad17135f2b94e3909ae28326cb797259c0fe1.jpg)

## 'A little factory making picture books' – 1950s and early 196os

Railways under London had attracted much interest: in 1949 it was chosen by the National Book League as one of the 5o best British books of the year, and as a result Max Parrish wanted 'more and more' books.²⁴ This resulted in an intense phase of publishing that resulted in two further series of books for children: 'Wonders of the modern world' and the 'Wonder world of nature'. The visual attributes of these books were frequently articulated in Max Parrish's publicity material. From 1961:

Each subject is clearly explained by means of simplified but accurate drawings printed in bright colours. This makes the series exceptionally stimulating to children, and helps them to follow the well-spaced and concise text. Full colour is used with meaning, to add to the clarity of the visual statement, rather than as decoration, and the clean, refreshing brilliance of the drawings never fails to appeal to children and to capture their enthusiasm.

23. 'Visual science', Science in the home (London: Max Parrish, 1950), p. 5.

24. Letter Marie Neurath to Kenneth James, 9 February 1949 (Ic 1/21). She also noted in another letter to him on 26 May 1949: 'Foges is now back in full swing and wants us to produce more and more – especially books of the RAILWAYS UNDER LONDON type. (IC 1/21)

According to the publisher, the 'Wonders of the modern world' series comprised both 'simple “how-and-why" books' for younger readers and 'books for older children on modern scientific and engineering topics'. 'The wonder world of nature' series provided 'simple explanations of the strange things that happen in nature' for children aged 7–10.

With their vibrant and typographically distinctive covers, these two series offered some excellent examples of explanation, and of the process of transformation. They were characterized by bold colours and the well-established format of double-page spreads devoted to one particular topic. The Times Literary Supplement (29 May 1959) praised
