FROM TELEGRAPH TO TELSTAR

![](images/1d93abafd38c386906a8dee5da01df0f7ac390a457938f63e70c6107e581da88.jpg)

![](images/caa69018673faee8eccf958c8b8ec32c73bd6bbfd02a7048f718583dfb3404e4.jpg)

the approach taken in presenting often quite complex technical and scientific subjects to children using 'diagrams ... so clear that her [Marie Neurath's] text is almost superfluous. Even in these days of so much visual learning her books must stamp information indelibly on the young mind.'

The Parrish Junior colour books' (1961–3) were described by the publisher as a response to parents' and teachers' demands for a series for younger children (aged 5-8) to complement the 'Wonder world of nature' and ‘Wonders of the modern world'. In the Junior colour books' the child and his or her experience was the starting point. Topics such as ‘growing', 'keeping clean', 'homes', and 'food' were explored with reference to other aspects of the natural environment. The books were organized in short sections between four and ten pages long. The series was produced economically: black plus yellow, or black plus red were used on alternate double-page spreads throughout each book. The second colour was used to identify significant elements of the illustrations referred to in the text (see pp. 436 below). The explanations relied on established Isotype techniques such as cross-section and minimal details, though many of the illustrations had a representational, pictorial quality. The Times Literary Supplement (9 July 1964) found the books to have softened over the years: the illustrations have a more human quality, are less coldly diagrammatic'.

## New approaches: integrating contemporary culture in They lived like this'

The series ‘They lived like this', published between 1964 and 1971, was developed from the Institute's work for the filmstrip publisher Common Ground.2⁵ It was a departure in style from earlier series, as Marie Neurath explained:

25. Neurath & Kinross, The transformer, p. 68–71. A list of the filmstrips published by Common Ground between 1952 and 1972 is given in [University of Reading], Graphic communication through Isotype, p. 45.

Drawings and photographs lend themselves equally well to being used on film and we were able to mix chronological tables, diagrams and maps with photographs of landscapes, people and works of art. For Egypt the magnificent murals and reliefs of Egyptian tombs can be used directly as pictures of their lives. ...

The attempt to cover the same themes in a similar way in books failed for a long time because of printing difficulties. Eventually we settled for a simplified form, both in content and technically. All twenty books in the series followed the Egyptian example, as far as possible. ...

Text and pictures combine loosely, as in the earlier series of Junior colour books'.²⁶

The illustrations in They lived like this' involved research in the British Museum, the Victoria & Albert Museum, and the London Library. The drawings in the books – of contemporary artefacts, buildings, friezes and writing systems – were often based on tracings or copies taken from photographs and drawings in academic texts. Each of the books was co-written by one of three artists: Evelyn Worboys, John Ellis, and Muriel Turner, and it is clear from the preparatory sketches for books that the pictures and text were considered alongside one another (see p. 437 below).

In 1974, Marie Neurath noted that she was occasionally asked what 'They lived like this' had to do with Isotype; she responded thus:

Certainly, there are only a few Isotype diagrams in these books. What I have done here is assemble pictures made by certain peoples in the past which can be read in a similar way to Isotype charts, and which thereby give some information about the people who made them; at the same time we could give an impression of the style in which these people worked, without even mentioning anything which reminded of a history of art. We gave a visual report about the way of life of the peoples, about the magic they applied, beliefs they had, customs, working methods, houses and temples. We arranged the books in a picture/text style, making the people's own pictures part of the story. In a way, we had learned our Isotype language from the ancient Egyptians; to make a book about Egypt with Egyptian pictures was natural and delightful. I could not fail to notice that once our method had been called 'modern hieroglyphics'.²7

Here she reminded her readers that Isotype was more than a stylistic attribution, and that she was assembling pictures that could be 'read' in a similar way to Isotype charts. The working method for these books - in particular the transformation of complicated material into childfriendly text and pictures - was no different to the procedures followed for the earlier books.

The journal Books and Bookmen (December 1968), commenting on They lived like this in ancient Rome, remarked that the work 'demonstrates clearly that it is possible to write a serious history book for young readers which is both informative and absorbing – and above all does not talk down to them'.

![](images/9cc02376fca0eb8eeda85b27288019eb01d18f4e04058738602d3338e31e58fa.jpg)  
THEY LIVED LIKE THIS IN ANCIENT ROME

![](images/0609a1015d9b0c338571430e39cb68d0f6f5d5c701e469f156e02faaab2d8d86.jpg)

![](images/e508fb94cbd1be21405a85917185431acdadcae45a9acdfe8081072c2d156874.jpg)  
THEY LIVED LIKE THIS IN ANCIENT MESOPOTAMIA

![](images/abea3cb87c5152f0ad2f16dc57dba9c4b44ed319a21700fc69f88d1d18d6a7d4.jpg)

26. Marie Neurath, 'Wie es zu den Isotype-Kinderbüchern kam', Bulletin Jugend + Literatur, October, 1972, pp. 68–70. Marie Neurath reported elsewhere about the difficulty in arousing interest in publishing 'They lived like this': 'I tried for a long time to interest our book publisher in a series of ancient civilisations. But it took many years of attempts with various publishers before we could start our series 'They lived like this' for which we have produced 2o titles. The books are in our 'picture-text style'; many of the illustrations are copied from pictures made by the people themselves of whom the book deals, for what they show as well as for the way in which they show it; here and there, diagrams in Isotype style are added.' ('Otto Neurath and Isotype', p.18.)

27. Marie Neurath, 'Isotype' (1974), pp.149-50.

Otto & Marie Neurath and J. A. Lauwerys, 'Visual history of mankind', Living in early times (London: Max Parrish, 1948).

Eight colours are used in this chart: green is used consistently for plant material and linen thread; brown, red, yellow, and green represent different kinds of cloth; grey is used for background or supporting elements; orange, blue, and black are used for the visual explanation of weaving.

![](images/98217a76c48a3607e92b441dcb04c124b8e03677bfb452207ea78c23bee8e9d4.jpg)

## Using colour to add to meaning

A distinctive feature of Isotype children's books was the use of colour, something mentioned by the publishers in their marketing and commented on favourably in reviews. The Isotype approach was to encourage children to think about how the colours were used as they read the charts. In the introduction to Living in the world, part of the 'Visual history' series, the young readers were told:

We hope that you will like the bright colours we use, just as we like them, but their main purpose is to help make the meaning of our charts clearer and not just to make them more attractive.

The first editions of books in the series ‘Visual history of mankind' featured a lavish use of colour, which the Neuraths considered an important visual resource, despite its financial implications. Eight solid colours (i.e. separate printings) were used: green, black, orange, brown, yellow, blue, red, and grey. The use of tints (dots of varying size, straight and wavy lines) added depth to this colour range, and helped to create a very colourful impression, particularly on spreads where all eight colours were used. In the first editions of the ‘Visual history', colour usage followed many of the principles that were set out in International picture language.28 Thus, green was associated with wood, grass, field, young, early; red with industry, machine, metal, warm, present, and so on. Printing in eight colours, however, conflicted with the publisher's aim to price the books so that schools could afford them, and fewer colours were used in later editions. In the 1962 edition of Living in villages and towns only red, black, green, and brown are used, with red and black

28. See International picture language (1936), p. 41 ff.

![](images/d416b6762daee26a8396e889aa98f2fa8755f55023a0411746a600c03e65e66e.jpg)

only on alternate spreads; this reduced the effectiveness of many of the charts.

'Visual science' was designed and produced from the outset using fewer colours. Colour was used on alternate spreads throughout the books, and was restricted to two or three colours in addition to black. Even with a limited colour palette, the Isotype principle of consistent usage prevailed, as explained in the ‘How to use this book' section in -Plants and animals:

Every line and colour has something to tell you. On page 18, for instance, you will see that black is used to show carbon and orange to show oxygen. When we next speak of carbon and oxygen, on page 22, the same colours are used to show them.

Colours were used consistently within a page, though not necessarily for the same function as elsewhere in the book. When colour was not

Marie Neurath & J. A. Lauwerys, 'Visual science', Science in the home (London: Max Parrish, 1951).

This example from one of the black and white spreads in the book shows how different tones and shapes help to explain both the constituent elements and the processes involved in making bread.

![](images/61ca1bc4053afc856ec8f772a3cad4f9077810f9ee2355d95d0ee0230693f0b2.jpg)

available, the designers used tone and pattern to show different substances and processes, also in a consistent way and to aid the graphic explanation.

The publisher promoted the colourful attributes of the Isotype books. The 'Wonders of the modern world' and The wonder world of nature' series were described in publicity as 'Max Parrish colour books' and as being ‘simple and vivid' in colour. Vivid colours were particularly evident in the covers of these books, but internally most of them employed only three colours plus black on alternate spreads, and black plus one colour on the others.

The cockle carries its house on its back like a snail. When the starfish, hunched up, rawls near it, thecockle draws back into its shell. T'he two halves of the shell close so tightly that a strong man could not open them. The starfish fixes one or two of its arms, cdgcd with suckers, to cach half and begins to pull. It rests a few suckers at a timne but it never stops pulling. At last the cockle gives way. Its shell snaps open and the starfish has won a meal.

## Marie Neurath, Fire (London: Max Parrish, 1950).

Following isotype convention, blue is used to represent water; and different tones of blue distinguish water in a liquid state from its presence as a component of foam used to put out fires. In the same book, on alternate spreads, two colours are used: black for the text and a red-brown for the pictures. These monochrome pictures, while demonstrating effective use of tone, are not nearly as effective as the pictures that employ two colours or more.

## Publishing overseas and editions in other languages

The Neuraths were committed to ensuring that their work had international reach and took pains to ensure that books were distributed and promoted internationally. The 'Visual history' series provides a good example of this and there is evidence to suggest that it was wellreceived in Europe and further afield in the usA and India.² The series was shown in 195o in a German exhibition: 'Deutsche und englische Schülbucher der letzten 3o Jahre' where it elicited comments such as:

Concisely formulated questions, asking for an answer. Exceptionally good visual material: simple, symbolised, impressive, reduced to essentials, well arranged and therefore clear. Technical processes are better explained by visual material than by a lot of text.³º

We like the clear and impressive symbolism very much indeed and it seems most useful for teaching.³1

Many of the children's books were co-published through the us division of Adprint, Chanticleer Press. From around 195o Lothrop, Lee & Shepard, publisher of children's books, appears to have taken over from Chanticleer Press, and thereafter many of the co-editions were referred to as the Chanticleer edition'. 'Visual science', 'Wonders of the modern world', and the 'Wonder world of nature' were published in uK and us editions sometimes with different titles and covers, such as A message round the world (uk) and Around the world in a flash (us), and Inside the atom (UK) and Exploring the atom (Us)

Some overseas editions were characterized by the re-purposing of original charts. 'A new look at world history', for example, a threevolume series, first published in the usA and subsequently by Parrish in the UK, used charts and illustrations from the 'Visual history' and 'Visual Science' series. The approach taken reflected different pedagogical

![](images/70dc3b07fbacbe28241883775fa475129298fe7d9b55393500be784aec972d50.jpg)

![](images/c6f3bbbd1a514fb7bc678d56818a838ac1352b767793691d8eda17437e7ceb74.jpg)

Marie Neurath and J. A. Lauwerys, 'Visual history of mankind', Living in early times (London: Max Parrish, 1948), chart 8.

Cover and pages from Marie Neurath, Il meraviglioso mondo degli animali (Milan: Fratelli Fabbri Editori, 1958).

![](images/2892c239adab7b61d5d050b68a5296af5a07849db74bdcec9085da3b9ced966c.jpg)

![](images/0f2402eb71aa5322c2ad294cf1fd29d5bb09d60411cf083f38925817dc043e73.jpg)

methods in the two countries. Some of the charts from the ‘Visual history' were used, but without the sets of questions that accompanied them; the publisher opted instead for explanatory narrative text, and captions to describe the content of the chart. This was explained by Waldemar Kaempffert to Marie Neurath:

I notice that the charts are to be used in connection with a series of questions. In this country [the usA] the answers are given somewhere in such a book. I do not know whether or not this is a good principle. Most of our books for children (that is educational books) present a simple descriptive text. At the end of each chapter are a series of questions which are based on the text and which are to be answered with its aid.³²

In other examples of re-purposing, some European publishers changed both the format and style of the illustrations so that, although they clearly derived from Marie Neurath's work, they used different colours and introduced more representational and figurative elements. Others used the same diagrams, but in different configurations.

32. Letter Kaempffert to Marie Neurath, 23 May 1948 (IC 1/47) Kaempffert (Otto Neurath's cousin) was a regular correspondent with Marie Neurath and offered much encouragement for publishing in the USA.

Many of the children's books appeared in translation in several European languages including French, Italian, German, Swedish, Danish, and Dutch, thus furthering the reach of the Institute's work. Some incorporated changes in style of illustration and in configuration, as mentioned above, but in many cases printing of the foreign-language editions was done simply by changing the black printing plate; the coloured pages were kept the same to keep costs down.

![](images/189f114212af276c2e79b8fa4f80e0e0afeed5205e92a4afad5318f4aa71246f.jpg)  
Cover and pages from Marie Neurath, Manmade moons (London: Max Parrish, 196o).  
Cover and pages from Marie Neurath, Über Raketen und Raumschiffe (Klagenfurt: Verlag Buch und Welt, 1971).

Publication outside Europe seems to have been more difficult. Isotype correspondence reveals that Marie Neurath had numerous enquiries from India, Indonesia and other countries overseas asking for translation rights. Most of these requests did not come to fruition because the publishers were unable to reach agreement over fees. An exception to this was the Nippon Hyoron-Sha Publishing company. In early 195o the company's representative, Goro Yoshida, sent a letter to Marie Neurath praising Isotype work, of which he had become aware through Japanese translations of International picture language and Modern man in the making.³³ Marie Neurath helped him acquire translation rights for the first five Isotype children's books, and to secure an agreement between Max Parrish and Nippon Hyoron-Sha that they could be produced and published in Japan.³⁴ During the the production process, Marie Neurath commented to Yoshida: 'I like the proof prints, and I am glad that you have the best possible help and advice for a good translation.'³⁵ Yoshida also asked her for a 'message' to children, teachers and parents and she replied:

33. Letter Goro Yoshida to Otto and Marie Neurath, 1 February 1950; English translation of Esperanto original (IC 1/29).

34. Letter Max Parrish to Goro Yoshida, 14 June 1950, and draft agreement (IC 1/29).

35. Letter Marie Neurath to Goro Yoshida, 28 September 1950 (ic 1/29). However, In a letter to Yoshida of 4 July 195o, thanking him for sending the first, completed Japanese editions, Marie Neurath wrote 'I am very interested in them and am glad to have them. They hardly look like their originals – nothing of the picture text style has been preserved'.

![](images/b26b08f1e1e8ff05a3a325d288df41245f41f237f31f0221bf114fad78c1e24a.jpg)  
Equivalent pages from the English and Japanese editions of If you could see inside.

![](images/d516b43331c2c1ba5533babb5d5124e0ddde0fc4e5b25eacc591faab72aa3dba.jpg)  
Covers and pages from the Japanese editions of If you could see inside and 'Visual history of mankind', Living in the world.

![](images/e3b42ecb2f63279eb7d5b92a95de8fe88f9c24086499612ce9ac662ef01e0676.jpg)

![](images/5898e1ba5e50e6538f11e824b7dc68b5914a817de20388d3f98c375e1a100ec3.jpg)

![](images/1e843e9cf306234cd2a579dccb9e4331536137a4c693133078466fa3ad4ad5e8.jpg)

![](images/c07defcb8b3f14d6016456aef3747097f0d10e50905f2d2916d6892381fce039.jpg)

I should like to say that we want to make the learning through the eye an enjoyment; that we hope that the children will like to look at our charts and puzzle them out and will be encouraged to look around and make observations and discover how things happen. Pictures are good from another point of view, they can cross language barriers. Words divide – pictures unite.³⁶

36. Letter Marie Neurath to Goro Yoshida, 28 September 1950 (IC 1/29).

The Japanese editions of If you could see inside, I'll show you how it happens, and the 'Visual history' series were published in 195o in large print runs: 30,000 each of the first two, and 20,000 of the latter. But it was not to be a success. A letter from Max Parrish & Co. to Marie Neurath in 1954 explained that the Japanese publisher had gone bankrupt, and included some sales figures which showed that considerably less than half of the print run for each title had been sold.³7

## Feedback and review

Getting feedback as the books were being made, by asking experts in particular fields to comment on working drawings, was a key part in the design process for the children's books. Marie Neurath also requested feedback and comment on the books after they had been published and seems to have sent out many copies for review. Isotype Institute correspondence contains comments from teachers, librarians, and publishers.

There was much positive feedback, but what stands out is the care and attention that her correspondents took in providing this, and particularly noteworthy is that many of the comments took account of the views of children. In one letter, for example, a mother reported her eight-year old son's opinion of the first two 'Visual science' books: ‘They are wizard books – I can read them by myself – I don't need help from anyone – and the little pictures and drawings are easy to follow. His mother went on: He spends a lot of time with my own set of Children's Encyclopaediae – but so often the diagrams are rather complicated and he has to appeal to the grown-ups, who naturally can't always stop what they are doing to explain the difficulties of diagrams etc.' 38

By the early 195os Marie Neurath and her colleagues were receiving feedback as a result of the books being used in classrooms. A letter from a school inspector, Hedwig Brookes, began by identifying 'two dangers in books of this kind' (referring to the 'Visual history of mankind').³ One was that while the wording of the questions could be simple throughout, children of average and below average ability may find it difficult to work out the answers from the charts; ⁴º and the second was that some of the charts were too complicated.⁴¹ She continued, however, that these were minor points of criticism and went on to list seven points in their favour, the most important in her view was inculcating a sense of proportion about time'. She concluded: 'Above all, the terrible bookish approach to history is jettisoned'.

An interesting observation by Hedwig Brookes in her letter to Marie Neurath was that several teachers she had shown the books to had never seen them before. In her reply, Marie Neurath also expressed her feeling that they were not well enough known (even though 10,00o

37. Letter and accompanying sales figures, Max Parrish & Co. Ltd to Marie Neurath, 14 June 1954 (1C 2/1).

38. Letter Miss Chester (Secretary to Professor Born, Department of Mathematical Physics at the University of Edinburgh) to Marie Neurath, 18 December 1951 (1C 1/24).

39. Letter Hedwig Brookes to Marie Neurath, 21 January 1951 (1C 1/24).

40. Others also expressed this concern: 'The text, which is based on the isotype pictures, consists almost entirely of questions, demanding from the pupils great concentration on the details of the illustrations. Many may prove to be much too difficult for pupils of this age.' The Middlesex Log, July 1952.

41. The example given by Hedwig Brookes is chart 13 in 'Visual history', Living in the world, and it is indeed the case that the intersecting coloured lines depicting how different fruits move around the world are hard to follow.

42. The 'Visual history of mankind' continued to appear in Max Parrish catalogues until at least 1965, though by this date the series received less prominence than, for example, 'Wonders of the modern world', 'The wonder world of nature', and 'They lived like this' By the 1g6os, however, editions of the 'Visual history' series were being produced with far fewer colours and, because of this, may have been less appealing and more difficult to understand than the original editions, in which colours were chosen and used with much care. (See the examples on p. 408 above.)

43. 'He [Mr Simpson, Coventry City Librarian] has bought 20 copies of each book on publication, with the exception of "Inside" [If you could see inside] and "Happens" [Ill show you how it happens]. Those two titles were published before Miss Fox, now in charge of children's libraries, was appointed. Miss Fox saw them for the first time yesterday and made a note to order 2o copies of each.' Typewritten transcript by M. H. Chandler, 18 December 1951 (1C 2/4).

copies had been sold). It is difficult to find out, beyond such anecdotal evidence, how widely the 'Visual history of mankind' series was used in schools.42 The London County Council list of books, maps, music and diagrams approved for use in schools maintained by the Council, for example, did not include it, though ‘Visual science' appears in the 1954 edition, as well as other Isotype books in the second supplement to the 1954 edition (published in 1955).

A particularly interesting document is a transcript of an interview with the Coventry City Librarian and his assistant in 1951. This was based on observations of nearly all the Isotype children's books published to that date.4³ The comments about their visual, linguistic and material attributes were varied and likely to have been very useful for Marie Neurath and her team, as well as to the publishers. On binding, for example, the librarians favoured cloth-bound books because, although more expensive, they lasted longer; they recommended that the title of the book should be stamped on the front of the cloth cover as well as on the spine ... This enables the librarian to display it in such a way that children soon get to know a) that it is available and b) what it is about.' On the typography, they thought that the typesize in Rockets and jets was just right for bright children of about 9 to 12', and particularly appreciated the 'generous leading'; but they thought that the two-column setting used in How the first men lived and The first great inventions was not appropriate for children. In commenting on the language used they noted that the text is more human and less textbookish than is usually the case in books of this kind', and that short sentences were beneficial. Both the librarian and his assistant put forward the view that children prefer books about particular topics, rather than ‘a medley of subjects' such as was presented in I'll show you how it happens and If you could see inside. It seemed that some young children visiting the library were attracted to the Isotype illustrations: 'one or two children, on returning Isotype books to the library have said: “Please have you got another book with pictures like these?".' Adults, however, were not always impressed by the pictures.

## Marie Neurath's legacy

The working material for Isotype children's books, together with the published editions, demonstrate Marie Neurath's ingenuity and skill as a transformer. As part of her process of designing she incorporated feedback from experts in particular subjects so that she could be sure

that her explanations were scientifically and factually accurate, and from the users of the materials she designed – the teachers and children who read the books. Such attention to detail, when combined with Isotype principles of clarity and consistency, resulted in some remarkable Isotype charts, such as those in the 'Visual history' and 'Visual science' series. More generally, though the books themselves now appear somewhat dated, they highlight attributes that are surely still relevant to designing effectively for children: consistency in the use of pictograms and other graphic images, and in the use of colour to enhance the meaning of an explanation; the use of child-friendly language and encouragement of children to engage with the illustrations; and the balance of just enough detail in the pictures to meet with a child's expectations. Moreover, the high quality of the books is evident from the standard of drawing and the attention to detail in their production. Inevitably there was compromise to be reached in terms of production standards: a commercially acceptable product was not compatible with some of the ambitious ideas that the Neuraths had, and this had some consequences on the effectiveness of some charts. But despite this, even today, the books display a fresh, vibrant quality.

The children's books demonstrate the value of engaging different kinds of people with different ideas and skills in the process of designing. This approach was ahead of its time, and is in many ways a more important legacy than the books themselves.

The following pages illustrate examples of Marie Neurath's working method, and a selection of pages from the main series of children's books produced by the Isotype Institute. This is a mere snapshot of what was produced, but it represents the range and ingenuity of this aspect of her work.

Unless otherwise stated, all books shown here were written by Marie Neurath, published in London by Max Parrish, and measure 220× 190 mm.

## Working method

The first stage in the design of all the children's books was the gathering together of background material. Many of the concepts in the books were technically and scientifically complicated, and understanding and accuracy were prereguisites for both the verbal and the visual explanations. Marie Neurath worked closely with experts in whatever area the topic fell. Such background information provided context for the transformers and designers, as well as technical detail. Marie Neurath and her team worked through ideas in the form of notes and sketches. Many of these were made in duplicate books that were landscape in format and approximated the actual size of the double-page spread of the finished book. The use of such duplicate books provided a monochrome record of the 'top copy', which was produced with coloured pencils and was used by the graphic artist to make the artwork for printing. Getting the words right was also an important part of the transformer's role, and Isotype working material shows evidence of careful drafting and iteration. Once the text was finalized it was sent for typesetting, based on a specification arising from the sketched pages, and returned as galley proofs for use in the final page artwork.

Sketch and resulting pages from Marie Neurath and J.A. Lauwerys, 'Visual science' Plants and animals (1951).

This sketch from one of the duplicate books shows the careful and detailed way that the transformer worked, and how the relationship between text and picture across the double-page spread was considered at this stage.

Sketch and resulting pages from Marie Neurath and J. A. Lauwerys, Exploring under the sea (1958). (IC 3.2/122)

The sketches were not always followed closely. In this example the left page has been adapted to provide room for the text.

Sketches and resulting pages from The wonder world of land and water (1957), (IC 3.2/156)

Detailed pencil drawings, and finished visuals using gouache and mechanical tints, show further development in preparation for printing. Many of the sketches include Marie Neurath's annotations, which show attention to detail and to technical accuracy.

Part of an amended and annotated typescript for The wonder world of land and water, 14 November 1956 (IC 3.2/156).

A typical example of the re-drafting and revision that Marie Neurath undertook. Note also that this functioned as a partial type specification detailing the number of lines in each block of text and the width of the line in ems.

## Jobbing Rack: F84 Fountains of Boiling Water

Sometimes strange fountains may be found near volcanoes. Steam and water burst out from a hole in the carth. Then the fountain dies down, and the water flows hack into the hot rocks underneath

## Gush out of the Ground

Soon the water begins to boil again. Steam tries to press out, and finally it throws out the column of water and rushes out itself. Now it no longer presses, and the water can flow back

## How the Fire U nder the Earth

When a volcano bursts into action under the sea, it can build itself up rapidly until its top may rise above the waves. Then red-hoi rocks and ashes are thrown up and fal back in the sea.

## Builds an Island in the Sea

Such a volcano, when it is dead', makes an island in the sea. and plants and animals and people can live on it. Sometimes, we think, islands like this have dlisappeared under the sea again.

## When the Solid Earth Cracks and Quakes

Sometimes a sudden crack below the surfacc of the carth shakes the layers around it so violenthy that it causes an earthquake. T'his may knock down houses. or even destroy whole cities. When this happens, cracks can appear in roads and fields. and the shaking is usuall worst nearest to the place

Galley proof showing part of the typesetting for The wonder world of land and water (ic 3.2/156)

An example of typesetting for a number of pages in the book. The alignment of the headings and the length of the lines of type was such that it fitted with the illustration to which it referred.

![](images/763406b45ad15a4cb08439726870ba85213a88e2e33250ba241882118215b455.jpg)

Sketches and resulting title page for The wonder world of birds (1953). (IC 3.2/150)

Considerable attention went into the design of parts of the book outside the main text, including title pages.

![](images/3cb182e56821899c1ecab3c4da44575a988625f4dfee3f560783405616290066.jpg)

The cross-section of a volcano shows the hot central core, lava pouring down the mountain side, and the eruption of hot lava and air. The fleeing figure provides a powerful visual statement.

![](images/7304368b309fac1d1e3165faac97c0e71ec9a0abe1fbcb769f9673d23e90fcda.jpg)

WE. have all been inside a house, but how different it looks when it ts cut through with our magsc knife! Here you can see how long the #1

chimneys really are, how the water gets to the tap, and where rt runs when you pull the bath-plug. Deep foundatsons make the house strong.

![](images/616825107cc62abf0db5c72e5317cfc588362495c00d20bb05e9c7b8ec8201c9.jpg)

Souf wasps build thcir nests under the eaves of a house. First the queen wasp grinds tiny scraps of wood between her saws into stuff called "wasyp-paper". With this she makes a pillar, hanging from the roof. and auks to it a round platform with many thimble. shaped cells beneath it where she lays her eggs. She adds a balloon-shaped covering to complete the nest. As the eggs hatch, the new wasps add more platfonms take off the old covering. and make a larger new one.

The analogy of a 'magic knife' is used in the text to explain a cross-section of a house: ‘We have all been inside a house, but how different it looks when it is cut through with our magic knife!'

![](images/838585c511cc43bcceff09353a2c9f714d3d1f6f7fb1aceb05bf063ffe0ce1ff.jpg)

The irregular placement of the wasps adds interest to this spread.

![](images/376b21d2b31870a33881011d27315ad3d3f4660ec1f303c1d59006face48343b.jpg)

The yellow diagonal lines represent the sun's rays and the black shaded area the part of the earth facing away from the sun. The pages are identical at first glance but, when the text directs the reader to look at the little house, the explanation becomes clear.

Consistent placing of the sequential illustration – top to bottom, left to right – directs the reader more effectively than the text, which does not have a consistent arrangement.

The mechanism that makes Tower Bridge work is emphasized by the use of red, and explained in the accompanying text. However, the typesetting across the full width of the spread is likely to have made reading difficult for children.

## A Platform Built for Speed

![](images/2a8e0485d2bd069fad7051847e7f3948128145651a973976e36794681bf8425e.jpg)  
the la ing  do t t pasn  tr w qul. Inlicators show where the nest train in guing: notieea show the way ant, and

![](images/169a40534937742b143fd9e948587a982053fe65345194f8e1a7a47cb947a488.jpg)  
This diagram explains how escalators work. A full explanation requires the reader to engage with the text, which is written in a very child-friendly way.

A clear explanation of how the incline of the track helps trains to slow down and speed up at an underground station. The larger picture shows what sources of information and help passengers can expect to find on a platform.

![](images/5acad392e701872b6269e823586c26a387e393c39edfc8a1259b5bf2a3f1453d.jpg)

![](images/ae581d8652589aee49428c40a9582bcf5d7815b29dff7d008add67495ecc1885.jpg)

Sketches for and resulting pages from Railways under London. (IC 3.2/113)

These sketches show the development of an explanation of how pneumatic brakes function. The first diagram shows the workings of the brakes, using four colours to represent different kinds of air; the second is one of a number of revised drawings. There is a distinction made between 'high pressure air' and reduced pressure air' through the use of purple and blue. In the printed version this distinction has been abandoned, with blue alone representing compressed air. To work this out, the reader has to read the text; there is no key to the colours.

Series cover size: 215 × 200 mm.

Otto & Marie Neurath and J. A. Lauwerys, Living in the world, chart 16.

A matrix configuration shows changes in the appearance of several inventions according to the periods indicated by guidepictures of clothing style. The use of generations to indicate periods of time makes a link with some of the other charts.

## Living in the world, chart 14.

A pictorial bar chart using pictograms to represent food sources at particular periods of time. The questions help readers to work out what the colours mean, such as: 'What colour is used to show each of the following: products found naturally; domesticated animals; cultivated crops; imported foodstuffs?' Black, brown, green, and blue are used respectively.

## Living in the world, chart 5.

A pictorial bar chart using guide-pictures of buildings to represent different eras. The wavy blue lines are divided into sections that represent 100 miles a day. The ships are placed on the lines to show distance travelled in one day. Consistent use of yellow for the sails of the wind-powered ships encourages comparison of the shapes and numbers of sails on each ship.

Otto & Marie Neurath and J. A. Lauwerys, Living in villages and towns, chart 5.

![](images/2ba974c583230ffdee96df16a7a0183af29bc4b8ed8af0d702113a87dbf360f2.jpg)

Living in the world, chart 18.

A map of the world is used to underpin a variety of graphic presentations in this book and, as with all Isotype maps, it is an equalarea projection. In this example, the Isotype 'man' pictogram is used to show population in different parts of the world.

Living in villages and towns, chart 13.

Schematic maps are used here to show the relationship and relative size of land masses (Britain, Continental Europe), to act as a backdrop for explanations. This approach was used only occasionally.

## Living in early times, chart 7.

In this pictorial array, there is no prescribed, presumed, or articulated direction of reading. Colour is used straightforwardly – blue for metal, brown for wood.

Marie Neurath and J.A. Lauwerys, Second book (1950).   
Series cover size:   
215× 200 mm.

In her article, 'Report on the last years of Isotype work' (Synthese, vol.8, nos.1/2, 1950–1, pp. 22–7), Marie Neurath explained the rationale behind some of the charts in the 'Visual science' books. These explanations show the thinking behind the visual solutions, and how the likely understanding of children was taken into account. The captions below and on p. 432 are extracts from the article.

![](images/f5247b6080f28f8de7cf323805733ea353735f6333b0785bb2c6ca08a9f19bd8.jpg)

'The science charts also start from the familiar. The law of gravity is only a mystery on a theoretical level. The children have become used to things falling, and we can use this experience to make some general statements about falling things, and thus induce them to make more detailed observations without mentioning the word gravity. They know

see-saws well, and if we show boys on see-saws in various arrangements it puts their former and perhaps future experience into order. And if we show certain kinds of tools at the side of it, something which is similar in them, and some rules about levers may become obvious without being fully explained in the orthodox way.' (pp. 24-5)

![](images/1bcd7efbd9d91f06c5b8b3592b9a7a097b0f109c9338745b8f7b7deef5b347f9.jpg)  
Marie Neurath and J.A. Lauwerys, First book (1950).

'Floating in water, or feeling oneself lighter in water, are part of children's experience. We can go on to ask: which things sink, which are suspended, which float on top? And we can go on to make a visual statement which makes use of a simplified balance to tell "lighter" and "heavier than water" and "of the same weight”, at the side of a picture which shows

"heavier” at the bottom of a vessel filled with water (which, in Isotype, is shown as a section of a vessel with a wavy line texture inside), and the "lighter" sticking out of the surface of the water; the one of "equal weight" being somewhere in the middle. Such statements take less space visually than verbally, and certainly require less effort to be understood.' (p. 5)

![](images/84bfeb0a82100723c82b72c44a36d6d1378167e75179da7b0881256fe49142ed.jpg)

'We have made a chart: "air is needed for burning”, "less air slows burning down", "more air makes burning stronger and quicker". We have now made another chart which modifies this, and states, that it is not true for all sorts of air, and that there are different sorts. We had to introduce a symbolism which distinguishes between the different materials, for example we represent oxygen by a red dot texture, carbon by black shapes, carbon dioxide by red and black dots; and enlargement of the texture can tell how the arrangement of atoms is changed in the chemical process of burning.' (p. 27)

![](images/e2cf24795a1afdafd6d9bcdd3462ba96e43181ddafbad5a77fce9e4fb431f62a.jpg)

'We have to show anything about which we want to make visual statements. There is for instance the story of water: ice – water – steam. We have designed symbols for these. We can even make the statement: steam is invisible. We made two pictures side by side, one symbolic, the other naturalistic: – we show in our symbolism stipples for steam, droplets for condensing part – which in fact looks like this: – a kettle with a cloud some distance from the spout.' (p. 25)

![](images/97023c510a36548fc293e76426a21ff983c04abd8cbe95d99ed2d274b164c855.jpg)

![](images/66d69748e9161940047ec9d626c58320196cfdb6633fe4cefc193723f695d8bf.jpg)

## Fire (1950).

A cross-section is used to show the inside of a burning house. Colour and tone are carefully used. The bright green ladders highlight how the firemen access the building when the stairs are obstructed. Blue is used for water, and red for fire throughout the book.

## Building big things (1958).

When the fly presses with ts claws, the sticks pad and hairs are released and then it can wah ou fs awas

Sixty million years ago the first horses, no bigger tlian growing puppies, padded through the hot jungle, cropping the green plants. They had four toes on the fore feet and three on the hind ones, and small hooves. They were at the mercy of giant birds, taller than the talest man. Much later, when the earth was cooler and the forests less dense, horses were larger. They now had three toes on each foot and could run very well. Though. they were no longer eaten by giant birds, they were uften attacked by savage animals. Ages passed by and the earth's climatc altered. Where forests once grew there were now grassy plains. On these lived horses much more like those we see to-day. Now only one toe on each foot touched the ground. With long legs and strong hooves, they were swift, powerful runners.

## Tree-top Plants in Which Animals Live

![](images/1f806e44ab99fe04d57239bb0948af5305b4f0300bb38ce1e8fe7af4c0c9509d.jpg)  
This plant of the pineapple family cannot live on the ground in tropical forests, shut out from the sun. Instead it grows high up on a tree-branch, which its roots grip for support. It takes in water through the leaves alonc, which form a cup to catch the rain. In this tree-top pond, tree-frogs and mosquitoes lay their cggs, tadpoles and grubs start their life. Very soon, dead leaves and insects form soil between the roots, on which the plant can feed.

![](images/c89e9f6de287dbf0bb20e1f16294e478ef8748474242fb367062a2fa231257b1.jpg)

The wonder world of long ago (1957).

This pictorial timeline can be read in three parts, or as one long sequence. The trees in the green backgrounds diminish in size as the environment changes, while the shape and size of the horses change, too.

The wonder world of trees and flowers (1961).

A fish stops making scales when it is stiłl

He leaves skin, fur and bones all over the place. But once the lion is out of sight, the hyena comes out of hiding and gobbles them up.

All through your life dead skin is peeling off your body and new skin is growing in its place. How is it you can grow these new parts? Do you add them yourself, like blocks to the tower? Of course not!

## Future Books & Future magazine 1946–52

The four 'Future Books' and five subsequent volumes (29 numbers) of Future magazine addressed many facets of post-war economic and social development principally in Britain but also elsewhere, especially in Britain's overseas territories. The books were published in 1946; numbers of the magazine were published between 1947 and early 1952. They were produced by Adprint, under the direction of Wolfgang Foges and his production manager Walter Neurath (subsequently founder of Thames & Hudson). The integration of text, graphic, and photographic content in both the books and the magazine, much of it in colour and including many Isotype contributions, was sophisticated and engaging.

The Isotype Institute became involved in Adprint's 'periodical project' (as Otto Neurath called it) in early 1944. From the start, Fortune magazine, the lavish, large-format business monthly published in the usA, was taken as a point of comparison and a model. Neurath envisioned the proposed periodical as a 'symposium', to which Isotype would make a substantial contribution through charts and other graphics that were partly self-standing, offering a counterpoint to the articles they accompanied rather than simply illustrating them. But Neurath also understood that the new periodical should not contain too much Isotype work in general, or pictorial statistics in particular. Whatever was included should be 'informative, educational and explanatory' and visually impressive.

Although Otto Neurath directed work on lsotype contributions to Future Books, his death in December 1945 meant he did not see them reach publication. But these, and subsequent contributions to Future magazine made under the direction of Marie Neurath, would by the end of the magazine's run amount to perhaps the most varied body

of Isotype work within a single project. Graphically, it encompassed different kinds of maps (thematic, relief, cross-sectional), illustrated diagrams and views, organízational charts, and pictorial statistics. In places, principally in the magazine, the work employed features untypical of isotype, suggesting a loosening (or compromising) of its rules in search of novelty and impact. The work was also technically varied in response to the several methods of print production employed for both Future Books and Future magazine, which alternated between (and usually combined) photogravure, lithography, and letterpress, and monochrome, spot colours, and 4-colour process, all a consequence of the complex printing and logistical arrangements adopted by Adprint.

In total, the Isotype Institute contributed to each of the four Future Books, and to roughly two-thirds of the magazine numbers. Under the art editorship of Bauhaus-trained George Adams, which continued through the first half of the magazine's run, Isotype charts featured consistently and often prominently. But they increasingly shared space with what might be called 'information illustrations', many designed by Abram Games in a style similar to those commissioned by Fortune from Ladislav Sutnar, Herbert Bayer, and other designers in the usA. The use of such illustrations in Future was probably due in part to the encouragement of F. H. K. Henrion, who served as an editorial consultant under Adams, before replacing him as art editor. During Henrion's tenure, Isotype contributions decline in number and then largely disappear.

The Future Books and Future magazine were identical in format, with an average page size of 295 × 215 mm. The books were bound with a flexible hard cover; the magazine was soft cover. (εκ)

## British Council Study Boxes 1950–1

British Council Study Boxes presented information on a range of subjects relating to governance, health, education, and social and economic life and welfare in Britain. According to a British Council prospectus (1951–2), the boxes were produced for students and professional workers overseas who wished to 'acquaint themselves with British practice in their specialist fields'. They were classified as primarily for 'more advanced' or 'less well-developed' countries, depending on the subject addressed.

The subject of each Study Box was surveyed through a text written by an expert, complemented by books and pamphlets, specimen documents and official reports, wall charts, filmstrips and photographs, and a gramophone recording of a discussion/interview. The contents were assembled in a 'brown fiberite container, carried by a webbing strap, of a size that will allow it to be held comfortably under the arm'. The boxes were designed for group study, which was to take place during five or six meetings convened by an 'inexpert leader'. The leader was supported by a guidebook containing 'notes on discussion group method, group organisation, the use of teaching aids, a suggested syllabus, and a list of topics for discussion'.1

Two of the Study Boxes intended for advanced countries dealt with Britain's new National Health Service (in operation from July 1948), and industrial relations. The British Council commissioned the Isotype Institute to design a series of charts for both. The four charts for the box, ‘The National Health Service' (1g5o), employed a combination of diagrams and pictorial statistics to present the organization of the NHs, its estimated cost and personnel for 1949–5o, how a family accessed treatment, and how it was paid for. The five charts for the box, 'Industrial relations' (1951), employed similar means to show the function of the Ministry of Labour and National Service, trade union structure and growth in Britain, and the nature and resolution of industrial disputes.

In 1953–4, the British Council reported that 57 copies of 'The National Health Service' Study Box had been sent to 34 countries, while 74 copies of 'Industrial relations' had been sent to 37 countries.² It also noted that the NHs box was being recalled for renewal and revision'. The NHs charts were printed lithographically on card at crown size (15 x 2o in.); because it is unlikely that they were folded, this size may have established the size of the box overall. The charts on industrial relations were quad crown (3o x 4o in.); unusually for Isotype work, they were screen printed on newsprint, though the latter allowed them to be conveniently folded twice (French fold) to crown. (Eκ)

11 Isotype in Africa Gold Coast, Sierra Leone, and the Western Region of Nigeria 1952-8

Eric Kindel

Early in the development of the Vienna Method, its international aspects became plainly evident, at first in its content, and then in its spread to parts of Europe beyond Austria, to the Soviet Union, and to North America. In time, this internationalism was embedded in the Vienna Method's new designation, 'Isotype', invented just prior to the publication of Otto Neurath's International picture language. But if, in this book, which offers the fullest explanation of Isotype, Neurath argued that it was comprehensible internationally, the claim necessarily lacks force since in practice Isotype had not yet been used beyond the modernized West. Indeed, looking back over more than four decades of Isotype work, the question of how well it functioned in the developing, non-Western world is not easy to answer since few opportunities arose to deploy it there. But in the 195os, one opportunity did arise in British colonial West Africa where, under the direction of Marie Neurath, the Isotype Institute completed projects in the Gold Coast (Ghana), Sierra Leone, and the Western Region of Nigeria. Work in these territories, as each advanced towards independence, provides valuable evidence of Isotype in action at the farther reaches of the international sphere it was intended to serve.

## Africa not met

Before the Isotype Institute became involved in West Africa, Marie Neurath had never visited the continent; nor had Otto Neurath during his lifetime. Yet Africa presented a notional proving ground for Isotype, as Marie Neurath later recalled. 'Otto had said that we were creating the [Vienna] method not, finally, for the Viennese, but rather for the Africans'.¹ While Marie Neurath would eventually do precisely this, for Otto Neurath Africa remained unmet. In the context of her admittedly

1. Marie Neurath, 'What I remember', p. 9g. I have not found this exact statement in Otto Neurath's writing or correspondence. Reference to the Viennese suggests that Africa figured early on as an ultimate measure of success.

2. For Vienna Method / Isotype depictions of Africa and black people, see pp. 105, 214–15 above, 517 below. For additional discussion of colonial politics, cultural stereotypes and pictograms of humans, see Sybilla Nikolow: '"Society and Economy": an atlas in Otto Neurath's pictorial statistics from 193o', <economix.fr/pdf/journees/ hpe/2oo6-06-16\_Nikolow.pdf> (2006), pp. 6–8; and 'Gesellschaft und Wirtschaft: an encyclopedia in Otto Neurath's pictorial statistics from 1930', p. 270.

3. Otto Neurath, 'Visual education' (1945), pp. 264, 292, 333–4 (quotation at p. 334). Christopher Burke observes that 'on several occasions he [Neurath] described Isotype as a "bridge" connecting the illiterate and the literate'. See his introduction to Otto Neurath, From hieroglyphics to Isotype (2010), p. xv and n. 34; see also p. 201 above.

brief remark, it seems that Otto Neurath's conception of Africa was largely undifferentiated. Africa, singular, is invoked to identify a general audience to which the Vienna Method should be directed, an audience whose need for information and knowledge was apparently most pressing and whose benefit would be greatest. Separately but perhaps in parallel, Vienna Method depictions of Africa are also generalized, contributing (very intentionally) to statements about the world but without treating the continent or its people in any significantly differentiated way. Apart from summary physical or cultural features, Africa and Africans are mostly unspecific; more nuanced depictions were simply not required in the circumstances.²

If Otto Neurath's conception of Africa took on general and summary forms in the context of Vienna Method work, there is evidence that later his thinking about Isotype's relationship to less developed parts of the world became more specific, at least operationally. In his text, 'Visual education', written between 1943 and 1945, Neurath considered how visual aids could assist those he identified as semi-literates, of whom there might be a large proportion in colonial territories. Visual aids such as posters and 'picture sheets' containing health instructions or information about agriculture or citizenship, accompanied by a minimum of words, could be more effective, in a shorter time, than teaching through reading and writing, which because of its demands and relatively slow results might lead to feelings of inferiority. Visual aids had the advantage of being non-technical, in that their use did not require special equipment or indeed a power supply, something even large villages might be without. Neurath also recognized that in colonial territories visual education could overcome another kind of illiteracy brought about by a lack of vernacular words and expressions able to convey modern (Western) concepts. While it was difficult to adapt vernacular languages to this purpose, much less introduce a Western language, pictures and symbolic presentations' might instead 'overcome the gap.'

Otto Neurath's predictions for the effectiveness of visual aids in circumstances of colonial literacy, while insightful, were also mostly conjectural since they remained an extrapolation of his own experiences in Europe and North America. But it transpires that his thoughts on the matter were not limited to those he recorded in ‘Visual education'; rather, they coincide with other work then underway that would draw him into closer proximity to Africa and Africans. This work was a project for an exhibition about Britain's colonies, in which the

![](images/872f84a2bf76c90448f1ca19dbf130a49aee1b7cce861d3a80bbfba71b609c31.jpg)  
Otto Neurath, sketch in a letter to Paul Rotha, 24 May 1943 (IC 1/44).

Isotype Institute became involved in late 1942. The exhibition had been proposed to the British Colonial Office by H.V. Meyerowitz, a trained artist, teacher, educationalist, and friend of the documentary filmmaker Paul Rotha.4 The initial idea was in fact for a pair of exhibitions, one permanently displayed in London, the other (a duplicate) to travel. Rotha had been asked to help 'from a film point of view' and he, in turn, recommended that the Isotype Institute be put in overall charge. In early 1943, a committee was formed to develop the proposal; in addition to Meyerowitz, Rotha and Neurath, and representatives from the Colonial Office and Ministry of Information, the committee also included Sir Hanns Vischer of the International Institute of African Languages & Cultures.⁵

Following meetings among committee members, Otto Neurath assembled a draft description of the aims, content, and arrangement of the exhibition under the title 'Human life in Africa'. The exhibition plan had evolved into something that would now strike a balance between Britain and Africa: it would be informed by the view that Africa should not be treated as an ethnographic curiosity or characterized yet again by a savage' ideology; instead, Britain and Africa would be presented as similar since life in both places incorporated many of the same essential features, if not always in the same way or to the same extent. The exhibition's aim, in a phrase Neurath often employed,

4. Herbert Vladimir Meyerowitz (190o-45) was at this time supervisor of arts and crafts at Achimota College, near Accra in the Gold Coast. In his teaching, Meyerowitz aimed to 'develop modern art and design rooted in local techniques'. He was also instrumental in establishing the Institute of West African Arts, Industries, and Social Science, which opened in 1943,

linked to Achimota College. The institute's effort to direct local craft work into larger scale production had attracted the support of the Colonial Office, which was seeking to develop indigenous industries throughout British colonial West Africa. See [Meyerowitz], 'The institute of West African Arts, Industries, and Social Science', Man, vol. 43, Sep.-Oct., 1943.

pp. 112–14; 'The Institute of West African Arts, Industries and Social Science', Journal of the Royal African Society, vol. 42, no. 16g, Oct., 1943, pp. 183-90; and Tanya Harrod, The last sane man: Michael Cardew, modern pots, colonialism and the counterculture (London & New Haven: Yale University Press, 2012), pp. 153-7, 193-5 (quotation above at p. 155).

5. Letter Rotha to Neurath, 28 December 1942 and 21 January 1943 (IC 1/44). The Colonial Office was represented by Sir Ralph Furse and Noel Sabine; the Ministry of Information by Jervas Huxley. Hanns Vischer (1876–1945), specialist in education in colonial Africa and an authority on Nigeria, was secretary general of the International Institute of African Languages & Cultures (later renamed the International African Institute).

6. 'Human life in Africa', July 1943 (Ic 1/44); the document carries two other headings: 'Isotype Institute first draft' and 'For Rotha Films'. While this and associated documents mostly refer to an undifferentiated Africa, it seems correct to assume that the content of 'Human life in Africa' would relate to Britain's West Africa colonies. Notably, in an order of 26 January 1943 to the press clippings agency Romeike & Curtis (London), Neurath requested items relating to 'West Africa, colonial problem, Africans, administration etc. Gold Coast, etc etc'. (IC 1/6)

7. The concept of 'humanization of knowledge' recurs in Neurath's contemporary writing; see especially 'Visual education' (1945), pp. 254-9, where it is explained.

8. 'Human life in Africa' (Ic 1/44). The arrangement of the exhibition would follow a familiar Neurath form, 'like an onion', comprising a core, with elements that could travel or be used in 'corner shop exhibitions' reminiscent of sites employed in Vienna. Within the exhibition itself, a 'contents room' would introduce and lead into the exhibition. Visual aids would include Isotype charts, documentary photographs, and possibly Isotype models; viewer-controlled films and question & answer apparatus (like that used in the 1938 exhibition 'Rondom Rembrandt') might also be employed. Neurath envisioned a seriousness of intent that did not preclude attractiveness, charm, and entertainment; the exhibition should offer spectacle as a means of encouraging interest, engagement, and understanding. An Isotype exhibition guide would be available for visitors to take away.

9. Letter Neurath to Vischer,   
7 August 1943 and 2 September   
1943 (ic 1/6); emphasis in original.   
10. Letter Neurath to Vischer,   
12 August 1943 (IC 1/6).

would be the 'humanization of knowledge'; through shared means, the people of Britain and Africa would learn about what they had in common and about their differences.' The arts, industry, technology, education, health, private life and history in both places would be presented and compared. No concessions would be made to differences among those attending, whether in Britain or in Africa; rather, the exhibition would be a meeting on (more or less) equal terms, through communication techniques capable of reaching both audiences. In sum, 'the cosmopolitan pure human approach should prevail. Human brotherhood as a start.'8

After submitting the draft of 'Human life in Africa' to the Colonial Office and Ministry of Information, Neurath and the Isotype Institute waited while the plan was considered. During this time, Neurath continued corresponding with Vischer who proved to be both a helpful source of information about Africa and someone to whom Neurath felt a strong personal and intellectual attraction. In these letters, Neurath repeats his long-standing wish that Africans might encounter Isotype: 'I feel very stimulated by thinking of the planned exhibition on African Life. It is a particular[ly] nice idea to think that the Africans will look at our isotypes some day, ... who did not learn too much of Western languages'; and 'For years we hoped that some day Isotypes would be used for Non-European countries, and now you and your institute are just interested in such activities'.º Neurath was excited by the possibility that Isotype work would be seen ‘in one mould' by Europeans and Africans, clearly as a kind of test or demonstration of its suitability for both. Mention by Vischer of the importance of local museums in Africa gave Neurath further ideas, such as establishing visual education centres that would act like lending libraries, or organizing museums in Africa and Britain to collect material characterizing life in each place, which could then be exchanged through barter.1º Reflecting on these ideas, Neurath admitted that 'I myself have not much knowledge of African life' but affirmed that the exhibition had sparked in him some considerable excitement to find out more.

In November 1943, Vischer regretfully informed Neurath that the exhibition plans had been put on hold and would be reviewed again in six months. Neurath had been prepared for this and agreed with Vischer that a postponement would allow them to develop their plans more fully, reiterating his feeling that [as one's] own sensibility is increasing, one looks at Africa in a different way, when one imagines that some day perhaps Isotypes blended with photos will appear educating people

there and here.'" In mid-1944, Vischer invited Neurath to join the International African Institute (1ai), as it was now called, which it appears Neurath did, thereafter attending several lectures. By November, Neurath mentioned that 'he was becoming more and more interested in the educational problems' raised by the 'African-Europe exhibition' and that he now wanted to meet Africans to hear their views on pictures of an informative character, including Isotype work.¹² Two months later, in early 1945, Neurath asked Vischer for his help in organizing such a get-together,1³ and by mid-February Vischer had arranged for him to meet twelve African students at the IAI later that month. As Neurath was preparing material, he received unexpected news of Vischer's death; the meeting was cancelled and never re-scheduled. Without his connection to Vischer, it appears that Neurath's involvement in the 1Ai came to at end.¹4 There is no indication of what direction, if any, Neurath's interest in Africa took thereafter or if he made further progress in his research prior to his own death later that year.

## First contact

As these events show, Otto Neurath clearly wished to engage with Africa and Africans to discover what insights such encounters might bring to Isotype. If, for Neurath, this wish went unrealized, some years later an opportunity was presented to Marie Neurath and the Isotype Institute to do work in Africa. The work would be associated with Britain's three West African colonies, the Gold Coast, Sierra Leone, and Nigeria, where in the immediate post-war period a process of increased self-determination and de facto decolonization was gathering pace. The working arrangement wculd be a partnership linked to Adprint, the book packaging company with which the Isotype Institute had maintained a productive relationship for almost a decade.¹⁵ The partnership would be built around a new Adprint subsidiary, Buffalo Books, set up in 1951.16 The intention was to draw together the complementary expertise of Adprint's book packaging capabilities, the Isotype Institute's well-established and 'internationally recognised system of conveying

the published edition). There is no mention of a final decision on the exhibition but it was presumably abandoned some time in 1944. 13. Letter Otto Neurath to Hanns Vischer, 16 January 1945. (IC 1/11) 14. In a letter of condolence to the lAi, Neurath wrote 'would you be kind enough to tell his

15. A notable product of this relationship was the Isotype Institute's work on 'Future Books' and Future magazine, which included contributions to articles on Africa and Britain's African colonies. See especially 'Nigeria: test case of empire', Future, [vol. 2, no. 1, 1947] ('Overseas number'), pp. 70–8o, which features Isotype charts and a map; and Future, vol. 3, 'Special number: 'Trade with the colonies', 1948.

16. Wolfgang Foges, material relating to Aldus Books, including a summary of his firms' output dictated by Foges; see remarks under 'Buffalo Books', dictated 27–31 Oct 1983 (Wiener Library, London [hereinafter wLL], item no. 1361/29–85). The origin of the name 'Buffalo Books' is not stated. Note that Foges's recollections are faulty in places, as suggested by Foges himself, who recorded them some 3o years after the events described and without supporting documents. See also David Lambert, 'Wolfgang Foges and the new illustrated book in Britain: Adprint, Rathbone Books, and Aldus Books' Typography Papers, 8: Modern typography in Britain, pp. 113-28.

12. Letter Neurath to Vischer, 24 November 1944 (Ic 1/11); Neurath also remarked: 'In writing my book "From hieroglyphics to Isotype" I mention also African drawings, of course only in passing, but reproducing examples' (e.g. pp. 86-7 in

17. 'Reaching the people' (1c 3.2/178); this text is discussed in greater detail below. The precise nature of the partnership is not entirely clear: Buffalo Books stationery describes the company as an Adprint subsidiary, while the Isotype Institute and Purnell and Sons remained commercially independent; the 'partnership' therefore was probably an informal one. Isotype working material shows Buffalo Books acting both as an 'umbrella' and as an equal among partners. Confusingly, Purnell and Sons, based in Paulton, Somerset, had recently acquired Adprint. See below (p. 491) for two sketch diagrams of the partnership.

18. Isabel Frame-Smith (active 1940s-197os) is described by former colleagues as Wolfgang Foges's 'fixer' and 'right hand'; she was clearly integral to Adprint. Scottish by birth, she was married to Graham Hastings, whose family owned the Architectural Press. In the later 194os she worked for Contact Publications and is listed as assistant editor in several volumes of the 'Contact' book series; as 'Frame Hastings' her editing work is acknowledged by Herbert Spencer in Design for business printing (1952). She joined Adprint at some point in this period, later working for its successors, Rathbone Books and Aldus Books, and died suddenly while still employed by Aldus Books. Colleagues remember her as a forceful personality, charming, and with a deep voice. She generally referred to herself as 'Frame Smith', 'Frame-Smith', or 'Frame'; she is said to have 'revelled in the Africa connections'. Personal communications with Barbara Cooper, David Lambert (Adprint employees), and Hilda Goldman (Frame Smith's secretary), November 2011 – February 2012.

19. Throughout this chapter, emphasis will be placed on the work of the Isotype Institute, within the Buffalo Books partnership.

information', and the quality colour printing of Purnell and Sons. Wolfgang Foges, Adprint's managing director, was also named managing director of Buffalo Books, though its day-to-day operations would be in the hands of the energetic and capable Frame Smith. 18

The first project taken on by the partnership was a prospective colour magazine entitled Forward.¹9 According to Foges, the Colonial Office 'wanted parts of West Africa, which were soon to become independent, to be linked by magazines and books'; Forward appears to be a contribution towards this goal. Work on the magazine began in 1952, apparently in association with the Colonial Office and the governments of the Gold Coast and (later) the Western Region of Nigeria.2º A trial issue, priced 9 pence, was circulated later that year under the editorship of Frame Smith, to gauge demand; it incorporated illustrated articles and comic strips dealing with culture, adventure, sport, and practical topics such as bicycle repair, first aid, and personal health. Isotype contributions were limited to illustrated diagrams on power and heat, and on digestion. An issue 'number 1', priced 1 shilling, was published a year or so later, in late 1953 or early 1954, for circulation in the Gold Coast; it was produced in English and three vernacular languages.²¹ Articles and Isotype diagrams from the trial issue were re-used and several new articles and a comic strip were introduced. Contributors were predominantly from the Gold Coast and Nigeria, while the cover featured the Gold Coast missionary and educationalist James Aggrey. Inside, a message from the editor (Frame Smith) set out aims and policy: Forward would be educational, informative, and entertaining, non-political and free of commercial influence, and as far as possible the product of Africans. Printing, however, would be done in the Uk to take advantage of high-quality colour work not then available in West Africa.

Despite its encouraging blend of content, Forward made it no further than its first issue. Although the magazine was apparently ‘well received by the public', it was concluded that ‘under the conditions of marketing

# FORWARD

# Power and Heat from the Depths of the Earth

![](images/241a2f61edbc4ff5c63a1031d5d55c3c83278fa971045d85a76f52561e2e1a72.jpg)
