##

A

![](images/e679e37e6e3b469a3590c501dc9eac38ec3b4cf4d8ac7237080979d1d66b453f.jpg)

![](images/d2d869ad36d63a2e4e2f051f151030f34783e81f903c355e326d1959d1831029.jpg)

Forward magazine, no.1, 1953/4. Front cover and pages with Isotype illustrated diagram. 325 × 250 mm.

and distribution obtaining at the time, the regular issue of such a periodical, however well suited to its purpose, was not at the moment an economic possibility.'²²But if Forward itself failed, its association with the government of the Western Region of Nigeria is notable. A product of this association was an unused Isotype colour supplement, 'What does government do for us?', produced for the magazine. One side shows the Western Region's House of Assembly, with links to education, medical services, public works, and development; the other side presents a diagram of the government's revenue and expenditure streams. The supplement was apparently the only one worked out in detail, though there is evidence that other issues were identified as important, and suitable for visual presentation. And while these remained undeveloped in the context of Forward, many appear in other work then getting underway in the Western Region.²3

(Left) 'What does government do for us?', proof copy of an unpublished supplement to Forward, front and back.

## 22. 'Reaching the people'. (IC 3.2/178)

23. In addition to the colour supplement on government provision, working material includes typewritten notes, probably compiled in 1953-4, outlining ideas for further Western Region supplements. These would deal with cocoa and other products and resources, women in work, child care, trades and professions, land and town-planning, and taxes and revenue. A recently published series of government White Papers were also identified as suitable supplement topics; these dealt with education, agriculture, forest policy, social welfare, health, and cooperative policy. A group of sketches suggest how some of these rough ideas might have been developed for visual explanation (iC 3.2/165).

![](images/e588d60af42298d95f9e4e8670f81558f2720e3ffe130f5022b3bc3e7c3baea4.jpg)  
Obafemi Awolowo, c. 195os.

The link established by Buffalo Books and the Isotype Institute to the Western Regional Government came during a unique phase of Nigerian history. Over the course of the 195os, Nigeria made increasing gains in self-determination until, in 196o, it achieved independence from Britain. During the 195os, much of Nigeria's growing autonomy was exercised regionally, and of its several regions the governance of the Western Region was the most politically and socially progressive. The decade was punctuated by a series of conferences convened by the British government that were intended to strengthen and resolve Nigeria's constitutional arrangements, leading up to its anticipated independence. The conference held in London in July and August 1953 attracted delegates from all regions of Nigeria; the most prominent figure among the Western Region's delegates was Obafemi Awolowo, then Leader of the Party in Power, the Action Group.²ª

24. The 1953 London conference was convened to deal with issues raised by the Macpherson Constitution (1951), which had defined the nature and remit of regional legislative bodies. The constitution contained loopholes that allowed for increasing assertions of Nigerian control. Amendments made in 1953-4 led to federal elections in 1954 and regional elections in 1956. Constitutional conferences resumed in 1957–8, as pressure grew for Britain to accede to Nigerian demands for independence. Richard L. Sklar, Nigerian political parties: power in an emergent African nation (Princeton, NJ: Princeton University Press, 1963) As his title suggests, Obafemi Awolowo (1909-1987) was, in effect, the Western Region's prime minister. Awolowo studied law in London from 1944 to 1946 before returning to the Western Region where he practised as a barrister until late 1951. In early 1952, about a year after the founding of the Action Group, he became Leader of the Party in Power and Minister for Local Government; in October 1954, he was named the region's Premier and Minister of Finance. 'Obafemi Awolowo – life sketch' (IC 3.2/166). The Action Group was principally the party of the Western

While in London, Awolowo visited the Adprint offices where Foges introduced him to Marie Neurath and Isotype. The intention was to show him how Isotype might help communicate the Western Regional Government's programme of development to its people.2⁵ Marie Neurath recalled the encounter:

One day Awolowo ... was expected. Foges came to me with a short text by him and said: 'I would like to make clear to these people that they need visual methods in their country; read through this text quickly and make drawings for its main theses; not that he [Awolowo] wishes to illustrate it; he should just see how pictures would help him.' So I read quickly, plucked out a few points, and made some pencil sketches on slips of paper. I was there when Awolowo arrived in his Yoruba robes, together with his private secretary. After a while my sketches were given to him; he looked at them quietly and began to talk with his secretary; how could we best use them?'26

The sketches shown to Awolowo included simple visualizations of the Action Group's position on issues such as the structure of federal government, direct and indirect elections, political hierarchies, and

25. It seems likely that Marie Neurath met Awolowo in London in August 1953, though there is no explicit confirmation of this in Isotype working material. Awolowo attended the constitutional

conference in July and August, and in September Marie Neurath wrote that she had had a first meeting with a Nigerian minister whose name she does not give. See Obafemi Awolowo, Awo: the autobiography of Chief Obafemi Awolowo (Cambridge: Cambridge University Press, 196o), p. 180; letter Marie Neurath to Waldemar Kaempffert, 23 September 1953 (1C 1/47).

Region's majority Yoruba population. Its platform was social democratic and welfarist and included progressive policies in education, health care, agriculture, infrastructure, and enfranchisement.

![](images/fe77830a34c361f01ba75855d2506ebcf55a5da203240899a8da7c6e7d9cce25.jpg)  
Sketches shown to Obafemi Awolowo by Marie Neurath. 1953. Each 126 × 203 mm. The sketches illustrate positions taken by the Action Group on issues under discussion at  
the 1953 constitutional conference in London. The issue of indirect elections (upper left sketch) was of particular concern since it was most susceptible to corruption.

constituency make-up – that is to say, those issues then under discussion at the London conference.²7

Awolowo's response must have given Marie Neurath cause for optimism, as shortly afterwards she wrote to long-time friend Waldemar Kaempffert:28 'The most exciting recent development is our contact with developments in Nigeria. I have met one minister of the Western Region government, who was very excited about our work and the possibilities for his requirements.' Looking forward, she continued, 'You can imagine how much we could help in their programme of general education and of raising the standard of agricultural work. It is a pleasure to see how quickly these highly intelligent representatives grasp the roughest of my sketches, and how the method seems to appeal to them.'29

29. Letter Marie Neurath to Kaempffert, 23 September 1953. A week later, Kaempffert replied: 'What you write about the interest that the Nigerian government is displaying in your work is exciting. I hope that they will carry out their plans. It astonishes me that supposedly backward countries should be willing to adopt new methods in education. I regard this Nigerian interest as another piece of evidence that the masses of this world are in a state of fermentation.' On 6 December 1953, he wrote again: 'I hope that something comes of Nigeria's plans. Whoever is responsible for them is a person of vision.

He is probably one of a bright band of idealists of the kind that always start fine movements only to be crushed by so-called "practical" politicians who are concerned chiefly with economic policies. I hope that this time the idealists will triumph.' (ic 1/47)

30. Marie Neurath, 'Memorandum: visual aids for the Western Region' (ic 3.2/169). It should be noted that during this time the Isotype Institute was already working on charts for the Action Group publication, Lagos belongs to the West, which grew out of the 1953 London conference where the Secretary of State for the Colonies ruled that the (then) national capital Lagos, previously within the jurisdiction of the Western Region, should be separated from it, a decision the Action Group strongly opposed. Sklar, Nigerian political parties, pp. 134–5; Action Group, Lagos belongs to the West London, 1953.

31. In describing her expertise, Marie Neurath drew attention to Isotype work in Vienna, the Soviet Union, and Mexico (though not the uSA), and to its institutes in Vienna, the Netherlands, and England. Marie Neurath, 'Memorandum: visual aids for the Western Region' (IC 3.2/16g).

32. 23/24 June to 25 September 1954; see documents relating to preparations for the first trip (IC 3.2/16g). Dates of the second trip, and a third trip made in late 1955, are noted below.

33. 'Visual education expert visits Ibadan schools' (C 3.2/169); this report was apparently written by a government press officer, Marie Neurath's itinerary indicates that she visited advanced schools in Ibadan, village schools nearby, and a local visual aids advisor.

In the months that followed, further discussions with the Western Regional Government took place, leading in early 1954 to a memorandum drawn up by Marie Neurath setting out in general terms a programme of work to be undertaken by the Isotype Institute in the Western Region. The object of the work would, in effect, be public relations, to illustrate, explain, and bring home to the people of the Western Region what the aims of the Government are; everybody should understand, be interested and take part in the affairs which are their own.'³º The memorandum described the Isotype method for producing visual work and Marie Neurath's expertise in transformation, which was especially relevant to the requirements of the Western Regional Government.³¹ The proposed sum of activities would encompass research and transformation leading to visual work, as well as – and importantly – the establishment of a local workshop to carry out similar work on a permanent basis by trained Nigerians.

## In Africa

Having reached agreement with the Western Regional Government on a programme of work, Marie Neurath was invited to the Western Region. She would be based in the capital Ibadan, and would be given the title 'Visual Aids Expert'. A first trip would take place from June to September 1954, with a second planned for the following year.³² Although she declined an initial proposal of a single six-month stay, the two trips would nevertheless keep her away from London and the Isotype Institute for lengthy periods. That she accepted such arrangements as necessary suggests the importance she placed on local immersion. This would give her direct and regular contact with ministers and civil servants in the Western Region, and allow her to experience conditions on the ground. She could observe how, where, and by whom visual aids were used before resolving on how Isotype work might be best integrated into the local scene.

Among the first items on Marie Neurath's itinerary after arriving in the Western Region were visits to schools in and around Ibadan. She hoped to observe classroom teaching and learning that involved visual aids ‘with a view to improving her own work in visual education'.³ª She encountered a range of visual aids made by pupils that presented topics such as water supply, imports and exports, geography, economics, health, agriculture, and counting methods. She was impressed by the work being done and commended the progress, drive, and optimism

of all those she met. Her visits were reported in at least three regional newspapers.³⁴

## White Paper booklets

Together with her visits to local schools, it seems that Marie Neurath's encounter with life in Ibadan also had important and immediate consequences.

Already in London I had been given a white paper' on education to read ... . From this I had designed a large summary chart; it went straight into the wastepaper basket after I had walked through the streets of Ibadan; why should these people have to struggle with my chart? I made a booklet of 16 pages out of it; problems, tasks, solutions were shown step by step. This format was retained for all the other subjects.'³⁵

The booklet to which Marie Neurath refers, Education for all in the Western Region, was one in a series that would first depict existing circumstances in the Western Region, then show how the government's programme of development would lead to improvements in the lives of all. Other booklets in the series would address agriculture, health, and fiscal policy under the titles Better farming for better living in the Western Region, Health for all in the Western Region, and Paying for progress in the Western Region. Each was commissioned by a government minister in connection with his portfolio and collectively became known as White Paper booklets, since they summarized policy in these areas.³⁶ Marie Neurath recalled how the commissioning happened:

I had a discussion about my work in the house of Awolowo, the prime minister; I first had to design a booklet about schools – general education was just being introduced – together with the minister of Education, Awokoya. During the discussions, the minister of agriculture, Akinloye, said that he wanted to have a book too. The minister of health, Ighodaro, soon introduced himself as well; Chief Akran gave me access every time I needed any help; I probably spoke with him most when I was preparing the more general booklet, Paying for progress'.³7

37. 'What I remember', p. 95. Stephen Oluwole Awokoya was Minister of Education and author of the Universal Primary Education (uPE) scheme; Augustus Meredith Adisa Akinloye was Minister of Agriculture and Natural Resources; S. O. Ighodaro was Minister of Health; and C. D. Akran was Minister for Local Development. Marie Neurath probably condensed her recollections in light of Isotype working material that indicates that already in March 1954, before her arrival in Nigeria, exploratory work had begun on how to group and present aspects of the Western Regional Government's programme of development. One proposal was a booklet titled 'Heath and happiness' that would address issues of diet, agriculture and productivity, export and import, knowledge, training and cooperation, and government initiatives. These issues were later distributed among several booklets that were eventually produced. See especially letter Marie Neurath to Mr Welch, 15 March 1954 (ic 3.2/168). Sklar, Nigerian political parties, p. 278, remarks that in 1955 Awolowo inaugurated the practice of holding informal 'leaders meetings' at his home, without an agenda or recorded minutes. Marie Neurath's recollections suggest that such meetings were already taking płace the previous year.

38. Page designs were almost always worked out as a 16-page sequence in codex form; at every stage of design, Marie Neurath refers to these as 'roughs'.

39. The White Paper booklets (and indeed most Isotype work for the Western Region) were produced in English- and Yorubalanguage versions. Working material indicates that texts were first drafted in English, from which a Yoruba translation was made. The original English was plain and direct, making it accessible to audiences of mixed reading abilities while anticipating the text's translation into the vernacular, in which technical and other special terms might not be available.

40. By the end of her first stay in the Western Region, it appears that Marie Neurath had completed designs and received approval for three of the four White Paper booklets; the design of the fourth, Better farming for better living, was completed in London following a more extensive process of consultation and approval.

41. Despite the availability of existing Isotype pictograms of black people (see p. 517 below), there are no indications that these were ever considered for use in West Africa. The development of pictograms for men, women, and children in the Western Region apparently took place during the preparation of the Action Group manifesto, Forward to freedom, issued for the 1954 federal

The aim of the booklets was not to instruct but to show and explain 'problems, tasks, solutions' simply and directly. For each booklet, work was first directed towards identifying the basic message to be communicated and devising a narrative strategy and structure to convey it. A sequence of sixteen pages then evolved from small pencil sketches through to full-size colour visualizations;³⁸ text was composed in parallel.³9 By the end of the process, the completed rough' was sufficiently detailed to enable ministers to approve the designs, which would then guide the making of art-work back in London. Throughout, ministers, civil servants, and British colonial administrators collaborated in shaping content, reviewing designs, and resolving details.⁴º

Apart from their subject-specific content, Marie Neurath pursued a broadly similar visual strategy for the booklets as a whole, in particular by balancing general and local depictions. General depictions consisted of graphic arrangements showing processes, relationships, and comparisons typical of Isotype. Local depictions included the flora, fauna, equipment, buildings (materials and structures), village and town scenes, and work roles typical of the Western Region, which Marie Neurath observed herself or had brought to her attention by government officials and specialists. With few exceptions, depictions of humans were also localized in their naturalistic features and in their cultural and social identities, conveyed mainly through attire. The latter consisted of Yoruba robes, wraps, and headwear, while professional or managerial roles were indicated by specialist work clothing or Western dress.4¹ All graphic depictions were then carefully structured and integrated with the text using the well-practised 'picture-text-style' common to other Isotype work. The result was a mutually reinforcing narrative in which text would frame graphics or be woven through them, but always working in parallel to the order of looking.ª²

elections. Work on the publication had begun before Marie Neurath's first visit to the Western Region but its visualizations were refined after her arrival. The work is described in several letters sent by her from Ibadan to Isotype Institute colleagues John Ellis and Evelyn Worboys in London. In one letter, Marie Neurath provides a brief description and sketches of a Yoruba man's attire (letter Marie Neurath to Ellis and Worboys, 5 July 1954); in a second, apparently still in connection with Forward to freedom, she remarked 'you will see that some Nigerian looking symbols are needed – also for the groups [of people] – so the job is perhaps slightly less simple than you thought!' Letter Marie Neurath to Worboys, 13 July 1954 (IC 3.2/16g). A week later, she advised at length (again with sketches) on improvements to pictograms of Yoruba men and women. Her remarks focus on gaining accuracy in the depiction of attire (including women's headwear for supporting loads), body shape, posture, and gait; the use of naturalistic profiles appears to be assumed. (Letter Marie Neurath to Worboys, 20 July 1954; IC 3.2/169.) It seems that these depictions

42. Throughout, graphic arrangements are given sufficient space to allow English texts to be substituted with Yoruba, though the extent of each was generally equal or nearly so. Texts were typeset in Gill Sans; diacritical marks required for transliterating the Yoruba were added manually with pen and ink at the art-work stage.

Education for all in the Western Region. 1955. 204×163 mm.

The aim of this booklet was to illustrate how education improved people's lives and the development prospects of the Western Region. The work would involve first counting all the children who needed to be educated, then building a sufficient number of schools for them; sufficient numbers of teachers also needed to be trained. The intention was to complete the plan in four or five years.

The government would help farmers by disseminating knowledge about how to improve crop yields through better plants, soil, and farming methods, by establishing cooperatives and processing plants, and by providing loans. Greater productivity meant export, which in turn meant 'better living at home'.

Better farming for better living in the Western Region. Progressive sequence of roughs for p. 13 (IC 3.2/168).

Working material for this White Paper booklet is extensive, the result of the Ministry of Agriculture's concern for accuracy. Its design was subjected to much development, proofing, and correction, as shown in this sequence of roughs.

(a) On page 13 of the booklet, the production of higher yield through improved farming methods is to be shown. A first sketch compares yields produced by hand and by mechanical cultivation, using elevation views.

(b-c) These two sketches retain elevation views, but better articulate the scenes: three farmers working manually are compared to two using mechanical methods; quantities are introduced to show relative productivity and the surplus needed to pay for the tractor.

(d-f) In the culminating sketch (d), the view is shifted upward to a %-plan, allowing the farmers' plots to be compared more easily, reinforced by the aligned dashed lines. This sketch is in fact a colour guide for the photo-mechanical print (e) sent to the ministry in Ibadan for review. Notes for changes (f) were returned to the Isotype Institute and implemented in the final design. These involved the planting patterns in the upper plots, and the implement pulled by the tractor.

The page, as printed, is shown overleaf.

Holdings in this country are mostly small and only partly farmed at any one time. Improved methods of farming are difficult.

All children will go to school.

This is what the Government of the Wester Region plans to provide for the care of the sick :  
![](images/0b9a0f985939dd9e175f9ea2d4b89fa619be4fd046d9765c2d4971b3a507bfa1.jpg)

Health for all in the Western Region, 1955.

The aim of this booklet was to show government plans to set up an integrated system of local dispensaries connected to regional hospitals by touring doctors supported by ambulances. Special emphasis was placed on children's health: 'health visitors' would give mothers advice at home; maternity centres would be set up; and children would receive health checks at school, where they would also learn about healthy living.

The Western Regional Government has set itself a great cask: to make medical services available to everybody in the country.

Paying for progress in the Western Region. 1955. Front cover and pages from Yoruba-language version.

This booklet shows how the benefits of education, agriculture, and health had to be paid for through taxes, but that this effort and sacrifice were worth it for the sake of the region.

## Poster-leaflets

43. Working material and correspondence associated with the poster-leaflets (ic 3.2/168-9). See also 'What I remember', p. 97, from where quotations in this paragraph are taken.

44. At the General (now Central) Hospital in Benin City, Marie Neurath formed what would prove to be a long working friendship with the resident, Dr Ogunlesi; at the Ossiomo Leprosy Centre, she spent a day with Dr Loube Mary-Madeleine Lengauer of the Nigeria Leprosy Service.

45. As discussed above, a precedent for this idea can be found in remarks by Otto Neurath in 'Visual education' (1945, p. 264): 'Sometimes one has to give health instruction or information on agriculture or on citizenship, without having time for preparatory teaching. To influence sub-literates posters and picture sheets combined with the minimum of words may be used with advantage.'

46. Letter L. M.-M. Lengauer to Marie Neurath, 13 August 1954 (iC 3.2/168). See also the later report on Lengauer's suggestions for improvements to the posterleaflets, as printed (5 April 1955; IC 3.2/16g). Her principal concerns were the depiction of mild leprosy, which she felt looked too much like a germ infection, and the depictions of an under-fed and a well-fed child (in 'Food'), which she felt should include differences in height.

47. Vincent William Joseph Hetreed (1916–2005) was serving as a medical officer in the Colonial Service.

48. Letter V. W. Hetreed to Marie Neurath, 26 October 1954. In reply (2 November 1954), Marie Neurath acknowledged that there was a 'general feeling in Ibadan that our TB booklet is too abstract for use in the country' but that the planned poster-leaflet she hoped to send to him for comment was simpler and more realistic, and had been accepted. (IC 3.2/16g)

49. Letter V. W. Hetreed to Marie Neurath, 27 November 1954 (IC 3.2/168).

Another project begun by Marie Neurath during her first stay in the Western Region was a series of 'poster-leaflets' on health topics. They would result from an extensive process of research and consultation both in the Western Region and in London.4³ The poster-leaflet idea came about on a trip Marie Neurath made to Benin province in early August 1954 to learn more about health care provision. While there she visited various health facilities including the General Hospital in Benin City, the Leprosy Centre at Ossiomo, several medical field units, and a doctor's surgery.⁴⁴ At the surgery, she saw a waiting room crammed full' of patients who (she was told) returned again and again with the same complaints. So she suggested producing educational pictures with which medical assistants could explain to patients, while they waited, the nature of their condition and how it could be prevented.They would then perhaps grasp what they were doing wrong and how they should do it better.'⁴Discussions with doctors and nurses at the General Hospital revealed their preference for illustrated wall charts that patients could also take home to study. Together with the resident, Dr Ogunlesi, Marie Neurath identified a group of health care issues in need of clear communication. They included the importance of good food and water, and the diagnosis and treatment of tuberculosis and leprosy.

On her return to Ibadan, having secured the agreement of the Minis ter of Health, Ighodaro, Marie Neurath set to work. She consulted with Dr Lengauer, who she had met at Ossiomo. Lengauer offered advice on the visual representation of leprosy, its mild and bad forms, its infectiousness, and its mutilations, which most people found frightening. 46 Back in London, she corresponded with V. W. Hetreed, a tuberculosis specialist connected with the Tuberculosis Survey in Ibadan, from whom she hoped to get feedback on her evolving designs.4 Initially, Hetreed expressed doubts about the value of visual methods, which he had already encountered in the Isotype booklet prepared for the National Tuberculosis Association some fifteen years earlier: 'I am however not at all certain that it [the NTA booklet] can be a successful method for the illiterate or semi literate, who does not readily relate diagrams, magnified pictures &c to the object they represent.'4⁸

After receiving Marie Neurath's rough, Hetreed offered feedback that was mostly encouraging.4 He began, however, with the general observation that a poster-leaflet predicated on patients visiting a doctor was unrealistic when, in Ibadan, for a population of nearly half a million, there was only one doctor assigned to treating tuberculosis and then only half-time; in Lagos, with a population of 28o,ooo, again only one doctor treated TB (though here full-time); elsewhere in the Western Region there were apparently no other doctors dedicated to TB treatment. Hetreed then commented on several of the visual representations, including one showing bad breath, which he predicted would be read both literally and as a sign of TB transmission, though not in the sense of germs or ‘tiny creatures'. He also offered thoughts on the use of colour: yellow, in his view, had been correctly deployed to designate a healthy person; red, by contrast, should represent tuberculosis because of its Yoruba associations with danger and misfortune; blue, suggesting happiness, was appropriate for depictions of treatment and protection, and for doctors.⁵º

![](images/677099ec724d97f320c0835ca1978cfabce64cdecba7c7e1a390f98b8e29656a.jpg)  
Rough for the poster-leaflet 'Leprosy'. August 1954. 203 × 258 mm. Second of two versions sent to L. M.-M. Lengauer for comment; Marie Neurath herself remarked that she found the inside of the segregation village (lower right) too confusing. (Marie Neurath to L. M.-M. Lengauer, 25 August 1954; IC 3.2/168). The poster-leaflet, as printed, is shown below (pp. 471–3).

By late 1954, designs for the poster-leaflets were finalized. As planned, each would function as a wall chart that could also be folded up and taken away. A two-fold (French fold) format was adopted that, as it was unfolded, presented the health care issue concerned: for food and water, emphasis was placed on variety and purity; for leprosy and tuberculosis, causes and symptoms were shown and described. Unfolded, each poster-leaflet offered a single, summary chart; for food, the constituents of a varied and healthy diet were shown; for water, its purification was explained. The charts for tuberculosis and leprosy laid out the process of medical consultation, diagnosis, and treatment a patient should follow if they contracted either disease. Like the White Paper booklets, the poster-leaflets placed locally recognizable figures and scenes into graphic arrangements typical of Isotype; in a few instances, general and local pictograms worked side-by-side.

![](images/b401d7c7b2c53ae56731c626ea6d18204767d505bdf95853cc62211ea80948bc.jpg)  
Detail from the posterleaflet 'Leprosy', 1955.  
50. Hetreed's views were based in part on his own consultation with a Yoruba nurse and clerk, both employed at the Tuberculosis Survey. Marie Neurath took up many of Hetreed's points in the final design of the TB poster-leaflet, though not his general criticism about patient treatment based on doctor visits.

person with mild TB

Tiny creatures living inside the diseased person come out with his breath.

It is dangerous to be in clase contact with someone who is suffering from bad TB.

## CURED

If you have mild TB, you can be treated and cured, and can go on with your work, if it is not too strenuous.

![](images/c5b282bcf9fa17a224839de66acedf481928f3f7278700f2ff763e16e79ae3b2.jpg)

![](images/5b2b9e753313e4b354e3502fc6297dc9613b38b1b50bd0ba04d62e28c8545523.jpg)  
If you do not go to the doctor, you will be a danger to yourself and others.

![](images/6f73ba84d8004cb7478ae7cb16e5741dbffd6c5c0d045540d56c9c5380e01772.jpg)

![](images/cfe572caec02a596ea73969798e5e7fd92457380cd310ea88a45f7c773a82a48.jpg)

![](images/a23b145653eeae29a1cd59f4c142e6ed221cc3fa1b603cc6670b482aeca77d6a.jpg)

![](images/5369f8209c823955d75553b6d7baa0677c8c37c0a1fd956cd5e3963d025c3db2.jpg)  
If you have bad TB, you will have to stay in bed, separated from other people and using your own personal dishes and bedding. You wili be treated and cured.

## LEPROSY

![](images/912f7f03b90c63fcf6b03a3dc82fe8fccfd4457a50ac817c05b8508a75eb2e43.jpg)  
A person with mild leprosy

![](images/829eb404309b35cade6b52515e4272f9b327a61d283526247280c9819c1f51e4.jpg)

![](images/bca35108f992d58d7fce4f367b859a7f4908c410fe39427d79ea455693e7620b.jpg)  
erson with bad leprosy

![](images/9a2c79be2d326975203f9b6caee167325ae10ab8551ad509319dca48021e868d.jpg)  
A healthy

## No child is ever born with leprosy.

But if the child lives with a person with bad leprosy the child will get the disease. That is how all cases start.

![](images/b8a7c7acac60cc4ddef6ffca1c85253ffb0c31ed88ebb19a440c1daa6bbc695d.jpg)

Inside a person with bad leprosy there are many tiny living creatures or germs. They appear on the skin. They can pass to other people or things by touch.

![](images/494347f4cdb2bf43c641db861d960d243d4d71324b2fb34f17149aafacde2847.jpg)  
Contact with a leprosy victim is dangerous.

![](images/d1a4c5f08858c1ef19bbdded600bd35d32190da91b02acff1cea966f3fbbce1e.jpg)  
Using the same dishes or bedding is dangerous.

People with mild leprosy are no danger to others.

![](images/7f6512c53e5b43f6ae56ab9bef0aa9357270e0bd29287a169bb2bbda5fde199e.jpg)

![](images/14cb0ceefa24502f3a4fe7e9ef6ddf4b84b9084f3d5b4fa55a052035421db7e0.jpg)  
But if they do not go to the doctor in good time they may become cripples.

![](images/143a8157262d595c1e19fb9889d827a0f1608671a439cc1cea629fb8b80483d7.jpg)

## Second visit

51. 18/28 February to 19 May 1955 (1o-day outbound stopover in Rome). See typed notes on Marie Neurath's objectives for her second visit, 'My own work while I am out [in the Western Region]', January 1955 (ic 3.2/171); they included testing Isotype material, designing new leaflets and booklets, suggesting further work, starting a 'centre for display', and identifying suitable Nigerians to train for lsotype work (discussed below).

52. Letter Frame Smith to Marie Neurath, 10 March 1955 (IC 3.2/171). In December 1954, the Premier's office confirmed the English / Yoruba quantities required for the booklets and the poster-leaflets: Health for all and Paying for progress, 25,000 / 25,000; Better farming for better living, 25,000 / 40,ooo; and Education for all, 50,000 / 50,000. 50,000 of each of the four poster-leaflets were also ordered, and the same number of a fifth, 'You and your government', that had been requested specifically by Awolowo; quantities in each of the languages were not specified. See print order of 15 December 1954 (IC 3.2/169). An earlier memorandum from the Ministry of Agriculture stated that the quantities required for the booklet, Better farming for better living, were based on one copy per 100 population; it also gives the distribution strategy for the different language versions, stating that the English version would be distributed in Benin and Delta provinces (i.e. mainly Edo speaking) while the Yoruba version would be distributed elsewhere in the Western Region. 'Agricultural pamphlet', 24 September 1954 (IC 3.2/168). Frame Smith records that the Minister of Education, Stephen Awokoya, was in the uk while the White Paper booklets were being printed in February and March 1955 and that he visited Purnell and Sons with her and Foges on 23 February; letter Frame Smith to Marie Neurath, 24 February 1955 (1C 3.2/171).

53. 'Pictorial booklets on government activities', M. S. MacPepple, assistant publicity officer, 3o March 1955 (IC 3.2/171).

54. 'Pictorial booklets on government activities' (Ic 3.2/171); ollowing quotation is also from this source.

In late February 1955, Marie Neurath arrived back in Ibadan in the Western Region for a second three-month stay.⁵¹ Since her last visit, work on the poster-leaflets and the White Paper booklets had been mostly completed and she was able to bring advance copies with her.s² Each carried a foreword by the commissioning minister calling on the people of the Western Region to play their part in the initiatives described within.

## School visits

With White Paper booklets in hand, Marie Neurath was eager to try them out on their intended audiences, and in mid-March she began a week-long tour of schools in the Ibadan area. An upbeat report written by a government publicity officer, probably for circulation among ministries that had commissioned a booklet, provides a record of the visits.⁵³ They were planned to give teachers an opportunity to present and discuss the booklets with their students, while allowing Marie Neurath to observe how the booklets were received. For younger pupils, this happened first through looking, then by reading:

In the lower classes it was intended to discover how much the pupils could recognise in the books certain objects familiar to them and to guess why they are shown there side by side with one another. That is to say, to build up a story by connecting a number of objects used together in the daily activities of the town. The teacher was then required to hold a reading lesson with them in the course of which he explains those objects in relation to the activities of the Regional Government. 54

Older pupils read the texts on their own, then took part in a questionand-answer session with the teachers and Marie Neurath. At one school where Education for all was distributed to several classes, its message was apparently received enthusiastically:

the children expressed unanimous opinion that education for all was better than that for a few, as the former would give equal opportunities in life for all. In the upper classes, where the book is better appreciated, the pupils held that the introduction of free primary education in the West was a bold step towards the abolition of illiteracy and ignorance in the Region.

While the report was in no doubt about the positive impression the booklets had made on the pupils, this was also confirmed by notes taken by teachers in classes at the Momo Mohude Memorial (Sabo)

![](images/21b9211bd639c2b6b10591ab53bc63be95c30cb9dfb6fa281ab71e15bf759c9f.jpg)

Teacher notes on pupil reactions to the booklet Educaton for all, March 1955, 203×158mm. (IC 3.2/171)

School.5⁵ In Class 1b (presumably six- or seven-year-olds), the teacher recorded:

When I showed the book to the children. All the different colours arouse their interest and they rushed to my table. I explained every page of the book to them with the illustrating pictures. Then the children found out themselves that schooling is very good. ... Many pupils wish to be teachers in future. A song []Bata mi a ro ko, ko, nile[] was sung to impress the lesson on their mind.

In the notes from Class 6 Special, the latent propaganda dimension of the booklet is more explicitly expressed:

They [the pupils] have clearly understood and agreed that the government is fighting really for the upliftment of the country as a whole: long live [Premier] Mr. Awolowo and [Minister of Education, Mr.] Awokoya amen.

As the report unabashedly concluded, the booklets admirably served their intended double purpose' of guiding lessons and providing a ‘very effective means of publicising Government activities.'⁵⁶

## Second booklet series

In the early weeks of Marie Neurath's second visit to the Western Region, it appears that much of her time was also devoted to the development of a second series of booklets. These included three about infrastructure commissioned by the Public Works Department under the titles What PwD does in the Western Region, Roads in the Western Region, and Paying for good water in the Western Region; another, Literacy campaign in the Western Region, commissioned by the Ministry of Education to

55. Quotations that follow are taken from these notes, which were later sent to Marie Neurath (IC 3.2/171).

56. 'Pictorial booklets on government activities' (IC 3.2/171). The visits to schools, as well as the content of Education for all, were connected to the launch of the Universal Primary Education scheme on 17 January 1955. In a letter to Awokoya describing the reception of Education for all, Marie Neurath quoted some of these remarks, including the reference to him (24 March 1955; IC 3.2/171). The report also offers a glimpse of how the booklets were received outside of schools: 'Townspeople also had the chance of reading the books, and where political persuasion did not bias their minds the books were acclaimed.' Marie Neurath recalls that Better farming for better living was tested in one school at her request, and among farmers, though exactly where and how the latter took place is not clear. She also writes of venturing into a forest one day to a nearby village where she gave the booklet to a family, to study their reactions. They were intrigued and asked to keep it. Reflecting on her experiences with all the booklets, Marie Neurath concluded: 'the way to the people for these book[lets] was through the schools [where] they were looked at collectively ... and were then given to the children to take home.' ('What I remember', p. 98.)

What PwD does in the Western Region. 1955-6.

57. Working material for the five booklets (ic 3.2/173). The process of work on these booklets was apparently similar to that followed for the White Paper booklets. Roughs for all five carry the date 1955'; Paying for good water and Voting appear to have been completed that year; Roads and What PwD does were probably completed in late 1955 or early 1956. Literacy campaign was not completed until 1958. Elements that appear in Paying for good water and What PwD does were apparently first combined in a booklet titled 'How regional and local government works together that was later abandoned. See IC 3.2/16g for print order details for Paying for good water, Roads, and What PWD does.

58. For work on the infrastructure booklets, the Public Works Department arranged visits to construction sites where Marie Neurath sketched equipment and operations that later featured in the booklets.

59. Working material also remains for an abandoned poster, 'Can you read? Please become an instructor to all those who can "read" pictures only' (ic 3.2./173).

60. Letter Peter H. Balmer to Marie Neurath, 29 June 1955, stating that the booklet's text had been personally approved by Awolowo (IC 3.2/173). Balmer was a Colonial Administrative Officer and the Electoral Commissioner for the Western Region, attached to the Premier's office; his documents from this period are in the Bodleian Library, Oxford: 'Papers of Peter H. Balmer relating to self-government

![](images/c65e4e3a8ff9aaf5d2361cb2f58472feefed0fe9b39967702e60d43903f4eb4b.jpg)

promote adult literacy; and a fifth, Voting in the Western Region of Nigeria, to encourage registration and participation, commissioned by the Premier's office. While not designated White Paper booklets, each explained and illustrated important areas of government policy and works.7 The booklets on infrastructure formed a group: the first showed the process by which public works such as buildings, roads, and water supplies were planned, financed, and built; the other two explained in greater detail the value of good roads and water and, in the latter instance, why good water was worth paying for.5⁸ Literacy campaign was in part a follow-on to Education for all: the campaign of the booklet's title was aimed at literate adults who the government hoped would both promote the literacy drive and become instructors of their illiterate fellow citizens.sª The aim was for all adults to be able to sign their name, keep accounts, send and receive letters, read newspapers, and in general expand their knowledge of the world.

The fifth booklet, Voting, was prepared in anticipation of regional elections due to take place in 1956, and provided instructions on how to register, and how and where to vote.6º Work on Voting extended to the design of several posters that would function in tandem with the booklet. Two of these dealt with registration: one directed people to register by October 1955, so that they could vote the following year; the other identified registration offices. Two further posters were deployed during the election: 'Vote early at your polling station', showing the process of voting; and another identifying polling stations.61

Yoruba versions. Isotype working material suggests that the design of these posters was underway by mid-1955 (IC 3.2/169 and 3.2/175). See also notes on the ballot box symbols used for 'Vote early': as

You willl only be oble to vote If your nome Is on the lhst or register of electors.   
When the election is being held, you should try to vote early in the day. If you go late, you may have to stand In queues for a long time.

![](images/dfed22de869283e67ceef219dc15c73263d96e71dccf981bec5bfee66fec9162.jpg)  
If your name is found on the register of electors you wl be given a ballot paper

You will then go into a Polling Booth alone. There you will find the ballot boxes.

![](images/af2a8012a7cc2f0db48043898948a612fd5fe6ee38ebf45c13f31b6e3e21db34.jpg)  
You will put your ballot paper into the box which has the symbol of the candidate you want to be elected.

Every year the register of electors will be revised and corrected.

![](images/8da548658b2e8132bf034c2dad1099c31a4952e6a5117395a13a34233b105d5a.jpg)  
The next time the Registration Offices will be open will be in January 1957.

All qualified persons whose names are not In the 1956 register will be able to register then.

![](images/8119c17ae8d295114f8f8ebe2f9327a24a1f243905cdea0648100d7988037fa4.jpg)  
All those who have by then become 21 can register, and also those who have missed an earlier chance can do so. 15

## HOW OUR GOVERNMENT WORKS

![](images/f1fa89f5e16918a96e16b72d851d204d299fb98e8658b9813921bba1a74d9c13.jpg)

# If you are not already registered to vote REGISTER IN JANUARY 1957

![](images/710744dfc10afe3cadde6c865d094c1b46908f6469ee5381ffccbc1b15d931b1.jpg)  
and complete the necessary forms  
'If you are not already registered to vote register in January 1957'. 1956. 510×765mm.

Another poster, How our government works', which laid out the entire structure of government in the Western Region, was specified for outdoor display, presumably to be consulted during registration or polling. The poster was unusual in that it was one of the very few items produced for the Western Region that relied entirely on generalized pictograms of humans.6² Following the 1956 regional elections, Voting was superseded by a revised booklet, Registration and voting in the Western Region of Nigeria, that contained a new timetable for voter registration in 1957, in preparation for the 1958 local government council elections; as before, it was paired with a poster encouraging voter registration.6ª Overall, the programme of information about registration and voting was rated a success, at least by Rotimi Williams, then Minister for Local Government, who informed Marie Neurath that twice as many voters had turned out for the Western Region elections than had in the Eastern Region, for which he felt Isotype deserved the credit.“⁴

## Visual aids office

As Marie Neurath had outlined in her original memorandum of work, the establishment of a visual aids office, or workshop, in the Western Region was a key aim. From the start, the intention was to use Isotype Institute activities as a means to train someone in research and transformation, who might then lead a team of artists dedicated to the

62. The poster was commissioned by the Premier's office in a letter of 20 October 1955 (IC 3.2/16g). Although Marie Neurath accepted the commission, she replied a week later that the (considerable) detail of the poster would (only) be acceptable if the poster could be studied at length but that this would make it unsuitable for outdoor display. The poster was apparently not completed until the latter part of 1956 or early 1957, in English and Yoruba versions (IC 3.2/176). The poster-leaflet, 'You and your government', commissioned by the Premier's office during Marie Neurath's first visit to the Western Region, was probably completed by mid-1955, and distributed at registration offices, polling stations, and elsewhere.

63. This booklet was probably completed in 1956.

64. 'What I remember', p. 98. The turnout of voters for the 1956 elections in the Western Region, to which Williams probably refers, was 68%. Sklar, Nigerian political parties, pp. 32–3.

65. See 'Memorandum: visual aids for the Western Region', 1954 (iC 3.2/16g) for details of Marie Neurath's initial conception of visual aids production in the Western Region.

66. Letter Marie Neurath to Frame Smith, 16 September 1954 (1C 3.2/16g).

67. Marie Neurath, 'My own work while I am out [in the Western Region]', January 1955 (IC 3.2/171). This document lists a number of matters relating to training and the organization of a visual aids office to be addressed during her upcoming visit.

68. See 'Visit to Nigerian College of Art, Science and Technology' (probably Ibadan), 23 March 1955; and report on visits to Edo College (Benin City) and Warri College (Warri), 4 April 1955 (IC 3.2/171). It seems that none of the students was suitable. Edo College provided several recommendations of people already working in art and publicity related jobs. Marie Neurath also received a letter from Dr Ogunlesi, who proposed his wife as a candidate (20 February 1955; IC 3.2/171).

69. 'Memorandum on training for visual education' (IC 3.2/171). This document and the second (discussed below) were prepared for and submitted to the Minister of Home Affairs.

production of visual aids. Marie Neurath envisioned the training taking place mostly in the Western Region, though recruits might also travel to London for additional training there. The objective was to eventually create a permanent facility, to which the Isotype Institute would then serve as consultants. Marie Neurath noted that such a facility could also produce visual aids for neighbouring regions of West Africa and so become partly self-funding.⁶s

During her first stay in the Western Region, Marie Neurath made little progress on plans for training or a visual aids office, though shortly before she returned to London, in September 1954, she met briefly with Awolowo to discuss the matter."⁶ Awolowo reiterated his wish for a training scheme to be initiated and suggested offering a scholarship for training in London. Finding appropriate candidates was the first difficulty to be tackled and Marie Neurath turned her attention to this during her second visit, along with resolving the details of training and the organization of a visual aids office.67 By early April 1955, some five weeks after her return to Ibadan, she had visited at least three colleges where she spoke with officials and teachers and looked at student work in an effort to identify someone who might be suitable for ‘our kind of work'. The results were mixed.68 But Marie Neurath pressed on and by mid-April she had completed drafts of two documents setting out the aims and proposed arrangements for a visual aids office.

The first of these documents described how a team of local workers would be formed and trained.6º The team would consist of a teacher, an artist, and a technical assistant. Marie Neurath would initially take charge of training the team, with the greatest effort directed towards the teacher' since this person would act as the transformer and eventually lead the team. Training would be done by carrying out projects similar to those already completed by the Isotype Institute, and by practising at working as a team. Testing was seen as crucial to the training. Marie Neurath was uncertain about exactly how effective Isotype work done for the Western Region had been up to that time; after remarking that 'our visual material should be understandable to the literate, and easily explained to the illiterate', she admitted that 'how far it fulfils these claims has still to be tested.' To find out, she suggested setting up a permanent exhibition where work could be shown and the public's reactions noted, as a guide to refinements or redesign. She also recommended conducting tests in locations where the work would be deployed, including at schools in villages and towns, in hospital waiting rooms, among village farmers, and in adult education classes. It was estimated that the training would require a year in total, though even this was considered brief. Awolowo's offer of a scholarship for training in London was noted, as was an agreement by Marie Neurath to return to the Western Region to monitor the training, if necessary.

The second document set out in greater detail the twelve-month scheme for training, giving the time, cost, and logistics involved.7º The training would at first be in the hands of Marie Neurath, who would extend her stay in the Western Region for a further month; she would then be replaced by another Isotype Institute employee brought in from London."1 In her introductory remarks, Marie Neurath sounded a not entirely optimistic note about the immediate prospects of success, perhaps to justify the need for the scheme and its duration: 'In the course of training, a number of such pamphlets [i.e. like those already made by Isotype] should be produced by the Nigerian trainees under the supervision and guidance of members of the Isotype Institute. The rate of such production will obviously be handicapped; unsatisfactory work will have to be repeated; unsuitable trainees will have to be replaced.' She then made an account of the salary, equipment, and accommodation costs, and suggested again that additional training could take place in London as well as in connection with further work the Isotype Institute might carry out in the Western Region. The document ends with the suggestion that progress be reviewed after twelve months had passed.

Alongside these two documents, Marie Neurath devised a timeline of decision-making that, if followed, would enable the training scheme to go ahead as proposed. But as the latter days of her second visit passed, there were few signs that a decision to commence the scheme would be made; and indeed by the time of her departure, for reasons that are not entirely clear but which likely stem in part from the failure to identify suitable candidates, the scheme – at least as it might be conducted in the Western Region – had been given up." Despite the setback, efforts shifted to recruiting and training a Nigerian in London. A promising candidate was eventually identified who it seems had already begun a comparable programme of study at the University of London. The programme involved intensive exposure to cultural products including films, exhibitions, and books, and instruction in developing critical and artistic faculties. But for reasons that arc again unclear, efforts to draw this candidate into the activities of the Isotype Institute also faltered, bringing work on the scheme of training finally to an end.

70. 'Scheme for twelve months' training in Isotype work' (Ic 3.2/171).

71. This was to be John Ellis. See letters between Marie Neurath and Ellis regarding his preparations for an extended stay in the Western Region as an Isotype teacher (IC 3.2/171).

72. See letter Marie Neurath to John Ellis, 16 April 1955, expressing some exasperation at the delay in decision making (ic 3.2/171); and letter Marie Neurath to Obafemi Awolowo, 2 May 1955, in which she submits the 'Memorandum' and 'Scheme' directly to him and asks for his suggestions and wishes before her departure (IC 3.2/171). On 17 May 1955, Marie Neurath recorded that after a meeting with Awolowo it had become clear that the local training scheme was being abandoned (iC 3.2/16g).

73. The candidate was Jindu Isi Afiari; see letters and documents associated with the training scheme in London, including assessment sheets for use with films, filmstrips, wall charts, and so on (iC 3.2/172), Afiari's training began in October 1955 at the University of London's Institute of Education, where, together with other international students, he was enrolled in a programme of study relating to visual teaching methods and materials for developing countries, under the direction of Helen Coppen. (IC 3.2/169)

74. Letter Marie Neurath to [Mr] Henry, 23 June 1955 (Ic 3.2/169): 'Please let us have any reports, comments, criticisms which the Information Service may have when they distribute the material [i.e. the booklets and poster-leaflets]'. Henry replied on 8 July: 'I will try to send you some "customer reactions" later', though there is no record of these being sent (ic 3.2/173; working material for Voting booklet). Some months later, on 17 October 1955, Marie Neurath wrote to Awolowo: 'I am longing to hear what the general experience is with our booklets; from such reports we could learn a lot, and improve' (ic 3.2/16g). In a letter to the Ministry of Home Affairs the following month, she wrote: 'Please could you also write a few words about the response to our pamphlets? Have they proved useful in the campaign for registration of voters? Have the posters been widely used and have they been understood? Could I hear from the Ministry of Agriculture what their experience with the farmers has been in connection with our pamphlet? Could you make the persons responsible for distribution, at the Information Service, write me their long promised report?'. Letter Marie Neurath to Mr Atkinson, 14 November 1955 (IC 3.2/169).

75. Topics identified for possible visual explanation in Forward supplements included education, health, taxation, the cocoa and other industries, the future of women, and building and slum clearance; the topic of the Volta River Dam project (discussed below) was also identified but was not then thought promising (Ic 3.2/165, 'rough ideas', probably 1953-4). That the Gold Coast was in little apparent need of foreign expertise in public relations or other kinds of communications may be indicative of the country more generally, where initiatives of mass education and literacy had begun earlier and where progress towards self-government and independence (declared in March 1957) was well advanced.

76. Working material and correspondence for Volta River Project booklet (IC 3.2/167).

77. Trip dates: 28 November to late December 1955 or early January 1956. In mid-December, Marie Neurath travelled on to the Western Region of Nigeria.

## Gold Coast, Sierra Leone

Following Marie Neurath's return from the Western Region in May 1955, production continued on the second series of booklets, and on posters and several other items of work. During this period, she asked government officials and Awolowo himself for feedback on the use and effectiveness of Isotype material in the Western Region, but there is little evidence that much was supplied, despite her persistence.'

Projects in the Western Region were not, however, the only ones underway in West Africa. The Isotype Institute was also still active in the Gold Coast, as well as Sierra Leone, though in relative terms this work would prove limited. Following the Forward magazine initiative of 1952–4, no further projects in the Gold Coast had been immediately forthcoming. Although a variety of development topics had been identified as possibly in need of visual explanation, it was clear that the country's Information Services Department in Accra was already well organized and in little apparent need of servives of the kind provided by the Isotype Institute." In this context, the only project later completed for the Gold Coast was the booklet, The Volta River Project: what it means to you.7 The booklet presented what the Prime Minister, Kwame Nkrumah, described in his foreword as the 'most momentous development undertaking' in the country's history. The project would involve the coordinated development of hydroelectric power on the Volta River and bauxite extraction from surrounding deposits that together would generate cheap electricity and revenue from aluminium production and export. According to Nkrumah, the decision to go ahead with the project was in the hands of the people and so the booklet was to be both technically informative and persuasive, explaining why the project should be undertaken, how it would be organized and paid for, and what benefits it would bring.

To support the process of work, Marie Neurath travelled to the Gold Coast in late November 1955." While there, she worked on the booklet's text and illustrations, and agreed its overall design with Gold Coast officials. The booklet's size initially matched that of the Western Region booklets, though this was forcibly enlarged to accommodate what had become a lengthy text. The Isotype Institute produced maps, section views, depictions of processes, and pictorial statistics, which were then integrated with the text. Graphics were captioned and at the request of Gold Coast officials many pictograms and other depictions were (redundantly) labelled. The booklet's sensitive political and commercial

![](images/f7c6a6aa5c26e8bd9ccc564c5c0632f0deb6d7854038400fda41103512a42295.jpg)

![](images/94615c056574a0def46f02a55f90bbd3e525ab5b14077434e54c2efaa1eb1daa.jpg)

content meant that the entire process of work was accompanied by much discussion and revision, and indeed the content was not finally approved until several months later.78 While in the Gold Coast, Marie Neurath visited the Volta River Project site, about 8o km north-east of Accra. She also agreed a similar scheme of work for a second publication about the project, modelled on the first and to be printed in similar numbers. Provisionally titled 'The Volta River Project: how it will affect you', this booklet would explain the project's human impact, including flood displacement and the rehousing of local people, and its effect on health, agriculture, fishing, communications, and employment.

78. Some revisions took account of ongoing negotiations between the Gold Coast and its partners, which included the British government and various commercial interests. 100,000 copies were printed, half in English, the remainder divided among five vernaculars: Twi, Ewe, Fanti, Dangbe, and Ga; captions and labels remained in English for all the vernacular editions. See Letter R. Raymond (Information Services Department, Gold Coast) to Frame Smith, 31 December 1955 (IC 3.2/167).

Although a text was drafted, the booklet was later abandoned, possibly in anticipation of its likely undermining effect on public opinion.

In Sierra Leone, the Isotype Institute completed only one project, the booklet, Voting, general election 1957, Sierra Leone, accompanied by two posters.8º These were based on the voting materials produced for the Western Region in order to avoid waste of time or money'. A fragmentary letter explains how Voting in the Western Region of Nigeria should be adapted to Sierra Leone, including alterations to the text (inserting local words and place names), to a pair of charts (to more accurately depict Sierra Leone's structure of government), and to female figures wearing ‘head-ties' (to be removed because too Yoruban).⁸¹A poster identifying polling stations similar to the one for the Western Region was produced, now with an orange (rather than blue) ballot box; a second poster, Your vote is your voice in government / Make sure you go to the polls 3 May / Your vote is secret', was also completed. For each of the three items, only English language versions were made.

Apart from Isotype projects commissioned specifically for each of Britain's three colonial territories, at least two other items of work produced for use in West Africa should be mentioned. The first was a series ofleaflets for Plant Protection Ltd, a UK agriculture business trading internationally.8² The three- and four-colour leaflets explain the purpose, method, and frequency of applying various agricultural chemicals, principally for the protection of cocoa trees, and were apparently distributed with the chemicals. Seven leaflets were completed between 1955 and 1958, with texts in English and one of three vernaculars.8³ The second item was a poster-leaflet for the London-based Central Council for Health Education (CCHE), designed to support a malaria prevention campaign initiated by John Burton, medical director of the CCHE. The poster-leaflet that resulted, 'How to save your children from sickness – malaria', was modelled on those produced for the Western Region.84 Marie Neurath made a first rough for it during her second stay in the Western Region that later went through several revisions.
