copies carry the date 1958 and state that it was prepared and tested by John Burton'. The nature of this testing is unclear but may help explain the three-year period that elapsed between design and production. The poster-leaflet's text was initially printed in English, but with sufficient space left around it to allow for the overprinting of a vernacular text, depending on the poster-leaflet's intended region of distribution – presumably anywhere in West Africa that malaria was endemic.8ª

## Reaching the people

Having completed a sizeable number of projects in the Western Region of Nigeria, with further projects underway there and elsewhere, work was begun on a Buffalo Books capabilities booklet that offers perhaps the clearest statement of the partnership's aims, which had previously been only implicit in its activities. The booklet, entitled 'Reaching the people' and whose text was probably written by Frame Smith, had several intended functions.8⁶ It would describe the knowledge, expertise, and experience Buffalo Books could offer its clients, illustrate completed projects, and position its services in a larger context of spreading post-war democracy, social provision, and development, about which governments needed to inform their citizens and win support. The opening paragraphs of the text are rousing and optimistic, and worth quoting at length:

This has been called the Age of the Common Man. It is certainly the age in which, all over the world, the common man is becoming concerned as never before with the public responsibilities extending far beyond the traditional framework of family, village or tribe. New democracies are developing, in which men and women have votes, and their votes not only affect the destinies of their own peoples but in turn affect the history of the world, for the interdependence of all the countries and communities of the world becomes daily more apparent and inescapable.

Everywhere men and women see opening before them new opportunities and possibilities of better and fuller life for themselves and for their children. They demand the means to seize these opportunities and to realise these possibilities.

To meet the challenge, new political and social organisations are being evolved, new forms of relationship are being established between State and State, between government and people, between public authority and private enterprise, between management and labour.

85. The poster-leaflet also states that it was produced 'with the assistance of the Shell Company of West Africa'. Shell's support probably had a commercial dimension: in 195o it had introduced the insecticide Dieldrin for use against mosquitoes and other insects.

86. [Isabel Frame-Smith], 'Reaching the people: Buffalo Books Ltd', draft text (typescript), undated, but probably 1956 (IC 3.2/178); the text is subtitled: 'A specialised advisory and technical service for the designing and production of printed and pictorial matter to the requirements of oversea {sic] Government Departments Public Relations Officers, Local Authorities, Public Corporations, Trade Commissioners, Tourist Developments Agencies, Literature Bureaux, Political Organisations, Industrial and Commercial Enterprises, etc.' Quotations that follow in this section are from this text. Isotype working material includes numerous roughs for an eight-page colour booklet. No printed copy of the booklet is present among the working material, raising uncertainty about whether it was ever completed.

87. Also mentioned is work in which the Isotype Institute apparently played no part: 'In the Western Region of Nigeria, Buffalo Books Limited have entered into a special arrangement with the Government under which they have undertaken, as Managing Agent, the planning, installation and running of the Government Printing Corporation's factory at Ibadan for the manufacture of school exercise books.' Elsewhere in lsotype working material mention is made several times of a colour printing works that was apparently also to be established in the Western Region with the help of Buffalo Books, in part to support a visual aids office. See letters: Frame Smith to Marie Neurath, 24 February 1955; Marie Neurath to John Ellis and Evelyn Worboys, 6 April 1955 (IC 3.2/171). There is evidence, too, that Buffalo Books was consulting on the production of the electoral register for the Western Region, presumably in advance of the 1956 regional elections; see letter Peter H. Balmer to Marie Neurath, 29 June 1955 (ic 3.2/173; working material for the booklet, Voting).

None of this great popular movement can go forward successfully, none of the organisations can function, none of the relationships can be developed without means of communication.

Communications is the essential tool of progress. Recognising this, governments, public authorities, political leaders and business enterprises are going to immense trouble to discover the most effective and practical means of spreading information amongst the people. It is a new problem, for which little guidance can be found in the past experience of Western countries. Much has been learned in the past few years, but much has still to be learned. It is not even a single problem, for every locality and community has its own special conditions calling for its own special approach, and even in one country the conditions vary greatly from place to place and from time to time.

In the sections that follow, emphasis is placed on decisions made by the authorities concerned' about the nature, content, and general approach to the messages they wished to send out. The implementation of these decisions – the design and production of messages able to communicate with maximum effectiveness – was thereafter a technical matter, for which Buffalo Books was well suited. The partnership drew on the complementary expertise of Adprint, the Isotype Institute, and Purnell and Sons, expertise that was of the utmost relevance to the problem of communication of information or ideas in countries where literacy is not universal or is of recent acquisition.

The text goes on to describe the range of experience Buffalo Books had acquired since 1952, beginning with Forward, which is described as largely the work of African writers and artists'; other projects mentioned included the election booklets and posters for the Western Region and Sierra Leone, the Volta River Project booklet for the Gold Coast, the series of booklets on government policy and development in the Western Region and for the Action Group, and the leaflets produced for Plant Protection Ltd. Mention is made of Marie Neurath's visits to West Africa to study the background and visual experience of the people and to work out the best method of presenting facts to them in a simple and understandable way', experience that built on previous work in India, Mexico, Britain, and the USA (work in the Soviet Union is not mentioned). While the text acknowledged that Buffalo Books had been active mainly in West Africa, it was felt that this experience could, with 'suitable adaptation', be applied to other countries where, though conditions may be very different, similar basic problems arise.'87

![](images/ba12c8f3345af849de23767d995171e99a17b52dc596fb99bf72b116d174eefe.jpg)

![](images/ecd26588e4c874fe50622fcb81ec612dbee3d12f242fc4040bc7381674859ba5.jpg)

Roughs for 'Reaching the people'. c.1956. Cover (204 × 163 mm) and pages.

The cover (above) shows groups of people from the regions mentioned in the text (left to right: West Africa, UK/usA, Mexico, India); illustrations are from the booklets Roads and Better farming for better living, from the Perenox leaflet for Plant Protection Ltd., and a poster from the registration and voting work.

Pages from other roughs show identifiable projects, and two organization diagrams of the Buffalo Books partnership.

## An end of work

With its impressive range of projects and hopeful declarations, 'Reaching the people' was part of ongoing efforts to generate new work in West Africa and (by intention at least) in other developing regions. Marie Neurath's visit to the Gold Coast in late 1955 was part of this effort: in addition to managing work on the Volta River Project booklet while there, she and Frame Smith planned for her to meet various Gold Coast officials, to encourage a programme of work like that underway in the Western Region.8⁸ After her stay in the Gold Coast, Marie Neurath's onward journey to the Western Region was apparently also seen as an opportunity to not only look after existing projects and strengthen relationships, but to encourage new projects as well.89

In the early months of 1956, after Marie Neurath's return from West Africa, the Isotype Institute concentrated its efforts on completing the second series of booklets and posters for the Western Region, and the Volta River Project booklet for the Gold Coast. Correspondence and working material from March and April list other projects apparently active in the Western Region.9º But frustration is occasionally registered that the status of some projects is unclear for lack of communication from the Western Regional Government, while other signs of strain in the relationship can also be detected, no doubt the result of working at a distance, with its accompanying delays, expense, and logistical challenges.9¹ Soon after, correspondence and datable working material for the Western Region ceases, implying no new projects getting started, only the marshalling of existing ones toward completion.

88. 'Gold Coast contacts for Mary', typewritten notes compiled by Frame Smith (iC 3.2/167). Among the people listed (and in places amusingly described) were British colonial civil servants, Gold Coast ministers and other officials (including in the Information Services Department), and the Prime Minister. It is unclear if Marie Neurath met Nkrumah at this time. In her memoir, 'What I remember', this third trip to West Africa is referred to only in passing.

89. There are few references in Isotype working material to Marie Neurath's activities during this visit to the Western Region. Frame Smith also made several trips to West Africa during this period, to expedite projects and develop new contacts and relationships; and plans were made for Wolfgang Foges to visit the region, but this apparently never happened.

There seems to be no single reason why Isotype Institute work in West Africa ended. As mentioned, the acknowledged efficiency of the Information Services Department in the Gold Coast seemed to forestall projects there, while in Sierra Leone the booklet and posters for the 1957 elections were perhaps too limited in scope to provide impetus for other work. In the Western Region, where by far the greatest number of projects had been completed, their cessation may be explained by the simple fact that the programme of public relations outlined by Marie Neurath in her memorandum of 1954 had been largely achieved, leaving little else to do. But the end of work can also be linked to the abandoned plan to train locals and establish a visual aids office in the Western Region. Had the plan gone ahead, the relationship between the Isotype Institute and the Western Regional Government would surely have continued. But it seems that the government – and perhaps Awolowo, in particular – was unable to envision a future for visual aids and so demurred from any long-term commitment. Signs of disengagement may also be read into the government's failure to provide the Isotype Institute with substantive feedback on its work (Rotimi Williams's remarks notwithstanding), despite Marie Neurath's repeated requests for it to do so. Possibly, too, there was an inability to shift the application of visual aids away from the sphere of public relations where they were originally situated, into an educational context where their applications could have been expanded. Efforts to do this, it seems, had been pursued by Marie Neurath and Frame Smith in relation to Stephen Awokoya, the dynamic Minister of Education."² But in the first half 1956, Awokoya was relieved of his portfolio and with his departure any influence he might have brought to widening the use of Isotype would have been lost as well.9ª

In her recollections, Marie Neurath takes note of the failure to initiate a local training scheme in the Western Region, and to establish a visual aids office there, concluding that 'the whole situation was too uncertain in the long run for such a plan.' Her remark, though unspecific, clearly refers at least in part to the broader dynamics of government and politics in the Western Region at the time. There is little doubt that the several years of Isotype activity coincide with a period of regional opportunity and growing autonomy brought about by the constitutional changes of 1953-4, a process that apparently occasioned

94. 'What I remember', pp.98-9.

95. In 1957–8, further amendments were made to the constitution that redirected some powers to the centre, to strengthen federal mechanisms in advance of independence (1g6o); these were accompanied by a redistribution of regional income also towards the centre, which was disadvantageous to the Western Region's cocoaderived prosperity.

96. In his autobiography (cited above), published in 196o as he made the transition from Premier of the Western Region to Leader of the Opposition in the federal parliament, Awolowo makes no reference to the work of the Isotype Institute, though he does mention other achievements in public relations (p. 289). In his foreword, however, he thanks Wolfgang Foges and Frame Smith at length for their help in bringing the book to publication. Awolowo's fortunes after independence were mixed. Following a constitutional crisis and the declaration of a state of emergency in the Western Region in 1962, precipitated by disagreements between Awolowo and his successor, Samuel Akinloye, Awolowo was arrested, tried and convicted on charges of treason; he was jailed in 1963. He survived the military coup of 1966 that resulted in the assassination of prime minister Abubakar Tafawa Balewa and other political figures. He was brought back into government through appointment but later failed to win election to the country's presidency despite two attempts. Awolowo is remembered as a largely unifying figure and for his policies of social welfare and development, for the high quality of the Western Region's civil service during his time as Premier, and for his firmly-held view that a federal state with strong regions organized along cultural and linguistic lines was the best arrangement for Nigeria.

97. 'What I remember, p. 99.

Awolowo's original contact with and subsequent interest in Isotype. But by 1956, the work of encouraging and communicating regional identity and policy, insofar as Isotype was concerned, was largely in hand and so interest seems to have slackened. This, too, coincides with the evolving position of the Western Region within Nigeria as a whole, as new rounds of constitutional changes in 1957–8 began to shift power away from the region and towards a federal centre, as the country advanced towards independence.9⁵ Awolowo's concerns as Premier of the Western Region had probably moved on and would indeed be much concentrated on own his political fortunes in the years ahead.º⁶

## International relations

Whatever reasons may be assigned to the end of work in the Western Region and elsewhere in West Africa, the Isotype Institute's engagement there was nevertheless significant and resulted in work of a high standard. The people Marie Neurath encountered in many different contexts were, it seems, enthusiastic about Isotype as a method for showing and explaining through intelligible and recognizable means those developments that would affect their future. In her memoir, written some 25 years after the West African experience, Marie Neurath recounts it in some detail. But she offers little by way of summary, only that (as quoted at the start of this chapter) 'it was good that we were able to have this experience. Otto had said that we were creating the method not, finally, for the Viennese, but rather for the Africans, and so it proved.' She continued, 'That we had to alter the symbols for man, woman, house and so on in the process, was within the scope of the rule that the symbols should "speak", that is, be immediately understandable. The international character was maintained in the basic approach.'97

These remarks, apparently the last about West Africa she committed to writing, summarize issues about pictograms (symbols), graphic configurations, and the work of tranformation. They round off similar statements made at greater length earlier, in three articles published in the fifteen or so years after projects in West Africa had ended. In each, Marie Neurath offers observations about the nature of working in international contexts including West Africa, and how these contexts compelled an expansion of Isotype's features, while at the same time confirming its 'basic approach'. Thus in ‘The origin and theory of Isotype' (196o), she wrote in regard to pictograms:

The international character of the symbols has occasionally been tested, but only in the Western world and in temperate climates. ... New symbols were needed for tropical Africa for such things as crops, tools, animals, man, woman, child, house, etc. The existing symbols had to be altered to make Isotype charts comprehensible to these Africans.

These, she went to remark, should be accompanied by alterations to the larger scheme of graphic depiction as well:

Every chart has to represent a familiar visual background – adherence to the method cannot go as far as imposing an alien background on those unable to share one's experience of it.

## And yet this only needed to go so far:

Any modifications of shapes and arrangement does not imply that the system had to be radically changed. The Isotype system is an elaboration of the child's approach which is more or less universal, and it appeals to a certain level of commonsense. Anthropologists who tend to concentrate on the differences between peoples predicted that Isotype would be misunderstood and misleading, but there has been no evidence of this at all. In adapting the method to the needs of the semi-literate, only a certain slowing down of the rate at which new ideas are represented is required. ... A whole booklet was designed to instruct the people of the Western Region of Nigeria how to become registered voters. The high percentage who responded showed how well the message was grasped.98

In 1971, Marie Neurath returned to the issue of the pictograms, in particular which ones and how many were required for an international' picture language, and, in this respect, what the West Africa work had led her to conclude:

The number of symbols cannot be foreseen: new tasks and subject matters often require new symbols. The method and the approach are, I think, more universal than the symbols are. I had to discover this when I worked for Africans for some time. I had to make things clear to them, and I could not force our international symbols' on them. Many symbols, of man, woman, house, tree, field, etc. had to be specially designed for them. When things are equal all over the world the symbols can be the same. 99

## And finally, in 1974:

Becoming citizens of the world we gradually recognised that our symbolism was often international for western man only. More than before I recognised this when I had to work out ways of informing the Nigerian people about health, education, agriculture, voting etc., in

98. Marie Neurath, 'The origin and theory of Isotype', pp. 117-8. These quotations are taken from the section, 'The universality of Isotype'. The article includes an illustration of a polling station adapted from pp. 4-5 of Voting, general election 1957, Sierra Leone (1956/7).

99. Marie Neurath, 'Otto Neurath and Isotype', p. 19. The article illustrates pp.10-11 of the booklet, Voting in the Western Region of Nigeria.

100. Marie Neurath, 'Isotype' (1974), p. 147. 101. International picture language (1936), pp. 32, 40.

visual terms. Man, woman, house, plants, markets, trees ... all had to be drawn in a different way to be understandable in that country. Also the approach, the speed of information, the colour scheme, the ways to catch the attention – all had to be different. 100

In these remarks, Marie Neurath remains consistent with Otto Neurath's conception of Isotype as a method of visual education that was comprehensible internationally and whose pictograms (and by extension other graphic depictions) could be supplemented or updated as required. Otto Neurath's view, however, was that this should be done in a restrained manner, stipulating in International picture language that pictograms should be contained in 'one general list of a limited number of signs needed for international use', carefully designed and accessioned under the control of one chief organization', by implication to insure their international effectiveness.1º1 Marie Neurath, in light of the West Africa experience, seemingly arrived at a more pragmatic position in accommodating international differences. This included the use of pictograms and graphic depictions whose pictorial content (or elements of it) would be meaningful locally but not always more widely, and also the transformation of narrative content into configurations wħose structure, pace, and density were adapted to culturally and linguistically specific audiences. One can only speculate on whether Otto Neurath's outlook might have evolved in a similar direction had he finally met the developing world of the colonies. Certainly the production of Isotype elsewhere, in the Soviet Union and the United States for example, signalled his enthusiasm to take Isotype into other cultures where work was done under – and indeed partly in response to – local conditions.

Looking back again at the sum of Isotype work, those projects completed for the Gold Coast, Sierra Leone, and the Western Region of Nigeria represent Isotype's farthest international extension. The need to respond directly to circumstances of culture, language, and governance in these territories brought productive challenges to the Isotype Institute, and salutary results. Marie Neurath in her recollections describes at length and in detail not only the work that was done but also the many and varied people and places she encountered. For her, these experiences were crucial to getting the work right. And yet in this new and unfamiliar context, there was much that remained typical of Isotype, whether in the 'basic approach' of the work, in the wish to establish indigenous, self-standing visual aids production, or in the location of work in the broader currents of progressive, social

democratic development, welfare, and enfranchisement – in the Western Region of Nigeria above all. But the work done in the Western Region in particular prompts another observation, of the close and sometimes instrumental relationship that developed between the Isotype Institute and the Western Regional Government, its party in power and its policies. The relationship was an implicitly sympathetic one ideologically, though with it came all the social and political uncertainties of approaching independence. The price for the Isotype Institute, it seems, in Nigeria but also elsewhere in British colonial West Africa, was to operate in regions whose aspirations but unsettled circumstances meant that opportunities for work would only be short-lived.

![](images/90e8e901e81fb80d08350c38c9f8b62aba807ed9a60fe5c0ea4ff06a05e97b04.jpg)  
Original artwork by Gerd Arntz for 'unemployed' man and woman. 1933. Actual size. (iC 4/10)

## 12 Pictogram design Vienna and beyond

Christopher Burke

It is commonly assumed that the prevalence of pictograms in today's public signing is the principal legacy of Isotype. But it is difficult to establish a direct connection. Otto and Marie Neurath considered Isotype to be more than the sum of its pictograms – it was a method for making 'visual arguments'. Otto Neurath explained this to a client (referring to pictograms as ‘symbols’):

I maintain the point: it is not the use of some symbols [that is] the essential element, but the procedures [for] HOw TO USE vIsUAL AIDs FOR AN ARGUMENT. That is the reason why we object to the application of our tools WITHOUT ISOTYPE TRANSFORMATION. You see, our international prestige is based on the COMPLETE ISOTYPE CHARTS, not on the symbols as such (of which we think that they are carefully made according to the general visual principles). We start with our dictionary of about 2ooo symbols (printing blocks etc.) only as BRıCks, and as an architecture is not characterized by the ELEMENTs only of which it is composed but by the PATTERN of the architecture, so our IsOTYPE method is characterized by the pattern and the procedures how the pattern is reached.

Nevertheless, the pictograms designed by Gerd Arntz for Isotype from the late 192os display elements of standardization, modularity, and schematization that mark them as precursors of the sets of pictograms developed for public information decades later.

Marie Neurath recalled the attempts at defining the graphic style of the Vienna Method during its formative years:

The art education of that time in Vienna did not favour what we required. The artist Bruno Zuckermann was one of the few for whom the schematization was compatible; but his angular forms were not yet what Otto wanted. ... Things went better with the Swiss Erwin Bernath than with the Viennese; for about ten years he was a reliable colleague,

1. Letter Neurath to M. Wynants, 16 October 1942 (IC 1/7). Capitalization in original.

![](images/0f4de6b33448803df9db3b4128d0f141c1c6b30582cee8d92b953ec178f1d45d.jpg)

T56h Police interventions in Vienna, February 1925 1925

Solid figures = under influence of alcohol; outline figures = not under influence of alcohol.

leaving us only in the depth of the crisis in Holland. ... The achievement of symbol-like simplification was not in his blood – as it was with the man whom Otto finally found: Gerd Arntz.²

Indeed Otto Neurath was candid about the shortcomings of pictogram design during the early years of the Vienna Method:

As we can only draw on a weakly developed tradition, new forms that are immediately clear must be created at the Gesellschafts- und Wirtschaftsmuseum. Drastic, even grotesque figures achieve that, but they distract the viewer all too easily. ... All of this presses us to leave behind interesting' representations, but above all to sacrifice naturalism.³

In particular, Neurath examined the inadequacy of pictograms used in some charts of 1925:

2. Marie Neurath, 'What I   
remember', p. 35.   
3. 'Statistische Hieroglyphen' (1926), p. 41.

Initially, the Museum tried to enliven the graphics; for example, in the chart on police interventions. This kind of enlivening distracts from the essential theme of the chart; one is more interested in the individual gestures than in the statistical relations. Because one is not acquainted with the the individual cases of arrest, this says more than one knows. If one knows nothing except ‘arrests under the influence of alcohol', one must just make a single type for it and repeat that to the degree that the statistical data requires. There was at first a certain timidity about repetition to be overcome.

![](images/f0cc83d4553bdbba84007766b35d1fb0820751d5d429749e254f37557e385601.jpg)

![](images/71ef067c60e73d21de035835e520a28a1b802f72d5ea3c7a907594128f126cb4.jpg)  
Births and deaths in Vienna 1925 Two charts using the same data: the earlier version is on the left. (See pp.149-50 above for further discussion of these charts.)

The changes made between two charts about Births and deaths in Vienna' (shown here) were explained by Neurath as follows:

Coffins were replaced by gravestones, because in guided tours [of the Museum] it was noticed that certain visitors thought the chart was concerned with comparison of births and infant mortality. This misunderstanding is thoroughly supported by the coffins having the same size as the children. To enlarge the coffins would not work well, because then the weight' of the pictograms would be much too different. ... So now the gravestone has been chosen; it gives no suggestion of the age of the dead. This rather detailed discussion of one example may show what sorts of questions of representation lead to modifications. Nothing is more dangerous than a pictogram that says more to some visitors than one really wished to express.

One clear principle of pictogram design emerges from Otto Neurath's early writings about the Vienna Method: they should be units of a picture script (Bilderschrift):

Above all pictorial signs must be created that can be read’, just as we can all read letters and experts can read musical notation. It is a matter of creating a kind of hieroglyphic script suitable for international application.

It was necessary that these signs be suitable for repetition in sequence along a line, to indicate statistical quantity; this differentiates Isotype

## 4. 'Schwarzweißgrafik'(1926), p. 55.

5. 'Statistische Hieroglyphen (1926), p. 40. See also 'Bildliche Darstellung sozialer Tatbestände' (1926), p. 59. For more on the concepts of 'picture script' and 'picture language' in Isotype, see Burke, 'The linguistic status of Isotype'.

![](images/2709665b0fea7f4b3c4926cb0e9ad61b70923b1193234b3e210e14e31a128dbd.jpg)

Versions of the same pictograms with qualifying symbols, made in different sizes. From a single page of the the Isotype 'picture dictionary' (iC 4/1). Note that the number of cogs or spokes on the wheels are reduced in the smaller versions; also that the shape of the head changes slightly.

6. 'Statistische Hieroglyphen' (1926), pp. 40, 49. Neurath repeated the comparison with letters throughout his life: for example, in International picture language (1936, p. 32): 'They have to be different from one another, so that there will be no doubt of their right name, when they are seen again. They have to be so simple that they may be put in lines like letters.' And in From hieroglyphics to Isotype (1945, p. 105): 'A further essential element of Isotype symbols is that they should be suitable for assembling together like the characters of a written or printed line.'

7. 'Das Sachbild' (193o), p. 163. In 'Visual education' (1945, p. 308), he wrote: 'Enlarged newspaper print does not look like the print in large type, as types are specially designed for their various sizes.' Michael Twyman gives examples of this phenomenon at work in Isotype pictograms in his essay, 'Observations on Isotype symbols and their varied applications'.

8. Marie Neurath, 'Isotype' (1974), p.138.

9. Marie Reidemeister, 'Die moderne Hieroglyphenschrift' (1928), p. 4.

10. 'Form und Farbe der Mengenbilder des Gesellschafts- und Wirtschaftsmuseums' (1929), p. 193. This text is uncredited, but the voice is Neurath's; it may have been co-written with Marie Reidemeister.

pictograms from their successors in public signing, in which each pictogram normally appears only once on individual signs. Neurath likened Isotype pictograms to alphabetic characters in typesetting: 'There must initially exist a simple wish to create international, statistical letters, with which one can typeset picture tables.'

Pictogram design at the Vienna Museum can be further likened to typeface design due to the sensitivity of both Neurath and Arntz to optical scale. Neurath commented: 'A bigger symbol can under certain circumstances be more richly formed than the smaller. The main thing is that the symbol remains unaltered in its basic features.'’ Marie Neurath recalled that Otto Neurath

spent much time with Arntz, to create the basic man symbol, which could stand on its own, in rows side by side or in groups, which could carry symbols or be modified to show certain distinctions. Outline symbols were also needed beside the usual full colour ones. Different sizes were considered, the simplifications necessary when size was reduced etc.8

In 1928 Marie Reidemeister had summarized overlapping principles of pictogram design for the Vienna Method:

These pictures should not be too naturalistic. An over-precise adherence to details would distract from the true content of the picture, which lies in the quantitative relationships. It should also be remembered that we are dealing not only with pictures, but also with kinds of characters' in a script; this means that we must see to it that the individual characters could be reused in other circumstances, in order to facilitate rapid reading through recognition.º

Otto Neurath explained this in terms that evoke the old' Sachlichkeit (objectivity) of the German Werkbund:

Since the aim of the quantity-pictures from the Gesellschafts- und Wirtschaftsmuseum is to teach, the educational consideration must come first. The individual forms must be clear and understandable, the layout and choice of colour must lead to attentiveness. For this purpose very specific ground rules have arisen and a unified style is being developed. This style is determined by objects to such a far-reaching degree that it has become the style of the whole group of workers at the Museum and that only an initiate can recognize the individual creator.

The figures must be recognizable. On the other hand they must be repeated in the quantity-pictures, which requires simplification. The figures must not function like individuals, rather as symbols for a genus [Gattung].1º

![](images/78b829eba8f4685ebab2c4ca9d328a70ad1bbcce46b6924bd06dc49918f4c2e9.jpg)

Pages for ‘man' from the Isotype 'picture dictionary'. Begun c. 193o in Vienna, with later additions: no.337 on the right page dates from c. 1936. (ic 4/1)

In the booklet Health education by Isotype (1939, p.6), Neurath and his co-author H.E. Kleinschmidt stated: 'In making these designs, only the barest essentials are used; all immaterial details are omitted. For example the symbol representing “"man” .. is unmistakable, yet there are no eyes, ears, buttons, or shoe laces. This lack of detail is an advantage, for details detour our attention away from the main idea or train of thought. The educated man and the pre-school child alike recognize this symbol as man in its generic sense.'

Neurath set Arntz the task of instituting precisely such an impersonal' style at the Museum (see Arntz's recollection on p.72 above). Neurath was not seeking to smother individuality by stating that pictograms should signify categories of people or things; instead this was a functional requirement of the modular graphic technique he wanted to develop for depicting social themes. He expressed this succinctly towards the end of his life: 'A symbol cannot give all qualities; there will have to be pars pro toto and specimen for species.'ⁿ Photographs hardly figured in Isotype for this reason. After several years of experience with the Vienna Method, Neurath commented: 'In very rare cases one can use photographs instead of individual signs. This can only really be considered when, for example, some uncommon process is represented statistically, for which a sign cannot be employed.'1² (See overleaf.)

11. 'Visual education' (1945), in Neurath, Empiricism and sociology p. 244; see also Nemeth & Stadler (ed.), Encyclopedia and Utopia, p.320. 12. Bildstatistik nach Wiener Methode in der Schule (1933), p.282.

## T71f Dermotubine embrocation in the first year of elementary school for diagnosing tuberculosis infection c.1928

The photograph is used as a repeated pictogram to compare numbers of infected and uninfected children (differentiated by superimposed colour in the original) in two areas of Vienna.

## T398e Healing of a broken thighbone 1930

14 cases handled in accident hospital of Vienna 18 cases handled elsewhere

Here photographs are used for the opposite purpose of pictograms, to indicate individual character.

## 13. 'Das Sachbild' (1930), pp.155, 158.

14. 'Bildstatistiken des Gesellschafts- und Wirtschaftsmuseums in Wien' (1932), p. 212.

![](images/d22a46e84eb3a00ae40d31b2fca8aa5c9888b5f41863422accc00d603db3a17c.jpg)  
Heilung eines Oberschenkelbruches  
Fale  Unfallkrankenhous Wien behandelt - Prmarius Dr Larenz Bohler

![](images/14d7c0869efd68ff7ec317bad3eca339cc18b96b6258c57f23df2f4aabe033fb.jpg)  
18 Falle ousworts behandelt

![](images/ed00cc0427adf2e63fd93e8e287522993d2ac8a712ed2c0f8a141fbc853e267f.jpg)

In 193o, at the point when the Vienna Method was reaching maturity, Neurath reflected on the task of pictogram design:

But then one must ask, how should such signs be designed? Should one reproduce the objects in the most naturalistic way possible, or would the spirit of enlightenment be best served by giving them a minimum of features? ...

Here standardization is inevitable.¹³

The Museum's internal guideline notes, which date from around the same time, stated: 'The pictogram may not denote more than is necessary to the statement of facts for which it is chosen.' When proposing that 'characters must be designed, the simpler the better', Otto Neurath issued a warning: 'But let this not awaken designers' ambitions! Standardization is here the requirement.’ 14

While the standard Isotype pictogram for [hu]man' was intended to generically denote all kinds of people, the creators of Isotype were never concerned to boil down the depiction of all material life to a limited number of pictograms. The Isotype picture dictionary' was always being expanded, with local or national differences depicted when necessity arose. Indeed, where different costumes were needed to convey the theme of a chart, a great variety of clothing was depicted. Yet some uniformity had to be accepted for depicting a worker', for example, as Neurath considered:

![](images/5ce85b83033232e6cefe864b6a617aad648a160bb2052e6b0d1a52bbd3cfe3b0.jpg)

It is a difficult problem, to describe workers of different lands differently. It is indeed a distinguishing characteristic of our time that forms of clothing are becoming more similar to one another, so that one cannot differentiate a German, French or English worker at a mere glance.1⁵

Pages for 'worker' from the Isotype 'picture dictionary'. c. 1932 (ic 4/3). This version contains various examples made for work done at Izostat in the ussR. On the right page, aspects of typical Russian dress have been icorporated into the pictograms.

However, some attempts were made to add national character to pictograms representing workers in the ussR.

The Isotype pictogram for woman' may seem dated to twenty-firstcentury eyes due to the clothing that defines the figure (see overleaf). Arntz recalled that, even around 193o in Vienna, there was criticism from the women's movement that we made the skirts too short or too long'.¹⁶ But Neurath felt that current fashion could not be the sole consideration:

Giving a sign its fixed form for international use, probably for a great number of years, is responsible work. The signs have to be like good letters. It will not do to take the taste of the present day as our only guide; we have to take into account the experience of history. 17

15. 'Statistische Hieroglyphen' (1926), pp. 42-3.   
16. 'Gerd Arntz', interview in Pulchri, p. 4.   
17. International picture language (1936), pp, 40–1.

![](images/faefa27a2127f628fa003491da18f81ca31701c25c3a7dcd1f9d8b56b5eeb5cf.jpg)

Pages for 'costume' and 'woman' from the Isotype 'picture dictionary'. c. 1930 (1C 4/1-2).

18. Bildstatistik nach Wiener Methode in der Schule (1933) p. 279. 19. Marie Neurath, '26 September 1924 and after’, p. 6o.

By this hè meant that symbols/pictograms used in the past should be taken into account, but also the traditional appearance of things. (An example from today is the persistence of the pictogram for telephone' in the form of an old-fashioned apparatus.)

The Museum's guideline notes on pictogram design were substantial (see the translation in chapter 2), and included a recommendation to stress 'the rectangular forms of the male and the rounded forms of the female' in imitation of the 'manifestations of nature'. The comparison of sharp and round forms might also provide a graphic distinction required internally by the Isotype picture-lexicon. Neurath suggested this: 'Experience has shown that it is not sufficient to contrast trousers and skirt, it is important to introduce different forms of the head for the eye skimming along the rows.'18 This is intriguing, implying a principle similar to that detected in typeface legibility studies, whereby the top half of letters are most important for reading. Indeed a conscious difference in the shape of male and female heads can be detected in pictograms from around 193o onwards. This was what Marie Neurath referred to when she recalled that 'the language principle had to be followed in every detail' during Neurath's long hours of collaboration with Arntz and his colleagues.19

## Original drawings by Gerd Arntz for pictograms. Ink or paper. 1933. Shown at 65% of actual size. The dimensional marking c '9 cm' for height implies tha these were to be reduced photographically, not cut in lino as usual at the Vienna Museum. These drawings were made for charts of 'standard' pictograms issued by the Mundaneum in Vienna (reproduced overleaf). (Above and left) 'Man', 'woman'. (Below) 'White collar worker' (with beret); 'worker' (front and profile).

These charts all bear the signature 'Standard Mundaneum Vienna' and date from 1933. They were probably intended to serve as models for the Mundaneum branches. The experiences of adapting the Vienna Method to the ussR and of attempting to introduce it to the usA may also have spurred this standardizing approach. The use of Arntz's artwork for Mundaneum standards explains why only these pictogram drawings survive from the Vienna period: they must have been taken to the office of the Mundaneum and International Foundation for Visual Education in The Hague when that became the refuge for the core of the Vienna staff in 1934.

![](images/9d4771fdba025b58bf42586639c4f6cc1873d7b312d3edac994b47d0a96d5f54.jpg)

Exhibition charts made at the Vienna Mundaneum for showing in London, 1933. 420 × 63o mm. (iC 7.2/15)

The original signature on these charts stated: 'Mundaneum Wien'; this was pasted over with the Isotype name and symbol during the 1940s.

![](images/7e3a1877cd7ae18c0613ae8962d068c83d3ff549be25c89debc9f49af21030c8.jpg)

In general, pictograms from the mature period of Isotype are not excessively geometric, and much less so than some of their successors in public signing. Human figures in particular are marked by an attachment to a recognizable human silhouette. Again, this was deliberate: Arntz recalled that he and his fellow artists of the Progressives 'were no pure constructivists, we were too sensory for that. We could not forsake round, womanly forms in our pictures, in contrast to the Dutch and Russian constructivists.'2º Neurath stated: 'Viewers are already far more accustomed to a departure from naturalism in terms of colour than in form. A green person meets with less resistance than one that is squared-up [eckiger]’.21

## T143 Parliamentary elections in Berlin c. 1929

Some early experiments were made at the Museum with more geometric human figures. The early chart on method (1926; p. 146 above) shows a variant intended for counting the heads of figures, in which the head is a circle floating free of the body. This proposal did not survive long in the Vienna Method; perhaps it was thought that internal breaks in the body compromised the integrity of pictograms when repeated in lines. (One later exception is the symbol/brandmark for Isotype itself, which is never repeated within a chart.) However, the severed, circular head became a common motif of public information pictograms designed from the 196os onwards. Masaru Katzumie's pictograms for the 1964 Tokyo Olympics included this feature, as did Otl Aicher's for the Munich Olympics. These examples led to the set of pictograms designed by Roger Cook and Don Shanosky in 1974 for the United States Department of Transportation (DOT), which include the human pictograms most commonly used in signs for public toilets today.

This chart from Arntz's first year working in Vienna shows human pictograms that have not quite reached the mature Isotype style. Note the oval head of the figure just right of centre.

![](images/77805f5484d0091248a92a01b2ce32e86e5895024eb326d2828a8b5721cdc410.jpg)  
Isotype pictograms, C.1930.

![](images/f9bfa2f375c02f948216ce1e279a7168e2b643cf3cfcc2b10c417bff80b1a1cb.jpg)  
An early proposal by the Vienna Museum, 1926.

![](images/47f0839df69c970f52654eb7a4faf04af01020d0fffd3a5543f20cdb3af47dd8.jpg)  
Tokyo Olympics, 1964.

![](images/e83f76ad509e2c042d494be38be9ee699f2dcece27ade7899cc972a91b4c35d8.jpg)  
Munich Olympics, 1972.

![](images/c52a7fb55aca45102dc210cfbafb25fb5e2de1754b245fd2bc84ddd3508a5aae.jpg)

DOT pictograms, 1974.

Pages from International picture language (1936) showing a proposal for design and placement of traffic signs to indicate a change in which side of the road to drive on. The use of a triangular sign with red outline to warn of danger accords with the standards proposed in 1931 by the Geneva conference of the League of Nations on traffic signs (and subsequently ratified by several countries including Austria and the Netherlands).

23. International picture language (1936), p. 37. Neurath repeated this suggestion in consultation for the New York World's Fair of 1939 (see p.340 above, n. 145).

24. See Kinross, 'Neurath one-off roadsign was years ahead of its time', Design, no. 369, 1979, p.13.

25. A League of Nations conference on urban traffic signs took place in Vienna in 1927. See the useful summary of landmarks in traffic sign standardization in Aicher & Krampen, Zeichensysteme, pp. 106–7.

26. Bildstatistik nach Wiener Methode in der Schule (1933), p. 283.

![](images/df37641ef551b99ec72cfeba92ad816bfccf3d0f7f8479510be0d53ab125c4bb.jpg)

Isotype pictograms were never applied to public information signs in practice; the closest they came to being used in this way was a proposal made by Marie Neurath for signposts to the organizers of the Festival of Britain in 1951, but this was not accepted. Otto Neurath commented that things designed for one purpose were often adapted successfully to another, and he suggested this explicitly for Isotype pictograms in International picture language: 'Most of the signs may be used not only in lines but as separate designs.'²³ He gave as an example the automnobile pictogram, which was shown both in a statistical chart and in some proposals for traffic signs.²⁴ Neurath was no doubt aware of the efforts towards standardizing traffic signs which had accelerated during the 192os and early 193os,2⁵ but he did not live long enough to see the development of pictogram systems during the 195os and 196os. Standardized pictograms on signs in public places were not common in his lifetime, yet he foresaw their utility for such purposes. In 1933 he wrote:

Conventional signs are usual especially in cartography. Some of these signs would be classed as speaking signs in the sense of the Vienna Method. Also certain crate-labelling pictograms that indicate content can be counted here; the lightning sign to indicate strong current, and many other signs in transport, hotels, etc., the systematic organization of which is surely only a question of time.

A universal lexicon of all signs must be centrally worked out and distributed internationally. It is advisable that we use as far as possible the same signs for quantity-pictures, cartograms, and maps, inasmuch as it is not a requirement to distinguish between statistical units and plain object description. In this way a kind of international information script [Sachschrift] would develop, which would fundamentally ease international communication [Verkehr].2⁶

![](images/e8c31de6529bf7d058fce6a6553701544c3425267baa26aa67fcc0edcbf638f1.jpg)  
Pages from International picture language (1936) showing the proposed use of pictograms on hotel signs (left) and instructional pictures for how to use a telephone (right).

Neurath attempted to instigate production of signs for transport using Isotype pictograms towards the end of the Second World War. He proposed this idea to his cousin Gustav, who managed an electroplating factory in Birmingham (uk), which was involved in munitions production at that time. Neurath envisaged that Gustav's company could produce 'pressed metal plates in colour with symbols on', suggesting that Isotype pictograms could be used 'in ships, railroads, hotels, indicating everything of general interest: Boat, Lavatory, Gentlemen, Ladies, Water, Cloak room, Luggage'. He added that, in addition to pictograms for land and sea, 'I think one should as soon as possible try to get orders for such symbols dealing with airplanes'.² Neurath died within a year of making these proposals and they were never realized. After his death some consideration was given to copyright protection of Isotype pictograms, which would perhaps have been desirable in the context of commercial manufacture of such signs. Yet Marie Neurath was reluctant to pursue the matter, indicating perhaps her continued opinion that Isotype was something more than pictograms.28 Additionally, Arntz did not work with the Isotype Institute during or after the Second World War and it relied mainly on the stock of pictograms that he had designed before the war.

The boom in international travel during the 196os prompted the standardization of pictogram sets by organizations such as the Union Internationale de Chemins de Fer (uiC), Icograda, and eventually the International Organization for Standardization (iso). Yet, before this, Marie Neurath was involved with a short project that may have helped to prepare the ground for these initiatives. In April 1956 Rudolf Modley asked her to work with him on a 'study of communications through symbols', which he had formulated at the request of the industrial designer Henry Dreyfuss. After support for it was secured from the

27. Letters Otto to Gustav Neurath, 2o January, 8 March, and 28 March 1945 (önB 1222/43). Italic corresponds to underlining in the typewritten original. Neurath told Gustav that he had begun thinking about putting this idea into practice in the Netherlands for instructions in telephone boxes using pictures instead of four languages (Dutch, English, French, German). See the proposal in International picture language (above). He had discussed with a technician there the possibility of using pressed wood pulp or plastic as a substrate, as well as pressed metal. Letter Otto to Gustav Neurath, 16 January 1945 (ÖNB 1222/43).

28. See Matthew Eve, 'Isotype in trouble, 1946–1948'. The inventor of 'Semantography', Charles K. Bliss, offered some sound advice on copyrighting isotype pictograms to Marie Neurath but, again, she declined to pursue it. Letter Bliss to Marie Neurath, 8 April 1950 (ic 1/19), published in 'Semantography and Neurath's Isotypes', Semantography series, no. 172, pp. 9-11.

Fund for the Advancement of Education (established by the Ford Foundation), she was employed for one year from June 1958 as the European representative of the project under its formal title Exploratory survey of graphic signs and symbols'. The initial aim of the survey was to collect and classify existing signs and symbols, with a view to compiling a dictionary of them.29 Marie Neurath expressed her willingness to catalogue the examples collected, and she prepared some notes on how to proceed, remarking that, in general,

we shall classify only such symbols which are

1. standardized (by usage or formal acceptance)

2. adaptable or suitable to standardization

3. self-explanatory.³º

She made contacts with organizations involved in collecting and classifying pictograms and symbols, including standardization institutes in Britain, France and Germany. She also commissioned some empirical research from psychologist Rivka Eifermann, who was then studying a related subject for her doctorate at the University of London. Eifermann tested the relative comprehensibility of affirmative and negative signs and words. Her report, along with a report on symbol classification by Martin Krampen, were significant outcomes of the exploratory survey, but it received no further funding after its initial year of activity.³¹ Although no dictionary of pictograms resulted directly from this project, Dreyfuss and Modley each went on to compile pictogram sourcebooks published in the 197os: Dreyfuss's Symbol sourcebook (1972; to which Marie Neurath contributed a short essay on Isotype) and Modley's Handbook of pictorial symbols (1976). Both men also continued to be involved in international projects for pictogram standardization.³²

Martin Krampen, who had studied visual communication with Otl Aicher at the Hochschule für Gestalting Ulm, also went on to participate

29. Letters Modley to Marie Neurath, 4 August 1956 and 20 June 1958 (IC 1/49).

30. Marie Neurath, 'Preliminary standards of classification of symbols', handwritten notes, 1 August 1958. She discussed with Modley the matter of whether to impartially record or to recommend good practice: 'For the time being we shall collect everything. Later we shall see – whether to recommend? Even our own field of Isotype symbols and their cousins wil create a problem – all variations of man symbols?' Letter Marie Neurath to Modley, 26 June 1958

31. Modley found Eifermann's results encouraging: 'Summing up the report very briefly, I would say that it shows that known words are better means of command than recently learned, not self-explanatory pictures. ... What gives me

encouragement is the fact that repetition shows a considerable improvement in results in pictures. If pictorial symbols were well-taught - or self-explanatory - they might give drastically better results than words.' Letter Modley to Marie Neurath, 10 September 1959 (ic 1/49; italic corresponds to underlining in original). Martin Krampen's report has been made available online by Steven Heller: <www.printmag.com/designeducation/learning-from-symbolsyesterday- and-today/>. On reading Modley's proposal for continuation of the survey project, Marie Neurath

in further projects concerning pictograms, and he became a prolific analyst of the subject. He criticized the approach evident at the 1964 Tokyo Olympics of designing new pictograms instead of helping to standardize already established pictographic systems (such as isoTypE)' The tendency he diagnosed continues to this day as a kind of branding for successive public events. In his view, the 1967 world exhibition held in Montreal (for which he became an adviser) offered ‘an occasion to weigh the usefulness of existing pictographic systems in international communication'; he added that 'isoTYPE still seems to represent a basically sound starting-point'.³³ But Isotype pictograms were not adopted on that occasion.³⁴

A generic idea of a geometric human pictogram familiar from public information signs has sometimes mistakenly been referred to as an Isotype man'.3⁵ While this is not strictly correct, Gerd Arntz himself recognized the connection. Late in his life he commented: 'When I now go to the station and see the pictograms, then I see the work that we did already in the thirties. Of course alterations have appeared, but in principle it has remained the same.'³⁶

The use of standardized pictograms in airports and other public buildings (in particular the AIGA's DoT series) connects with the modernist aspiration to international communication in Isotype. A postmodern usage is also now well established: pictograms are employed as a branding accessory for everything from dating agencies to left-wing pressure groups. The ubiquity of pictograms in the public environment has led to a familiarity that begins to breed contempt, or at least irreverence. Satirical and ironic variations on the geometric human pictograms now abound.³7 Indeed, in Otto Neurath's home town of Vienna, an intriguing experiment was undertaken by the city administration to subvert the traditional forms of pictograms on public information

signs. This was referred to as an example of ‘gender mainstreaming', and took the form of introducing recognizably female figures into signs traditionally occupied by male (or non-gender-specific) figures, such as that for roadworks.³⁸ It is doubtful that Isotype's creators would have considered it the role of pictograms alone to challenge preconceptions. Otto Neurath in particular was conscious of the need to accord with expectations to a large extent in design, especially for the immediacy of communication he required of Isotype. In 1931 he wrote:

Actual life prevents us from confronting' all of our customs. The ridiculous rules of the western white man's clothes are obviously deeply rooted and will change only slowly. Whoever takes up the struggle in this single field excludes himself from the rest of life. ... Even large social upheavals which create sudden changes in a short time, have only a slow effect, for traditional habits are very tenacious. 39

## And later, in 1936, he observed:

Our whole life consists in two opposite movements: in the one we tend to acquire always new concepts and to modify those that tradition has left us; but in the other we are obliged to take the traditional statements as the basis for our departure. We can never make a tabula rasa from which to begin – if I dare to say it – a new life.4º

38. See <www.wien.gv.at/ english/administration/gendermainstreaming/example.html>; see also Pedro Bessa, 'Skittish skirts and scanty silhouettes: the tribulations of gender in modern signage', Visible Language, vol. 42, no, 2, 2008, pp. 119–41. A subtler example of a similar stategy is the British traffic sign for 'children crossing', designed in the 196os by Margaret Calvert. In the pictogram she depicted an older girl leading a young boy by the hand, deliberately reversing the emphasis of the previous sign. See: <www.bbc.co.uk/ news/magazine-15990443>.

39. 'Empirical sociology' (1931) p. 375.

40. Encyclopedia as model' (1936), p. 150. Neurath did imply on a couple of occasions that he would ideally prefer to use non-representational symbols, but that pragmatic considerations prevented this: 'Wholy abstract figures would correspond to the task of the Museum. But since teaching can only be achieved by effect on the observer, it must be taken into account that many people initially have a prejudice against abstract figures. Education towards logical consideration takes some time, it cannot be the matter of an institute, rather that of an epoch. The collaborators in the Museum believe themselves to be working in the spirit of the painter, who from inner impulse prefers more abstract forms in artistic representation.' ('Gesellschaft und Wirtschaft im Lehrbild', 1927, p. 98.) And: 'When people become accustomed to certain signs, one can go further in the direction of simplification.' ('Form und Farbe der Mengenbilder des Gesellschafts- und Wirtschaftsmuseums', 1929, p.193.)
