Appendix A

Otto Neurath

# Social and economic museum of Vienna

1925

'Geselischafts- und Wirtschaftsmuseum in Wien', Österreichische Gemeinde-Zeitung, Jg. 2, Nr. 16, 15 August 1925, pp. 1–12. Translated by Robin Kinross.

## 1: Aims

The present age requires of all of us a rational understanding of social correlations. One can no longer limit general knowledge to reading, arithmetic, writing, and to some knowledge of the natural sciences, literature, and history; one must also explain the social process and make its ways understandable. The pedagogy of this subject is still in its infancy. We possess, however, a whole series of exemplary museums, films, books, through which scientific education is transmitted. Among other things, this means that the educated population supplies the productive sector with a large number of well-prepared enginéers, doctors, printers, electro-technicians. The extraordinarily rapid development of radio may be explained in part thus, that technical ability is to be found in the widest circles. The nineteenth century was rightly called a century of science, a technical century.

Many think that the century of social regulation is here; a century in which the shaping of social life will be comprehended at its deepest levels. The social revolution of recent years, the democratization of public administration, the extension of the rights of workers and the white-collared in industry, has led to more and more people demanding education in social and economic processes. It is not easy to satisfy this demand. The proven results of research in this area are not very comprehensive; also the correlations described are extraordinarily complicated. As regards methods of presentation, we are still just at the beginning.

Modern man has been rather spoiled by cinema and illustration. He takes a great part of his education in pleasurable ways, partly during his leisure time, through visual impressions. If one wishes to spread social education widely, one

must use such means of presentation. The modern poster shows us the way! Scientific processes may be depicted, as it were, directly. One can capture the star system with the help of a series of slide projectors, as has been demonstrated at Jena; now the year's movement of the planets, speeded-up, is run before the spectator within an hour. One can build models of human hearts and can demonstrate the pumping process in detail. But how can one show the inner processes of the social body, the shifts in class structure, the circulation of money and goods, the activities of banks, the connections between income and tuberculosis, between birth figures and mortality rates? Here models are also possible, and graphic presentations. But they require much greater distancing from reality, they place greater demands on the person who devises them, and on the spectator. To create a centre for social and economic education through predominantly visual media – graphics and models – is harder than to establish a centre for technical or medical education, where mistakes can do much less damage. Perhaps the time will come when established conventions have been formulated for the presentation of social-economic findings, so that graphic statistics can be generally read', just as easily as one can now read books or musical notation. Until this is achieved, one must use methods that are direct and comprehensible without such a convention. This is the task of the social museums that will come into being in the present phase of history. These museums are not required to show peculiarities or to collect mementoes. It is therefore not a matter of bringing sentimental objects together, but of forming a collection of educational pictures, models etc., so that it is a systematic whole and a real course of instruction for anyone who, without preparation, wishes to concern himself with social or economic questions. The means to be considered are, above all, graphic presentations, pictures, models, films, slides; but also, illustrations, lectures, publications, and all other appropriate means. The necessity for such a museum can hardly be doubted our age demands it

The museums of the past were first of all places for curios and rarities, collections of loved objects, treasure chests; money-value and scarcity of single show-pieces were fundamental factors. Princes, rich private citizens, city goverņments, and monasteries have later made such unsystematically acquired collections accessible to the common visitor. It is evident that the museums that originate from such collections have something lifeless about them. The visitor feels them to be 'of the past'; he is respectfully pushed past various lances swords, helmets, tattered flags, busts, autographs – mostly arranged decoratively, speaking more to feeling than to understanding, they satisfy the delight in display and have little effect on the sense of purpose. The modern museum seeks to be a teaching museum, a teaching process that works with compact materials. In a modern technical museum, in order to show the development of weapons, one has to show the weapons themselves! To establish a modern museum implies being a teacher. Technical museums will now attach themselves to social museums.

Whereas a technical museum leads one round the highest achievements of the human spirit, a social-economic museum will show what goes to make up the life of the people. In so far as technical things are touched upon it is, therefore, to show the spread of a technical achievement, perhaps to show by how many people it is used – not how it works. A technical ruseum shows building methods, a social-economic museum shows how many houses are unfit for dwelling, how many are fit. But the social-economic museum will occasionally also display technical objects, with explanation; architectural models as well, and anatomical structures for education in health and its social meaning.

It is proposed that the main work of the Museum will, in general, be carried out from a centre – because experience has taught that it is much harder than it seems at first glance to produce useful graphic presentations for the purpose of popular education. Even if one has got over initial faults and mistakes, such as are commonly encountered in presentations designed for popular education, it is not possible to apply immediately and successfully the principles that have perhaps been established. For, although a graphic presentation of statistical quantities and relations may be no longer mistaken, so too it may be no longer stimulating. We must consider factors that can as little be formulated, as recipes, as the means that a good poster artist uses. The training of appropriate skills for the production of really useful pictorial and plastic presentations takes a great deal of time. Not only must the methods of presentation be thoroughly examined for their intellectual quality, but also, dimensions, colours, distribution of space, and so on, must always be revised until a result that will then be satisfactory is achieved; but after some time, when the pedagogy of such a museum has advanced, it will again demand improvement.

## 2: Origins and tasks

The Gemeinde Wien (Municipality of Vienna), which is doing so much for general enlightenment and for education, has resolved to assume responsibility for a social and economic museum. The Museum has been constituted in law as an association [Verein], as explained in a municipal decree, in order to enable other interested organizations to play a part in its development. Besides those of the Gemeinde Wien, the greater part of the funds are provided by the Wiener Arbeiter- und Angestelltenkammer (Vienna chamber of workers and salaried staff), the Gewerkschaftskommission (Organization of trade unions) and the Sozialversicherungsinstitute (Social insurance institutes).

The Gesellschafts- und Wirtschaftsmuseum has not come into existence in one step. First, the Österreichisches Siedlungsmuseum (Austrian settlement museum) was created – this has now been turned into the 'Siedlung und Städtebau' (settlement and town-planning) section of the Gesellschafts- und Wirtschaftsmuseum. This Siedlungsmuseum originated in connection with the last great Siedlungs- und Wohnbauausstellung (settlement and housing exhibition) of 1923,

organized by the Österreichischer Verband für Siedlungs- und Kleingartenwesen (Austrian associ ation for settlement and allotment gardens), with the strongest support of the Gemeinde Wien and the Society of Friends. The Arbeiter- und Angestell tenkammer for Vienna and Lower Austria, the Gemeinwirtschaftliche Siedlungs- und Baustoffanstalt (Cooperative institution for provision of housing and building materials; abbreviated as 'Gesiba'), the Grosseinkaufsgesellschaft der Konsumvereine (wholesale purchasing body of the cooperative movement) and other organizations provided means for its establishment. Graphics, photographs, and models from the exhibition were brought together here. Further material was purchased and constructed, and thus the basic stock for a collection was created. It shows, on a general economic basis, how human settlements develop, how cities are sometimes created by planning, how they sometimes develop through uncoordinated erection of buildings, how the large apartment blocks of the nineteènth century were gradually superseded in almost all areas by the construction of garden suburbs and garden cities.

While important parts of the Kleingarten-, Siedlungs- und Wohnbauausstellung of 1923 could be saved at the last moment for the Siedlungsmuseum, it was arranged in advance that certain items of the Gemeinde Wien's, made for the Hygiene exhibition, would be secured for the Museum. Through close cooperation between the director of the Hygiene exhibition, the muni cipal authorities and the Museum management, unified dimensions were determined for the graphics and photographs and for the production of permanent models in glass cases. The objects exhibited by the Gemeinde Wien will now stay together permanently in the Gesellschafts- und Wirtschaftsmuseum. A partial exhibition is now on display at Parkring 12, Wien I. The principles that were applied to the joint display sections of the Gemeinde Wien and Gesellschafts- und Wirtschaftsmuseum, to the details of frames in particular, also hold good for the sections

prepared by the Arbeiterkammer Wien. A further unification was achieved by the fact that the technical department of the Museum produced graphics not only for itself, but also for the Gesundheitsamt (Department of health), the sanitary, street cleaning, and ambulance services of the Gemeinde Wien.

In this way the section of'settlement and town-planning' and the subject of 'social hygiene' (in the section called 'living standard and culture'), come to be represented most completely in the Gesellschafts- und Wirtschaftsmuseum. But already, once the Siedlungsmuseum had been formed, various things had been done for the 'work and organisation' section, in establishing an introductory section that would be concerned with population and economy. Here the development of organizations for production is shown; the development of industry, banks, and so on; the development of the workers' movement, through unions and cooperative organizations.

The need to turn to the best advantage the experience of the Museum in the field of instructional presentation has led to discussions with official bodies and educational organizations about the production of material for exhibition and educational purposes.

The Museum, as a thoroughly modern educational centre, will supply teaching materials as required. The pedagogy practised here will become a normal part of general educational practice.

Guided tours through the Museum, of parties from schools and associations, will spread the appreciation of this kind of presentation. Successful pictorial presentations could be chosen for slide series and for reproduction.

What are for most people 'dead' statistics will be brought to life. To this end, the Museum has already joined in association with the Statistisches Amt der Gemeinde Wien (Statistical office of the municipality), and is going to collaborate in publishing illustrated pages in the monthly statistical bulletins of the Gemeinde Wien – which will awaken interest in statistics about the community across a wider section of the public. One may hope that other bodies will join in collaboration with the Museum, or take up the suggestion that is offered here.

The Museum will first of all show Austria to the Austrians – its present economic and social structure is still unknown to many people. In this way many will for the first time learn to grasp Austria as a unity, and its connection with the outside world. Here a wide view, over everything that is new, will be drawn upon; something that has come into existence in Vienna above all in recent years. Each Viennese will get to know far more systematically, more simply and more visually than through newspaper articles and lectures, what his city government (for which he does indeed share responsibility) has achieved and what it has yet to do. Here the housing problem and the housing campaign will be shown; the struggle against tuberculosis, alcoholism, and venereal diseases – welfare education of all kinds – will present themselves as, so to say, visual relationships. The foreigner who wishes to inform himself about the new Vienna will obtain an overall view through broad outlines – which he can complete through visiting the places and the sites themselves. Much that is hard to take in 'in reality' – a new housing site, an enormous swimming bath – will be shown in model form here and, at the same time, its place in the whole city and in the totality of the municipal organization.

But the Museum will have to reach out beyond Austria and Vienna, in order to show the making of Austria in the context of world historical development. Here the past is exclusively a means towards understanding the movements of the present. The Museum will present purely factual changes in the common social life: how people have organized production in continually changing forms; how they have provided housing, nourishment, clothing, education, entertainment, science, and construction; to what extent they have been successful in this; how the different groups have participated in work and in human development; reality as a whole, in its sorrows and

its joys, will be presented. What is from the past here is mainly a preparation; for the Museum is devoted to the present and to its transformations.

[3: Organization; not translated here]

Appendix B

# Marie Reidemeister The modern hieroglyphic script 1928

'Die moderne Hieroglyphenschrift: Bildstatistik des Gesellschaftsund Wirtschaftsmuseums in Wien', Daheim, Jg. 65, Nr.7,   
17 November 1928, pp. 3-4.   
Translated by Christopher Burke.

Hieroglyphic script and modern – how do these go together? A 3,ooo year-old script from the childhood of humanity, which has long been superseded and can be nothing more to people today than a pleasing ornament. – But only because we cannot recognize the little pictures quickly enough, nor grasp their sense. Modern people are especially accustomed to reading pictures. We watch films, and we like them all the more when they have little text. We read whole novels from films as we did earlier from books; films go much faster – an expression or a movement must not first be described with many words; on the detour through reading the individual ideas follow much slower than they do in the actual processes that are being described! We also know foreign lands better from photographs than from long descriptions, which we do not take the time to read. In short, we read pictures everywhere because it is quicker. – At the same time, a picture script is beginning to be formed, although not everybody is yet aware of it – there were also illiterates in ancient Egypt. The people today who are concerned with dissemination and popularization of statistical facts feel the need to develop a picture script. Everyone knows that numbers are thoroughly unsuitable for spreading statistical knowledge. Nobody likes to read statistical tables. Most people find statistics boring. In reality it is not

VÖLKER DER ERDE

![](images/3b220f1ebaf2fab18e6100d7fc1565d5eacda2356458ee7468d66841fbb4db38.jpg)  
Eine ganze Figur -100 Millionen Menschen, abgerundet.

1 Weisse und Mischlinge   
2.Neger. Orientalen (Araber, Perser usw)u.Jnder, Jndianer u.Mestizen   
3. Mongolen u. Malaíen

statistics but the endless rows of numbers that bore them. Fundamentally, it is of interest to everybody that there are innumerable millions of black people in the interior of Africa, who may one day burst forth; and it will interest them even more to know that this is not at all true, and that black people only constitute roughly one twentieth of the whole population of earth. If we were to present a table about the relative proportions of peoples on earth, many people would become either scared or bored. – Instead of this we present a picture and we need to waste no more words. – It is apparent what kind of people is represented by each figure, and how many they represent is stated alongside: 1oo million. So each person can count how large the individual human groups are, and do it faster than if they had a numerical table in front of them. Above all, the individual groups can be more quickly compared with each other and the relationships can be better perceived, if this picture has been studied in detail. – In this way other quantities can be represented, for example the division of the same people by

religion. Is this not very like a picture script? One need only know that a certain sign represents a certain quantity; if a sign is repeated, say, three times, then it means that three times the quantity is shown. So quantitative relationships can be directly depicted and grasped, without having to make the detour through those despicable numbers. So it follows that, in this case, picture script is more rapidly readable than numerals and letters This picture script is much closer to the concrete facts of the matter that it conveys, much closer than alphabetic script. That is its charm, which it works on many people, in particular on those unspoiled by too much education, and consequently also on young people. – These pictures should not be too naturalistic. An over-precise adherence to details would distract from the true content of the picture, which lies in the quantitative relationships. It should also be remembered that we are dealing not only with pictures, but also with kinds of 'characters' in a script; this means that we must see to it that the individual characters could be reused in other circumstances in order to facilitate rapid reading through recognition.

It is particularly important for rapid reading that a unified and rational method be generally maintained for the layout and representation. Objections may be heard, like: such homogeneity is boring; people of today need variety! People must be attracted as they are by advertising! Multiple forms are needed to depict the multiformity of reality itself! How back to front all this is! What would the reader say if a new alphabet or a new system of figures was constantly introduced? Instead of reading books, they would be obliged to constantly decode mysterious scripts and would not be able to penetrate meaning due to formal difficulties. Puzzle games are certainly a pleasant activity and it cannot be denied that currently, while the method of pictorial statistics is not familiar to people, part of its charm consists in such mystery. But should this be forever the aim? Should not the aim be instead that readers can quickly and easily orient themselves? Should they not get quickly to the meaning, which in itself provides enough interest?

There is a Social and Economic Museum in Vienna that consists almost entirely of pictures made with the 'Vienna Method'. Here each individual picture is not a curiosity, rather a part of the whole. The knowledge that this Museum mediates is always assumed as common knowledge but it is rarely made known to ordinary people, and in other respects it is difficult to make it known to them: the face' of our environment (division of labour, land use, population density, and a thousand other things that lend a country its character or its appearance), which we all know in isolated, incomplete facets but can never completely know from personal experience. The newspapers, for example, report little about this face'; they explain more about its contortions and scars. In order to understand the reports in the newspapers, each reader must have access to an overview, which, without requiring special effort, informs them of the essentials about lands and peoples. The goal of this Museum is a kind of statistical travelogue in pictures, of which the tendency is indicated by the example above. The Vienna Method has worked out statistical visualization as a strictly systematic hieroglyphic script for the sake of rapid orientation.

Picture script has swiftly conquered wide areas during recent years: major exhibitions cannot do without it any more; nutritional information, charts about the spread of broadcasting, growth in branches of industry, ways of employing the earth's surface, population statistics – all of these can be shown in a clear and easily comprehensible form with it. Increasingly, picture script becomes a framework for exhibitions, around which everything else can be organized.

Appendix C

Marie Neurath

# Social and economic museum of Vienna, 1925-1934

'Gesellschafts- und Wirtschaftsmuseum in Wien, 1925-1934'. Written in German for Josef Docekal, director of the reformed Österreichisches Gesellschafts- und Wirtschaftsmuseum. Typescript ic 8.1a. First published (in German) in Stadler (ed.), Arbeiterbildung in der Zwischenkriegszeit, pp. 246–50. Translation by Christopher Burke.

## FOUNDING

The Österreichische Verband für Siedlungs- und Kleingartenwesen (Austrian association for settlement and allotment gardens), General Secretary Dr Otto Neurath, had a permanent exhibition, 'Museum für Siedlung und Städtebau' (Museum for settlement and town-planning), Parkring 12; the material originated from an open-air exhibition in the Rathauspark, 1923.

Otober 1924: Neurath led city councillor Hugo Breitner through the museum and suggested the founding and creation of a Gesellschaftsund Wirtschaftsmuseum (Social and economic museum). Before the end of the year the district council decided to support and subsidize the association of the 'Gesellschafts- und Wirtschaftsmuseum in Wien'.

Work began on 1.1.1925.

## 1925

OFFIcE xv [borough], Moeringgasse 7, sublet from the Settlers association, where Neurath still carried out his affairs and made the transition. COLLEAGUES Otto Neurath (Director); other employees: Josef Jodlbauer (administration), Marie Reidemeister (office work, practice in handling statistics, under Neurath's direction). Others employed on an hourly basis: draftspeople, who probably also worked for the Settlers association, Rosa Weiser, architect, and a former co-worker of the Deutsches Museum in Munich. Others came later.

PERMANENT EXHIBITION: MuSeum für Siedlung und Städtebau on Parkring, which was gradually redesigned by replacement of charts and models and (in either 1925 or 1926) was reopened as the Gesellschafts- und Wirtschaftsmuseum in Wien. EXHIBITIONS (for which charts of the GeWiMu were provided): Hygiene exhibition in Vienna, May/June.

TECHNIQuE Design sketches initially by Neurath, then by Neurath and Reidemeister together; execution by drawing with indian ink, mostly only black & white; lettering with Redis pen. (See article in the [Österreichische] Gemeinde-Zeitung, also Arbeit und Wirtschaft, Der Aufbau).

## 1926

OFFICE III, Karl Borromäusplatz 3 (borough council office): two small rooms and a bigger room with a dozen workplaces; adjacent room with water and sink.

COLLEAGUES (apart from the three permanent employees): Rosa Weiser (for a while longer); Edith Matzalik, also an architect, for planning of charts and all lettering; Bruno Zuckermann, draftsman, specialist in national costume; Josef Scheer, bookbinder, for mounting charts, assembly, colour mixing and all technical requirements; Fritz Jahnel, talented in drawing, but suited to many things, including administration; Erwin Bernath, Swiss graphic artist, who was in Vienna for further study; many came and went according to the amount of work. Matzalik and Jahnel later became permanent employees and remained until 1934; Scheer and Bernath also became permanently employed and emigrated with us to the Netherlands; Bernath was leading graphic artist of the GeWiMu, at first alone, then alongside Arntz.

## PERMANENT EXHIBITION Parkring 12.

EXHIBITIONS Above all Gesolei (Gesundheitspflege, soziale Fürsorge und Leibesübungen [Health care, social welfare, and physical education]) in Düsseldorf, with many GeWiMu charts for the Vienna municipality and social insurance institutes; city building exhibition in Vienna (Künstlerhaus?).

TECHNiQuE The work process becomes clearer. Reidemeister systematically makes the design sketches (transformation). Execution in technical office: pictogram design, chart planning, application of pictograms, lettering. Charts now in colour, employing large figures. Use of coloured paper: figures cut out and stuck down. Standardization of chart formats.

NEW coNTActs Gerd Arntz in Düsseldorf. Bauhaus: Neurath was invited to its re-opening in Dessau and thereafter several times for lectures, discussions.

1927

OFFicE Karl Borromäusplatz; additionally, during the year, use of rooms in the Zentralsparkasse (Central municipal savings bank), xIv, Ullmannstrasse 44, followed by moving there; two floors, internally connected by a circular staircase.

cOLLEAGUES In addition to permanent employees, Neurath, Jodlbauer, Reidemeister, Bernath, Scheer, Matzalik, Jahnel, came: Dr Friedrich Bauermeister, who had been active at the Settlers association until then, and Elisabeth Buchmann, porcelain painter, who coloured in our slides. In addition, many short term workers. Commissions sent by post to Arntz.

PERMANENT EXHIBITION The principal exhibition in the Volkshalle of the Neues Rathaus (New Town Hall) was opened on 7 December 1927. Parkring 12 became branch exhibition for housing and town-planning. Further branch exhibition: xI1, Am Fuchsenfeld.

EXHIBITIONS Wien und die Wiener (Vienna and the Viennese), in the Messepalast; Burgenland exhibition, Eisenstadt; Das junge Deutschland (Young Germany), Berlin; Health exhibition Calau (Brandenburg Marches); own travelling exhibitions, also in following years (Nuremberg, Hamburg, Breslau, Stuttgart, Berlin etc.). Charts come back to us for our permanent exhibition after use in travelling exhibitions. Stock of charts grows.

TECHNIQUE Now two for transformation (Reidemeister and Bauermeister) and two corresponding working groups, though not strictly divided. Normal charts were joined by lightcharts (on glass, backlit) and magnetic charts (iron sheets, on which magnetic signs were placed); simplified models of buildings, park grounds etc., glass models with floor plans of several storeys. Standardized exhibition stands for the standardized charts; initially used in the travelling exhibitions, then in the Volkshalle and Am Fuchsenfeld.

NEW CoNTACTS Professor Lehmann, geographer, who was consulted about the production of the 8×4 m large magnetic chart of Austria for the anteroom of the Volkshalle; he made us aware of Dr Alois Fischer; architect Dr Josef Frank, who advised on the design of the Volkshalle; Dr Gottlieb, adviser on medical and biological matters; Dr Leo Lederer, whom we commissioned to go to Calau and collect material for publication.

## 1928

NEW COLLEAGUES Erich Meixner (design); Walter Pfitzner (who soon gives up drawing and becomes the photographer of the GeWiMu); Richard Heurteur, compositor and printer; Gerd Arntz comes to Vienna for the duration.

TECHNIQuE Futura type is acquired, for setting and printing of all inscriptions applied to charts. Arntz, woodcut artist, designs pictograms and cuts them in linoleum. Matzalik is taught linocutting. Neurath and Arntz begin to develop the basic forms of pictograms. Compilation of the picture dictionary and the files for T-numbers and N-numbers – the photographs of charts and of natural subjects. Among the latter, photographs of professions for career advice.

ExHIBITIOns Frau und Kind (Woman and child), Messepalast, Vienna; housing and town-planning conference, Paris; Pressa, Cologne.

PUBLICATIONS (Under oWn imprint) Der Kreis Calau; Entwicklung von Landwirtschaft und Gewerbe in Deutschland; Die Gewerkschaften (small, black-andwhite publications). Preparation of the first

publication in colour: Die bunte Welt. Many slides made for own lectures and for lending out.

NEW CONTACTS El Lissitzky, who had designed the Soviet pavillion at Pressa; meeting in Cologne; he then came to Vienna and visited us with his wife Sophie. Frequent meetings later in Russia.

## 1929

EXHIBITIONS Wohnung und Siedlung (Housing and settlement), Linz; Kärnten 1929, Klagenfurt; Wien im Bild (Vienna depicted), Berlin; exhibition at the International peace palace, in Ridderzaal, The Hague; exhibition for the international pedagogical conference, Geneva.

NEW CONTACTS Paul OtIet, Palais Mondial, Brussels, with Lothian Small (League of Nations) and Marie Louise Berg; meeting in Geneva, then visit to Vienna. Discussion of collaboration on the idea of the 'Mundaneum', reproducible museum of the world. (Name later used by Neurath with Otlet's permission.)

PUBLICATIONs Die bunte Welt. Supplement 'Gesellschafts- und Wirtschaftsmuseum in Wien' in Das Bild. Commission from the Bibliographisches Institut in Leipzig to produce a luxury publication. Neurath developed the idea of the folder Gesellschaft und Wirtschaft: bildstatistisches Elementarwerk. Colleagues for the scientific preparation ('the academy'; weekly discussions): Professor Karl Peucker (cartographer), who prepared many new map designs; Dr Robert Bleichsteiner of the Völkerkundemuseum (Ethnology museum); Dr Alois Fischer, the statistician; Dr Schwieger, art historian under Prof. Strygowski at the university, and occasionally a historian of technology. Temporary colleagues for design of charts: Peter Alma, Amsterdam; August Tschinkel, Prague; Hans Thomas, Emil Maenner, Vienna; Heinz Allner, Lotte Beese, Bauhaus; Jan Tschichold for some typographical jobs.

## 1930

PERMANENT EXHIBITIONS PremiseS as before; but new opening at Am Fuchsenfeld with all the

original charts for Gesellschaft und Wirtschaft.   
Second room for the history of Vienna.

EXHIBITIONs Werkbund exhibition, Vienna; Maria Theresa exhibition, Vienna, Künstlerhaus (Dr Buschbeck); Health week, Calau.

schooLs Class visits to the Museum. Conferences with teachers at the Museum office: discussion of themes for school experiments. The city schools inspector allocated us a secondary school for experiments in all classes and subjects. Reports in Die Quelle and Das Bild.

GUIDED TOURS through Vienna. The Vienna municipality becomes world famous; the GeWiMu was commissioned by the town hall to lead guided tours for certain foreigners through the new housing, social facilities, and our Museum in the Volkshalle. We all contributed, but especially Fritz Jahnel and Rudolf Modley; the latter, a law student, worked half days on our administration until he received his doctorate in 1931 and went to the USA. Other, occasional co-workers: Paul Boschan, mathematical statistician with visual ideas; Mrs Daniels-Engelmann, exile from Hungary, in the office.

## 1931

EXHIBITIONS International exhibition for housing and town-planning, Berlin; International hygiene exhibition, Dresden; travelling exhibition, Zagreb. Charts for the Museum of Science and Industry, Chicago (director Waldemar Kaempffert, Neurath's cousin, who looked after Modley); charts and slide presentation for the World Social Economic Planning congress in Amsterdam, organized by Mary van Kleeck, New York, and Mary L. Fleddérus, International Industrial Relations Institute (IRI), The Hague.

coNTACTs M.L. Fleddérus important for our future in the Netherlands, and M. van Kleeck in the USA; they became committee members of the Mundaneum Wien and the Mundaneum in The Hague. – Dr Carl Herz, mayor of Berlin-Kreuzberg, visited the Volkshalle and then Neurath; ordered a museum for Berlin-Kreuzberg. Lively contacts and something like a satellite

branch for us (until 1933). – Members of the Soviet embassy in Vienna reported about our Museum to Moscow. Neurath was invited there to negotiate a contract for instruction. On average five of us should alternate being in Moscow; the work in Vienna carried on in parallel, possible due to double teams: transformation: Reidemeister and Bauermeister; design: Arntz and Bernath; drawing and linocutting: Matzalik and Mrs Bernath; printing etc.: Scheer and [Friedrich] Vojtíšek; Peter Alma, Fritz Jahnel, Mrs Arntz, Heinz Kaufler (transformation) helped out occasionally. - The work in Moscow lasted until 1934. Bauermeister alone remained in Moscow [Matzalik also].

FOUNDING of the 'Mundaneum Wien' for international work.

## 1932

OFFICE One floor was given up.

cOLLEAGUES Sociologically interested volunteers: Marie Jahoda, Oskar Umrath, Rudolf Brunngraber. PUBLICATIONS (Under own imprint) Fernunterricht, renamed Bildstatistik, with material and depictions for school use. Technik und Menschheit.

coNTACTs Mrs Ann Brenner from Survey Graphic, New York, comes to Vienna and requests illustrations for Survey Graphic; years of collaboration. Dr de Kanter and his wife, The Hague, request the production of several charts for their 'Vredeshuis', a private, pacifist institute.

BRANCHES ABROAD Professor Posthumus, director of the Economic-historical Library in Amsterdam, offers a room for a branch exhibition. Not far from there we are given a room in the Museum van den Arbeid (Museum of work). Administration of the branch by Miss Mariouw-Smith under supervision of M.L. Fleddérus. – In London the World Association for Adult Education (Mr [A. E.] Twentyman) offers us its address for a branch office; administration by Brenda Voysey, former colleague of M. L. Fleddérus at IRI. Advisory committee: Prof. Delisle Burns, historian; Prof. Macmurray, philosopher; Mr Farquharson, LePlay House,

sociologist; Mr Lambert, The Listener, BBC. Charts at the agent Kathleen Boland. – In New York our interests taken on by M. van Kleeck (Russell Sage Foundation); under her supervision Rudolf Modley explored possibilities for our work in the USA.

## 1933

PERMANENT EXHIBITIONS Alongside those already established, the Zeitschau (Topical show) was added, in a corner shop of the Tuchlauben, next to the head office of the Städtische Versicherungsanstalt (Municipal insurance service), which wanted to attract people to this corner. Photos and diagrams of topical events; apparatuses for testing skill or intelligence (adviser for this, Dr Gustav Ichheiser); in addition charts, as elsewhere. Many visitors.

TRiPs Neurath went back and forth between Vienna and Moscow often between 1931 and 1934; apart from that lectures in Sweden and Norway, and for the first time to the usA. In summer, participation in an international architectural congress on a ship between Marseilles and Athens; slide presentation in Athens; contact with Sigfried Giedion (Zurich), Cornelis van Eesteren (Amsterdam), László Moholy-Nagy (Bauhaus), among others.

FOuNDING of the International Foundation for Visual Education in The Hague, with help from M.L. Fleddérus, Dr de Kanter (lawyer) and Mrs de Kanter.

PUBLICATIONS Bildstatistik nach Wiener Methode in der Schule. Beginning of work on two books in Basic English; correspondence with C. K. Ogden, who developed Basic English and propagated it. Illustrations in a book for the London Press Exchange, together with an exhibition of the charts.

## 1934

cLosuRE After the February unrest we prepare to move. Sealing off and dissolution of the 'Gesellschafts- und Wirtschaftsmuseum in Wien' in April 1934. To Holland go: Neurath,

Reidemeister, Arntz, Bernath, Scheer; a few others are active in Moscow until autumn 1934: Bauermeister, Matzalik, Vojtíšek.

coNTACTS Norwegian workers' education centre, orders a series of charts for exhibition and for reproduction as a slide series; continuation and execution in Holland. Equally so with the books in Basic English. In these the name Isotype was used for the first time.

![](images/2671a1387a63a9936f1e4e2d0fb8d95e89621ea38fdce00ad6d98c34d0f51a69.jpg)

![](images/6907be94e0f77854e7f9a83eb56fb37f83ce68f63c431be406c29901b76bb59e.jpg)

![](images/95d083aa0156e0b294ea19360dd14f11f400fa9b5c22c5d63fead4a40b317ff8.jpg)

The work in graphic communication carried out by Otto Neurath and his associates – now commonly known as Isotype – was conceived and developed in the 192os as 'the Vienna Method of Pictorial Statistics'. This approach to designing information had from its inception the power to grow and spread internationally. Political developments in Europe played their part in its development, and production moved to the Netherlands (1934) and to England (194o), where the Isotype Institute continued to produce work until 1971.

The Austrian, Dutch, and English years of Isotype are described here freshly and extensively. There are chapters on the notable extensions of Isotype to the ussR, the usA, and Africa. Isotype work in film and in designing for children is fully documented and discussed.

Bringing together the latest research, and based on a wealth of archival material, this book is the first comprehensive, detailed account of its subject.

## A人中寅家宁党院曾中火
